## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define a Deterministic Transition System (DTS) with a formal tuple. State the key property that distinguishes a DTS from a general Labeled Transition System (LTS).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

A Deterministic Transition System is a 4-tuple:
$$DTS = (S, \ S_0, \ \Sigma, \ \rightarrow)$$

where:
- $S$ is a finite non-empty set of states **(1 mark)**
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of initial states **(0.5 marks)**
- $\Sigma$ is a finite set of action labels **(0.5 marks)**
- $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times \Sigma \times S$ is the transition relation **(0.5 marks)**

**Distinguishing Property**: In a DTS, for every state $s \in S$ and every action $\alpha \in \Sigma$, there is **at most one** successor state $s'$ such that $s \xrightarrow{\alpha} s'$. Formally: $\forall s, \forall \alpha : \mid \{s' \mid s \xrightarrow{\alpha} s'\} \mid \leq 1$. **(0.5 marks)**

This determinism axiom does **not** hold in a general LTS, where a state may have multiple successors for the same action.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**State and explain the "Path Uniqueness Theorem" for Deterministic Transition Systems. Mention its significance in automated verification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Theorem Statement (1.5 marks)**: In a DTS, given an initial state $s_0 \in S_0$ and a finite action sequence $\sigma = \alpha_0 \alpha_1 \ldots \alpha_{n-1} \in \Sigma^*$, there exists **at most one** execution path:
$$\pi = s_0 \xrightarrow{\alpha_0} s_1 \xrightarrow{\alpha_1} \ldots \xrightarrow{\alpha_{n-1}} s_n$$

**Explanation (1 mark)**: The theorem follows directly from the determinism axiom. Once $s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_{k-1}$ are uniquely determined, the transition $s_{k-1} \xrightarrow{\alpha_{k-1}} s_k$ yields a unique $s_k$ because the post-image set has cardinality at most 1.

**Significance (0.5 marks)**: It reduces the verification of branching-time properties to the analysis of a single path, enabling polynomial-time algorithms for LTL model checking and avoiding the state explosion inherent in non-deterministic systems.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Consider a Deterministic Transition System modeling a **2-bit binary counter** with the following specification:
- $S = \{00, 01, 10, 11\}$
- $S_0 = \{00\}$
- $\Sigma = \{increment\}$
- Transitions: $00 \xrightarrow{inc} 01$, $01 \xrightarrow{inc} 10$, $10 \xrightarrow{inc} 11$, $11 \xrightarrow{inc} 00$ (overflow)

**(i)** Draw the DTS graph. **(2 marks)**
**(ii)** Verify using reachability analysis that the property $\mathbf{EF} \ (s = 11)$ holds. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Prove that the system is deadlock-free. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **determinization procedure** (subset construction) that converts a non-deterministic LTS into an equivalent DTS. Illustrate with a small example showing the state-space blow-up. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question A

**(a)(i) DTS Graph (2 marks)**:

```mermaid
graph LR
    c00((00)) -- inc --> c01((01))
    c01((01)) -- inc --> c10((10))
    c10((10)) -- inc --> c11((11))
    c11((11)) -- inc --> c00((00))
```

The graph has 4 nodes (states) and 4 directed edges (transitions), forming a single cycle of length 4. The initial state is `00` (double-circled or marked).

**(a)(ii) Reachability Analysis (2 marks)**:

To verify $EF (s = 11)$:
- Step 1: Initialize $Reached = \{00\}$.
- Step 2: $Post(00) = \{01\}$. Update $Reached = \{00, 01\}$.
- Step 3: $Post(01) = \{10\}$. Update $Reached = \{00, 01, 10\}$.
- Step 4: $Post(10) = \{11\}$. Update $Reached = \{00, 01, 10, 11\}$.
- Step 5: State $11$ is in $Reached$. Therefore $EF (s = 11)$ is **TRUE**. ✓

**(a)(iii) Deadlock-Freeness Proof (3 marks)**:

A deadlock exists in a state $s$ if $s$ has **no outgoing transitions** for any action in $\Sigma$.

In the 2-bit counter DTS, for every state $s \in S$:
- $Post(00) = \{01\} \neq \emptyset$
- $Post(01) = \{10\} \neq \emptyset$
- $Post(10) = \{11\} \neq \emptyset$
- $Post(11) = \{00\} \neq \emptyset$

Since every state has at least one outgoing transition, the DTS is **deadlock-free**. ∎

**(b) Determinization via Subset Construction (7 marks)**:

**Concept (2 marks)**: Given a non-deterministic LTS $LTS = (S, S_0, \Sigma, \rightarrow_N)$ where $|\{s' \mid s \xrightarrow{\alpha}_N s'\}|$ may be greater than 1, the **subset construction** (also called *Rabin-Scott powerset construction*) builds an equivalent DTS over the *powerset* of the original state space.

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks)**:

1. New state space: $S_{DTS} = 2^S$
2. Initial state: $S_{0,DTS} = S_0$ (a singleton set if $|S_0| = 1$)
3. Transition: $T \xrightarrow{\alpha}_{DTS} T'$ where $T' = \bigcup_{s \in T} Post_{LTS}(s, \alpha)$

**Example with State-Space Blowup (2 marks)**:

Consider an LTS with 3 states $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ where $q_0$ has two successors on action $a$ (namely $q_1$ and $q_2$).

- Original LTS: 3 states.
- Derived DTS: $2^3 = 8$ states: $\emptyset, \{q_0\}, \{q_1\}, \{q_2\}, \{q_0,q_1\}, \{q_0,q_2\}, \{q_1,q_2\}, \{q_0,q_1,q_2\}$.

This exponential blowup ($2^{|S|}$) is the **central cost** of determinization and is the main reason why verification of non-deterministic systems remains computationally hard.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define a Deterministic Transition System. With a suitable example, differentiate between a DTS and a Kripke Structure. Show that any DTS can be viewed as a Kripke Structure (or vice versa) under appropriate mapping. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a DTS modeling a **3-state mutual exclusion protocol** with states $\{N (Non-Critical), T (Trying), C (Critical)\}$, $S_0 = \{N\}$, and transitions $N \xrightarrow{request} T$, $T \xrightarrow{enter} C$, $C \xrightarrow{release} N$.

**(i)** Enumerate all execution paths of length 4. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Verify the LTL safety property $\mathbf{G} \ (\neg (s_1 = C \land s_2 = C))$ under the assumption that this is a single-process system. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Discuss how the analysis would change if the system were extended to two processes. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question B

**(a) Definition + DTS vs. Kripke (7 marks)**:

**DTS Definition (1 mark)**: A DTS is a 4-tuple $TS = (S, S_0, \Sigma, \rightarrow)$ where transitions are labeled by *actions*.

**Kripke Structure Definition (1 mark)**: A Kripke Structure is a tuple $K = (S, S_0, R, L)$ where $R \subseteq S \times S$ is a *stateless* transition relation and $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is a labeling function over atomic propositions.

**Key Differences (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | DTS | Kripke Structure |
|---|---|---|
| Transition label | Action $\alpha \in \Sigma$ | None (unlabeled) |
| State label | None (or implicit) | Set of atomic propositions $L(s)$ |
| Primary logic | Action-based / process algebra | Temporal logic (CTL, LTL) |
| Determinism | Optional, constrained by axiom | Optional, constrained by $\mid Post(s) \mid \leq 1$ |

**Inter-translation (2 marks)**:
- DTS → Kripke: For each transition $s \xrightarrow{\alpha} s'$, introduce a fresh intermediate state $s_\alpha$ labeled with proposition $\alpha$. This makes actions into state-labels.
- Kripke → DTS: For each transition $(s, s') \in R$, introduce a unique action label $\alpha_{s,s'}$. This embeds the state-labeling structure into the action labels.

---

**(b)(i) Enumeration of Paths of Length 4 (3 marks)**:

The DTS has a single path of length 4 (due to determinism):
$$\pi = N \xrightarrow{request} T \xrightarrow{enter} C \xrightarrow{release} N \xrightarrow{request} T$$

States along the path: $N, T, C, N, T$.

Action sequence: $request, enter, release, request$.

**Note**: Because the system is deterministic, the path is **unique** for any given action sequence. If we consider *all possible* action sequences of length 4, each sequence yields a unique path.

---

**(b)(ii) LTL Safety Verification (2 marks)**:

The property $\mathbf{G} \ (\neg (s_1 = C \land s_2 = C))$ asserts that two processes can never be in the critical section simultaneously.

For a single-process system, $s_2$ does not exist. The property reduces to $\mathbf{G} \ (s = C \rightarrow \text{valid})$, which is trivially true since the DTS has well-defined transitions.

Using model checking:
- $Sat(\mathbf{G} \phi) = \bigcap_{i \geq 0} Pre^i(Sat(\phi))$
- Initial state $N$ satisfies $\phi$ trivially.
- By forward simulation, every reachable state satisfies $\phi$. **Property holds**. ✓

---

**(b)(iii) Extension to Two Processes (2 marks)**:

For two processes, the state space explodes: $S = \{N, T, C\}^2$ has $3^2 = 9$ states. The interleaving of transitions from two processes creates a non-deterministic structure (e.g., from $(N, N)$, either process may take the `request` action). The system is **no longer a pure DTS** because the scheduler introduces non-determinism in the interleaving of actions.

To restore determinism, one must fix an interleaving policy (e.g., round-robin), effectively building a *product DTS* whose state space is $3^2 \times 2 = 18$ states (including the scheduler's turn). Verification of mutual exclusion in the two-process case requires CTL model checking over the larger state space.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing determinism in transitions with determinism in initial states**: Students often write "$S_0$ must be a singleton in a DTS." This is **WRONG**. A DTS can have $|S_0| > 1$; the determinism constraint applies only to the **transition relation**, not the initial state set.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the action label in the formal definition**: A DTS tuple must include the action set $\Sigma$. Omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mistaking "labeled" for "deterministic"**: All deterministic systems are labeled (or can be made labeled), but not all labeled systems are deterministic. Do not use the terms interchangeably.
>
> 4. **In path enumeration, ignoring action sequences**: When asked to "enumerate all paths," students must specify both the **state sequence** and the **action sequence** that generates each path. Showing only the state sequence is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the $2^{|S|}$ complexity note**: Whenever the question involves determinization, mentioning the **exponential state-space blowup** is a high-yield extra credit point.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Recap**: A DTS is $(S, S_0, \Sigma, \rightarrow)$ with the **determinism axiom** $\mid Post(s, \alpha) \mid \leq 1$.
- **Core Differentiator**: Determinism in a DTS applies to *transitions*, **not** to *initial states*.
- **Key Theorem**: **Path Uniqueness** — same action sequence from the same start state yields a **unique** path.
- **DTS vs. LTS**: DTS has $\leq 1$ successor per (state, action); LTS allows multiple.
- **DTS vs. Kripke**: DTS has *action labels* on transitions; Kripke has *atomic proposition labels* on states.
- **Determinization Cost**: Converting LTS → DTS uses the **subset construction**, costing $O(2^{|S|})$ states.
- **Algorithmic Win**: DTS enables **polynomial-time** LTL model checking via path uniqueness.
- **Deadlock Test**: A DTS is deadlock-free iff every reachable state has at least one outgoing transition.
- **Reachability**: $EF \phi$ is verified by computing $Post^*(S_0)$ and checking if $Sat(\phi) \cap Post^*(S_0) \neq \emptyset$.
- **Engineering Domains**: Hardware FSMs, communication protocols, discrete control systems, embedded software.
- **Kripke Conversion Rule**: To convert DTS → Kripke, split each action-labeled transition $s \xrightarrow{\alpha} s'$ into two transitions via an intermediate state labeled with $\alpha$.
- **Notation Reminder**: Use $(S, S_0, \Sigma, \rightarrow)$ for DTS; use $(S, S_0, R, L)$ for Kripke Structure; do not mix.
- **Common Trap**: The "deterministic" in DTS does **not** imply *complete* — a state may have zero outgoing transitions (deadlock), and the system is still a valid DTS.
- **Complexity Class**: Reachability and cycle detection in DTS are in **P** (polynomial time), unlike the **PSPACE** complexity in general LTS.
- **Verification Tool Tip**: SPIN, NuSMV, and CBMC all internally use DTS-style or BDD-compressed models for efficient verification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Executions - Execution Fragment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Executions - Execution Fragment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Transition System — The Foundation Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **KTU 2024 PECNT526 syllabus**, executions and execution fragments are defined over a **Transition System (TS)** — also known as a **Kripke Structure** in the model-checking literature (Clarke, Grumberg, Peled; Manna & Pnueli).

A **Transition System** is a 5-tuple:
$$TS = \langle S, \rightarrow, I, AP, L \rangle$$

where:
- $S$ — a **non-empty finite set of states**.
- $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times S$ — the **transition relation** (each pair $(s, s')$ is a directed edge).
- $I \subseteq S$ — the **set of initial states**.
- $AP$ — a set of **atomic propositions** (Boolean variables describing state properties).
- $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ — the **labeling function** mapping each state to the propositions true in it.

### 1.2 Execution (Full / Infinite Path)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Execution).** An **execution** of a transition system $TS$ is an **infinite sequence of states**
> $$\sigma \;=\; s_0,\, s_1,\, s_2,\, \ldots$$
> such that for every $i \geq 0$, the pair $(s_i, s_{i+1})$ belongs to the transition relation, i.e., $s_i \rightarrow s_{i+1}$.

Formally:
$$\sigma : \mathbb{N} \rightarrow S, \quad \text{with} \quad \forall i \ge 0 : (s_i, s_{i+1}) \in \rightarrow$$

Executions are required to be **infinite** because temporal logics (LTL, CTL) are interpreted over **infinite behaviors** of reactive and concurrent systems.

### 1.3 Execution Fragment (Finite Path)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Execution Fragment).** An **execution fragment** (also called a *path fragment* or *finite path*) is a **finite non-empty sequence of states**
> $$\rho \;=\; s_0,\, s_1,\, \ldots,\, s_k \quad (k \ge 0)$$
> such that for every $0 \le i < k$, the transition $s_i \rightarrow s_{i+1}$ holds.

A fragment can have any **length $\ge 1$**. A fragment consisting of a single state $s$ (with $k=0$) is called a **state fragment** and is trivially valid.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Metro Map

Imagine a metro rail network:

| Concept in TS | Metro Analogy |
|---------------|---------------|
| State $s \in S$ | A metro station |
| Transition $s \rightarrow s'$ | A track connecting two stations |
| Initial state $s_0 \in I$ | The originating/depot station |
| **Execution** $\sigma$ | A metro train that runs **forever** (looping service) — covers an infinite itinerary |
| **Execution Fragment** $\rho$ | A **finite ride** — boarding at one station and alighting at a later one |
| **Initial Fragment** | A ride that **starts at the depot** (an initial state) |
| **Maximal Fragment** | A ride that goes as far as the line allows — either hits a **terminal station** (dead-end state with no outgoing track) or loops forever |

This makes the abstraction immediately clear: a fragment is just a **window** or **clip** of an execution.

### 1.5 Variants of Execution Fragments

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following special classes of fragments are **frequently tested** in KTU examinations and are central to LTL/CTL semantics.

- **Initial Fragment:** $\rho$ such that $\rho[0] \in I$ — starts from an initial state.
- **Maximal Fragment:** A fragment that **cannot be extended further**, either because:
  1. It is **infinite** (so it is already complete), or
  2. It is finite and its last state is a **terminal/dead-end state** (no outgoing transition).
- **Prefix / Suffix:** $\rho$ is a prefix of $\tau$ if $\rho$ appears at the start of $\tau$. A suffix appears at the end.
- **Induced Fragment of an Execution:** Any finite window cut out from an infinite execution $\sigma$ is an execution fragment of $TS$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition — How a Fragment is Built

An execution fragment is constructed by iteratively applying the transition relation:

1. **Choose a starting state** $s_0 \in S$. (For an *initial* fragment, additionally require $s_0 \in I$.)
2. **Apply a transition:** Pick a state $s_1$ such that $s_0 \rightarrow s_1$.
3. **Repeat step 2** for each subsequent index until a termination criterion is met.
4. **Termination conditions** that produce a *maximal* fragment:
   - **Infinite extension** — the transition relation produces an endless sequence; or
   - **Deadlock** — at the last state $s_k$, there exists **no** $s' \in S$ with $s_k \rightarrow s'$.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Property

- **Why infinite executions?** Reactive and concurrent systems (operating systems, controllers, servers) are designed to **never terminate** — they keep responding to inputs. Model checkers must therefore reason about *infinite behaviors*.
- **Why study fragments?** A *correctness argument* (proof) typically focuses on a **finite prefix** that establishes a property (e.g., "the request is eventually granted"), and a fragment gives the precise mathematical handle on such finite reasoning.
- **Why the maximality condition?** In verification, an execution fragment represents a **partial observation**. The maximal fragment closes the observation, giving the verifier the **full witness** of system behavior starting from $s_0$.

### 2.3 Concatenation of Fragments

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two fragments can be **concatenated** only when the last state of the first matches the first state of the second. This is the foundation of *compositional* reasoning in model checking.

For fragments $\rho = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k$ and $\tau = t_0 t_1 \ldots t_m$:

$$\rho \cdot \tau \;=\; s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k t_1 t_2 \ldots t_m \quad \text{defined iff} \quad s_k = t_0$$

The shared state $s_k = t_0$ is "absorbed" — it is counted only once in the concatenation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Defining Condition | Length / Bound |
|--------|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| $TS$ | Transition System | $\langle S, \rightarrow, I, AP, L \rangle$ | Finite components |
| $\sigma = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ | Execution (infinite) | $\forall i \ge 0 : s_i \rightarrow s_{i+1}$ | $\vert\sigma\vert = \aleph_0$ |
| $\rho = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k$ | Execution Fragment | $\forall 0 \le i < k : s_i \rightarrow s_{i+1}$ | $\vert\rho\vert_{\text{states}} = k+1$ |
| $\vert\vert\rho\vert\vert$ | Number of transitions in $\rho$ | $\vert\vert\rho\vert\vert = k$ | $k \ge 0$ |
| $\rho[0]$ | First state of $\rho$ | $s_0$ | State |
| $\rho[\text{last}]$ | Last state of $\rho$ | $s_k$ | State |
| $\rho^i$ | Suffix of $\rho$ starting at index $i$ | $s_i s_{i+1} \ldots s_k$ | Sub-fragment |
| $\rho \cdot \tau$ | Concatenation | $s_k = t_0$ | $\vert\rho \cdot \tau\vert = k + m$ |
| $\rho_0$ | Initial Fragment | $\rho_0[0] \in I$ | — |
| $\rho_{\max}$ | Maximal Fragment | Cannot be extended | Terminal or infinite |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Model Checking (CTL/LTL):** Formulas like $\mathbf{G}\, p$ ("globally $p$") or $\mathbf{F}\, q$ ("eventually $q$") are evaluated by examining **executions** rooted at initial states; **fragments** are used to construct counter-examples.
- **Runtime Verification:** A monitor observes a **finite execution fragment** of the running system and checks if a temporal property holds.
- **Hardware Verification (RTL/SoC):** State-space exploration of a digital circuit produces a transition system; executions correspond to **input sequences** and fragments to **prefix traces**.
- **Concurrent Software:** Executions of multi-threaded programs model all possible **interleavings** of thread actions; fragments model partial schedules seen during testing.
- **Cyber-Physical Systems:** Executions of a controller correspond to **sensor-actuator histories**; fragments correspond to sampled observation windows.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Length Arithmetic on Fragments

Let $\rho = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k$ and $\tau = t_0 t_1 \ldots t_m$ be two valid execution fragments of $TS$ such that $s_k = t_0$.

**Step 1 — Count the states in each fragment:**
The fragment $\rho$ contains the states $\{s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_k\}$, so its state count is:

$$\text{states}(\rho) \;=\; k + 1$$

**Step 2 — Count the transitions in each fragment:**
Each transition corresponds to one index $i$ with $0 \le i < k$:

$$\text{trans}(\rho) \;=\; \vert\vert\rho\vert\vert \;=\; k$$

**Step 3 — Form the concatenation and count its states:**
The concatenated fragment is $\rho \cdot \tau = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_{k-1} s_k t_1 t_2 \ldots t_m$. The state $s_k = t_0$ is shared, so the total state count is:

$$\text{states}(\rho \cdot \tau) \;=\; (k+1) + m \;=\; k + m + 1$$

**Step 4 — Count the transitions of the concatenation:**
The shared state contributes transitions both from the end of $\rho$ and from the start of $\tau$, but the *internal* transition $s_{k-1} \rightarrow s_k$ (or $s_k \rightarrow t_1$) is consistent — so total transitions is simply the sum:

$$\text{trans}(\rho \cdot \tau) \;=\; k + m \;=\; \vert\vert\rho\vert\vert + \vert\vert\tau\vert\vert$$

**Step 5 — Verify closure under concatenation:**
If $\rho$ satisfies $s_i \rightarrow s_{i+1}$ for $0 \le i < k$ and $\tau$ satisfies $t_j \rightarrow t_{j+1}$ for $0 \le j < m$, and the join $s_k = t_0$ holds, then the union of conditions is precisely the condition required for $\rho \cdot \tau$. Hence the concatenation is a valid execution fragment.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

Consider a transition system with states $S = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and transitions $A \rightarrow B$, $A \rightarrow C$, $B \rightarrow C$, $C \rightarrow D$, $D \rightarrow C$, $D \rightarrow E$.

**Fragment $\rho_1 = A\, B\, C$:**

$$\text{states}(\rho_1) = 3, \quad \text{trans}(\rho_1) = 2$$

**Fragment $\rho_2 = C\, D\, E$:**

$$\text{states}(\rho_2) = 3, \quad \text{trans}(\rho_2) = 2$$

**Concatenation** (valid because last$(\rho_1) = C =$ first$(\rho_2)$):

$$\rho_1 \cdot \rho_2 \;=\; A\, B\, C\, D\, E$$

$$\text{states}(\rho_1 \cdot \rho_2) = 5, \quad \text{trans}(\rho_1 \cdot \rho_2) = 4$$

Verification by the formula:
$$\text{states} \;=\; k + m + 1 \;=\; 2 + 2 + 1 \;=\; 5 \quad \checkmark$$
$$\text{trans} \;=\; k + m \;=\; 2 + 2 \;=\; 4 \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Extension of a Fragment to a Maximal Fragment

**Algorithm (informal proof):** Given a fragment $\rho = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k$ that is **not maximal**, there exists at least one state $s' \in S$ with $s_k \rightarrow s'$. Append $s'$:

$$\rho' \;=\; s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k s'$$

Repeat the operation. Either:

- The process produces an **infinite sequence** (a maximal fragment of infinite length), or
- The process **terminates** at a state with no outgoing transitions (a finite maximal fragment).

By the **König's Lemma** (applied to the tree of all extensions rooted at $\rho$), at least one maximal extension is guaranteed to exist in any finite-branching transition system.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Fragment Classification

| Property | Definition | Necessary? | KTU Significance |
|----------|------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Validity | Each consecutive pair is a transition | Always | Definition |
| Initiation | $\rho[0] \in I$ | For initial fragment | Counter-example starts here |
| Maximality | No extension possible | For completeness | LTL evaluated on maximal/infinite |
| Suffixed form | $\rho^i$ for some $i$ | Used in LTL semantics | $\sigma^i \models \phi$ definition |
| Non-empty | $\vert\rho\vert \ge 1$ | Always | Empty path excluded |
| Concatenable | last$(\rho) =$ first$(\tau)$ | Optional | Compositional reasoning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Transition System with Highlighted Executions and Fragment

```mermaid
graph LR
    s0((s0 - initial)) -->|tick| s1((s1))
    s1 -->|a| s2((s2))
    s2 -->|b| s3((s3))
    s3 -->|c| s4((s4))
    s4 -->|d| s2
    s3 -->|e| s5((s5 - terminal))
    classDef init fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef exec fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef frag fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:3px
    classDef term fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    class s0 init
    class s1,s2,s3,s4 exec
    class s2 exec
    class s5 term
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **green** node $s_0$ is the **initial state**.
- The **blue** path $s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow s_4 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow \ldots$ forms an **infinite execution** $\sigma$ (it loops through the cycle $s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow s_4$).
- The **red** node $s_5$ is a **terminal state** — no outgoing transitions.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Relation Between Execution, Fragment, and Maximal Fragment

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Transition System TS] --> B[Initial States I]
    A --> C[State Space S]
    A --> D[Transition Relation R]
    B --> E[Start Point of Execution]
    C --> F[Build Sequence of States]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Execution sigma - infinite]
    F --> H[Execution Fragment rho - finite]
    G --> I[Maximal Fragment - infinite]
    H --> J[Maximal Fragment - finite - terminal]
    H --> K[Extend via transition relation]
    K --> I
    K --> J
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style J fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Constructing an Execution Fragment

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User/Verifier
    participant TS as Transition System
    participant Buf as Fragment Buffer
    U->>TS: Query initial state s0
    TS-->>Buf: s0
    U->>TS: Request successor of s0
    TS-->>Buf: s1
    U->>TS: Request successor of s1
    TS-->>Buf: s2
    U->>TS: Check if s2 has successor
    alt s2 has no successor
        TS-->>Buf: TERMINAL marker
        Buf-->>U: Maximal fragment s0 s1 s2
    else s2 has successor
        TS-->>Buf: s3
        Buf-->>U: Continued fragment
    end
```

### 4.4 Worked Example Diagram — Concrete Mutual-Exclusion-Like System

```mermaid
graph LR
    n0((n0 idle)) -->|request| n1((n1 waiting))
    n1 -->|grant| n2((n2 critical))
    n2 -->|release| n0
    n1 -->|abort| n0
    classDef state fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px
    class n0,n1,n2 state
```

- A fragment of length 3: $n_0 \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2$ (initial fragment, finite, not maximal because $n_2$ has a successor).
- A maximal fragment of length 3: $n_0 \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_0$ — but wait, since $n_0$ has a successor, the only **truly maximal finite** fragment would be a path ending at a dead-end. If we add a transition $n_2 \rightarrow n_2$ (self-loop), then $n_0 \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \ldots$ is the unique maximal **infinite** execution.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECNT526]**

**Q1.** Define an *execution fragment* of a transition system. Differentiate between an *initial fragment* and a *maximal fragment* with a one-line distinction each. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
An execution fragment of a transition system $TS$ is a finite (or infinite) sequence of states $\rho = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k$ such that $s_i \rightarrow s_{i+1}$ for every $0 \le i < k$. An **initial fragment** has its first state in $I$ (i.e., $\rho[0] \in I$), while a **maximal fragment** cannot be extended — it is either infinite or ends in a state with no outgoing transition. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for the two distinctions.)*

---

**Q2.** State the formal definition of an *execution* in model checking. Why is an execution required to be infinite rather than finite? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
An execution of $TS = \langle S, \rightarrow, I, AP, L \rangle$ is an infinite sequence $\sigma = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ such that for all $i \ge 0$, $s_i \rightarrow s_{i+1}$. Executions are infinite because reactive and concurrent systems are *non-terminating* by design, and temporal logic semantics (LTL/CTL) are defined over infinite traces to properly capture properties like *liveness* (e.g., "eventually $p$"). *(3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for justification.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice — Two alternatives given. Answer ANY ONE full question.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a)** Define a *transition system* formally. With a neat diagram of a 5-state transition system, label the components $S, \rightarrow, I, AP, L$ and show one execution and one execution fragment. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A transition system is a 5-tuple $TS = \langle S, \rightarrow, I, AP, L \rangle$ where:
- $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$ — finite non-empty set of states.
- $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times S$ — transition relation: $\{s_0 \rightarrow s_1, s_1 \rightarrow s_2, s_2 \rightarrow s_3, s_3 \rightarrow s_2, s_2 \rightarrow s_4\}$.
- $I = \{s_0\}$ — initial state.
- $AP = \{p, q\}$ — atomic propositions.
- $L(s_0) = \{p\}$, $L(s_1) = \{p\}$, $L(s_2) = \{q\}$, $L(s_3) = \{p, q\}$, $L(s_4) = \emptyset$.

**Execution:** $\sigma = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_2, s_3, s_2, \ldots$ (infinite, looping through $s_2, s_3$).

**Execution Fragment:** $\rho = s_0, s_1, s_2$ — a valid initial fragment of length 3. *(7 marks — 2 for definition, 3 for diagram with labels, 2 for execution/fragment identification.)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of *concatenation* of execution fragments. State the necessary condition. With an example from part (a), show how two fragments are combined and verify the resulting state count. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Concatenation $\rho \cdot \tau$ of two execution fragments is defined iff the last state of $\rho$ equals the first state of $\tau$.

Formally, for $\rho = s_0 \ldots s_k$ and $\tau = t_0 \ldots t_m$:

$$\rho \cdot \tau \text{ is valid iff } s_k = t_0, \quad \rho \cdot \tau = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k t_1 t_2 \ldots t_m$$

**Example:** Let $\rho_1 = s_0 s_1 s_2$ and $\rho_2 = s_2 s_3 s_2$ from part (a). Then:

$$\rho_1 \cdot \rho_2 = s_0 s_1 s_2 s_3 s_2$$

Verification:
- $\rho_1$ has $k = 2$ transitions, $\rho_2$ has $m = 2$ transitions.
- The shared state $s_2$ is the join point.
- Resulting state count: $k + m + 1 = 2 + 2 + 1 = 5$ states $\checkmark$.
- Each consecutive pair is a valid transition by construction $\checkmark$. *(7 marks — 2 for definition, 1 for condition, 4 for worked example.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Differentiate between a *finite execution fragment* and an *infinite execution* of a transition system. Construct a transition system in which a fragment can be extended to *two distinct* maximal fragments, and justify. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Finite Fragment $\rho$ | Infinite Execution $\sigma$ |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Length | $k+1$ states, $k$ transitions | Countably infinite |
| Termination | Ends in terminal state | Never ends |
| Suited for | Counter-examples, prefix reasoning | LTL/CTL satisfaction |
| Maximality | Only if terminal | Always maximal |

**Construction (two maximal extensions):** Consider $TS$ with $I = \{s_0\}$, transitions $s_0 \rightarrow s_1$, $s_0 \rightarrow s_2$, $s_1 \rightarrow s_1$ (self-loop), and $s_2 \rightarrow s_2$ (self-loop).

Take the fragment $\rho = s_0$. It can be extended as:
- $\sigma_1 = s_0 s_1 s_1 s_1 \ldots$ (infinite maximal execution).
- $\sigma_2 = s_0 s_2 s_2 s_2 \ldots$ (infinite maximal execution).

Both are maximal because they are infinite. The fragment $s_0$ itself is **not** maximal (it has two successors), and it can be extended into two distinct maximal fragments, illustrating *non-determinism* in the system. *(7 marks — 2 for table, 3 for construction, 2 for justification.)*

**(b)** With reference to LTL/CTL model checking, explain why a *maximal execution fragment* is the appropriate object on which to evaluate temporal formulas. Illustrate with the formula $\mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\, q)$ (globally, $p$ implies eventually $q$). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Temporal logic formulas like $\mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\, q)$ assert properties about the **entire future** of a state. To evaluate such formulas truthfully, the verifier must examine the **complete behavior** starting from that state — i.e., a **maximal** execution. A non-maximal fragment ends prematurely and could *falsely* satisfy or violate a liveness property (e.g., $q$ may not have occurred yet simply because we stopped observing).

**For the formula** $\phi \equiv \mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\, q)$:
- For every state in the path, if $p$ holds, then $q$ must hold at some **future** state.
- If the path is finite and ends with $p$ true but no $q$ observed, the formula *falsely* holds under naive finite-trace semantics.
- Hence, LTL evaluation mandates **infinite** paths (or paths extended to maximality).

**Illustration:** Suppose $\rho = s_0 s_1 s_2$ with $L(s_0) = \{p\}$, $L(s_1) = \{p\}$, $L(s_2) = \{q\}$ and $s_2$ is a terminal state. Under *finite* trace semantics, $p \rightarrow \mathbf{F} q$ holds at $s_1$ because $q$ appears later. But if we extend $\rho$ to a maximal execution and discover that from $s_0$ another path $s_0 s_3 s_4 \ldots$ never reaches $q$, the universal quantifier over all maximal executions *falsifies* $\phi$. Thus, model checking evaluates formulas over **all maximal executions** rooted at initial states. *(7 marks — 3 for conceptual reasoning, 2 for formula explanation, 2 for illustration.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing execution with execution fragment** — the biggest single source of lost marks. Always state explicitly whether the sequence is **finite** or **infinite**.
> 2. **Forgetting the empty-pipe rule in tables** — when writing $\vert\rho\vert$ in handwritten or typed answers, use proper notation. Examiners will *not* penalize, but sloppy notation invites ambiguity.
> 3. **Omitting the join condition** in concatenation — many students write $\rho \cdot \tau$ without stating that $s_k = t_0$ is **required**. Always state the *precondition*.
> 4. **Treating executions as starting from any state** — in CTL/LTL model checking, the path quantifier $\mathbf{A}/\mathbf{E}$ ranges over executions starting from a *specific* state, often the initial state. Be precise.
> 5. **Mark loss on definitions** — for 3-mark questions, examiners expect: **(i)** formal statement, **(ii)** a clear distinguishing example or property, **(iii)** a one-line justification. Missing any of these costs at least 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Transition System (Kripke Structure):** $TS = \langle S, \rightarrow, I, AP, L \rangle$ — the foundational model for model checking.
- **Execution** $\sigma = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ is **infinite**; each consecutive pair is in $\rightarrow$.
- **Execution Fragment** $\rho = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k$ is **finite** (or possibly infinite); same transition condition.
- **Initial Fragment:** $\rho[0] \in I$.
- **Maximal Fragment:** cannot be extended — either infinite or ends in a dead-end state.
- **Concatenation** $\rho \cdot \tau$ requires last$(\rho) =$ first$(\tau)$; result has $k + m + 1$ states and $k + m$ transitions.
- **Length notation:** $\vert\rho\vert_{\text{states}} = k+1$, $\vert\vert\rho\vert\vert_{\text{trans}} = k$.
- **Suffix of execution:** $\sigma^i$ is the $i$-th suffix, central to LTL semantics $\sigma \models \phi \iff \sigma^0 \models \phi$.
- **König's Lemma** guarantees the existence of a maximal extension for any fragment in a finite-branching TS.
- **Real-world use:** model checking, runtime verification, hardware verification, concurrent system analysis.
- **Always specify finiteness/infinity** — this single qualifier distinguishes an execution from a fragment in both definition and application.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximal and Initial Execution Fragment
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximal and Initial Execution Fragment

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Foundations: Transition Systems (Kripke Structures)

Before defining execution fragments, we must first establish the underlying mathematical model used in automated verification. A **Kripke Structure** (or Transition System) is the standard semantic model.

Let $AP$ be a non-empty set of **atomic propositions**. A Kripke Structure is formally defined as the 4-tuple:

$$
TS = (S,\, S_0,\, R,\, L)
$$

Where the components are defined as:
- **$S$**: A finite (or countably infinite) non-empty set of **states**.
- **$S_0 \subseteq S$**: A non-empty set of **initial states** (the entry points of the system).
- **$R \subseteq S \times S$**: A **transition relation**, where $(s, s') \in R$ means the system can move from state $s$ to $s'$ in a single atomic step.
- **$L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$**: A **labeling function** that maps each state to the set of atomic propositions true in that state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> In many verification frameworks, the transition relation $R$ is required to be **total** — meaning every state has at least one outgoing transition. If $R$ is total, every state is non-terminal, and maximal execution fragments will always be **infinite sequences**.

## 1.2 Definition of an Execution Fragment

An **execution fragment** of a transition system $TS$ is a sequence of states (finite or infinite) representing one possible way the system may behave.

Formally, an execution fragment is a sequence:

$$
\sigma = s_0,\, s_1,\, s_2,\, \dots
$$

Such that for **every** index $i \geq 0$, the pair $(s_i,\, s_{i+1})$ is a valid transition:

$$
\forall\, i \geq 0 : (s_i,\, s_{i+1}) \in R
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Train Track Analogy):**
> Imagine a model railway network. The **states** are the stations, and the **transitions** are the railway tracks connecting them. An **execution fragment** is any route a train can take — it can be:
> - A short trip from Station A → Station B → Station C (a *finite* fragment).
> - An endless loop the train runs forever (an *infinite* fragment).
> - A trip that suddenly stops at a dead-end station with no outgoing tracks (a *finite* fragment ending in a *terminal* state).
>
> The rules of the tracks are dictated by $R$, and the journey must respect the physical layout of the railway at every step.

## 1.3 Initial Execution Fragment

An execution fragment $\sigma = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ is called an **Initial Execution Fragment** if and only if its first state belongs to the set of initial states:

$$
\sigma \text{ is initial} \iff s_0 \in S_0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** An initial execution fragment is a *plausible system run* — one that the system could actually exhibit starting from power-on. The "initial" qualifier guarantees we are analyzing a behavior that is *reachable* from the system's startup, not just any arbitrary fragment floating in the state space.

## 1.4 Maximal Execution Fragment

An execution fragment $\sigma = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ is called a **Maximal Execution Fragment** if and only if it **cannot be extended** any further. Formally, $\sigma$ is maximal if and only if one of the following two conditions holds:

1. **The fragment is infinite** — i.e., $\sigma$ has no last state and the sequence $s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ is unbounded.

2. **The fragment is finite and ends in a terminal state** — i.e., $\sigma = s_0, s_1, \dots, s_n$ and there is **no** $s_{n+1} \in S$ such that $(s_n, s_{n+1}) \in R$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Maximal" Matters in Verification:**
> A maximal fragment captures the **complete, un-interrupted** behavior of the system for a particular run. When we evaluate a temporal property like "eventually the system reaches a safe state," we are not interested in *partial* fragments that were arbitrarily cut off — we want to know what the system does *on its own, until it stops or runs forever*. This is the standard semantic object over which LTL and CTL formulas are interpreted.

## 1.5 Path: The Most Important Special Case

In the context of model checking (especially for **LTL**), the term **path** has a very precise meaning:

> A **path** is a **maximal initial execution fragment** of the transition system.

So a path is simultaneously:
- **Initial** → it starts in $S_0$.
- **Maximal** → it cannot be extended (it is either infinite or ends in a deadlocked state).

Mathematically:

$$
\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots \quad \text{where} \quad s_0 \in S_0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma \text{ is maximal}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:**
> Some textbooks use the words "run", "trace", "execution", and "path" interchangeably. In KTU-style automated verification questions, **"path" almost always means maximal + initial**. Do not write a "path" that starts in a non-initial state or one that is abruptly truncated.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Execution Fragments

The terms we have defined form a strict conceptual hierarchy. Understanding this hierarchy is **critical** for KTU exam answers because questions often ask you to classify a given sequence.

### Classification Logic (Step-by-Step):

1. **Step 1 — Is it an execution fragment?**
   - Verify: For every consecutive pair $(s_i, s_{i+1})$ in the sequence, check if $(s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$.
   - If even one pair is missing, the sequence is **not** an execution fragment.

2. **Step 2 — Is it initial?**
   - Verify: Is the very first state $s_0$ an element of $S_0$?
   - If yes → it is an **initial execution fragment**.
   - If no → it is a *non-initial* fragment (relevant for sub-system analysis, not full-system verification).

3. **Step 3 — Is it maximal?**
   - **Case A**: The fragment is infinite → automatically maximal.
   - **Case B**: The fragment is finite, ending at $s_n$. Check: does $s_n$ have *any* outgoing transition? If $(s_n, s') \in R$ for some $s'$, then the fragment is **not maximal** (it can be extended). If there is no such $s'$, then $s_n$ is a **terminal/deadlock state** and the fragment is maximal.

4. **Step 4 — Combining classifications:**
   - **Maximal + Initial** = **Path** (the canonical object of model checking).
   - **Non-maximal + Initial** = a *prefix of a path* (relevant when reasoning about partial system runs).
   - **Maximal + Non-initial** = *liveness tail* (relevant for fairness/recovery analysis).
   - **Non-maximal + Non-initial** = *arbitrary fragment* (least interesting for verification).

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Definition Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Condition / Formula | Notation Used | Exam Relevance |
|---|---------|----------------------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Kripke Structure | $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ | $S$ = states, $R$ = relation | Always required to define TS first |
| 2 | Execution Fragment | $\forall i \geq 0 : (s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$ | $\sigma = s_0, s_1, \dots$ | Base object of all reasoning |
| 3 | Initial Fragment | $s_0 \in S_0$ | First state in $S_0$ | Filters out unreachable runs |
| 4 | Maximal Fragment | (infinite) $\lor$ ($\text{finite} \land \lnot \exists s' : (s_n, s') \in R$) | Cannot be extended | Guarantees complete behavior |
| 5 | Path | Maximal **AND** Initial | $\pi = s_0, s_1, \dots$ with $s_0 \in S_0$ | Object over which LTL is evaluated |
| 6 | Length of Fragment | $\vert \sigma \vert = n+1$ for $\sigma = s_0 \dots s_n$ | Cardinality of state sequence | Used in Kripke/Witness proofs |
| 7 | Suffix of a Path | $\pi^k = s_k, s_{k+1}, \dots$ | Drop first $k$ states | Used in fixpoint semantics of LTL |
| 8 | Prefix of a Path | $\pi^{\leq k} = s_0, s_1, \dots, s_k$ | First $k+1$ states | Used in bounded model checking |
| 9 | Total $R$ Condition | $\forall s \in S : \exists s' \in S : (s, s') \in R$ | Every state has outgoing edge | Ensures all maximal fragments are infinite |
| 10 | Stuttering Step | $s = s'$ where $(s, s') \in R$ | Self-loop | Important in stuttering-invariant LTL |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Hardware Verification (RTL/Netlist):** Each state is a bit-vector snapshot of all registers. A path corresponds to one possible execution of the silicon — model checkers like Cadence JasperGold enumerate these to prove safety properties.
> - **Protocol Verification (5G, Bluetooth, Cache Coherence):** Each state is a global configuration of messages and channel states. Maximal initial execution fragments model the *longest* possible protocol runs, including deadlocks.
> - **Software Verification (Model Checking C/Java):** Tools like CBMC and Java PathFinder construct all paths up to a bound to prove memory safety, absence of division-by-zero, etc.

## 2.3 The Role of Maximal Fragments in LTL Semantics

In **Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)**, every formula $\varphi$ is interpreted over a path $\pi$. For a transition system $TS$, the satisfaction relation is:

$$
TS \models \varphi \iff \forall\, \pi \in \text{Paths}(TS) : \pi \models \varphi
$$

The symbol $\text{Paths}(TS)$ denotes the **set of all maximal initial execution fragments** of $TS$. This is the precise reason *maximal* and *initial* are both essential — without maximality, we could cheat by truncating a path right before a property is violated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Style Key Insight:**
> LTL's semantics treats a path as if it *runs forever*. Even if a maximal fragment is finite (ending in a deadlock), LTL treats the deadlock as a special "looping at a sink" — the finite tail is implicitly extended with infinite stuttering at the terminal state. This convention exists so that *every* maximal fragment can host an LTL truth value.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Classifying Any Given Sequence

Below is a **decision procedure** (exhaustive step-by-step algorithm) that examiners expect you to demonstrate in 7–14 mark answers. We will use a generic transition system to illustrate the procedure.

### Worked Example Setup

Consider a transition system with:
- $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$
- $S_0 = \{s_0, s_1\}$
- $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_2), (s_1, s_3), (s_2, s_2), (s_3, s_4)\}$
- $L(s) = \{p\}$ for all states (labeling is not central to this topic).

### Classification Procedure

**Step 1: List candidate sequences** from the transition system.

$$
\sigma_1 = s_0, s_1, s_2
$$
$$
\sigma_2 = s_0, s_1, s_3, s_4
$$
$$
\sigma_3 = s_1, s_2, s_2, s_2, \dots \text{ (infinite loop)}
$$
$$
\sigma_4 = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_2, s_3
$$
$$
\sigma_5 = s_2, s_3, s_4
$$

**Step 2: Apply the execution fragment test** (check that every adjacent pair is in $R$).

For $\sigma_1 = s_0, s_1, s_2$:
- $(s_0, s_1) \in R$ ✓
- $(s_1, s_2) \in R$ ✓
- Hence, $\sigma_1$ is a valid execution fragment.

For $\sigma_4 = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_2, s_3$:
- $(s_0, s_1) \in R$ ✓
- $(s_1, s_2) \in R$ ✓
- $(s_2, s_2) \in R$ ✓ (self-loop)
- $(s_2, s_3) \in R$ ✗ — **NOT** in $R$, since $R$ contains no edge from $s_2$ to $s_3$.
- Hence, $\sigma_4$ is **not** an execution fragment.

**Step 3: Apply the initial test** (check whether the first state is in $S_0$).

$$
\text{For } \sigma_1 = s_0, s_1, s_2: s_0 \in S_0 \implies \text{initial}.
$$
$$
\text{For } \sigma_3 = s_1, s_2, s_2, \dots: s_1 \in S_0 \implies \text{initial}.
$$
$$
\text{For } \sigma_5 = s_2, s_3, s_4: s_2 \notin S_0 \implies \textbf{not initial}.
$$

**Step 4: Apply the maximal test** (cannot be extended).

For $\sigma_1 = s_0, s_1, s_2$ (finite):
- Does $s_2$ have any outgoing transition? Yes: $(s_2, s_2) \in R$.
- Therefore, $\sigma_1$ **can be extended** to $s_0, s_1, s_2, s_2, \dots$
- Hence, $\sigma_1$ is **not maximal**.

For $\sigma_2 = s_0, s_1, s_3, s_4$ (finite):
- Does $s_4$ have any outgoing transition? **No** — there is no pair $(s_4, x)$ in $R$.
- Therefore, $\sigma_2$ **cannot be extended**.
- Hence, $\sigma_2$ is **maximal**.

For $\sigma_3 = s_1, s_2, s_2, \dots$ (infinite):
- By definition, an infinite fragment cannot be extended.
- Hence, $\sigma_3$ is **maximal**.

**Step 5: Combine to identify paths**.

| Sequence | Execution Fragment? | Initial? | Maximal? | Path? |
|----------|---------------------|----------|----------|-------|
| $\sigma_1 = s_0, s_1, s_2$ | Yes | Yes | No | No (prefix of a path) |
| $\sigma_2 = s_0, s_1, s_3, s_4$ | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Yes (a path)** |
| $\sigma_3 = s_1, s_2, s_2, \dots$ | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Yes (a path)** |
| $\sigma_4 = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_2, s_3$ | **No** | — | — | No |
| $\sigma_5 = s_2, s_3, s_4$ | Yes | No | Yes | No |

## 3.2 Derived Property: Uniqueness of Maximal Extension

**Claim:** Every initial execution fragment can be extended to *at least one* maximal initial execution fragment (provided $S$ is finite and $R$ is total, or $TS$ is finite-state).

**Derivation (Sketch):**

Consider an initial execution fragment $\sigma = s_0, s_1, \dots, s_n$ that is **not** maximal. By definition, it is not maximal only if there exists some $s_{n+1}$ such that $(s_n, s_{n+1}) \in R$. We append $s_{n+1}$ to obtain:

$$
\sigma' = s_0, s_1, \dots, s_n, s_{n+1}
$$

We repeat this extension step. Two outcomes are possible:
1. The extension process **continues forever** (because $R$ is total at every reached state) → we obtain an infinite sequence, which is maximal.
2. The extension process **reaches a state $s_m$ with no outgoing transition** → we obtain a finite sequence ending in a deadlock state, which is maximal.

In a finite state space, since the sequence cannot revisit a state infinitely often without eventually exceeding the finite bound, the process is well-defined and terminates in one of the two outcomes. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance of This Derivation:**
> This theorem is what makes **explicit-state model checking** algorithms (like the one in SPIN/NuSMV) work. The algorithm must, given a reachable state, be able to compute *some* path through it in order to evaluate an LTL formula. The existence of maximal extensions guarantees that this search always terminates (in finite state spaces) with a well-defined truth value.

## 3.3 Relating Paths to Suffixes and Prefixes (Operational Toolset)

Given a path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$, the following derived objects are used repeatedly in LTL semantics:

$$
\pi^k = s_k, s_{k+1}, s_{k+2}, \dots \quad \text{(the } k\text{-suffix)}
$$

$$
\pi^{\leq k} = s_0, s_1, \dots, s_k \quad \text{(the } k\text{-prefix)}
$$

**Important fact:** Every suffix $\pi^k$ of a path is itself a *maximal* execution fragment (because maximality is preserved under suffixing — an infinite tail remains infinite, and a finite tail ending in deadlock remains deadlock-ending).

However, $\pi^k$ is **initial** only when $k = 0$. For $k > 0$, $\pi^k$ is maximal but not initial. Such objects are called **paths through state $s_k$** and are critical in defining the CTL "exists-next" operator $\exists \bigcirc$:

$$
s \models \exists \bigcirc \varphi \iff \exists\, \pi^k \text{ starting at } s : \pi^{k+1} \models \varphi
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kripke Structure (Mermaid State Diagram)

The diagram below visualizes a sample Kripke Structure. Initial states are drawn with a double circle. Self-loops indicate stuttering behavior.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateA
    stateA --> stateB
    stateA --> stateC
    stateB --> stateB
    stateB --> stateD
    stateC --> stateE
    stateC --> stateF
    stateD --> stateG
    stateE --> stateE
    stateF --> stateF
    stateG --> stateH
    stateH --> [*]

    stateA : S0 INITIAL
    stateB : S1
    stateC : S2 INITIAL
    stateD : S3
    stateE : S4 LOOP
    stateF : S5 LOOP
    stateG : S6
    stateH : S7 TERMINAL
```

**Diagram Reading Guide (for KTU Answers):**

- **Double-entry from `[\\*]`** marks initial states: `stateA` and `stateC`.
- **Self-loops** on `stateB`, `stateE`, `stateF` are **stuttering transitions** — they allow infinite fragments without leaving the state.
- **State `stateH`** is a **terminal (deadlock) state** — no outgoing transition. Any maximal fragment that reaches it must end there.
- **Every other state** has at least one outgoing transition, ensuring that any maximal fragment not reaching `stateH` is infinite.

## 4.2 Classification Tree: All Possible Execution Fragments

The following Mermaid flowchart acts as a **decision tree** for classifying any given sequence of states. This is the algorithm a student should mentally execute when solving a KTU Part B question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startSequence([Given sequence sigma]) --> checkExecution{Is every adjacent pair in R?}
    checkExecution -- No --> notFragment[Not an execution fragment]
    checkExecution -- Yes --> isFragment[Valid execution fragment]
    isFragment --> checkInitial{Is s0 in S0?}
    checkInitial -- No --> nonInitial[Non-initial fragment]
    checkInitial -- Yes --> isInitial[Initial execution fragment]
    isInitial --> checkMaximal{Can sigma be extended?}
    checkMaximal -- Yes, finite and extendable --> notMaximal[Initial but NOT maximal]
    checkMaximal -- No, infinite OR deadlocked --> isMaximal[Maximal execution fragment]
    isMaximal --> isPath[PATH: Maximal AND Initial]
    isPath --> pathUse{Used for...}
    pathUse --> ltlCheck[LTL formula evaluation]
    pathUse --> ctlCheck[CTL quantifier domain]
    pathUse --> witnessGen[Counterexample generation]

    notMaximal --> extension[Can be extended to a path]
    nonInitial --> nonInitUse[Used for sub-system analysis]
    notFragment --> reject[Reject the sequence]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How a Model Checker Uses Paths

The diagram below illustrates how a model checking engine (e.g., NuSMV, SPIN) consumes and produces maximal initial execution fragments. This is a **functional architecture block diagram** rather than a physical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputSys[Kripke Structure TS equals S S0 R L] --> bmcEngine[Explicit State Explorer]
    bmcEngine --> reachability{Reachable from S0?}
    reachability -- No --> pruneState[Prune unreachable state]
    reachability -- Yes --> fragmentBuilder[Fragment Builder]
    fragmentBuilder --> extendLoop[Extend until maximal]
    extendLoop --> sinkCheck{Terminal state hit?}
    sinkCheck -- Yes --> finitePath[Finite maximal path ends in deadlock]
    sinkCheck -- No --> cycleCheck{Cycle detected?}
    cycleCheck -- Yes --> infinitePath[Infinite maximal path loops forever]
    cycleCheck -- No --> extendLoop
    finitePath --> pathPool[Path Pool Paths of TS]
    infinitePath --> pathPool
    pathPool --> ltlEvaluator[LTL / CTL Evaluator]
    ltlEvaluator --> verdict{All paths satisfy property?}
    verdict -- Yes --> safeOutput[SYSTEM IS SAFE]
    verdict -- No --> counterEx[Output offending path as counterexample]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the following terms with respect to a Kripke Structure $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$:**
**(a)** Execution fragment
**(b)** Initial execution fragment
**(c)** Maximal execution fragment

#### Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per clean definition):

**(a) Execution Fragment:** A sequence of states $\sigma = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ such that for every $i \geq 0$, the transition $(s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$. The sequence may be finite or infinite.

**(b) Initial Execution Fragment:** An execution fragment $\sigma$ whose first state belongs to the set of initial states, i.e., $s_0 \in S_0$. This represents a run of the system starting from power-on.

**(c) Maximal Execution Fragment:** An execution fragment that cannot be extended further. Formally, $\sigma$ is maximal if it is infinite, or if it is finite ending in a state $s_n$ for which no $s'$ exists with $(s_n, s') \in R$ (i.e., $s_n$ is a deadlock/terminal state).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often confuse "maximal" with "longest possible." A maximal fragment is **not** the longest — it is the **un-extendable** one. If a transition system has cycles, the longest fragment is infinite, and *every* infinite fragment in such a system is maximal.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is a *path* in the context of model checking? Why is maximality an essential requirement for the semantic object of LTL?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

A **path** in a Kripke Structure is a **maximal initial execution fragment** — that is, a sequence $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ such that $s_0 \in S_0$ (initial) and $\pi$ cannot be extended (maximal).

**Why maximality is essential for LTL semantics:**

LTL formulas are evaluated over paths, and the semantics requires the path to represent the **complete, un-interrupted** behavior of the system. If we allowed non-maximal fragments, a system could "cheat" by truncating its execution right before a violation occurs. Maximality ensures that we are reasoning over the *full* behavior — including infinite loops and deadlocks — so that liveness properties (e.g., "eventually $p$") receive their true truth value.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice.** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Consider the Kripke Structure defined as:
- $S = \{a, b, c, d, e\}$
- $S_0 = \{a\}$
- $R = \{(a, b), (a, c), (b, d), (c, d), (d, e), (e, e)\}$

For each of the following sequences, determine whether it is (i) a valid execution fragment, (ii) an initial execution fragment, (iii) a maximal execution fragment, and (iv) a path. Justify each classification. **(7 marks)**

1. $\sigma_1 = a, b, d, e, e, e, \dots$
2. $\sigma_2 = a, c, d, e$
3. $\sigma_3 = b, d, e, e, \dots$
4. $\sigma_4 = a, b, c$

**(b)** Prove that in a Kripke Structure with a finite state space and a total transition relation $R$, every initial execution fragment can be extended to a maximal initial execution fragment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A:

**(a) Classification of each sequence:**

**Sequence 1:** $\sigma_1 = a, b, d, e, e, e, \dots$

- **Execution fragment?** Check each adjacent pair: $(a,b) \in R$ ✓, $(b,d) \in R$ ✓, $(d,e) \in R$ ✓, $(e,e) \in R$ ✓. → **Yes, valid execution fragment.**
- **Initial?** First state is $a$, and $a \in S_0$ → **Yes, initial.**
- **Maximal?** The sequence is infinite (it loops forever on $e$ via the self-loop). → **Yes, maximal.**
- **Path?** Maximal AND initial → **Yes, this is a path.** ✓

**Sequence 2:** $\sigma_2 = a, c, d, e$

- **Execution fragment?** $(a,c) \in R$ ✓, $(c,d) \in R$ ✓, $(d,e) \in R$ ✓. → **Yes, valid.**
- **Initial?** $a \in S_0$ → **Yes.**
- **Maximal?** Finite sequence ending at $e$. Does $e$ have an outgoing transition? Yes, $(e,e) \in R$. Therefore, the sequence **can be extended** to $\sigma_1$. → **No, not maximal.**
- **Path?** Not maximal → **No, this is a path *prefix*.**

**Sequence 3:** $\sigma_3 = b, d, e, e, \dots$

- **Execution fragment?** $(b,d) \in R$ ✓, $(d,e) \in R$ ✓, $(e,e) \in R$ ✓. → **Yes.**
- **Initial?** First state is $b$. Is $b \in S_0$? No, $S_0 = \{a\}$. → **No, not initial.**
- **Maximal?** Infinite → **Yes.**
- **Path?** Maximal but not initial → **No, this is a non-initial maximal fragment.**

**Sequence 4:** $\sigma_4 = a, b, c$

- **Execution fragment?** $(a,b) \in R$ ✓, but $(b,c) \notin R$ (the only outgoing edge from $b$ is to $d$). → **No, not an execution fragment.**
- Initial: Irrelevant (not a fragment).
- Maximal: Irrelevant.
- Path: **No.**

**(b) Proof of the Maximal Extension Theorem:**

**Theorem:** Let $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ be a Kripke Structure with $S$ finite and $R$ total. Then every initial execution fragment of $TS$ can be extended to at least one maximal initial execution fragment.

**Proof:**

Let $\sigma = s_0, s_1, \dots, s_n$ be an initial execution fragment. We construct an extension via the following iterative procedure:

- **Base step:** $\sigma$ is initial, so $s_0 \in S_0$. ✓
- **Inductive step:** If the current fragment ends at $s_k$ and $s_k$ is not a terminal state (i.e., $\exists s' \in S$ with $(s_k, s') \in R$), append any such $s'$ to the fragment.
- **Termination argument:** Since $R$ is total, every state in a finite space has at least one outgoing transition. So either:
  - (i) The process never terminates → the resulting sequence is **infinite** and therefore maximal.
  - (ii) The process terminates at some state $s_m$ with no outgoing transition (i.e., $R$ is not total at $s_m$). Since $S$ is finite and the sequence is constructed by appending states from $S$, case (ii) can only occur if the construction reaches a state with no successors.

In case (i), the resulting infinite sequence is a maximal initial execution fragment by definition.

In case (ii), the finite sequence ending at the terminal state $s_m$ cannot be extended, hence it is maximal by definition. Initiality is preserved because we only append states after the initial state $s_0$.

In both cases, we have constructed a maximal initial execution fragment extending $\sigma$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> The most common mistake is to **omit the finite-state assumption**. The theorem *fails* in infinite state spaces — for example, a system that always increments a counter has no maximal initial fragment ending in a terminal state, and the construction never terminates. Always state the **finite state + total relation** precondition explicitly in your proof.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a)** Define a Kripke Structure. With a suitable example, illustrate the concepts of: execution fragment, initial execution fragment, maximal execution fragment, and path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why a maximal initial execution fragment is the correct semantic object for evaluating LTL formulas. Discuss what happens, semantically, when the maximal fragment is finite and ends in a deadlock state. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B:

**(a) Definition with Example:**

**Definition:** A Kripke Structure over a set of atomic propositions $AP$ is a 4-tuple $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ where:
- $S$ is a finite set of states,
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the non-empty set of initial states,
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ is the transition relation,
- $L: S \to 2^{AP}$ is the labeling function.

**Example:** Consider a simple mutual-exclusion protocol with two processes, each having a "trying", "critical", or "idle" status. Let:
- $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$
- $S_0 = \{s_0\}$
- $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_2), (s_1, s_3), (s_2, s_4), (s_3, s_4), (s_4, s_4)\}$
- $L(s_0) = \emptyset$, $L(s_1) = \{p\}$, $L(s_2) = \{q\}$, $L(s_3) = \{\lnot q\}$, $L(s_4) = \{\text{deadlock}\}$

**Illustration of concepts:**

- **Execution fragment:** $\sigma_1 = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_4$ (all consecutive pairs are in $R$).
- **Initial execution fragment:** Any fragment starting at $s_0$. Example: $\sigma_2 = s_0, s_1, s_3, s_4$.
- **Maximal execution fragment:** $\sigma_3 = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_4, s_4, s_4, \dots$ (infinite via the self-loop on $s_4$, hence un-extendable).
- **Path:** Both $\sigma_3$ above, and any infinite initial fragment, qualify as paths. The prefix $\sigma_1$ is **not** a path because it can be extended to $\sigma_3$.

**(b) Why Maximal Initial Execution Fragments are the Semantic Objects of LTL:**

LTL formulas describe properties of *traces* of system behavior. To assign a definitive truth value to a formula like $\mathbf{G}\, p$ ("globally $p$") or $\mathbf{F}\, q$ ("eventually $q$"), we must commit to a *complete* trace — one that is not artificially cut short.

**Why maximality is required:**
If we allowed non-maximal fragments, a system could "evade" a violation by stopping just before the bad state is reached. Maximality forces us to consider the *full* behavior, including infinite loops and deadlocks. The standard LTL semantics defines:

$$
TS \models \varphi \iff \forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(TS) : \pi \models \varphi
$$

where $\text{Paths}(TS)$ is the set of all maximal initial execution fragments.

**Why initiality is required:**
Initiality restricts attention to behaviors *reachable from the system's startup*. Properties of the system should describe what happens when we *turn the system on*, not what could happen in arbitrary (possibly unreachable) regions of the state space.

**Semantic handling of finite maximal fragments ending in deadlock:**
When a maximal fragment is finite and ends in a deadlock state $s_n$ (no outgoing transitions), standard LTL semantics treats the fragment as if it is *implicitly extended* by infinite stuttering at $s_n$. That is:

$$
s_n, s_n, s_n, s_n, \dots
$$

This convention ensures that *every* maximal fragment — finite or infinite — can host an LTL truth value. Under this convention:
- $\mathbf{G}\, p$ evaluated at $s_n$ is true iff $p$ holds in $s_n$ (because $p$ will hold forever via stuttering).
- $\mathbf{F}\, q$ evaluated at $s_n$ is true iff $q$ holds at $s_n$ (because the "eventual" event is satisfied immediately upon reaching the deadlock).

This stuttering-extension convention is **not** part of the basic LTL syntax but is a well-established semantic convention used by tools like SPIN and NuSMV.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B):**
> A common valuation deduction: students often claim that a finite maximal fragment "violates" liveness properties. This is wrong — by the stuttering-extension convention, a finite maximal fragment satisfies $\mathbf{F}\, p$ for any $p$ that holds at its terminal state. To get an actual liveness violation, you need a **deadlock-free** system or one with a cycle that **never** visits a state satisfying $p$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Kripke Structure** = $(S, S_0, R, L)$. State this tuple explicitly whenever you begin a verification problem.
- **Execution Fragment** = sequence of states where every consecutive pair is in $R$. Finite or infinite.
- **Initial Execution Fragment** = execution fragment with $s_0 \in S_0$.
- **Maximal Execution Fragment** = cannot be extended. Either infinite, or finite ending in a state with no outgoing transition.
- **Path** = Maximal **AND** Initial execution fragment. The canonical object for LTL evaluation.
- **Total $R$** → every state has an outgoing transition → every maximal fragment is infinite.
- **Non-total $R$** → some states are terminal/deadlock → finite maximal fragments exist.
- **Finite maximal fragments** are semantically extended by infinite stuttering at the terminal state for LTL evaluation.
- **Suffix of a path** = maximal but not initial (for $k > 0$). Used heavily in LTL fixpoint semantics.
- **Prefix of a path** = initial but not maximal. Used in bounded model checking.
- **Theorem to remember:** In a finite Kripke Structure, every initial fragment extends to a maximal initial fragment. (Requires the finite state assumption — *do not omit this in proofs!*)
- **Common classification table** — Memorize the 2×2 matrix of (initial? × maximal?) to instantly classify any given sequence in an exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Execution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Execution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Execution (in Automated Verification)

In the formal semantics of programming languages and in **automated verification**, an **Execution** of a system (or program) is formally defined as a (possibly infinite) sequence of state transitions induced by the operational semantics of the system, starting from an initial configuration and proceeding according to a transition relation.

Mathematically, an execution is a sequence:

$$ \sigma = s_0 \xrightarrow{\alpha_0} s_1 \xrightarrow{\alpha_1} s_2 \xrightarrow{\alpha_2} \cdots $$

where each $s_i$ is a **state (configuration)** of the system, and each $\alpha_i \in \Sigma$ is a **transition label (action)** drawn from a fixed alphabet of observable or internal events.

An **Execution Trace** (or **path**) is the projection of the execution onto the sequence of states or onto the sequence of labels:

$$ \pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots \quad \text{(state trace)} $$
$$ \tau = \alpha_0, \alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots \quad \text{(action trace)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECNT526 Module 1):**
> Execution is the process of traversing a **Transition System (Kripke Structure / Labeled Transition System)** by repeatedly applying its transition relation, producing a maximal sequence of states that is the central object of study in model checking, temporal logic, and reachability analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Conveyor Belt of States"

Imagine a **factory assembly line**:

- The **factory** = your program or system.
- Each **workstation** the product visits = a **state** $s_i$ (e.g., values of all program variables, program counter location).
- The **belt moving the product** = the **execution engine / semantics**.
- The **instruction taped to the product at each station** = the **action / label** $\alpha_i$ that triggered the move.

Once you press "Start" (i.e., fix an **initial state** $s_0$), the belt deterministically or non-deterministically carries the product through stations, and the **sequence of stations visited is the execution**. If the belt never stops, the execution is **infinite** — which is the typical case for reactive / concurrent systems in verification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Execution" matters in Automated Verification:**
> Every property we wish to verify (e.g., "the system never enters an error state", "every request is eventually acknowledged") is a property of an **execution / trace**. Model checkers like SPIN, NuSMV, and UPPAAL work by **enumerating**, **symbolically exploring**, or **abstracting** the set of all executions of a model.

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary Used Throughout This Topic

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **State / Configuration** | A snapshot of all variables, program counter, and memory of the system at one instant. |
| **Initial State** | A designated state $s_0$ from which execution begins. |
| **Transition Relation** | A binary relation $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times S$ (or $S \times \Sigma \times S$) defining legal moves. |
| **Action / Label** | An observable or internal event $\alpha \in \Sigma$ annotating a transition. |
| **Step** | A single application of the transition relation: $s \rightarrow s'$. |
| **Trace / Path** | The sequence of states (or actions) visited during execution. |
| **Maximal Execution** | An execution that cannot be extended further (either infinite or ending in a deadlock state). |
| **Stutter** | Two consecutive identical states: $s \rightarrow s$. |

### 1.4 Two Paradigms of Execution Semantics

In formal semantics — and therefore in verification — there are **two principal ways to define what "executing a program" means**:

1. **Small-Step Semantics (Operational Semantics)** — also called *transition semantics*. Defines execution *one atomic step at a time* using a transition relation $\rightarrow$. The standard for model checkers.
2. **Big-Step Semantics (Natural Semantics)** — also called *relational semantics*. Defines execution *from initial state directly to final state* using a relation $\Downarrow$. The standard for interpreters and program-provers.

Both produce the same executions overall, but **small-step semantics is the de-facto framework in automated verification** because it makes intermediate states — where invariants and properties must hold — explicit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Execution — A Structured Breakdown

A complete execution of a transition system $TS = (S, S_0, \rightarrow, L, AP)$ can be decomposed into the following logical building blocks:

1. **State Identification** — A state $s \in S$ is a tuple that completely describes the system. For a sequential program, $s = \langle pc, \sigma \rangle$ where $pc$ is the program counter and $\sigma$ is the variable-to-value store.

2. **Initial Configuration** — One or more designated start states $S_0 \subseteq S$. Every execution must begin in some $s_0 \in S_0$.

3. **Atomic Step** — An *atomic* (indivisible) action that changes the state. In hardware, a clock edge; in software, a single statement evaluation; in a protocol, a single message exchange.

4. **Transition Rule** — A logical or operational rule that, given a state, determines:
   - Whether the system can take a step.
   - If yes, what the successor state is.
   - In the labeled case, what label $\alpha$ annotates the step.

5. **Path Construction** — Successive application of the transition rule, yielding a (possibly infinite) sequence of states.

6. **Termination / Non-termination** — The execution **terminates** if it reaches a state with no outgoing transitions (a *deadlock* or *halt* state). It is **non-terminating** (a *divergence*) if it can produce an infinite path.

> [!TIP]
> **"Why" and "How" each piece matters:**
> - State identification is critical because **two executions are equal iff they pass through the same sequence of states**.
> - The transition relation is the *only* input to a model checker; everything else is computed.
> - Labeling with atomic propositions $AP$ is what lets temporal-logic formulas like $G\,(req \Rightarrow F\,ack)$ be evaluated on executions.

## 2.2 Deterministic vs. Non-Deterministic Execution

A transition system is **deterministic** if for every state $s$ and every action $\alpha$, there is **at most one** successor state. Formally:

$$ \forall s \in S,\ \forall \alpha \in \Sigma :\ \vert \{ s' \mid s \xrightarrow{\alpha} s' \} \vert \le 1 $$

Otherwise, the system is **non-deterministic**: at least one state has two or more $\alpha$-successors.

> [!NOTE]
> Non-determinism is the *mathematical model* for concurrency, abstraction (data non-determinism), and underspecification. Every real concurrent program yields a non-deterministic model.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transition System | $TS = (S, S_0, \Sigma, \rightarrow, AP, L)$ | The formal model whose executions are studied. |
| 2 | Transition Relation | $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times \Sigma \times S$ | Set of all legal atomic steps. |
| 3 | Direct Successor | $Post(s) = \{ s' \mid \exists \alpha: s \xrightarrow{\alpha} s' \}$ | All states reachable from $s$ in one step. |
| 4 | Direct Predecessor | $Pre(s) = \{ s' \mid \exists \alpha: s' \xrightarrow{\alpha} s \}$ | All states that can reach $s$ in one step. |
| 5 | Execution | $\pi = s_0 \xrightarrow{\alpha_0} s_1 \xrightarrow{\alpha_1} \ldots$ | A (possibly infinite) path through the system. |
| 6 | Trace | $tr(\pi) = \alpha_0\, \alpha_1\, \alpha_2\, \ldots$ | Sequence of observable actions. |
| 7 | Reachable States | $Reach(s_0) = \bigcup_{n \ge 0} Post^n(s_0)$ | All states ever visited. |
| 8 | State Count Bound | $\vert Reach \vert \le \vert S \vert$ | Foundation of finite-state model checking. |
| 9 | Big-Step Eval | $\langle c, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma'$ | Whole-program evaluation result. |
| 10 | Small-Step Step | $\langle c, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c', \sigma' \rangle$ | Single-step reduction. |
| 11 | Stutter Equivalence | $\pi_1 \approx_s \pi_2$ | Two executions equivalent up to finite repetition of identical states. |
| 12 | Enabledness | $enabled(s) = \Sigma \setminus \{ \alpha \mid \nexists s': s \xrightarrow{\alpha} s' \}$ | Actions available at state $s$. |
| 13 | Deadlock | $Post(s) = \emptyset$ | A state with no outgoing transitions — an execution terminates here. |
| 14 | Maximal Path Length | $0, 1, 2, \ldots, \omega$ | Finite, infinite, or $\omega$-length executions. |
| 15 | State Formula (at $s_i$) | $s_i \models \varphi$ | A proposition true at state $s_i$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in KTU Context:**
> Reachability computation $Reach(s_0)$ is the algorithmic backbone of:
> - **CTL / LTL model checking** (fix-point computation).
> - **Safety analysis** ("can the system ever reach an error state?").
> - **Bug hunting** ("is there ANY execution on which property $P$ is violated?").
> Tools like **SPIN**, **NuSMV**, **UPPAAL**, and **CBMC** all internally implement variants of $Post$ / $Pre$ to enumerate executions.

## 2.4 Classifying Executions by Termination Behavior

- **Finite (terminating) execution:** length $n \in \mathbb{N}$, ends in a state with no successors.
- **Divergent execution:** an *infinite* execution that stays within a finite strongly-connected component (a "loop").
- **Lasso execution (lollipop):** a finite prefix leading into a cycle: $s_0 \rightarrow^* s_c \rightarrow^+ s_c$. Critical for CTL* model checking.
- **Failing execution:** a finite execution that ends in a designated *error / bad* state — the target of every safety verifier.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Operational Rules to the Transition Relation

> This is the central derivation you must be able to reproduce for KTU Module 1.

We start with a toy **While-language** whose abstract syntax is:

$$ c \in Com ::= \text{skip} \mid x := e \mid c_1; c_2 \mid \text{if } b \text{ then } c_1 \text{ else } c_2 \mid \text{while } b \text{ do } c $$

A *configuration* is a pair $\langle c, \sigma \rangle$, where $c$ is the command being executed and $\sigma$ is a store (a function $Var \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}$).

### Step 1 — Define the semantic objects

A **state of the program** is the store $\sigma$ together with the program counter $pc$. We model this as the configuration $\langle c, \sigma \rangle$ since $c$ uniquely determines $pc$ structurally.

### Step 2 — Introduce the small-step relation
