We define $\langle c, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c', \sigma' \rangle$ meaning: *in one atomic step, configuration $\langle c, \sigma \rangle$ reduces to $\langle c', \sigma' \rangle$*.

### Step 3 — Write the structural operational semantics (SOS) rules

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{[SKIP]}    & \frac{}{\langle \text{skip}, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \epsilon, \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[ASSIGN]}  & \frac{}{\langle x := e, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \epsilon, \sigma[x \mapsto \llbracket e \rrbracket \sigma] \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[SEQ-1]}   & \frac{\langle c_1, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1', \sigma' \rangle}{\langle c_1; c_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1'; c_2, \sigma' \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[SEQ-2]}   & \frac{}{\langle \text{skip}; c_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_2, \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[IF-T]}    & \frac{\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma = \mathbf{tt}}{\langle \text{if } b \text{ then } c_1 \text{ else } c_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1, \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[IF-F]}    & \frac{\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma = \mathbf{ff}}{\langle \text{if } b \text{ then } c_1 \text{ else } c_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_2, \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[WHILE-T]} & \frac{\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma = \mathbf{tt}}{\langle \text{while } b \text{ do } c, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c; \text{while } b \text{ do } c, \sigma \rangle} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[WHILE-F]} & \frac{\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma = \mathbf{ff}}{\langle \text{while } b \text{ do } c, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \text{skip}, \sigma \rangle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of each line:**
- **[SKIP]**: skip takes one step to the empty program $\epsilon$ without changing the store.
- **[ASSIGN]**: $x := e$ takes one step to $\epsilon$ and updates the store so that $x$ maps to the value of $e$ in $\sigma$.
- **[SEQ-1]**: a sequence steps by stepping its first component; the second is carried along.
- **[SEQ-2]**: when the first component finishes, control passes to the second.
- **[IF-T/F]**: the guard $b$ is evaluated in the current store; one of the branches is taken in **one** step.
- **[WHILE-T/F]**: when the guard is true, the loop body is executed followed by the loop itself; when false, the loop exits in one step.

### Step 4 — Define the big-step relation

A whole-program big-step evaluation $\langle c, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma'$ is defined coinductively / inductively. The crucial rules are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{[BS-ASSIGN]} & \frac{}{\langle x := e, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma[x \mapsto \llbracket e \rrbracket \sigma]} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[BS-SEQ]}    & \frac{\langle c_1, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma_1 \quad \langle c_2, \sigma_1 \rangle \Downarrow \sigma_2}{\langle c_1; c_2, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma_2} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[BS-IF-TT]}  & \frac{\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma = \mathbf{tt} \quad \langle c_1, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma'}{\langle \text{if } b \text{ then } c_1 \text{ else } c_2, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma'} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{[BS-WHILE-FF]}& \frac{\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma = \mathbf{ff}}{\langle \text{while } b \text{ do } c, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Equivalence Theorem (often asked in KTU)

> **Theorem (Small-step $\equiv$ Big-step).** For every command $c$ and stores $\sigma, \sigma'$:
> $$ \langle c, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma' \iff \langle c, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow^* \langle \epsilon, \sigma' \rangle $$

That is, the big-step result equals the store reached when the small-step execution terminates at the empty program.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Examples of Executions

| Program $c$ | Initial $\sigma$ | First Step $\rightarrow$ | Resulting $\sigma'$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x := 1$ | $\{x \mapsto 0\}$ | $\langle x:=1, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \epsilon, \sigma[x \mapsto 1] \rangle$ | $\{x \mapsto 1\}$ |
| $x := 1;\, y := x$ | $\{x \mapsto 0,\, y \mapsto 0\}$ | 2-step sequence to $\epsilon$ | $\{x \mapsto 1,\, y \mapsto 1\}$ |
| `if true then x:=1 else x:=2` | $\{x \mapsto 0\}$ | applies [IF-T] | $\sigma[x \mapsto 1]$ |
| `while false do x:=1` | $\{x \mapsto 0\}$ | applies [WHILE-F] | $\{x \mapsto 0\}$ (terminates in 1 step) |
| `while true do x:=1` | $\{x \mapsto 0\}$ | infinite loop | divergent execution |

## 3.3 Reachability Fixpoint — Foundational Algorithm

The set of states reachable from $S_0$ is the **least fixpoint** of the post-image operator $Post$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& R_0 = S_0 \\
& R_{i+1} = R_i \cup Post(R_i) \\
& Reach = \bigcup_{i \ge 0} R_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the system is finite ($S$ finite), the sequence $R_0 \subseteq R_1 \subseteq \ldots$ stabilizes after at most $\vert S \vert$ iterations, giving an executable algorithm with time complexity $O(\vert S \vert + \vert \rightarrow \vert)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This Kleene-style fixpoint iteration is the **core algorithm of every model checker**. LTL and CTL model checking reduce to repeated fixpoint computations over $Reach$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture: From Program to Set of Executions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Program or System Description] --> B[Parser / Frontend]
    B --> C[Abstract Syntax Tree AST]
    C --> D[Semantics Definition: SOS Rules]
    D --> E[Transition System TS]
    E --> F[Initial State Set S0]
    E --> G[Transition Relation R-arrow]
    E --> H[Labeling Function L over AP]
    F --> I[Execution Engine: Post / Pre]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Set of All Executions Pi]
    J --> K[Temporal Logic Model Checker]
    J --> L[Reachability Analyzer]
    J --> M[Equivalence Checker]
    K --> N[Verification Result: SAT or UNSAT]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Concrete Execution of a Toy Program

Consider `x := 1; y := x + 1` starting from $\sigma_0 = \{x \mapsto 0,\, y \mapsto 0\}$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["C0: x:=1; y:=x+1 with sigma0 x=0 y=0"] -->|ASSIGN x:=1| S1["C1: skip; y:=x+1 with sigma1 x=1 y=0"]
    S1 -->|SEQ-2: skip vanishes| S2["C2: y:=x+1 with sigma1 x=1 y=0"]
    S2 -->|ASSIGN y:=x+1| S3["C3: skip with sigma2 x=1 y=2"]
    S3 -->|HALT| SF["Final: sigma2 x=1 y=2"]
```

Each node $Ci$ is a configuration $\langle c_i, \sigma_i \rangle$. The edges are SOS rules. The **execution is the path** $C0 \rightarrow C1 \rightarrow C2 \rightarrow C3 \rightarrow$ final.

## 4.3 Reachability Fixpoint Computation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["R0 = S0 initial states"] -->|Post| R1["R1 = R0 union Post R0: 1-step successors"]
    R1 -->|Post| R2["R2 = R1 union Post R1: 2-step successors"]
    R2 -->|Post| R3["R3 = R2 union Post R2: 3-step successors"]
    R3 -->|Post| R4["R4 = R3 ... and so on"]
    R4 -->|until fixpoint| Rfix["Rfix = Reach S0: union of all Ri"]
```

## 4.4 Determinism vs. Non-Determinism — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Deterministic
        D0["s0"] -->|alpha| D1["s1"]
        D1 -->|beta| D2["s2"]
        D2 -->|gamma| D3["s3"]
    end
    subgraph NonDeterministic
        N0["s0"] -->|a| N1a["s1 left branch"]
        N0 -->|a| N1b["s1 right branch"]
        N1a -->|b| N2a["s2 left"]
        N1b -->|b| N2b["s2 right"]
    end
```

In the **deterministic** part, each state-action pair has at most one successor. In the **non-deterministic** part, $s_0$ on action $a$ has two possible successors. A verification tool must explore **both** branches to confirm a property holds on **all** executions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define an "execution" of a transition system. How does it differ from a "trace"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **execution** of a transition system $TS = (S, S_0, \rightarrow, \Sigma, L, AP)$ is a sequence of the form

$$ \pi = s_0 \xrightarrow{\alpha_0} s_1 \xrightarrow{\alpha_1} s_2 \xrightarrow{\alpha_2} \ldots $$

where each $s_i \in S$, $s_0 \in S_0$, and $(s_i, \alpha_i, s_{i+1}) \in \rightarrow$.

A **trace** is the projection of an execution onto the **sequence of actions** (labels) only: $tr(\pi) = \alpha_0\, \alpha_1\, \alpha_2\, \ldots$

**Difference:** an execution records *both* the visited states and the labels annotating each step, whereas a trace records *only* the labels. Two different executions may therefore share the same trace if they differ only in the states visited — this is the basis of **stutter equivalence**. (3 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]`
**What is the difference between small-step and big-step execution semantics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Small-step (operational) semantics** defines execution as a transition relation $\langle c, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c', \sigma' \rangle$ producing *one atomic step at a time*. It exposes every intermediate state and is used in **model checking** and **abstract machines**.
- **Big-step (natural) semantics** defines execution as a relation $\langle c, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma'$ that *directly relates* the initial and final stores, skipping intermediate states. It is used in **interpreters** and **proof assistants**.

Both yield the same final result for terminating programs; the proof of equivalence relies on showing that the reflexive-transitive closure $\rightarrow^*$ of the small-step relation equals the big-step relation. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Rule (PECNT526):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and carries an internal choice — i.e., **either (a) OR (b)**. We model this below as **Question A** and **Question B**, both addressing the topic "Execution" with a different sub-focus.

### `Question A` (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Define a transition system formally. Show, with the help of a structured operational semantics (SOS), how the execution of a `while` program is defined step-by-step. Write down **at least five SOS rules**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Consider the program

```
x := 0;
while x < 3 do
    x := x + 1
```

starting from the store $\sigma_0 = \{x \mapsto 0\}$. **Enumerate the complete execution**, listing every configuration $\langle c, \sigma \rangle$ visited, the rule applied at each step, and identify whether the execution terminates. State the final store.

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

A **transition system** is a tuple $TS = (S, S_0, \Sigma, \rightarrow, AP, L)$ where:
- $S$ — non-empty set of states,
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ — initial states,
- $\Sigma$ — set of actions/labels,
- $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times \Sigma \times S$ — transition relation,
- $AP$ — set of atomic propositions,
- $L: S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ — labeling function. (2 marks)

**SOS rules for execution** (showing 5 rules, each ~1 mark):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{}{\langle \text{skip}, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \epsilon, \sigma \rangle} \quad \text{[SKIP]} \\[4pt]
&\frac{}{\langle x := e, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \epsilon, \sigma[x \mapsto \llbracket e \rrbracket \sigma] \rangle} \quad \text{[ASSIGN]} \\[4pt]
&\frac{\langle c_1, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1', \sigma' \rangle}{\langle c_1; c_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_1'; c_2, \sigma' \rangle} \quad \text{[SEQ-1]} \\[4pt]
&\frac{}{\langle \text{skip}; c_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c_2, \sigma \rangle} \quad \text{[SEQ-2]} \\[4pt]
&\frac{\llbracket b \rrbracket \sigma = \mathbf{tt}}{\langle \text{while } b \text{ do } c, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle c; \text{while } b \text{ do } c, \sigma \rangle} \quad \text{[WHILE-T]}
\end{aligned}
$$

These rules define **one atomic reduction step**; an execution is the reflexive-transitive closure of these steps. (5 marks)

**(b) — 7 marks**

Let $\sigma_i$ denote the store after $i$ loop iterations, with $\sigma_0 = \{x \mapsto 0\}$.

| Step | Configuration $\langle c, \sigma \rangle$ | Rule Applied |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\langle x:=0;\,\text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_0 \rangle$ | initial |
| 1 | $\langle \text{skip};\,\text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_0 \rangle$ | [ASSIGN] |
| 2 | $\langle \text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_0 \rangle$ | [SEQ-2] |
| 3 | $\langle (x:=x+1);\,\text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_0 \rangle$ | [WHILE-T] (0 < 3) |
| 4 | $\langle \text{skip};\,\text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_1 \rangle$, $\sigma_1 = \{x \mapsto 1\}$ | [ASSIGN] |
| 5 | $\langle \text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_1 \rangle$ | [SEQ-2] |
| 6 | $\langle (x:=x+1);\,\text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_1 \rangle$ | [WHILE-T] (1 < 3) |
| 7 | $\langle \text{skip};\,\text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_2 \rangle$, $\sigma_2 = \{x \mapsto 2\}$ | [ASSIGN] |
| 8 | $\langle \text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_2 \rangle$ | [SEQ-2] |
| 9 | $\langle (x:=x+1);\,\text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_2 \rangle$ | [WHILE-T] (2 < 3) |
| 10 | $\langle \text{skip};\,\text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_3 \rangle$, $\sigma_3 = \{x \mapsto 3\}$ | [ASSIGN] |
| 11 | $\langle \text{while } x<3 \text{ do } (x:=x+1),\, \sigma_3 \rangle$ | [SEQ-2] |
| 12 | $\langle \text{skip}, \sigma_3 \rangle$ | [WHILE-F] (3 ≮ 3) |
| 13 | $\langle \epsilon, \sigma_3 \rangle$ | [SKIP] — **HALT** |

**Termination:** Yes, the execution terminates at step 13. **Final store:** $\sigma_3 = \{x \mapsto 3\}$. (7 marks)

---

### `Question B` (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Define $Post(s)$ and $Pre(s)$ operators. Explain how repeated application of $Post$ from the initial state set $S_0$ computes the set of all reachable states, and discuss why this algorithm terminates for finite transition systems.

**(b) [7 marks]** A vending machine has the following transition system (states: $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, actions $\Sigma = \{\text{coin}, \text{select}, \text{dispense}\}$):

$$ s_0 \xrightarrow{\text{coin}} s_1 \xrightarrow{\text{select}} s_2 \xrightarrow{\text{dispense}} s_0 $$

State the set of maximal executions of this system. Identify the trace language $L(TS)$ (set of all finite traces accepted by the system). Also classify whether the system is deterministic.

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

For a transition system $TS = (S, S_0, \rightarrow, \Sigma)$:

$$ Post(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid \exists \alpha \in \Sigma: s \xrightarrow{\alpha} s' \} $$
$$ Pre(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid \exists \alpha \in \Sigma: s' \xrightarrow{\alpha} s \} $$

The reachable-state computation is: (3 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_0 &= S_0 \\
R_{i+1} &= R_i \cup \bigcup_{s \in R_i} Post(s) \\
Reach(TS) &= \bigcup_{i \ge 0} R_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Termination argument:** For a finite-state system, $\vert S \vert$ is a fixed finite number. The sequence $R_0 \subseteq R_1 \subseteq R_2 \subseteq \ldots$ is **monotonically increasing** and each $R_i \subseteq S$. By the finiteness of $S$, the chain can grow at most $\vert S \vert$ times before stabilizing, i.e., $R_{k+1} = R_k$ for some $k \le \vert S \vert$. The fixpoint is then $R_k = Reach(TS)$. Worst-case time complexity is $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert \rightarrow \vert)$. (4 marks)

**(b) — 7 marks**

The transition system is the cycle $s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_0$. (1 mark)

**Maximal executions:** Every execution of the vending machine is an *infinite repetition* of the cycle. The unique maximal execution (up to cyclic shift) is:

$$ \pi = (s_0 \xrightarrow{\text{coin}} s_1 \xrightarrow{\text{select}} s_2 \xrightarrow{\text{dispense}})^{\omega} $$

That is, the $\omega$-regular expression $(s_0\, s_1\, s_2)^{\omega}$. (2 marks)

**Trace language** $L(TS)$ — the set of all finite prefixes of the action sequence:

$$ L(TS) = \{ \epsilon,\ \text{coin},\ \text{coin}\cdot\text{select},\ \text{coin}\cdot\text{select}\cdot\text{dispense}, $$
$$ (\text{coin}\cdot\text{select}\cdot\text{dispense})^2,\ \ldots,\ (\text{coin}\cdot\text{select}\cdot\text{dispense})^n \mid n \ge 0 \} $$

In other words, $L(TS) = \{ (\text{coin}\cdot\text{select}\cdot\text{dispense})^n \mid n \in \mathbb{N} \}$. (2 marks)

**Determinism:** Yes, this system is **deterministic** — for every state $s_i$ and every action $\alpha$, there is **at most one** successor. In particular, $Post(s_i) = \{s_{i+1 \bmod 3}\}$ is a singleton for every state. (2 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks on "Execution":**
> 1. **Confusing trace with execution:** A trace is only the *action sequence*; an execution carries both states and actions. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. Always mention **both** projections in your definition.
> 2. **Forgetting the initial state condition:** An execution must begin in $S_0$. A path starting from an arbitrary state is just a "path", not a valid "execution" in KTU terminology.
> 3. **Mixing up small-step and big-step notation:** Using $\Downarrow$ when the question wants $\rightarrow$ (or vice versa) is a 1-mark deduction at minimum. Be precise.
> 4. **Omitting intermediate configurations in trace enumerations:** When asked to "list the execution" of a program, every configuration $\langle c, \sigma \rangle$ must be listed — not just the stores. Skipping steps loses up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the termination argument for reachability:** A bare fixpoint equation without the *monotonicity + finiteness* argument is incomplete (1–2 marks lost).
> 6. **Stating non-determinism as "random"**: Non-determinism is *adversarial*, not probabilistic. Examiners will deduct for this misuse of terminology.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Execution** = a sequence $\pi = s_0 \xrightarrow{\alpha_0} s_1 \xrightarrow{\alpha_1} \ldots$ of states and labels, beginning in $S_0$, governed by $\rightarrow$.
- **Trace** = the *label-only* projection of an execution. Two executions sharing a trace is the basis of **stutter equivalence** (used in LTL model checking under fairness).
- **Small-step (SOS)** semantics gives $\rightarrow$ and is the foundation of operational verification; **big-step** semantics gives $\Downarrow$ and is used in proof assistants and interpreters.
- A transition system is **deterministic** iff for every $(s, \alpha)$ there is at most one successor; otherwise it is **non-deterministic** (default model for concurrency).
- $Post(s)$ returns the set of all one-step successors; iterating $Post$ from $S_0$ yields $Reach(TS)$ — a Kleene fixpoint that terminates in at most $\vert S \vert$ steps for finite systems.
- Maximal executions are of three kinds: **finite** (terminate in deadlock), **lasso** (finite prefix + cycle), or **infinite** ($\omega$-length).
- The **equivalence theorem** states $\langle c, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \sigma' \iff \langle c, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow^* \langle \epsilon, \sigma' \rangle$.
- Every temporal-logic property (LTL/CTL) and every safety property is evaluated **on executions**; hence execution is the central object of study in **automated verification**.
- **Real-world use:** model checkers (SPIN, NuSMV, UPPAAL) and bounded model checkers (CBMC, JBMC) all internally construct, enumerate, or symbolically represent executions of the system under verification.
- **Key SOS rule categories to memorize:** SKIP, ASSIGN, SEQ-1, SEQ-2, IF-True, IF-False, WHILE-True, WHILE-False.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reachable States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reachable States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Model Checking** and **Formal Verification**, a **Reachable State** is any state `s ∈ S` of a transition system `TS = (S, S₀, →, L)` for which there exists a finite execution path

$$s_0 \xrightarrow{\,a_0\,} s_1 \xrightarrow{\,a_1\,} s_2 \xrightarrow{\,a_2\,} \dots \xrightarrow{\,a_{n-1}\,} s_n = s$$

starting from some **initial state** `s₀ ∈ S₀` and following valid transitions in `→`. The set of *all* such states is called the **Reachability Set** (denoted `Reach(TS)` or `R*`).

Formally, the reachability set is the **least fixpoint** of the monotone operator `τ(X) = S₀ ∪ Post(X)`:

$$R^{*} = \mu X.\; S_0 \cup Post(X)$$

where `Post(X) = { s' ∣ ∃ s ∈ X : s → s' }` is the *post-image* (or *next-state*) operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** A state is **reachable** if and only if it lies on some path originating from an initial state. Unreachable states are *irrelevant* for verifying safety/liveness properties — model checkers exploit this by **restricting the state space to `R*` only**, often yielding exponential reductions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Subway Map of Computation

Imagine a **Kerala State Road Transport (KSRTC) bus network** as a transition system:
- Each **bus stop** = a state.
- Each **road connecting two stops** = a transition.
- The **main depot** (e.g., Thiruvananthapuram Central) = the initial state `s₀`.

**Reachable states** = every bus stop the bus can *ever visit* during its service, no matter how many routes it takes. Stops sitting in a remote forest path with no connecting road from the depot are **unreachable** and need never be examined.

In hardware verification, this is *literally true*: a designer does not care whether a flip-flop can hold the bit pattern `0xDEADBEEF` if the reset/clock logic can never set it — the pattern is unreachable and the property "`X = 0xDEADBEEF is impossible`" is **trivially true** without proof.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Reachability Matters in KTU Automata Module:**
> 1. Defines the *effective state space* over which CTL*/LTL model checking is performed.
> 2. Forms the basis of **state-space exploration** algorithms (BFS, DFS, BDD-based symbolic search).
> 3. Underpins **deadlock detection** (deadlocks are reachable states with no outgoing transitions).
> 4. Enables **on-the-fly / partial-order reductions** by ignoring unreachable subgraphs.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notation Summary

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Form |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| `S` | Finite set of all states | `\|S\|` denotes cardinality |
| `S₀` | Set of initial states | `S₀ ⊆ S` |
| `→` | Transition relation | `→ ⊆ S × S` |
| `Post(X)` | Set of immediate successors of `X` | `Post(X) = {s′ ∣ s → s′ ∧ s ∈ X}` |
| `Pre(X)` | Set of immediate predecessors of `X` | `Pre(X) = {s ∣ s → s′ ∧ s′ ∈ X}` |
| `R*` | Set of all reachable states | Smallest fixpoint of `τ` |
| `μ` | Least fixpoint operator | Kleene iteration |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps of Reachability Computation

The reachability set is constructed **iteratively** starting from the initial states and accumulating new states until **saturation** (no new states can be added):

1. **Seed the frontier** with all initial states: `R₀ = S₀`.
2. **Compute the image**: compute `Post(Rᵢ)` — every state that can be reached in *exactly one* step from some already-known reachable state.
3. **Union and grow**: set `Rᵢ₊₁ = Rᵢ ∪ Post(Rᵢ)`.
4. **Saturation check**: if `Rᵢ₊₁ = Rᵢ`, terminate — fixpoint reached.
5. **Otherwise**, increment `i` and return to step 2.

This is the **Kleene fixed-point theorem in action** on a finite lattice `(2^S, ⊆)`.

### 2.2 Why This Works — The "Why" Behind the Math

Because `S` is **finite** and `Post` is **monotone** (if `X ⊆ Y` then `Post(X) ⊆ Post(Y)`), the sequence

$$R_0 \subseteq R_1 \subseteq R_2 \subseteq \dots \subseteq R_k = R_{k+1} = \dots = R^{*}$$

is a **non-decreasing chain** bounded above by `S`. By the **Knaster–Tarski theorem**, it must stabilise after at most `|S|` iterations, giving us `R*` in **finite time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Termination Guarantee (exam-favourite):** A BFS/DFS-style reachability algorithm always terminates on a finite transition system after at most `|S|` iterations because the state set is finite and the chain is strictly increasing until the fixpoint is hit.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Transition System | `TS = (S, S₀, →, L)` | Mathematical model of HW/SW |
| 2 | Post-image | `Post(X) = {s′ ∣ s → s′ ∧ s ∈ X}` | Forward state exploration |
| 3 | Pre-image | `Pre(X) = {s ∣ s → s′ ∧ s′ ∈ X}` | Backward counterexample search |
| 4 | Reachability operator | `τ(X) = S₀ ∪ Post(X)` | Monotone on `(2^S, ⊆)` |
| 5 | Reachability fixpoint | `R* = μX. τ(X) = ⋃ᵢ₌₀^∞ Rᵢ` | Kleene iteration |
| 6 | Iterative step | `R₀ = S₀`, `Rᵢ₊₁ = Rᵢ ∪ Post(Rᵢ)` | BFS/DFS-style exploration |
| 7 | Termination | `R* = R*` (idempotent) | At most `\|S\|` iterations |
| 8 | Complexity (explicit) | `O(\vert S\vert + \vert \to \vert)` | BFS time |
| 9 | Complexity (symbolic) | `O(\vert V\vert² \cdot k)` for `k` iterations | BDD image computation |
| 10 | Deadlock definition | `s ∈ R*` such that `Post({s}) = ∅` | Safety verification |
| 11 | Bounded reachability | `Reachₖ(s₀) = ⋃ᵢ₌₀^k Postⁱ({s₀})` | Bounded Model Checking (BMC) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Reachability analysis is the **backbone** of every commercial model checker — *SPIN* (explicit BFS/DFS), *NuSMV* (BDD-based symbolic), *CBMC* (SAT-based bounded), *UPPAAL* (timed automata zones), and *JKind* (inductive invariant generation). The same fixpoint loop also powers abstract interpretation engines like *Astrée* (used to verify Airbus flight control software).

### 2.4 Forward vs. Backward Reachability

* **Forward reachability** starts from `S₀` and explores `Post` repeatedly. Used to compute `R*` and detect reachable error states.
* **Backward reachability** starts from a set of "bad" states `Bad` and explores `Pre` repeatedly. Used to generate **counterexamples** when the property is violated.

Both converge to the same fixpoint; the choice is dictated by which side has the **smaller frontier** (heuristic-driven in industrial tools).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Manual Reachability Computation on a Toy TS

Consider a transition system modelling a **traffic light controller** with four states:

$$S = \{ G, Y, R, F \}$$

where `G = Green`, `Y = Yellow`, `R = Red`, `F = Fault`. Transitions are:

$$G \xrightarrow{} Y,\quad Y \xrightarrow{} R,\quad R \xrightarrow{} G,\quad R \xrightarrow{} F,\quad F \xrightarrow{} F$$

Initial state: `S₀ = {G}`.

**Step 1 — Seed:**
$$R_0 = S_0 = \{G\}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Post-image of R₀:**
$$Post(\{G\}) = \{ Y \}$$
because `G → Y` is the only transition out of `G`.

**Step 3 — Union:**
$$R_1 = R_0 \cup Post(R_0) = \{G\} \cup \{Y\} = \{G, Y\}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Post-image of R₁:**
$$Post(\{G, Y\}) = \{Y, R\}$$

**Step 5 — Union:**
$$R_2 = \{G, Y\} \cup \{Y, R\} = \{G, Y, R\}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Post-image of R₂:**
$$Post(\{G, Y, R\}) = \{Y, R, G, F\}$$

**Step 7 — Union:**
$$R_3 = \{G, Y, R\} \cup \{Y, R, G, F\} = \{G, Y, R, F\}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Post-image of R₃:**
$$Post(\{G, Y, R, F\}) = \{Y, R, G, F, F\} = \{G, Y, R, F\}$$

**Step 9 — Saturation check:**
$$R_4 = R_3 \cup Post(R_3) = \{G, Y, R, F\} \cup \{G, Y, R, F\} = \{G, Y, R, F\} = R_3$$

The fixpoint is reached. Therefore:

$$R^{*} = \{ G, Y, R, F \} = S$$

**Conclusion:** Every state is reachable. This is a *complete* system — no dead-code elimination possible.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** In KTU model-checking problems, the answer `R*` must always be presented as a **set**, with the **number of iterations** explicitly stated. Marks are awarded separately for (a) the iteration sequence, (b) the fixpoint check, and (c) the final answer.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Symbolic Reachability via BDD-Style Image

Suppose the same traffic light is encoded with **two boolean state variables** `x₁, x₂`:

$$G \equiv \neg x_1 \neg x_2,\quad Y \equiv \neg x_1 x_2,\quad R \equiv x_1 \neg x_2,\quad F \equiv x_1 x_2$$

Transition relation `T(x₁, x₂, x′₁, x′₂)` is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \;=\;& (\neg x_1 \neg x_2 \land \neg x'_1 x'_2) \;\lor\; (\neg x_1 x_2 \land x'_1 \neg x'_2) \\
     & \lor\; (x_1 \neg x_2 \land \neg x_1 \neg x'_2) \;\lor\; (x_1 \neg x_2 \land x'_1 x'_2) \\
     & \lor\; (x_1 x_2 \land x'_1 x'_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Initial-state predicate:**
$$I(x_1, x_2) = \neg x_1 \neg x_2$$

**Step 2 — Kleene iteration with image computation:**
Image formula: `Post(X) = ∃x₁ ∃x₂ [ X(x₁, x₂) ∧ T(x₁, x₂, x′₁, x′₂) ]` with output projected onto `x′₁, x′₂`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_0(x') \;&= I(x') = \neg x'_1 \neg x'_2 \\
Post(R_0) \;&= \neg x'_1 x'_2 \\
R_1 \;&= \neg x'_1 \neg x'_2 \;\lor\; \neg x'_1 x'_2 \;=\; \neg x'_1 \\
Post(R_1) \;&= \neg x'_1 x'_2 \;\lor\; x'_1 \neg x'_2 \\
R_2 \;&= \neg x'_1 \;\lor\; \neg x'_1 x'_2 \;\lor\; x'_1 \neg x'_2 \;=\; \neg x'_1 \;\lor\; x'_1 \neg x'_2 \\
Post(R_2) \;&= \neg x'_1 x'_2 \;\lor\; x'_1 \neg x'_2 \;\lor\; x'_1 x'_2 \\
R_3 \;&= \neg x'_1 \;\lor\; x'_1 \neg x'_2 \;\lor\; x'_1 x'_2 \;=\; \neg x'_1 \;\lor\; x'_1 \;=\; \text{true}
\end{aligned}
$$

Fixpoint reached in **3 iterations**: `R* = true`, i.e., the entire state space is reachable.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Reachability Algorithm

| Algorithm | State Representation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Best Use Case |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------------|------------------|---------------|
| BFS (Explicit) | Adjacency list | `O(\vert S\vert + \vert \to \vert)` | `O(\vert S\vert)` | Small-to-medium concurrent systems |
| DFS (Explicit) | Adjacency list | `O(\vert S\vert + \vert \to \vert)` | `O(\vert S\vert)` worst-case | On-the-fly / partial-order reduction |
| BDD-Based Symbolic | Boolean predicates via BDDs | `O(\vert BDD\vert² · k)` | `O(\vert BDD\vert²)` | Huge hardware circuits (10¹⁰⁰+ states) |
| SAT-Based Bounded | CNF clauses | `O(2^L · \vert\phi\vert)` | Linear in formula | Bounded depth, bug-hunting |
| Petri-Net Reachability | Marking vectors | EXPSPACE-complete | `O(n · b)` | Asynchronous / distributed systems |
| Timed Automata Zones | DBMs (Difference Bound Matrices) | EXPSPACE | `O(n² · M)` | Real-time / embedded controllers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State-Transition Diagram of the Traffic-Light TS

```mermaid
graph LR
    s0_init(["S0 = Initial States: G"])
    stateG["G Green"]
    stateY["Y Yellow"]
    stateR["R Red"]
    stateF["F Fault"]

    s0_init -.initial.-> stateG
    stateG -- "tick" --> stateY
    stateY -- "tick" --> stateR
    stateR -- "tick" --> stateG
    stateR -- "error" --> stateF
    stateF -- "stay" --> stateF

    classDef init fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef safe fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#155724
    classDef fault fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#721c24
    class s0_init init
    class stateG,stateY,stateR safe
    class stateF fault
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — Kleene Fixpoint Reachability Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Transition System TS = S, S0, arrow, L"] --> B["Initialise: R = S0, Frontier = S0, i = 0"]
    B --> C{"Frontier empty?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Output: R* = R (fixpoint reached)"]
    C -- "No" --> E["Compute NewFrontier = Post Frontier"]
    E --> F["R = R union NewFrontier"]
    F --> G["i = i + 1"]
    G --> H{"R changed since last iteration?"}
    H -- "No" --> D
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Frontier = NewFrontier"]
    I --> C

    classDef ioNode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#856404
    classDef procNode fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#0c5460
    classDef decisionNode fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#721c24
    classDef outNode fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#155724

    class A,D ioNode
    class B,E,F,G,I procNode
    class C,H decisionNode
    class D outNode
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Symbolic vs. Explicit Reachability Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input_Layer [INPUT LAYER]
        modelFile["Model File SMV / Promela / HDL"]
        propertyFile["Property Spec LTL or CTL"]
    end

    subgraph Front_End [FRONT END]
        parser["Parser and AST Builder"]
        tsBuilder["Transition System Builder"]
        propertyEncoder["Property Encoder"]
    end

    subgraph Reach_Engine [REACHABILITY ENGINE]
        explicitBFS["Explicit BFS or DFS on Sparse Graph"]
        bddImage["BDD Image Computation via CUDD or BuDDy"]
        satBMC["SAT based Bounded Reachability"]
    end

    subgraph Back_End [BACK END]
        stateSet["Reachable State Set R-star"]
        counterexample["Counterexample Trace"]
        witness["Invariant Witness"]
    end

    modelFile --> parser --> tsBuilder
    propertyFile --> propertyEncoder
    tsBuilder --> explicitBFS
    tsBuilder --> bddImage
    tsBuilder --> satBMC
    explicitBFS --> stateSet
    bddImage --> stateSet
    satBMC --> stateSet
    stateSet --> counterexample
    stateSet --> witness
    propertyEncoder --> counterexample
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define a *reachable state* in a transition system. State the least-fixpoint characterisation of the reachability set.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A state `s` is **reachable** in `TS = (S, S₀, →, L)` if there exists a finite sequence of transitions from some `s₀ ∈ S₀` to `s`. The reachability set is the least fixpoint of the operator `τ(X) = S₀ ∪ Post(X)`, expressed as
> $$R^{*} = \mu X.\; S_0 \cup Post(X) = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} R_i$$
> where `R₀ = S₀` and `Rᵢ₊₁ = Rᵢ ∪ Post(Rᵢ)`. (1 mark: definition; 1 mark: fixpoint form; 1 mark: iterative construction.)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between *forward* and *backward* reachability. In which situation is backward reachability preferred?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Forward reachability starts from `S₀` and applies `Post` to grow the reachable set. Backward reachability starts from a set of *bad* states `Bad` and applies `Pre` repeatedly to compute predecessors. **(2 marks)**. Backward reachability is preferred when `|Bad| ≪ |S₀|` or when the model checker needs to produce a **counterexample** showing how a violation can occur. **(1 mark).**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Consider the transition system `TS` with states `S = {s₀, s₁, s₂, s₃, s₄, s₅}`, initial state `S₀ = {s₀}`, and transition relation:
`{ s₀→s₁, s₀→s₂, s₁→s₃, s₂→s₃, s₃→s₄, s₄→s₅, s₅→s₄ }`. **(7 marks)**
Compute the reachability set `R*` by Kleene iteration, showing each `Rᵢ` and the corresponding `Post(Rᵢ)`.

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
>
> *R₀ = S₀ = { s₀ }; Post(R₀) = { s₁, s₂ }.*
> *R₁ = { s₀, s₁, s₂ }.*
>
> *Post(R₁) = { s₃ } ∪ { s₃ } = { s₃ }.* (since s₁→s₃ and s₂→s₃)
> *R₂ = { s₀, s₁, s₂, s₃ }.*
>
> *Post(R₂) = { s₄ }.*
> *R₃ = { s₀, s₁, s₂, s₃, s₄ }.*
>
> *Post(R₃) = { s₅ }.*
> *R₄ = { s₀, s₁, s₂, s₃, s₄, s₅ }.*
>
> *Post(R₄) = { s₄ } ⊆ R₄. Fixpoint reached.*
>
> **Answer:** `R* = { s₀, s₁, s₂, s₃, s₄, s₅ } = S`. **(Full marks: 1 per iteration step, 2 for the fixpoint identification.)**

**(b)** Explain the BDD-based *symbolic* image computation used to compute `Post` in BDD-driven model checkers such as NuSMV. Why is symbolic reachability often able to handle 10¹⁰⁰+ states? **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
> **Symbolic Image Computation (4 marks):**
> Given a set of states encoded as a BDD `R(x⃗)` and a transition relation `T(x⃗, x⃗′)`, the image is computed as:
> $$Post(R)(x⃗′) = \exists x⃗.\; \big[ R(x⃗) \wedge T(x⃗, x⃗′) \big]$$
> followed by renaming `x⃗′` → `x⃗`. The conjunction and existential quantification are performed *directly on the BDDs*, exploiting the canonical, compressed representation. Implementations use **quantifier elimination via the smooth operator** and **monolithic vs. partitioned transition relation** heuristics.
>
> **Why It Scales (3 marks):**
> 1. BDDs share sub-functions — many structurally similar states collapse into one BDD node.
> 2. The fixed-point loop operates on *symbolic predicates*, not individual states.
> 3. Each iteration is polynomial in the BDD size, which is often logarithmic in `|S|`.
> 4. Industrial circuits (e.g., a 32-bit multiplier has ~10¹⁹ states) have BDDs of only a few thousand nodes.

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** State and prove that the Kleene iteration sequence `R₀ ⊆ R₁ ⊆ R₂ ⊆ …` on a finite transition system always reaches a fixpoint in at most `|S|` steps. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
> **Statement (2 marks):** The sequence `R₀ = S₀, Rᵢ₊₁ = Rᵢ ∪ Post(Rᵢ)` is non-decreasing and stabilises within `|S|` iterations.
>
> **Proof (5 marks):**
> *Step 1 (Monotonicity):* For any `X ⊆ Y ⊆ S`, we have `Post(X) ⊆ Post(Y)` because every successor of a state in `X` is also a successor of that state considered in `Y`. Hence `Rᵢ ⊆ Rᵢ₊₁`.
> *Step 2 (Boundedness):* `Rᵢ ⊆ S` for all `i`, so the chain is bounded above by `S`.
> *Step 3 (Strict growth or saturation):* Since `S` is finite with `|S|` elements, the chain `R₀ ⊂ R₁ ⊂ …` can have at most `|S|` strict inclusions. By the **Knaster–Tarski fixed-point theorem** on the complete lattice `(2^S, ⊆)`, the limit exists and equals `R* = μX.τ(X)`. Therefore the iteration terminates in at most `|S|` steps.

**(b)** Distinguish between *Bounded Model Checking (BMC)* and *full* reachability analysis. Under what condition does BMC give a *complete* answer? **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer (7 marks):**
> **BMC (3 marks):** BMC unrolls the transition relation up to depth `k` and asks a SAT solver whether any bad state is reachable within `k` steps. It produces counterexamples of bounded length but cannot prove *absence* of errors.
> **Full reachability (2 marks):** Full (Kleene / BDD) reachability computes the entire fixpoint `R*` and can prove that no bad state is reachable (complete verification).
> **Completeness of BMC (2 marks):** BMC is complete for a property if the system diameter `d` (longest shortest path between any two states) satisfies `k ≥ d`. In that case, any state reachable in *any* number of steps is reachable within `k` steps, so BMC + SAT-decision at depth `d` is equivalent to full model checking.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Reachability Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Post computation per iteration:** Many students jump directly to the final set `R*` without showing the iterative sequence. *Loss: up to 4 marks per Part B question.*
> 2. **Missing the fixpoint termination check:** You must explicitly state `Rᵢ₊₁ = Rᵢ` and label it as the *fixpoint*. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Confusing `Post` and `Pre`:** `Post` gives successors; `Pre` gives predecessors. Writing the wrong direction silently invalidates the iteration.
> 4. **Not listing the elements as a set:** The answer must be enclosed in `{ … }` and treated as a subset of `S`.
> 5. **In symbolic/image-computation questions,** failing to rename `x⃗′` → `x⃗` after existential quantification is a recurring deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Reachable state** = a state lying on some path from an initial state.
- **Reachability set** = `R* = μX. (S₀ ∪ Post(X))`, the *least* fixpoint of a monotone operator on `(2^S, ⊆)`.
- **Kleene iteration** computes `R*` by `R₀ = S₀`, `Rᵢ₊₁ = Rᵢ ∪ Post(Rᵢ)`, terminating in at most `|S|` steps.
- **Forward** reachability uses `Post`; **backward** reachability uses `Pre` (used for counterexample generation).
- **BFS / DFS** is the explicit-state algorithm; **BDD image** is the symbolic algorithm; **SAT unrolling** is the bounded algorithm.
- A **deadlock** is a reachable state `s` with `Post({s}) = ∅`.
- **State-space reduction:** Unreachable states are pruned *before* CTL/LTL model checking — a major source of the efficiency of industrial model checkers.
- **Termination proof** depends on three facts: `Post` is monotone, `S` is finite, and the Kleene chain is bounded by `S`.
- **Complexity:** Explicit `O(|S| + |→|)`; symbolic BDD `O(|BDD|² · k)`; SAT-bounded `O(2^L · |φ|)`.
- **Applications** span hardware verification (Intel/AMD), software model checking (SLAM, CBMC), protocol verification (SPIN), and real-time embedded systems (UPPAAL).
- **Kleene vs. Tarski:** Kleene iteration is the constructive method; Knaster–Tarski guarantees existence of the fixpoint.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear-Time (LT) Properties - Deadlock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear-Time (LT) Properties - Deadlock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Linear-Time (LT) Properties — Formal Definition

In the framework of **Model Checking** (Clarke, Emerson; and later formalized by Baier & Katoen in *Principles of Model Checking*), a **Linear-Time (LT) property** is a set of infinite execution traces (sequences of states) over the atomic propositions of a transition system. Formally, an LT property $P$ is a subset:

$$P \subseteq (2^{AP})^{\omega}$$

where $AP$ is a finite set of atomic propositions and $(2^{AP})^{\omega}$ denotes the set of all infinite words (omega-words) over the alphabet $2^{AP}$. A transition system $\mathcal{TS}$ satisfies $P$ (written $\mathcal{TS} \models P$) if and only if **every** infinite path of $\mathcal{TS}$ is a member of $P$:

$$\mathcal{TS} \models P \quad\iff\quad \text{Traces}(\mathcal{TS}) \subseteq P$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** LT properties are evaluated on a *path-by-path* basis. They describe what *can* or *must* happen along an execution sequence. The two principal subclasses are **Safety** ("something bad will never happen") and **Liveness** ("something good will eventually happen").

### 1.2 Deadlock — Formal Definition

A **deadlock** in a transition system $\mathcal{TS} = (S, Act, \to, I, AP, L)$ is a reachable state $s \in \text{Reach}(\mathcal{TS})$ from which **no outgoing transition** is enabled. In formal notation:

$$\text{Deadlock}(s) \;\equiv\; \forall \alpha \in Act \;:\; s \not\xrightarrow{\alpha}$$

If the transition system models concurrency, two sub-classes are distinguished:

| Sub-class | Definition | Reachability |
|---|---|---|
| **Complete Deadlock** | No transition of *any* process can fire | $s$ is reachable and $Post(s) = \emptyset$ |
| **Partial Deadlock** | Some but not all processes are blocked (e.g., a process waits for another in a cycle) | $s$ is reachable, and a *wait-for cycle* exists |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Gridlock

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a four-way intersection where cars from the North, South, East, and West all arrive at the same time. Each car *needs* the right-of-way that the others occupy, and *none* of them can move forward. The intersection has reached a **deadlock** — no rule of the road allows a safe transition. In software, a deadlock is the digital twin of this gridlock: every thread is holding a lock that another thread needs, and no thread can release its lock until it acquires the next one.

### 1.4 Why Deadlock is an LT Property

Deadlock is classified as a **safety property** because it is a *finite* violation: a deadlock can be witnessed by examining a *finite prefix* of a path (the prefix that leads into the deadlock state). This is in contrast to liveness properties, whose violations require infinite observation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Cue:** Whenever a question asks "What type of property is deadlock?" — answer: *a state-based safety property (a special case of the 'something bad will happen' class), but the LT-property variant is called **deadlock-freeness***.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics and Conventions (Bolded Constants)

- **SCCS / CSP semantics** for concurrency: process algebra.
- **LTL** (Linear Temporal Logic) alphabet: $\{ \neg, \vee, \bigcirc, \mathcal{U} \}$ — and the derived operators $\Diamond$ (eventually) and $\Box$ (always).
- The standard *trap state* constant used when converting a transition system to one in which every deadlock state is labelled with a special proposition, e.g., $deadlock \in AP$, is denoted as the **"deadlock proposition"**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Logic of Classifying Deadlock as an LT Property

1. **Identify the property over a single path.**  
   A *path* $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ is *deadlocking* if there exists an index $k \ge 0$ such that $s_k$ is a deadlock state. Equivalently:

   $$\pi \models \text{HasDeadlock} \;\iff\; \exists k \ge 0 \;:\; \text{Deadlock}(s_k)$$

2. **Quantify universally over all paths.**  
   The transition system is **deadlock-free** iff *no* initial path contains a deadlock:

   $$\mathcal{TS} \models \text{DeadlockFree} \quad\iff\quad \forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(\mathcal{TS}) \;:\; \pi \not\models \text{HasDeadlock}$$

   The negation yields: $\mathcal{TS} \not\models \text{DeadlockFree}$ iff there exists a finite path $s_0 \to s_1 \to \cdots \to s_k$ with $Post(s_k) = \emptyset$.

3. **Choose the logic — LTL.**  
   In Linear Temporal Logic, deadlock-freeness for a system with a designated proposition $deadlock$ is expressed as the **safety assertion**:

   $$\varphi_{\text{DF}} \;=\; \Box \, \neg\, deadlock$$

   This formula reads: *"globally, the proposition deadlock is false"*. The violation of $\Box \neg deadlock$ is a finite witness — a path prefix that ends in a state labelled $deadlock$, which is the hallmark of a safety property.

### 2.2 Why the 'Why' Matters (Engineering Justification)

- **Why classify deadlock as safety?**  
  Because it can be detected on a *finite* prefix; a model checker (NuSMV, SPIN, UPPAAL) can terminate as soon as it visits a deadlock state — it does not need to explore infinitely many steps.

- **Why use $\Box$ (always) and not $\Diamond$ (eventually)?**  
  $\Diamond$ would assert "the system eventually deadlocks", which is a *liveness-shaped* claim about an undesirable event. We want the *negation* of an unwanted finite event — that is exactly the syntactic shape $\Box \neg p$.

- **How is deadlock encoded in the Kripke structure?**  
  Engineers introduce a self-loop absorbing "trap state" $s_\bot$ such that every state $s$ with $Post(s) = \emptyset$ is rewired with a transition $s \to s_\bot$, and $s_\bot$ is labelled with the atomic proposition $deadlock$. The formula $\Box \neg deadlock$ then becomes checkable.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Item | Symbolic Form | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LT Property (set-theoretic) | $P \subseteq (2^{AP})^{\omega}$ | $AP$ = atomic propositions, $\omega$ = infinite word |
| 2 | Satisfaction of LT property | $\mathcal{TS} \models P \iff \text{Traces}(\mathcal{TS}) \subseteq P$ | Universal quantification over paths |
| 3 | Path definition | $\pi = s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to \ldots$ | Infinite by convention |
| 4 | Deadlock predicate (state) | $\text{Deadlock}(s) \iff Post(s) = \emptyset$ | $Post(s) := \lbrace s' \in S \mid s \to s' \rbrace$ |
| 5 | Deadlock-freeness (LTL) | $\varphi_{\text{DF}} = \Box \, \neg\, deadlock$ | A **safety** formula |
| 6 | Existence of a deadlock (negation) | $\exists \pi : \pi \models \Diamond \, deadlock$ | Equivalent to $\neg \Box \neg deadlock$ |
| 7 | LTL safety-grammar fragment | $\varphi ::= p \;\mid\; \neg \varphi \;\mid\; \varphi \vee \varphi \;\mid\; \bigcirc \varphi \;\mid\; \varphi \mathcal{U} \varphi$ | Closed under $\Box$ |
| 8 | Liveness form | $\psi_{\text{progress}} = \Box \Diamond \, enabled$ | Often required *with* deadlock-freeness |
| 9 | Büchi automaton (LT property) | $\mathcal{B}_\varphi = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, Q_0, F)$ | $F$ = accepting set, infinite visits required |
| 10 | Model checking complexity (LTL) | $\mathcal{O}( \vert M \vert \cdot 2^{\mathcal{O}(\vert \varphi \vert)} )$ | Linear in model, exponential in formula |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Mark-Winning Note:** When asked to *prove* deadlock-freeness, do NOT confuse it with *fairness* or *progress*. A deadlock-free system may still starve a process — it just never enters a state from which *no* transition is possible.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Deadlock detection as an LT property is the theoretical backbone of:

- **Embedded / Safety-Critical Software** — RTCA DO-178C level A & B (avionics) and ISO 26262 ASIL-D (automotive) verification workflows.
- **Industrial Model Checkers** — *SPIN* (Holzmann) for Promela, *NuSMV* (CMU/Carnegie Mellon / FBK), and *UPPAAL* (Aalborg / Uppsala) all provide a built-in `deadlock` keyword or trap-state construct.
- **Operating Systems & Distributed Databases** — the Banker's Algorithm (Dijkstra, 1965) and the *wait-for graph* cycle detection are practical implementations of the same idea: detect a reachable state with empty post-set.
- **Hardware Design Verification** — SystemVerilog Assertions (SVA) such as `assert property (always !deadlock)` map directly to $\Box \neg deadlock$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From Deadlock Predicate to LTL Formula

**Step 1 — Define a transition system.**  
Let $\mathcal{TS} = (S, \to, I, AP, L)$ with $S$ the finite state set and $I \subseteq S$ the initial states. For every state $s \in S$ we have a *post-image*:

$$Post(s) \;=\; \lbrace s' \in S \mid \exists \alpha \in Act : s \xrightarrow{\alpha} s' \rbrace$$

**Step 2 — Encode deadlock as an atomic proposition.**  
Augment $AP$ with a new proposition $deadlock$ and a new state $s_\bot$ (the trap). Define the labelling function $L'$ on the modified state space $S' = S \cup \lbrace s_\bot \rbrace$:

$$
L'(s) \;=\; 
\begin{cases}
L(s) \cup \lbrace deadlock \rbrace & \text{if } s \in S \text{ and } Post(s) = \emptyset \\
L(s) & \text{if } s \in S \text{ and } Post(s) \neq \emptyset \\
\lbrace deadlock \rbrace & \text{if } s = s_\bot
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Rewire outgoing transitions.**  
For each deadlock state $s$ in the original system, add the self-loop $s \to s_\bot$, and for $s_\bot$, add a self-loop $s_\bot \to s_\bot$. All other transitions are inherited unchanged.

**Step 4 — Express the LT property in LTL.**  
The deadlock-freeness requirement is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{\text{DF}} \;=&\;\; \Box \, \neg\, deadlock
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Negate to obtain a *liveness-shaped* violation witness.**  
A system *violates* deadlock-freeness iff there exists a path on which $deadlock$ is eventually true:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg \varphi_{\text{DF}} 
\;=&\;\; \neg (\Box \neg deadlock) \\
\stackrel{\text{LTL dual}}{=}{}&\;\; \Diamond \, deadlock
\end{aligned}
$$

This equivalence $\neg \Box \neg p \equiv \Diamond p$ is one of the most heavily tested identities in KTU model-checking questions.

**Step 6 — Büchi automaton construction (sketch).**  
Convert $\varphi_{\text{DF}} = \Box \neg deadlock$ into a generalized Büchi automaton $\mathcal{B}_{\varphi_{\text{DF}}} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, Q_0, F)$ with:

- $Q = \lbrace q_0 \rbrace$ (a single state, since $\Box \neg p$ accepts only words that *never* show $p$),
- $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$,
- $\delta(q_0, X) = \lbrace q_0 \rbrace$ for every $X \subseteq AP$ with $deadlock \notin X$, and $\delta(q_0, X) = \emptyset$ otherwise,
- $Q_0 = \lbrace q_0 \rbrace$,
- $F = \lbrace q_0 \rbrace$.

A path of $\mathcal{TS}$ satisfies $\Box \neg deadlock$ iff the *synchronous product* automaton $\mathcal{TS} \otimes \mathcal{B}_{\varphi_{\text{DF}}}$ has an accepting run — which is the case precisely when $\mathcal{TS}$ never visits a state labelled $deadlock$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Path Properties of Deadlock

| Path Shape | LT Property Satisfied? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Infinite path with no deadlock | $\Box \neg deadlock$ ✓ | No state labelled $deadlock$ is ever visited |
| Finite path terminating in a deadlock | $\Box \neg deadlock$ ✗ | Property violation witnessed at the deadlock state |
| Path with a deadlock, then a non-deadlock successor (impossible in a trap-state encoding) | $\Box \neg deadlock$ ✗ | Once $deadlock$ holds, $\Box$ is irreparably violated |
| Path with a *temporal* liveness violation only (e.g., starvation) | $\Box \neg deadlock$ ✓ | Deadlock is *not* starvation — distinct properties |

### 3.3 Worked Example — A Two-Process Mutual Exclusion System

Consider two processes $P_1, P_2$ that may each be in one of three states: `non-critical (N)`, `trying (T)`, or `critical (C)`. Assume the (buggy) transition rules:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1:&\quad T \to C \quad\text{(unconditional, ignoring the lock)} \\
P_2:&\quad T \to C \quad\text{(unconditional, ignoring the lock)}
\end{aligned}
$$

If both processes are in $C$ and the system has no transition out of $C$ (no `exit` rule), then the global state $(C, C)$ is a deadlock state. The set of reachable states is:

$$\text{Reach} = \lbrace (N,N), (N,T), (T,N), (T,T), (C,N), (N,C), (C,T), (T,C), (C,C) \rbrace$$

The state $(C, C)$ satisfies $Post((C,C)) = \emptyset$. Therefore, with the augmented atomic proposition $deadlock$, we have $L((C,C)) = \lbrace deadlock \rbrace$. The LTL check $\Box \neg deadlock$ on this model **fails** at the state $(C, C)$, and the model checker returns a counter-example trace:

$$\pi_{\text{bad}} \;=\; (N,N) \to (T,N) \to (C,N) \to (C,T) \to (C,C)$$

which is a finite prefix of length 5. This confirms the *safety* (finite-witness) nature of the violation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Kripke Structure with a Deadlock State

```mermaid
graph TD
    s0["s0 : start, idle"] --> s1["s1 : p1 trying"]
    s0 --> s2["s2 : p2 trying"]
    s1 --> s3["s3 : p1 in CS"]
    s2 --> s4["s4 : p2 in CS"]
    s3 --> deadlock["sDeadlock : both blocked"]
    s4 --> deadlock
    s3 --> s1
    s4 --> s2
    deadlock --> trap["sBot : trap state labelled deadlock"]
    trap --> trap
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- `s0` is the unique initial state.
- `s3` and `s4` represent each process holding the lock.
- `sDeadlock` is the *partial-deadlock* hub: when one process is in CS and the other is trying, the system still progresses (via the self-loops out of `s3` and `s4`).
- `sBot` is the trap state, labelled with the proposition `deadlock`. Any path reaching `sBot` violates the LTL property $\Box \neg deadlock$.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Büchi Automaton for $\Box \neg deadlock$

```mermaid
graph LR
    q0(("q0 accepting initial"))
    q0 -- "X subset of AP with deadlock not in X" --> q0
    q0 -. "X subset of AP with deadlock in X (rejecting)" .-> sink(("sink rejecting"))
    sink --> sink
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The single accepting state `q0` accepts *any* omega-word in which the symbol $deadlock$ is **never** present.
- The dashed arrow represents the rejecting transition; from that point on, the run falls into the rejecting sink.
- A synchronized product with the Kripke structure yields a non-empty accepting run iff the system is deadlock-free.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Deadlock Verification

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Transition System Model M"] --> C["Product Construction M tensor B"]
    B["Buchi Automaton for phi_DF"] --> C
    C --> D["Search for Accepting Cycle"]
    D --> E{"Cycle Found ?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Return Counter-Example Path"]
    E -- "No" --> G["System Satisfies Box not deadlock"]
    F --> H["Diagnose Deadlock State s with Post s = empty"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The verifier takes the system model and the LTL formula (compiled to a Büchi automaton) and synchronously explores their product.
- If a *reachable accepting cycle* is found, the system satisfies the property.
- If no such cycle exists, the verification produces a *lasso-shaped* counter-example that ends in the deadlock state — the exact diagnostic the engineer needs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Linear-Time (LT) property over a transition system. How is deadlock classified within the LT-property hierarchy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Linear-Time property $P$ is a set of infinite execution traces over the atomic propositions of a transition system, i.e., $P \subseteq (2^{AP})^{\omega}$. A transition system satisfies $P$ iff all of its traces are members of $P$.  
**Classification of deadlock:** Deadlock-freeness is a **safety property** because its violation can be detected on a finite prefix of a path (the moment the system reaches a state $s$ with $Post(s) = \emptyset$). Within the LT-property taxonomy of Baier & Katoen, safety properties are the largest sub-class, and deadlock-freeness is a state-based instance expressed in LTL as $\varphi_{\text{DF}} = \Box \, \neg\, deadlock$. *(3 marks — full credit: 1 mark for the formal definition, 1 mark for the trace-subset satisfaction, 1 mark for the safety classification and the LTL formula.)*

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between complete deadlock and partial deadlock. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Complete Deadlock | Partial Deadlock |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | All processes are blocked; no transition is enabled in the global state | A subset of processes forms a cyclic dependency and is blocked, while others may still progress |
| Process state | Every process is in a `wait` state | At least one process is `runnable` |
| Detection | Reachability of a state $s$ with $Post(s) = \emptyset$ | Detection of a *wait-for cycle* in the resource-allocation graph |
| Real-world example | Two processes each holding a unique lock the other requests — neither can proceed | A 5-process dining-philosophers configuration where 4 of the 5 philosophers are deadlocked in a 4-cycle, but the 5th is still eating |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition of complete, 1 mark definition of partial, 1 mark for the examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule (as per 2024 Scheme):** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split typically as 7 + 7 across two sub-parts. Students answer **ONE** of the two choices.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define a transition system formally. With reference to the formal definition, **prove** that the LTL formula $\Box \, \neg\, deadlock$ expresses the deadlock-freeness property. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Construct the Büchi automaton corresponding to the LTL formula $\Box \, \neg\, deadlock$, and explain, with a labelled state-diagram, the product construction between this automaton and a given Kripke structure. Use a concrete 3-state example to illustrate the *trap-state encoding*. *(7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

A **transition system** is a 6-tuple $\mathcal{TS} = (S, Act, \to, I, AP, L)$ where:

- $S$ is a finite set of states,
- $Act$ is a set of actions,
- $\to \subseteq S \times Act \times S$ is the transition relation,
- $I \subseteq S$ is the set of initial states,
- $AP$ is a set of atomic propositions,
- $L : S \to 2^{AP}$ is the labelling function.

**Proof that $\Box \neg deadlock$ expresses deadlock-freeness:**

We prove the biconditional:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathcal{TS} \models \Box \, \neg\, deadlock \\
\iff&\;\; \text{(definition of } \Box \text{ in LTL) } \forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(\mathcal{TS}) \;:\; \forall k \ge 0 \;:\; deadlock \notin L(\pi_k) \\
\iff&\;\; \forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(\mathcal{TS}) \;:\; \forall k \ge 0 \;:\; \text{Post}(\pi_k) \neq \emptyset \\
\iff&\;\; \text{(by definition of deadlock predicate) } \forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(\mathcal{TS}) \;:\; \pi \text{ contains no deadlock state} \\
\iff&\;\; \mathcal{TS} \text{ is deadlock-free.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first biconditional is the LTL semantics of $\Box$ (path quantifier is universal by default in LTL). The second uses the trap-state construction that places $deadlock \in L(s)$ iff $Post(s) = \emptyset$. The third is the definition of deadlock. **Hence proved.** *(7 marks: 2 for the TS definition, 3 for the chain of equivalences, 2 for the concluding statement.)*

##### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Büchi automaton $\mathcal{B}_{\Box \neg deadlock}$:**

$$\mathcal{B} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, Q_0, F)$$

with:

- $Q = \lbrace q_0 \rbrace$,
- $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$,
- $\delta(q_0, X) = \lbrace q_0 \rbrace$ if $deadlock \notin X$, else $\delta(q_0, X) = \emptyset$,
- $Q_0 = \lbrace q_0 \rbrace$,
- $F = \lbrace q_0 \rbrace$ (Büchi acceptance: $q_0$ must be visited infinitely often — trivially true since the only state IS $q_0$).

**Product construction:** $\mathcal{TS} \otimes \mathcal{B}$ has states $S \times Q$ and a transition $(s, q) \to (s', q')$ iff $s \to s'$ in $\mathcal{TS}$ and $q' \in \delta(q, L(s'))$. A path of the product is accepting iff it visits $S \times F = S \times \lbrace q_0 \rbrace$ infinitely often. If **no** such path exists, the model checker reports a counter-example — a finite prefix ending in a deadlock state $s$ with $deadlock \in L(s)$.

**3-state example (trap-state encoding):**

Original states: $s_0$ (initial), $s_1$ (intermediate), $s_2$ (deadlock).  
Rewired with trap:

- $s_0 \to s_1$, $s_1 \to s_2$ (and $s_2 \to s_2$ in the modified system).
- $L(s_0) = \emptyset$, $L(s_1) = \lbrace working \rbrace$, $L(s_2) = \lbrace deadlock \rbrace$.

The product $\mathcal{TS} \otimes \mathcal{B}$ contains the state $(s_2, q_0)$. At this state, the transition reads $L(s_2) = \lbrace deadlock \rbrace$, and $\delta(q_0, \lbrace deadlock \rbrace) = \emptyset$. Hence the product is *empty* from this point on — no accepting infinite run is possible, and the system **fails** $\Box \neg deadlock$. The counter-example path returned to the user is $\pi = s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2$. *(7 marks: 2 for the Büchi definition, 2 for the product description, 3 for the worked 3-state example.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **safety–liveness** decomposition of LT properties as given by Baier & Katoen. Show, with a proof, that **deadlock-freeness** is a safety property. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a 3-process mutual-exclusion system with the buggy rule *"if a process is in the trying state and the lock is free, it may enter the critical section"*. Construct the reachable state space, identify the deadlock states, and write the LTL property whose violation would detect the bug. Provide one counter-example trace. *(7 marks)*

##### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Safety–Liveness decomposition (Baier & Katoen, *Principles of Model Checking*, Chapter 6):**

An LT property $P_{safe} \subseteq (2^{AP})^\omega$ is a **safety property** if for every word $\sigma \notin P_{safe}$, there exists a *finite prefix* $\hat{\sigma}$ of $\sigma$ such that no extension of $\hat{\sigma}$ is in $P_{safe}$. The set of all such *minimal bad prefixes* is denoted $\text{MinBadPref}(P_{safe})$.

An LT property $P_{live} \subseteq (2^{AP})^\omega$ is a **liveness property** if for every finite word $w \in (2^{AP})^*$ there exists an extension $\sigma$ of $w$ with $\sigma \in P_{live}$. Equivalently, $P_{live}$ has *no* bad prefixes.

**Theorem:** Every LT property $P$ can be decomposed as $P = P_{safe} \cap P_{live}$.

**Proof that deadlock-freeness is a safety property:**

Let $P_{\text{DF}} = \lbrace \sigma \in (2^{AP})^\omega \mid \forall k \ge 0 : deadlock \notin \sigma_k \rbrace$ be the deadlock-freeness LT property, where $deadlock \in AP$ is the trap-state proposition. Take any $\sigma \notin P_{\text{DF}}$. Then there exists some index $k$ with $deadlock \in \sigma_k$. Let $\hat{\sigma} = \sigma_0 \sigma_1 \ldots \sigma_k$. For any extension $u \in (2^{AP})^\omega$, the $k$-th symbol of $\hat{\sigma} u$ is still $deadlock$, so $\hat{\sigma} u \notin P_{\text{DF}}$. Hence every bad word has a *finite bad prefix* — the formal condition of safety. ∎

*(7 marks: 2 marks safety definition, 2 marks liveness definition, 1 mark decomposition theorem, 2 marks the proof for deadlock-freeness.)*

##### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**State space of 3-process mutex (states per process: N, T, C).** Total $3^3 = 27$ global states. The transition rules are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(N, *, *) &\to (T, *, *) \quad \text{(non-critical} \to \text{trying for any process)} \\
(T, *, *, \text{lock-free}) &\to (C, *, *) \quad \text{(enter CS if no other process is in C)} \\
(C, *, *) &\to (N, *, *) \quad \text{(exit CS)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Buggy rule:** The condition "lock is free" is not checked, so two processes can simultaneously transition $T \to C$ — but then no transition out of the global state where two processes are in $C$ is possible, because each $C$ can only go to $N$ and the *exiting* rule was never instrumented with a guard. The state space thus has the following deadlock-reachable subset:

$$\text{Deadlock states} = \lbrace (C, C, N), (C, N, C), (N, C, C), (C, C, T), (C, T, C), (T, C, C), (C, C, C) \rbrace$$

**LTL property to detect the bug:**

$$\varphi \;=\; \Box \, \neg\, deadlock$$

**Counter-example trace:**

$$
\pi_{\text{bad}} \;=\; (N, N, N) \to (T, N, N) \to (C, N, N) \to (C, T, N) \to (C, C, N)
$$

The final state $(C, C, N)$ is in the deadlock set, and the trace length is finite (5 states), confirming the safety nature of the violation. The model checker will return this trace as the diagnostic. *(7 marks: 2 marks for state-space construction, 2 marks for deadlock identification, 1 mark for the LTL formula, 2 marks for the counter-example trace.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Deadlock Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating deadlock with starvation** — Deadlock is a *static* (state) property; starvation is a *temporal* (liveness) property. Do not interchange them.
> 2. **Writing $\Diamond \neg deadlock$ instead of $\Box \neg deadlock$** — This reverses the meaning. The negation pattern is a heavily tested trap.
> 3. **Forgetting the trap-state encoding** — Without introducing a self-loop on deadlock states, the transition system is *not* infinite-state-alphabet-friendly and the LTL encoding is not well-defined.
> 4. **Omitting the universal path quantifier** — In LTL, the universal path quantifier is implicit. Writing $\exists \pi : \pi \models \Box \neg deadlock$ is technically the correct semantic form, but in LTL syntax, one writes just $\Box \neg deadlock$ and assumes the universal quantifier externally.
> 5. **Skipping the proof of the safety-classification** — KTU's 2024 scheme explicitly tests proof-style answers (Bloom's *Analyze / Evaluate* level) and a verbal statement without the bad-prefix argument will attract only 2 of the 7 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **LT Property:** Subset of $(2^{AP})^\omega$; satisfied iff *all* paths of $\mathcal{TS}$ belong to it.
- **Deadlock predicate (state level):** $Post(s) = \emptyset$.
- **Deadlock-freeness LTL formula:** $\varphi_{\text{DF}} = \Box \, \neg\, deadlock$.
- **Negation:** $\neg \varphi_{\text{DF}} = \Diamond \, deadlock$ — the canonical violation is a *finite* witness ending in a deadlock state.
- **Classification:** *Safety* property — every bad word has a *bad prefix*; witness is finite.
- **Encoding trick:** Introduce a trap state $s_\bot$ and label it with the atomic proposition $deadlock$.
- **Büchi automaton for $\Box \neg p$:** single accepting state, self-loops on $p$-free symbols, no outgoing transition on $p$-containing symbols.
- **Product automaton:** $\mathcal{TS} \otimes \mathcal{B}_\varphi$; an *accepting run* of the product corresponds to a *satisfying path* in $\mathcal{TS}$.
- **Distinction table to memorize:** Deadlock $\neq$ Starvation $\neq$ Livelock $\neq$ Partial Deadlock.
- **Real-world engines:** SPIN (`never { /* deadlock */ }`), NuSMV (`LTLSPEC G !deadlock`), UPPAAL (`A[] not deadlock`).
- **Time complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert \mathcal{TS} \vert \cdot 2^{\mathcal{O}(\vert \varphi \vert)})$ — linear in the model, exponential in the formula.
- **Common exam trap:** "Express deadlock in LTL" → $\Box \neg deadlock$, **not** $\neg \Diamond deadlock$ (these are equivalent, but the first is the canonical form).
- **Examiner favourite line:** *"A deadlock-free system may still violate progress"* — never lose sight of the safety-vs-liveness boundary.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear-Time Behavior - Paths and State Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear-Time Behavior - Paths and State Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Linear-Time Behavior

In the formal framework of **automated verification** and **model checking**, a hardware or software system is represented as a finite **state graph** (also called a **transition system** or **Kripke structure**). The system’s *linear-time behavior* is the set of all possible *execution paths* (traces) that the system could follow as time progresses linearly from an initial state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear-Time Behavior (KTU 2024 Definition)**
>
> The **linear-time behavior** of a transition system $\mathcal{TS}$ is the collection of all *infinite* sequences of states (called *paths* or *traces*) that can be generated by repeatedly following the transition relation of the system, starting from any of its initial states. Each path represents one possible execution of the system over time, viewed as a discrete, totally ordered timeline $\mathbb{N} = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$.

### 1.1.1 State Graph (Transition System)

Formally, a state graph is a 6-tuple:

$$
\mathcal{TS} = (S, \; Act, \; \rightarrow, \; I, \; AP, \; L)
$$

Where:
- $S$ — a **finite** non-empty set of **states**.
- $Act$ — a set of **action labels** (often omitted in pure Kripke structures).
- $\rightarrow \;\subseteq\; S \times Act \times S$ — the **transition relation**.
- $I \;\subseteq\; S$ — the set of **initial states**.
- $AP$ — a set of **atomic propositions** (Boolean variables describing state properties).
- $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ — a **labeling function** that assigns to every state the set of atomic propositions true in that state.

> [!NOTE]
> When $Act$ is empty and the transition relation is unlabeled, the structure is called a **Kripke Structure** $\mathcal{M} = (S, R, I, AP, L)$, the standard model for LTL/CTL verification.

### 1.1.2 Path (Execution Trace)

A **path** (or **execution trace**) starting from a state $s_0 \in S$ is an infinite sequence:

$$
\pi = s_0 \xrightarrow{a_0} s_1 \xrightarrow{a_1} s_2 \xrightarrow{a_2} s_3 \xrightarrow{a_3} \ldots
$$

such that for every $i \ge 0$:

$$
(s_i, \; a_i, \; s_{i+1}) \;\in\; \rightarrow
$$

Equivalently, the path is an element of $S^{\omega}$ (the set of all infinite sequences over $S$). The notation $s_0 \xrightarrow{a_0 a_1 a_2 \ldots} s_{\omega}$ is also common.

### 1.1.3 Path Notation and Slicing

- $\pi[i]$ — denotes the $(i+1)$-th state in the path (zero-indexed), i.e., $\pi[i] = s_i$.
- $\pi^{i}$ — denotes the **path suffix** starting at position $i$, i.e., $\pi^{i} = s_i, s_{i+1}, s_{i+2}, \ldots$
- $\mathrm{first}(\pi) = s_0$ — the first state of the path.
- $\mathrm{Trace}(s)$ — the set of all paths starting from state $s$.
- $\mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS}) = \bigcup_{s \in I} \mathrm{Trace}(s)$ — the linear-time behavior of the entire system.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip (KTU Pattern):** Always explicitly state the **infinite** nature of paths. KTU examiners specifically look for the $\omega$-word (omega-word) interpretation. A *finite* sequence is called a **path fragment**, **prefix**, or **computation** — not a *path*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **metro rail network map** of a city:

- **Stations** = *States* in the state graph. Each station has a name and a set of properties (e.g., "has free Wi-Fi", "is an interchange").
- **Tracks between stations** = *Transitions* in the state graph.
- **An actual metro ride** = a *Path* — once a passenger boards a train, the journey is an infinite, never-ending sequence of stations (because the train keeps running, looping back along the network).
- **Every possible ride starting from "Central Station"** = the *Linear-time behavior* of the system from that initial state.

The *state graph* is the **map**, the *path* is the **ride**, and *linear-time behavior* is the **set of all possible rides**. Just as two passengers boarding at "Central Station" can take completely different rides, a non-deterministic transition system generates many paths from the same starting state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction — LTL vs. CTL**
>
> - **Linear-Time Logic (LTL)** reasons about *individual paths* ("on *all* executions, the request is *eventually* granted").
> - **Computation Tree Logic (CTL)** reasons about the *branching tree* of all paths simultaneously ("on *some* execution path, the request is *eventually* granted").
>
> This module focuses on **LTL-style** linear-time reasoning.

## 1.3 Deadlocks and Terminal States

> [!WARNING]
> A state $s \in S$ is called a **deadlock state** (or *terminal state*) if it has **no outgoing transition**, i.e., $\mathrm{Post}(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid s \rightarrow s' \} = \emptyset$. In such a state, no path can be extended infinitely. KTU examiners often test the student's ability to identify such states in a given state graph.

To ensure every path is genuinely infinite, the state graph must be **deadlock-free**, or each deadlock state must be equipped with a self-loop $s \rightarrow s$.

## 1.4 Linear-Time Properties

A **linear-time property** $P$ over $AP$ is a set of infinite words over $2^{AP}$, i.e., $P \subseteq (2^{AP})^{\omega}$. The transition system $\mathcal{TS}$ *satisfies* the property $P$ (written $\mathcal{TS} \models P$) if and only if every path of $\mathcal{TS}$ belongs to $P$:

$$
\mathcal{TS} \;\models\; P \quad \iff \quad \mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS}) \;\subseteq\; P
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The labeling function $L$ converts a state-sequence $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ into the corresponding *propositional trace* $L(\pi) = L(s_0) L(s_1) L(s_2) \ldots \in (2^{AP})^{\omega}$. This is the object on which LTL formulas are evaluated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the Concept

The concept of *linear-time behavior* is built from the following layered hierarchy:

- **Layer 1 — Atomic Level (State)**
  - A state $s \in S$ is a snapshot of all system variables at one instant of time.
  - Each state carries a set of true atomic propositions $L(s) \subseteq AP$.

- **Layer 2 — Relational Level (Transition)**
  - A transition $(s, a, s') \in \rightarrow$ defines a *single step* of evolution.
  - The **post-image** of $s$ is $\mathrm{Post}(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid (s, a, s') \in \rightarrow \}$.

- **Layer 3 — Sequential Level (Path)**
  - A path is an *unbroken* chain of transitions, indexed by $\mathbb{N}$.
  - Mathematically: $\pi : \mathbb{N} \rightarrow S$ such that $\forall i \in \mathbb{N} : \pi[i] \rightarrow \pi[i+1]$.

- **Layer 4 — Collective Level (Linear-Time Behavior)**
  - The collection of *all* such paths, beginning from initial states, is the linear-time behavior.
  - This set is the semantic object for LTL model checking.

## 2.2 Why "Linear-Time"?

The qualifier *linear* refers to the fact that **time is modelled as a totally ordered set** (specifically $\mathbb{N}$, the natural numbers), with a well-defined *next* moment. This is in contrast to *branching-time* models, where every moment in time *branches* into multiple possible futures, forming a tree.

## 2.3 Key Path Operations

For a path $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$:

- **State Projection:** $\pi[i] = s_i$.
- **Suffix (Sub-path):** $\pi^{i} = s_i s_{i+1} s_{i+2} \ldots$
- **Prefix:** $\mathrm{pref}(\pi, n) = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_{n-1}$ (length $n$).
- **State Sequence Length:** $|\pi| = \infty$ (paths are always infinite in LTL semantics).

## 2.4 Classification of Paths

| Path Type | Definition | KTU Significance |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| **Maximal Path** | A path that cannot be extended further (always infinite here). | Default in LTL. |
| **Initial Path** | A path that begins at an initial state $s_0 \in I$. | The *only* paths considered when checking $\mathcal{TS} \models P$. |
| **Stuttering Path** | A path where a state may repeat consecutively without change in atomic propositions. | Central to *stuttering equivalence*. |
| **Fair Path** | A path that visits every *fairness set* infinitely often. | Used in *fairness assumptions*. |
| **Cycle / Loop** | A finite sub-sequence of states that repeats periodically within a path. | Detected by DFS in graph algorithms. |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula, notation, and condition that a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. Pay special attention to the *quantifier placement* and the *suffix operator*.

| # | Concept | Symbol / Formula | Explanation / Condition |
|---|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Transition System | $\mathcal{TS} = (S, Act, \rightarrow, I, AP, L)$ | 6-tuple definition. |
| 2 | Kripke Structure | $\mathcal{M} = (S, R, I, AP, L)$ | Transition system without actions. |
| 3 | Transition | $(s, a, s') \in \rightarrow$ | Directed labelled edge. |
| 4 | Post-Image | $\mathrm{Post}(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid s \rightarrow s' \}$ | All immediate successors. |
| 5 | Pre-Image | $\mathrm{Pre}(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid s' \rightarrow s \}$ | All immediate predecessors. |
| 6 | Path (Trace) | $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots \in S^{\omega}$ | Infinite sequence. |
| 7 | Path Validity | $\forall i \ge 0 : (s_i, s_{i+1}) \in \rightarrow$ | Every consecutive pair is a transition. |
| 8 | State at Position $i$ | $\pi[i] = s_i$ | Zero-indexed lookup. |
| 9 | Path Suffix | $\pi^{i} = s_i s_{i+1} s_{i+2} \ldots$ | Sub-path from index $i$. |
| 10 | First State | $\mathrm{first}(\pi) = s_0$ | Starting state. |
| 11 | Traces from State $s$ | $\mathrm{Trace}(s) = \{ \pi \in S^{\omega} \mid \mathrm{first}(\pi) = s \}$ | All paths starting at $s$. |
| 12 | System Traces | $\mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS}) = \bigcup_{s \in I} \mathrm{Trace}(s)$ | All initial paths. |
| 13 | Propositional Trace | $L(\pi) = L(s_0) L(s_1) L(s_2) \ldots$ | $\omega$-word over $2^{AP}$. |
