| 14 | Satisfaction | $\mathcal{TS} \models P \iff \mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS}) \subseteq P$ | All traces satisfy $P$. |
| 15 | Deadlock State | $\mathrm{Post}(s) = \emptyset$ | No outgoing transition. |
| 16 | Reachability | $s \in \mathrm{Reach}(I)$ | Some path from $I$ reaches $s$. |
| 17 | Invariant | $P$ holds in every reachable state | $\forall s \in \mathrm{Reach}(I) : s \models P$. |
| 18 | LTL Next | $\pi \models X\varphi \iff \pi^{1} \models \varphi$ | True in the *next* state. |
| 19 | LTL Eventually | $\pi \models F\varphi \iff \exists k \ge 0 : \pi^{k} \models \varphi$ | True in *some* future state. |
| 20 | LTL Globally | $\pi \models G\varphi \iff \forall k \ge 0 : \pi^{k} \models \varphi$ | True in *all* future states. |
| 21 | LTL Until | $\pi \models \varphi \,U\, \psi \iff \exists k \ge 0 : \pi^{k} \models \psi \land \forall 0 \le j < k : \pi^{j} \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds until $\psi$ becomes true. |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world verification, linear-time behavior is the semantic foundation of:
> - **Hardware model checking** (e.g., verifying that a CPU pipeline never produces an invalid memory address).
> - **Software model checking** (e.g., SPIN, NuSMV, CBMC tools).
> - **Protocol verification** (e.g., proving that a mutual-exclusion protocol never deadlocks).
> - **Safety-critical aerospace and automotive systems** (DO-178C, ISO 26262 standards).

## 2.6 Worked Walkthrough — Identifying Linear-Time Behavior

Consider a tiny system with 3 states $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, initial state $I = \{s_0\}$, and transitions:
- $s_0 \rightarrow s_1$
- $s_0 \rightarrow s_2$
- $s_1 \rightarrow s_1$ (self-loop)
- $s_2 \rightarrow s_1$

The linear-time behavior is:

$$
\mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS}) = \left\{ \; \pi \in S^{\omega} \;\middle|\; \mathrm{first}(\pi) = s_0 \;\right\}
$$

This includes infinitely many paths such as:
- $\pi_1 = s_0, s_1, s_1, s_1, s_1, \ldots$
- $\pi_2 = s_0, s_2, s_1, s_1, s_1, \ldots$
- $\pi_3 = s_0, s_1, s_1, s_2, s_1, s_1, s_2, s_1, \ldots$ (and infinitely many interleavings)

> [!NOTE]
> The linear-time behavior of *any* non-trivial finite-state system is an *uncountably infinite* set, because non-deterministic choices can be made at every step.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cardinality of $\mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS})$

We rigorously derive the size of the linear-time behavior for a system with branching factor $b$ and no cycles.

**Assumption:** Suppose $\mathcal{TS}$ has $|I| = 1$ initial state, and at every step there are exactly $b \ge 1$ possible successors. We also assume the system has no deadlock (every state has a successor).

**Step 1.** A path is an infinite sequence $s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ where each $s_{i+1} \in \mathrm{Post}(s_i)$ and $|\mathrm{Post}(s_i)| = b$.

**Step 2.** The number of *length-$n$* prefixes is $b^{n}$, because at each of the $n$ steps the choice is among $b$ options.

**Step 3.** The set of all infinite paths is the *limit* of the prefix sets:

$$
\mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS}) \;=\; \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} \left\{ \pi \in S^{\omega} \;\middle|\; \mathrm{pref}(\pi, n) \text{ is a valid length-}n \text{ prefix} \right\}
$$

**Step 4.** The cardinality is:

$$
\bigl\vert \mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS}) \bigr\vert \;=\; b^{\aleph_{0}} \;=\; 2^{\aleph_{0}} \;=\; \mathfrak{c}
$$

where $\mathfrak{c}$ denotes the cardinality of the continuum (the size of the real numbers).

**Conclusion:** Even a *finite* state graph gives rise to an *uncountably infinite* linear-time behavior. This is the fundamental reason why LTL model checking must use *finite* symbolic representations (e.g., Büchi automata) rather than explicit enumeration.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sufficient Condition for a Property to be a *Safety* Property

A linear-time property $P$ is a *safety property* iff for every $\sigma \notin P$ there exists a *finite prefix* $x$ of $\sigma$ such that *no* extension of $x$ belongs to $P$.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
P \text{ is a safety property} \quad\iff\quad \forall \sigma \in (2^{AP})^{\omega} : \bigl(\sigma \notin P\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(\exists n \ge 0 : \forall \tau \in (2^{AP})^{\omega} : \mathrm{pref}(\sigma, n) \frown \tau \notin P \bigr)
$$

where $\frown$ denotes concatenation.

**Derivation Sketch:**

- **($\Rightarrow$)** Suppose $P$ is a safety property. By definition, the *bad prefixes* $\mathrm{BadPref}(P) = \{ x \in (2^{AP})^{*} \mid \forall \tau : x \frown \tau \notin P \}$ is the set of finite words that cannot be extended into $P$. For each $\sigma \notin P$, take $x$ to be any bad prefix of $\sigma$.

- **($\Leftarrow$)** Conversely, if such a finite witness $x$ exists for every $\sigma \notin P$, then we may define $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ as the union of all such minimal $x$'s, and $P = (2^{AP})^{\omega} \setminus (\mathrm{BadPref}(P) \cdot (2^{AP})^{\omega})$.

**Engineering Interpretation:** Safety properties say *"something bad never happens"*. The finite witness $x$ is the *first moment* at which the bad event becomes inevitable. **Invariance** (e.g., "mutual exclusion is never violated") is the canonical safety property.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing LTL Operators

The following matrix explicitly derives the satisfaction conditions for the four core LTL path operators. Let $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ be a path and $\varphi, \psi$ be LTL formulas.

| Operator | Formula | Satisfaction Condition on Path $\pi$ | Plain-English Meaning |
|----------|---------|--------------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Next (X)** | $X\varphi$ | $\pi \models X\varphi \iff \pi[1] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds in the very next state. |
| **Globally (G)** | $G\varphi$ | $\pi \models G\varphi \iff \forall k \ge 0 : \pi[k] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds in *every* state of the path, now and forever. |
| **Finally / Eventually (F)** | $F\varphi$ | $\pi \models F\varphi \iff \exists k \ge 0 : \pi[k] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds in *some* future state (now or later). |
| **Until (U)** | $\varphi \, U \, \psi$ | $\pi \models \varphi U \psi \iff \exists k \ge 0 : \bigl(\pi[k] \models \psi \land \forall j, 0 \le j < k : \pi[j] \models \varphi\bigr)$ | $\varphi$ holds *continuously* until the *first* moment $\psi$ becomes true. |
| **Release (R)** | $\varphi \, R \, \psi$ | $\pi \models \varphi R \psi \iff \forall k \ge 0 : \bigl(\pi[k] \models \psi \lor \exists j < k : \pi[j] \models \varphi \bigr)$ | Dual of Until: $\psi$ holds *until and including* the moment $\varphi$ becomes true (or $\varphi$ never does). |
| **Weak Until (W)** | $\varphi \, W \, \psi$ | $\pi \models \varphi W \psi \iff (\pi \models G\varphi) \lor (\pi \models \varphi U \psi)$ | $\varphi$ holds until $\psi$ happens, *or* $\varphi$ holds forever. |

## 3.4 Derivation: Equivalence of $F$ and $U$

We show the identity $F\varphi \equiv \mathrm{true} \, U \, \varphi$:

**Step 1.** $(\Rightarrow)$ Assume $\pi \models F\varphi$. Then by definition, $\exists k \ge 0 : \pi[k] \models \varphi$.

**Step 2.** Choose $k$ as the smallest such index. Then for all $0 \le j < k$, $\pi[j] \models \mathrm{true}$ (trivially). And $\pi[k] \models \varphi$. This is exactly the condition for $\pi \models \mathrm{true} \, U \, \varphi$.

**Step 3.** $(\Leftarrow)$ Assume $\pi \models \mathrm{true} \, U \, \varphi$. Then $\exists k \ge 0 : \pi[k] \models \varphi$ and for all $0 \le j < k$, $\pi[j] \models \mathrm{true}$. The first condition alone gives $\pi \models F\varphi$.

**Conclusion:**

$$
F\varphi \;\equiv\; \mathrm{true} \, U \, \varphi
$$

This identity is critical when converting LTL to Büchi automata — it lets the converter treat $F$ and $U$ uniformly.

## 3.5 Derivation: Stuttering Equivalence

Two paths $\pi$ and $\pi'$ are *stuttering equivalent* (denoted $\pi \sim_{st} \pi'$) iff they can be obtained from each other by *merging* or *splitting* consecutive identical labelled states.

**Formal Definition:** $\pi \sim_{st} \pi'$ iff there exist two strictly increasing index sequences $0 = i_0 < i_1 < i_2 < \ldots$ and $0 = j_0 < j_1 < j_2 < \ldots$ such that for all $k \ge 0$:

$$
L(\pi[i_k]) \;=\; L(\pi[i_k + 1]) \;=\; \ldots \;=\; L(\pi[i_{k+1} - 1]) \;=\; L(\pi'[j_k]) \;=\; \ldots \;=\; L(\pi'[j_{k+1} - 1])
$$

**Key Theorem:** Two transition systems are *stuttering equivalent* iff they satisfy exactly the same set of LTL formulas that do *not* use the $X$ (next-time) operator.

$$
\mathcal{TS}_1 \sim_{st} \mathcal{TS}_2 \quad\iff\quad \forall \varphi \in \mathrm{LTL} \setminus X : \bigl(\mathcal{TS}_1 \models \varphi \iff \mathcal{TS}_2 \models \varphi\bigr)
$$

**Engineering Utility:** This theorem justifies *abstraction*: a verifier can replace complex sub-modules by simpler "summary" states that share the same atomic-proposition label, without changing the truth value of any $X$-free LTL specification. This is the foundation of *compositional verification* and *predicate abstraction*.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Conceptual Architecture of Linear-Time Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["System Model\nTransition System TS\nKripke Structure M"] --> B["State Graph\nNodes S\nEdges R\nLabels L"]
    B --> C["Linear-Time Behavior\nSet of all infinite paths"]
    C --> D["Propositional Trace\nL pi equals L s0 L s1 L s2 ..."]
    D --> E["LTL Formula phi\nPath Operators X F G U R"]
    E --> F{"Model Checking\nTS satisfies phi?"}
    F -->|Yes| G["VERIFIED\nProperty Holds on All Paths"]
    F -->|No| H["COUNTEREXAMPLE\nFailing Path Witness"]
    B --> I["Buchi Automaton A phi\nNegation of Specification"]
    I --> F
    I --> J["Emptiness Check\nLanguage A phi intersect Trace TS"]
    J --> H
    H --> K["Root Cause Analysis\nTrace back to source line"]
```

## 4.2 State Graph — Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0(("s0\n{a}\nINITIAL")) -->|alpha| s1(("s1\n{a, b}"))
    s1 -->|beta| s2(("s2\n{c}"))
    s2 -->|gamma| s1
    s2 -->|delta| s3(("s3\n{b, c}\nDEADLOCK"))
    s0 -.->|next step| sX(["..."])
    s1 -.->|next step| sY(["..."])
    s3 -.->|self loop required| s3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each node is a state, with its name, the set of atomic propositions $L(s)$ true in it, and an indicator if it is the *initial* state (double circle in formal texts) or a *deadlock* state. Each arrow is a labelled transition. The dotted arrows on the right represent the *continuation* of the infinite path beyond the visible fragment. Note that $s_3$ has no outgoing transition in the original graph; to make all paths infinite, it must be equipped with a self-loop $s_3 \rightarrow s_3$.

## 4.3 Path Tree — Unfolding of the State Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root(["Trace TS\nAll paths from s0"]) --> pA["Path A\ns0 -> s1 -> s2 -> s1 -> s2 -> ..."]
    root --> pB["Path B\ns0 -> s1 -> s2 -> s3 -> s3 -> s3 -> ..."]
    root --> pC["Path C\ns0 -> s1 -> s2 -> s1 -> s2 -> s3 -> s3 -> ..."]
    root --> pD["Path D\ns0 -> s1 -> s1 -> s1 -> ...\nif s1 self-loop existed"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Strategy:** The path tree rooted at the initial state grows downward. Each *level* corresponds to a *time step*. Each *branch* corresponds to a *non-deterministic choice*. An *infinite* path corresponds to a *root-to-leaf-infinity* branch in this tree. KTU questions often ask students to *enumerate* the first few steps of all paths in such a tree.

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Verification Toolchain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Source Code\nor Hardware RTL"] --> parse["Parser /\nFront-End"]
    parse --> kripke["Kripke Structure\nM equals S R I AP L"]
    spec["Specification\nLTL Formula phi"] --> nba["Buchi Automaton\nA sub not phi"]
    kripke --> product["Product\nTS x A sub not phi"]
    nba --> product
    product --> dfs["DFS /\nEmptiness Check"]
    dfs -->|Empty| sat["SATISFIED\nTS satisfies phi"]
    dfs -->|Non-Empty| cex["Counterexample\nFailing Path"]
    cex --> diag["Diagnostic\nVisualizer"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** This is the *actual* architecture used by industrial model checkers such as **SPIN** (for LTL), **NuSMV**, **CBMC**, and **Cadence JasperGold**. The "emptiness check" on the product automaton is a *graph-theoretic* operation (nested DFS, strongly-connected-components, etc.) that runs in $O(|S| + |R|)$ time. This is the *only* tractable way to verify properties over an uncountable set of paths.

## 4.5 Decision Matrix — LTL Operator Truth Tables

The following compact matrix shows, for each combination of the *current* state and the *next* state label, whether the LTL formula is satisfied at that position on the path.

| Current $L(s_i)$ | Next $L(s_{i+1})$ | $X \varphi$ | $F \varphi$ | $G \varphi$ | $\varphi \, U \, \psi$ |
|------------------|------------------|-------------|-------------|-------------|---------------------|
| $\varphi$ true | $\varphi$ true | T | T | T (locally) | depends on $\psi$ |
| $\varphi$ true | $\varphi$ false | F | T | F | T until $\psi$ |
| $\varphi$ false | $\varphi$ true | T | T | F | F (must reset) |
| $\varphi$ false | $\varphi$ false | F | depends on rest | F | F |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answers, always justify *why* an operator is satisfied or violated by referring back to the *temporal scope* (now / next / eventually / always) and the *truth value* of the sub-formula at each relevant position.
