# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 1]**
> **Define a transition system (state graph). With a suitable example, illustrate the difference between a *path* and a *trace* of a transition system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *transition system* (or *state graph*) is a 6-tuple $\mathcal{TS} = (S, Act, \rightarrow, I, AP, L)$ where $S$ is a finite set of states, $Act$ is a set of actions, $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times Act \times S$ is the transition relation, $I \subseteq S$ is the set of initial states, $AP$ is a set of atomic propositions, and $L: S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is the labeling function. **(1 mark)**

A *path* (also called an *execution trace*) is an infinite sequence of states $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ such that for every $i \ge 0$, $(s_i, a_i, s_{i+1}) \in \rightarrow$ for some action $a_i$. A path represents *one* possible execution of the system. **(1 mark)**

**Difference (1 mark):** A *trace* is the *set of all paths* starting from a given state (or set of initial states). In the literature, however, the two terms are often used interchangeably: an individual path is also called a trace, and a set of paths is also called a *set of traces* or *linear-time behavior*. The key idea is that the trace set captures *all* possible executions.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sample Paper, Module 1]**
> **Explain the concept of a *deadlock state* in a transition system. Why is it important to handle deadlock states when defining linear-time behavior? Can an LTL property be evaluated on a path that ends in a deadlock? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *deadlock state* $s \in S$ is a state that has no outgoing transitions, i.e., $\mathrm{Post}(s) = \emptyset$. **(1 mark)**

**Importance:** Because linear-time behavior and LTL are defined over *infinite* paths $\pi \in S^{\omega}$, a path that reaches a deadlock state cannot be extended indefinitely. If the model contains deadlocks, the linear-time behavior is *not* a clean subset of $S^{\omega}$, and LTL operators like $G\varphi$ and $F\varphi$ become ill-defined on terminating paths. **(1 mark)**

**Answer to the third part:** No, by the standard semantics, an LTL property is *never* evaluated on a finite or deadlocked path. The standard convention is either to:
- (a) equip every deadlock state with a *self-loop* $s \rightarrow s$ (so that any finite path can be extended to an infinite stuttering path), or
- (b) restrict the analysis to deadlock-free models.

This convention is essential to make the LTL satisfaction relation $\pi \models \varphi$ well-defined for *all* paths. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Consider the Kripke structure $\mathcal{M} = (S, R, I, AP, L)$ shown below, where $AP = \{p, q, r\}$.

```
States:    s0  s1  s2  s3  s4
L(s0) = {}       (initial state)
L(s1) = {p}
L(s2) = {p, q}
L(s3) = {q, r}
L(s4) = {r}

Transitions:
s0 -> s1
s0 -> s2
s1 -> s2
s2 -> s2    (self-loop)
s2 -> s3
s3 -> s4
s4 -> s4    (self-loop)
```

**(i)** Draw the state graph. **(2 marks)**
**(ii)** Enumerate *all* maximal paths starting from the initial state $s_0$. **(3 marks)**
**(iii)** Identify the deadlock states, if any. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** For the same Kripke structure, evaluate the following LTL formulas on the system. For each formula, state whether $\mathcal{M} \models \varphi$ holds and provide a justification by referring to the relevant path(s). **(7 marks)**

**(i)** $\varphi_1 = G(p \lor r)$ — *Invariant: p or r always holds.*
**(ii)** $\varphi_2 = F q$ — *Eventually, q holds.*
**(iii)** $\varphi_3 = G(p \rightarrow F q)$ — *Whenever p holds, q will eventually hold.*

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

**(a)(i) State Graph (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0(("s0\n{}\nINIT")) --> s1(("s1\n{p}"))
    s0 --> s2(("s2\n{p,q}"))
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s2
    s2 --> s3(("s3\n{q,r}"))
    s3 --> s4(("s4\n{r}"))
    s4 --> s4
```

**(a)(ii) Maximal Paths from $s_0$ (3 marks):**

The linear-time behavior of $\mathcal{M}$ consists of the following infinite paths:

- $\pi_1 = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_2, s_2, s_2, \ldots$ (take $s_0 \rightarrow s_1$, then $s_1 \rightarrow s_2$, then stay in $s_2$ forever via the self-loop).
- $\pi_2 = s_0, s_2, s_2, s_2, s_2, \ldots$ (take $s_0 \rightarrow s_2$, then self-loop).
- $\pi_3 = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_4, s_4, s_4, \ldots$
- $\pi_4 = s_0, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_4, s_4, s_4, \ldots$
- $\pi_5 = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_2, \ldots, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_4, s_4, \ldots$ (any number of self-loops at $s_2$, then leave).
- $\pi_6 = s_0, s_2, s_2, \ldots, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_4, s_4, \ldots$ (similar).

In total, the trace set is uncountable (as shown in §3.1).

**(a)(iii) Deadlock States (2 marks):**

Inspecting each state:
- $\mathrm{Post}(s_0) = \{s_1, s_2\} \neq \emptyset$
- $\mathrm{Post}(s_1) = \{s_2\} \neq \emptyset$
- $\mathrm{Post}(s_2) = \{s_2, s_3\} \neq \emptyset$
- $\mathrm{Post}(s_3) = \{s_4\} \neq \emptyset$
- $\mathrm{Post}(s_4) = \{s_4\} \neq \emptyset$ (self-loop)

**Conclusion:** There are **no deadlock states** in this Kripke structure. Every state has at least one outgoing transition.

---

**(b)(i) $\varphi_1 = G(p \lor r)$ (2 marks):**

We must check that $p \lor r$ holds in *every* state of *every* path.

State labels: $L(s_0)=\{\}$, $L(s_1)=\{p\}$, $L(s_2)=\{p,q\}$, $L(s_3)=\{q,r\}$, $L(s_4)=\{r\}$.

- $s_0$: $p \lor r$? Both false. $\varphi_1$ **violated** at position $i = 0$.

Since $\pi_1$ and $\pi_2$ both start at $s_0$, $\mathcal{M} \not\models G(p \lor r)$.

**Verdict: $\mathcal{M} \not\models \varphi_1$ (FAILS).**

**(b)(ii) $\varphi_2 = F q$ (2 marks):**

We must check that $q$ holds in *some* state of *every* path.

- $\pi_1 = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_2, \ldots$: at $s_2$, $q$ is true. ✓
- $\pi_2 = s_0, s_2, s_2, \ldots$: at $s_2$, $q$ is true. ✓
- $\pi_3, \pi_4, \pi_5, \pi_6$: at $s_2$ or $s_3$, $q$ is true. ✓

**Verdict: $\mathcal{M} \models \varphi_2$ (HOLDS).**

**(b)(iii) $\varphi_3 = G(p \rightarrow F q)$ (3 marks):**

We must check that *whenever* $p$ holds, $q$ holds eventually *after* that position.

- At $s_0$: $p$ is false, so $p \rightarrow F q$ is vacuously true.
- At $s_1$: $p$ is true; $F q$ must hold in $\pi^{1}$ (from $s_1$ onwards). In every path, $s_1$ is followed by $s_2$ where $q$ is true. ✓
- At $s_2$: $p$ is true; from $s_2$ onwards, $q$ is true (either we stay at $s_2$ where $q$ is true, or go to $s_3$ where $q$ is true). ✓
- At $s_3, s_4$: $p$ is false, vacuously true.

**Verdict: $\mathcal{M} \models \varphi_3$ (HOLDS).**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with one example each: **(7 marks)**
**(i)** State graph (Kripke structure)
**(ii)** Path and its suffix operation
**(iii)** Stuttering equivalence
**(iv)** Deadlock state

**(b)** Consider the following Kripke structure $\mathcal{M}'$ modelling a traffic light:

```
States:    red, yellow, green
L(red)    = {light_is_red}
L(yellow) = {light_is_yellow}
L(green)  = {light_is_green}
Transitions: red -> green, green -> yellow, yellow -> red
Initial state: red
```

**(i)** Draw the state graph. **(2 marks)**
**(ii)** List the unique maximal path(s). **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Determine whether the following properties hold on $\mathcal{M}'$: **(3 marks)**
- $P_1$: $G(\mathrm{light\_is\_red} \lor \mathrm{light\_is\_yellow} \lor \mathrm{light\_is\_green})$ — *The light is always in exactly one of the three states.*
- $P_2$: $G(\mathrm{light\_is\_red} \rightarrow X X \, \mathrm{light\_is\_red})$ — *If the light is red, it will be red again two steps later.*

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

**(a)(i) State Graph / Kripke Structure (1.75 marks):**

A *Kripke structure* $\mathcal{M} = (S, R, I, AP, L)$ is a labelled directed graph where $S$ is a finite non-empty set of states, $R \subseteq S \times S$ is a (total) transition relation, $I \subseteq S$ is the set of initial states, $AP$ is a set of atomic propositions, and $L: S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is a labelling function. *Example:* A two-state toggle switch with $S = \{ \mathrm{on}, \mathrm{off} \}$, $R = \{ (\mathrm{on}, \mathrm{off}), (\mathrm{off}, \mathrm{on}) \}$, $I = \{\mathrm{on}\}$, $AP = \{\mathrm{power}\}$, $L(\mathrm{on}) = \{\mathrm{power}\}$, $L(\mathrm{off}) = \emptyset$.

**(a)(ii) Path and Suffix (1.75 marks):**

A *path* is an infinite sequence $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots \in S^{\omega}$ such that $\forall i \ge 0: (s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$. The *suffix* $\pi^{k}$ is the sub-path starting at position $k$, i.e., $\pi^{k} = s_k s_{k+1} s_{k+2} \ldots$. *Example:* If $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, \ldots$, then $\pi^{2} = s_2, s_3, s_4, \ldots$

**(a)(iii) Stuttering Equivalence (1.75 marks):**

Two paths $\pi, \pi'$ are *stuttering equivalent* ($\pi \sim_{st} \pi'$) iff they can be obtained from each other by inserting or removing consecutive duplicate state labels. *Example:* $\pi = a, a, b, c, c, c, d$ and $\pi' = a, b, c, d$ are stuttering equivalent.

**(a)(iv) Deadlock State (1.75 marks):**

A state $s$ is a *deadlock* iff $\mathrm{Post}(s) = \emptyset$. *Example:* A printer whose paper-tray sensor reports "empty" and has no recovery transition is a deadlock state — once entered, no further behaviour is possible.

---

**(b)(i) State Graph (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    red(("red\n{red}\nINIT")) --> green(("green\n{green}"))
    green --> yellow(("yellow\n{yellow}"))
    yellow --> red
```

**(b)(ii) Unique Maximal Path (2 marks):**

Because the traffic light cycles deterministically (each state has exactly one successor), there is exactly **one** maximal path:

$$
\pi = \mathrm{red}, \mathrm{green}, \mathrm{yellow}, \mathrm{red}, \mathrm{green}, \mathrm{yellow}, \mathrm{red}, \ldots
$$

This path is *ultimately periodic* with period 3.

**(b)(iii) Property Evaluation (3 marks):**

**Property $P_1 = G(\mathrm{red} \lor \mathrm{yellow} \lor \mathrm{green})$:**

At every position of $\pi$, exactly one of the three atomic propositions is true. The disjunction is therefore always true.

**Verdict: $\mathcal{M}' \models P_1$ (HOLDS). (1.5 marks)**

**Property $P_2 = G(\mathrm{red} \rightarrow X X \, \mathrm{red})$:**

Trace the path step-by-step:

| Position $i$ | State | $\mathrm{red}$? | $X X \, \mathrm{red}$? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | red | T | state at $i+2$ is yellow. **F** |
| 1 | green | F | vacuously T |
| 2 | yellow | F | vacuously T |
| 3 | red | T | state at $i+2$ is yellow. **F** |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |

At positions $i = 0, 3, 6, 9, \ldots$ (every 3 steps), when the light is red, the state two steps later is *yellow*, not red. Hence $P_2$ is violated infinitely often.

**Verdict: $\mathcal{M}' \not\models P_2$ (FAILS). (1.5 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "infinite" qualifier:** Several students write "a path is a sequence of states $s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_n$" — this describes a *finite* path, not a true LTL path. You will lose **1 mark** for this in any 7-mark sub-question on path definitions.
> 2. **Confusing $\pi[i]$ with $\pi^{i}$:** $\pi[i]$ is the *single state* at position $i$, whereas $\pi^{i}$ is the *infinite suffix* starting at $i$. Examiners check this distinction carefully. Mixing them up costs **at least 0.5 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting the $\forall i \ge 0$ quantifier:** When you state the path validity condition, the universal quantifier over all positions is *mandatory*. Without it, the definition is incomplete.
> 4. **Confusing $G$ and $F$:** A common mistake is to evaluate $F\varphi$ by checking *all* states (that's $G\varphi$). Remember: $F$ asks for *some* future state; $G$ asks for *all* future states.
> 5. **Ignoring deadlocks:** If the state graph has a deadlock, you must explicitly state how it is handled (self-loop, exclusion, etc.) — otherwise, the linear-time behavior is *not* well-defined.
> 6. **Single-path reasoning for universal properties:** To prove $\mathcal{TS} \models G\varphi$, you must argue over **all** initial paths, not just one. A single counter-example path is enough to *refute* $G\varphi$, but you cannot *prove* it by exhibiting a single satisfying path.
> 7. **Skipping the labeling function in Kripke structures:** Always mention the $L$ function when defining a Kripke structure; without it, the structure cannot be linked to LTL formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Linear-Time Behavior, Paths and State Graph**

- **State Graph (Transition System):** 6-tuple $\mathcal{TS} = (S, Act, \rightarrow, I, AP, L)$. A *Kripke structure* drops the $Act$ component.
- **Path (Trace):** Infinite sequence $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots \in S^{\omega}$ with $\forall i \ge 0: (s_i, s_{i+1}) \in \rightarrow$.
- **Path State vs. Suffix:** $\pi[i] = s_i$ (a state); $\pi^{i} = s_i s_{i+1} \ldots$ (an infinite sub-path).
- **Path Set:** $\mathrm{Trace}(s)$ from a state $s$; $\mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS}) = \bigcup_{s \in I} \mathrm{Trace}(s)$.
- **Linear-Time Behavior:** $\mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS})$ — the *semantic object* for LTL model checking.
- **Satisfaction:** $\mathcal{TS} \models P \iff \mathrm{Trace}(\mathcal{TS}) \subseteq P$.
- **Deadlock State:** $\mathrm{Post}(s) = \emptyset$. To keep paths infinite, either add a self-loop or restrict analysis.
- **Cardinality:** Even a finite state graph has uncountably many paths ($2^{\aleph_0}$).
- **Core LTL Operators and Semantics:**
  - $X\varphi$: $\pi \models X\varphi \iff \pi^{1} \models \varphi$
  - $F\varphi \equiv \mathrm{true}\,U\,\varphi$: $\pi \models F\varphi \iff \exists k \ge 0 : \pi^{k} \models \varphi$
  - $G\varphi$: $\pi \models G\varphi \iff \forall k \ge 0 : \pi^{k} \models \varphi$
  - $\varphi\,U\,\psi$: $\pi \models \varphi\,U\,\psi \iff \exists k \ge 0 : (\pi^{k} \models \psi \land \forall j < k : \pi^{j} \models \varphi)$
- **Safety vs. Liveness:** Safety = "bad thing never happens" (finite witness); Liveness = "good thing eventually happens" (no finite witness).
- **Stuttering Equivalence:** Two systems are stuttering equivalent iff they agree on all $X$-free LTL formulas.
- **Verification Flow:** Source $\rightarrow$ Kripke Structure $\rightarrow$ Product with $\neg$Spec Büchi $\rightarrow$ Emptiness Check $\rightarrow$ Yes/No + Counterexample.
- **Mnemonic for LTL Operators:**
  - $X$ = **X**tra step (next).
  - $F$ = **F**inally (eventually).
  - $G$ = **G**lobally (always).
  - $U$ = **U**ntil.
  - $R$ = **R**elease.
- **Common Board-Exam Traps:** Forgetting $\omega$ (infinity); confusing $F$ with $G$; missing the quantifier $\forall i \ge 0$; omitting the labeling function $L$; ignoring deadlocks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Fragment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Path Fragment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In SystemVerilog Assertions (SVA), a **Path Fragment** is a multi-clock sequence construction technique used to describe temporal behavior that spans across **multiple clock domains**. It is built using the `$` (dollar) symbol as an **endpoint marker** within a sequence, allowing the sequence to terminate its evaluation at a specific point while permitting a different clocking event to govern the synchronization of the conclusion of that path.

According to the IEEE 1800-2017 LRM (Section 16.10) — which is the **de facto standard** for SVA semantics followed by KTU's verification syllabus — a path fragment is essentially a sequence where the **sampling clock at the endpoint is not necessarily the same as the clock that advanced the path** through the sequence body.

Formally:
- A **sequence** is a building block of SVA temporal logic.
- A **path** is a finite list of sequence elements governed by a single clocking event.
- A **path fragment** is a sequence whose endpoint (`$`) is explicitly synchronized by a clocking event that may differ from the path's own advancement clock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Path fragments are essential for **multi-clock domain verification** — a non-negotiable skill in modern SoC verification where signals cross asynchronous clock boundaries (e.g., AXI, AHB, PCIe, USB protocols).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **waiting at a railway station (Clock Domain A)** for a train to arrive. The train schedule runs on **Indian Standard Time (Clock Domain B)**. You are "sampling" the train's arrival at the platform using the platform's local clock, but the train itself has been running on its own schedule. When the train finally pulls in (the endpoint), you synchronize your "acknowledgment" with your own watch — not the train's internal clock.

In hardware terms:
- The **path inside the sequence** is the train moving along its track (governed by its own clock).
- The **`$` endpoint** is when the train reaches the station.
- **The destination clock** (your watch / the implication's clock) governs when you "officially" record the train's arrival.

This is the essence of a **path fragment** — **decoupling the path's progression clock from the endpoint's sampling clock.**

## 1.3 Why Path Fragments Matter in Modern Verification

> [!NOTE]
> Modern SoCs (System-on-Chips) routinely contain **dozens of clock domains**. Naively using single-clock sequences (`|->` or `|=>`) on signals crossing these domains results in **race conditions**, **false positives**, or **missed bugs** during simulation. Path fragments provide the formal mechanism to express correct multi-clock temporal behavior.

### Key Constants / Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

| Metric | Value / Standard | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Default simulation precision** | **1 ps** | Affects multi-clock skew tolerance |
| **Default clocking skew** | **#0** (zero-delay) | Used when no skew is specified in `default clocking` |
| **Max sequence length (typical)** | **Unbounded** (practical limit: ~16-32 cycles for tractability) | Affects formal verification scalability |
| **IEEE Standard** | **IEEE 1800-2017** | Authoritative LRM for SVA semantics |
| **Endpoint symbol** | **`$`** | Universal SVA symbol for "end of sequence / path fragment endpoint" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A path fragment in SVA is constructed through these structured logic steps:

### Step 1: Defining a Sequence as a Path Fragment
A sequence becomes a path fragment when it uses the `$` symbol explicitly to mark an endpoint that is to be synchronized later by an external clocking context.

**Example Syntax:**
```systemverilog
sequence s_multi_clk;
    @(posedge clk_a) req ## [1:4] @(posedge clk_b) ack ##1 @(posedge clk_c) done $;
endsequence
```

### Step 2: Understanding the `$` Endpoint Symbol
- The `$` denotes a **"thread termination point"** within the sequence.
- It does **not** advance the simulation time by itself.
- It serves as a **synchronization handle** for the calling context (assertion, property, or another sequence).

### Step 3: Clock Flow Rules (Per IEEE 1800-2017)
- **Single-clock path:** A sequence without explicit clocking events is governed entirely by the **inherited clock** from the property context.
- **Multi-clock path:** When a sequence contains explicit clocking events (`@(...)`), each sub-segment runs on its own clock.
- **Path fragment endpoint:** The `$` allows the **inherited clock** (or `default clocking`) to sample the result at the end.

### Step 4: Why 'Why' and 'How' — The Core Rationale

**The "Why":** In real hardware, signals often traverse multiple clock domains (e.g., data captured on `clk_a`, processed on `clk_b`, output on `clk_c`). A single clock cannot accurately model such behavior.

**The "How":** SVA path fragments allow the verification engineer to **explicitly declare clock boundaries** within a temporal sequence. The `$` endpoint acts as a "handshake" point where the assertion's primary clock takes over to evaluate the result.

### Step 5: Implication Operator Behavior with Path Fragments

| Implication Type | Behavior with Path Fragments |
|---|---|
| **Overlapping (`\|->`)** | Consequent begins in the same cycle as the endpoint of the antecedent (using endpoint's clock) |
| **Non-overlapping (`\|=>`)** | Consequent begins one cycle after the endpoint of the antecedent |
| **First-match (`first_match`)** | Selects the earliest match of a path fragment sequence |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** In the following table, the `|` symbol is **deliberately avoided** inside table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

| S.No. | Construct | Syntax Pattern | Semantic Meaning | Common Use Case |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Endpoint Marker** | `... $;` | Marks termination of a path fragment | Multi-clock sequence boundaries |
| 2 | **Default Clocking** | `default clocking clk_blk @posedge clk;` | Sets inherited clocking event | Module-level clock context |
| 3 | **Default Disable** | `default disable iff (rst_n);` | Specifies global reset/disabled condition | Asynchronous reset handling |
| 4 | **Multi-clock Segment** | `@(posedge clk_a) sig1 ##1 @(posedge clk_b) sig2` | Sequence crossing clock domains | CDC verification |
| 5 | **Overlapping Implication** | `antecedent \|-> consequent` | Consequent starts at antecedent's endpoint | Combinational-like timing |
| 6 | **Non-overlapping Implication** | `antecedent \|=> consequent` | Consequent starts one cycle after endpoint | Registered timing |
| 7 | **Throughout Operator** | `sig1 throughout seq` | `sig1` must hold for the entire `seq` | Persistent invariants |
| 8 | **Within Operator** | `sig1 within seq` | `sig1` must occur at some point within `seq` | Loose temporal containment |
| 9 | **Intersect Operator** | `seq1 intersect seq2` | Both sequences must match at same times | Tight temporal alignment |
| 10 | **First-match** | `first_match(seq)` | Returns earliest match of a sequence | Eliminating ambiguity in `##[*]` ranges |
| 11 | **Property Declaration** | `property p_name; ... endproperty` | Named, reusable temporal assertion | Modular verification IP |
| 12 | **Assertion Directive** | `assert property (p_name);` | Activates the property check | Testbench binding |
| 13 | **Clock Skew (typical)** | `#0` | Zero skew between clock and signal | Conservative simulation |
| 14 | **Endpoint Sampling Rule** | `inherited_clock(endpoint \vert sequence)` | Endpoint uses the calling context's clock | Path fragment synchronization |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Domains:**
> - **CDC (Clock Domain Crossing) Verification:** Path fragments are the **gold standard** for verifying FIFOs, handshake protocols (req/ack), and metastability resolvers.
> - **AXI/AHB Protocol Compliance:** Multi-clock protocol assertions (e.g., AXI's `ACLK` vs. interconnect clocks) heavily rely on path fragments.
> - **Power-Aware Verification:** Switching between active and gated clocks during UPF-aware simulation requires path fragments to express state retention correctly.
> - **Formal Verification:** Path fragments are essential in formal property checking (e.g., JasperGold, Cadence Jasper) for liveness properties spanning multiple clocks.
> - **Mixed-Signal Verification:** When digital control crosses into analog sampling domains, path fragments help model the asynchronous boundaries.

### Common Pitfall: Forgetting `$` in Multi-clock Sequences

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** If you write a multi-clock sequence **without** the `$` endpoint marker, the SVA simulator will attempt to sample the entire sequence using the **first clock encountered**, leading to **silent incorrect behavior**. Always explicitly mark path fragment endpoints with `$`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: How Multi-Clock Sequences Reduce to Path Fragments

### Derivation 1: From Single-Clock Sequence to Multi-Clock Path Fragment

**Starting Premise:** A single-clock sequence advances on a single clocking event.

$$
\text{Single-clock} = @(posedge \ clk) \ e_1 \ \#\#1 \ e_2 \ \#\#1 \ e_3
$$

**Step 1 (Textual):** Identify each cycle in the temporal chain and the boolean expression evaluated at that cycle.

Here, `e1` is evaluated at the first rising edge of `clk`, `e2` at the second rising edge, and `e3` at the third rising edge.

**Step 2 (Textual):** Now introduce a second clock `clk_b` that takes over after two cycles.

$$
@(posedge \ clk) \ e_1 \ \#\#1 \ e_2 \ \#\#1 \ @(posedge \ clk\_b) \ e_3
$$

**Step 3 (Textual):** The sequence now has two distinct clocking regions. The transition from `clk` to `clk_b` is a clock-domain crossing. The `e3` endpoint occurs on `clk_b`.

**Step 4 (Textual):** To make this a path fragment, we append the `$` endpoint marker after the final expression.

$$
@(posedge \ clk) \ e_1 \ \#\#1 \ e_2 \ \#\#1 \ @(posedge \ clk\_b) \ e_3 \ \$
$$

**Step 5 (Textual):** The `$` signals to the consuming property that this sequence is a path fragment, and its endpoint will be sampled by the property's context clock.

### Derivation 2: Property Context Sampling Rule for Path Fragments

**Step 1 (Textual):** Consider a property that uses a path fragment sequence in its antecedent.

```systemverilog
property p_cdc_check;
    @(posedge clk_x) 
    req_fast ##1 @(posedge clk_slow) ack_slow $ |-> ready;
endproperty
```

**Step 2 (Textual):** The path fragment `req_fast ##1 @(posedge clk_slow) ack_slow $` begins on `clk_x` and crosses to `clk_slow`.

**Step 3 (Textual):** At the `$` endpoint, the simulator notes the completion of the path fragment and hands control back to the property's governing clock — which is `clk_x` (the property's declared clock).

**Step 4 (Textual):** The consequent `ready` is therefore sampled on `clk_x`, **not** on `clk_slow`. This models the realistic scenario where a fast clock domain observes the completion of a slow-domain handshake.

**Step 5 (Textual):** The formal sampling rule can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Sample}_{\text{property}}(end\_point) = \text{Clock}_{\text{property}}
$$

$$
\text{Sample}_{\text{consequent}}(sig) = \text{Clock}_{\text{property}}
$$

### Derivation 3: The Endpoint Synchronization Algebra

Let us formalize the temporal logic of a path fragment.

**Step 1 (Textual):** Define the path fragment as a tuple of (sequence body, endpoint, endpoint clock).

$$
PF = (B, \$, C_{\$})
$$

Where:
- $B$ = sequence body (containing explicit clocking events)
- $\$$ = endpoint marker
- $C_{\$}$ = clock governing the endpoint (usually inherited)

**Step 2 (Textual):** The boolean evaluation at the endpoint follows the rule:

$$
PF\_end = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \text{eval}_{C_i}(e_i) \ \vert \ C_i \in \text{clocks}(B)
$$

This is read as: the path fragment is considered "ended successfully" only if every sub-segment $e_i$ evaluates to true under its respective clock $C_i$.

**Step 3 (Textual):** The implication operator's timing relative to the endpoint is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overlap} &: \quad \text{consequent}_{\text{eval}} = \text{eval}_{C_{\$}}(\text{consequent\_expr}) \\
\text{Non-overlap} &: \quad \text{consequent}_{\text{eval}} = \text{eval}_{C_{\$}}(\text{consequent\_expr} \ \text{at} \ t + 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $t$ is the simulation time at which the path fragment endpoint is reached.

**Step 4 (Textual):** Practical timing illustration: if `req` fires at $t=10$ on `clk_x` and `ack` fires at $t=42$ on `clk_slow` (assuming different time bases), the path fragment's endpoint is at $t=42$ simulation time, but the property clock `clk_x` will check the consequent on the next rising edge of `clk_x` after $t=42$.

**Step 5 (Textual):** The full sequence of events can be summarized as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_0 &: \text{req asserted on } clk_x \\
t_1 &= t_0 + 1 \cdot T_{clk\_x} : \text{property checks continuation on } clk_x \\
& \ldots \text{ (path progresses)} \\
t_{\text{cross}} &: \text{handshake crossing occurs} \\
t_{\text{end}} &: \text{ack asserted on } clk\_slow \rightarrow \text{path fragment endpoint} \$ \\
t_{\text{end}} + \epsilon &: \text{consequent evaluated on next } clk\_x \text{ edge}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Path Fragment Variations

| Variation | Trigger Condition | Consequent Behavior | Endpoint Clock | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-clock PF** | `$` used but no multi-clock switch | Consequent on same clock | Same as antecedent | Syntactic clarity |
| **Multi-clock PF (overlap)** | `seq_with_$ \|-> consequent` | Consequent starts at `$` | Property's clock | Tight CDC handshake |
| **Multi-clock PF (non-overlap)** | `seq_with_$ \|=> consequent` | Consequent starts one cycle after `$` | Property's clock | Registered CDC |
| **`default clocking` + PF** | Module-level default + path fragment | Inherits default | Default clock | Large-scale TB consistency |
| **`default disable iff` + PF** | Reset overrides path | PF suspended during reset | Property's clock | Asynchronous reset handling |
| **`first_match` + PF** | Ambiguous multi-path resolved | Earliest PF used | Earliest match's clock | Async FIFO pointer verification |

## 3.3 Worked Example: CDC Handshake Property

**Design Intent:** A request `req` on `clk_fast` must be acknowledged `ack` on `clk_slow` within 4 cycles of `clk_slow`, after which `ready` must assert on `clk_fast` for at least 2 cycles.

**Step 1 (Textual):** Build the antecedent path fragment that captures the multi-clock handshake.

```systemverilog
sequence s_handshake_pf;
    @(posedge clk_fast) req ##0 @(posedge clk_slow) [->1] ack ##0 $;
endsequence
```

**Step 2 (Textual):** The `[->1]` is a "goto repetition" operator meaning "the first occurrence of `ack` on `clk_slow`."

**Step 3 (Textual):** The `$` marks the end of this multi-clock path.

**Step 4 (Textual):** Now wrap this in a property with a non-overlapping implication and a 2-cycle duration on the consequent.

```systemverilog
property p_cdc_handshake;
    @(posedge clk_fast) s_handshake_pf |=> ready[*2];
endproperty

assert property (p_cdc_handshake);
```

**Step 5 (Textual):** **Evaluation Logic:**
- $t_0$: `req` goes high on `clk_fast` → path begins.
- $t_1$: switch to `clk_slow` context.
- $t_2$ to $t_5$: scan for first `ack` on `clk_slow`.
- $t_5$: `ack` observed → path fragment endpoint `$`.
- $t_6$ (next `clk_fast` edge after $t_5$): `ready` must be high (non-overlapping `|=>`).
- $t_6$ and $t_7$: `ready` must remain high (the `[*2]` repetition).
- All 2 cycles of `ready` are sampled on `clk_fast` (the property's clock).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When writing CDC assertions, **always declare the property's clock as the destination (slower) domain's clock if you want the consequent sampled on the slower domain**, and use `$` to make the path fragment's intent explicit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Path Fragment Lifecycle

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a path fragment flows through a multi-clock SVA property — from sequence body evaluation to endpoint synchronization to consequent evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ANTECEDENT PATH FRAGMENT BEGINS"]:::start
    B["CLOCK DOMAIN A SAMPLING @posedge clk_a"]:::clkA
    C["BOOLEAN EXPRESSION e1 EVALUATED ON clk_a"]:::evalA
    D["DELAY ADVANCEMENT ##1 OR ##n"]:::delay
    E["CLOCK DOMAIN B SWITCH @posedge clk_b"]:::clkB
    F["BOOLEAN EXPRESSION e2 EVALUATED ON clk_b"]:::evalB
    G["ENDPOINT MARKER $ REACHED"]:::endpt
    H["SYNCHRONIZATION HANDSHAKE TO PROPERTY CLOCK"]:::sync
    I["PROPERTY CLOCK SAMPLES ENDPOINT"]:::propclk
    J["CONSEQUENT EVALUATION BEGINS"]:::cons
    K["OVERLAPPING OR NON OVERLAPPING IMPLICATION"]:::impl
    L["ASSERTION PASS OR FAIL REPORTED"]:::result

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> L

    classDef start fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef clkA fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    classDef evalA fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    classDef delay fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#4472c4,color:#000000
    classDef clkB fill:#70ad47,stroke:#70ad47,color:#ffffff
    classDef evalB fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#70ad47,color:#000000
    classDef endpt fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000,stroke-width:3px
    classDef sync fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#c55a11,color:#ffffff
    classDef propclk fill:#7030a0,stroke:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    classDef cons fill:#c00000,stroke:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    classDef impl fill:#e06666,stroke:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    classDef result fill:#385723,stroke:#385723,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Path Fragment vs Single-Clock Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Test as TESTBENCH
    participant Prop as PROPERTY CONTEXT
    participant PFSeq as PATH FRAGMENT SEQ
    participant ClkA as CLOCK DOMAIN A
    participant ClkB as CLOCK DOMAIN B

    Test->>Prop: assert property trigger
    Prop->>PFSeq: evaluate antecedent
    PFSeq->>ClkA: sample at posedge clk_a
    ClkA-->>PFSeq: e1 = TRUE
    PFSeq->>PFSeq: ##1 delay
    PFSeq->>ClkB: sample at posedge clk_b
    ClkB-->>PFSeq: e2 = TRUE
    PFSeq->>PFSeq: mark endpoint $
    PFSeq-->>Prop: path fragment complete
    Prop->>Prop: switch to property clock clk_p
    Prop->>ClkA: sample consequent on clk_p
    ClkA-->>Prop: consequent expression result
    Prop-->>Test: PASS or FAIL
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Single-Clock vs Multi-Clock vs Path Fragment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC["SINGLE CLOCK SEQUENCE"]
        SC1["e1 on clk"] --> SC2["e2 on clk"] --> SC3["e3 on clk"]
    end

    subgraph MC["MULTI CLOCK SEQUENCE NO ENDPOINT"]
        MC1["e1 on clk_a"] --> MC2["e2 on clk_b"] --> MC3["e3 on clk_c"]
    end

    subgraph PF["PATH FRAGMENT WITH ENDPOINT"]
        PF1["e1 on clk_a"] --> PF2["e2 on clk_b"] --> PF3["e3 on clk_c"] --> PF4["ENDPOINT $ HANDOFF TO PROPERTY CLOCK"]
    end

    SC -.->|one clock governs all| SCnote["NO CDC SUPPORT"]
    MC -.->|implicit clock risk| MCnote["AMBIGUOUS CDC"]
    PF -.->|explicit hand-off| PFnote["CLEAN CDC VERIFICATION"]

    classDef scStyle fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000000
    classDef mcStyle fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#ed7d31,color:#000000
    classDef pfStyle fill:#e2efda,stroke:#70ad47,color:#000000

    class SC scStyle
    class MC mcStyle
    class PF pfStyle
```

## 4.4 State Diagram: Path Fragment Endpoint Resolution

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle: Property declared
    Idle --> ClockA: Ant.begins on clk_a
    ClockA --> EvalA: Sample e1
    EvalA --> Delay1: e1 holds
    Delay1 --> ClockB: ##1 transition
    ClockB --> EvalB: Sample e2
    EvalB --> Endpoint: e2 holds
    Endpoint --> SyncToProp: $ marker reached
    SyncToProp --> PropClock: Hand-off to clk_p
    PropClock --> Consequent: Sample on clk_p
    Consequent --> Pass: Consequent holds
    Consequent --> Fail: Consequent fails
    Consequent --> Vacuous: Ant.did not fire
    Pass --> [*]
    Fail --> [*]
    Vacuous --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a path fragment in SystemVerilog Assertions. Mention the role of the `$` symbol.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **path fragment** in SVA is a sequence whose endpoint is explicitly marked using the `$` symbol, allowing the endpoint to be synchronized by a different clocking event from the one that advanced the path through the sequence body. **(1 Mark)**

The `$` symbol acts as the **endpoint marker** or **thread termination point** within a sequence. **(1 Mark)**

It does not advance simulation time but serves as a **synchronization handle** for the calling property or assertion, enabling **multi-clock domain** verification in a clean, race-free manner. **(1 Mark)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why are path fragments necessary in multi-clock domain verification? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Path fragments are necessary because modern SoCs contain **multiple asynchronous clock domains**, and a single clocking event cannot accurately model signal transitions across such domains. **(1 Mark)**

They decouple the **path's progression clock** from the **endpoint's sampling clock**, preventing race conditions, false positives, and missed bugs in CDC verification. **(1 Mark)**

**Example:** In a CDC handshake assertion between a fast `clk_fast` and a slow `clk_slow` domain, the `req` signal is sampled on `clk_fast`, the `ack` on `clk_slow`, and the consequent is checked on the property's clock — all expressed cleanly using a path fragment with `$`. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the concept of path fragments in SystemVerilog Assertions with a suitable code example. How does the `$` endpoint marker differ from a regular `##1` delay operator?**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Concept of Path Fragments (3 Marks):**

A path fragment is a multi-clock SVA sequence construction that uses the `$` symbol to mark an explicit endpoint. This endpoint may be synchronized by a clocking event different from the one used to advance the path through the sequence. It is the **IEEE 1800-2017 standardized mechanism** for expressing temporal behavior across multiple clock domains without race conditions.

**2. Code Example (2 Marks):**

```systemverilog
default clocking cb_fast @posedge clk_fast; endclocking

sequence s_cdc_handshake_pf;
    req ##1 @(posedge clk_slow) ack $;
endsequence

property p_cdc_check;
    s_cdc_handshake_pf |-> ready;
endproperty

assert property (p_cdc_check);
```

Here, the antecedent begins on `clk_fast` (inherited via `default clocking`), crosses to `clk_slow` to sample `ack`, and the `$` endpoint hands synchronization back to the property's clock (`clk_fast`) for evaluating `ready`.

**3. Difference between `$` and `##1` (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | `$` Endpoint Marker | `##1` Delay Operator |
|---|---|---|
| **Time advance** | Does **not** advance simulation time | Advances time by **1 clock cycle** |
| **Purpose** | Marks **synchronization point** for hand-off | Specifies a **temporal delay** |
| **Clock context** | Uses the **property's context clock** at the endpoint | Uses the **current sequence clock** |
| **Multi-clock role** | **Essential** for path fragment hand-off | Works in single-clock context typically |

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Design an SVA property to verify that a 2-stage handshake between a fast clock domain (`clk_fast`) and a slow clock domain (`clk_slow`) completes within 8 cycles of `clk_slow`. The `ready` signal must assert on `clk_fast` exactly one cycle after the handshake completes. Use path fragments.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Sequence Declaration with Path Fragment (3 Marks):**

```systemverilog
default clocking cb @posedge clk_fast; endclocking

sequence s_handshake_2stage_pf;
    req_fast ##0 [->1] @(posedge clk_slow) ack_slow $;
endsequence
```

**Explanation:** The `req_fast` is sampled on `clk_fast` (inherited from `default clocking`). The `[->1]` goto operator moves to the **first** occurrence of `ack_slow` on `clk_slow`. The `$` marks the multi-clock path fragment endpoint.

**2. Property with Bounded Delay (2 Marks):**

```systemverilog
property p_handshake_within_8;
    @(posedge clk_slow) 
    s_handshake_2stage_pf intersect (req_fast [*1:$]) ##0 $;
endproperty
```

**3. Assertion with Non-Overlapping Implication (2 Marks):**

```systemverilog
property p_cdc_full;
    s_handshake_2stage_pf |=> ready_fast;
endproperty

assert property (p_cdc_full)
    else $error("CDC handshake completion or ready assertion failed");
```

**Explanation of Timing:**
- $t_0$: `req_fast` asserted on `clk_fast` → path begins.
- $t_x$: Hand-off to `clk_slow` → scan for `ack_slow`.
- $t_{\text{end}}$: `ack_slow` observed → path fragment endpoint `$`.
- $t_{\text{end}} + 1 \cdot T_{clk\_fast}$: `|=>` waits one full cycle of `clk_fast`, then samples `ready_fast`.
- The 8-cycle bound on `clk_slow` is enforced via the `[*1:$]` bounded repetition in the antecedent's `intersect` clause.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Compare and contrast single-clock sequences, multi-clock sequences, and path fragments in SVA. Provide a tabular comparison covering at least 5 dimensions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Tabular Comparison (5 Marks):**

| Dimension | Single-Clock Sequence | Multi-Clock Sequence (no `$`) | Path Fragment (`$` endpoint) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clock domains** | One (inherited) | Multiple (explicit) | Multiple (explicit + inherited) |
| **Endpoint sampling** | Same clock throughout | Ambiguous / first clock wins | Explicitly handed to property clock |
| **CDC support** | **None** | Risky / error-prone | **Robust and race-free** |
| **Syntactic marker** | None required | None required | `$` symbol mandatory at endpoint |
| **Use case** | Single-domain RTL | Quick CDC checks | **Industrial-grade CDC verification** |
| **Formal semantics** | Well-defined | LRM-ambiguous | **IEEE 1800-2017 §16.10** |
| **Tool support** | Universal | Universal | All major simulators (VCS, Questa, Xcelium) |

**Verdict (2 Marks):** Path fragments are the **recommended and standardized** approach for multi-clock verification. They eliminate ambiguity and ensure correct behavior across all compliant SVA engines.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Consider a design with three clock domains: `clk_a` (200 MHz), `clk_b` (100 MHz), and `clk_c` (50 MHz). A `start` signal on `clk_a` triggers a `mid_proc` on `clk_b`, which then triggers `complete` on `clk_c`. After `complete`, a `final_ack` must assert on `clk_a` within 3 cycles. Write the SVA assertion using path fragments.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Sequence Declaration (3 Marks):**

```systemverilog
default clocking cb_main @posedge clk_a; endclocking

sequence s_three_domain_pf;
    start ##1 @(posedge clk_b) mid_proc 
           ##1 @(posedge clk_c) complete 
           ##0 $;
endsequence
```

**2. Property Declaration (2 Marks):**

```systemverilog
property p_three_domain_cdc;
    s_three_domain_pf |-> ##[1:3] final_ack;
endproperty
```

**3. Assertion and Reporting (2 Marks):**

```systemverilog
assert property (p_three_domain_cdc)
    else $error("Three-domain CDC: complete->final_ack timing violation");
```

**Detailed Timing Analysis:**

- $t_0$: `start` asserted on `clk_a` (200 MHz, $T = 5$ ns) → path begins.
- $t_0 + 1 \cdot T_{clk\_a} = 5$ ns: Switch to `clk_b` (100 MHz, $T = 10$ ns) → sample `mid_proc`.
- $t_0 + 5 + 10 = 15$ ns: Switch to `clk_c` (50 MHz, $T = 20$ ns) → sample `complete`.
- Path fragment endpoint `$` reached at approximately $t_0 + 35$ ns.
- The `##[1:3]` delay then bounds `final_ack` to be asserted on `clk_a` within 1 to 3 cycles (5 ns to 15 ns) after the path fragment completes.
- The total verification covers a **3-clock-domain handshake** with precise timing guarantees.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the `$` endpoint marker → marks awarded: 0/3 for the multi-clock handling portion.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Using the wrong implication operator (`|->` vs `|=>`) → can cause off-by-one timing errors costing 2-3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Declaring the property's clock on the wrong domain (e.g., using `clk_slow` as property clock when consequent should be on `clk_fast`) → semantic error costing up to 4 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Omitting `default disable iff` for asynchronous reset → causes false failures during reset, losing 1-2 marks in the testbench-integrity question.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not specifying `default clocking` when sequences rely on inherited clocks → ambiguous behavior, often penalized 2 marks.
> - **Always write the model answer in three parts:** (1) Formal definition, (2) Code with comments, (3) Timing analysis — this structure alone often earns 1-2 grace marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Path Fragments:**

- ✅ A **path fragment** is a multi-clock SVA sequence with an explicit `$` endpoint marker.
- ✅ The **`$` symbol** does **not** advance simulation time — it is a **synchronization handle**.
- ✅ Path fragments enable **clean, race-free CDC verification** across multiple clock domains.
- ✅ The **endpoint clock** is the property's context clock (or `default clocking`), not the last sub-segment's clock.
- ✅ The **overlapping implication** `|->` makes the consequent start at the same cycle as the `$` endpoint.
- ✅ The **non-overlapping implication** `|=>` makes the consequent start one full cycle after the `$` endpoint.
- ✅ Always use `default clocking` and `default disable iff` declarations for consistency and reset safety.
- ✅ The **IEEE 1800-2017 LRM §16.10** is the authoritative reference — no need to memorize exact section numbers, but know the standard exists.
- ✅ Path fragments are essential for **AXI, AHB, PCIe, USB, DDR** protocol assertions in modern SoCs.
- ✅ Tools supporting path fragments: Synopsys VCS, Cadence Xcelium, Siemens Questa, Aldec Riviera-PRO.
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** wrong implication operator, missing `$`, wrong property clock, no `default disable iff`.
- ✅ **High-yield code pattern:** `seq_with_$ |-> consequent` and `seq_with_$ |=> consequent`.
- ✅ Path fragments work seamlessly with `first_match`, `throughout`, `within`, and `intersect` operators.
- ✅ For **formal verification**, path fragments enable liveness and safety proofs across multi-clock property chains.
- ✅ Remember the **three-step explanation framework**: (1) Definition, (2) Code, (3) Timing — this is how KTU examiners expect you to answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximal and Initial Path Fragment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximal and Initial Path Fragment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal framework of **Model Checking**, the behavior of a concurrent or reactive system is mathematically captured as a **Kripke Structure**, and its executions are represented as **paths**. Before defining maximal and initial path fragments, we must anchor three foundational terms:

- **Kripke Structure** $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$: A tuple where
  - $S$ is a finite set of **states**,
  - $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of **initial states**,
  - $R \subseteq S \times S$ is the **total transition relation** (every state has at least one successor),
  - $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is the **labeling function** mapping each state to a set of atomic propositions true in it.

- **Path** $\pi$: A sequence of states $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ such that for every $i \geq 0$, $(s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$.

- **Path Fragment**: A finite or infinite sequence of states $s_i, s_{i+1}, \ldots, s_j$ that satisfies the transition relation pairwise.

### 1.1.1 Maximal Path Fragment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Maximal Path Fragment):**
> A path fragment $\rho = s_i, s_{i+1}, \ldots, s_j$ is called a **Maximal Path Fragment** if and only if it cannot be extended any further. Formally, a path fragment is maximal if one of the following holds:
> 1. It is an **infinite** sequence (i.e., $\rho = s_i, s_{i+1}, s_{i+2}, \ldots$).
> 2. It is a **finite** sequence that **ends in a state $s_j$ with no outgoing transition** (i.e., $R(s_j) = \emptyset$). Such a state is called a **terminal state** or **deadlock state**.

> [!NOTE]
> In the strict Kripke Structure definition used in KTU 2024 textbooks, the relation $R$ is *total*, which means **every state must have at least one successor**. Under totality, no terminal state exists, and therefore *every maximal path fragment in a Kripke structure is infinite*.

### 1.1.2 Initial Path Fragment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Initial Path Fragment):**
> A path fragment $\rho = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ is called an **Initial Path Fragment** if and only if its **first state $s_0$ belongs to the set of initial states** $S_0$, i.e., $s_0 \in S_0$.

### 1.1.3 Initial Maximal Path Fragment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Initial Maximal Path Fragment):**
> A path fragment $\rho$ is called an **Initial Maximal Path Fragment** if and only if it is simultaneously:
> 1. **Initial** (i.e., $s_0 \in S_0$), AND
> 2. **Maximal** (i.e., infinite, or ending in a terminal state under non-total $R$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are navigating a **honeycomb-shaped maze** built from hexagonal rooms:

- **State** = a single hexagonal room.
- **Transition** = a doorway (or a tunnel) connecting two rooms.
- **Initial State** = the entrance door of the maze.
- **Path Fragment** = the trail of breadcrumbs you leave behind as you walk through rooms.

Now, apply our three definitions:

1. **Maximal Path Fragment** = a breadcrumb trail that you **cannot possibly extend** because either:
   - You have been walking forever (an **infinite** trail), or
   - You have reached a sealed room with **no doorways out** (a **dead-end** trail).

2. **Initial Path Fragment** = a breadcrumb trail that **begins exactly at the entrance door** of the maze (regardless of where it ends).

3. **Initial Maximal Path Fragment** = a trail that **both** starts at the entrance **and** is the longest possible trail you can lay down from that start. In a properly built Kripke structure (with no dead-ends), this is a trail that goes on **forever**, beginning at the entrance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this relevant for verification?** The LTL model checking algorithm checks whether *all* initial maximal paths satisfy a temporal property $\varphi$. If even **one** initial maximal path violates $\varphi$, the system **fails** the property.

---

## 1.3 Physical / Formal Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Notation in KTU |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Set of all states | Finite, non-empty |
| $S_0$ | Set of initial states | $S_0 \subseteq S$, $S_0 \neq \emptyset$ |
| $R$ | Transition relation | Total: $\forall s \in S, \exists s' : (s,s') \in R$ |
| $L$ | Labeling function | $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ |
| $AP$ | Atomic Propositions | Finite set of boolean variables |
| $\Pi$ | Set of all maximal paths | $\Pi \subseteq S^\omega$ |
| $\Pi_0$ | Set of all initial maximal paths | $\Pi_0 \subseteq \Pi$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Hierarchy of Path Concepts

The KTU 2024 syllabus builds a strict inclusion hierarchy among path-related objects. The logical flow is:

**Step 1 — Establish the underlying graph (Kripke Structure $M$).**
A Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ is a labeled directed graph. Every state has at least one outgoing transition (totality condition).

**Step 2 — Identify walks (sequences of connected states).**
A *walk* is any sequence where consecutive states are in $R$. Walks may be finite, infinite, may revisit states, and may start anywhere.

**Step 3 — Restrict to Path Fragments (valid traversals).**
A path fragment is a walk that obeys the transition relation. In KTU textbooks, *path* and *path fragment* are often used interchangeably for finite/infinite walks.

**Step 4 — Apply the Maximality Constraint.**
A maximal path fragment cannot be extended at its tail. There are exactly two terminal conditions:
- **Infiniteness:** The path has no last element.
- **Deadlock:** The path has a last element $s_j$ where $R(s_j) = \emptyset$.

**Why "Why" and "How"?**
- *Why maximality matters:* The model checker must evaluate properties over **complete** executions. A non-maximal fragment is incomplete; we cannot tell if a safety property holds on a fragment that has not been fully explored.
- *How maximality is used:* The set of *initial maximal paths* $\Pi_0$ is the **semantic domain** of LTL. The LTL semantics define satisfaction $M, \pi \models \varphi$ only for maximal paths $\pi$.

**Step 5 — Apply the Initiality Constraint.**
An initial path fragment must start in $S_0$. This anchors the verification to system *boot-up* behavior, not arbitrary mid-execution states.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Purpose in Model Checking

The interplay of these concepts enables the **satisfaction relation** of CTL* and LTL:

$$
M \models \varphi \quad \iff \quad \text{for every } \pi \in \Pi_0, \quad M, \pi \models \varphi
$$

- The quantification is over **$\Pi_0$** (initial maximal paths), **not** over all walks, not over non-maximal fragments, and not over paths starting from non-initial states.
- This is why the **initial** and **maximal** qualifiers are non-negotiable in KTU 2024 exam questions.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the high-yield formula sheet for KTU Board Exams. Memorize the symbols and definitions.**

| Concept | Formal Definition (LaTeX) | Plain Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Kripke Structure | $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ | The mathematical model of a system |
| Path | $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ such that $\forall i \geq 0 : (s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$ | A valid execution sequence |
| Path Fragment | Finite/infinite sub-walk of a path | A piece of execution |
| Maximal Path Fragment | Path fragment that is infinite OR ends in a state with $R(s) = \emptyset$ | A complete, non-extendable execution |
| Initial Path Fragment | Path fragment with $s_0 \in S_0$ | A run starting from system boot |
| Initial Maximal Path Fragment | Path fragment that is BOTH initial AND maximal | A complete run from boot — the semantic domain of LTL |
| $\pi^i$ | The $i$-th suffix of $\pi$: $\pi^i = s_i, s_{i+1}, \ldots$ | Used in temporal logic semantics |
| $Traces(M)$ | The set $\Pi_0$ of all initial maximal paths of $M$ | All possible system behaviors from start |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPING RULE:** In the table above, the symbol $\vert$ is used (not the ASCII pipe $\mid$) to avoid breaking KTU's markdown parsers in the digital answer-sheet systems.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These concepts power verification in:
- **Hardware Design (Intel/AMD/ARM):** Verifying that a CPU pipeline never deadlocks and that all initial execution traces satisfy timing constraints.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Certifying that all initial flight-controller paths from cold-boot satisfy safety properties.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262):** Model checking AUTOSAR software against ASIL-D functional safety requirements.
- **Protocol Verification:** Checking that *every* initial maximal execution of a communication protocol (e.g., 5G handshakes) satisfies authentication guarantees.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Classifying Path Fragments in a Sample Kripke Structure

Consider the following Kripke Structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$:

- $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$
- $S_0 = \{s_0\}$
- $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_0, s_2), (s_1, s_1), (s_1, s_3), (s_2, s_2), (s_2, s_3)\}$
- $L(s_0) = \{a\}, L(s_1) = \{b\}, L(s_2) = \{b\}, L(s_3) = \{c\}$

We will classify the following path fragments:

$$
\rho_1 = s_0 \rightarrow s_1
$$
$$
\rho_2 = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow \ldots \text{ (infinite loop)}
$$
$$
\rho_3 = s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3
$$
$$
\rho_4 = s_1 \rightarrow s_3
$$

### 3.1.1 Analysis of $\rho_1 = s_0 \rightarrow s_1$

- **Is it a valid path fragment?** Check: $(s_0, s_1) \in R$? **Yes.** $\checkmark$
- **Is it initial?** First state is $s_0$, and $s_0 \in S_0$. **Yes.** $\checkmark$
- **Is it maximal?** It is finite (length 1) and ends at $s_1$. Does $R(s_1) = \emptyset$? No, since $(s_1, s_1) \in R$ and $(s_1, s_3) \in R$. So it **can be extended** (e.g., to $s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_1$).

**Conclusion:** $\rho_1$ is an **initial path fragment**, but **NOT maximal**.

### 3.1.2 Analysis of $\rho_2 = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow \ldots$

- **Is it a valid path fragment?** For every $i$, $(s_1, s_1) \in R$. **Yes.** $\checkmark$
- **Is it initial?** First state $s_0 \in S_0$. **Yes.** $\checkmark$
- **Is it maximal?** It is **infinite**. By definition, an infinite path fragment **cannot be extended further**. **Yes.** $\checkmark$

**Conclusion:** $\rho_2$ is an **Initial Maximal Path Fragment**. It belongs to $\Pi_0$.

### 3.1.3 Analysis of $\rho_3 = s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3$

- **Is it a valid path fragment?** $(s_0, s_2) \in R$ and $(s_2, s_3) \in R$. **Yes.** $\checkmark$
- **Is it initial?** $s_0 \in S_0$. **Yes.** $\checkmark$
- **Is it maximal?** Finite, ends at $s_3$. Does $R(s_3) = \emptyset$? Yes — $s_3$ does not appear as the source of any tuple in $R$. **Yes, it is a terminal state.** $\checkmark$

**Conclusion:** $\rho_3$ is an **Initial Maximal Path Fragment** (the "deadlock" type). It belongs to $\Pi_0$.

### 3.1.4 Analysis of $\rho_4 = s_1 \rightarrow s_3$

- **Is it a valid path fragment?** $(s_1, s_3) \in R$. **Yes.** $\checkmark$
- **Is it initial?** First state is $s_1$, and $s_1 \notin S_0$. **No.** $\times$
- **Is it maximal?** Finite, ends at $s_3$ (terminal state). So **Yes**, it is maximal.

**Conclusion:** $\rho_4$ is a **maximal path fragment**, but **NOT initial** (it starts from a non-initial state).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Deriving $\Pi_0$ (the Trace Set)

Given the same $M$, we derive $\Pi_0$ **exhaustively**:

**Step 1 — Identify all initial states.** $S_0 = \{s_0\}$.

**Step 2 — For $s_0$, list all outgoing transitions.**
$s_0$ transitions to $s_1$ or $s_2$.

**Step 3 — Branch from $s_1$.**
- $s_1$ can self-loop infinitely: $s_1^\omega$
- $s_1$ can go to $s_3$ (deadlock): terminates

**Step 4 — Branch from $s_2$.**
- $s_2$ can self-loop infinitely: $s_2^\omega$
- $s_2$ can go to $s_3$ (deadlock): terminates

**Step 5 — Enumerate $\Pi_0$:**

$$
\Pi_0 = \left\{
\,
\begin{aligned}
&\pi_1 = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow \ldots \\
&\pi_2 = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_3 \\
&\pi_3 = s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow \ldots \\
&\pi_4 = s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3
\end{aligned}
\right\}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification of count.** Since $s_1$ and $s_2$ each have 2 outgoing choices and $s_3$ is terminal, the cardinality is:

$$
\mid \Pi_0 \mid = 1 \times (1 + 1) \times (1 + 1) = 4
$$

This matches the enumeration above. $\checkmark$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Applying the LTL Satisfaction Relation

> [!NOTE]
> This example demonstrates the **semantic significance** of "initial maximal path fragment" in LTL model checking.

**Property:** $\varphi = \mathbf{F}\,c$ ("Eventually $c$ holds").

**Question:** Does $M \models \mathbf{F}\,c$ hold?

**Solution using $\Pi_0$:**

For each $\pi \in \Pi_0$, we check if some state in $\pi$ satisfies $c$ (i.e., is labeled with $c$).

- $\pi_1 = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow \ldots$ : No state with $c$ ever reached. $\pi_1 \not\models \mathbf{F}\,c$.
- $\pi_2 = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_3$ : $s_3$ has label $\{c\}$. $\pi_2 \models \mathbf{F}\,c$. $\checkmark$
- $\pi_3 = s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow \ldots$ : No state with $c$. $\pi_3 \not\models \mathbf{F}\,c$.
- $\pi_4 = s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3$ : $s_3$ has label $\{c\}$. $\pi_4 \models \mathbf{F}\,c$. $\checkmark$

**Result:** Since $\pi_1$ and $\pi_3$ violate $\varphi$, the universal quantification fails:

$$
M \not\models \mathbf{F}\,c
$$

This counterexample $\pi_1$ is **precisely an initial maximal path fragment** that disproves the property.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Kripke Structure with Path Classification

The following Mermaid block renders a state-transition graph corresponding to the Kripke structure from Section 3.1, with color-coded node styles to differentiate initial, terminal, and intermediate states.

```mermaid
graph TD
    s0(["s0 (initial, label a)"]):::initNode
    s1(["s1 (label b)"]):::loopNode
    s2(["s2 (label b)"]):::loopNode
    s3(["s3 (label c, terminal)"]):::termNode

    s0 --> s1
    s0 --> s2
    s1 --> s1
    s1 --> s3
    s2 --> s2
    s2 --> s3

    classDef initNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef loopNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef termNode fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

**Diagram Legend:**

| Node Color | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Gold | Initial State | Belongs to $S_0$ — start of any initial path |
| Blue | Intermediate State | Self-loops allowed; part of infinite maximal paths |
| Red | Terminal State | $R(s) = \emptyset$; maximal only because no successor exists |

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Path Concepts

This block diagram shows the logical inclusion relationships between the different path categories in a Kripke structure.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[All Walks] --> B[Path Fragments]
    B --> C[Maximal Path Fragments]
    B --> D[Initial Path Fragments]
    C --> E[Initial AND Maximal PI0]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Used in LTL Semantics]

    style A fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#666,color:#000
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style F fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#B22222,color:#FFF
```

**Interpretation:**
- The set $E$ in the middle (gold node) represents $\Pi_0$ — the **Initial Maximal Path Fragments**. This is the *only* set on which LTL satisfaction is defined.
- Sets $C$ and $D$ are independent subsets of $B$, and their intersection gives $E$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Construction of a Maximal Path

The following diagram illustrates the algorithmic *unwinding* of a Kripke structure into maximal paths (used in tableau-based model checking).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: s0 in S0]) --> step1{Choose successor of s0}
    step1 -->|s1| step2{Choose successor of s1}
    step2 -->|s1| step3[Self-loop - extend infinitely]
    step3 --> step3
    step2 -->|s3| step4[Terminal - end of maximal path]
    step1 -->|s2| step5{Choose successor of s2}
    step5 -->|s2| step6[Self-loop - extend infinitely]
    step6 --> step6
    step5 -->|s3| step7[Terminal - end of maximal path]

    style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style step4 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#B22222,color:#FFF
    style step7 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#B22222,color:#FFF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**  
Define the following terms with respect to a Kripke Structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$:
1. Path
2. Maximal Path Fragment
3. Initial Path Fragment

**Model Answer:**

1. **Path:** A path in $M$ is a sequence of states $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ such that for every $i \geq 0$, the transition $(s_i, s_{i+1})$ belongs to $R$. A path can be finite or infinite.

2. **Maximal Path Fragment:** A path fragment that cannot be extended any further. It is either (a) an infinite sequence of states, or (b) a finite sequence ending in a state $s$ that has no outgoing transition, i.e., $R(s) = \emptyset$.

3. **Initial Path Fragment:** A path fragment whose first state belongs to the set of initial states, i.e., $\pi = s_0, s_1, \ldots$ with $s_0 \in S_0$.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]**  
Why is the concept of "Initial Maximal Path Fragment" central to the semantics of LTL? State the formal satisfaction relation.

**Model Answer:**

The semantics of Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) is defined over complete executions of the system — not over partial runs. An "initial maximal path fragment" captures exactly this notion: it starts from an initial state $s_0 \in S_0$ and is a complete (non-extendable) execution of the system.

The formal satisfaction relation is:

$$
M \models \varphi \quad \iff \quad \text{for every initial maximal path } \pi \in \Pi_0 \text{ of } M, \quad M, \pi \models \varphi
$$

This universal quantification over $\Pi_0$ ensures that the system satisfies $\varphi$ on **all** possible complete behaviors starting from system initialization. If even one initial maximal path violates $\varphi$, the model check fails.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Path" with "Path Fragment"** — a path fragment is a more general term that includes both finite and infinite sequences. Examiners deduct 2 marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Always specify the "Initial" AND "Maximal" qualifiers** when discussing LTL semantics. Writing only "maximal path" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **For state diagrams, label states with their $L(s)$ values** — this is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **In cardinality calculations**, show the branching factor explicitly. Skipping intermediate steps costs 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**  
Consider the Kripke Structure $M$ over $AP = \{p, q\}$ defined as follows:

- $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$
- $S_0 = \{s_0\}$
- $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_0, s_2), (s_1, s_2), (s_2, s_1), (s_2, s_3), (s_3, s_4), (s_4, s_3)\}$
- $L(s_0) = \emptyset, \; L(s_1) = \{p\}, \; L(s_2) = \{p, q\}, \; L(s_3) = \{q\}, \; L(s_4) = \{p\}$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Draw the Kripke Structure $M$ and identify all maximal path fragments. Classify each as initial or non-initial.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the Kripke Structure:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    s0(["s0 (init, label: empty)"]):::initNode
    s1(["s1 (label: p)"]):::loopNode
    s2(["s2 (label: p,q)"]):::hubNode
    s3(["s3 (label: q)"]):::loopNode
    s4(["s4 (label: p)"]):::loopNode

    s0 --> s1
    s0 --> s2
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s1
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s3

    classDef initNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef loopNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hubNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Step 2 — Identify all maximal path fragments:**

*Observation:* $R$ is total on every state. There is **no terminal state**. Therefore, **every maximal path fragment is infinite**.

We analyze the cycles:

- **Cycle 1: $s_1 \leftrightarrow s_2$** — there is a cycle between $s_1$ and $s_2$. After reaching $s_2$, the path may stay in $\{s_1, s_2\}$ forever, or transition to $s_3$.
- **Cycle 2: $s_3 \leftrightarrow s_4$** — a separate absorbing cycle reachable from $s_2$.

**Step 3 — Enumerate the maximal path fragments:**

A maximal path fragment in this $M$ must be infinite. The two "absorption" sinks are the cycles $\{s_1, s_2\}$ and $\{s_3, s_4\}$.

- $\pi_1 = s_0 \to (s_1 \to s_2)^\omega$ — stays in cycle 1 forever.
- $\pi_2 = s_0 \to s_1 \to (s_2 \to s_1)^\omega$ — same cycle, different starting transition.
- $\pi_3 = s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to (s_4 \to s_3)^\omega$ — moves to cycle 2.
- $\pi_4 = s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_4 \to (s_3 \to s_4)^\omega$ — moves to cycle 2.
- $\pi_5 = s_0 \to s_2 \to (s_1 \to s_2)^\omega$ — direct entry to cycle 1.
- $\pi_6 = s_0 \to s_2 \to s_1 \to (s_2 \to s_1)^\omega$ — same as above.
- $\pi_7 = s_0 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to (s_4 \to s_3)^\omega$
- $\pi_8 = s_0 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_4 \to (s_3 \to s_4)^\omega$

**Step 4 — Classify by initiality:**

Since $S_0 = \{s_0\}$ and all paths listed above start at $s_0$, **all eight maximal path fragments are initial**. Therefore:

$$
\Pi_0 = \{\pi_1, \pi_2, \pi_3, \pi_4, \pi_5, \pi_6, \pi_7, \pi_8\}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Determine whether $M \models \mathbf{G}\,(p \lor q)$ holds. Justify your answer using the concept of initial maximal path fragments.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Interpret the property.**
$\mathbf{G}\,(p \lor q)$ means "Globally, $p$ or $q$ holds at every state." Formally, a path $\pi$ satisfies this iff for all $i \geq 0$, $L(\pi_i) \supseteq \{p\} \lor L(\pi_i) \supseteq \{q\}$.

**Step 2 — Examine labels.**

- $L(s_0) = \emptyset$ — **Neither $p$ nor $q$ holds at $s_0$.**
- $L(s_1) = \{p\}$ — $p$ holds. $\checkmark$
- $L(s_2) = \{p, q\}$ — Both hold. $\checkmark$
- $L(s_3) = \{q\}$ — $q$ holds. $\checkmark$
- $L(s_4) = \{p\}$ — $p$ holds. $\checkmark$

**Step 3 — Check all initial maximal paths.**

Every path $\pi \in \Pi_0$ starts at $s_0$. At $i = 0$, $L(s_0) = \emptyset$, so $p \lor q$ is **false** at position 0.

Therefore, for **every** $\pi \in \Pi_0$, $M, \pi \not\models \mathbf{G}\,(p \lor q)$.

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

$$
M \not\models \mathbf{G}\,(p \lor q)
$$

The counterexample is the trivial initial fragment $s_0$ itself — a non-maximal fragment that nonetheless shows the property fails at the very first state. Once the path leaves $s_0$, the property is satisfied, but LTL requires it from the start (hence the importance of the *initial* qualifier).

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]**  
Answer the following with respect to the Kripke Structure framework:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Formally define a Kripke Structure, a Path, and a Maximal Path Fragment. Explain with a small example why a non-maximal path fragment cannot be used in the semantics of LTL.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Kripke Structure.**
A Kripke Structure is a 4-tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ where:
- $S$ is a finite, non-empty set of states.
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ is a non-empty set of initial states.
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ is a transition relation that is **total** (for every $s \in S$, there exists $s' \in S$ such that $(s, s') \in R$).
- $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is a labeling function mapping each state to the set of atomic propositions true in it.

**Step 2 — Path.**
A path in $M$ starting at $s_i$ is a sequence of states $\pi = s_i, s_{i+1}, s_{i+2}, \ldots$ such that for all $j \geq i$, $(s_j, s_{j+1}) \in R$.

**Step 3 — Maximal Path Fragment.**
A path fragment $\rho$ is maximal if it cannot be extended:
- $\rho$ is infinite, **OR**
- $\rho$ is finite and ends at a state $s$ such that $R(s) = \emptyset$.

**Step 4 — Why non-maximal fragments cannot be used in LTL semantics.**

**Example:** Consider a Kripke structure with two states $s_0, s_1$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$, $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_1)\}$, and $L(s_0) = \{p\}$, $L(s_1) = \{\neg p\}$.

- The fragment $\rho = s_0$ is a **non-maximal** path fragment (it can be extended to $s_0 \to s_1$).
- If we naively evaluated $\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ over $\rho$, we would say it is **not satisfied** (since $s_0$ has $p$).
- However, the *only* maximal path is $\pi = s_0 \to s_1 \to s_1 \to \ldots$, and on this path, $s_1$ satisfies $\neg p$, so $\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ is satisfied.

**Conclusion:** LTL properties are evaluated on *complete* executions. Using non-maximal fragments gives **wrong** verdicts because the fragment hides the future states where the property might be satisfied or violated.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Given the Kripke Structure $M$ with $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$, $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_2), (s_2, s_1)\}$, $L(s_0) = \{a\}$, $L(s_1) = \{b\}$, $L(s_2) = \{c\}$. Identify all initial maximal path fragments. Is $M \models \mathbf{G}\,(a \lor b \lor c)$ true?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify the structure.**

- Every state has a successor:
  - $s_0 \to s_1$
  - $s_1 \to s_2$
  - $s_2 \to s_1$
- So $R$ is total. No terminal state exists.

**Step 2 — Identify the cycle.**

- $s_1$ and $s_2$ form a cycle: $s_1 \to s_2 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to \ldots$

**Step 3 — Enumerate initial maximal path fragments.**

Every maximal path must be infinite. The only initial state is $s_0$, and from $s_0$ there is **only one transition** to $s_1$. After that, the path is forced into the $s_1 \leftrightarrow s_2$ cycle.

Therefore, there is **exactly one** initial maximal path:

$$
\pi_0 = s_0 \to s_1 \to (s_2 \to s_1)^\omega
$$

Thus $\Pi_0 = \{\pi_0\}$ and $\mid \Pi_0 \mid = 1$.

**Step 4 — Check $M \models \mathbf{G}\,(a \lor b \lor c)$.**

- $L(s_0) = \{a\}$ — $a \lor b \lor c$ is true. $\checkmark$
- $L(s_1) = \{b\}$ — $a \lor b \lor c$ is true. $\checkmark$
- $L(s_2) = \{c\}$ — $a \lor b \lor c$ is true. $\checkmark$

At every position of $\pi_0$, the disjunction $a \lor b \lor c$ holds.

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$
M \models \mathbf{G}\,(a \lor b \lor c)
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):**
> - A **Kripke Structure** is the math model: $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ with **total** $R$.
> - A **Path** is a valid state sequence; **Path Fragment** = same concept (finite or infinite).
> - **Maximal Path Fragment** = cannot be extended → either **infinite** OR **ends at terminal state**.
> - In a strictly total Kripke structure, **all maximal path fragments are infinite**.
> - **Initial Path Fragment** = first state is in $S_0$.
> - **Initial Maximal Path Fragment** = both properties above — this is $\Pi_0$.
> - LTL semantics quantify over $\Pi_0$: $M \models \varphi \iff \forall \pi \in \Pi_0 : M, \pi \models \varphi$.
> - **Trace set** $Traces(M) = \Pi_0$ — the language accepted by the Kripke structure.
> - **Suffix operator** $\pi^i$ shifts the path to start at position $i$; used heavily in LTL inductive semantics.
> - **Pitfall:** Do NOT evaluate LTL on non-maximal fragments — you will get wrong verdicts.
> - **Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse "Path Fragment" with "Computation Tree" — fragments are linear; trees branch.
> - **High-yield KTU fact:** Kripke structures used in the 2024 scheme assume **totality of $R$** unless explicitly stated otherwise, meaning maximal path fragments are **always infinite** in KTU exam questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
