<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In **Automated Verification** and **Model Checking**, a **Path** (also called a *computation*, *execution*, or *trace*) is a sequence of states traversed by a transition system that captures every possible dynamic behavior of a finite-state concurrent or reactive program.

Let $M = (S,\ S_0,\ R,\ L)$ be a **Kripke Structure** (or Labeled Transition System) modeling a hardware circuit or software module, where:

- $S$ is a finite set of **states** (memory configurations),
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of **initial states**,
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ is the **transition relation** (next-state function),
- $L: S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is a **labeling function** mapping each state to a set of atomic propositions true in that state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard Wording):**
> A **path** starting from a state $s_0 \in S$ is an infinite sequence of states
> $$\pi = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow \dots$$
> such that for every $i \geq 0$, the transition $(s_i,\ s_{i+1}) \in R$. The state $s_0$ is called the **starting state** of the path, and the sequence is **maximal** if it cannot be extended further under $R$.

The set of all such paths starting at state $s$ is conventionally denoted by $\text{Paths}(s)$, and the set of all paths in the model is $\text{Paths}(M)$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The GPS Navigation Analogy"

Imagine you are driving a car through a city using a GPS:

| Verification Concept | Real-World GPS Analogy |
|---|---|
| Kripke Structure $M$ | The complete city map (all intersections and roads) |
| State $s$ | A specific intersection/road junction |
| Transition $R$ | A drivable road between two intersections |
| Initial State $S_0$ | Your current parking location |
| Labeling $L(s)$ | Street signs / landmarks at that intersection |
| **Path $\pi$** | **One specific complete route you can take through the city — possibly infinitely long if you keep looping** |

A single path represents **one possible future** of the system. Because a system is **non-deterministic** (multiple transitions may be enabled at a state), there may be infinitely many paths starting from the same state — exactly as a single intersection may have multiple exit roads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exams:** A *path* is NOT a single state, NOT a transition, and NOT the model itself. It is the **infinite execution trace** (sequence of states) generated by repeatedly applying the transition relation. The word **"maximal"** means the path is taken as far as the model allows — usually to infinity in Kripke structures.

## 1.3 Why "Infinite" Paths?

In KTU board problems, a path is always considered **infinite** by convention because:

- Model checking temporal logics (CTL, LTL) is defined over infinite behaviors (reactive systems run forever).
- If a deadlock state has no outgoing transition, an **infinite path** is still defined by **stuttering** (looping on that state forever).
- This convention makes the mathematical semantics of $\forall$ and $\exists$ path quantifiers clean and well-defined.

## 1.4 Path Notation Summary

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \dots$ | A path is a sequence (sometimes written without arrows) |
| $\pi^i$ | The $i$-th suffix path $\pi^i = s_i\ s_{i+1}\ s_{i+2}\ \dots$ |
| $\pi[i]$ | The $i$-th state on the path, i.e., $s_i$ |
| $\pi[0]$ | The first state $s_0$ (starting state) |
| $\text{Paths}(s)$ | Set of all paths starting from state $s$ |
| $\text{Paths}(M)$ | Set of all paths in the entire model $M$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Path — Step-by-Step Construction

A path is constructed by **chaining transitions** starting from an initial state. The construction obeys these rules:

1. **Starting Rule:** Pick a state $s_0 \in S_0$ (or any state $s_0 \in S$ depending on the question).
2. **Transition Rule:** For each $i \geq 0$, the next state $s_{i+1}$ must satisfy $(s_i,\ s_{i+1}) \in R$. This means $s_{i+1}$ is a valid *successor* of $s_i$.
3. **Non-determinism Rule:** If multiple successors exist, the path may pick **any one** of them — this is what creates multiple distinct paths from a single starting state.
4. **Maximality Rule:** A path is *maximal* if either it is infinite, or it ends in a state with no successor. For Kripke structures used in CTL, all paths are **total** (i.e., always infinite), achieved through **stuttering** on terminal states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stuttering (KTU Board Favorite):**
> If a state $s$ has no outgoing transition, the path is extended by self-looping: $s \rightarrow s \rightarrow s \rightarrow \dots$. This guarantees every state participates in an **infinite** path, which is mandatory for CTL semantics.

## 2.2 Types of Paths in KTU 2024 Module 1

| Type | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Path (general)** | An infinite sequence $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \dots$ with each $(s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$ | CTL, LTL, CTL* |
| **Finite Path** | A sequence ending in a state with no successors (or a "prefix" of a maximal path) | Reachability analysis |
| **Path Prefix** | The first $k$ states $s_0 s_1 \dots s_k$ of a longer path | Bounded model checking |
| **Path Suffix** | The tail $\pi^k = s_k s_{k+1} \dots$ | Fixed-point characterizations |
| **Maximal Path** | A path that cannot be extended further; in Kripke structures, this is always infinite | CTL semantics |
| **Initial Path** | A path whose first state $s_0 \in S_0$ | Property verification |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Path Quantifiers)

In **CTL** (Computation Tree Logic), paths are quantified using two path quantifiers:

| Operator | Formal Meaning | Reads As |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ (universal) | $\forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(s)$ — **All** paths from $s$ satisfy $\varphi$ | "Along **All** paths…" |
| $E$ (existential) | $\exists \pi \in \text{Paths}(s)$ — **There exists** a path from $s$ satisfying $\varphi$ | "**There Exists** a path…" |

Combined with **state formulas**, the canonical CTL operators using paths are:

| CTL Operator | Expansion (Path Form) | Intuitive Reading |
|---|---|---|
| $A X\ \varphi$ | $\forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \pi[1] \models \varphi$ | For all paths, the next state satisfies $\varphi$ |
| $E X\ \varphi$ | $\exists \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \pi[1] \models \varphi$ | There exists a path whose next state satisfies $\varphi$ |
| $A G\ \varphi$ | $\forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \forall i \geq 0,\ \pi[i] \models \varphi$ | Globally $\varphi$ holds on all paths |
| $E G\ \varphi$ | $\exists \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \forall i \geq 0,\ \pi[i] \models \varphi$ | There is a path on which globally $\varphi$ holds |
| $A F\ \varphi$ | $\forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \exists i \geq 0,\ \pi[i] \models \varphi$ | Along all paths, $\varphi$ will eventually hold |
| $E F\ \varphi$ | $\exists \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \exists i \geq 0,\ \pi[i] \models \varphi$ | There exists a path that eventually reaches $\varphi$ |
| $A[\varphi\ U\ \psi]$ | $\forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \exists j \geq 0,\ \pi[j] \models \psi \wedge \forall 0 \leq k < j,\ \pi[k] \models \varphi$ | Along all paths, $\varphi$ holds Until $\psi$ |
| $E[\varphi\ U\ \psi]$ | $\exists \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \exists j \geq 0,\ \pi[j] \models \psi \wedge \forall 0 \leq k < j,\ \pi[k] \models \varphi$ | There is a path where $\varphi$ holds Until $\psi$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Paths

| Engineering Domain | Role of Paths |
|---|---|
| **Hardware Verification (RTL Design)** | Paths enumerate all clock-cycle state sequences of a digital circuit; used to check liveness (no deadlocks) and safety (no illegal memory states). |
| **Protocol Verification (e.g., IEEE 802.11)** | Paths represent all possible message-exchange sequences; tool **SPIN** checks Deadlock-freedom and message-ordering invariants. |
| **Embedded / Safety-Critical Software (DO-178C, ISO 26262)** | Paths in the state-space of an avionics/automotive controller are exhaustively explored to certify that critical states (e.g., "brakes applied") are reachable. |
| **Concurrency Bug Detection** | A path that violates mutual exclusion corresponds to a real **race condition** exploitable in the deployed system. |
| **Cyber-Physical Systems** | Paths model hybrid system trajectories; used to verify stability and timing constraints. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Mathematical Derivation: From Transition System to Path

We derive the **existence conditions** for a path from a state $s$ in a Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$.

### Step 1 — Define the Successor Set

For any state $s \in S$, define its **post-set** (set of immediate successors) as:

$$
\text{Post}(s) = \{\, s' \in S \mid (s, s') \in R \,\}
$$

For a path to exist starting at $s$, the condition is that $\text{Post}(s)$ must be non-empty, OR if we allow stuttering, even terminal states give rise to an infinite path.

### Step 2 — Iterative Path Construction

A path is built by repeatedly selecting a successor. Formally, define the sequence $s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ recursively:

$$
s_{i+1} \in \text{Post}(s_i) \quad \text{for each } i \geq 0
$$

Equivalently, in set-builder form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi &= (s_0,\ s_1,\ s_2,\ \dots) \quad \text{where} \\
\forall i &\geq 0:\ s_{i+1} \in \text{Post}(s_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Path Existence Theorem

> **Theorem (Path Existence):**
> For every state $s \in S$ of a finite Kripke structure $M$, there exists **at least one** path $\pi$ with $\pi[0] = s$.
>
> **Proof:**
> Consider the sequence of finite prefixes $\pi_k = s_0 s_1 \dots s_k$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ By König's lemma applied to the (finite) state space, since each prefix can always be extended (either by a real successor or by stuttering on a terminal state), the set of all prefixes forms an infinite tree of bounded branching. König's lemma guarantees the existence of an infinite path. $\blacksquare$

### Step 4 — Counting the Number of Paths (Bounded)

For a finite prefix of length $n$, the number of distinct paths starting at $s_0$ is bounded by:

$$
N_n = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} \vert \text{Post}(s_i) \vert
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows the **state-explosion problem**: even modest branching factors blow up the number of paths exponentially, motivating symbolic and BDD-based model checking.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Constructing Paths on a Kripke Structure

Consider the Kripke structure $M$ modeling a simple **traffic light controller** with the following state space (atomic proposition $\ell =$ "light is green"):

| State | Label $L(s)$ | Successors $\text{Post}(s)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $s_0$ (initial) | $\{\ell\}$ | $\{s_1, s_2\}$ |
| $s_1$ | $\{\neg \ell\}$ | $\{s_0\}$ |
| $s_2$ | $\{\neg \ell\}$ | $\{s_3\}$ |
| $s_3$ | $\{\neg \ell\}$ | $\{s_0\}$ (stutter-loop allowed) |

**Task:** Enumerate all paths $\pi$ of length **3** starting from $s_0$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path } \pi_1 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \\
\text{Path } \pi_2 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \\
\text{Path } \pi_3 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow s_0 \\
\text{Path } \pi_4 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow s_3 \quad \text{(stutter)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total count matches the formula:

$$
N_3 = \vert \text{Post}(s_0) \vert \cdot \vert \text{Post}(s_1) \vert \cdot \vert \text{Post}(s_2) \vert = 2 \times 1 \times 1 = 2
$$

> Wait — re-counting: at the third step, both branches offer only one successor each ($s_0 \rightarrow s_1$ or $s_0 \rightarrow s_2$, then $s_0$ from $s_1$ or $s_3$ from $s_2$). The product formula gives 2 distinct infinite *prefix* families, but each can be extended in many ways; the **4 explicit paths** above are just samples.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Path Properties in CTL Semantics

The following matrix classifies the **eight basic CTL operators** by their path-quantifier and state-temporal sub-formula. This is a **high-yield KTU board matrix** that examiners often reproduce.

| Path Quantifier $\backslash$ Temporal Modality | $X\varphi$ (Next) | $G\varphi$ (Globally) | $F\varphi$ (Finally) | $[\varphi\, U\, \psi]$ (Until) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ (All paths) | $AX\varphi$ | $AG\varphi$ | $AF\varphi$ | $A[\varphi\, U\, \psi]$ |
| $E$ (Exists a path) | $EX\varphi$ | $EG\varphi$ | $EF\varphi$ | $E[\varphi\, U\, \psi]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall (Repeated Examiner Warning):**
> The semantics of $EX$ and $AX$ are **not** the same. $EX\varphi$ requires only **one** successor to satisfy $\varphi$, while $AX\varphi$ requires **every** successor to satisfy $\varphi$. Similarly, $AF\varphi$ ≠ $EF\varphi$, etc. Many students lose marks by writing the wrong quantifier.

### 3.3.1 Derivation of $AF$ in terms of $\mu$-calculus (Bonus for Higher Marks)

The operator $AF\varphi$ can be derived as the **least fixed point**:

$$
AF\varphi = \mu Z.\ \varphi \vee AX Z
$$

Reading: "$AF\varphi$ is the smallest set $Z$ of states such that $\varphi$ holds in $Z$, or $\varphi$ is true now and $AX Z$ holds for the next step." This characterization is used in CTL fixed-point model checking and is a favorite 14-mark derivation question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — A Path Through a Kripke Structure

The diagram below shows a finite Kripke structure with **two concrete paths** highlighted (a green "happy path" and a red "error path"). This is a typical KTU 2024 Module 1 illustration.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0["S0: initial and idle"] -->|transition a| S1["S1: request sent"]
    S0 -->|transition b| S2["S2: error branch"]
    S1 -->|transition c| S3["S3: grant given"]
    S1 -->|transition d| S2
    S3 -->|transition e| S0
    S2 -->|transition f| S2

    S0 -. "Path alpha: S0 to S1 to S3 to S0 to ..." .- S1
    S1 -. "alpha continues..." .- S3
    S3 -. "alpha continues..." .- S0

    S0 -. "Path beta: S0 to S2 to S2 to S2 to ..." .- S2
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Path $\alpha$ (happy)** traverses $S0 \rightarrow S1 \rightarrow S3 \rightarrow S0 \rightarrow S1 \rightarrow \dots$ — an *infinite* alternating sequence.
> - **Path $\beta$ (error)** enters $S2$ and stutters forever: $S0 \rightarrow S2 \rightarrow S2 \rightarrow S2 \rightarrow \dots$ because $S2$ is its own successor.
> - The set $\text{Paths}(S0) = \{\alpha, \beta, \dots\}$ contains infinitely many paths because at $S0$ we may take either transition $a$ or $b$.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Path-Based Verification Flow

When a KTU exam asks *"How are paths used in verification?"*, the following block diagram captures the verification pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System Model: M = Kripke Structure with States S and Transitions R"] --> B["Initial State Set: S0"]
    B --> C["Path Explorer: generates all Paths from S0"]
    C --> D["Path Quantifier Layer: A for all paths OR E for exists path"]
    D --> E["Temporal Logic Evaluator: X, G, F, U operators"]
    E --> F{"Property Phi satisfied?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["VERIFIED: Model satisfies Phi"]
    F -- No --> H["COUNTER-EXAMPLE PATH returned to user"]
    H --> I["Engineer debugs model and re-runs verification"]
```

> **Reading the block diagram:**
> 1. The model $M$ and initial states $S_0$ are inputs.
> 2. The **Path Explorer** enumerates all infinite paths starting from $S_0$.
> 3. The **Path Quantifier Layer** applies $A$ (universal) or $E$ (existential) over the path set.
> 4. The **Temporal Logic Evaluator** checks $X$, $G$, $F$, or $U$ modalities on each state along each path.
> 5. If the property $\varphi$ holds on every quantified path, the model is **verified**; otherwise a **counter-example path** is returned (this is the famous diagnostic output of tools like **NuSMV**, **SPIN**, and **UPPAAL**).

## 4.3 Decomposition Table — Path vs. Trace vs. Run

| Aspect | Path | Trace | Run |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formal name in KTU syllabus | **Path** | Execution sequence | Behavior |
| Captures | States only | States + actions / labels | States + actions + time |
| Used in | CTL, LTL | LTS (Labeled Transition Systems) | Timed automata, real-time systems |
| Infinity | Always infinite (with stutter) | May be finite | Usually infinite |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a *path* in a Kripke structure. What is meant by a *maximal* path?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A path in a Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ starting from a state $s_0 \in S$ is an infinite sequence of states
$$\pi = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow \dots$$
such that for every $i \geq 0$, the transition $(s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$. A path is called **maximal** if it cannot be extended any further by the transition relation — in Kripke structures used in CTL, this means every maximal path is infinite, achieved by allowing stuttering on terminal states (self-loops).

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between $A X \varphi$ and $E X \varphi$ using the concept of paths.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- $AX\varphi$ means: **for every** path $\pi$ starting from $s$, the **next** state $s_1 = \pi[1]$ satisfies $\varphi$. In other words, **all** successors of $s$ satisfy $\varphi$.
- $EX\varphi$ means: **there exists** a path $\pi$ starting from $s$ such that the **next** state $s_1 = \pi[1]$ satisfies $\varphi$. In other words, **at least one** successor of $s$ satisfies $\varphi$.
- The two coincide only when $\text{Post}(s)$ is a singleton; otherwise they differ.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Define a *Kripke structure* formally. With a suitable example, explain the concept of a **path** and discuss why paths are considered **infinite** in CTL semantics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the Kripke structure shown below, list **all paths of length 3** starting from $s_0$ and verify whether the CTL formula $AG(\text{req} \rightarrow AF\ \text{grant})$ holds. **(7 marks)**

States and transitions:
- $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$
- $S_0 = \{s_0\}$
- $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_0), (s_0, s_2), (s_2, s_3), (s_3, s_0)\}$
- $L(s_0) = \{\text{req}\}$, $L(s_1) = \{\neg \text{req}\}$, $L(s_2) = \{\text{req}, \neg \text{grant}\}$, $L(s_3) = \{\text{grant}\}$

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Kripke Structure Definition (2 marks):** A Kripke structure over a set of atomic propositions $AP$ is a 4-tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ where $S$ is a finite set of states, $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of initial states, $R \subseteq S \times S$ is a total transition relation (every state has at least one successor), and $L: S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ labels each state with the atomic propositions true there.
2. **Path Concept (2 marks):** A path is an infinite sequence $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \dots$ such that $(s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$ for all $i \geq 0$. For example, in a traffic-light model, the sequence *red → green → yellow → red → …* is a path.
3. **Why Infinite (3 marks):** CTL evaluates properties over reactive (always-on) systems that should be checked over *all* future time. A finite path would leave temporal operators like $G$ (Globally) and $F$ (Finally) undefined on the tail. Hence, by convention, even terminal states are given a self-loop (stuttering), guaranteeing every path is **total** and **infinite**. This is essential for the well-defined semantics of path quantifiers $A$ and $E$.

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Enumerate all paths of length 3 from $s_0$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \\
\pi_2 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \\
\pi_3 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow s_0 \\
\pi_4 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow s_3 \quad \text{(stutter on terminal state if no outgoing edge; but } s_3 \to s_0 \text{ exists)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Re-evaluating: Since $R$ contains $(s_3, s_0)$, the paths of length 3 are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_1 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \\
\pi_2 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \\
\pi_3 &= s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow s_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Check the formula $AG(\text{req} \rightarrow AF\ \text{grant})$ (4 marks):**

- The formula says: **on every path, in every state, if `req` holds then along all future paths `grant` will eventually hold.**
- Trace $\pi_3 = s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow s_0$: at state $s_2$, `req` is true and `grant` is false; in the very next state $s_3$, `grant` becomes true. So $AF\ \text{grant}$ holds after $s_2$. ✓
- Trace $\pi_1 = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_0 \rightarrow s_1$: at $s_0$, `req` is true; the next occurrence of $s_0$ along the path again leads to $s_2$ eventually, which leads to $s_3$ where `grant` holds. So $AF\ \text{grant}$ is true. ✓
- All paths are checked, and in every state where `req` is true, `grant` is eventually satisfied.
- **Conclusion:** The formula **HOLDS** on this Kripke structure.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain with examples the difference between **path quantifiers** $A$ and $E$ in CTL. State and explain four basic CTL operators involving paths. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same Kripke structure as in Question A, verify whether the property $EF\ \text{grant}$ holds, and whether $AG\ EF\ \text{grant}$ holds. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Path Quantifiers $A$ and $E$ (3 marks):**
- **$A$ (All paths — universal):** The formula $A\varphi$ holds at state $s$ iff $\varphi$ holds on **every** path starting from $s$.
- **$E$ (Exists a path — existential):** The formula $E\varphi$ holds at state $s$ iff $\varphi$ holds on **at least one** path starting from $s$.
- **Example:** In a coin-toss model, "$A$ (eventually both heads)" is **false** because the path "T, T, T, …" never sees two heads; "$E$ (eventually both heads)" is **true** because the path "H, H, H, …" satisfies it.

**2. Four Basic CTL Path-Based Operators (4 marks):**

| Operator | Meaning | Path Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $AX\varphi$ | Next on all paths | For every successor $s'$ of $s$, $s' \models \varphi$ |
| $EF\varphi$ | Possibly eventually | $\exists \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \exists i \geq 0,\ \pi[i] \models \varphi$ |
| $AG\varphi$ | Invariantly | $\forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(s):\ \forall i \geq 0,\ \pi[i] \models \varphi$ |
| $E[\varphi\, U\, \psi]$ | Exists path with Until | $\exists \pi$ such that $\varphi$ holds until some state where $\psi$ holds |

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Check $EF\ \text{grant}$ (3 marks):**
- The path $\pi_3 = s_0 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow s_0$ reaches $s_3$ where `grant` is true.
- Hence $\exists$ a path on which **eventually** `grant` is true.
- **Conclusion:** $EF\ \text{grant}$ **HOLDS** at $s_0$.

**Step 2 — Check $AG\ EF\ \text{grant}$ (4 marks):**
- This means: along every path, in every state, there exists a future path that eventually reaches `grant`.
- From $s_1$, the only outgoing transition is to $s_0$; from $s_0$ we can reach $s_2$ then $s_3$ (where `grant` holds). So $EF\ \text{grant}$ holds from $s_1$ too.
- From $s_2$, we reach $s_3$ directly. $EF\ \text{grant}$ holds.
- From $s_3$, $s_3 \to s_0 \to s_2 \to s_3$ — `grant` reappears. $EF\ \text{grant}$ holds.
- From $s_0$, $EF\ \text{grant}$ holds (shown above).
- Since $EF\ \text{grant}$ holds at **every state of every path**, $AG\ EF\ \text{grant}$ is **TRUE** on this model.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse $A$ and $E$:** $AG\varphi$ means "globally on ALL paths" — not just on the chosen one. Many students erroneously treat $AG$ as a path-local operator and lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Path vs. State formulas:** A path formula ($\varphi\, U\, \psi$, $X\varphi$, $G\varphi$, $F\varphi$) **must** be preceded by a path quantifier ($A$ or $E$). Writing $G\varphi$ alone in a CTL expression is grammatically invalid.
> 3. **Stuttering states:** If you forget to make terminal states self-loop, your path count will be off, and your property check may be marked wrong. Always **explicitly draw the stutter loop** in your Kripke diagram.
> 4. **Counter-example path:** When a property fails, the model checker returns a *single* path as the counter-example. Students often answer with a state instead of a path — deduct 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Path (Module 1, Automated Verification)**

- ✅ A **path** is an **infinite** sequence of states $s_0 s_1 s_2 \dots$ in a Kripke structure where every consecutive pair is a valid transition under $R$.
- ✅ A path is **maximal** if it cannot be extended; in CTL, maximal paths are infinite (stuttering is used for terminal states).
- ✅ **$\text{Paths}(s)$** = set of all paths starting from state $s$. The notation $\pi[i]$ refers to the $i$-th state; $\pi^i$ is the suffix starting at $i$.
- ✅ Two **path quantifiers** in CTL: $A$ (universal — all paths) and $E$ (existential — at least one path).
- ✅ Four **temporal path operators**: $X$ (next), $G$ (globally), $F$ (finally/eventually), $U$ (until).
- ✅ **Eight basic CTL operators** are formed by combining one path quantifier with one temporal operator.
- ✅ A **finite path** (a prefix) is used in reachability checks and bounded model checking; CTL requires infinite paths.
- ✅ The number of distinct paths of length $n$ from $s_0$ is bounded by $N_n = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1} \vert \text{Post}(s_i) \vert$ — illustrating the **state-explosion problem**.
- ✅ A **counter-example** to a failed CTL formula is always a **path**, not a state.
- ✅ **Tools that use paths explicitly:** SPIN (LTL), NuSMV (CTL), UPPAAL (timed automata paths), CBMC (bounded paths).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traces - Trace and Trace Fragment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Traces - Trace and Trace Fragment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Trace

In the formal framework of **automated verification**, all behavioural analysis of concurrent and reactive systems is grounded in the concept of a **transition system** (also called a **Kripke Structure**), formally defined as the tuple:

$$TS = (S,\ S_0,\ R,\ L)$$

where:
- $S$ — a finite (or countably infinite) set of **states**.
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ — the set of **initial states**.
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ — the **transition relation** (a binary relation over states).
- $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ — the **labelling function** that maps each state to the set of atomic propositions $AP$ that hold in it.

Against this backdrop, a **trace** (also known as a **path** or **execution sequence**) of a transition system $TS$ is formally defined as:

> A **trace** $\pi$ of $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ is a sequence of states
> $$\pi = s_0\, s_1\, s_2\, \ldots$$
> such that:
> 1. $s_0 \in S_0$ (the trace starts from an initial state), and
> 2. $\forall\, i \geq 0 : (s_i,\, s_{i+1}) \in R$ (every consecutive pair of states is a valid transition).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Traces form the semantic backbone of *Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)* and are the objects over which *Computation Tree Logic (CTL)* path quantifiers ($A$ — "for all paths" and $E$ — "there exists a path") range. Every verification query ultimately resolves to a quantification over traces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A "Movie Reel" of the System

Imagine a transition system as the **complete blueprint of a city**, with intersections representing **states** and one-way roads representing the **transitions** $R$. A **trace** is then analogous to a **single car journey** through that city, recorded on a black-box dash-cam:

- The **first frame** of the reel must be filmed at one of the **city entry points** (the initial state $s_0 \in S_0$).
- Every **subsequent frame** must be filmed at a location reachable by following a single valid road from the previous frame.
- The car may never "teleport"; the dash-cam is bound to the city road network.

A **trace fragment** is then a **short clip cut from that dash-cam footage** — say, the 17 seconds from second 45 to second 62 of the recording. It is a *finite, contiguous window* of the larger journey, and is sufficient to reason about *local* temporal properties (e.g., "what happened in the first 5 steps?", "did the system visit state $q$ within 10 steps of startup?").

### 1.3 Formal Definition — Trace Fragment

> A **trace fragment** of a trace $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ is a *finite contiguous subsequence* of $\pi$, denoted as
> $$\pi[i..j] = s_i,\, s_{i+1},\, \ldots,\, s_j \qquad \text{where } 0 \leq i \leq j < \vert \pi \vert$$

where $\vert \pi \vert$ denotes the length of the trace (infinite in the most general case).

Special cases of trace fragments that recur in KTU questions:

| Fragment Type | Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Prefix** | $\pi[0..i]$ | Trace fragment from the initial state up to $s_i$. |
| **Suffix** | $\pi[i..\infty)$ | Trace fragment from $s_i$ onwards (infinite in general). |
| **Single-state fragment** | $\pi[i..i] = s_i$ | Degenerate fragment of length 1. |
| **Empty fragment** | $\pi[i..i-1]$ | Conventionally denoted $\varepsilon$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximal Trace** — A trace is called **maximal** if it is *not* a strict prefix of any other trace. Concretely, a maximal trace is either:
> 1. An **infinite** trace, or
> 2. A **finite** trace $s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_n$ such that $\nexists\, s_{n+1} \in S$ with $(s_n, s_{n+1}) \in R$ (i.e., it terminates in a state with no outgoing transition).
>
> Maximal traces are the *only* traces over which temporal formulas are *canonically* evaluated in CTL*/LTL semantics.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Notation

- **Length of a trace** $\pi = s_0 \ldots s_n$ is $n+1$ (number of states).
- **Length of a trace fragment** $\pi[i..j]$ is $j - i + 1$.
- **State at position $i$**: written $\pi[i]$ or $\pi(i)$.
- **Number of outgoing transitions from $s$**: written $\mathsf{Post}(s) = \{ s' \in S \mid (s, s') \in R \}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition — Anatomy of a Trace

A trace is not merely "a sequence"; it is the **primitive semantic object** against which every linear-time property is checked. Let us decompose the operational logic step-by-step:

- **Step 1 — Anchoring at Initiality:** The very first state $s_0$ of any trace must belong to $S_0$. Traces that do *not* start from an initial state are called **non-initial** or **suffixes**; they are *not* first-class citizens in the model-checking semantics.
- **Step 2 — Local Conformance to $R$:** At every step $i$, the system "decides" which successor $s_{i+1}$ to visit, but it is constrained to choose only from $\mathsf{Post}(s_i)$. This *nondeterministic branching* is precisely what makes the system *nondeterministic*.
- **Step 3 — Infinite Reachable Realisation:** If $R$ admits at least one successor from every reachable state, the trace is necessarily infinite. If at some point $\mathsf{Post}(s_i) = \emptyset$, the trace must terminate, becoming a finite maximal trace.
- **Step 4 — Projection onto $L$:** Each state $s_i$ carries a label $L(s_i) \subseteq AP$. Thus a trace induces a *labelled* sequence $L(s_0), L(s_1), L(s_2), \ldots$ over which temporal logic formulas are evaluated.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Trace-Based Semantics

The reason automated verification is built around traces (rather than, say, the state graph itself) is the **decomposition principle**:

> A complex temporal property $\phi$ is satisfied by a system $TS$ **if and only if** every maximal trace of $TS$ satisfies $\phi$ (for *universal* LTL), or *if and only if* there exists some maximal trace of $TS$ that satisfies $\phi$ (for *existential* branching).

This decomposition reduces a *global graph-theoretic* question to a *family of local sequence-theoretic* questions, each of which can be discharged by a finite-state automaton (Büchi automaton for the infinite case, finite automaton otherwise).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Statement | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transition System (Kripke Structure)** | $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ | Foundational semantic object. |
| 2 | **Trace (Infinite)** | $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ with $s_0 \in S_0$ and $\forall i \ge 0 : (s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$ | Default object of LTL/CTL* semantics. |
| 3 | **Finite Trace** | $\pi = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_n$ with $\mathsf{Post}(s_n) = \emptyset$ | Allowed when terminal states exist. |
| 4 | **Trace Fragment** | $\pi[i..j] = s_i, s_{i+1}, \ldots, s_j$ with $0 \le i \le j$ | Contiguous finite window. |
| 5 | **Length of Fragment** | $\vert \pi[i..j] \vert = j - i + 1$ | Measured in number of states. |
| 6 | **Maximal Trace** | A trace that is infinite, or finite and terminated at a state with no outgoing transition | Canonical object for satisfaction. |
| 7 | **Set of all traces** | $\mathsf{Traces}(TS) = \{\, \pi \mid \pi \text{ is a maximal trace of } TS \,\}$ | Denoted $\Pi$ in many texts. |
| 8 | **Stutter Equivalence** | $\pi \sim_s \pi'$ if they differ only by repetition of identical states | Critical for *partial-order reduction*. |
| 9 | **Prefix Set** | $\mathsf{Pre}(\pi) = \{\, \pi[0..i] \mid 0 \le i \le \vert \pi \vert \,\}$ | Used in the inductive semantics of LTL. |
| 10 | **Reachability via Traces** | $s \in \mathsf{Reach}(S_0) \iff \exists\, \pi \in \mathsf{Traces}(TS),\, \pi[i] = s$ for some $i$ | Connects graph reachability to trace existence. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In industrial hardware verification (Intel, IBM, Cadence), the same Kripke-structure trace concept underlies **Bounded Model Checking (BMC)** and **k-induction**, where trace fragments of bounded length are unrolled into SAT/SMT queries. In software verification (Java PathFinder, CBMC, model checkers for C/C++), finite trace fragments are exhaustively enumerated up to a bound $k$ to refute or confirm safety properties.

### 2.4 Engineering Relevance

- **Hardware design (RTL/Verilog/VHDL):** Every micro-architectural state machine is reduced to a Kripke structure; trace fragments of length $k$ are unrolled to check for race conditions and deadlocks.
- **Communication protocols (TCP, Bluetooth, 5G):** Protocol verification (e.g., via SPIN, NuSMV) generates *all possible interleavings* as traces.
- **Cyber-physical systems (autonomous vehicles, medical devices):** Trace fragments encode bounded execution histories used to test safety invariants like "the brake must engage within 100 ms of an obstacle detection".

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — From a Transition System to its Set of Traces

**Given:** $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ with $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$, $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_2), (s_1, s_3), (s_2, s_0), (s_3, s_3)\}$.

**Goal:** Enumerate the maximal traces of $TS$ and identify the trace fragments of length 3 that begin at the initial state.

**Step 1 — Identify the initial state.**
The set of initial states is $S_0 = \{s_0\}$. Hence every trace must begin with $s_0$.

**Step 2 — Resolve the successors of $s_0$.**
From the transition relation, we have $(s_0, s_1) \in R$ and only this. Therefore the *forced* second state of any trace is $s_1$.

**Step 3 — Resolve the successors of $s_1$ (branching point).**
From $(s_1, s_2) \in R$ and $(s_1, s_3) \in R$, the trace may branch nondeterministically into either $s_2$ or $s_3$ at the second step. Let us denote the choice as a *branch index* $b \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Step 4 — Trace the branch $b = 0$ (take $s_2$).**
After $s_1 s_2$, the only outgoing transition from $s_2$ is $(s_2, s_0) \in R$. Hence the trace must return to $s_0$ and the cycle $\{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$ repeats indefinitely.

The **first maximal trace** is therefore the infinite cycle:
$$\pi_0 = s_0\, s_1\, s_2\, s_0\, s_1\, s_2\, s_0\, s_1\, s_2\, \ldots$$

**Step 5 — Trace the branch $b = 1$ (take $s_3$).**
After $s_1 s_3$, the only outgoing transition from $s_3$ is $(s_3, s_3) \in R$, a *self-loop*. Hence the trace stays at $s_3$ forever.

The **second maximal trace** is therefore:
$$\pi_1 = s_0\, s_1\, s_3\, s_3\, s_3\, s_3\, s_3\, \ldots$$

**Step 6 — Verify maximality.**
- $\pi_0$ is infinite ⇒ maximal by definition.
- $\pi_1$ is infinite (it never terminates; $s_3$ always has a self-loop) ⇒ maximal.

**Step 7 — Enumerate trace fragments of length 3 beginning at $s_0$.**
These are the prefixes $\pi[0..2]$ for each maximal trace $\pi$:

| Maximal Trace | Fragment of length 3 |
|---|---|
| $\pi_0$ | $\pi_0[0..2] = s_0\, s_1\, s_2$ |
| $\pi_1$ | $\pi_1[0..2] = s_0\, s_1\, s_3$ |

There are **exactly two distinct trace fragments of length 3** rooted at the initial state.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Counting Trace Fragments

**Problem.** Given a transition system $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ with branching factor bounded by $b$ (i.e., $\vert \mathsf{Post}(s) \vert \le b$ for every $s \in S$), derive an *upper bound* on the number of distinct trace fragments of length $k$ that originate from a given initial state $s_0 \in S_0$.

**Derivation.**

**Step 1 — Encode the first state.**
There is exactly $1$ choice for $s_0$ (it is fixed). So the number of fragments of length $1$ is $1$.

**Step 2 — Encode the second state.**
The second state $s_1$ may be any of at most $b$ successors of $s_0$. Hence at most $b$ fragments of length $2$.

**Step 3 — Encode the third state.**
Given any choice of $s_1$, the state $s_2$ has at most $b$ successors. Hence at most $b \cdot b = b^2$ fragments of length $3$.

**Step 4 — Induct.**
By induction on the position index $i$ (where $i \ge 0$), the number of distinct trace fragments of length $i+1$ is bounded above by $b^i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{frag}}(k) &\;\le\; b^{\,k-1} \quad \text{for } k \ge 1 \\
&\text{where } b = \max_{s \in S} \vert \mathsf{Post}(s) \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total bound over all initial states.**
If $\vert S_0 \vert = m$, the *global* bound is:
$$N_{\text{frag}}^{\text{global}}(k) \;\le\; m \cdot b^{\,k-1}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check on the example.**
In Section 3.1, $b = 2$ (the maximum out-degree is at $s_1$, with two successors $s_2, s_3$). For $k = 3$, the bound gives $b^{k-1} = 2^{2} = 4$. The actual number is $2$ (since at $s_2$ the branching factor collapses to $1$). The bound is therefore *valid* but not necessarily *tight* — exactly as a bound should be.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Trace Operations

The following matrix summarises the principal trace operations referenced throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus, with their formal semantics and computational complexity class (where applicable):

| Operation | Notation | Formal Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concatenation | $\pi \cdot \pi'$ | $\pi = s_0 \ldots s_n$ and $\pi' = s_n s_{n+1}' \ldots$ with $(s_n, s'_0) \in R$ | Constructing larger traces from smaller ones. |
| Prefix | $\mathsf{pref}_i(\pi)$ | $\pi[0..i]$ | Bounded model checking. |
| Suffix | $\mathsf{suff}_i(\pi)$ | $\pi[i..\infty)$ | Stream-based property checking. |
| Projection onto $AP'$ | $\pi \downarrow_{AP'}$ | Strip all atomic propositions not in $AP' \subseteq AP$ from each $L(s_i)$ | Property-driven reduction. |
| Stutter quotient | $\pi / \sim_s$ | Merge consecutive identical labels | Partial-order / symmetry reduction. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Kripke Structure of the Worked Example

The following Mermaid block renders the transition system analysed in Section 3.1. Node identifiers are strictly alphanumeric (per the Mermaid compilation safeguard), and labels are kept raw to avoid markup conflicts.

```mermaid
graph TD
    s0["S0 INITIAL STATE s0"]
    s1["S1 s1 BRANCH POINT"]
    s2["S2 s2"]
    s3["S3 s3 SELF LOOP"]

    s0 -->|transition| s1
    s1 -->|branch b equals 0| s2
    s1 -->|branch b equals 1| s3
    s2 -->|cycle back| s0
    s3 -->|self loop| s3

    classDef init fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branch fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class s0 init
    class s1 branch
    class s2,s3 sink
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Trace Generation Pipeline

This block diagram illustrates how a transition system is systematically traversed to enumerate its traces and trace fragments, the *de-facto* operational pipeline inside any model checker (SPIN, NuSMV, UPPAAL, CBMC).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TS[("TS = S, S0, R, L")]
    Init[("Initial States S0")]
    Reach[("Reachability Engine BFS or DFS")]
    Branch[("Branching Resolver max Post s")]
    TraceBuild[("Trace Builder")]
    FragCut[("Fragment Extractor pi i to j")]
    LTL[("LTL Evaluator")]
    Verdict[("SAT or UNSAT Verdict")]

    TS --> Init
    TS --> Reach
    Init --> Reach
    Reach --> Branch
    Branch --> TraceBuild
    TraceBuild --> FragCut
    FragCut --> LTL
    LTL --> Verdict

    classDef io fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sink fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class TS,Init io
    class Reach,Branch,TraceBuild,FragCut,LTL proc
    class Verdict sink
```

**Reading guide for the architecture diagram:**

1. **$TS$** feeds both the set of initial states and the reachability engine.
2. The **Reachability Engine** computes the reachable state subgraph (BFS or DFS over $R$).
3. The **Branching Resolver** enumerates nondeterministic choices $\mathsf{Post}(s)$ at each step.
4. The **Trace Builder** stitches together state choices into a maximal trace $\pi$.
5. The **Fragment Extractor** slices $\pi$ into $\pi[i..j]$ for bounded analysis.
6. The **LTL Evaluator** checks the fragment (or full trace) against the negated property.
7. The final **Verdict** is `SAT` (a counter-example trace fragment was found) or `UNSAT` (no violating trace exists up to bound).

### 4.3 Trace Fragment Visualisation

The diagram below renders the two maximal traces $\pi_0$ and $\pi_1$ of Section 3.1, with the length-3 fragment highlighted on each.

```mermaid
graph LR
    p0a["s0"]
    p0b["s1"]
    p0c["s2"]
    p0d["s0"]
    p0e["s1"]
    p0f["s2"]
    p0g["s0"]

    p0a --> p0b --> p0c --> p0d --> p0e --> p0f --> p0g
    p0a -.fragment pi0 0 to 2.-> p0c

    classDef frag fill:#ffe066,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    class p0a,p0b,p0c frag
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define a trace of a transition system. Differentiate between a finite maximal trace and an infinite maximal trace with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **trace** of a transition system $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ is a sequence of states $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ such that $s_0 \in S_0$ and $\forall i \ge 0 : (s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$.

A trace is **maximal** if it cannot be extended further. It is **infinite** if the transition relation $R$ admits a successor from every state in the trace; the trace is denoted $s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ without termination. It is **finite maximal** if it ends at a state $s_n$ with $\mathsf{Post}(s_n) = \emptyset$, i.e., the state has no outgoing transition.

*Example (infinite):* $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_1, s_1, \ldots$ where $(s_1, s_1) \in R$ is a self-loop.
*Example (finite maximal):* $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2$ where $R$ has no outgoing transition from $s_2$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*What is a trace fragment? How is it denoted formally, and what is its length?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **trace fragment** is a finite contiguous subsequence of a trace. For a trace $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$, the trace fragment from position $i$ to position $j$ is denoted:
$$\pi[i..j] = s_i,\, s_{i+1},\, \ldots,\, s_j \quad \text{where } 0 \le i \le j$$

The **length** of the fragment is the number of states it contains:
$$\vert \pi[i..j] \vert \;=\; j - i + 1$$

Trace fragments are the principal objects analysed in **Bounded Model Checking (BMC)**, where the system is unrolled up to a bound $k$ and every trace fragment of length $k$ is translated into a propositional satisfiability problem.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice given. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted] (7 marks)**
*For the transition system $TS$ with $S = \{p, q, r, s, t\}$, $S_0 = \{p\}$, $R = \{(p,q), (p,r), (q,s), (r,t), (s,t), (t,q)\}$ and $L(p) = \{a\}$, $L(q) = \{b\}$, $L(r) = \{c\}$, $L(s) = \{b\}$, $L(t) = \{b, c\}$:*
*(i) Draw the state-transition diagram of $TS$.*
*(ii) Enumerate all maximal traces of $TS$.*
*(iii) List all trace fragments of length 3 starting from an initial state.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*(i) Diagram:* The graph has five nodes $p, q, r, s, t$ with edges as listed in $R$. Node $p$ is the unique initial state and has out-degree $2$ (to $q$ and $r$).

*(ii) Maximal traces:* From $p$, we may branch to $q$ or to $r$.
- Branch to $q$: $p \to q \to s \to t \to q$ (cycle). The unique maximal trace is $\pi_0 = p, q, s, t, q, s, t, q, s, t, \ldots$
- Branch to $r$: $p \to r \to t \to q \to s \to t \to q$ (eventually joins the same cycle). The unique maximal trace is $\pi_1 = p, r, t, q, s, t, q, s, t, \ldots$

Both $\pi_0$ and $\pi_1$ are infinite, hence both are maximal.

*(iii) Trace fragments of length 3 starting from $p$ (i.e., $\pi[0..2]$):*
- $\pi_0[0..2] = p, q, s$
- $\pi_1[0..2] = p, r, t$

There are exactly **two** distinct trace fragments of length 3 rooted at the initial state.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted] (7 marks)**
*Explain the relationship between the set of traces $\mathsf{Traces}(TS)$ of a transition system and the satisfaction of a Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) property $\phi$. Use the Büchi-automaton perspective to justify your answer.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The verification of an LTL property $\phi$ over $TS$ is conceptually a two-step process:

**Step 1 — System automaton construction.** The transition system $TS$ is treated as a (generalised) Büchi automaton $\mathcal{A}_{TS}$ whose accepting runs are precisely the maximal traces of $TS$.

**Step 2 — Property automaton construction.** The LTL formula $\phi$ is converted (via standard algorithms such as those of Vardi-Wolper or Gerth et al.) into a Büchi automaton $\mathcal{A}_{\neg\phi}$ that accepts *exactly those traces that violate* $\phi$.

**Step 3 — Product construction.** The product automaton $\mathcal{A}_{TS} \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg\phi}$ is built, and a non-emptiness check (e.g., via nested-DFS) is performed.

**Step 4 — Verdict.**
- If the language $L(\mathcal{A}_{TS} \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg\phi}) = \emptyset$, then *no* maximal trace of $TS$ violates $\phi$, so $TS \models \phi$.
- If a trace fragment is found, it is a *counter-example trace* and $TS \not\models \phi$.

Formally:
$$TS \models \phi \quad \iff \quad \forall\, \pi \in \mathsf{Traces}(TS) : \pi \models \phi$$
$$TS \not\models \phi \quad \iff \quad \exists\, \pi \in \mathsf{Traces}(TS) : \pi \not\models \phi$$

This universal quantification over traces is *the* defining feature of LTL semantics and explains why every bounded trace fragment of length $k$ must be exhaustively (or symbolically) checked in Bounded Model Checking.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted] (7 marks)**
*Define a trace fragment formally. For a transition system with $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$ and $R = \{(s_0,s_1), (s_1,s_0), (s_1,s_2), (s_2,s_3), (s_3,s_1)\}$:*
*(i) Identify all maximal traces.*
*(ii) List the trace fragments of length exactly 4 that begin at the initial state.*
*(iii) If the property is "the system visits $s_3$ within 4 steps", determine which fragments satisfy it.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

*(i) Maximal traces:* From $s_0$, the only successor is $s_1$ (forced). From $s_1$, we may branch to $s_0$ or $s_2$.
- Branch 1 (back to $s_0$): $\pi_0 = s_0, s_1, s_0, s_1, s_0, s_1, \ldots$ (infinite oscillation).
- Branch 2 (forward to $s_2$): $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to \ldots$ (infinite cycle on $\{s_1, s_2, s_3\}$).

Both traces are infinite, hence both maximal.

*(ii) Trace fragments of length 4 (i.e., $\pi[0..3]$):*
- $\pi_0[0..3] = s_0, s_1, s_0, s_1$
- $\pi_1[0..3] = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3$

*(iii) Satisfaction of "visit $s_3$ within 4 steps":*
- Fragment $s_0, s_1, s_0, s_1$ does **not** contain $s_3$ ⇒ does **not** satisfy the property.
- Fragment $s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3$ contains $s_3$ at position 3 (which is within 4 steps) ⇒ **satisfies** the property.

Hence the system **does not universally satisfy** the property (since at least one maximal trace fails it). The set of *counter-example* trace fragments of length 4 includes $s_0, s_1, s_0, s_1$.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted] (7 marks)**
*Discuss, with derivation, the upper bound on the number of distinct trace fragments of length $k$ emanating from a fixed initial state in a transition system with maximum branching factor $b$. Explain the practical implication of this bound for Bounded Model Checking.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Derivation.** As shown in Section 3.2, the number of distinct trace fragments of length $k$ rooted at a fixed initial state $s_0 \in S_0$ is bounded above by $b^{k-1}$, where $b = \max_{s \in S} \vert \mathsf{Post}(s) \vert$.

This follows by induction on the fragment length:
- $k = 1$: exactly $1$ fragment (the singleton $s_0$).
- $k \to k+1$: each fragment of length $k$ can be extended in at most $b$ ways at its final state, yielding at most $b \cdot b^{k-1} = b^k$ fragments of length $k+1$.

**Practical implication for Bounded Model Checking.** BMC unrolls the transition relation $k$ times and translates "does there exist a trace fragment of length $k$ violating $\phi$?" into a SAT/SMT problem. The bound $b^{k-1}$ is *exponential in $k$*, which is the **state-space explosion** problem in disguise. In practice, this bound is rarely tight (most fragments merge or become infeasible early), which is why modern BMC tools use:
- **SAT/SMT encoding** to symbolically represent all $b^{k-1}$ fragments in $O(k \cdot \vert S \vert)$ propositional variables.
- **k-induction** to push the bound beyond mere refutation.
- **IC3 / Property-Directed Reachability** to avoid the explicit enumeration altogether.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the initial-state condition** — A common error is to list sequences like $s_1, s_2, s_3$ as "traces" without verifying $s_1 \in S_0$. Always anchor the trace at $S_0$.
> 2. **Confusing traces with paths in CTL** — In CTL, the term "path" is used; in LTL, the term "trace" is more common. Both refer to the *same mathematical object*, but mixing up the operator semantics (A vs E) costs marks.
> 3. **Treating a finite non-maximal trace as maximal** — A trace $s_0, s_1$ is *not* maximal if $(s_1, s_2) \in R$ for some $s_2$. Always check whether the final state has outgoing transitions.
> 4. **Off-by-one in fragment length** — A fragment $\pi[i..j]$ has $j - i + 1$ states, *not* $j - i$. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 5. **Ignoring the labelling function $L$** — When an LTL property is involved, $L(s)$ determines which atomic propositions are "true" at $s$. Forgetting to project through $L$ leads to incorrect satisfaction verdicts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Trace** = sequence of states $s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ with $s_0 \in S_0$ and $(s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$ for all $i \ge 0$.
- **Finite trace** = trace that ends in a state with no outgoing transition; **infinite trace** = trace with no termination.
- **Maximal trace** = an infinite trace *or* a finite trace that cannot be extended.
- **Trace fragment** = contiguous finite subsequence $\pi[i..j]$, of length $j - i + 1$.
- **Set of all maximal traces** of $TS$ is denoted $\mathsf{Traces}(TS)$ and is the *primary object* of LTL/CTL* satisfaction.
- **Branching bound**: at most $b^{k-1}$ distinct fragments of length $k$ from a fixed initial state, where $b = \max_s \vert \mathsf{Post}(s) \vert$.
- **LTL semantics** = universal quantification: $TS \models \phi$ iff $\forall \pi \in \mathsf{Traces}(TS) : \pi \models \phi$.
- **Bounded Model Checking** = exhaustive (or symbolic) analysis of trace fragments of length $\le k$ — direct practical application.
- **Stutter equivalence** of traces is the foundation of *partial-order* and *symmetry* reduction in industrial model checkers.
- Always **anchor at $S_0$**, always **check maximality**, always **project through $L$** before evaluating any temporal formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LT Properties - LT Property
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** LT Properties - LT Property

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# LT Properties – LT Property: Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LT Property = Linear-time Temporal Logic (LTL) Property**
> An **LTL property** is a formal specification expressed in *Linear-time Temporal Logic* — a modal temporal logic introduced by **Amir Pnueli (1977)** — used to describe the *temporal ordering* of events along a single, linear execution path of a reactive (hardware/software) system.

Formally, an LTL property is a **well-formed formula (WFF)** built from:
- A finite set of **Atomic Propositions (AP)** — denoted $AP = \{p, q, r, \dots\}$
- **Propositional connectives**: $\neg, \land, \lor, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$
- **Temporal operators**: $\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{G}, \mathbf{F}, \mathbf{U}, \mathbf{R}, \mathbf{W}$

The syntax is defined by the BNF grammar:

$$
\varphi ::= \text{true} \;\vert\; \text{false} \;\vert\; p \;\vert\; \neg \varphi \;\vert\; \varphi \land \varphi \;\vert\; \mathbf{X}\,\varphi \;\vert\; \varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\varphi
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Every other LTL operator is **syntactic sugar** derived from the core operators above.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Conductor" Intuition

Imagine you are a **train conductor** with an infinite ticket-stamping rulebook:

| Logic Element | Train Analogy |
|---------------|---------------|
| Atomic Proposition $p$ | "A passenger has boarded" |
| **Next** $\mathbf{X}\,p$ | "On the *very next* station, a passenger boards" |
| **Globally** $\mathbf{G}\,p$ | "On *every single future* station, a passenger boards" |
| **Finally** $\mathbf{F}\,p$ | "On *some future* station, eventually, a passenger boards" |
| **Until** $p\,\mathbf{U}\,q$ | "Passengers keep boarding ($p$) *until* the train empties ($q$)" |
| **Release** $p\,\mathbf{R}\,q$ | "The train *stays* full ($q$) *until released by* the conductor's signal ($p$)" |

The property is a **promise** the system must keep along an *infinite timeline* — the conductor never gets to "stop reading" the rulebook.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Weightage)

- **2 marks**: Direct definition / operator meaning.
- **3 marks**: Write an LTL formula for a given English statement.
- **7–14 marks**: Classify, convert to NNF, prove equivalences, and evaluate over a given path.

## 1.4 LTL vs. CTL — Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LTL operates over a **single linear path** of execution, while CTL (Computation Tree Logic) quantifies over **branching trees** of all possible futures. KTU Module 1 focuses exclusively on LTL.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# LT Properties – LT Property: Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Syntax (BNF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi \;::=\;& \text{true} \;\vert\; \text{false} \;\vert\; p \;\vert\; \neg \varphi \\
&\vert\; \varphi \land \varphi \;\vert\; \varphi \lor \varphi \;\vert\; \varphi \rightarrow \varphi \\
&\vert\; \mathbf{X}\,\varphi \;\vert\; \mathbf{G}\,\varphi \;\vert\; \mathbf{F}\,\varphi \\
&\vert\; \varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\varphi \;\vert\; \varphi\,\mathbf{R}\,\varphi \;\vert\; \varphi\,\mathbf{W}\,\varphi
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.2 Formal Semantics — The Satisfies Relation ($\models$)

An LTL formula is interpreted over an **infinite path** $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ of states drawn from a Kripke structure $M$.

The **satisfies relation** $\pi \models \varphi$ (read as "path $\pi$ satisfies formula $\varphi$") is defined inductively:

| Operator | Formal Semantics | Plain English |
|----------|------------------|---------------|
| $\mathbf{X}\,\varphi$ | $\pi \models \mathbf{X}\,\varphi \iff \pi[1..] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ is true at the *next* state |
| $\mathbf{G}\,\varphi$ | $\forall i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ is true at *all* future states |
| $\mathbf{F}\,\varphi$ | $\exists i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ is true at *some* future state |
| $\varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\psi$ | $\exists j \ge 0 : \pi[j..] \models \psi \;\land\; \forall 0 \le i < j : \pi[i..] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds *until* $\psi$ first becomes true |
| $\varphi\,\mathbf{R}\,\psi$ | $\forall j \ge 0 : (\forall 0 \le i < j : \pi[i..] \not\models \varphi) \rightarrow \pi[j..] \models \psi$ | $\psi$ holds *until* released by $\varphi$ |
| $\varphi\,\mathbf{W}\,\psi$ | $\varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\psi \;\lor\; \mathbf{G}\,\varphi$ | Like $\mathbf{U}$, but $\psi$ may *never* occur |

Here, $\pi[i..]$ denotes the *suffix* path starting at state $s_i$.

## 2.3 The Core LTL Operator Duality Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most frequently tested theorem in KTU Module 1 — **temporal De Morgan dualities**:
> - $\neg \mathbf{X}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{X}\,\neg \varphi$
> - $\neg \mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi$
> - $\neg \mathbf{F}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{G}\,\neg \varphi$
> - $\neg (\varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\psi) \equiv \neg \varphi\,\mathbf{R}\,\neg \psi$

These four equivalences are the foundation for **Negation Normal Form (NNF)** conversion in model checking.

## 2.4 Classification of LTL Properties (KTU High-Yield)

| Class | LTL Form | Meaning | Example |
|-------|----------|---------|---------|
| **Safety** | $\mathbf{G}\,\text{``good condition''}$ | "Bad thing *never* happens" | $\mathbf{G}\,\neg(\text{req} \land \text{gnt})$ — mutual exclusion |
| **Liveness** | $\mathbf{F}\,\text{``good event''}$ | "Good thing *eventually* happens" | $\mathbf{F}\,\text{ack}$ — every request is acknowledged |
| **Strong Fairness** | $\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{F}\,p$ | "$p$ is *infinitely often* true" | $\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{F}\,\text{req\_enabled}$ |
| **Weak Fairness** | $\mathbf{F}\,\mathbf{G}\,p$ | "From some point, $p$ *always* true" | $\mathbf{F}\,\mathbf{G}\,\neg \text{reset}$ |
| **Response** | $\mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,q)$ | "Every $p$ is *followed by* $q$" | $\mathbf{G}(\text{req} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack})$ |
| **Precedence** | $\mathbf{G}(q \rightarrow (\neg p\,\mathbf{U}\,r))$ | "$p$ cannot occur *before* $r$" | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hardware verification** (Intel, AMD, ARM): SVA (SystemVerilog Assertions) is *syntactically* LTL embedded in HDLs.
- **Protocol verification**: TCP, cache coherence, bus arbitration.
- **Aerospace & automotive (DO-254, ISO 26262)**: Mandates formal proof of liveness/safety properties.
- **Compiler optimizations**: Dead-code elimination verified via $\mathbf{G}$ over control-flow paths.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equivalence | Use Case |
|---|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{F}\,\varphi \equiv \text{true}\,\mathbf{U}\,\varphi$ | Define $\mathbf{F}$ via $\mathbf{U}$ |
| 2 | $\mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \neg \mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi$ | Negate "always" to "eventually not" |
| 3 | $\mathbf{F}\,\mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \neg \mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi$ | Dual of strong fairness |
| 4 | $\varphi\,\mathbf{R}\,\psi \equiv \neg(\neg \varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\neg \psi)$ | $\mathbf{R}$ = negated $\mathbf{U}$ of negated operands |
| 5 | $\mathbf{G}\,\varphi \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,\varphi$ | "Always" implies "eventually" |
| 6 | $\mathbf{G}(\varphi \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,\psi)$ | Standard *response* property |
| 7 | $\mathbf{G}(\neg \varphi) \equiv \neg \mathbf{F}\,\varphi$ | Safety = globally negated liveness |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# LT Properties – LT Property: Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Four Temporal De Morgan Duals

We prove each equivalence formally from the inductive semantics.

### 3.1.1 Proof of $\neg \mathbf{X}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{X}\,\neg \varphi$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \models \neg \mathbf{X}\,\varphi 
&\iff \pi \not\models \mathbf{X}\,\varphi \\
&\iff \neg(\pi[1..] \models \varphi) \quad \text{[by X-semantics]}\\
&\iff \pi[1..] \models \neg \varphi \\
&\iff \pi \models \mathbf{X}\,\neg \varphi \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Proof of $\neg \mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \models \neg \mathbf{G}\,\varphi 
&\iff \neg(\forall i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \varphi) \\
&\iff \exists i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \not\models \varphi \\
&\iff \exists i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \neg \varphi \\
&\iff \pi \models \mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Proof of $\neg \mathbf{F}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{G}\,\neg \varphi$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \models \neg \mathbf{F}\,\varphi 
&\iff \neg(\exists i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \varphi) \\
&\iff \forall i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \not\models \varphi \\
&\iff \forall i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \neg \varphi \\
&\iff \pi \models \mathbf{G}\,\neg \varphi \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Proof of $\neg (\varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\psi) \equiv \neg \varphi\,\mathbf{R}\,\neg \psi$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \models \neg(\varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\psi) 
&\iff \neg \big[\exists j \ge 0 : \pi[j..] \models \psi \;\land\; \forall 0 \le i < j : \pi[i..] \models \varphi \big] \\
&\iff \forall j \ge 0 : \neg \big[\pi[j..] \models \psi \;\land\; \forall 0 \le i < j : \pi[i..] \models \varphi \big] \\
&\iff \forall j \ge 0 : \pi[j..] \not\models \psi \;\lor\; \exists 0 \le i < j : \pi[i..] \not\models \varphi \\
&\iff \forall j \ge 0 : (\forall 0 \le i < j : \pi[i..] \not\models \varphi) \rightarrow \pi[j..] \models \psi \quad \text{[contrapositive rearrangement]}\\
&\iff \pi \models \neg \varphi\,\mathbf{R}\,\neg \psi \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Property Classification of Common Specifications

| # | English Specification | LTL Property | Class |
|---|----------------------|--------------|-------|
| 1 | "The system never enters an error state." | $\mathbf{G}\,\neg \text{error}$ | Safety |
| 2 | "Every request is eventually acknowledged." | $\mathbf{G}(\text{req} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack})$ | Response / Liveness |
| 3 | "Process P gets the resource infinitely often." | $\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{F}\,\text{P\_holds}$ | Strong Fairness |
| 4 | "Once enabled, the interrupt fires immediately." | $\mathbf{G}(\text{en} \rightarrow \mathbf{X}\,\text{fire})$ | Next-step Response |
| 5 | "Variable $x$ stays non-zero until reset is asserted." | $\mathbf{G}(x \neq 0 \;\mathbf{U}\; \text{reset})$ | Until / Safety |
| 6 | "After the init signal, the bus is always ready." | $\mathbf{G}(\text{init} \rightarrow \mathbf{G}\,\text{ready})$ | Nested Safety |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Evaluating an LTL Formula on a Given Path

**Given path** $\pi$ over AP $\{p, q\}$:

$$
\pi : \{p, q\}, \;\{\neg p, q\}, \;\{\neg p, \neg q\}, \;\{\neg p, \neg q\}, \dots
$$

**Check**: Does $\pi \models \mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,q)$ hold?

| Step | State $i$ | $p$? | $q$? | $p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,q$ at $i$? | $\mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,q)$ so far? |
|------|-----------|------|------|----------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| 0 | $\{p,q\}$ | T | T | T (q holds at $i=0$, so $\mathbf{F}\,q$ is T) | T |
| 1 | $\{\neg p, q\}$ | F | T | T (vacuously, antecedent false) | T |
| 2 | $\{\neg p, \neg q\}$ | F | F | T (vacuously, antecedent false) | T |
| 3 | $\{\neg p, \neg q\}$ | F | F | T (vacuously) | T |

**Conclusion**: $\pi \models \mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,q)$ — **PROPERTY HOLDS**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# LT Properties – LT Property: Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Architecture of an LTL Property Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Model M: Kripke Structure] --> B[Compute All Infinite Paths]
    B --> C[Path 1: s0 s1 s2 ...]
    B --> D[Path 2: s0 s1 s2 ...]
    B --> E[Path N: s0 s1 s2 ...]
    C --> F[Check Path1 satisfies phi]
    D --> G[Check Path2 satisfies phi]
    E --> H[Check PathN satisfies phi]
    F --> I{All Paths satisfy phi?}
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[YES: Property Holds]
    I --> K[NO: Generate Counterexample Path]
    K --> L[Debug: Trace back to design flaw]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Syntactic Structure of an LTL Formula

```mermaid

    Root[LTL Formula phi] --> Atomic1[Atomic Proposition p]
    Root --> Op1[Operator G Globally]
    Op1 --> Inner1[Inner Formula psi]
    Inner1 --> Atomic2[Atomic Proposition q]
    Inner1 --> Op2[Operator U Until]
    Op2 --> Left[Left: p]
    Op2 --> Right[Right: r]
    style Root fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Op1 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style Op2 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff

    style Atomic2 fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style Left fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
    style Right fill:#9467bd,color:#fff
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GX[neg X phi] -- dual --> XN[X neg phi]
    GG[neg G phi] -- dual --> FN[F neg phi]
    GF[neg F phi] -- dual --> GN[G neg phi]
    UU[neg phi U psi] -- dual --> RR[neg phi R neg psi]
    style GX fill:#17becf
    style XN fill:#bcbd22

```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LTL Model Checking Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage[Input Stage]
        DES[Design RTL or FSM]
        SPEC[LTL Property phi]
    end
    subgraph BuildStage[Build Stage]
        KSTR[Kripke Structure M]
        NNF[Negation Normal Form of phi]
    end
    subgraph SolveStage[Solve Stage]
        AUT[Automata Intersection: A_M x A_neg phi]
        EMPTY[Emptiness Check: Buchi Algorithm]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage[Output Stage]

        CE[COUNTEREXAMPLE: Property Fails]
    end
    DES --> KSTR
    SPEC --> NNF
    KSTR --> AUT
    NNF --> AUT
    AUT --> EMPTY
    EMPTY --> SAT
    EMPTY --> CE
```
