<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# LT Properties – LT Property: Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define LTL property. List any four LTL temporal operators with their meaning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Linear-time Temporal Logic (LTL) property** is a formal specification expressed in LTL that describes the temporal behavior of a reactive system along an *infinite* computation path, built from atomic propositions, propositional connectives, and temporal operators.

Four LTL operators:
1. **$\mathbf{X}\,\varphi$ (Next)** — $\varphi$ is true in the *immediately following* state.
2. **$\mathbf{G}\,\varphi$ (Globally)** — $\varphi$ is true in *all* future states.
3. **$\mathbf{F}\,\varphi$ (Finally)** — $\varphi$ becomes true at *some* future state.
4. **$\varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\psi$ (Until)** — $\varphi$ holds *until* $\psi$ first becomes true.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State and prove the LTL duality $\neg \mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement**: $\neg \mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi$ for any LTL formula $\varphi$ and any infinite path $\pi$.

**Proof**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \models \neg \mathbf{G}\,\varphi 
&\iff \neg(\forall i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \varphi) \\
&\iff \exists i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \not\models \varphi \\
&\iff \exists i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \neg \varphi \\
&\iff \pi \models \mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students forget to apply the $\neg$ to the *atomic* proposition ($\neg \varphi$) and incorrectly write $\neg \mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{F}\,\varphi$. This costs the **negation step (1 mark)**.

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Classify LTL properties into Safety, Liveness, and Fairness. For each class, give the LTL syntactic form and one real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Convert the LTL property $\neg \mathbf{G}(\text{req} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack})$ into Negation Normal Form (NNF). Show every dual-application step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a) — Classification of LTL Properties (7 marks)

| Class | Syntactic Form | Real-World Example |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Safety** | $\mathbf{G}\,\psi$ where $\psi$ is a "good" condition | **Traffic light never shows red and green simultaneously**: $\mathbf{G}\,\neg(\text{red} \land \text{green})$ |
| **Liveness (Reachability)** | $\mathbf{F}\,\psi$ — "good thing eventually" | **Elevator door eventually opens after press**: $\mathbf{F}\,\text{open}$ |
| **Response** | $\mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,q)$ | **Every bus request is answered**: $\mathbf{G}(\text{req} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,\text{grant})$ |
| **Strong Fairness** | $\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{F}\,p$ | **A persistently-enabled process gets CPU infinitely often**: $\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{F}\,\text{scheduled}$ |
| **Weak Fairness** | $\mathbf{F}\,\mathbf{G}\,p$ | **After startup transient, clock is forever active**: $\mathbf{F}\,\mathbf{G}\,\text{clk\_on}$ |

**(1 mark per row × 5 classes = 5 marks, 2 marks for the table synthesis & explanation)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b) — NNF Conversion (7 marks)

Given: $\neg \mathbf{G}(\text{req} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack})$

**Step 1**: Push $\neg$ inside $\mathbf{G}$ using the duality $\neg \mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi$:

$$
\mathbf{F}\,\neg(\text{req} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack})
$$

**Step 2**: Rewrite the implication $\text{req} \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack} \equiv \neg \text{req} \lor \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack}$:

$$
\mathbf{F}\,\neg(\neg \text{req} \lor \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack})
$$

**Step 3**: Apply De Morgan to the inner negation $\neg(p \lor q) \equiv \neg p \land \neg q$:

$$
\mathbf{F}\,(\text{req} \land \neg \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack})
$$

**Step 4**: Apply the duality $\neg \mathbf{F}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{G}\,\neg \varphi$ to $\neg \mathbf{F}\,\text{ack}$:

$$
\mathbf{F}\,(\text{req} \land \mathbf{G}\,\neg \text{ack})
$$

**Final NNF**:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{F}\,(\text{req} \land \mathbf{G}\,\neg \text{ack})\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often stop at Step 2 and forget to push the $\neg$ inside the $\mathbf{F}$ operator in Step 4. This is the **most common loss of 2 marks** in NNF conversions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the formal semantics of the LTL operators $\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{G}, \mathbf{F}, \mathbf{U}$ with the satisfies relation $\models$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the infinite path $\pi$:

$$
\pi : \{p, q\}, \;\{\neg p, q\}, \;\{\neg p, \neg q\}, \;\{\neg p, \neg q\}, \dots
$$

Evaluate whether $\pi \models \mathbf{F}\,(\mathbf{G}\,p \lor q)$ holds. Show every step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a) — Formal Semantics (7 marks)

Let $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ be an infinite path, and $\pi[i..]$ the suffix from state $s_i$.

| Operator | Satisfies Relation $\pi \models \varphi$ | Plain Meaning |
|----------|---------------------------------------|----------------|
| $\mathbf{X}\,\varphi$ | $\pi \models \mathbf{X}\,\varphi \iff \pi[1..] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds at the **next** state |
| $\mathbf{G}\,\varphi$ | $\pi \models \mathbf{G}\,\varphi \iff \forall i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds at **every** future state |
| $\mathbf{F}\,\varphi$ | $\pi \models \mathbf{F}\,\varphi \iff \exists i \ge 0 : \pi[i..] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds at **some** future state |
| $\varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\psi$ | $\pi \models \varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\psi \iff \exists j \ge 0 : \pi[j..] \models \psi \;\land\; \forall 0 \le i < j : \pi[i..] \models \varphi$ | $\varphi$ holds **until** $\psi$ becomes true |

**(1.5 marks per operator × 4 = 6 marks, 1 mark for the inductive definition preamble)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b) — Path Evaluation (7 marks)

We evaluate $\pi \models \mathbf{F}\,(\mathbf{G}\,p \lor q)$.

The path:
- $s_0 = \{p, q\}$
- $s_1 = \{\neg p, q\}$
- $s_2 = \{\neg p, \neg q\}$
- $s_3, s_4, \dots = \{\neg p, \neg q\}$ (stuck in $\neg p, \neg q$)

**Step 1 — Check $i = 0$**: $s_0 = \{p, q\}$.  
- $p$ true, so $\mathbf{G}\,p$ at $\pi[0..]$? We need $p$ in *all* future states — false (since $s_1$ has $\neg p$).  
- $q$ true, so $\mathbf{G}\,p \lor q$ at $\pi[0..]$ is $F \lor T = T$.  
- $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{G}\,p \lor q)$ at $\pi[0..]$: exists $i=0$ where inner is true ⟹ **TRUE**.

**Step 2 — Verification by direct existential check**:

| $i$ | State | $\mathbf{G}\,p$ at $\pi[i..]$? | $q$ at $\pi[i..]$? | $\mathbf{G}\,p \lor q$? | $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{G}\,p \lor q)$? |
|-----|-------|-------------------------------|---------------------|-------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| 0 | $\{p,q\}$ | F (fails at $i=1$) | T | **T** | **T** ✓ |
| 1 | $\{\neg p,q\}$ | F (fails at $i=2$) | T | T | T |
| 2 | $\{\neg p,\neg q\}$ | F | F | F | T (already satisfied) |

**Step 3 — Conclusion**:

$$
\boxed{\;\pi \models \mathbf{F}\,(\mathbf{G}\,p \lor q) \quad \text{— PROPERTY SATISFIED.}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students mis-evaluate $\mathbf{G}\,p$ at $s_0$ as TRUE (because $s_0$ has $p$), forgetting that $\mathbf{G}$ must hold at *all* future states. This single-point error loses **3 marks** out of 7.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **LTL** = Linear-time Temporal Logic (Pnueli, 1977) — semantics over a **single infinite path**.
- ✅ **Five Core Temporal Operators**: $\mathbf{X}$ (Next), $\mathbf{G}$ (Globally/Always), $\mathbf{F}$ (Finally/Eventually), $\mathbf{U}$ (Until), $\mathbf{R}$ (Release).
- ✅ **Derived Operators**: $\mathbf{F}\,\varphi \equiv \text{true}\,\mathbf{U}\,\varphi$, $\mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \text{false}\,\mathbf{R}\,\varphi$.
- ✅ **Four De Morgan Duals** (MUST memorize):
  1. $\neg \mathbf{X}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{X}\,\neg \varphi$
  2. $\neg \mathbf{G}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{F}\,\neg \varphi$
  3. $\neg \mathbf{F}\,\varphi \equiv \mathbf{G}\,\neg \varphi$
  4. $\neg(\varphi\,\mathbf{U}\,\psi) \equiv \neg \varphi\,\mathbf{R}\,\neg \psi$
- ✅ **Three Property Classes**: Safety ($\mathbf{G}$), Liveness ($\mathbf{F}$), Fairness ($\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{F}$ / $\mathbf{F}\,\mathbf{G}$).
- ✅ **NNF Conversion Rule**: Push $\neg$ inward using duals; rewrite $\rightarrow$ as $\lor$ before negating.
- ✅ **LTL vs. CTL**: LTL = linear time (one path), CTL = branching time (quantified over tree).
- ✅ **Path satisfaction is existential for $\mathbf{F}$ and universal for $\mathbf{G}$** — examiners love testing this.
- ✅ **Vacuous truth**: $\text{false} \rightarrow \varphi$ is always TRUE — crucial in $\mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,q)$ when $p$ never holds.
- ✅ **Counterexample for $\mathbf{G}$**: One violating state suffices. **Witness for $\mathbf{F}$**: One satisfying state suffices.

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: LT Properties - LT Property**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Satisfaction Relation for LT Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Satisfaction Relation for LT Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Satisfaction Relation for LT Properties

In the theory of **Model Checking** (Clarke, Emerson, Sistla; standard KTU PECNT526 reference), the **Satisfaction Relation** (denoted by the turnstile symbol $\models$) is a binary relation that formally decides *whether* a model (the system under verification) fulfills a *property* expressed in a temporal logic such as **LTL (Linear Temporal Logic)**.

A property $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ over an alphabet $\Sigma$ of *atomic propositions* is called a **Linear-Time (LT) property** if, and only if, the satisfaction of a path $\pi$ in a Kripke structure $TS$ by $P$ depends exclusively on the **trace** $\text{trace}(\pi)$ produced by that path. Formally:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LT Property Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
>
> A set $P \subseteq (2^{AP})^\omega$ is an **LT property** if for every pair of paths $\pi$ and $\pi'$ in the transition system $TS$:
>
> $$\text{trace}(\pi) = \text{trace}(\pi') \;\Longrightarrow\; (\pi \models P \iff \pi' \models P)$$

The **Satisfaction Relation** is then defined as the membership test:

$$\pi \models P \;\;\iff\;\; \text{trace}(\pi) \in P$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** LT properties are *trace-monotone* in the satisfaction sense. Two paths that "look identical" from the perspective of the atomic propositions must either both satisfy the property or both violate it. This is the cornerstone of linear-time model checking.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a security guard (the *satisfaction relation*) reviewing the CCTV footage (the *trace*) of a bank's automated doors (*the model*). The guard has one rulebook: "The vault door must never open when the alarm is on" (an *LT safety property*). 

- The guard doesn't care *which specific* camera angle recorded the event — only the **sequence of facts** ("Alarm=ON, Vault=CLOSED, Alarm=ON, …").
- Two different camera recordings of the same event will receive the **same verdict**.
- If the trace (the fact-sequence) belongs to the "rule-conforming" set, the path satisfies the property. If even *one* fact in the trace breaks the rule, the property is violated.

This "trace-only" decision process is the essence of the LT satisfaction relation.

### 1.3 Formal Setting: Kripke Structure

A **Kripke Structure** (the canonical model in KTU Module 1) is the tuple:

$$TS = (S, \; S_0, \; R, \; L)$$

where:

- $S$ — a **finite** set of states (using standard KTU notation; the alphabet $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$ is implicit)
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ — the set of *initial* states
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ — the *transition* relation (required to be **total**, i.e., every state has at least one successor)
- $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ — the *labeling* function mapping each state to the set of atomic propositions true in that state

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$AP$** denotes the finite set of **Atomic Propositions** — the indivisible Boolean facts about each state (e.g., $\{$`x=1`, `lock=off`$\}$).

A **path** is an infinite sequence of states $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ such that $(s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$ for all $i \geq 0$.

The **trace** of a path is the corresponding sequence of label sets:

$$\text{trace}(\pi) = L(s_0)\,L(s_1)\,L(s_2)\,\ldots \;\; \in (2^{AP})^\omega$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Decomposition of the Satisfaction Relation

The satisfaction relation $\models$ is defined inductively on the syntax of LTL formulas, which is the standard vehicle for expressing LT properties in KTU 2024.

**LTL Syntax (Backus–Naur Form):**

$$\varphi \;::=\; \text{true} \;\mid\; a \;\mid\; \neg\varphi \;\mid\; \varphi_1 \vee \varphi_2 \;\mid\; \operatorname{X}\varphi \;\mid\; \varphi_1 \operatorname{U} \varphi_2$$

where $a \in AP$ is any atomic proposition, and $\operatorname{X}$, $\operatorname{U}$ are the **Next** and **Until** temporal operators. The derived operators are:

$$\operatorname{F}\varphi \;\equiv\; \text{true}\operatorname{U}\varphi \quad\text{(Eventually / Future)}$$

$$\operatorname{G}\varphi \;\equiv\; \neg\operatorname{F}\neg\varphi \quad\texttext{(Globally / Always)}$$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Satisfaction Rules for LTL (Path Semantics)** — directly from the Baier–Katoen *Principles of Model Checking* framework adopted in KTU 2024.

| # | Formula $\varphi$ | Satisfaction Condition $\pi \models \varphi$ | Intuition |
|---|----|------|---------|
| 1 | $\text{true}$ | Always | Trivially satisfied |
| 2 | $a \in AP$ | $a \in L(\pi[0])$ | Atomic prop. $a$ holds in the *current* state |
| 3 | $\neg\psi$ | $\pi \not\models \psi$ | Logical negation |
| 4 | $\psi_1 \vee \psi_2$ | $\pi \models \psi_1 \;\text{or}\; \pi \models \psi_2$ | Disjunction |
| 5 | $\operatorname{X}\psi$ | $\pi[1..] \models \psi$ | $\psi$ holds in the **next** step |
| 6 | $\psi_1 \operatorname{U} \psi_2$ | $\exists\, j \geq 0.\; \pi[j..] \models \psi_2 \;\text{and}\; \forall\, 0 \leq i < j.\; \pi[i..] \models \psi_1$ | $\psi_1$ holds *until* $\psi_2$ becomes true |

**State-vs-Path Satisfaction (the bridge used in KTU 2024 model-checking questions):**

| Concept | Notation | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| State satisfies atomic prop. | $s \models a$ | $a \in L(s)$ |
| State satisfies LTL formula | $s \models \varphi$ | for **all** paths $\pi$ starting at $s$: $\pi \models \varphi$ |
| Model satisfies property | $TS \models P$ | for **all** initial paths $\pi$: $\text{trace}(\pi) \in P$ |

> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall (no table breakage):** The "next-state" operator $\operatorname{X}$ has an empty semantics on a path of length zero. Always write $\pi[1..]$ (the *suffix* path starting at index 1) — never $\pi[0..]$ — to avoid the common error of confusing "current" with "next".

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The satisfaction relation is the **decision engine** of every industrial model checker (SPIN, NuSMV, CBMC, UPPAAL). Engineers use it to:

- Verify that a **hardware cache coherence protocol** always grants exclusive access ($\operatorname{G}(\text{req} \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,\text{grant})$).
- Check that an **airbag controller** never deploys when the seat is empty ($\operatorname{G}(\text{empty} \rightarrow \neg\text{deploy})$).
- Validate that a **mutex** eventually grants every request ($\operatorname{G}(\text{req} \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,\text{grant})$ — a *liveness* LT property).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every LTL formula denotes an LT property. Conversely, not every LT property is LTL-expressible; the path quantifier-free fragment of $\text{CTL}^*$ restricted to a single path equals LTL — a result the KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Trace-Based and Path-Based Satisfaction

**Claim:** For any LT property $P \subseteq (2^{AP})^\omega$ and any two paths $\pi, \pi'$ in the transition system $TS$:

$$\text{trace}(\pi) = \text{trace}(\pi') \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \big(\pi \models P \;\;\iff\;\; \pi' \models P\big)$$

**Step 1 — Define the path-satisfaction relation.**

By KTU Module 1 definition, a path $\pi$ satisfies an LT property $P$ if and only if its trace is a member of $P$:

$$\pi \models P \;\;\stackrel{\text{def}}{=}\;\; \text{trace}(\pi) \in P$$

**Step 2 — Apply the hypothesis.**

The premise states that the traces are identical:

$$\text{trace}(\pi) \;=\; \text{trace}(\pi') \;\stackrel{\text{def}}{=}\; \tau$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the satisfaction condition.**

Now we may rewrite both sides of the biconditional using $\tau$:

$$\pi \models P \;\iff\; \text{trace}(\pi) \in P \;\iff\; \tau \in P$$

and independently,

$$\pi' \models P \;\iff\; \text{trace}(\pi') \in P \;\iff\; \tau \in P$$

**Step 4 — Conclude by transitivity of equivalence.**

Since both $\pi \models P$ and $\pi' \models P$ reduce to the *same* proposition $\tau \in P$, they are equivalent:

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi \models P \;&\iff\; \tau \in P \\
\pi' \models P \;&\iff\; \tau \in P \\
\therefore\;\; \pi \models P \;&\iff\; \pi' \models P
\end{aligned}$$

$\blacksquare$

This derivation confirms the central postulate of KTU Module 1: **satisfaction of an LT property is a *function* of the trace alone**, never of the underlying state sequence.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Satisfaction of Common LT Properties on a Sample Path

Consider the path $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 s_3 \ldots$ in a transition system where the labels are:

$$L(s_0) = \{a\}, \quad L(s_1) = \{a, b\}, \quad L(s_2) = \{b\}, \quad L(s_3) = \{\}, \quad L(s_4) = \{c\}, \ldots$$

| LTL Formula $\varphi$ | Trace suffix checked | Verdict $\pi \models \varphi$? |
|---|---|---|
| $a$ | $\pi[0..] \models a \iff a \in L(s_0) = \{a\}$ | **Yes** |
| $a \wedge b$ | $a \in L(s_0)$ and $b \in L(s_0)$ | **No** (only $a$ holds) |
| $\operatorname{X}\,b$ | $\pi[1..] \models b \iff b \in L(s_1) = \{a, b\}$ | **Yes** |
| $\operatorname{F}\,c$ | $\exists j \geq 0$ s.t. $c \in L(s_j)$ — at $j=4$ | **Yes** |
| $\operatorname{G}\,b$ | $\forall j \geq 0$ s.t. $b \in L(s_j)$ — fails at $j=0$ | **No** |
| $a \operatorname{U}\,c$ | $\exists j = 4$ s.t. $c \in L(s_4)$, and $\forall 0 \leq i < 4$, $a \in L(s_i)$ — fails at $i=2, 3$ | **No** (violated at $s_2$) |
| $\operatorname{G}(a \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,b)$ | For every $i$ with $a \in L(s_i)$, some $j \geq i$ with $b \in L(s_j)$ | **Yes** ($a$ at $s_0, s_1$; $b$ at $s_1 \geq s_0$ and $b$ at $s_2 \geq s_1$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix above is the **exam-ready analytical tool** for KTU 2024 valuation. Examiners award partial credit for *identifying the correct suffix path* used in the satisfaction check, even if the final Boolean verdict is wrong.

### 3.3 Worked Example: State Satisfaction of $\operatorname{G}\,(p \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,q)$

**Question.** Given a state $s$ with $L(s) = \{p\}$, and the unique outgoing path $\pi = s\,s_1\,s_2\,\ldots$ with $L(s_1) = \{\}$, $L(s_2) = \{q\}$, $L(s_3) = \{q\}, \ldots$ Does $s \models \operatorname{G}(p \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,q)$?

**Step 1.** By the *state-satisfaction* rule, we must check the LTL property on *all* paths starting from $s$:

$$s \models \varphi \;\iff\; \forall\, \pi \in \text{Paths}(s).\; \pi \models \varphi$$

**Step 2.** For each index $i \geq 0$ of $\pi$, verify the implication $p \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,q$:

$$\begin{aligned}
i=0:&\; p \in L(s_0) = \{p\} \quad\text{and}\quad \operatorname{F}\,q \text{ holds on } \pi[0..] \;(\exists j = 2 : q \in L(s_2)) \\
i=1:&\; p \notin L(s_1) = \{\} \quad\text{implication vacuously true} \\
i=2:&\; p \notin L(s_2) = \{q\} \quad\text{implication vacuously true}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.** Since the implication holds at *every* index, $\pi \models \operatorname{G}(p \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,q)$.

**Step 4.** If this were the only path, conclude $s \models \operatorname{G}(p \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,q)$. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram: Where the Satisfaction Relation Lives

The figure below maps how the satisfaction relation $\models$ connects the model, the paths it generates, the traces those paths produce, and the LT property being verified. Read the arrows as "*informs / constrains the satisfaction verdict*".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TS["Kripke Structure TS over S S0 R L"]
    P0["Initial State s0 in S0"]
    PathPi["Infinite Path pi = s0 s1 s2 ..."]
    TrPi["Trace tau = Ls0 Ls1 Ls2 ..."]
    PropP["LT Property P subset of 2AP to omega"]
    SatsPath["Path Satisfaction pi models P"]
    SatsModel["Model Satisfaction TS models P"]
    LTL["LTL Formula phi"]
    SatsLTL["LTL Satisfaction pi models phi"]

    TS --> P0
    P0 --> PathPi
    PathPi --> TrPi
    TrPi --> SatsPath
    PropP --> SatsPath
    SatsPath --> SatsModel
    LTL --> SatsLTL
    SatsLTL -.induced by.-> PropP
    TS --> SatsModel
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow Diagram: The Satisfaction Algorithm

The figure below depicts the **recursive descent** the KTU Module 1 valuation key uses when grading a satisfaction claim. Note how each LTL operator delegates the satisfaction question to a *simpler* sub-question on a *shorter* path suffix.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start: pi models phi?"]
    Q1{"Is phi = a in AP?"}
    Q2{"Is phi = not psi?"}
    Q3{"Is phi = psi1 OR psi2?"}
    Q4{"Is phi = X psi?"}
    Q5{"Is phi = psi1 U psi2?"}
    R1["Yes iff a in L of pi 0"]
    R2["Yes iff pi NOT models psi"]
    R3["Yes iff pi models psi1 OR pi models psi2"]
    R4["Yes iff suffix pi 1.. models psi"]
    R5["Find j ge 0: pi j.. models psi2 and for all i lt j, pi i.. models psi1"]
    Done["Return Boolean verdict"]

    Start --> Q1
    Start --> Q2
    Start --> Q3
    Start --> Q4
    Start --> Q5
    Q1 -->|yes| R1 --> Done
    Q2 -->|yes| R2 --> Done
    Q3 -->|yes| R3 --> Done
    Q4 -->|yes| R4 --> Done
    Q5 -->|yes| R5 --> Done
    Q1 -->|no| Q2
    Q2 -->|no| Q3
    Q3 -->|no| Q4
    Q4 -->|no| Q5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** In the diagrams, $\operatorname{X} = $ "Next", $\operatorname{U} = $ "Until". The decision flow matches the inductive clauses of Section 2.2 exactly — this is the algorithm an examiner mentally executes while grading a long-answer script.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Set A]**
> Define an **LT property** formally. Why is it called "linear-time"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A property $P \subseteq (2^{AP})^\omega$ is a **Linear-Time (LT) property** if for any two paths $\pi, \pi'$ of a transition system $TS$:

$$\text{trace}(\pi) = \text{trace}(\pi') \;\Longrightarrow\; \pi \models P \iff \pi' \models P$$

It is called *linear-time* because the satisfaction of $P$ is evaluated along a *single*, linear (totally ordered) execution trace, as opposed to branching-time logics (like CTL) that quantify over the **tree** of all possible futures. *(1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the trace-equivalence condition, 1 mark for the "single-trace" justification.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Set B]**
> Differentiate between **path satisfaction** ($\pi \models \varphi$) and **state satisfaction** ($s \models \varphi$) in LTL.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Path Satisfaction $\pi \models \varphi$ | State Satisfaction $s \models \varphi$ |
|---|---|---|
| Object | An infinite path $\pi$ | A single state $s$ |
| Meaning | $\varphi$ holds on the trace of $\pi$ | $\varphi$ holds on **all** paths starting at $s$ |
| Quantifier | None (path quantifier-free) | Universal over $\text{Paths}(s)$ |
| Used for | LTL path formulas | Embedding LTL into state-based reasoning |

State satisfaction is defined as $s \models \varphi \iff \forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(s).\; \pi \models \varphi$. *(1 mark per row.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q. 1(a)–(b)]**
>
> **(a)** For the Kripke structure shown below, where $AP = \{a, b, c\}$ and the state labels are:
> $L(s_0) = \{a\}$, $L(s_1) = \{a, b\}$, $L(s_2) = \{b, c\}$, $L(s_3) = \{c\}$,
> and the transitions are $s_0 \to s_1$, $s_1 \to s_2$, $s_2 \to s_3$, $s_3 \to s_3$ (self-loop),
> evaluate the satisfaction of the LTL formula $\varphi = a \operatorname{U}\,c$ on the path $\pi = s_0\,s_1\,s_2\,s_3\,s_3\,s_3\,\ldots$ **(7 marks)**.
>
> **(b)** Prove that the formula $\operatorname{G}\,\varphi$ is satisfied by a path $\pi$ if and only if **every suffix** $\pi[k..]$ for $k \geq 0$ satisfies $\varphi$. State the LTL identity that allows the rewriting of $\operatorname{F}\,\varphi$ as $\neg\operatorname{G}\,\neg\varphi$ and justify it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

Apply the **Until** satisfaction rule inductively along the path:

$$\pi \models a \operatorname{U}\,c \;\;\iff\;\; \exists j \geq 0.\; \pi[j..] \models c \;\;\text{and}\;\; \forall\, 0 \leq i < j.\; \pi[i..] \models a$$

| Index $i$ | State $s_i$ | $L(s_i)$ | $a \in L(s_i)$? | $c \in L(s_i)$? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $s_0$ | $\{a\}$ | ✓ | ✗ |
| 1 | $s_1$ | $\{a, b\}$ | ✓ | ✗ |
| 2 | $s_2$ | $\{b, c\}$ | ✗ | ✓ |
| 3 | $s_3$ | $\{c\}$ | ✗ | ✓ |

Choose $j = 2$. Then $\pi[2..] \models c$ because $c \in L(s_2)$. Verify the "until" condition: for all $0 \leq i < 2$ (i.e., $i = 0, 1$), $\pi[i..] \models a$ — both true.

$$\therefore\;\; \pi \models a \operatorname{U}\,c \;\; \text{is TRUE.} \qquad \blacksquare$$

*(3 marks for setting up the rule, 2 marks for the table, 2 marks for the verdict.)*

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Claim:** $\pi \models \operatorname{G}\varphi \iff \forall k \geq 0.\; \pi[k..] \models \varphi$.

*Proof.* By definition, $\pi \models \operatorname{G}\varphi$ means "$\varphi$ holds at every position of $\pi$". The position $k$ of $\pi$ corresponds precisely to the *first* state of the suffix path $\pi[k..]$. Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
\pi \models \operatorname{G}\varphi \;&\stackrel{\text{def}}{=}\;\; \forall k \geq 0.\;\; \pi \text{ satisfies } \varphi \text{ at position } k \\
&\stackrel{\text{rewrite}}{=}\;\; \forall k \geq 0.\;\; \pi[k..] \models \varphi \qquad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**LTL identity:** $\operatorname{F}\varphi \equiv \neg\operatorname{G}\neg\varphi$ *(Dual of G)*.

*Justification:* $\pi \models \operatorname{F}\varphi$ means $\varphi$ holds *eventually* on $\pi$, i.e., $\exists k \geq 0$ with $\pi[k..] \models \varphi$. Negating "$\varphi$ always holds" ($\operatorname{G}\neg\varphi$) gives "$\varphi$ holds at some point" — exactly the meaning of $\operatorname{F}\varphi$. Formally:

$$\neg(\pi \models \operatorname{G}\neg\varphi) \;\iff\; \neg(\forall k \geq 0.\; \pi[k..] \models \neg\varphi) \;\iff\; \exists k \geq 0.\; \pi[k..] \models \varphi \;\iff\; \pi \models \operatorname{F}\varphi \qquad \blacksquare$$

*(3 marks for the G-suffix proof, 2 marks for the identity statement, 2 marks for the formal justification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Q. 2(a)–(b)]**
>
> **(a)** Define a **Kripke Structure**. For the Kripke structure with $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$, $L(s_0) = \{p\}$, $L(s_1) = \{q\}$, $L(s_2) = \{p, q\}$, and $R = \{(s_0,s_1), (s_1,s_2), (s_2,s_1)\}$, determine the set of all initial paths and verify whether $s_0 \models \operatorname{G}(p \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,q)$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain, with a formal proof, **why** the satisfaction relation $\pi \models \varphi$ for an LTL formula $\varphi$ depends only on the trace $\text{trace}(\pi)$ and not on the actual state labels themselves. Conclude with the definition of an LT property. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

A Kripke structure is the 4-tuple $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ where $S$ is a finite state set, $S_0 \subseteq S$ are initial states, $R \subseteq S \times S$ is the (total) transition relation, and $L : S \to 2^{AP}$ labels each state with the set of atomic propositions true in it. *(2 marks)*

**Initial paths (paths starting at $s_0$):** The transition $s_0 \to s_1$ is forced; from $s_1$ we go to $s_2$; from $s_2$ back to $s_1$. The path is therefore the **unique** infinite path:

$$\pi = s_0\,s_1\,s_2\,s_1\,s_2\,s_1\,s_2\,\ldots$$

with corresponding trace $\tau = \{p\}\,\{q\}\,\{p,q\}\,\{q\}\,\{p,q\}\,\ldots$

**Check $s_0 \models \operatorname{G}(p \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,q)$:** Since there is only one path, the universal quantification over $\text{Paths}(s_0)$ is trivially satisfied if $\pi$ satisfies the formula. Verify the implication position by position:

| $i$ | $L(s_i)$ | $p \in L(s_i)$? | Does $q$ hold later? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\{p\}$ | Yes | Yes, at $i = 1$ ($q \in L(s_1)$) ✓ |
| 1 | $\{q\}$ | No | Implication vacuously true ✓ |
| 2 | $\{p, q\}$ | Yes | Yes, at $i = 3$ ✓ |
| … | … | … | … |

The implication $p \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,q$ holds at every index. Therefore:

$$s_0 \models \operatorname{G}(p \rightarrow \operatorname{F}\,q) \quad \text{is TRUE.} \qquad \blacksquare$$

*(2 marks for the paths, 3 marks for the table-style verification.)*

**(b) — 7 marks**

The LTL satisfaction relation $\pi \models \varphi$ is defined inductively on the syntax of $\varphi$. The atomic-proposition case $\pi \models a$ checks $a \in L(\pi[0])$, i.e., a *set* of labels, not the state identity. Every recursive clause — $\neg$, $\vee$, $\operatorname{X}$, $\operatorname{U}$ — refers only to the *traces* of the path or its suffixes. Therefore, two paths with identical traces produce *identical* satisfaction verdicts for *every* LTL formula.

*Formal proof:* Let $\pi, \pi'$ be paths with $\text{trace}(\pi) = \text{trace}(\pi')$. We prove by structural induction on $\varphi$ that $\pi \models \varphi \iff \pi' \models \varphi$.

**Base case ($\varphi = a$):** $\pi \models a \iff a \in L(\pi[0]) \iff a \in L(\pi'[0]) \iff \pi' \models a$. ✓

**Inductive step ($\varphi = \operatorname{X}\psi$):** $\text{trace}(\pi[1..]) = \text{trace}(\pi'[1..])$ (suffixes preserve trace equality). By the induction hypothesis, $\pi[1..] \models \psi \iff \pi'[1..] \models \psi$. Hence $\pi \models \operatorname{X}\psi \iff \pi' \models \operatorname{X}\psi$. ✓

**Inductive step ($\varphi = \psi_1 \operatorname{U}\psi_2$):** For every $j \geq 0$, $\text{trace}(\pi[j..]) = \text{trace}(\pi'[j..])$, so by the induction hypothesis each suffix-satisfaction agrees. Hence the existential/universal quantifiers over $j$ agree. ✓

($\neg$ and $\vee$ are immediate by the induction hypothesis.)

**Conclusion:** The set $\{\, \pi \in \text{Paths}(TS) \mid \pi \models \varphi \,\}$ is a function of $\text{trace}(\pi)$ alone. Therefore every LTL formula $\varphi$ defines an LT property:

$$P_\varphi = \{\, \text{trace}(\pi) \mid \pi \models \varphi \,\} \subseteq (2^{AP})^\omega$$

satisfying the defining condition of an LT property from Section 1.1. $\blacksquare$

*(3 marks for the inductive structure, 2 marks for the trace-suffix argument, 2 marks for the LT-property conclusion.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on "Satisfaction Relation for LT Properties" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing state vs. path satisfaction (–3 marks):** Writing "$s \models \operatorname{F}\,p$" without first expanding it as "$\forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(s).\; \pi \models \operatorname{F}\,p$" is the single most common error. State satisfaction in LTL implicitly *universally quantifies* over all paths.
> 2. **Wrong suffix on $\operatorname{X}$ (–2 marks):** The $\operatorname{X}$ operator checks the *next* state, not the *current* one. Always use $\pi[1..]$, never $\pi[0..]$.
> 3. **Omitting the "until" condition in $\operatorname{U}$ (–2 marks):** When checking $\psi_1 \operatorname{U} \psi_2$, students often locate the first $j$ where $\psi_2$ holds but forget to verify that $\psi_1$ held at *all* earlier positions.
> 4. **Treating $\operatorname{G}$ and $\operatorname{F}$ as primitive instead of derived (–1 mark):** KTU 2024 expects $\operatorname{F}\varphi = \text{true} \operatorname{U}\varphi$ and $\operatorname{G}\varphi = \neg\operatorname{F}\neg\varphi$ to be quoted from the syntax.
> 5. **Using $\pi$ where $\text{trace}(\pi)$ is required (–2 marks):** The satisfaction relation for an LT property $P$ is **defined** on traces, not on paths. Mixing the two levels in the formal statement is a common deduction trigger.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **LT property** = a set of traces $P \subseteq (2^{AP})^\omega$ such that trace-equality implies satisfaction-equality.
- ✅ **Satisfaction relation** $\models$ is the *membership* of a trace in $P$: $\pi \models P \iff \text{trace}(\pi) \in P$.
- ✅ **Kripke structure** $TS = (S, S_0, R, L)$ with **total** $R$ is the canonical model.
- ✅ **Path** = infinite state sequence obeying $R$; **trace** = corresponding label sequence in $(2^{AP})^\omega$.
- ✅ **LTL syntax:** $\varphi ::= \text{true} \mid a \mid \neg\varphi \mid \varphi \vee \varphi \mid \operatorname{X}\varphi \mid \varphi \operatorname{U} \varphi$.
- ✅ **Derived operators:** $\operatorname{F}\varphi \equiv \text{true} \operatorname{U}\varphi$ and $\operatorname{G}\varphi \equiv \neg\operatorname{F}\neg\varphi$.
- ✅ **Until rule:** $\pi \models \psi_1 \operatorname{U} \psi_2 \iff \exists j \geq 0.\; \pi[j..] \models \psi_2$ **and** $\forall 0 \leq i < j.\; \pi[i..] \models \psi_1$.
- ✅ **Next rule:** $\pi \models \operatorname{X}\psi \iff \pi[1..] \models \psi$ (suffix of $\pi$, never the prefix).
- ✅ **State vs. path:** $s \models \varphi \iff \forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(s).\; \pi \models \varphi$.
- ✅ **Model vs. property:** $TS \models P \iff \forall$ initial path $\pi.\; \text{trace}(\pi) \in P$.
- ✅ **LTL ⇒ LT property** (every LTL formula defines an LT property by trace projection).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trace Equivalence and LT Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Trace Equivalence and LT Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Trace Equivalence — Formal Definition

In the formal theory of concurrent and communicating systems (CCS, CSP, LTS), a **trace** of a labelled transition system (LTS) is a finite sequence of observable actions that the system can perform from its initial state, following the transition relation. Given an LTS $\mathcal{T} = (S, Act, \rightarrow, s_0)$ with states $S$, action alphabet $Act$, transition relation $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times Act \times S$ and initial state $s_0$, the set of **traces** of $\mathcal{T}$ is:

$$Tr(\mathcal{T}) = \{\, a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n \in Act^{*} \mid \exists\, s_0 \xrightarrow{a_1} s_1 \xrightarrow{a_2} s_2 \cdots \xrightarrow{a_n} s_n \,\}$$

where $Act^{*}$ denotes the set of all finite sequences (including the empty trace $\varepsilon$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trace Equivalence (KTU 2024 Definition):** Two LTSs $\mathcal{T}_1$ and $\mathcal{T}_2$ are **trace equivalent**, written $\mathcal{T}_1 \approx_T \mathcal{T}_2$ (or $P \approx_T Q$ for processes), if and only if they exhibit exactly the same set of observable traces:
> $$\mathcal{T}_1 \approx_T \mathcal{T}_2 \quad \iff \quad Tr(\mathcal{T}_1) = Tr(\mathcal{T}_2)$$

Equivalently, for every finite sequence of actions $\sigma \in Act^{*}$:

$$\mathcal{T}_1 \xrightarrow{\sigma} \quad \iff \quad \mathcal{T}_2 \xrightarrow{\sigma}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two waiters in a restaurant, **Waiter-A** and **Waiter-B**, each described by a rule-book. If we record every possible *order of events* (take order → serve soup → serve main course → give bill → receive payment) that a waiter *can* produce from start to finish, then:

- If Waiter-A and Waiter-B can produce **the exact same collection of event-sequences**, an outside observer who only watches the *flow of visible actions* (without caring which internal state the waiter is in) cannot tell them apart.
- This is precisely **trace equivalence**: the two systems are indistinguishable on the basis of which *action sequences* are possible — even if their internal state structures differ wildly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule-of-Thumb:** Trace equivalence compares *what sequences of actions* are possible, **not** *at which internal states* they are produced. It is therefore a **black-box / external behaviour** equivalence, the weakest among the standard behavioural equivalences in concurrency theory.

## 1.3 LT Properties — Formal Definition

A **property** of a system is any subset of the set of all (finite or infinite) executions that the system *might* produce. Formally, let $\Sigma$ be a fixed alphabet of actions. A **linear-time (LT) property** over $\Sigma$ is a set of infinite words:

$$P \;\subseteq\; \Sigma^{\omega}$$

A system $\mathcal{T}$ (or process $P$) **satisfies** the property $P$ if and only if the set of infinite executions of $\mathcal{T}$ is contained in $P$:

$$\mathcal{T} \models P \quad \iff \quad Exec^{\omega}(\mathcal{T}) \;\subseteq\; P$$

Equivalently, an LT property is a set of behaviours that a system may or may not exhibit — and verification asks whether the system's actual behaviour lies *inside* this set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Linear-Time"?** Because properties are characterised along each *individual execution path* (a single linear timeline), as opposed to **branching-time (BT)** properties which quantify over the system's tree of all possible futures at every state.

## 1.4 Intuitive View of LT Properties

Think of a property as a *checklist* of admissible histories of the world. The verification question is then: *"Does every possible future of the system belong to the checklist?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **The two canonical subclasses of LT properties** — every LT property $P$ can be written as the conjunction of a safety property and a liveness property:
> $$P \;=\; \text{Safety}(P) \;\cap\; \text{Liveness}(P)$$

## 1.5 Key Notation Standards (KTU Board Standard)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:--|:--|
| $Act$ | Set of all observable action labels |
| $Act^{*}$ | All finite sequences (including $\varepsilon$) |
| $Act^{\omega}$ | All infinite sequences |
| $Tr(P)$ | Set of finite traces of process $P$ |
| $\sigma, \tau$ | Traces (action sequences) |
| $\mathcal{T} \xrightarrow{\sigma}$ | There exists a path labelled $\sigma$ from the initial state |
| $P \models \varphi$ | Process $P$ satisfies property $\varphi$ |
| $\Sigma$ | Alphabet of actions for an LT property |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECNT526 / Module 1):** Trace equivalence and LT properties together form the **observational foundation** of model checking. Every model checker (SPIN, NuSMV, CADP) ultimately reduces verification to a question about traces and trace-containment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Trace

A trace is built inductively from the empty trace $\varepsilon$ and the transition relation. The set of traces $Tr(P)$ of a process $P$ is the smallest set such that:

1. **Base case:** $\varepsilon \in Tr(P)$ (every process has the empty trace).
2. **Inductive step:** If $\sigma \in Tr(P)$ and $P \xrightarrow{\sigma} P'$ and $P' \xrightarrow{a} P''$, then $\sigma a \in Tr(P)$.

> [!NOTE]
> In some textbooks a distinction is drawn between $Tr(P)$ (all finite traces) and $Tr^{\max}(P)$ (all *maximal* traces, i.e. those that cannot be extended because the process has deadlocked) and $Tr^{fin}(P)$ (the *completed / terminated* traces, ending at a state labelled $\surd$). The empty trace is **always** present.

## 2.2 Properties of Trace Equivalence as a Relation

Trace equivalence satisfies the three axioms of an equivalence relation:

- **Reflexivity:** $P \approx_T P$, because $Tr(P) = Tr(P)$.
- **Symmetry:** If $P \approx_T Q$ then $Tr(P) = Tr(Q)$ implies $Tr(Q) = Tr(P)$, hence $Q \approx_T P$.
- **Transitivity:** If $P \approx_T Q$ and $Q \approx_T R$ then $Tr(P) = Tr(Q) = Tr(R)$, so $P \approx_T R$.

Additionally:

- **Pre-order form:** Trace *pre-order* (containment) is written $P \sqsubseteq_T Q$ iff $Tr(P) \subseteq Tr(Q)$.
- **Kernel:** $\approx_T$ is the kernel of $\sqsubseteq_T$ (its symmetric part).

## 2.3 Hierarchy of Behavioural Equivalences (KTU High-Yield)

The standard lattice from **finest** to **coarsest** equivalence (each one identifies *more* processes):

$$\text{Strong Bisim.} \;\succ\; \text{2-Nested Simulation} \;\succ\; \text{Simulation} \;\succ\; \text{Trace Eq.} \;\succ\; \text{Failure Eq.} \;\succ\; \text{Failure-Trace Eq.} \;\succ\; \text{Testing Eq.}$$

where $\succ$ means "is strictly finer than" (i.e. distinguishes more pairs of processes).

> [!WARNING]
> A **common KTU board mistake** is to write "trace equivalence is finer than bisimulation" — the direction is **reversed**: bisimulation is finer (more discriminating), trace equivalence is coarser.

## 2.4 The Four Standard Classes of LT Properties

| Class | Informal Meaning | Formal Definition |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Safety** | "Nothing bad ever happens" | $\forall \sigma \in P:\ \exists$ "bad prefix" $x$ s.t. $\sigma x \notin P$ |
| **Liveness** | "Something good eventually happens" | $\forall \sigma \in \Sigma^{*}:\ \exists \tau$ with $\sigma\tau \in P$ |
| **Deadlock-Freedom** | "An action is always possible" | A safety property, formally: $\forall s \in reach(s_0):\ \exists a.\ s \xrightarrow{a}$ |
| **Invariant** | "A predicate $\psi$ holds in every reachable state" | A safety property expressed state-wise |

## 2.5 Closure Properties — KTU Formula Sheet

For a property $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ to be a **valid LT property** (i.e. a *prefix-closed, limit-closed* set), it must satisfy:

1. **Prefix-Closure for Safety:** If $\sigma \in P$ and $\tau$ is a prefix of $\sigma$, then $\tau \in P$.
2. **Limit-Closure:** If an infinite word $\alpha$ has *every* one of its finite prefixes belonging to $P$, then $\alpha \in P$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alper-Sistla Theorem (used heavily in KTU Q-bank):** Every LT property $P$ can be uniquely written as $P = \mathsf{Safe}(P) \cap \mathsf{Live}(P)$, where:
> - $\mathsf{Safe}(P) = \{\sigma \in \Sigma^{\omega} \mid \text{no finite prefix of }\sigma\text{ is a "bad prefix"}\}$
> - $\mathsf{Live}(P) = $ smallest liveness property containing $P$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description |
|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $P \approx_T Q \iff Tr(P) = Tr(Q)$ | Trace equivalence definition |
| 2 | $P \sqsubseteq_T Q \iff Tr(P) \subseteq Tr(Q)$ | Trace pre-order (containment) |
| 3 | $\mathcal{T} \models P \iff Exec^{\omega}(\mathcal{T}) \subseteq P$ | System satisfies LT property |
| 4 | $P = \mathsf{Safe}(P) \cap \mathsf{Live}(P)$ | Alper-Sistla decomposition |
| 5 | $\sigma \in P \Rightarrow$ every prefix of $\sigma$ in $P$ | Prefix-closure (safety) |
| 6 | $\forall$ prefixes of $\alpha$ in $P \Rightarrow \alpha \in P$ | Limit-closure (liveness) |
| 7 | $\varepsilon \in Tr(P)$ for any $P$ | Empty trace axiom |
| 8 | $\text{Bisim} \Rightarrow \text{Trace Eq.} \Rightarrow \text{Failure Eq.}$ | Strict implication chain |
| 9 | $\sigma \in Tr(P) \wedge P \xrightarrow{\sigma} P' \Rightarrow \varepsilon \in Tr(P')$ | Trace inheritance |
| 10 | $P \models \Box \varphi$ (always) iff all states along trace satisfy $\varphi$ | LTL semantics |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Trace Equivalence & LT Properties |
|:--|:--|
| **Hardware Verification** | Checking that two RTL implementations are trace-equivalent (industrial equivalence checking) |
| **Protocol Verification** | Verifying security protocols (TLS, Kerberos) using safety LT properties |
| **Software Model Checking** | SLAM, BLAST, Java PathFinder verify safety LT properties on C/Java programs |
| **Compiler Correctness** | Showing that *comp(P)* and *P* are trace-equivalent (CompCert-style proofs) |
| **Cyber-Physical Systems** | Liveness LT properties guarantee eventual response in automotive ECUs |
| **Smart Contracts (Solidity)** | Trace properties detect re-entrancy and integer-overflow bugs |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Computing the Trace Set of a CCS Process

Consider the CCS process (in Milner's notation):

$$P \;=\; a.\,b.\mathbf{0} \;+\; c.\,d.\mathbf{0}$$

This process may *either* execute action $a$ followed by $b$ and terminate, *or* execute $c$ followed by $d$ and terminate. The initial choice is non-deterministic.

**Step 1 — Identify the initial actions.**
From the initial state, the possible first actions are $a$ and $c$ (the guards of the two summands of $+$).

**Step 2 — Extend each first action to its continuations.**

- If $a$ is chosen, the residual process becomes $b.\mathbf{0}$, whose only action is $b$.
- If $c$ is chosen, the residual process becomes $d.\mathbf{0}$, whose only action is $d$.

**Step 3 — Enumerate all traces.**

The complete trace set, by structural induction, is:

$$Tr(P) \;=\; \{\, \varepsilon,\; a,\; c,\; a\,b,\; c\,d \,\}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of prefix-closure.**

For each non-empty trace, every prefix must also belong to $Tr(P)$:
- $a$ → prefix $\varepsilon \in Tr(P)$ ✓
- $c$ → prefix $\varepsilon \in Tr(P)$ ✓
- $ab$ → prefixes $a, \varepsilon \in Tr(P)$ ✓
- $cd$ → prefixes $c, \varepsilon \in Tr(P)$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the trace $ac \notin Tr(P)$: the process cannot first choose $a$ and then $c$, because once $a$ is chosen, the residual $b.\mathbf{0}$ has no $c$-transition. Trace equivalence would *not* equate $P$ with a process that *could* execute $ac$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Trace Equivalence vs. Strong Bisimulation

We now demonstrate the **concrete distinction** between trace equivalence and bisimulation on a textbook example.

Let:

$$P \;=\; a.\,(b.\mathbf{0} \;+\; c.\mathbf{0})$$
$$Q \;=\; a.\,b.\mathbf{0} \;+\; a.\,c.\mathbf{0}$$

### Step-by-step trace computation

**Traces of $P$:**
- Initial actions: $a$. After $a$, the residual is $b.\mathbf{0} + c.\mathbf{0}$, which can do $b$ *or* $c$.
- Hence $Tr(P) = \{\varepsilon,\ a,\ ab,\ ac\}$.

**Traces of $Q$:**
- Initial actions: $a$ (twice, in two different summands). After $a$, the residual is $b.\mathbf{0}$ in one branch and $c.\mathbf{0}$ in the other.
- The set of possible second actions is again $\{b, c\}$.
- Hence $Tr(Q) = \{\varepsilon,\ a,\ ab,\ ac\}$.

**Conclusion on trace equivalence:**

$$Tr(P) \;=\; Tr(Q) \;=\; \{\varepsilon, a, ab, ac\}$$

$$\Longrightarrow\quad P \approx_T Q$$

**Bisimulation check (showing the difference):**

A bisimulation $\mathcal{R}$ must relate $P$ and $Q$ and also match transitions. After the common $a$-action, the residuals are:

$$P' \;=\; b.\mathbf{0} + c.\mathbf{0} \quad\quad Q' \;=\; b.\mathbf{0} \;\;\text{(in one branch)} \;\;\text{or}\;\; c.\mathbf{0} \;\;\text{(in the other)}$$

The state $P'$ has a $b$-transition **and** a $c$-transition, but $Q'$ in *each individual branch* has only one. Therefore $P'$ is *not* bisimilar to either $b.\mathbf{0}$ or $c.\mathbf{0}$ alone, and **no bisimulation can relate $P$ to $Q$**.

$$\Longrightarrow\quad P \not\sim Q \quad \text{(not bisimilar)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The asymmetry:** $P \approx_T Q$ but $P \not\sim Q$. This *single counter-example* is the standard KTU exam illustration of "trace equivalence is strictly coarser than strong bisimulation."

## 3.3 Derivation — Alper–Sistla Safety/Liveness Decomposition

**Claim:** Every LT property $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ can be decomposed uniquely as $P = \mathsf{Safe}(P) \cap \mathsf{Live}(P)$.

**Step 1 — Define the "bad prefixes" of $P$.**
A finite word $x \in \Sigma^{*}$ is a *bad prefix* of $P$ if for every infinite extension $\alpha \in \Sigma^{\omega}$ we have $x\alpha \notin P$. Equivalently, no infinite word in $P$ has $x$ as a prefix.

**Step 2 — Construct the safety component.**

$$\mathsf{Safe}(P) \;=\; \{\, \alpha \in \Sigma^{\omega} \mid \text{no finite prefix of } \alpha \text{ is a bad prefix of } P \,\}$$

By construction, $\mathsf{Safe}(P)$ is *prefix-closed* and contains $P$.

**Step 3 — Construct the liveness component.**

$$\mathsf{Live}(P) \;=\; \{\, \alpha \in \Sigma^{\omega} \mid \forall x \in \Sigma^{*}:\ \exists \beta \in \Sigma^{\omega}:\ x\beta \in P \;\text{and}\; x\beta \text{ is a prefix of } \alpha \,\}$$

i.e., $\mathsf{Live}(P)$ is the *smallest liveness property containing $P$*; every finite prefix can be extended to land inside $P$.

**Step 4 — Verify the intersection.**

We must show $P = \mathsf{Safe}(P) \cap \mathsf{Live}(P)$:

- $\subseteq$: Take $\alpha \in P$. Then no prefix of $\alpha$ is a bad prefix of $P$ (else that prefix could not be extended into $P$, contradicting $\alpha \in P$). So $\alpha \in \mathsf{Safe}(P)$. Also $P \subseteq \mathsf{Live}(P)$ by construction. Hence $\alpha \in \mathsf{Safe}(P) \cap \mathsf{Live}(P)$.
- $\supseteq$: Take $\alpha \in \mathsf{Safe}(P) \cap \mathsf{Live}(P)$. If $\alpha \notin P$, then by limit-closure there must exist a finite prefix $x$ of $\alpha$ such that no extension $x\beta$ is in $P$, making $x$ a bad prefix — contradicting $\alpha \in \mathsf{Safe}(P)$. So $\alpha \in P$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering significance:** The decomposition is the *theoretical basis* for safety/liveness checking in tools like SPIN — you can verify safety and liveness properties using *different algorithmic techniques* (reachability for safety, cycle-detection / Büchi emptiness for liveness) and combine the verdicts.

## 3.4 Verification Worked-Example — Mutual Exclusion as an LT Property

Consider two concurrent processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ each having *critical-section* and *non-critical-section* actions: $n_1, c_1, n_2, c_2$. The **mutual-exclusion safety property** is:

$$ME \;=\; \{\, \alpha \in \{n_1, c_1, n_2, c_2\}^{\omega} \mid c_1 \text{ and } c_2 \text{ never appear consecutively without a } n \text{ between them} \,\}$$

**Step 1 — Identify bad prefixes.**
A bad prefix is any finite word in which $c_1 c_2$ or $c_2 c_1$ appear as adjacent letters. (These cannot be extended into $ME$.)

**Step 2 — Apply the safety-liveness decomposition.**
Here $ME$ is *purely* a safety property, because every violation is witnessed by a finite bad prefix. Hence $\mathsf{Safe}(ME) = ME$ and $\mathsf{Live}(ME) = \{n_1, c_1, n_2, c_2\}^{\omega}$ (the trivial liveness — every finite prefix is extendable).

**Step 3 — Verification via a model checker.**
The model checker explores the LTS of $P_1 \parallel P_2$ and checks reachability of any state containing both processes in their critical section. If unreachable, $\mathcal{T} \models ME$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Behavioural Equivalences (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Strong Bisimulation\nfinest, ~] --> B[2-Nested Simulation]
    B --> C[Simulation]
    C --> D[Trace Equivalence\n~_T]
    D --> E[Failure Equivalence]
    E --> F[Failure-Trace Equivalence]
    F --> G[Testing Equivalence\ncoarsest]

    H[Notes] --- A
    H[Notes] --- D
    H[Notes] --- G

    note1["Finer = distinguishes more processes\nCoarser = identifies more processes"] -.-> A
    note2["P ~ Q  implies  P ~_T Q\nbut not vice versa"] -.-> D
```

## 4.2 LT-Property Classification Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[LT Property P subset of Sigma^omega] --> S[Safety Properties\nprefix-closed\nbad-prefix structure]
    P --> L[Liveness Properties\nlimit-closed\nevery prefix extendable]

    S --> S1[Mutual Exclusion]
    S --> S2[Type Safety]
    S --> S3[Deadlock Freedom]
    S --> S4[Partial Correctness]

    L --> L1[Termination]
    L --> L2[Starvation Freedom]
    L --> L3[Response]
    L --> L4[Total Correctness]

    D[Safety-Liveness Decomposition:\nP = Safe P intersect Live P] --- P
    D --- S
    D --- L
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Trace-Based Verification

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[System Source\nCCS, Promela, SMV] --> mdl[Build LTS Model\nStates, Transitions, Labels]
    mdl --> lts[LTS T = S Act arrow s0]

    lts --> c1[Compute Tr T\nall finite action sequences]
    lts --> c2[Check Property Containment\nExec omega T subset P]

    c1 --> ve[Verification Engine]
    c2 --> ve

    spec[Property Specification\nLTL, Buchi, Regex] --> trn[Translate to Automaton A_P]
    trn --> c2

    ve --> res{Result}
    res -->|Yes| ok[System satisfies P]
    res -->|No| cex[Counter-example Trace\nreturned to user]
```

## 4.4 Trace-Equivalence Verification Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input: Two LTS T1 and T2] --> B[Enumerate Tr T1]
    A --> C[Enumerate Tr T2]

    B --> D[Set Comparison:\nTr T1 == Tr T2 ?]
    C --> D

    D -->|Yes| E[Equivalent:\nT1 ~_T T2]
    D -->|No| F[Witness Trace sigma:\nsigma in Tr T1 minus Tr T2\nor vice versa]

    E --> G[Output: EQUIVALENT]
    F --> H[Output: NOT EQUIVALENT\nWitness: sigma]
```

## 4.5 Safety vs. Liveness — Decision Tree for Students

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q[Property under verification] --> Q1{Is every violation\nwitnessed by a finite prefix?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| SAFE[Safety Property\nverify by reachability]
    Q1 -->|No| LIVE[Liveness Property\nverify by cycle detection / Buchi emptiness]

    SAFE --> SAFE1[Model Checker: check\nany reachable state violates phi]
    LIVE --> LIVE1[Model Checker: search\nfor a fair cycle violating phi]

    SAFE1 --> RES1[Decidable: PSPACE-complete in general]
    LIVE1 --> RES2[Decidable: PSPACE-complete for LTL]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define trace equivalence for two labelled transition systems $\mathcal{T}_1$ and $\mathcal{T}_2$. State the formal condition using the trace sets $Tr(\mathcal{T}_1)$ and $Tr(\mathcal{T}_2)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *trace* of an LTS $\mathcal{T}$ is a finite sequence of actions $\sigma = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$ such that there exist states $s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_n$ with $s_0 \xrightarrow{a_1} s_1 \xrightarrow{a_2} \cdots \xrightarrow{a_n} s_n$. The set of all such traces is denoted $Tr(\mathcal{T})$.

Two LTSs $\mathcal{T}_1$ and $\mathcal{T}_2$ over a common action alphabet are **trace equivalent**, written $\mathcal{T}_1 \approx_T \mathcal{T}_2$, if and only if they have identical sets of traces:

$$\mathcal{T}_1 \approx_T \mathcal{T}_2 \quad\iff\quad Tr(\mathcal{T}_1) \;=\; Tr(\mathcal{T}_2)$$

**Mark Split:** Definition of trace (1) + formal set condition (1) + conclusion (1).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between safety and liveness properties. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **safety property** states that "nothing bad ever happens." Every violation is witnessed by a finite *bad prefix*. Formally, it is a prefix-closed set of traces.  
  *Example:* Mutual exclusion — two processes are never simultaneously in their critical sections.

- A **liveness property** states that "something good eventually happens." Every finite sequence of actions can be extended so that the property holds. Formally, it is limit-closed.  
  *Example:* Termination — every execution eventually reaches a halting state.

**Mark Split:** Safety definition + example (1.5) + Liveness definition + example (1.5).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of trace equivalence between two processes $P$ and $Q$. Show that trace equivalence is a congruence with respect to the choice operator $+$.  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the CCS processes:
$$P \;=\; a.\,(b.\mathbf{0} + c.\mathbf{0}) \quad\quad Q \;=\; a.\,b.\mathbf{0} + a.\,c.\mathbf{0}$$
Compute $Tr(P)$ and $Tr(Q)$. Are $P$ and $Q$ trace equivalent? Are they strongly bisimilar? Justify your answer with the bisimulation game argument.  
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Trace equivalence and congruence under choice**

A *trace* of a process $P$ is any finite sequence of actions $a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$ that $P$ can perform starting from its initial state, written $P \xrightarrow{a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n} P'$. The set $Tr(P)$ collects all such sequences, including $\varepsilon$.

**Trace equivalence:**
$$P \approx_T Q \quad\iff\quad Tr(P) \;=\; Tr(Q)$$

**Congruence proof (sketch):** We show that if $P \approx_T Q$ then for any $R$:
$$P + R \;\;\approx_T\;\; Q + R$$

We have $Tr(P + R) = Tr(P) \cup Tr(R)$ because the choice operator selects non-deterministically between the two operands, so the traces of $P + R$ are exactly the union of the traces of $P$ and $R$. Similarly $Tr(Q + R) = Tr(Q) \cup Tr(R)$. Therefore:

$$Tr(P + R) = Tr(P) \cup Tr(R) = Tr(Q) \cup Tr(R) = Tr(Q + R)$$

Hence $P + R \approx_T Q + R$, confirming congruence under $+$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Trace computation and bisimulation check**

**Step 1 — Compute $Tr(P)$.**

The process $P = a.\,(b.\mathbf{0} + c.\mathbf{0})$ first performs $a$ and then behaves as $b.\mathbf{0} + c.\mathbf{0}$, which can do $b$ or $c$ (and then terminate). Hence:

$$Tr(P) \;=\; \{\, \varepsilon,\ a,\ a\,b,\ a\,c \,\}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Tr(Q)$.**

The process $Q = a.b.\mathbf{0} + a.c.\mathbf{0}$ has two summands, each starting with $a$. After $a$, the residual is either $b.\mathbf{0}$ (in the first branch) or $c.\mathbf{0}$ (in the second). The set of possible second actions is therefore $\{b, c\}$ overall, yielding:

$$Tr(Q) \;=\; \{\, \varepsilon,\ a,\ a\,b,\ a\,c \,\}$$

**Step 3 — Trace equivalence verdict.**

$$Tr(P) \;=\; Tr(Q) \;\Longrightarrow\; P \approx_T Q \quad\text{(TRACE EQUIVALENT)} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Bisimulation game.**

Suppose, for contradiction, that $P \sim Q$ via a relation $\mathcal{R}$. Then $(P, Q) \in \mathcal{R}$.

- From $P$, the *only* initial action is $a$, leading to state $P' = b.\mathbf{0} + c.\mathbf{0}$.
- From $Q$, the only initial action is also $a$, but the residual state is *either* $Q'_1 = b.\mathbf{0}$ *or* $Q'_2 = c.\mathbf{0}$ (depending on which summand the non-deterministic choice selected).

By the bisimulation game, $Q$ must be able to *match* every transition of $P$. The state $P' = b.\mathbf{0} + c.\mathbf{0}$ has a $b$-transition **and** a $c$-transition. But neither $Q'_1$ nor $Q'_2$ alone has both transitions. Therefore no relation can pair $P'$ with a single $Q'_i$, and:

$$P \not\sim Q \quad\text{(NOT BISIMILAR)}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

The pair $(P, Q)$ is the *canonical KTU counter-example* showing that **trace equivalence is strictly coarser than strong bisimulation**:

$$P \approx_T Q \quad\text{but}\quad P \not\sim Q$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (Internal Choice to Question A)

**(a)** What is a Linear-Time (LT) property? State the formal definition as a set-theoretic characterisation. Explain, with a diagram, the safety–liveness decomposition theorem of Alper and Sistla.  
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Express each of the following requirements as an LTL formula and classify each as safety or liveness:
  (i) The system never grants two requests simultaneously.  
  (ii) Every request is eventually acknowledged.  
  (iii) After a button is pressed, the system eventually resets.  
**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) LT properties and the Alper–Sistla theorem**

**Definition.** Let $\Sigma$ be a non-empty finite set of action labels. A **linear-time property** over $\Sigma$ is a set $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ of infinite words over $\Sigma$. A system $\mathcal{T}$ with alphabet $\Sigma$ satisfies $P$ iff its set of infinite executions is contained in $P$:

$$\mathcal{T} \models P \quad\iff\quad Exec^{\omega}(\mathcal{T}) \subseteq P$$

**Two canonical subclasses.**

- A *safety property* is one in which every violation is witnessed by a finite "bad prefix" $x$ — no infinite word in $P$ has $x$ as a prefix. Equivalently, $P$ is *prefix-closed* under taking limits.
- A *liveness property* is one in which every finite word can be extended into $P$ — i.e., no finite prefix is "bad."

**Alper–Sistla theorem.** Every LT property $P$ can be *uniquely* decomposed as:

$$P \;=\; \mathsf{Safe}(P) \;\cap\; \mathsf{Live}(P)$$

where

$$\mathsf{Safe}(P) = \{\alpha \in \Sigma^{\omega} \mid \text{no finite prefix of }\alpha\text{ is a bad prefix of }P\}$$

$$\mathsf{Live}(P) = \text{smallest liveness property containing }P$$

(See Section 3.3 above for the rigorous proof sketch; the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 illustrates the classification.)

**(b) LTL encodings**

We let $r$ = "request granted", $q$ = "request issued", $a$ = "acknowledge", $b$ = "button pressed", $s$ = "system reset."

**(i)** "The system never grants two requests simultaneously."  
This is a safety property (any violation is a finite bad-prefix event). A standard LTL encoding: $\varphi_1 = \Box\, \neg(r \wedge r) = \Box\, \neg r$, or more accurately, if $r_i$ denotes "request $i$ granted":

$$\varphi_1 \;=\; \Box\, \neg(r_1 \wedge r_2)$$

**Class: SAFETY** (prefix-closed; bad prefix is any word containing $r_1 r_2$ consecutively).

**(ii)** "Every request is eventually acknowledged."  
This is a liveness property — every finite prefix can be extended into an acknowledgement. The LTL formula is:

$$\varphi_2 \;=\; \Box\,(q \;\Rightarrow\; \Diamond\, a) \quad \equiv \quad \Box\, q \;\mathcal{U}\; a$$

**Class: LIVENESS** (a request $q$ is *followed*, not preceded, by an acknowledgement $a$ — a typical response property).

**(iii)** "After a button is pressed, the system eventually resets."  
Another response / liveness pattern. The LTL formula is:

$$\varphi_3 \;=\; \Box\,(b \;\Rightarrow\; \Diamond\, s)$$

**Class: LIVENESS** (the obligation is "eventually", not "never").

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — read carefully!**
>
> 1. **Confusing direction of the equivalence hierarchy.** Several students wrote "trace equivalence is finer than bisimulation" — this is the **opposite** of the truth. Bisimulation is finer (distinguishes more processes); trace equivalence is coarser.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the empty trace $\varepsilon$.** The set $Tr(P)$ *always* contains $\varepsilon$. Omitting it costs a mark in "compute the trace set" questions.
>
> 3. **Misclassifying $\Diamond$ properties as safety.** The $\Diamond$ (eventually) operator makes a formula liveness, **not** safety. Only $\Box$ (always) and $\Box$ over negations are typically safety.
>
> 4. **In trace-equality questions, students often write the wrong union/difference.** Remember: $Tr(P+Q) = Tr(P) \cup Tr(Q)$, $Tr(P \parallel Q) = $ interleavings of $Tr(P)$ and $Tr(Q)$, $Tr(P \setminus L) = Tr(P)$ with actions in $L$ hidden.
>
> 5. **Conflating *completed traces* with *maximal traces*.** A maximal trace ends in deadlock; a completed trace ends at a $\surd$ state. The two are not identical when a process can *only* deadlock without terminating.
>
> 6. **In LTL questions, forgetting the outer $\Box$.** Writing $q \Rightarrow \Diamond a$ is *not* equivalent to $\Box(q \Rightarrow \Diamond a)$. The first is satisfied by traces that *never* see a $q$, the second is not.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Trace Equivalence and LT Properties**

- [ ] A **trace** is a *finite* sequence of actions reachable from the initial state. The empty trace $\varepsilon$ is always present.
- [ ] **Trace equivalence** is defined as $P \approx_T Q \iff Tr(P) = Tr(Q)$. It is a congruence for all standard CCS operators.
- [ ] Trace equivalence is **strictly coarser** than strong bisimulation — the canonical counter-example is $a.(b+c)$ vs. $a.b + a.c$.
- [ ] A property $P$ over a system $\mathcal{T}$ is a set of behaviours; **verification** asks $\mathcal{T} \models P$, i.e. $Exec^{\omega}(\mathcal{T}) \subseteq P$.
- [ ] An **LT property** is a set $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$. It is checked *along each individual execution*, hence "linear-time".
- [ ] **Safety properties** are *prefix-closed*: every violation has a finite bad prefix. Examples: mutual exclusion, type safety, deadlock-freedom, partial correctness.
- [ ] **Liveness properties** are *limit-closed*: every finite word is extendable. Examples: termination, starvation-freedom, response, total correctness.
- [ ] **Alper–Sistla theorem**: every LT property uniquely decomposes as $P = \mathsf{Safe}(P) \cap \mathsf{Live}(P)$.
- [ ] **LTL semantics for common patterns**:
    - $\Box \varphi$ — *always* $\varphi$ → **safety**
    - $\Diamond \varphi$ — *eventually* $\varphi$ → **liveness**
    - $\Box(p \Rightarrow \Diamond q)$ — *every $p$ is eventually followed by $q$* → **liveness (response)**
    - $\Diamond \Box \varphi$ — *eventually $\varphi$ holds forever* → **liveness**
    - $\Box \Diamond \varphi$ — *$\varphi$ holds infinitely often* → **liveness**
- [ ] The set of traces of $P + Q$ is $Tr(P) \cup Tr(Q)$ — a frequent KTU board question.
- [ ] For trace-equivalence questions, *always enumerate* $Tr(P)$ and $Tr(Q)$ *inductively* and check prefix-closure.
- [ ] The four standard *kinds* of LT properties to remember: **safety, liveness, deadlock-freedom (safety), invariant (safety)**.
- [ ] Büchi automata, LTL, and regular expressions are the *three* standard ways to specify LT properties.
- [ ] Tools: SPIN (Promela), NuSMV (CTL/LTL), CADP (LNT/BCG) all implement LT-property verification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Invariants

In the context of automated verification and model checking, an **invariant** is a state predicate (a Boolean-valued function over program/system states) that is asserted to evaluate to `true` at **every reachable state** of the system under analysis.

Let $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ be a Kripke structure (the standard semantic model in KTU-referenced model checking literature), where:
- $S$ is a non-empty finite set of states
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of initial states
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ is the total transition relation
- $L: S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is the state-labeling function over atomic propositions $AP$

Then a state formula $P$ (a propositional logic formula over $AP$) is an **invariant** of $M$ if and only if:

$$M \models \Box P \quad \iff \quad \forall s \in \text{Reach}(M) : s \models P$$

where $\text{Reach}(M)$ denotes the set of all states reachable from $S_0$ via zero or more $R$-transitions, and $\Box$ is the LTL/CTL "globally" temporal operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Invariants are the **simplest, most fundamental, and most frequently encountered subclass of safety properties**. Every invariant is a safety property, but not every safety property is an invariant (e.g., "every request is eventually followed by a response with no specific order restriction" is a safety liveness mixture).

## 1.2 Intuition via Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Thermometer**
>
> Imagine a bank's vault with a thermometer that **must always read between 15°C and 25°C** to preserve its contents. The statement *"the temperature is in [15°C, 25°C]"* is an **invariant** of the vault's climate-control system. The cooling engineers do not care *when* the temperature enters or leaves that range along an infinite timeline — they only care that **in every observable state, the safety condition holds**.
>
> - If the thermometer ever reads 30°C, the invariant is *violated* (a **bad state** has been reached).
> - The check is performed by a safety monitor (the *verifier*) on the infinite sequence of states produced by the system.
> - This mirrors exactly what an automated verifier does: it exhaustively checks that a predicate holds over the entire reachable state space, never relaxing its scrutiny to "eventual" or "likely" outcomes.

In software terms, common invariants include:
- "*`x ≥ 0`* throughout execution" (a memory-safety invariant)
- "*`lock_count ≥ 0`* at every point" (a deadlock-freedom invariant)
- "*`head ≠ null ⇒ head.next ≠ null`*" (a list well-formedness invariant)
- "*mutual exclusion: at most one process in the critical section*" (a concurrent-programming invariant)

## 1.3 Why Invariants Matter in KTU-Style Automated Verification

- **They are decidable** for finite-state systems via standard fixpoint computation.
- **They underpin proof-based verification** (Hoare logic, deductive verification), where invariants are the cornerstone of partial correctness.
- **They are the natural target of dataflow analysis** (a compiler-level invariant-generation technique).
- **They provide compositional reasoning**: a system built from components preserves the conjunction of its components' invariants (under suitable composition rules).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: Inductive vs. Non-Inductive Invariants

A predicate $I$ is an **invariant** of a transition system $M$ when it holds at every reachable state. The proof obligations depend on *how* $I$ is structured.

### 2.1.1 Inductive Invariant (Self-Consistent)

$I$ is **inductive** (or a *fixpoint invariant*) if it satisfies the classical two-clause *inductive invariant proof rule*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(Initiation)} \quad & \forall s \in S_0 : I(s) \\
\textbf{(Consecution)} \quad & \forall s, s' \in S : \big(I(s) \land R(s, s') \big) \Rightarrow I(s')
\end{aligned}
$$

If both clauses hold, then by **Noetherian induction on the length of execution paths**, $I$ is provably true at every reachable state. This is the standard KTU board-exam formulation.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "inductive"?** The name arises from the fact that $I$ acts as an *inductive hypothesis*: if it holds at step $k$, the consecution clause guarantees it holds at step $k+1$, and initiation guarantees it holds at step $0$. The two clauses are exactly the base case and inductive step of a mathematical induction on path length.

### 2.1.2 Non-Inductive Invariant

A predicate $J$ may be a valid invariant (true at every reachable state) without being inductive. Such a $J$ requires a **stronger auxiliary inductive invariant** $I$ to prove it:

$$I \Rightarrow J, \quad I \text{ is inductive}, \quad \Rightarrow J \text{ is an invariant}$$

The art of **invariant strengthening / weakening** (the *abstraction–refinement loop* in CEGAR-style model checking) revolves around finding such an $I$.

## 2.2 Safety Property Characterization

A **safety property** $P_{safe}$ is formally defined as a property whose violation can be witnessed by a **finite bad prefix** (Alpern & Schneider, 1987; Büchi & Landweber):

> A property $\mathcal{P}$ is a safety property iff for every infinite execution $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots$ of the system, $\pi \not\models \mathcal{P}$ implies there exists a finite prefix $\hat{\pi} = s_0 s_1 \ldots s_k$ such that every infinite extension of $\hat{\pi}$ also violates $\mathcal{P}$. The prefix $\hat{\pi}$ is called a **minimal bad prefix**.

**The Fundamental Theorem of Safety Verification (Büchi–Landweber):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A property is a safety property **if and only if** it is characterized by a regular set of bad prefixes recognized by a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) — the **safety automaton** $A_{\text{safety}}$.

**Invariants ↔ Safety Properties with Singleton Bad-Prefix Automata:**

An invariant $\Box P$ is the special case of a safety property in which the "bad prefix" is any state where $\neg P$ holds. The safety automaton degenerates to a single sink state labelled with $\neg P$.

## 2.3 Computing Invariants — The Three Classical Algorithms

### 2.3.1 Forward Reachability (Naïve Enumeration)

Compute the least fixpoint of the predecessor/post-state transformer:

$$\text{Reach}(M) = \mu X.\, S_0 \cup \text{Post}(X)$$

Then check $\forall s \in \text{Reach}(M) : P(s)$.

### 2.3.2 Dataflow Analysis Interpretation

Interpret invariants as solutions to a monotone dataflow equation system over a lattice $(\mathcal{L}, \sqsubseteq, \sqcup, \sqcap, \bot, \top)$. Standard formulations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Reaching-Definitions:} \quad & \text{out}[B] = \text{gen}[B] \cup (\text{in}[B] - \text{kill}[B]) \\
\textbf{Available-Expressions:} \quad & \text{out}[B] = \bigcap_{P \in \text{pred}(B)} \text{out}[P] \quad \text{(forward)} \\
\textbf{Live-Variables:} \quad & \text{in}[B] = \text{use}[B] \cup (\text{out}[B] - \text{def}[B]) \quad \text{(backward)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each yields an **invariant fact** at every program point.

### 2.3.3 Abstract Interpretation (Cousot & Cousot, 1977)

A Galois connection $(\alpha, \gamma)$ between the concrete state lattice $\langle \wp(S), \subseteq \rangle$ and an abstract lattice $\langle \mathcal{A}, \sqsubseteq \rangle$ induces a **sound abstract transformer** $F^{\#}$ whose least fixpoint is a **sound over-approximation** of the concrete invariant set:

$$\alpha(\text{lfp}\, F) \sqsubseteq \text{lfp}\, F^{\#}$$

Widening operators $(\nabla)$ guarantee termination in finite time.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Cheat Sheet — All Invariant-Related Formulas at a Glance**

| Concept | Formal Statement | Domain of Use |
|---|---|---|
| Invariant definition | $\forall s \in \text{Reach}(M) : s \models P$ | Model checking |
| Inductive invariant (Init) | $\forall s \in S_0 : I(s)$ | Deductive proof |
| Inductive invariant (Cons) | $\forall s, s' : I(s) \land R(s, s') \Rightarrow I(s')$ | Deductive proof |
| LTL global form | $M \models \Box P$ | LTL model checking |
| CTL global form | $M, s_0 \models AG\, P$ | CTL model checking |
| Safety property | $\forall \pi \not\models \mathcal{P} : \exists k : \text{all extensions of } s_0 \ldots s_k \text{ violate } \mathcal{P}$ | Theory of properties |
| Bad prefix | A finite word $w$ s.t. every infinite extension $\overline{w}$ violates $\mathcal{P}$ | Büchi–Landweber theorem |
| Fixpoint of Post | $\text{Reach}(M) = \mu X.\, S_0 \cup \text{Post}(X)$ | Reachability analysis |
| Abstract soundness | $\alpha(\text{lfp}\, F) \sqsubseteq \text{lfp}\, F^{\#}$ | Abstract interpretation |
| Hoare triple (partial) | $\{I\}\,\text{stmt}\,\{I\}$ | Floyd–Hoare logic |
| Hoare triple (total) | $[I]\,\text{stmt}\,[I]$ | Total correctness |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler optimization:** Loop-invariant code motion depends on inferring that an expression evaluates to the same value at every iteration.
- **Static analysis tools:** Astrée, Polyspace, CBMC all report invariant facts at every program point to prove absence of runtime errors.
- **Hardware verification:** In Intel, AMD, and ARM processor verification, invariants ("the pipeline is never stalled for more than 5 cycles", "no two loads retire out-of-order") are the dominant property class verified.
- **Cyber-physical systems:** Invariant monitoring (Simplex architecture, runtime verification with STL invariants) guards safety-critical controllers in avionics and autonomous vehicles.
