# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Soundness of the Inductive Invariant Proof Rule

> **Claim:** If $I$ is inductive in $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$, then $\forall s \in \text{Reach}(M) : I(s)$.

### Step 1 — Define reachable states formally

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reach}(M) \;=\; \big\{\, s \in S \;:\; \exists\, n \geq 0, \exists\, s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_n = s \text{ with } s_0 \in S_0 \text{ and } \forall\, i < n : (s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R \,\big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the union of all states reachable in exactly $k$ steps for some $k \geq 0$.

### Step 2 — Set up the induction

Define the bounded-reachability predicate $R_k(s)$: "state $s$ is reachable from some initial state in at most $k$ steps." Formally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_0(s) &\;\iff\; s \in S_0 \\
R_{k+1}(s) &\;\iff\; R_k(s) \;\lor\; \exists\, s' : \big( R_k(s') \land (s', s) \in R \big)
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition, $s \in \text{Reach}(M) \iff \exists\, k \geq 0 : R_k(s)$.

### Step 3 — Base case (k = 0)

The **initiation clause** of the inductive invariant is:

$$\forall s \in S_0 : I(s)$$

This is exactly $\forall s : R_0(s) \Rightarrow I(s)$. ✓

### Step 4 — Inductive step (k → k+1)

Assume $\forall s : R_k(s) \Rightarrow I(s)$ (induction hypothesis). Take any $s$ with $R_{k+1}(s)$. Then either $R_k(s)$, in which case $I(s)$ follows directly, or there exists $s'$ with $R_k(s')$ and $(s', s) \in R$. By IH, $I(s')$. By the **consecution clause**:

$$\forall\, s, s' : I(s') \land (s', s) \in R \Rightarrow I(s)$$

we conclude $I(s)$. ✓

### Step 5 — Quantifier extension

Since for each $k$, $R_k \Rightarrow I$, and $\text{Reach}(M) = \bigcup_{k \geq 0} R_k$, we obtain:

$$\forall s \in \text{Reach}(M) : I(s) \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Reachability via Kleene Iteration (Model-Checking Algorithm)

> **Claim:** The sequence $X_0, X_1, X_2, \ldots$ defined by $X_0 = S_0$ and $X_{i+1} = X_i \cup \text{Post}(X_i)$ converges in $|S|$ iterations to $\text{Reach}(M)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_0 &= S_0 \\
X_1 &= S_0 \cup \text{Post}(S_0) \\
X_2 &= X_1 \cup \text{Post}(X_1) \\
\vdots \quad &= \quad \vdots \\
X_k &= \bigcup_{i=0}^{k} \text{Post}^i(S_0) \quad \text{(Post}^i \text{ denotes $i$-fold composition)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S$ is finite (size $N = \vert S \vert$) and the sequence is monotonically increasing ($X_i \subseteq X_{i+1}$), the sequence stabilizes by iteration $N$ at the latest, yielding $X_N = \text{Reach}(M)$. Termination is guaranteed; the algorithm runs in $O(N \cdot \vert R \vert)$ time using BFS/DFS.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Three Methods of Establishing an Invariant

| Method | Strength | Weakness | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductive proof** | Mechanically checkable; gives strongest guarantees | Requires invention of $I$ | Small to medium systems with good intuition |
| **Reachability computation** | Fully automatic; decidable for finite state | State-space explosion | Finite-state hardware protocols, small software |
| **Abstract interpretation** | Scalable; works on infinite-state programs | May produce false positives (over-approx.) | Large C/C++ codebases, embedded controllers |
| **Predicate abstraction + CEGAR** | Automatic refinement reduces false alarms | Refinement loops may not terminate | Boolean programs, SLAM/blast-style tools |
| **Craig interpolation** | Powerful automatic invariant generation | Limited to first-order theories | Bounded model checking + BMC unrolled proofs |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Mutual Exclusion Invariant

> **System:** Two processes $P_1, P_2$, each with a local flag $f_1, f_2 \in \{0, 1\}$ and a critical section bit $c_1, c_2 \in \{0, 1\}$.
> **Claimed Invariant:** $I \equiv \neg(c_1 \land c_2)$ (mutual exclusion: never both in critical section).

**Initiation check:** Initially $c_1 = c_2 = 0$, so $\neg(0 \land 0) = \text{true}$. ✓

**Consecution check:** Every transition either (a) changes $f_i$ without altering $c_j$, or (b) sets $c_i = 1$ only if $f_j = 0$ (the other process is not requesting entry). In case (a), $I$ holds trivially. In case (b), the precondition $f_j = 0$ plus the protocol's invariant $f_j = 0 \Rightarrow c_j = 0$ (an auxiliary invariant) ensures $c_j = 0$, hence $c_1 \land c_2 = \text{false}$. ✓

Therefore $I$ is an inductive invariant of the mutual-exclusion protocol, and mutual exclusion is verified for all reachable states. $\blacksquare$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Invariant Proof Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Model M = S, S0, R, L] --> B[Identify Candidate Invariant I]
    B --> C{I is Inductive?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply Initiation Clause]
    C -- No --> E[Invariant Strengthening: Find I-prime with I-prime implies I]
    D --> F[Apply Consecution Clause]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Both Clauses Hold?}
    G -- Yes --> H[By Path-Length Induction: I holds in Reach M]
    G -- No --> I[Refine I or Use Abstract Interpretation]
    I --> B
    H --> J[Output: M models Globally I]
    J --> K[Result: Invariant Verified for all Reachable States]
```

## 4.2 Invariant Generation & Verification Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Source Code or Transition System] --> S1[Parse to Control Flow Graph]
    S1 --> S2[Choose Abstract Domain: Intervals, Octagons, Polyhedra]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Abstract Transformer F-sharp]
    S3 --> S4[Solve Fixpoint Equation with Widening]
    S4 --> S5[Concretize: gamma of Abstract Fixpoint]
    S5 --> S6[Candidate Invariant I]
    S6 --> S7[Check I is Inductive]
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Verified Invariant: M models Globally I]
    S7 -->|No| S9[Refine Domain / Add Predicates]
    S9 --> S2
```

## 4.3 Relationship Between Invariants and the Safety Property Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Properties: omega-languages over infinite executions] --> S[Safety Properties: bad prefix exists]
    P --> L[Liveness Properties: every finite prefix is extendable to a good execution]
    S --> I[Invariants: bad state in the safety automaton]
    S --> GP[General Safety: complex bad-prefix regular language]
    I --> I1[State Invariant: Globally P]
    I --> I2[Transition Invariant: For all edges, condition holds]
    I1 --> I3[Inductive Invariant: Initiation plus Consecution]
    I1 --> I4[Non-Inductive Invariant: requires auxiliary I]
```

## 4.4 Counter-Example Guided Abstraction Refinement (CEGAR) Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Concrete System M] --> C2[Construct Abstract Model M-hat]
    C2 --> C3[Model Check: M-hat models Globally I]
    C3 -->|Yes| C4[Sound: M models Globally I: DONE]
    C3 -->|No| C5[Spurious Counterexample: path in M-hat not in M]
    C5 --> C6[Analyze Why Path is Spurious]
    C6 --> C7[Refine Abstraction: Add Predicates]
    C7 --> C2
```
