# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define an invariant in the context of model checking. How is it formally expressed using temporal logic?**

**Model Answer:**

An *invariant* is a property asserting that a state predicate $P$ holds at **every reachable state** of a transition system $M$. Formally, in LTL: $M \models \Box P$, meaning "globally, at every point along every execution path, $P$ is true." In CTL: $M, s_0 \models AG\, P$ (along all paths, globally $P$). The set of states satisfying $P$ forms a **safety set** — once an execution leaves it, the property is permanently violated. Invariants are the simplest, most decidable subclass of safety properties and are central to verification of hardware, protocols, and safety-critical software. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is an inductive invariant? State the two clauses required to prove a predicate $I$ is an inductive invariant of a transition system.**

**Model Answer:**

An inductive invariant $I$ is a state predicate that is preserved by system execution. The two required clauses are:

1. **Initiation (Base case):** $I$ holds in every initial state, i.e., $\forall s \in S_0 : I(s)$.
2. **Consecution (Inductive step):** $I$ is preserved by every transition, i.e., $\forall s, s' \in S : I(s) \land R(s, s') \Rightarrow I(s')$.

Together, by induction on path length, these guarantee that $I$ holds at **every reachable state**, establishing $I$ as an invariant. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** *Define safety properties formally. State and explain the Büchi–Landweber characterization theorem for safety properties. Show that every invariant is a safety property.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Consider the transition system $M$ with states $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, initial state $\{s_0\}$, and transitions $s_0 \to s_1$, $s_1 \to s_1$, $s_1 \to s_2$, $s_2 \to s_3$, $s_3 \to s_3$. Prove that $P \equiv (s \neq s_3)$ is an invariant of $M$.*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Formal definition of safety property:** A property $\mathcal{P} \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ over infinite words is a **safety property** if for every infinite word $w \notin \mathcal{P}$, there exists a finite prefix $\hat{w}$ of $w$ such that **no** infinite extension of $\hat{w}$ belongs to $\mathcal{P}$. The prefix $\hat{w}$ is called a *minimal bad prefix*.

**Büchi–Landweber theorem (1969):** *A property $\mathcal{P}$ is a safety property if and only if the set of its minimal bad prefixes is a regular language recognizable by a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) called the safety automaton $A_{\mathcal{P}}$.*

**Proof that invariants are safety properties:** Let $I = \Box P$ be an invariant. The bad prefixes are exactly those finite words whose last state violates $P$. The corresponding safety automaton has two states — a "good" state (accepting) and a "bad" sink state (entered on $\neg P$, rejecting thereafter). Since this automaton is a DFA recognizing a regular language, the invariant is a safety property by Büchi–Landweber. $\blacksquare$ **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $M = (\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}, \{s_0\}, R, L)$ with $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_1), (s_1, s_2), (s_2, s_3), (s_3, s_3)\}$. Predicate $P(s) \equiv (s \neq s_3)$.

**Method 1 — Inductive proof:**

*Initiation:* $s_0 \in S_0$ and $s_0 \neq s_3$, so $P(s_0) = \text{true}$. ✓

*Consecution:* For each transition $(s, s') \in R$, check $P(s) \Rightarrow P(s')$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(s_0, s_1) &: s_0 \neq s_3, \; s_1 \neq s_3 \;\; \checkmark \\
(s_1, s_1) &: s_1 \neq s_3, \; s_1 \neq s_3 \;\; \checkmark \\
(s_1, s_2) &: s_1 \neq s_3, \; s_2 \neq s_3 \;\; \checkmark \\
(s_2, s_3) &: s_2 \neq s_3, \; s_3 = s_3 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; P(s_2) = \text{true} \text{ but } P(s_3) = \text{false} \;\; \mathbf{\times}
\end{aligned}
$$

⚠️ **Consecution fails at $(s_2, s_3)$!** This means $P$ is **not inductive** as stated. However, $P$ may still be an invariant if $s_3$ is *not reachable* from $s_0$.

**Method 2 — Reachability proof:**

Compute the reachability fixpoint:
- $X_0 = \{s_0\}$
- $X_1 = \{s_0, s_1\}$ (via $s_0 \to s_1$)
- $X_2 = X_1 \cup \{s_2\}$ (via $s_1 \to s_2$)
- $X_3 = X_2 \cup \{s_3\}$ (via $s_2 \to s_3$)
- $X_4 = X_3$ (stable)

Thus $\text{Reach}(M) = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$.

**Verification of $P$:** Check $P(s)$ for each $s \in \text{Reach}(M)$: $P(s_3) = \text{false}$. Therefore $\exists s \in \text{Reach}(M) : \neg P(s)$, and **$P$ is NOT an invariant of $M$**.

**Correction:** The correct invariant is $P'(s) \equiv s \notin \{s_3\}$ — wait, the same predicate. Re-examination: with transition $s_2 \to s_3$ present, $s_3$ **is** reachable, so $P$ fails. If we **remove** the transition $s_2 \to s_3$, then $\text{Reach}(M) = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$ and $P$ becomes a valid invariant. **The corrected claim: $P$ is an invariant if and only if the edge $s_2 \to s_3$ is removed from $R$.** **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often stop at the *consecution* check without realizing that *failure* of consecution does not mean the predicate is not an invariant. Conversely, *passing* consecution only matters if initiation also passes. **The full sequence of operations — (1) verify initiation, (2) attempt consecution, (3) if consecution fails, fall back to reachability analysis — must be shown explicitly in your answer.** A common 2-mark loss comes from students reporting "$P$ is an invariant" without rigorously computing $\text{Reach}(M)$ when consecution fails.

### Question B [14 Marks, Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the method of generating invariants via abstract interpretation. Define the Galois connection and state the soundness theorem.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Apply the dataflow analysis framework to derive the invariant "variable `x` is always non-negative" for the following code:*
```
1.  if (x > 0) {
2.      y = x + 5;
3.  } else {
4.      y = 0;
5.  }
6.  z = y - 3;
7.  assert(z >= 0);
```

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Abstract Interpretation (Cousot & Cousot, 1977):** A general theory of *sound approximation* of program semantics. The concrete semantics is computed in the *concrete domain* $\langle \wp(S), \subseteq \rangle$ (power-set lattice), while the analysis is performed in a simpler *abstract domain* $\langle \mathcal{A}, \sqsubseteq \rangle$ (e.g., intervals, octagons, polyhedra, or even $\{ \top, \bot\}$).

**Galois Connection:** Two monotone functions $\alpha : \wp(S) \to \mathcal{A}$ (abstraction) and $\gamma : \mathcal{A} \to \wp(S)$ (concretization) form a Galois connection $\wp(S) \xleftrightarrow[\alpha]{\gamma} \mathcal{A}$ if and only if for all $X \in \wp(S)$ and $a \in \mathcal{A}$:

$$\alpha(X) \sqsubseteq a \iff X \subseteq \gamma(a)$$

**Soundness theorem:** If $F : \wp(S) \to \wp(S)$ is a concrete transformer and $F^{\#} : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{A}$ is a sound abstract transformer satisfying $\alpha \circ F \sqsubseteq F^{\#} \circ \alpha$, then the least fixpoint satisfies:

$$\alpha(\text{lfp}\, F) \sqsubseteq \text{lfp}\, F^{\#}$$

The right-hand side is computable (often with a widening operator $\nabla$ to enforce termination in finite time) and yields a **sound over-approximation** of the invariant set. The result is an invariant: every state actually reachable in the concrete system is contained in $\gamma(\text{lfp}\, F^{\#})$, so any safety property entailed by this set is verified.

**Engineering utility:** Tools like Astrée (embedded avionics C code), Polyspace (MATLAB/Simulink), and Infer (Facebook) implement abstract interpretation over carefully chosen numeric domains to verify absence of runtime errors in millions of lines of code. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Use the **interval domain** $\mathcal{A} = \{[a, b] : a \leq b, a \in \mathbb{Z} \cup \{-\infty\}, b \in \mathbb{Z} \cup \{+\infty\}\}$.

**Assume:** Before line 1, abstract state is $A_0 = [x_0^{\min}, x_0^{\max}]$ for `x`, and $A_0(y) = \top = [-\infty, +\infty]$.

**Line 1 (branch on `x > 0`):**
- *Then-branch (line 2):* $x > 0 \Rightarrow x \in [1, +\infty]$. So $A_1^{then}(x) = [1, +\infty]$.
  - Line 2: $y = x + 5 \Rightarrow y \in [6, +\infty]$. $A_2^{then}(y) = [6, +\infty]$, $A_2^{then}(x) = [1, +\infty]$.
- *Else-branch (line 4):* $x \leq 0$. $A_1^{else}(x) = [-\infty, 0]$.
  - Line 4: $y = 0 \Rightarrow y \in [0, 0]$. $A_2^{else}(y) = [0, 0]$, $A_2^{else}(x) = [-\infty, 0]$.

**Line 5 (join, control-flow merge):**
$$A_5(y) = A_2^{then}(y) \sqcup A_2^{else}(y) = [6, +\infty] \sqcup [0, 0] = [0, +\infty]$$
$$A_5(x) = [1, +\infty] \sqcup [-\infty, 0] = [-\infty, +\infty]$$

**Line 6:** $z = y - 3 \Rightarrow z \in [0 - 3, +\infty - 3] = [-3, +\infty]$.

**Line 7 (assertion):** We need $z \geq 0$, but $A_6(z) = [-3, +\infty]$ includes negative values.

**Conclusion:** The invariant analysis **cannot prove** $z \geq 0$ at line 7. The abstract domain is too coarse: it loses the correlation between the two branches. Using a **relational domain** (octagon or polyhedron) would preserve $y \geq 0$ as an invariant and yield $z \geq -3$ but would not tighten the lower bound. The tool reports a **potential false positive**, requiring manual review or domain refinement. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often forget the **join (⊔) at control-flow merge points** and write $[6, +\infty]$ for $y$ as the result at line 5. This loses the contribution of the `else` branch and is a 2-mark deduction. Also, **never forget the widening step** in iterative analysis — the examiner expects mention of either explicit widening or an explicit acknowledgement of termination, even for non-loop programs.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Invariant** = state predicate true at every reachable state; written $M \models \Box P$ (LTL) or $M, s_0 \models AG\, P$ (CTL).
- **Inductive invariant** must satisfy two clauses: (1) *Initiation* — true in all initial states; (2) *Consecution* — preserved by every transition.
- **Soundness** of the inductive proof rule follows by Noetherian induction on path length from initial states.
- Every invariant is a safety property, but not every safety property is an invariant.
- **Büchi–Landweber theorem:** A property is a safety property iff its set of minimal bad prefixes is regular (recognized by a DFA — the safety automaton).
- The **reachable state set** is the least fixpoint: $\text{Reach}(M) = \mu X.\, S_0 \cup \text{Post}(X)$, computable in $O(N \cdot \vert R \vert)$ time for $|S| = N$.
- **Abstract interpretation** uses a Galois connection $(\alpha, \gamma)$ to obtain a sound over-approximation $\alpha(\text{lfp}\, F) \sqsubseteq \text{lfp}\, F^{\#}$.
- **Widening operators** guarantee termination of abstract fixpoint iteration on infinite lattices.
- **Dataflow analyses** (reaching-definitions, available-expressions, live-variables) are concrete instances of invariant generation over specific lattices.
- **Non-inductive invariants** require an auxiliary inductive strengthening $I$ with $I \Rightarrow J$ — the CEGAR loop automates this search.
- **Common board-exam traps:** confusing *inductive* with *invariant*; forgetting the join at merge points; omitting the consecution check; reporting false positives as confirmed bugs.
- **Engineering applications:** hardware pipeline verification, embedded controller safety, compiler optimization, runtime verification, and avionics software (DO-178C compliance).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safety Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the formal framework of **Automated Verification** and **Model Checking**, a system execution (also called a computation, trace, or ω-word) is modeled as an infinite sequence of states:

$$\sigma = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow s_i \rightarrow \ldots$$

A **Safety Property** is a temporal logic specification that asserts: **"Something bad never happens during system execution."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):**
> A property $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ (where $\Sigma^\omega$ is the set of all infinite words over alphabet $\Sigma$) is called a **safety property** if and only if for every infinite word $\sigma \notin P$ (a "bad" computation), there exists a *finite prefix* $u$ of $\sigma$ such that **no extension of $u$ can ever satisfy $P$**. Such a prefix $u$ is termed a **bad prefix** (or **minimal bad prefix**) of $P$.

In the LTL (Linear Temporal Logic) notation preferred by KTU boards, every safety property can be written in the canonical form:

$$P \;\equiv\; \Box\,\phi \quad \text{(i.e., } G\,\phi \text{ — Globally } \phi\text{)}$$

where $\phi$ is a state formula (a purely propositional assertion evaluated on a single state).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Traffic Light Analogy**
> Imagine a pedestrian crossing a road. A **safety property** is the rule: *"The pedestrian is never hit by a car."* 
> - The moment a car and a pedestrian occupy the crossing simultaneously, the rule has been **violated** — and importantly, we can detect the violation by looking only at a *finite* snapshot of history (a finite prefix of the execution).
> - Once a violation is recorded, **no continuation of the universe can ever "undo" the accident**. This irreversibility is the defining fingerprint of safety.
> - In contrast, a *liveness* property like "the traffic light eventually turns green" cannot be refuted by any finite prefix — we always have to keep waiting.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchors

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Trace / ω-word** | Infinite sequence $\sigma = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ over states of the Kripke structure $M$ |
| **Prefix (cl(σ))** | The set of all finite prefixes of an infinite trace |
| **Bad Prefix** | A finite word $u$ such that $\forall \beta \in \Sigma^\omega : u\beta \notin P$ |
| **Invariance** | Property of the form $G\,\phi$ where $\phi$ is a state predicate |
| **Canonical Form** | The syntactic shape $\Box \phi$ that every safety LTL formula can be rewritten into |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Notation

- The set of **bad prefixes** of a property $P$ is denoted $\text{BadPref}(P)$.
- The **closure** of $P$ is: $\text{cl}(P) = P \cup \text{Pref}(P)$.
- The shorthand for "and so on" (infinite extension) is $\Sigma^\omega$.
- The shorthand for "any finite extension" is $\Sigma^*$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition — The Three Logical Pillars

A safety property rests on three independent pillars that examiners love to test:

1. **Pillar 1 — Refutability by a Finite Prefix**  
   If a system violates a safety property, the violation is *witnessed* by a finite observable prefix. Formally, a counterexample to a safety property is *always finite*.

2. **Pillar 2 — Prefix-Closure under Good Words**  
   If $\sigma \models P$ and $u$ is a finite prefix of $\sigma$, then $u$ is **not** a bad prefix. Equivalently, $\text{Pref}(P) \subseteq \text{Pref}(\Sigma^\omega) \setminus \text{BadPref}(P)$.

3. **Pillar 3 — Limit-Closure**  
   The property $P$ is **limit-closed**: $P = \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$, meaning $P$ contains exactly those infinite words whose every finite prefix is good.

## 2.2 Classification of Common LTL Safety Patterns

| LTL Formula | Natural-Language Reading | Safety Class? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $G\,p$ | $p$ is true in every reachable state | ✓ Pure safety (invariant) |
| $G(p \rightarrow X\,q)$ | Whenever $p$ holds, $q$ must hold in the next state | ✓ Safety (bounded horizon) |
| $G(p \rightarrow (\neg q \,U\, r))$ | After $p$, $q$ is forbidden until $r$ occurs | ✓ Safety |
| $G\,\neg(p \wedge q)$ | Mutual exclusion between $p$ and $q$ | ✓ Safety (canonical invariant) |
| $F\,G\,p$ | Eventually $p$ holds forever | ✗ **Liveness** (no finite witness of failure) |
| $G\,F\,p$ | $p$ occurs infinitely often | ✗ **Liveness** |
| $F\,p$ | $p$ will eventually occur | ✗ **Liveness** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Heuristic:** If the LTL formula has the **shape $G\,\psi$ or $\Box \psi$ at its outermost operator**, it is a **safety property**. If the outermost operator is $F$ (eventually) without an enclosing $G$, it is *not* a safety property.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Critical, No Pipes Inside Cells)

| # | Concept | Mathematical Formulation | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| F1 | Property Domain | $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ | Set of all infinite words |
| F2 | Bad Prefix Definition | $u \in \text{BadPref}(P) \iff \forall \beta \in \Sigma^\omega : u\beta \notin P$ | Finite counterexample |
| F3 | Safety Property Axiom | $P \text{ is safe} \iff \forall \sigma \notin P : \exists u \sqsubseteq_{\text{fin}} \sigma : u \in \text{BadPref}(P)$ | The defining equivalence |
| F4 | Closure Operator | $\text{cl}(P) = P \cup \text{Pref}(P)$ | Used to detect safety |
| F5 | Limit Closure Test | $P = \lim\bigl(\text{Pref}(P)\bigr)$ | $P$ contains all limit points of its prefixes |
| F6 | Canonical LTL Form | $P \equiv \Box \phi$ where $\phi$ is state-formula | Equivalent normal form |
| F7 | Safety Monus (Set Diff.) | $P \,\setminus\, Q = \lim\bigl(\text{Pref}(P) \setminus \text{Pref}(Q)\bigr)$ | Difference of two safety properties |
| F8 | Bad Prefixes Set | $\text{BadPref}(P) = \Sigma^+ \setminus \text{Pref}(P)$ | Complement of good prefixes |
| F9 | Inductive Invariant | $\text{Inv} = \{\sigma \in \Sigma^\omega : \forall i \ge 0, \sigma_i \models \phi\}$ | The set of "always-$\phi$" traces |
| F10 | Verification Equation | $M \models \Box \phi \iff \text{Init}(M) \subseteq \text{Inv}_\phi(M)$ | Model satisfies iff all reachable states obey $\phi$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hardware Verification (RTL/Netlist):** Verifying that no two control signals are ever simultaneously asserted — encoded as $G(\neg(\text{req} \wedge \text{ack}_{\text{dup}}))$.
- **Embedded & Automotive (ISO 26262):** Proving that a brake-by-wire controller never commands full throttle and full brake at the same instant.
- **Protocol Design:** Showing that in a mutual-exclusion protocol (e.g., Peterson's, Bakery), no two processes are in the critical section together — formal invariant: $G(\neg(\text{in\_CS}_1 \wedge \text{in\_CS}_2))$.
- **Compiler Correctness:** Proving that optimized code never accesses memory out of bounds — encoded as a safety invariant over the memory model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Bad-Prefix Characterization Theorem

**Theorem (Sistla, 1994 — KTU-Standard):**  
A property $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is a safety property if and only if for every infinite word $\sigma$ that is *not* in $P$, there exists a *finite* prefix $u$ of $\sigma$ such that no infinite continuation of $u$ belongs to $P$.

### Proof (Forward Direction: $\Rightarrow$)

Assume $P$ is a safety property.

**Step 1 — Choose a violating word.**  
Let $\sigma \in \Sigma^\omega$ be an arbitrary infinite word such that $\sigma \notin P$.

**Step 2 — Apply the safety definition.**  
By the safety property definition given in §1.1, there must exist a finite prefix $u$ of $\sigma$ such that:

$$\forall \beta \in \Sigma^\omega : u \cdot \beta \notin P$$

**Step 3 — Declare $u$ a bad prefix.**  
This $u$ is, by construction, a member of $\text{BadPref}(P)$. The witness is finite, completing the forward direction. $\blacksquare$

### Proof (Reverse Direction: $\Leftarrow$)

Assume that for every $\sigma \notin P$, a finite bad prefix exists.

**Step 1 — Construct the closure.**  
Define $\text{cl}(P) = P \cup \text{Pref}(P)$, the set of all words that are either in $P$ or finite prefixes of some word in $P$.

**Step 2 — Show $\text{cl}(P) = \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$.**  
A word $\sigma$ is in the limit of $\text{Pref}(P)$ if and only if every finite prefix of $\sigma$ is in $\text{Pref}(P)$, which by contrapositive means no finite prefix is in $\text{BadPref}(P)$. By our assumption, this implies $\sigma \in P$. Hence:

$$\lim\bigl(\text{Pref}(P)\bigr) \subseteq P$$

The reverse inclusion is trivial (every $\sigma \in P$ has all its prefixes in $\text{Pref}(P)$). Therefore:

$$P = \lim\bigl(\text{Pref}(P)\bigr) = \text{cl}(P)$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the safety property.**  
Since $P$ is the limit of its prefix set, any violation is detectable by a finite witness, confirming the safety property. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: How to Test Whether a Given LTL Formula is a Safety Property

**Algorithm (KTU-Examiner Method):**

**Step 1.** Identify the **outermost temporal operator** of the LTL formula $\varphi$.

**Step 2.** Check the syntactic shape:

$$\text{Safety}(\varphi) \iff \varphi \equiv G\,\psi \;\text{ for some state formula } \psi$$

**Step 3.** Verify using the semantic test:

$$\forall \sigma \in \Sigma^\omega : \sigma \not\models \varphi \implies \exists n \ge 0 : \forall \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : \sigma_{\le n} \cdot \tau \not\models \varphi$$

where $\sigma_{\le n}$ denotes the finite prefix of length $n+1$.

**Step 4.** If the formula fails Step 3 (e.g., $F\,p$), it is a **liveness** property and is **not** a safety property.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Safety vs. Liveness Classification

| Property Class | Witness of Violation | Typical LTL Shape | Bounded Check? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Safety** | Finite prefix | $\Box \phi$ (outermost $G$) | Yes — model checking terminates on counterexample |
| **Liveness** | No finite witness; needs infinite horizon | $\Diamond \phi$, $\Box \Diamond \phi$ | No — requires Büchi-cycle search |
| **Persistence** (sub-liveness) | After some point, property holds forever | $\Diamond \Box \phi$ | Hybrid |
| **Recurrence** (sub-safety) | Property holds infinitely often | $\Box \Diamond \phi$ | Hybrid |

## 3.4 Derivation: Construction of the Bad-Prefix Automaton

**Step 1 — Input:** A safety LTL formula $G\,\phi$.

**Step 2 — Translation:** Convert the negated formula $\neg G\,\phi \equiv F\,\neg\phi$ into a **Büchi automaton** $\mathcal{B}_{\neg\varphi}$ using standard tableau-based LTL-to-Büchi construction.

**Step 3 — Complement:** Take the complement $\overline{\mathcal{B}_{\neg\varphi}}$ to obtain a *good-prefix* automaton recognizing $\text{Pref}(P)$.

**Step 4 — Bad-Prefix Automaton:** Complement once more to get a **deterministic finite automaton (DFA)** $\mathcal{A}_{\text{bad}}$ that accepts exactly $\text{BadPref}(P)$.

**Step 5 — Verification:** The system $M$ satisfies $G\,\phi$ if and only if $L(M) \cap L(\mathcal{A}_{\text{bad}}) = \emptyset$, i.e., no execution of $M$ drives the system into a state where the bad-prefix DFA accepts.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Anatomy of a Safety Property

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Infinite Trace sigma in Sigma^omega] --> B{Does sigma satisfy the property P?}
    B -- Yes --> C[GOOD INFINITE WORD: sigma is in P]
    B -- No --> D[BAD INFINITE WORD: sigma not in P]
    D --> E[Extract finite prefix u of sigma]
    E --> F{Is u a bad prefix?}
    F -- Yes, and ALL extensions fail --> G[u is in BadPref of P]
    F -- No, some extension succeeds --> H[u is just a temporary violation, not a witness]
    G --> I[CONCLUSION: P is a SAFETY PROPERTY]
    H --> J[CONCLUSION: P is NOT a safety property]
    C --> K[CONCLUSION: sigma satisfies P - no violation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Verification Pipeline for a Safety Property

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Kripke Structure M] --> B[Build Reachable State Space]
    B --> C[Apply Invariant Checker for phi]
    C --> D{All reachable states satisfy phi?}
    D -- Yes --> E[VERIFIED: M satisfies G phi]
    D -- No --> F[Produce Counterexample Trace]
    F --> G[Identify Minimal Bad Prefix u]
    G --> H[Return Finite Witness to User]
    E --> I[Report SUCCESS to Examiner]
    H --> J[Report VIOLATION with Diagnostic Path]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Classification Tree — LTL Property Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[LTL Property phi] --> Op{Outermost Operator}
    Op -->|G globally| SafeBox[Box-Shape Candidate]
    Op -->|F eventually| LiveDiam[Diamond-Shape Candidate]
    Op -->|G F infinitely often| Recur[Recurrence - Liveness]
    Op -->|F G eventually forever| Persist[Persistence - Liveness]
    SafeBox --> SafeCheck{Inner formula psi is state-local?}
    SafeCheck -- Yes --> PureSafe[PURE SAFETY PROPERTY]
    SafeCheck -- No --> MaybeSafe[Check via bad-prefix test]
    LiveDiam --> PureLive[PURE LIVENESS PROPERTY]
    Recur --> PureLive
    Persist --> PureLive
    MaybeSafe --> Safe2{Finite witness exists?}
    Safe2 -- Yes --> PureSafe
    Safe2 -- No --> PureLive
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Safety Model Checker

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputA[System Model: Kripke Structure M] --> Front[Frontend Parser]
    InputB[Safety Formula: G phi in LTL] --> Front
    Front --> Neg[Negation Module: convert G phi to F not-phi]
    Neg --> Tableau[Tableau Builder: LTL to Buchi]
    Tableau --> Comp[Complement Buchi to DFA]
    Comp --> Prod[Product Construction: M cross A_bad]
    Prod --> Reach[Reachability Analysis on Product]
    Reach -->|Empty intersection| ResultA[VERIFIED: System Safe]
    Reach -->|Non-empty intersection| ResultB[UNSAFE: Counterexample Path Found]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a safety property in the context of linear-time temporal logic. State the canonical syntactic form of a safety property.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **safety property** is a class of linear-time temporal specifications whose violations are always witnessed by a **finite prefix** of the execution trace. In other words, if a system violates a safety property, there exists a finite sequence of states that *already* constitutes a counterexample — no future behavior can "undo" the violation.

The canonical syntactic form of any safety property in LTL is:

$$P \;\equiv\; \Box\,\phi \;\;\text{(equivalently written as } G\,\phi\text{)}$$

where $\phi$ is a **state formula** — a purely propositional assertion evaluated on a single state with no temporal operators.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for finiteness-of-witness clause, 1 mark for canonical form.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a "bad prefix" of a safety property $P$? Show that the property $G(p \rightarrow X\,q)$ has a bad prefix of length 2 when $p$ holds initially and $q$ does not hold in the next state.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **bad prefix** of a property $P$ is a finite word $u \in \Sigma^+$ such that **no infinite extension** of $u$ can satisfy $P$:

$$u \in \text{BadPref}(P) \iff \forall \beta \in \Sigma^\omega : u \cdot \beta \notin P$$

For the property $G(p \rightarrow X\,q)$:

- Suppose the initial state satisfies $p$ (i.e., $p = \text{true}$) and the next state satisfies $\neg q$.
- The finite prefix of length 2 is $u = \langle p \wedge \neg q, \neg q \rangle$ (state 0 has $p$ true, state 1 has $q$ false).
- Any infinite continuation $\beta$ will have $u\beta$ starting with a state where $p$ holds but $X\,q$ fails at position 1, which is a direct violation of $G(p \rightarrow X\,q)$.
- Therefore $u$ is a bad prefix of length 2.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark construction, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

## **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert**
> Students frequently lose **2 to 3 marks** on safety-property questions by:
> 1. **Confusing safety with liveness** — they classify $F\,G\,p$ (a persistence/liveness property) as safety. Remember: *outermost* $F$ ⟹ liveness, *outermost* $G$ ⟹ safety.
> 2. **Skipping the finite-witness clause** in definitions — a definition of safety without the phrase "finite prefix" or "finite counterexample" is **incomplete** and will cost at least 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the canonical form** — KTU boards expect the explicit $\Box \phi$ (or $G \phi$) rewriting.
> 4. **Mixing up bad prefixes with violating words** — a bad prefix is **finite**; a violating word is **infinite**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]**  
**Formally define a safety property in terms of the "bad prefix" characterization. Prove the equivalence between this definition and the limit-closure characterization $P = \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$.**

**(b) [7 Marks]**  
**Consider two safety properties $P_1 \equiv G(p \rightarrow X\,q)$ and $P_2 \equiv G\,\neg(p \wedge q)$. Construct the bad-prefix automaton for the conjunction $P_1 \wedge P_2$ and demonstrate that the system violating $P_1$ in step (a) also violates the conjunction.**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**  
A property $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is a **safety property** if and only if for every $\sigma \notin P$, there exists a finite prefix $u \sqsubseteq \sigma$ such that:

$$u \in \text{BadPref}(P) \iff \forall \beta \in \Sigma^\omega : u \cdot \beta \notin P$$

**Limit-Closure Definition (1 mark):**  
The **limit** of a set of finite words $A \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is the set of all infinite words whose every finite prefix belongs to $A$:

$$\lim(A) = \{\sigma \in \Sigma^\omega : \text{Pref}(\sigma) \subseteq A\}$$

**Proof of Equivalence (4 marks):**

$(\Rightarrow)$ Assume $P$ is safe. We show $P \subseteq \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$ and $\lim(\text{Pref}(P)) \subseteq P$.

- $P \subseteq \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$: For any $\sigma \in P$, by definition every prefix of $\sigma$ is in $\text{Pref}(P)$, so $\sigma \in \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$.
- $\lim(\text{Pref}(P)) \subseteq P$: Let $\sigma \in \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$. Suppose for contradiction that $\sigma \notin P$. By the safety property, there exists a finite bad prefix $u \sqsubseteq \sigma$. But $u \in \text{Pref}(P)$ would require some $\sigma' \in P$ with $u \sqsubseteq \sigma'$, contradicting $u \in \text{BadPref}(P)$. Hence $\sigma \in P$.

$(\Leftarrow)$ Assume $P = \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$. Let $\sigma \notin P$. Since $\sigma \notin \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$, there exists a finite prefix $u \sqsubseteq \sigma$ such that $u \notin \text{Pref}(P)$. By definition, no continuation of $u$ is in $P$, so $u \in \text{BadPref}(P)$. Thus $P$ is a safety property. $\blacksquare$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Bad-Prefix Set for $P_1$ (1.5 marks):**  
$\text{BadPref}(P_1)$ consists of all finite words where some position $i$ has $p = \text{true}$ and position $i+1$ has $q = \text{false}$.

**Bad-Prefix Set for $P_2$ (1.5 marks):**  
$\text{BadPref}(P_2)$ consists of all finite words where some single state has both $p$ and $q$ true.

**Conjunction $P_1 \wedge P_2$ (2 marks):**  
A bad prefix for $P_1 \wedge P_2$ is any finite word that is bad for *either* $P_1$ or $P_2$:

$$\text{BadPref}(P_1 \wedge P_2) = \text{BadPref}(P_1) \cup \text{BadPref}(P_2)$$

The corresponding **bad-prefix automaton** $\mathcal{A}_{\text{bad}}$ has states tracking whether $p$ is asserted in the current state and whether the next state violates $q$ (for $P_1$) or whether a state contains both $p$ and $q$ (for $P_2$). The accepting states are those in which either condition is detected.

**Demonstration of Violation (2 marks):**  
Consider the trace $u = \langle p \wedge \neg q, q \rangle$. This trace satisfies $P_2$ (no state has both $p$ and $q$) but **violates $P_1$** at the first transition ($p$ holds in state 0, but $q$ does not hold in state 1). Hence $u$ is a bad prefix for $P_1 \wedge P_2$, and the conjunction is violated. The system is **unsafe**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]**  
**Explain with suitable examples the classification of LTL properties into safety, liveness, and persistence classes. Provide a procedure (with justification) to determine whether a given LTL formula represents a safety property.**

**(b) [7 Marks]**  
**For a mutual-exclusion system with two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$, express the safety requirement that no two processes are in the critical section simultaneously as an LTL formula. Then, using the bad-prefix method, describe how a model checker (e.g., SPIN/NuSMV) would refute the property if the system has a faulty interleaving.**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Classification (3 marks):**

- **Safety Properties:** Assert that *something bad never happens*. Violations are witnessed by finite prefixes. Canonical form: $G \phi$.  
  *Example:* $G(\neg(\text{cs}_1 \wedge \text{cs}_2))$ — mutual exclusion invariant.

- **Liveness Properties:** Assert that *something good eventually happens*. No finite prefix can refute them. Canonical form: $F \phi$ or $G F \phi$.  
  *Example:* $F(\text{request} \rightarrow \text{grant})$ — every request is eventually granted.

- **Persistence Properties:** Assert that *something holds continuously from some point on*. Canonical form: $F G \phi$.  
  *Example:* $F G \neg \text{error}$ — errors eventually cease forever.

**Procedure to Determine Safety (4 marks):**

1. **Parse the formula** and identify the outermost temporal operator.
2. **Syntactic test:** If the outermost operator is $G$ (or $\Box$), and the inner formula is a state predicate (no nested temporal operators referring to the *future*), the formula is a safety property.
3. **Semantic test (necessary, not sufficient):** Check that the formula's negation $\neg \varphi$ is recognizable by a *finite automaton* (a property is safety iff its negation is co-Büchi or finite-state).
4. **Bad-prefix construction:** Convert $\varphi$ to a Büchi automaton, take the complement to get a DFA over finite words, and verify that this DFA recognizes exactly the bad prefixes.

Justification: The procedure is sound because the limit-closure theorem (§3.1) shows that safety properties are exactly those whose violations have finite witnesses.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**LTL Encoding of Mutual Exclusion (2 marks):**

Let $\text{cs}_1$ denote "process $P_1$ is in its critical section" and $\text{cs}_2$ similarly for $P_2$. The safety requirement is:

$$\varphi_{\text{ME}} \;\equiv\; G\,\neg(\text{cs}_1 \wedge \text{cs}_2)$$

This is a *canonical safety property* because it has the form $\Box \phi$ with $\phi = \neg(\text{cs}_1 \wedge \text{cs}_2)$ being a state formula.

**Bad-Prefix Construction (2.5 marks):**

The bad-prefix DFA $\mathcal{A}_{\text{bad}}$ has a single accepting trap state $q_{\text{bad}}$ with a self-loop on every input. There is a transition from the initial state $q_0$ to $q_{\text{bad}}$ labeled with the input symbol $\langle \text{cs}_1 = \text{true}, \text{cs}_2 = \text{true} \rangle$ (i.e., the state where both processes are simultaneously in the critical section). All other symbols loop at $q_0$.

**Refutation by Model Checker (2.5 marks):**

1. The model checker constructs the product automaton $M \times \mathcal{A}_{\text{bad}}$.
2. It performs a **reachability analysis** to determine if the accepting state $q_{\text{bad}}$ of $\mathcal{A}_{\text{bad}}$ is reachable from the initial state of $M$ via the product's transition relation.
3. If $q_{\text{bad}}$ is reachable, the model checker has found a finite counterexample — a trace $s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_n$ where $s_n$ has both $\text{cs}_1$ and $\text{cs}_2$ true. This trace is a **bad prefix**, and the model checker returns it as the diagnostic output.
4. The engineer inspects the faulty interleaving (e.g., a missing semaphore acquisition in the transition from $s_{n-1}$ to $s_n$) and patches the system.

This illustrates the **decisive advantage** of safety properties: their counterexamples are *finite* and *easily inspected* by humans.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Print and Revise Before Exam**

- ✅ **Safety = "Bad things never happen."** Always finite witness of violation.
- ✅ Canonical LTL shape: $\Box \phi$ or $G \phi$ with $\phi$ being a **state formula**.
- ✅ **Bad prefix** = finite word $u$ such that no continuation can ever satisfy the property.
- ✅ **Theorem (limit-closure):** $P$ is safe $\iff P = \lim(\text{Pref}(P))$.
- ✅ **Bad-prefix set:** $\text{BadPref}(P) = \Sigma^+ \setminus \text{Pref}(P)$.
- ✅ **Counterexamples to safety are always finite** — this is what makes model checking decidable and efficient for safety.
- ✅ **Common safety patterns:** mutual exclusion ($G \neg(p \wedge q)$), type safety, deadlock freedom (as a *negative* invariant), access control.
- ✅ **Common safety *non*-examples:** $F p$, $F G p$, $G F p$ — these are liveness/persistence.
- ✅ **Verification recipe:** Convert $G \phi$ to DFA over bad prefixes; check $L(M) \cap L(\mathcal{A}_{\text{bad}}) = \emptyset$.
- ✅ **Closure operator:** $\text{cl}(P) = P \cup \text{Pref}(P)$.
- ✅ **Safety monus:** Difference of two safety properties is again a safety property.
- ✅ **Tools:** SPIN (Promela), NuSMV, CBMC — all natively support safety-property checking via reachability analysis.
- ✅ **Industrial relevance:** Hardware verification, automotive (ISO 26262), avionics (DO-178C), security protocols.
- ✅ **Board-exam trap:** Always state the **finite-witness** clause explicitly — partial answers get partial marks.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** "*G*ood things *G*uaranteed forever = *G*lobal = *S*afety" — outermost $G$ → safety.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trace Equivalence and Safety Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Trace Equivalence and Safety Properties # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Trace Equivalence — Formal Definition In the theory of concurrent and reactive systems, a **trace** (also called a *computation sequence* or *execution sequence*) of a labelled transition system (LTS) is a finite or infinite sequence of observable actions that the system can perform starting from its initial state. Formally, for an LTS $\mathcal{M} = (S, Act, \rightarrow, s_0, AP, L)$: $$\text{Traces}(s) = \{\, \sigma \in Act^{*} \cup Act^{\omega} \mid s \overset{\sigma}{\Longrightarrow} \,\}$$ where $s \overset{\sigma}{\Longrightarrow}$ denotes that there exists an execution from state $s$ whose sequence of labels along the path is exactly $\sigma$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trace Equivalence (Definition):** Two LTSs $\mathcal{M}_1$ and $\mathcal{M}_2$ are **trace equivalent**, written $\mathcal{M}_1 \approx_t \mathcal{M}_2$, if and only if > $$\text{Traces}(\mathcal{M}_1) = \text{Traces}(\mathcal{M}_2)$$ > i.e., they generate **exactly the same set of observable action sequences**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two coffee vending machines placed side by side. A **trace** is the *receipt* printed by the machine — it records only the user-visible events: `INSERT_COIN`, `SELECT_COFFEE`, `DISPENSE`, `REFUND`. The internal micro-controllers, voltage fluctuations, and relay clicks are *not* recorded. Two machines are **trace equivalent** if, no matter what sequence of buttons a user presses and observes, the *receipts* they could possibly obtain from either machine are *identical as sets*. Notice that this does **not** require the machines to be internally identical — one might use a spring, the other a solenoid, but if a customer cannot tell the difference in what they observe, the machines are trace equivalent. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Trace equivalence is the most *liberal* (coarsest) behavioural equivalence. It is strictly weaker than **strong bisimulation** but coarser than many branching-time equivalences. It forms the semantic backbone of **linear-time** logics like LTL. --- ## 1.2 Safety Properties — Formal Definition A **safety property** is a property that, if violated, can be detected by examining a *finite prefix* of an execution. Equivalently, a property $P$ is a safety property iff: > *Whenever an execution $\pi$ violates $P$, there exists a finite prefix $\sigma$ of $\pi$ such that every extension of $\sigma$ also violates $P$.* The minimal such "bad" prefix is called a **bad prefix** (or *forbidden* prefix) of $P$. Formally, $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ is a safety property if and only if it is **prefix-closed** in the following sense: $$\forall \pi \in \Sigma^{\omega} : \big( \pi \notin P \;\Rightarrow\; \exists n \geq 0 : \forall \rho \in \Sigma^{\omega} : (\pi[0..n])\rho \notin P \big)$$ ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **safety property** as a *traffic law*: *"A red light must never be crossed."* The moment a driver crosses a red light, the violation is complete — you don't need to wait until the end of the journey to know that a violation occurred. The **finite bad prefix** is the exact moment the car passed the white line on red. Contrast this with a **liveness property** like *"Eventually the train arrives"*. You can never conclude from a finite observation that the train *will never* arrive — it might still be on its way. Liveness violations are *inherently infinite*; safety violations are *finitely detectable*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Alpern–Schneider Theorem (1985):** Every property $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ can be uniquely decomposed as the intersection of a safety property and a liveness property. This is the foundational result of property classification in model checking and is **directly examinable** under KTU's outcome CO1. --- ## 1.3 Why These Two Concepts Are Studied Together Trace equivalence and safety properties are the two pillars of **linear-time** verification: - **Trace equivalence** gives the *semantics* of when two systems "look the same from outside". - **Safety properties** give the *specification language* for what a system "must never do". Together they form the verification question: *"Does every trace of System $M$ satisfy the safety property $P$?"* — which is exactly the model checking problem for invariant-like specifications. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Equivalences Trace equivalence sits at a specific point in the spectrum of behavioural equivalences. The **strictness ordering** from finest to coarsest is: $$\text{Strong Bisimulation} \;\sqsubset\; \text{Weak Bisimulation} \;\sqsubset\; \text{Trace Equivalence} \;\sqsubset\; \text{Testing Equivalence}$$ Logic correspondence: - Strong bisimulation $\equiv$ **CTL\*** (branching-time) - Trace equivalence $\equiv$ **LTL** (linear-time) > [!NOTE] > **Key consequence:** If $M_1 \sim M_2$ (bisimilar), then $M_1 \approx_t M_2$ (trace equivalent). The converse is **false**. --- ## 2.2 Variants of Trace Equivalence ### (a) Strong Trace Equivalence Internal action $\tau$ is treated as observable. Two systems are strongly trace equivalent iff they have identical sequences of labels including $\tau$. ### (b) Weak Trace Equivalence Internal $\tau$-transitions are *abstracted away*. We define the **weak transition relation**: $$s \overset{a}{\Longrightarrow} s' \;\;\iff\;\; s \overset{\tau^{*}a\tau^{*}}{\longrightarrow} s'$$ where $\tau^{*}$ denotes zero or more $\tau$-steps. Then weak trace equivalence is: $$\mathcal{M}_1 \approx_{wt} \mathcal{M}_2 \;\iff\; \text{Traces}_{\Longrightarrow}(\mathcal{M}_1) = \text{Traces}_{\Longrightarrow}(\mathcal{M}_2)$$ | Aspect | Strong Trace | Weak Trace | |---|---|---| | $\tau$ visibility | Visible | Hidden | | Used for | Fully observable systems | Systems with internal actions | | Computational cost | Polynomial in $\mid S \mid$ | PSPACE-complete | | KTU weight | High (asked frequently) | High (asked in Part B) | --- ## 2.3 Safety Property Classification Tree ``` Properties (P ⊆ Σ^ω) | ________________|________________ | | SAFETY Liveness (finite bad prefix) (no finite bad prefix) | ________|________ | | Invariant Guarantee (G holds in (Q eventually all reachable holds along states) some path) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Invariant** (state-based) and **Guarantee** (trace-based) are both safety properties. The defining test: *if a bad prefix exists, the property is safety*. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table compiles every formula, notation, and threshold required for the KTU board exam on this topic. Memorise it for the **2-mark direct recall questions**. | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Standard Notation | |---|---|---| | $\text{Tr}(s)$ | Set of traces from state $s$ | $\subseteq Act^{*} \cup Act^{\omega}$ | | $s \overset{\sigma}{\Longrightarrow} s'$ | Weak transition sequence | $s \xrightarrow{\sigma} s'$ in strong form | | $\text{BadPref}(P)$ | Set of all bad prefixes of $P$ | $BP(P) \subseteq \Sigma^{*}$ | | $P = \Sigma^{\omega} \setminus \text{BadPref}(P)\cdot \Sigma^{\omega}$ | Safety property characterisation | Alpern–Schneider | | $M \models \mathbf{G}\, p$ | Invariant (always $p$) | CTL/LTL safety fragment | | $M_1 \approx_t M_2$ | Trace equivalence | $\text{Tr}(M_1) = \text{Tr}(M_2)$ | | $s_1 \sim s_2$ | Bisimulation equivalence | Branching-time | | $\square \varphi$ | "Always $\varphi$" (LTL) | Linear safety operator | | $\lozenge \varphi$ | "Eventually $\varphi$" (LTL) | Liveness operator | | $Tr_{fin}(M)$ | Finite traces of $M$ | Subset of $Act^{*}$ | | $\vert BadPref(P) \vert$ | Cardinality of bad-prefix set | Used in bounded verification | > Real-world engineering utility: Trace equivalence is the formal basis of **interface contract checking** in embedded systems (e.g., AUTOSAR), and safety properties underpin **DO-178C** avionics certification, **ISO 26262** automotive functional safety, and **IEC 61508** industrial safety standards. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Two Safety Property Characterisations We prove the foundational identity used in every KTU Part-B question on safety. > **Claim:** A property $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ is a safety property **iff** > $$P \;=\; \Sigma^{\omega} \setminus BP(P) \cdot \Sigma^{\omega}$$ **Proof (Standard):** *Step 1 — Direction $(\Rightarrow)$:* Assume $P$ is a safety property. By definition, for every $\pi \notin P$, there is a finite bad prefix $\hat{\pi} \in BP(P)$. Therefore $\pi \in BP(P) \cdot \Sigma^{\omega}$. *Step 2 — Direction $(\Leftarrow)$:* Assume $P = \Sigma^{\omega} \setminus BP(P) \cdot \Sigma^{\omega}$. Take any $\pi \notin P$. Then $\pi \in BP(P) \cdot \Sigma^{\omega}$, meaning there exists a finite prefix $\pi[0..n] \in BP(P)$. By definition of bad prefix, **every** extension $\pi[0..n] \cdot \rho$ violates $P$. Hence $P$ has a finite bad prefix for every violation, so $P$ is a safety property. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Trace Set of an LTS Given a 3-state LTS $M$ with states $\{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, transitions and labels as in the diagram of SECTION 4, the trace set is computed as: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Tr}(s_0) &= \{a\} \cdot \text{Tr}(s_1) \;\cup\; \{a\} \cdot \text{Tr}(s_2) \\[4pt] \text{Tr}(s_1) &= \{\varepsilon\} \;\cup\; \{b\} \cdot \text{Tr}(s_1) \;\cup\; \{b\} \cdot \text{Tr}(s_2) \\[4pt] \text{Tr}(s_2) &= \{\varepsilon\} \;\cup\; \{c\} \cdot \text{Tr}(s_2) \end{aligned} $$ Solving the recursive equations by the **Kleene fixed-point theorem** applied to finite automata: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Tr}(s_2) &= c^{*} \\ \text{Tr}(s_1) &= b^{*} \;\cup\; b^{+} \cdot c^{*} \;=\; b^{*} \cdot (c^{*}) \\ \text{Tr}(s_0) &= a \cdot b^{*} \cdot c^{*} \end{aligned} $$ > **Final closed form:** $\text{Tr}(M) = a \cdot b^{*} \cdot c^{*}$ --- ## 3.3 Decision Procedure for Trace Equivalence > **Theorem (Trace Equivalence is Decidable for Finite LTS).** Given finite LTSs $M_1, M_2$, the following steps yield a decision: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compute deterministic product } M_1 \times M_2. \\[2pt] \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Project to the synchronised alphabet } Act. \\[2pt] \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Build the canonical DFA } A_{\cap} = \text{Det}(\text{LTL}(M_1) \cup \text{LTL}(M_2)). \\[2pt] \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Check emptiness: } A_{\cap} = \emptyset \;\Leftrightarrow\; M_1 \approx_t M_2. \end{aligned} $$ The complexity is polynomial in $\vert S_1 \vert \cdot \vert S_2 \vert$ for finite-state systems. --- ## 3.4 Verification of a Safety Property — Worked Example > **Problem:** Let $M$ be a model and $P = \mathbf{G}\,\neg q$ (invariant: $q$ never holds). Verify that $M \models P$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Compute } Reach(M) &= \text{set of all states reachable from } s_0. \\[4pt] \text{Compute } \{s \in S \mid q \in L(s)\} &= \text{set of states violating } \neg q. \\[4pt] \text{If } Reach(M) \cap \{s \mid q \in L(s)\} &= \emptyset, \text{ then } M \models \mathbf{G}\,\neg q. \end{aligned} $$ This is the standard **invariant-checking** reduction, which runs in $O(\vert S \vert + \vert \rightarrow \vert)$. --- ## 3.5 Distinguishing Bisimulation vs Trace Equivalence — Counterexample Consider the LTSs: - $M_1$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1$, $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_2$ - $M_2$: $s_0' \xrightarrow{a} s_1'$, $s_0' \xrightarrow{a} s_2'$, plus $s_1' \xrightarrow{a} s_3'$ and $s_2' \xrightarrow{a} s_3'$ **Trace sets:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Tr}(M_1) &= \{a,\; a\cdot a\} \\ \text{Tr}(M_2) &= \{a,\; a\cdot a\} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore $M_1 \approx_t M_2$. However, the *branching structure* differs, so $M_1 \not\sim M_2$ in general (depends on the precise transitions). This proves the strictness of the equivalence hierarchy. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Equivalence Hierarchy (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD A[Isomorphism] --> B[Strong Bisimulation] B --> C[Weak Bisimulation] C --> D[Trace Equivalence] D --> E[Testing Equivalence] E --> F[Failure Trace Equivalence] B -. "preserves branching" .- G[CTL Star] D -. "linear time" .- H[LTL] C -. "abstracts tau" .- I[Internal Actions] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Trace Computation Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR A[LTS M with states S] --> B[Identify initial state s0] B --> C[DFS or BFS over transitions] C --> D[Record label sequence per path] D --> E[Union over all paths = Tr M] E --> F{Compare with Tr M2} F -- "Tr M1 equals Tr M2" --> G[M1 is trace equivalent to M2] F -- "Tr M1 differs Tr M2" --> H[Distinguishing trace exists] style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Property Decomposition: Alpern–Schneider Theorem (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TD P[Property P over Sigma omega] --> Q{Does a finite
bad prefix exist?} Q -- YES --> S[SAFETY PROPERTY] Q -- NO --> L[LIVENESS PROPERTY] S --> I[Invariant: G p] S --> G2[Guarantee: F q] L --> EV[Eventuality: F r] L --> PR[Persistence: G F r] I --> BAD1[BadPrefix = states where not p] G2 --> BAD2[BadPrefix = paths ending without q] style S fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style L fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style I fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 style G2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Safety Property Verification Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph TB A[Model M] --> B[Graph Reachability Engine] C[Safety Property P] --> D[Bad Prefix Automaton B] D --> E[Product Construction] B --> E E --> F{Is accepting
state reachable?} F -- NO --> G[P HOLDS in M] F -- YES --> H[Counterexample trace
bad prefix returned] G --> I[Verification PASS] H --> J[Verification FAIL] style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style J fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Example LTS for Trace Computation (Mermaid) ```mermaid graph LR s0((s0)) -- a --> s1((s1)) s0 -- a --> s2((s2)) s1 -- b --> s1 s1 -- b --> s2 s2 -- c --> s2 style s0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style s1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style s2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The LTS has initial state $s_0$, two outgoing $a$-transitions to $s_1$ and $s_2$, looping $b$-transitions at $s_1$, and a self-loop $c$ at $s_2$. This is the same LTS used in SECTION 3.2 for the worked derivation. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 1]** > Define *trace equivalence* between two labelled transition systems. How does it differ from bisimulation equivalence? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Two LTSs $M_1, M_2$ are trace equivalent, $M_1 \approx_t M_2$, if they have the same set of observable execution traces: $\text{Tr}(M_1) = \text{Tr}(M_2)$. - **(1 mark)** A trace is a sequence of actions (labels) along an execution, not the sequence of states. - **(1 mark)** Bisimulation additionally requires *branching structure* preservation (matching of outgoing transitions at every step), so bisimulation is strictly finer; bisimilar $\Rightarrow$ trace equivalent, but not conversely. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model 1]** > What is a *safety property*? Give one example from a real-time or embedded system. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** A safety property states that "something bad never happens" — every violation has a finite bad prefix that is itself a witness. - **(1 mark)** Formal definition: $P$ is safety iff $\forall \pi \notin P : \exists n : \forall \rho : \pi[0..n]\rho \notin P$. - **(1 mark)** Example: *"The aircraft autopilot must never command full throttle when altitude < 100 ft"* (avionics DO-178C), or in LTL: $\mathbf{G}(\text{altitude} < 100 \rightarrow \neg \text{fullThrottle})$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** > > **(a)** [7 Marks] Define *trace equivalence* and *weak trace equivalence* for LTSs. Prove or disprove: *"If $M_1$ is weakly bisimilar to $M_2$, then $M_1$ is trace equivalent to $M_2$."* > > **(b)** [7 Marks] Consider the LTSs $M_1$ and $M_2$ below. Compute $\text{Tr}(M_1)$ and $\text{Tr}(M_2)$ in closed-form regular expressions and determine whether $M_1 \approx_t M_2$. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks]** - **Definition of trace equivalence (1 mark):** $M_1 \approx_t M_2 \iff \text{Tr}(M_1) = \text{Tr}(M_2)$ where $\text{Tr}(M)$ is the set of all finite/infinite label sequences from initial state. - **Definition of weak trace equivalence (2 marks):** $M_1 \approx_{wt} M_2 \iff \text{Tr}_{\Rightarrow}(M_1) = \text{Tr}_{\Rightarrow}(M_2)$ where $s \overset{a}{\Rightarrow} s' \iff s \xrightarrow{\tau^{*} a \tau^{*}} s'$. The internal $\tau$ is abstracted. - **Proof of the claim (4 marks):** *Yes, the claim is true.* By the equivalence hierarchy theorem, weak bisimulation $\sqsubseteq$ trace equivalence. Proof sketch: if $M_1 \approx_w M_2$, then for every $s_1$ reachable in $M_1$ there exists $s_2$ in $M_2$ such that $s_1 \approx_w s_2$. Hence every weakly labelled sequence in $M_1$ has a corresponding one in $M_2$ (and vice versa by symmetry of bisimulation). Therefore the set of weak traces is identical: $M_1 \approx_{wt} M_2$. **(b) [7 marks]** Let the two LTSs share the alphabet $\{a,b,c\}$ and have transitions: - $M_1$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1$, $s_1 \xrightarrow{b} s_1$, $s_1 \xrightarrow{b} s_2$, $s_2 \xrightarrow{c} s_2$ - $M_2$: $t_0 \xrightarrow{a} t_1$, $t_0 \xrightarrow{a} t_2$, $t_1 \xrightarrow{b} t_1$, $t_2 \xrightarrow{b} t_2$, $t_1 \xrightarrow{c} t_1$, $t_2 \xrightarrow{c} t_2$ **Step 1 — Trace of $M_1$ (3 marks):** Following SECTION 3.2's derivation: $$\text{Tr}(M_1) = a \cdot b^{*} \cdot c^{*}$$ **Step 2 — Trace of $M_2$ (2 marks):** Two non-deterministic branches: $$\text{Tr}(M_2) = a \cdot b^{*} \;\cup\; a \cdot c^{*} \;=\; a \cdot (b^{*} \cup c^{*})$$ **Step 3 — Comparison (2 marks):** $a\cdot b^{*} \cdot c^{*} \neq a \cdot (b^{*} \cup c^{*})$ because trace $a \cdot b \cdot b \notin \text{Tr}(M_1)$ but $a \cdot b \cdot b \in \text{Tr}(M_2)$ (if $b$ is followed by $b$ alone, the $c^{*}$-path of $M_1$ does not match). **Conclusion:** $M_1 \not\approx_t M_2$. --- > ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** > > **(a)** [7 Marks] State and prove the Alpern–Schneider characterisation of safety properties. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] Show how safety properties are verified via *bad-prefix Büchi automata* construction. Use $\mathbf{G}\,\neg q$ as a running example. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks]** - **Statement (2 marks):** $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ is a safety property iff $P = \Sigma^{\omega} \setminus BP(P) \cdot \Sigma^{\omega}$, where $BP(P)$ is the set of all finite bad prefixes of $P$. - **Definition of bad prefix (1 mark):** A finite word $\sigma$ is a bad prefix of $P$ if every infinite extension of $\sigma$ violates $P$. - **Proof $(\Rightarrow)$ (2 marks):** Suppose $P$ is a safety property and $\pi \notin P$. By definition there exists a finite $n$ such that for all $\rho$, $\pi[0..n]\rho \notin P$. Hence $\pi[0..n] \in BP(P)$ and $\pi \in BP(P) \cdot \Sigma^{\omega}$. Thus $P^{c} \subseteq BP(P)\cdot \Sigma^{\omega}$. The reverse inclusion holds by definition of bad prefix. Hence $P = \Sigma^{\omega} \setminus BP(P)\cdot \Sigma^{\omega}$. - **Proof $(\Leftarrow)$ (2 marks):** Conversely, suppose $P = \Sigma^{\omega} \setminus BP(P)\cdot\Sigma^{\omega}$. For any $\pi \notin P$, $\pi$ belongs to $BP(P)\cdot\Sigma^{\omega}$, so a finite prefix $\hat{\pi} \in BP(P)$ exists. Since $\hat{\pi}$ is a bad prefix, all extensions of $\hat{\pi}$ also lie outside $P$. Hence $P$ satisfies the safety definition. **(b) [7 marks]** - **Büchi acceptance for safety (1 mark):** A safety property can be accepted by a *deterministic* Büchi automaton whose accepting states are those that have *not* yet encountered a bad prefix. - **Construction for $\mathbf{G}\,\neg q$ (2 marks):** The automaton $A_{\neg \mathbf{G} q} = A_{\mathbf{F} q}$ has states $\{q, \neg q\}$, initial state $\neg q$, transitions: on action $q$ go to state $q$ (and stay); on $\neg q$ stay in $\neg q$. State $q$ is the *only* accepting state — a *bad prefix* is any word that first reaches $q$. - **Product construction (2 marks):** The model checker computes $M \otimes A_{\neg \mathbf{G} q}$. The synchronous product's Büchi emptiness check decides $M \models \mathbf{G}\,\neg q$ iff the product has no accepting cycle. - **Algorithmic outcome (2 marks):** Reachability of the accepting state $\Rightarrow$ counterexample trace (a bad prefix, e.g., the first time $q$ becomes true). If unreachable, the safety property holds. This reduction to graph reachability is what makes safety checking *linear* in the size of $M$ and polynomial overall. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly confuse the direction of the Alpern–Schneider proof. The **$(\Leftarrow)$ direction** (showing that the closed-form equation implies safety) is *more often* incorrectly argued. Marks are lost for: (i) using "$\pi$" to mean both a trace and its length simultaneously, (ii) forgetting that the bad-prefix set must be **prefix-closed** (if $\sigma$ is bad, every prefix is also bad), and (iii) failing to mention that the automaton is **deterministic** for safety properties — a crucial fact for verification complexity analysis. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print and Carry to Exam Hall)** - ✅ **Trace** = sequence of *labels* (actions) along an execution; not the sequence of states. - ✅ **Trace equivalence** = equality of trace *sets*; coarsest of the standard equivalences. - ✅ **Strong trace equivalence** treats $\tau$ as observable; **weak trace equivalence** hides $\tau$. - ✅ **Bisimulation** is strictly finer than trace equivalence — preserves branching structure, not just linear behaviour. - ✅ **Safety property** = "something bad never happens"; every violation has a *finite* bad prefix. - ✅ **Liveness property** = "something good eventually happens"; no finite bad prefix exists. - ✅ **Alpern–Schneider Theorem**: every property is the intersection of one safety and one liveness property. - ✅ Invariants $\mathbf{G}\,p$ and guarantees $\mathbf{F}\,q$ are *safety*; eventualities $\mathbf{F}\,r$ and persistence $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,r$ are *liveness*. - ✅ Safety property verification reduces to **reachability** of a bad-prefix state — polynomial-time for finite models. - ✅ The standard KTU 14-mark question pattern: (a) definition + theorem, (b) worked example or counterexample distinguishing trace equivalence from bisimulation. - ✅ Common exam trap: stating that bisimulation and trace equivalence coincide — they do **not**; a small non-deterministic choice without differing traces is a counterexample. - ✅ Linear-time logics (LTL) cannot distinguish bisimilar systems that differ in trace sets, but bisimulation logics (CTL*) can. - ✅ Memorise: $M_1 \sim M_2 \;\Rightarrow\; M_1 \approx_t M_2 \;\Rightarrow\; M_1 \approx_{test} M_2$ (strict chain).

topics.title: Liveness Properties - Liveness Property
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Liveness Properties - Liveness Property

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Model Checking** and **Temporal Logic**, a **Liveness Property** is a class of linear-time properties that guarantees that "**something good will eventually happen**" along every execution path of a system. Formally, in the context of **Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)** and **Kripke Structures**, a property $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ (over infinite words/sequences) is a **liveness property** if and only if for every finite prefix $u \in \Sigma^*$, there exists a continuation $v \in \Sigma^\omega$ such that the concatenation $u \cdot v \in P$.

Mathematically, the condition is:

$$\forall u \in \Sigma^*, \quad \exists v \in \Sigma^\omega \mid u \cdot v \in P$$

This captures the essential intuition that **no finite observation** of a system's behavior is sufficient to conclude that a liveness property has been **violated** — because one can always extend the prefix in some manner that eventually satisfies the property.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Liveness vs. Safety**
> The **Safety-Liveness Classification** (proposed by **Lamport**, formalized by **Alpern & Schneider**) is a *canonical* classification of all linear-time properties. **Every** linear-time property $P$ can be expressed as the intersection of a safety property and a liveness property:
> $$P = \text{Safety}(P) \cap \text{Liveness}(P)$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **busy post office counter**:

- **Safety Property Analogy**: "The counter will *never* dispense money without receiving a payment first." — This is a **bad thing that must never happen**; we can verify it by examining a finite prefix of behavior. If the violation occurs, a finite trace is enough to witness it.

- **Liveness Property Analogy**: "Every customer who joins the queue will *eventually* be served." — This is a **good thing that must eventually happen**. Even if you observe a customer waiting for 10 minutes, you cannot conclude from any *finite* observation that the liveness property has failed — perhaps they will be served at minute 11, or minute 1000.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Distinction (KTU Board Exam Favorite)**:
> - **Safety** = "**Nothing bad ever happens**" → Verifiable on **finite** traces
> - **Liveness** = "**Something good eventually happens**" → Requires **infinite** traces for verification

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics and Constants

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Value / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Time Domain | $\mathbb{N}$ or $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Discrete / continuous time for temporal logic |
| Trace Alphabet | $\Sigma$ | Set of atomic propositions / states |
| Finite Prefixes | $\Sigma^*$ | Kleene star of the alphabet |
| Infinite Traces | $\Sigma^\omega$ | $\omega$-word set (infinite behaviors) |
| **Büchi Acceptance** | $\mathcal{F}$ | Set of accepting states for liveness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Liveness in Büchi Automata**: Liveness properties are precisely those that can be recognized by **Büchi Automata** (a finite automaton over infinite words). A Büchi automaton accepts an infinite word if it visits its accepting set $F$ **infinitely often**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Theoretical Breakdown

### The Five Pillars of Liveness Property Theory

**Pillar 1: Topological Characterization (Alpern-Schneider)**
- The set of all infinite traces $\Sigma^\omega$ is endowed with the **Cantor topology**.
- A property $P$ is **liveness** if and only if $P$ is a **dense** subset of $\Sigma^\omega$ in this topology.
- **Why?** Density captures the intuition that every finite prefix can be "completed" to satisfy $P$ — there is no "forbidden" finite prefix.

**Pillar 2: Closure Properties**
- Liveness properties are **closed under finite and infinite intersections** (i.e., under conjunction $\land$).
- Liveness properties are **closed under finite and infinite unions** (i.e., under disjunction $\lor$).
- **Engineering utility**: This allows composition — combining multiple liveness requirements yields another liveness requirement.

**Pillar 3: Density Decomposition Theorem**
- Every property $P$ can be uniquely decomposed (with respect to a fixed "natural" property used as a basis) as:
$$P = S \cap L$$
where $S$ is a **safety property** (closed in the Cantor topology) and $L$ is a **liveness property** (dense in the Cantor topology).

**Pillar 4: Liveness as a Limit of Stutter**
- A liveness property $P$ is one such that for any finite word $u$, the set of extensions $u \cdot \Sigma^\omega$ is a **"liveness subset"** of $P$.
- Equivalently: $P$ cannot be refuted by any finite trace.

**Pillar 5: Liveness Automata (Büchi Acceptance)**
- A **Büchi Automaton** $\mathcal{B} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, Q_0, F)$ accepts an infinite word $w = a_0 a_1 a_2 \dots$ if there exists a run $\rho$ such that:
$$\text{Inf}(\rho) \cap F \neq \emptyset$$
- $F$ is the set of accepting states; $\text{Inf}(\rho)$ is the set of states visited infinitely often.

## 2.2 Why and How — Pedagogical Deep Dive

| Step | Why (Motivation) | How (Mechanism) |
|------|------------------|------------------|
| Define liveness | Need to verify *"eventually"* properties | Use infinite traces in $\Sigma^\omega$ |
| Topological view | Reframe verification geometrically | Cantor topology $\to$ open/closed/dense sets |
| Büchi acceptance | Finite representation of $\omega$-languages | Automaton visits $F$ infinitely often |
| Safety-Liveness split | Decompose verification problem | $P = S \cap L$ unique decomposition |
| Stuttering closure | Hardware-level liveness analysis | Equivalence under stutter-equivalent traces |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Theorem | Statement | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Liveness Definition | $\forall u \in \Sigma^*, \exists v \in \Sigma^\omega \mid u \cdot v \in P$ | Verifying eventual properties |
| 2 | Density Characterization | $P$ is liveness $\iff \overline{P} = \Sigma^\omega$ (closure = whole space) | Topological test |
| 3 | Safety-Liveness Decomposition | $P = \text{Safety}(P) \cap \text{Liveness}(P)$ | Modular verification |
| 4 | Büchi Acceptance | $\text{Inf}(\rho) \cap F \neq \emptyset$ | LTL to automata translation |
| 5 | LTL Liveness Pattern | $\mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F} q)$ | "Every $p$ is eventually followed by $q$" |
| 6 | Liveness $\subseteq$ Topological Dense Sets | $P \text{ is liveness} \iff P \text{ is dense}$ | Theoretical check |
| 7 | Liveness is limit-closed | $\bigcap_{i} P_i$ of liveness sets is liveness | Infinite conjunction closure |
| 8 | Reachability Liveness | $\mathbf{F} p$ (eventually $p$) | Basic liveness formula |
| 9 | Recurrence Liveness | $\mathbf{G} \mathbf{F} p$ ($p$ holds infinitely often) | Strong liveness |
| 10 | Persistence Liveness | $\mathbf{F} \mathbf{G} p$ (eventually $p$ always) | Stability property |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat for Markdown Rendering**: All set-membership / divisibility bars use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking table syntax. For example, "$\vert x \vert$" is written as $\lvert x \rvert$ inside the LaTeX math mode.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Liveness properties are foundational in:

- **Hardware Verification**: Proving that a circuit's request line is *eventually* acknowledged (used in **Intel's** and **IBM's** formal verification flows for cache coherence protocols).
- **Protocol Verification**: Mutual exclusion (no two processes in critical section — **safety**); every process *eventually* enters the critical section — **liveness**).
- **Operating Systems**: Deadlock-freedom (liveness) and no partial deadlock (liveness combined with safety).
- **Embedded / Cyber-Physical Systems**: Sensor data is *eventually* delivered to the controller.
- **Distributed Systems**: Consensus protocols (e.g., Paxos, Raft) must guarantee **eventual agreement** — a liveness property under the FLP impossibility result.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalence of Liveness Definitions

**Claim**: A property $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is a liveness property **if and only if** for every finite word $u \in \Sigma^*$, there exists an infinite continuation $v \in \Sigma^\omega$ such that $u \cdot v \in P$.

We prove the equivalence by establishing the topological correspondence.

### Step 1 — Setup of Cantor Topology

Define the metric $d$ on $\Sigma^\omega$ for two infinite words $w_1, w_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(w_1, w_2) &= 2^{-n}, \quad \text{where } n = \max\{ k \mid w_1[0..k] = w_2[0..k] \} \\
&\text{with convention } n = \infty \implies d = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This metric induces the **Cantor topology** on $\Sigma^\omega$. The open balls in this topology are precisely the cylinder sets of the form:

$$
u \cdot \Sigma^\omega = \{ w \in \Sigma^\omega \mid w \text{ has prefix } u \}
$$

where $u \in \Sigma^*$.

### Step 2 — Density Characterization

**Theorem**: $P$ is liveness $\iff$ $P$ is dense in $\Sigma^\omega$.

**($\Rightarrow$ Direction)**: Suppose $P$ is liveness, i.e., $\forall u \in \Sigma^*, \exists v \in \Sigma^\omega \mid u \cdot v \in P$. For any non-empty open set $u \cdot \Sigma^\omega$ in the topology, there exists some element of $P$ in it (namely $u \cdot v$). Hence $P$ intersects every non-empty open set, which is the definition of a **dense set**.

**($\Leftarrow$ Direction)**: Suppose $P$ is dense. For any $u \in \Sigma^*$, the cylinder set $u \cdot \Sigma^\omega$ is non-empty and open. By density of $P$, there exists $w \in P \cap (u \cdot \Sigma^\omega)$. Writing $w = u \cdot v$ for some $v$, we conclude $\exists v \mid u \cdot v \in P$. Hence $P$ is liveness.

### Step 3 — Closure of Liveness

**Claim**: The class $\mathcal{L}$ of all liveness properties is closed under arbitrary intersections.

**Proof**: Let $\{ P_i \}_{i \in I}$ be a family of liveness properties. Let $u \in \Sigma^*$ be arbitrary. For each $i$, since $P_i$ is liveness, $\exists v_i \mid u \cdot v_i \in P_i$. By the axiom of choice, select one such $v_i$ for each $i$. Now define:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \bigcap_{i \in I} P_i \\
u \cdot \Sigma^\omega \cap P &= u \cdot \Sigma^\omega \cap \bigcap_i P_i \\
&= \bigcap_i (u \cdot \Sigma^\omega \cap P_i) \\
&\text{(each set in the intersection is non-empty)} \\
&\neq \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $P$ is liveness. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Safety-Liveness Decomposition (Alpern-Schneider)

**Theorem**: For any property $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$, there exist a safety property $S$ and a liveness property $L$ such that $P = S \cap L$.

### Step 1 — Construct the Safety Part

The **safety closure** of $P$ is:

$$
\text{Safety}(P) = \bigcup \{ u \cdot \Sigma^\omega \mid \forall v, u \cdot v \in P \}
$$

In words, $\text{Safety}(P)$ contains all infinite words $w$ such that *every* finite prefix of $w$ can be extended to an element of $P$.

### Step 2 — Construct the Liveness Part

The **liveness part** is:

$$
\text{Liveness}(P) = P
$$

### Step 3 — Show the Decomposition

We claim $P = \text{Safety}(P) \cap P$. The key observation is that $\text{Safety}(P) \supseteq P$ trivially (every $w \in P$ has all its prefixes extendable to $w$ itself). So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &\subseteq \text{Safety}(P) \cap P \\
\text{Conversely, } w \in \text{Safety}(P) \cap P &\Rightarrow w \in P
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify Liveness of the Liveness Part

The set $P$ itself is liveness **relative to the trace set**, but for the *absolute* decomposition, we use:

$$
\text{Liveness}(P) = \{ w \in \Sigma^\omega \mid \text{no finite prefix of } w \text{ "forces" violation} \}
$$

A canonical construction is: $L = P \cup (\Sigma^\omega \setminus \text{Safety}(P))$.

This is dense, hence liveness, and $P = S \cap L$ holds. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Liveness LTL Patterns

| LTL Formula | Liveness Type | Meaning | Example Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{F}\,p$ | Reachability | $p$ eventually holds | "Message is eventually delivered" |
| $\mathbf{G}(p \rightarrow \mathbf{F}\,q)$ | Response | Every $p$ is followed by $q$ | "Every request is acknowledged" |
| $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,p$ | Recurrence | $p$ occurs infinitely often | "Process enters CS infinitely often" |
| $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ | Persistence | Eventually $p$ holds forever | "System eventually stabilizes" |
| $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,p \rightarrow \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,q$ | Conditional recurrence | Recurrence implication | "If starvation occurs, recovery also recurs" |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Verifying a Simple Liveness Property

**Problem**: Consider a Kripke structure with two states $s_0, s_1$, both atomic propositions $p, q$ initially false, with transitions $s_0 \to s_1$ and $s_1 \to s_1$, and $p$ becomes true at $s_1$. Verify the liveness property $\mathbf{F}\,p$.

**Solution Path**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} &\quad \text{List all infinite paths from initial state } s_0 \\
& \quad \pi_1 = s_0 s_1 s_1 s_1 \dots \\
& \quad \pi_2 = s_0 s_0 s_1 s_1 \dots \quad \text{(if self-loop on } s_0 \text{ exists)} \\
\text{Step 2:} &\quad \text{Check } \mathbf{F}\,p \text{ on each path} \\
& \quad \pi_1 \models \mathbf{F}\,p \text{ since } s_1 \text{ (at time 1) has } p = \text{true} \\
& \quad \pi_2 \models \mathbf{F}\,p \text{ since } s_1 \text{ (at time 2) has } p = \text{true} \\
\text{Step 3:} &\quad \text{Since all paths satisfy } \mathbf{F}\,p, \text{ the system satisfies the liveness property} \\
\text{Step 4:} &\quad \text{Büchi automaton for } \mathbf{F}\,p \text{ has accepting state at } p = \text{true}
\end{aligned}
$$
