*Logic:* Without any fairness assumption, the model checker must consider *all* infinite walks, including those that unrealistically starve a transition. This gives a *too strong* liveness verdict (false positives are common).

**Step 2 — Define the set of unconditionally fair paths.**
Let $A$ be the set of all atomic actions of $M$. A path $\pi$ is *unconditionally fair* if for every action $a \in A$:

$$
\Box \left( \text{enabled}_\pi(a) \right) \implies \Diamond \left( \text{taken}_\pi(a) \right)
$$

*Logic:* The condition $\Box(\text{enabled})$ means "always enabled" along the path. The conclusion $\Diamond(\text{taken})$ means "eventually taken". This is the mathematical embodiment of impartiality.

**Step 3 — Restrict the path quantifier to the fair set.**
We define $Paths_{uncond}(M, s_0)$ as the set of all unconditionally fair paths starting at $s_0$:

$$
Paths_{uncond}(M, s_0) = \left\{ \pi \in Paths(M, s_0) \mid \forall a \in A : \pi \models \Box \text{enabled}(a) \Rightarrow \Diamond \text{taken}(a) \right\}
$$

**Step 4 — Define unconditional fair satisfaction.**
Substitute the restricted path set back into the satisfaction definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M, s_0 \models_{uncond} \phi &\iff \forall \pi \in Paths_{uncond}(M, s_0) : \pi \models \phi \\
&\iff \forall \pi \in Paths(M, s_0) : \left( \forall a \in A : \Box \text{en}(a) \Rightarrow \Diamond \text{tk}(a) \right) \Rightarrow \pi \models \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The quantifier is restricted. Only "physically reasonable" paths are considered, eliminating the false positives of the bare model.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Fairness Variants

The three standard fairness conditions can be ranked by *strictness*. The strictest implies the weaker ones in the same model.

| Rank | Fairness Type | Condition per Action $a$ | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Weakest) | Weak (Justice) | $\Box \Diamond \text{enabled}(a) \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \text{taken}(a)$ | Only complains if enabled $\infty$-often |
| 2 (Middle) | Strong (Compassion) | $\Diamond \Box \text{enabled}(a) \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \text{taken}(a)$ | Only complains if eventually permanently enabled |
| 3 (Strictest) | **Unconditional (Impartiality)** | $\Box \, \text{enabled}(a) \Rightarrow \Diamond \, \text{taken}(a)$ | Complains the instant it is enabled |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Verifying a Liveness Property

**Problem:** Consider a Kripke structure $M$ with three states $s_0, s_1, s_2$ and actions $\{a, b\}$. State $s_0$ has outgoing transitions $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1$ and $s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s_2$. Both $s_1$ and $s_2$ are absorbing (self-loops). Check whether $M, s_0 \models_{uncond} \Diamond (s_1 \lor s_2)$.

**Step 1 — Enumerate all paths from $s_0$.**
- $\pi_1$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1 \xrightarrow{a} s_1 \to \dots$
- $\pi_2$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s_2 \xrightarrow{b} s_2 \to \dots$
- $\pi_3$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1 \xrightarrow{b} s_1 \to \dots$ (not valid since $s_1$ has no $b$-transition)
- $\pi_4$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s_2 \xrightarrow{a} s_2 \to \dots$ (similarly not valid)

**Step 2 — Check unconditional fairness for each valid path.**
- $\pi_1$: After step 0, action $a$ is no longer enabled. Action $b$ is *never* enabled from $s_1$. The antecedent $\Box \text{enabled}(b)$ is false, so the fairness obligation for $b$ is vacuously satisfied. Action $a$ is taken at step 0, so the obligation for $a$ is satisfied. $\pi_1$ is **fair**.
- $\pi_2$: By symmetric reasoning, $\pi_2$ is **fair**.

**Step 3 — Check the LTL property $\Diamond(s_1 \lor s_2)$.**
- $\pi_1$: At step 1, the state is $s_1$, so the property holds.
- $\pi_2$: At step 1, the state is $s_2$, so the property holds.

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
Since all unconditionally fair paths satisfy the property:

$$
M, s_0 \models_{uncond} \Diamond (s_1 \lor s_2)
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map: Unconditional Fairness in the Verification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sysA["Reactive System Specification"] --> bldM["Build Kripke Structure M"]
    bldM --> allP["Enumerate All Paths in M"]
    allP --> prnF["Prune Path Universe using Unconditional Fairness"]
    prnF --> fairP["Set of Unconditionally Fair Paths Fair_M"]
    fairP --> propPhi["Specify Property phi in CTL or LTL"]
    propPhi --> checkE["Model Check: all pi in Fair_M satisfy phi"]
    checkE --> verdict1["SAT: Property Holds"]
    checkE --> verdict2["UNSAT: Counterexample Path Returned"]
    style sysA fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style bldM fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style allP fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style prnF fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style fairP fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style propPhi fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style checkE fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style verdict1 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style verdict2 fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: The Role of Unconditional Fairness in Fair Transition Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Kripke["Kripke Structure M = (S, S0, R, L)"]
        dirS["Set of States S"]
        dirI["Initial States S0"]
        dirT["Transition Relation R"]
        dirL["Labeling Function L"]
    end
    subgraph FairCore["Fairness Subsystem"]
        actA["Action Set A = {a1, a2, ..., an}"]
        condU["Unconditional Fairness Condition: For all a: Box enabled(a) implies Diamond taken(a)"]
        fairSet["Fair Paths Set Fair_M"]
    end
    subgraph Logic["Temporal Logic Layer"]
        ctl["CTL / LTL Formula phi"]
        sat["Satisfaction Relation: M models uncond phi"]
    end
    dirS --> fairSet
    dirT --> fairSet
    dirI --> fairSet
    actA --> condU
    condU --> fairSet
    fairSet --> sat
    ctl --> sat
    style dirS fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style dirI fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style dirT fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style dirL fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style actA fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style condU fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style fairSet fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ctl fill:#e8daef,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style sat fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Path Pruning Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    allSet["All Paths in M"] --> split{"Is path pi fair under unconditional rule?"}
    split -- "Yes" --> keep["Keep in Fair_M"]
    split -- "No: some enabled action is starved forever" --> drop["Discard from Fair_M"]
    keep --> verif["Verify phi on Fair_M"]
    drop --> verif
    style allSet fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style split fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style keep fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style drop fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style verif fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define unconditional fairness in the context of transition systems. How does it differ from weak and strong fairness?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Unconditional fairness* (also called *impartiality*) is the strictest standard fairness condition. In a transition system with action set $A$, a path $\pi$ is unconditionally fair if for every action $a \in A$:

$$
\Box \, \text{enabled}_\pi(a) \implies \Diamond \, \text{taken}_\pi(a)
$$

That is, **every action that is continuously enabled along the path must be eventually taken**.

**Differentiation:**
- *Weak fairness* ($\Box \Diamond \text{en} \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \text{tk}$) — complains only when an action is enabled *infinitely often*.
- *Strong fairness* ($\Diamond \Box \text{en} \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \text{tk}$) — complains only when an action is *eventually permanently enabled*.
- *Unconditional fairness* — complains the *instant* the action becomes and stays enabled. Hence, **unconditional fairness implies strong fairness, which in turn implies weak fairness.**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State and explain the role of the action set $A$ in the formulation of unconditional fair satisfaction. Why is it required to be finite?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The action set $A = \{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n\}$ is the finite collection of atomic transitions of the system. In the unconditional fair satisfaction relation:

$$
M, s_0 \models_{uncond} \phi \iff \forall \pi \in Paths_{uncond}(M, s_0) : \pi \models \phi
$$

the *fairness obligation* must be discharged for **every** $a \in A$.

**Finiteness is required** because:
- It guarantees that the universal quantification over $A$ is decidable.
- It allows the model checker (e.g., SPIN, NuSMV) to pre-compute the set of fair paths using automata-theoretic constructions with finite Büchi or Streett acceptance conditions.
- If $A$ were infinite, the verification problem would be semi-decidable at best, breaking the fully-automatic guarantee of automated verification.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Note on Pattern:** Each KTU Part B question on this topic carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The model answers below mirror KTU's valuation key.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operational semantics of unconditional fair paths in a Kripke structure. Highlight the role of the transition relation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a Kripke structure $M$ with states $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, initial state $s_0$, and actions $A = \{a, b\}$. Transitions are:
- $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1$
- $s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s_2$
- $s_1 \xrightarrow{a} s_3$
- $s_2 \xrightarrow{b} s_3$
- $s_3 \xrightarrow{a} s_3$ and $s_3 \xrightarrow{b} s_3$ (self-loop with both actions enabled)

Verify the liveness property $M, s_0 \models_{uncond} \Diamond s_3$ and show all valid paths. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

A Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ represents the system. Here $R \subseteq S \times S$ is the *transition relation*, and $L : S \to 2^{AP}$ labels each state with atomic propositions.

A *path* $\pi$ in $M$ is an infinite sequence $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ such that for all $i \ge 0$, $(s_i, s_{i+1}) \in R$. The set of *all* paths from $s_0$ is denoted $Paths(M, s_0)$.

The **unconditional fair path** subset is defined by:

$$
Paths_{uncond}(M, s_0) = \left\{ \pi \in Paths(M, s_0) \;\middle|\; \forall a \in A : \pi \models \Box \, \text{en}(a) \Rightarrow \Diamond \, \text{tk}(a) \right\}
$$

The transition relation $R$ *generates* the raw path universe; the fairness condition *filters* it.

**Diagram (Kripke Structure):**

```
        a
   s0 -----> s1
   |          |
   | b      a |
   v          v
   s2 -----> s3
        b
```
(Where $s_3$ has self-loops on both $a$ and $b$.)

**Operational Intuition:**
1. Start at $s_0$.
2. The transition relation $R$ permits the system to choose either $a$ or $b$.
3. A scheduler (environment) may attempt to "starve" one action. Unconditional fairness *forbids* this.
4. Hence, whichever action is *continuously enabled* must be taken.

**(Marks Breakdown: Diagram = 2, Definition of $R$ = 2, Filter formula = 2, Operational example = 1.)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Enumerate all paths from $s_0$.**

There are two major classes of paths:
- $\pi_{ab}$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1 \xrightarrow{a} s_3 \xrightarrow{*} s_3 \dots$
- $\pi_{ba}$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s_2 \xrightarrow{b} s_3 \xrightarrow{*} s_3 \dots$

(In $s_3$, both $a$ and $b$ are continuously enabled. The unconditional fairness rule forces *both* to be taken infinitely often, so $s_3$ must be visited infinitely often with both actions.)

**Step 2 — Check the fairness of each path.**
- $\pi_{ab}$: After step 0, action $a$ is taken. At $s_1$, action $b$ is *not* enabled (no $b$-transition from $s_1$). The antecedent $\Box \, \text{en}(b)$ is false → $b$'s fairness is vacuously satisfied. Action $a$ is continuously enabled in $s_3$ and is taken (the path visits $s_3$ via $a$). Fairness holds. ✓
- $\pi_{ba}$: Symmetric to $\pi_{ab}$. Action $b$ is enabled in $s_0$ and taken; in $s_3$, both are enabled and both are taken. Fairness holds. ✓

**Step 3 — Check the liveness property $\Diamond s_3$.**
- $\pi_{ab}$: state $s_3$ is reached at step 2 → property holds. ✓
- $\pi_{ba}$: state $s_3$ is reached at step 2 → property holds. ✓

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
All unconditionally fair paths satisfy the property:

$$
M, s_0 \models_{uncond} \Diamond s_3 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Compare and contrast unconditional fairness with weak and strong fairness using a common transition system example. Justify which one is the strictest. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Prove analytically that *unconditional fairness is strictly stronger than strong fairness*, and *strong fairness is strictly stronger than weak fairness*. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

Consider a transition system with two states $s_0$ and $s_1$, with actions $A = \{a, b\}$. Transitions:
- $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1$
- $s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s_0$ (self-loop)

**Three Example Paths:**
- $\pi_1$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1 \xrightarrow{a} s_1 \dots$ (action $a$ is continuously enabled in $s_1$, but is it taken? Yes if $s_1$ has a self-loop on $a$. Assume yes.)
- $\pi_2$: $s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s_0 \dots$ (action $a$ is *never* taken; action $b$ is always enabled and always taken).

**Fairness Evaluation of $\pi_2$:**

| Fairness Type | Status of $\pi_2$ | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Weak | Fair | $a$ is not enabled $\infty$-often (only at $s_0$ but is not taken), so antecedent $\Box \Diamond \text{en}(a)$ is false, and the obligation is vacuous. |
| Strong | Fair | $a$ is not eventually permanently enabled. The path is fair. |
| **Unconditional** | **UNFAIR** | Action $a$ is *always enabled* in $s_0$ (since $s_0$ has both $a$ and $b$ transitions). The path never takes $a$. Hence the antecedent $\Box \, \text{en}(a)$ is true but the conclusion $\Diamond \, \text{tk}(a)$ is false. **Violation.** |

**Conclusion:** Unconditional fairness is the *strictest* — it rejects $\pi_2$ which both weak and strong fairness accept.

**(Marks Breakdown: Example diagram = 2, Three-way table = 3, Justification = 2.)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Theorem 1 (Unconditional $\Rightarrow$ Strong):** Let $A$ be the action set. Consider any action $a \in A$. Unconditional fairness requires:

$$
\Box \, \text{en}(a) \implies \Diamond \, \text{tk}(a) \quad \text{(U)}
$$

If the antecedent of *strong fairness* holds, i.e., $\Diamond \Box \, \text{en}(a)$, then from some step $k$ onwards, $a$ is always enabled. But the unconditional rule (U) requires that whenever $a$ is *always* enabled, it must be *eventually* taken. So in particular, from step $k$ onwards, $a$ is taken at some step $j \ge k$. Now, since the unconditional rule keeps requiring this *at every future step* (because $a$ stays enabled), $a$ must be taken *infinitely often*. This is exactly the conclusion $\Box \Diamond \, \text{tk}(a)$ of strong fairness. Hence unconditional implies strong. ∎

**Theorem 2 (Strong $\Rightarrow$ Weak):** Suppose the antecedent of weak fairness holds: $\Box \Diamond \, \text{en}(a)$. We must show $\Box \Diamond \, \text{tk}(a)$. The weak antecedent gives a subsequence of steps $i_1, i_2, \dots$ where $a$ is enabled. Between these steps, the system may or may not have $a$ continuously enabled. However, the *strong* rule would be hard to apply directly. Instead, we reason as follows: assume for contradiction that $a$ is *not* taken after some step $N$. If it is enabled $\infty$-often, but never taken, the system must "disable" $a$ between enabled-steps. So $a$ is enabled, then disabled, then enabled, then disabled… This means $a$ is **not** eventually permanently enabled, hence the strong rule does not force a contradiction here. So we cannot derive weak from strong in general.

**Counterexample:** A path where $a$ is enabled at all even steps and disabled at all odd steps satisfies weak (if $a$ were taken at every even step, it would be taken $\infty$-often, but it isn't) — actually weak *requires* the consequent. Let us refine: a path where $a$ is enabled only at isolated steps but never taken satisfies neither weak nor strong. To get a path that satisfies strong but not weak, we need a path where $a$ is *eventually permanently enabled* but not taken $\infty$-often — impossible by strong fairness itself. Hence **strong $\Rightarrow$ weak** holds vacuously in the forward direction. ∎

**Conclusion:** Unconditional $>$ Strong $>$ Weak in the lattice of fairness conditions. (Marks: 3 for proof of U$\Rightarrow$S, 2 for S$\Rightarrow$W, 2 for conclusion.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "always" and "infinitely often"** — In CTL/LTL, $\Box$ means "always" (in all future steps) while $\Diamond$ means "eventually" (at least once in the future). The phrase "eventually permanently" is $\Diamond \Box$ and "infinitely often" is $\Box \Diamond$. Mixing these operators will cost you 2–3 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting to restrict the path quantifier** — When the question says "under unconditional fairness", you **must** restrict the path quantifier to $Paths_{uncond}(M, s_0)$. Writing just $\forall \pi \in Paths(M, s_0)$ will be marked partially correct only.
> 3. **Vacuously true fairness** — If an action is *never* enabled in a path, the fairness obligation is vacuously satisfied. This is a common KTU trick question.
> 4. **The action set $A$ must be explicitly stated** — Examiners allocate 1 mark just for properly defining $A$ in your answer.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Unconditional fairness = for every action $a$ in the action set $A$, if $a$ is *always* enabled in a path, it must be *eventually* taken.
- **LTL Formula:** $\Box \, \text{en}(a) \Rightarrow \Diamond \, \text{tk}(a)$ for each $a \in A$.
- **Strictness Ranking:** Unconditional $>$ Strong $>$ Weak $>$ No Fairness.
- **Effect on Safety:** None. Safety properties do not depend on fairness conditions.
- **Effect on Liveness:** Decisive. Without fairness, liveness may be falsified by "starved" counterexamples; with unconditional fairness, such counterexamples are pruned.
- **Path Quantifier:** Always restrict to $Paths_{uncond}(M, s_0)$ when verifying under unconditional fairness.
- **Vacuously True Rule:** If an action is never enabled, its fairness obligation is satisfied trivially.
- **Action Set $A$:** Finite and non-empty; must be declared explicitly in answers.
- **Algorithmic Implementation:** Model checkers like **SPIN** implement fairness via *fairness automata* and *Büchi acceptance*.
- **Use Cases:** Communication protocols, OS schedulers, distributed databases, VLSI arbitration.
- **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing $\forall \pi \in Paths(M, s_0)$ instead of $\forall \pi \in Paths_{uncond}(M, s_0)$ — this costs marks.
- **3-Mark Definition Template:** "Unconditional fairness ensures that every continuously enabled action in the action set $A$ is eventually taken along an execution path of the system."
- **7-Mark Diagram Template:** Always draw the Kripke structure, label the actions, list the paths, mark which are fair/unfair, then evaluate the property.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weak and Strong Fairness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Weak and Strong Fairness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Concept of Fairness in Model Checking

In the formal verification of concurrent and reactive systems, **fairness** is a critical semantic condition that restricts the set of execution paths considered during model checking. A concurrent system may have infinitely many possible execution paths, but many of these represent unrealistic scenarios where some process or transition is perpetually ignored, even when it is ready to make progress. Such paths are called **unfair paths**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fairness Assumption (KTU 2024 Definition):** Fairness is an abstraction mechanism that eliminates unrealistic infinite execution paths, ensuring that every component of a concurrent system that is "ready" infinitely often will eventually get a chance to execute.

Without fairness assumptions, many **liveness properties** (i.e., "something good eventually happens") cannot be verified. For example, in a mutual exclusion protocol, the property *"Process P will eventually enter the critical section"* can only be proved under a fairness assumption — otherwise, an adversarial scheduler could starve P forever.

## 1.2 Weak Fairness (Justice)

**Definition:** A transition (or statement) $t$ satisfies **Weak Fairness (WF)** if it cannot be continuously enabled without eventually being taken. In other words, if $t$ is enabled at all states from some point onward, it must be executed infinitely often.

In **Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)**, weak fairness is expressed as:

$$
\Diamond\Box\, \text{enabled}_t \;\Rightarrow\; \Box\Diamond\, \text{executed}_t
$$

This reads as: *"If transition $t$ is eventually always enabled, then it is infinitely often executed."*

## 1.3 Strong Fairness (Compassion)

**Definition:** A transition $t$ satisfies **Strong Fairness (SF)** if it is taken infinitely often whenever it is enabled infinitely often. Strong fairness does not require the transition to be *continuously* enabled; mere *infrequent* enablement still forces eventual execution.

In LTL, strong fairness is expressed as:

$$
\Box\Diamond\, \text{enabled}_t \;\Rightarrow\; \Box\Diamond\, \text{executed}_t
$$

This reads as: *"If transition $t$ is enabled infinitely often, then it is executed infinitely often."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Strong Fairness has a **weaker hypothesis** (only infinitely often enabled) and a **stronger conclusion** (must fire infinitely often). Therefore, **SF $\Rightarrow$ WF** when restricted to the same transition — strong fairness implies weak fairness because continuous enablement is a special case of infinite enablement.

## 1.4 Real-World Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a single-server **bank teller counter** serving two queues:

- **Weak Fairness Analogy:** Suppose Customer A is standing at the front of the queue **without ever leaving**. Weak fairness guarantees the teller will eventually serve A. But if A repeatedly steps out of the queue and rejoins at the back, weak fairness offers **no guarantee** — the teller may never serve A.

- **Strong Fairness Analogy:** Even if Customer A keeps stepping out and rejoining (i.e., is *enabled* infinitely often but not *continuously* enabled), strong fairness **still** guarantees eventual service. This is a more realistic model of a process repeatedly becoming ready in a concurrent system.

## 1.5 Engineering Significance

- **Mutual Exclusion Protocols** (e.g., Peterson's, Dekker's): Weak fairness suffices since the "trying" condition is held continuously.
- **Asynchronous Communication Protocols** (e.g., network routers, I/O subsystems): Strong fairness is required because a process may be enabled sporadically depending on external events.
- **Operating System Schedulers**: Round-robin scheduling embodies strong fairness, since any runnable process appears in the ready queue infinitely often.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Concepts

A **Kripke Structure** (or state-transition graph) is the formal model used to represent concurrent systems:

$$
M \;=\; (S,\; S_0,\; R,\; L)
$$

where:
- $S$ is a finite set of states,
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of initial states,
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ is the transition relation,
- $L : S \to 2^{AP}$ is the labeling function (assigns atomic propositions to states).

A **path** $\pi$ from a state $s_0$ is an infinite sequence:

$$
\pi \;=\; s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to \cdots
$$

## 2.2 The Operational Breakdown

Let us denote a transition (or guard condition) by $g$, and the corresponding action (or statement) by $a$. A state $s$ is said to be **$g$-enabled** if the guard $g$ holds in $s$. The transition **fires** when $a$ executes.

### Step-by-Step Logic for Weak Fairness

1. **Identify the guard $g_t$** of transition $t$.
2. **Check continuity:** Determine if there exists some position $k$ on path $\pi$ such that $g_t$ holds at every $s_i$ for $i \geq k$.
3. **Apply WF condition:** If step 2 holds, then $t$ must execute infinitely often on $\pi$.
4. **Otherwise:** No obligation — if the guard is dropped even once, the transition may never fire.

### Step-by-Step Logic for Strong Fairness

1. **Identify the guard $g_t$** of transition $t$.
2. **Check recurrence:** Determine if $g_t$ holds at infinitely many states $s_{i_1}, s_{i_2}, \ldots$ on $\pi$.
3. **Apply SF condition:** If step 2 holds, then $t$ must execute infinitely often on $\pi$.
4. **Otherwise:** If the guard is enabled only finitely many times, $t$ need not fire.

## 2.3 Hierarchy and Implication

$$
\text{Strong Fairness} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Weak Fairness}
$$

This is because:

$$
\Diamond\Box\, g \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Box\Diamond\, g
$$

(Continuous enablement from some point implies infinitely many enabled states.)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formal Notation (LTL)** | **Hypothesis (LHS)** | **Conclusion (RHS)** | **Strength** |
|-------------|---------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Weak Fairness (Justice) | $\Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ | $\Diamond\Box\, g$ (eventually always enabled) | $\Box\Diamond\, a$ (infinitely often taken) | Weaker guarantee |
| Strong Fairness (Compassion) | $\Box\Diamond\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ | $\Box\Diamond\, g$ (infinitely often enabled) | $\Box\Diamond\, a$ (infinitely often taken) | Stronger guarantee |
| Unconditional Liveness | $\Box\Diamond\, a$ | — | Always eventually fires | Strictest |
| Deadlock-Freedom | $\Box\, \Diamond\, \text{progress}$ | — | Always makes progress | No fairness needed |
| Generalized SF | $\bigwedge_{t \in T} \Box\Diamond\, g_t \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a_t$ | All guards enabled i.o. | All actions taken i.o. | Multi-transition form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, the symbol $\Rightarrow$ denotes **LTL implication**, not arithmetic. The horizontal line is the *entails* operator. In KTU board exams, the right-arrow form $\to$ is also accepted.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications Protocols (3GPP, 5G):** SF is used to model base station handovers where a mobile device is *enabled* for handover only when signal degrades (sporadic event).
- **Embedded Control Systems (AUTOSAR):** WF suffices for periodic tasks that are *always* schedulable.
- **Distributed Consensus (Paxos, Raft):** SF is essential because proposers are *enabled* only when receiving client requests (infrequent but recurring).
- **Hardware Verification (RTL, SystemVerilog Assertions):** SF is used to model arbitration logic where request lines are intermittent.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: LTL Expansion of Weak Fairness

**Goal:** Expand the WF formula $\Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ into state-wise LTL semantics on a path.

A path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ satisfies $\Diamond\Box\, g$ if and only if there exists an index $k$ such that for all $j \geq k$, $g$ holds at $s_j$. The LTL expansion yields:

$$
\pi \models \Diamond\Box\, g \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \exists\, k \geq 0 \;:\; \forall\, j \geq k \;:\; \pi[j] \models g
$$

where $\pi[j]$ denotes the $j$-th state of the path.

Similarly, the consequent $\Box\Diamond\, a$ means:

$$
\pi \models \Box\Diamond\, a \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall\, i \geq 0 \;:\; \exists\, j \geq i \;:\; \pi[j] \models a
$$

Combining both via LTL implication $\Rightarrow$:

$$
\pi \models WF \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall\, k \geq 0 \;\Big( \big(\forall\, j \geq k : g(s_j)\big) \;\Rightarrow\; \big(\forall\, i \geq 0 \; \exists\, j \geq i : a(s_j)\big) \Big)
$$

**Interpretation:** If there is **any** point $k$ from which the guard is permanently true, then the action must occur at **infinitely many** positions on the path.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LTL Expansion of Strong Fairness

**Goal:** Expand the SF formula $\Box\Diamond\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$.

The hypothesis $\Box\Diamond\, g$ expands as:

$$
\pi \models \Box\Diamond\, g \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \forall\, i \geq 0 \;:\; \exists\, j \geq i \;:\; g(s_j)
$$

This is the **infinitely often enabled** condition — at every position $i$, there is some later position $j$ where the guard fires.

The conclusion is identical to WF: $\Box\Diamond\, a$. Therefore:

$$
\pi \models SF \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \Big( \forall\, i \geq 0 \; \exists\, j \geq i : g(s_j) \Big) \;\Rightarrow\; \Big( \forall\, i \geq 0 \; \exists\, j \geq i : a(s_j) \Big)
$$

**Interpretation:** The action must fire at infinitely many positions whenever the guard is satisfied at infinitely many positions — even if those positions are interspersed with disabled states.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Formal Proof of SF $\Rightarrow$ WF

**Claim:** $\Box\Diamond\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ logically entails $\Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Assume} \quad \pi \models \Box\Diamond\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a \\
&\text{Let} \quad \pi \models \Diamond\Box\, g \\
&\text{Then} \quad \exists\, k \;:\; \forall\, j \geq k : g(s_j) \\
&\text{This implies} \quad \forall\, i \geq 0 \;:\; \exists\, j \geq \max(i, k) : g(s_j) \\
&\text{Hence} \quad \pi \models \Box\Diamond\, g \\
&\text{By our assumption, since } \pi \models \Box\Diamond\, g, \text{ we have } \pi \models \Box\Diamond\, a \\
&\text{Therefore} \quad \pi \models \Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Justification (English):** Continuous enablement from some point is a special case of infinite enablement. Therefore, if SF is satisfied on the path, WF is automatically satisfied as well.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Counter-Example Showing WF $\not\Rightarrow$ SF

Consider a transition $t$ that is enabled on every second state:

$$
\pi \;:\; s_0 \xrightarrow{\neg g} s_1 \xrightarrow{g} s_2 \xrightarrow{\neg g} s_3 \xrightarrow{g} s_4 \xrightarrow{\neg g} \cdots
$$

**Check WF hypothesis:** $\Diamond\Box\, g$ does **not** hold because the guard is not continuously enabled. So WF imposes no obligation.

**Check SF hypothesis:** $\Box\Diamond\, g$ holds because the guard is true at every odd-indexed state. SF requires the action to fire infinitely often.

If $t$ never fires on $\pi$, then $\pi$ violates SF but vacuously satisfies WF. **Therefore, WF does not imply SF.**

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Weak Fairness** | **Strong Fairness** |
|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| LTL form | $\Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ | $\Box\Diamond\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ |
| Hypothesis type | Eventual permanence | Infinite recurrence |
| Hypotheses strength | Stronger (harder to satisfy) | Weaker (easier to satisfy) |
| Conclusion | Same: $\Box\Diamond\, a$ | Same: $\Box\Diamond\, a$ |
| Overall guarantee | Weaker (easier to discharge) | Stronger (harder to discharge) |
| Typical use case | Continuously waiting processes | Sporadically enabled processes |
| Counter-example type | Intermittent enablement | Disabled but not forever |
| Real-world fit | Periodic tasks, busy-wait locks | Network I/O, event-driven handlers |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example: Kripke Structure

Consider a 3-state Kripke structure modeling a producer-consumer lock:

$$
S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}, \quad S_0 = \{s_0\}
$$

Transitions: $s_0 \to s_1$, $s_0 \to s_2$, $s_1 \to s_1$, $s_1 \to s_0$, $s_2 \to s_2$, $s_2 \to s_0$.

Suppose transition $t_1 : s_0 \to s_1$ has guard $g_1$ (true only at $s_0$).

**Path A:** $s_0 \to s_2 \to s_2 \to s_2 \to \cdots$

- $g_1$ is enabled only at $s_0$ (position 0), then never again. So $\Box\Diamond\, g_1$ is **false**.
- Both WF and SF are **vacuously true**.

**Path B:** $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_0 \to s_1 \to s_0 \to \cdots$

- $g_1$ is enabled at all even positions. $\Diamond\Box\, g_1$ is **false** (interleaved with $s_1$).
- $\Box\Diamond\, g_1$ is **true**.
- WF imposes no obligation. **SF requires $t_1$ to fire infinitely often — which it does.**

**Conclusion:** Path B satisfies SF but not necessarily WF (since WF hypothesis fails). This empirically demonstrates that SF is the correct fairness notion for event-driven systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State-Transition Flow Comparing WF and SF

The following Mermaid block diagrams the execution path for a transition $t$ with guard $g$ and action $a$. It visually contrasts Weak Fairness and Strong Fairness using a Block-Level Functional Architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Path Start s0]) --> n1
    n1[State s1: g is FALSE] --> n2
    n2[State s2: g is TRUE] --> n3
    n3{Did action a fire?}
    n3 -- YES --> n4[State s3: g is FALSE]
    n3 -- NO --> n5[State s4: g is TRUE again]
    n4 --> n6{Weak Fairness Check}
    n5 --> n7{Strong Fairness Check}
    n6 --> n8{Hypothesis: eventually always g holds?}
    n8 -- NO --> n9[WF Vacuously Satisfied]
    n8 -- YES --> n10[WF Requires a to fire i.o.]
    n7 --> n11{Hypothesis: g holds infinitely often?}
    n11 -- NO --> n12[SF Vacuously Satisfied]
    n11 -- YES --> n13[SF Requires a to fire i.o.]
    n9 --> nodeEnd([Path Continuation])
    n10 --> nodeEnd
    n12 --> nodeEnd
    n13 --> nodeEnd
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy of Fairness Notions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[No Fairness Assumption] --> B[Weak Fairness WF]
    B --> C[Strong Fairness SF]
    C --> D[Unconditional Liveness]
    nA[All paths considered] -.->|Includes unfair starvation paths| A
    nB[Continuous enablement forced] -.->|Eliminate some unfair paths| B
    nC[Recurrent enablement forced] -.->|Eliminate more unfair paths| C
    nD[Always fires] -.->|Strictest guarantee| D
```

## 4.3 Path Topology for Strong Fairness Failure

This block diagram represents a path that **violates** strong fairness — the guard is enabled infinitely often but the action never fires.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p0[State 0: g FALSE] --> p1
    p1[State 1: g TRUE - enabled] --> p2
    p2[State 2: g TRUE - enabled] --> p3
    p3[State 3: g TRUE - enabled] --> p4
    p4[State 4: g TRUE - enabled] --> p5
    p5[State 5: g TRUE - enabled] --> p6
    p6[State 6: g TRUE - enabled] --> p7
    p7[Action a NEVER fires]
    p7 --> p8[Path Loops Infinitely]
    p8 --> p1
    sfViolation{Strong Fairness Hypothesis TRUE}
    sfViolation -->|Box Diamond g holds| p7
    p7 --> sfFail{SF Consequence Fails: Box Diamond a is FALSE}
```

## 4.4 Path Topology for Weak Fairness Satisfaction

This block diagram represents a path that **satisfies** weak fairness despite sporadic enablement — the guard drops occasionally, so the WF hypothesis fails and no obligation is imposed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q0[State 0: g TRUE] --> q1
    q1[State 1: g FALSE - disabled] --> q2
    q2[State 2: g TRUE] --> q3
    q3[State 3: g FALSE - disabled] --> q4
    q4[State 4: g TRUE] --> q5
    q5[State 5: g FALSE - disabled] --> q6
    q6[State 6: g TRUE] --> q7
    q7[WF Hypothesis Diamond Box g is FALSE - guard never continuous]
    q7 --> q8[WF Vacuously Satisfied - no obligation]
    q8 --> q9[Path may continue forever without firing a]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each `State X` block represents a discrete state in the Kripke structure. The labels `g TRUE` / `g FALSE` indicate whether the guard condition is enabled. The Mermaid diagrams use simple uppercase alphanumeric text inside node labels to comply with rendering safety rules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECNT526]`
**Define Strong Fairness in the context of model checking. How does it differ from Weak Fairness?**

**Model Answer:**

Strong Fairness (also called **Compassion**) is a fairness condition that requires a transition $t$ to be executed infinitely often if it is *enabled* infinitely often, regardless of whether it is enabled continuously or sporadically. Formally, for a transition with guard $g$ and action $a$:

$$
\Box\Diamond\, g \;\Rightarrow\; \Box\Diamond\, a
$$

The key difference from Weak Fairness (Justice) is the **hypothesis**: Weak Fairness requires the guard to be *continuously* enabled from some point ($\Diamond\Box\, g$), whereas Strong Fairness only requires *recurrent* enablement ($\Box\Diamond\, g$). Since continuous enablement is a special case of recurrent enablement, every Strong Fairness condition is logically stronger (and harder to verify) than its Weak Fairness counterpart. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECNT526]`
**Give the LTL formulation of Weak Fairness for a transition $t$ with guard $g$ and action $a$, and explain each operator.**

**Model Answer:**

The LTL formulation of Weak Fairness is:

$$
\Diamond\Box\, g \;\Rightarrow\; \Box\Diamond\, a
$$

Explanation of each operator:
- **$\Diamond$ (Finally/Eventually):** $\Diamond\, \phi$ means $\phi$ holds at some future position on the path.
- **$\Box$ (Globally/Always):** $\Box\, \phi$ means $\phi$ holds at every position on the path.
- **$\Diamond\Box\, g$:** "Eventually always $g$" — there is some position from which $g$ holds forever.
- **$\Box\Diamond\, a$:** "Always eventually $a$" — $a$ holds at infinitely many positions.
- **$\Rightarrow$ (LTL Implication):** The antecedent must entail the consequent on every fair path.

Thus, WF says: *"If the guard is eventually always enabled, then the action must fire infinitely often."* **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECNT526, Module 1]`

**(a)** Define Weak Fairness and Strong Fairness formally. Show with a formal proof that **Strong Fairness implies Weak Fairness**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a Kripke structure with three states and a transition $t$ whose guard $g$ is enabled at only every alternate state. Demonstrate a path that satisfies Weak Fairness but violates Strong Fairness. Justify your answer using LTL semantics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Definition of Weak Fairness (Justice):**
A transition $t$ with guard $g$ and action $a$ is *weakly fair* if every fair execution path $\pi$ satisfies:

$$
\pi \models \Diamond\Box\, g \;\Rightarrow\; \Box\Diamond\, a
$$

**Definition of Strong Fairness (Compassion):**
A transition $t$ with guard $g$ and action $a$ is *strongly fair* if every fair execution path $\pi$ satisfies:

$$
\pi \models \Box\Diamond\, g \;\Rightarrow\; \Box\Diamond\, a
$$

**Proof that SF $\Rightarrow$ WF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Assume } \pi \models \Box\Diamond\, g \;\Rightarrow\; \Box\Diamond\, a \quad \text{(SF holds)} \\
&\text{Let } \pi \models \Diamond\Box\, g \quad \text{(WF hypothesis)} \\
&\text{This means } \exists\, k \geq 0 \;:\; \forall\, j \geq k \;:\; \pi[j] \models g \\
&\text{Then for any } i \geq 0, \text{ we have } \max(i, k) \geq k, \text{ so } \pi[\max(i, k)] \models g \\
&\text{Therefore } \forall\, i \geq 0 \;:\; \exists\, j \geq i \;:\; \pi[j] \models g \\
&\text{This is precisely } \pi \models \Box\Diamond\, g \\
&\text{By our SF assumption, } \pi \models \Box\Diamond\, a \\
&\text{Hence } \pi \models \Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

The proof is complete in 5 lines. **(7 marks — distributed as 2 marks for definitions, 4 marks for the proof, 1 mark for conclusion.)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Kripke Structure Construction:**

$$
S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}, \quad S_0 = \{s_0\}
$$

Transitions: $s_0 \to s_1$, $s_1 \to s_2$, $s_2 \to s_0$.

Guard $g$ for transition $t$ holds at $s_0$ and $s_2$ (the "even" cycle positions), but **not** at $s_1$.

**Construct the Path $\pi$:**

$$
\pi \;:\; s_0 \xrightarrow{t \text{ enabled}} s_1 \xrightarrow{t \text{ disabled}} s_2 \xrightarrow{t \text{ enabled}} s_1 \xrightarrow{t \text{ disabled}} s_2 \to \cdots
$$

where $t$ **never fires** on $\pi$ (it remains perpetually enabled but always skipped).

**LTL Semantics Verification:**

1. **Guard enabled pattern:** $g$ holds at positions $0, 2, 4, 6, \ldots$ (infinitely many).
2. **WF Hypothesis:** $\Diamond\Box\, g$ is **FALSE** because $g$ is false at every odd position (never continuously enabled from any point onward).
3. **WF Conclusion:** $\Box\Diamond\, a$ is **FALSE** (action $a$ never fires), but since the WF hypothesis is false, the WF formula $\Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ is **vacuously TRUE**.
4. **SF Hypothesis:** $\Box\Diamond\, g$ is **TRUE** because $g$ holds at infinitely many positions.
5. **SF Conclusion:** $\Box\Diamond\, a$ is **FALSE** (action never fires).
6. **SF Formula:** TRUE $\Rightarrow$ FALSE = **FALSE** — SF is **VIOLATED**.

**Conclusion:** Path $\pi$ satisfies WF vacuously but violates SF — proving that **WF does not imply SF**. **(7 marks — distributed as 2 marks for Kripke structure, 3 marks for LTL evaluation, 2 marks for conclusion.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECNT526, Module 1]`

**(a)** With a neat example of a producer-consumer system, explain why fairness assumptions are necessary for verifying liveness properties. Distinguish between Weak Fairness and Strong Fairness in this context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 2-process mutual exclusion protocol where Process P1 and P2 alternate between *trying*, *critical*, and *remainder* sections. Express in LTL the conditions: (i) Mutual Exclusion is preserved, (ii) Process P1 eventually enters the critical section under Weak Fairness, (iii) Process P1 eventually enters the critical section under Strong Fairness. Explain why the choice between WF and SF matters when the "trying" section can be interrupted by I/O events. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Example — Producer-Consumer with Bounded Buffer:**

Consider a producer process $P$ and consumer process $C$ communicating via a buffer of capacity 1. The producer is *enabled* (i.e., ready to write) only when the buffer is empty; the consumer is *enabled* only when the buffer is full.

**Why fairness is necessary for liveness verification:**

The liveness property *"the consumer will eventually consume a produced item"* cannot be proved without fairness. Consider the adversarial (unfair) path where the consumer is *always* skipped by the scheduler — even though it is *continuously* enabled (buffer permanently full). The system would deadlock from the consumer's perspective, but the Kripke structure would still contain this path. Fairness assumptions eliminate such paths:

- **Weak Fairness** for the consumer's "consume" transition guarantees that if the buffer is **permanently full** from some point, the consumer fires.
- **Strong Fairness** is required if the producer and consumer are *event-driven* — e.g., the consumer is enabled only when an I/O interrupt fires. The buffer may oscillate between full and empty, so the consumer is *infinitely often* enabled but not continuously.

**Distinguishing WF and SF:**

| Aspect | Weak Fairness | Strong Fairness |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| LTL for consume | $\Diamond\Box\, \text{full} \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, \text{consume}$ | $\Box\Diamond\, \text{full} \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, \text{consume}$ |
| When needed | Buffer stays full indefinitely | Buffer oscillates due to async events |
| Verifier cost | Lower | Higher (more paths to check) |

**(7 marks — 3 for example, 2 for necessity argument, 2 for distinction.)**

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

Let $T_1, T_2$ denote that processes P1, P2 are in the *trying* section, and $C_1, C_2$ denote they are in the *critical* section.

**(i) Mutual Exclusion (Safety Property):**

$$
\Box\, \neg(C_1 \wedge C_2)
$$

This is a safety property and does **not** require any fairness assumption — it must hold on **all** paths.

**(ii) Liveness under Weak Fairness:**

$$
\Diamond\Box\, T_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \Box\Diamond\, C_1
$$

This says: *"If P1 remains in the trying section forever, then P1 must enter the critical section infinitely often."* Suitable when P1 is in a *busy-wait loop* (continuously trying).

**(iii) Liveness under Strong Fairness:**

$$
\Box\Diamond\, T_1 \;\Rightarrow\; \Box\Diamond\, C_1
$$

This says: *"If P1 is in the trying section infinitely often, then P1 enters the critical section infinitely often."* Suitable when P1's "trying" status is interrupted by I/O events.

**Why the choice matters under I/O interruption:**

If P1's *trying* section includes a blocking `read()` system call that suspends the process until data arrives, then P1 is **not continuously trying** — it is in trying state only when data is available. A weak fairness assumption would not guarantee progress, because the hypothesis $\Diamond\Box\, T_1$ would fail (P1 is in trying only intermittently). Only **Strong Fairness** can capture the liveness guarantee in this realistic scenario, because the hypothesis $\Box\Diamond\, T_1$ holds naturally (P1 is enabled infinitely often due to recurring I/O events).

**(7 marks — 2 marks for each LTL formula, 1 mark for the comparison, 2 marks for the I/O discussion.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Reversing WF and SF:** Many students incorrectly state that SF is "easier" than WF. In fact, **SF makes a stronger guarantee** (it has a weaker hypothesis), making it **harder for the model checker to verify** because fewer paths satisfy it vacuously.
> 2. **Confusing the operator direction:** $\Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ is **NOT** the same as $\Box\Diamond\, g \Rightarrow \Diamond\Box\, a$. The former is WF; the latter is a meaningless formula (would require action to hold *continuously from some point*, which is essentially deadlock).
> 3. **Forgetting vacuous truth:** Students often forget that an implication with a false hypothesis is automatically true. This is why a path can satisfy WF vacuously even when the action never fires.
> 4. **Mixing CTL and LTL:** KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **LTL** for fairness in Module 1. CTL path quantifiers ($A$, $E$) are introduced later. Do not write $A(\Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a)$ in Part A answers unless explicitly asked.
> 5. **Losing 2 marks for missing the example:** When asked to "explain with an example," always provide a concrete Kripke structure or system, not just the LTL formula. Examiners specifically allocate 2–3 marks for the example.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Weak and Strong Fairness**

- ✅ **Weak Fairness (Justice):** $\Diamond\Box\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ — *eventually always enabled* forces *infinitely often taken*.
- ✅ **Strong Fairness (Compassion):** $\Box\Diamond\, g \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond\, a$ — *infinitely often enabled* forces *infinitely often taken*.
- ✅ **Implication Direction:** SF $\Rightarrow$ WF (stronger guarantee, weaker hypothesis).
- ✅ **Liveness Properties:** Cannot be verified without fairness assumptions.
- ✅ **Use WF when:** the guard is held continuously (busy-wait, periodic tasks, polling loops).
- ✅ **Use SF when:** the guard is held intermittently (event-driven, I/O-bound, network protocols).
- ✅ **Vacuous Truth:** An implication with false hypothesis is automatically true — critical for exam answers.
- ✅ **KTU LTL Operators:** Memorize $\Diamond$ (eventually), $\Box$ (always), $\Rightarrow$ (implies), $\wedge$ (and), $\vee$ (or), $\neg$ (not).
- ✅ **Kripke Structure Components:** $S$, $S_0$, $R$, $L$ — state set, initial states, transition relation, labeling.
- ✅ **Path Definition:** Infinite sequence $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to \cdots$ — fairness is a property of *paths*, not single states.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Specific:** The syllabus uses LTL (not CTL) for fairness in Module 1 — be careful with operator nesting.
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Do **not** confuse $\Diamond\Box$ (eventually always) with $\Box\Diamond$ (always eventually). The order matters!
- ✅ **Examiner Tip:** Always quote the **LTL formula** and provide at least **one LTL expansion** in 14-mark answers to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fairness Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fairness Strategies # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Fairness in Model Checking? **Fairness** is a fundamental concept in the verification of **concurrent**, **distributed**, and **reactive systems**. When verifying such systems using temporal logic (CTL, LTL, CTL*), the underlying computation tree may contain infinitely many paths, including ones that are mathematically valid but practically impossible or unrealistic. > [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)** > **Fairness** is a constraint imposed on the set of execution paths of a Kripke structure to eliminate behaviors that are syntactically valid but semantically unrealistic — for example, a process that is "continuously enabled" but never gets scheduled to run. In KTU terminology, a **fairness strategy** is a formal mechanism that restricts the set of paths under consideration to those in which certain "fair" conditions are satisfied infinitely often. The set of fair conditions is denoted **F = {f₁, f₂, ..., fₙ}**, where each fᵢ is a set of states (or a fairness constraint expressed as an LTL/CTL formula). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: The Round-Robin CPU Scheduler** > Imagine **four processes (P1, P2, P3, P4)** sharing a single CPU core with a queue. > - **Unconditional Fairness (Justice)**: *"Every process must execute infinitely often."* — Even if P1 just finished, it must get the CPU again eventually. No process is starved *absolutely*. > - **Strong Fairness (Compassion)**: *"If a process is continuously waiting, it will eventually run."* — Once P2 enters the ready queue and stays there, it cannot be ignored forever. > - **Weak Fairness**: *"If a process is eventually enabled and stays enabled, it must fire."* — Slightly weaker than strong fairness in formal logic. > > In verification, an *unfair* counterexample is like saying *"P1 runs forever and P2 never gets scheduled"* — mathematically allowed by the model, but practically absurd. ## 1.3 Why Do We Need Fairness? Without fairness, model checkers (like **SPIN**, **NuSMV**, **UPPAAL**) may produce **spurious counterexamples** — paths that satisfy negation of a property but represent impossible executions. Fairness eliminates this: - Reduces **state explosion** indirectly by pruning unfair branches. - Makes **liveness properties** (`◇p`, `□◇p`) verifiable without false negatives. - Models **realistic scheduling** assumptions of OS, networks, and protocols. ## 1.4 The Three Canonical Fairness Strategies | # | Strategy | LTL Form | Intuition | |---|----------|----------|-----------| | 1 | **Unconditional Fairness (Justice)** | $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Box\Diamond f_i$ | Each condition holds infinitely often, regardless of whether it was enabled. | | 2 | **Strong Fairness (Compassion)** | $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Box\Diamond \text{enabled}(f_i) \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond f_i$ | If condition is *infinitely often* enabled, it must be *infinitely often* taken. | | 3 | **Weak Fairness** | $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Diamond\Box \text{enabled}(f_i) \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond f_i$ | If condition is *eventually always* enabled, it must occur infinitely often. | > [!IMPORTANT] **Hierarchy of Strengths** > $$\text{Strong Fairness} \Rightarrow \text{Unconditional Fairness} \Rightarrow \text{Weak Fairness}$$ > The stronger the fairness, the more paths are filtered out, leading to a smaller (more refined) verification problem. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fair Kripke Structure — Formal Definition A **Fair Kripke Structure** is a 6-tuple: $$M = (S, S_0, R, L, F, AP)$$ where: - **S** — finite set of **states**. - **S₀ ⊆ S** — set of **initial states**. - **R ⊆ S × S** — total **transition relation** (every state has at least one successor). - **L : S → 2^AP** — **labeling function** mapping each state to a set of atomic propositions from **AP**. - **F ⊆ 2^S** — set of **fairness constraints** (each $f_i \in F$ is a subset of S). - **AP** — set of **atomic propositions**. > [!NOTE] The pair $(M, s)$ where $s \in S_0$ denotes a *fair model* rooted at $s$. ## 2.2 Path Semantics Under Fairness An infinite path in $M$ is: $$\pi = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow s_3 \rightarrow \cdots$$ The set of states visited infinitely often along $\pi$ is: $$\text{Inf}(\pi) = \{s \in S \mid s \text{ appears infinitely often in } \pi\}$$ A path $\pi$ is **fair** with respect to fairness set $F$ if and only if: $$\forall f \in F : f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset$$ That is, **every fairness condition must be visited infinitely often** along the path. ## 2.3 Fair Satisfaction Relation For a CTL* formula $\varphi$ and state $s$ in a fair structure $M$: $$M, s \models_F \varphi \iff \forall \pi \text{ starting at } s, \text{ if } \pi \text{ is fair then } \pi \models \varphi$$ For **CTL** quantifiers specifically: - **M, s ⊨_F E ψ** iff there exists a **fair path** $\pi$ from $s$ such that $\pi \models \psi$. - **M, s ⊨_F A ψ** iff for **all fair paths** $\pi$ from $s$, we have $\pi \models \psi$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Application | |------------------|---------|-------------| | $F = \{f_1, \ldots, f_n\}$ | Set of fairness constraints | Defines "fairness assumption" | | $\text{Inf}(\pi)$ | Set of states visited infinitely often | Used to test path fairness | | $\forall f \in F : f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset$ | Fairness predicate on a path | Decides if a path is fair | | $M, s \models_F \varphi$ | Fair satisfaction relation | CTL/LTL with fairness | | $\Box\Diamond f_i$ (LTL) | $f_i$ holds infinitely often | Unconditional fairness | | $\Box\Diamond \text{en}(f_i) \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond f_i$ | Strong fairness | Compassion condition | | $\Diamond\Box \text{en}(f_i) \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond f_i$ | Weak fairness | Justice-like, weaker | | $\pi \models_F \varphi$ | Path $\pi$ satisfies $\varphi$ under fairness | LTL/CTL* model checking | | $\text{Fair}(M) = \{\pi \in \text{Paths}(M) \mid \pi \text{ fair w.r.t. } F\}$ | Set of fair paths | Subset of all paths | | $F_{\text{strong}} \subseteq F_{\text{uncond}} \subseteq F_{\text{weak}}$ | Implication chain | Strength ordering | > [!IMPORTANT] **KRITISCHE RULE (Critical Rule)** > In CTL model checking under fairness, the **EX** and **EU** operators are computed by considering only fair successors. The standard fixpoint must be intersected with the fairness set $F$. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Fairness Strategies | Field | Application of Fairness | |-------|------------------------| | **Operating Systems** | Process scheduling verification (no starvation). | | **Network Protocols** | Token-ring / leader election — every node gets a chance. | | **Hardware Design** | Bus arbitration, cache coherency protocols (MESI). | | **Distributed Systems** | Consensus algorithms (Paxos, Raft) — every proposer must get response. | | **Embedded Control** | Real-time task scheduling on microcontrollers. | | **Security Protocols** | Ensuring intruder cannot permanently block a legitimate agent. | ## 2.6 Logical Hierarchy of Fairness > [!NOTE] For any Kripke structure $M$ and any formula $\varphi$: > $$\text{Strong Fairness} \vdash \text{Unconditional Fairness} \vdash \text{Weak Fairness}$$ > Therefore: if $M \models_{\text{strong}} \varphi$ then $M \models_{\text{uncond}} \varphi$ then $M \models_{\text{weak}} \varphi$. In model-checking tools like **SPIN**, fairness is specified via **`fairness { ... }`** blocks in the **PROMELA** language, which the verifier compiles into additional Büchi acceptance conditions. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Path to Fair Path **Given**: A Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L, F, AP)$ and an infinite path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ **To Prove**: $\pi$ is fair iff for all $f \in F$, $f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset$. ### Step-by-Step Deduction **Step 1 — Define the state visitation pattern of $\pi$.** Every state $s_i$ in the path occurs at some index $i \in \mathbb{N}$. The set of states that appear more than once (i.e., at infinitely many indices) is: $$\text{Inf}(\pi) = \{s \in S \mid \forall n \in \mathbb{N}, \exists k \geq n : s_k = s\}$$ **Step 2 — Interpret the fairness constraint.** Each $f \in F$ is a subset of $S$, representing a "fairness obligation" — a set of states that the path is *required* to visit infinitely often. **Step 3 — Establish the necessary condition.** Suppose $\pi$ is fair. By definition, every $f \in F$ must be visited infinitely often. This means that for each $f$, there exists some $s \in f$ such that $s$ appears in $\pi$ infinitely many times. Hence: $$\forall f \in F : f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset$$ **Step 4 — Establish the sufficient condition.** Conversely, suppose for some $\pi$, $\forall f \in F$, $f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset$. Then for each $f$, there is at least one state $s \in f$ appearing infinitely often in $\pi$, meaning the path satisfies the fairness requirement for every constraint. Therefore, $\pi$ is fair. **Step 5 — Combine both directions.** The two conditions are equivalent by construction: $$\pi \text{ is fair} \iff \forall f \in F : f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Fixpoint Characterization of Fair Satisfaction For the CTL formula $\text{E}(\varphi \,\text{U}\, \psi)$ under fairness $F$, the standard least fixpoint is modified as follows. ### Standard (Non-Fair) Computation $$\text{E}(\varphi \,\text{U}\, \psi) = \mu Z .\, \psi \lor (\varphi \land \text{EX}\, Z)$$ ### Fair Modification The fair version requires every continuation of $Z$ to eventually visit a fairness constraint. This is achieved by intersecting with the **greatest fixpoint** of "always staying in fair-progress states": $$\text{E}_F(\varphi \,\text{U}\, \psi) = \mu Z .\, \psi \lor (\varphi \land \text{EX}\, Z) \land \nu Y .\, \bigvee_{f \in F} \left( \text{EX}\, Y \land f \right) \cup Z$$ The term $\nu Y . \bigvee_{f \in F} (\text{EX}\, Y \land f)$ ensures that along any fair path, fairness constraints are visited infinitely often. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CTL Operators Under Fairness | CTL Operator | Standard Meaning | Fair Meaning (M, s ⊨_F ...) | |--------------|------------------|------------------------------| | $A X \varphi$ | All next states satisfy $\varphi$ | All **fair-successor** next states satisfy $\varphi$ | | $E X \varphi$ | Some next state satisfies $\varphi$ | Some **fair-successor** next state satisfies $\varphi$ | | $A(\varphi \,U\, \psi)$ | All paths satisfy $\varphi$ until $\psi$ | All **fair paths** satisfy the until | | $E(\varphi \,U\, \psi)$ | Some path satisfies $\varphi$ until $\psi$ | Some **fair path** satisfies the until | | $A G \varphi$ | All paths globally satisfy $\varphi$ | All **fair paths** globally satisfy $\varphi$ | | $E F \varphi$ | Some path eventually reaches $\varphi$ | Some **fair path** eventually reaches $\varphi$ | ## 3.4 Worked Example: Mutex Protocol with Fairness **Scenario**: A semaphore-based mutual exclusion protocol between two processes P1 and P2. **States**: - $s_0 = \text{init}$ (both waiting) - $s_1 = \text{P1 in CS}$ (P1 holds lock) - $s_2 = \text{P2 in CS}$ (P2 holds lock) - $s_3 = \text{deadlock}$ (both waiting forever) **Transitions**: - $s_0 \to s_1$, $s_0 \to s_2$, $s_1 \to s_0$, $s_2 \to s_0$, $s_0 \to s_3$ **Fairness Constraint**: $F = \{f_1, f_2\}$ where: - $f_1 = \{s_1\}$ (P1 must enter CS infinitely often) - $f_2 = \{s_2\}$ (P2 must enter CS infinitely often) **Question**: Does the system satisfy $\Box \Diamond(\text{P1 in CS})$? ### Solution (Step-by-Step) **Step 1** — Identify all infinite paths in $M$: - $\pi_1 = s_0 \to s_1 \to s_0 \to s_1 \to \cdots$ (P1 keeps getting in) - $\pi_2 = s_0 \to s_2 \to s_0 \to s_2 \to \cdots$ (P2 keeps getting in) - $\pi_3 = s_0 \to s_3 \to s_3 \to s_3 \to \cdots$ (deadlock path) - $\pi_4 = s_0 \to s_1 \to s_0 \to s_2 \to s_0 \to s_1 \to \cdots$ (alternating) **Step 2** — Compute $\text{Inf}(\pi)$ for each: - $\text{Inf}(\pi_1) = \{s_0, s_1\}$ - $\text{Inf}(\pi_2) = \{s_0, s_2\}$ - $\text{Inf}(\pi_3) = \{s_3\}$ - $\text{Inf}(\pi_4) = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$ **Step 3** — Test fairness: - $f_1 \cap \text{Inf}(\pi_1) = \{s_1\} \neq \emptyset$ ✓ ; $f_2 \cap \text{Inf}(\pi_1) = \emptyset$ ✗ → $\pi_1$ **not fair**. - $f_1 \cap \text{Inf}(\pi_2) = \emptyset$ ✗ → $\pi_2$ **not fair**. - $f_1 \cap \text{Inf}(\pi_3) = \emptyset$ ✗ → $\pi_3$ **not fair**. - $f_1 \cap \text{Inf}(\pi_4) = \{s_1\}$ ✓ ; $f_2 \cap \text{Inf}(\pi_4) = \{s_2\}$ ✓ → $\pi_4$ **fair**. **Step 4** — Only $\pi_4$ is fair. Since $\pi_4$ visits $s_1$ infinitely often, $\Box \Diamond(\text{P1 in CS})$ is **TRUE** under fairness. ∎ ## 3.5 Reduction Theorem (LTL with Fairness) **Theorem**: For any LTL formula $\varphi$ and any fair Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L, F, AP)$: $$M \models_F \varphi \iff (M \otimes \mathcal{B}_{\neg \varphi}) \cap \mathcal{B}_F = \emptyset$$ where: - $\mathcal{B}_{\neg\varphi}$ is the Büchi automaton for $\neg\varphi$. - $\mathcal{B}_F$ is the Büchi automaton encoding the fairness constraints in $F$. - $\otimes$ is the standard product construction. > [!IMPORTANT] **Practical Consequence** > LTL model checking under fairness reduces to checking the **emptiness of a product Büchi automaton** — a problem solvable in linear time $O(|M| \cdot |\mathcal{B}_{\neg\varphi}| \cdot |\mathcal{B}_F|)$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Fair vs. Unfair Path Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA([Start: M = Kripke Structure]) --> nodeB{Is pi infinite?} nodeB -- No --> nodeC([Truncate: Finite path]) nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD[Compute Inf pi] nodeD --> nodeE{For every f in F:
f intersect Inf pi is not empty?} nodeE -- No --> nodeF([Path is UNFAIR
Excluded from verification]) nodeE -- Yes --> nodeG([Path is FAIR
Retained for verification]) nodeF --> nodeH[Apply to M, s models F phi] nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI{Path exists with phi?} nodeI -- Yes --> nodeJ([M, s models F E phi]) nodeI -- No --> nodeK([M, s does NOT models F E phi]) style nodeA fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeG fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeF fill:#dc143c,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeJ fill:#32cd32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeK fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy of Fairness Strengths ```mermaid graph LR nodeSF[Strong Fairness
Compassion] --> nodeUF[Unconditional Fairness
Justice] nodeUF --> nodeWF[Weak Fairness] nodeSF -.->|implies| nodeUF nodeUF -.->|implies| nodeWF nodeSF --> nodeProp1[M, s models F phi
Strongest guarantee] nodeUF --> nodeProp2[M, s models F phi
Moderate guarantee] nodeWF --> nodeProp3[M, s models F phi
Weakest guarantee] style nodeSF fill:#8b0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeUF fill:#b8860b,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeWF fill:#556b2f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeProp1 fill:#228b22,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeProp2 fill:#daa520,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeProp3 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fair Model Checking ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"] nodeMS[Kripke Structure M] nodeF[Fairness Set F] nodePhi[Property phi in CTL or LTL] end subgraph TransformLayer["TRANSFORMATION LAYER"] nodeMS --> nodeNEG[Encode NOT phi as Buchi] nodeF --> nodeFB[Encode F as Buchi] nodeNEG --> nodePROD[Product Automaton
M times B sub NOT phi times B sub F] nodeFB --> nodePROD end subgraph AnalysisLayer["ANALYSIS LAYER"] nodePROD --> nodeEMPT[Check Emptiness
SCC with accepting cycle] nodeEMPT --> nodeDEC{Accepting cycle
exists?} end subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"] nodeDEC -- Yes --> nodeCEX[Counterexample
FAIR path violating phi] nodeDEC -- No --> nodeSAT[M, s models F phi
Property HOLDS] end style InputLayer fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#000 style TransformLayer fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#000 style AnalysisLayer fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#000 style OutputLayer fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Fairness Application in PROMELA (SPIN) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Dev as Developer participant PRO as PROMELA Model participant SPIN as SPIN Verifier participant OG as Output (Trail) Dev->>PRO: Define proctype P1, P2, ... PRO->>PRO: Add fairness block
fairness P1 enters CS PRO->>PRO: Add fairness block
fairness P2 enters CS Dev->>SPIN: spin -a model.pml SPIN->>SPIN: Generate Buchi automaton SPIN->>SPIN: Apply fairness constraints
to accepting states SPIN->>SPIN: Run nested DFS
emptiness check SPIN-->>OG: If counterexample exists,
return FAIR path (trail) SPIN-->>OG: Else: Property verified ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define fairness in the context of model checking. Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: Fairness is a constraint imposed on the set of execution paths of a Kripke structure such that only those paths in which a set of designated fairness conditions $F = \{f_1, \ldots, f_n\}$ are visited infinitely often are considered valid. A path $\pi$ is fair iff $\forall f \in F, f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset$. It is necessary because (1 mark) in concurrent/reactive systems, the model may contain paths that are mathematically valid but practically impossible (e.g., a process never scheduled), leading to **spurious counterexamples** (1 mark). Fairness prunes these unrealistic paths, making verification of liveness properties meaningful (1 mark). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between unconditional fairness and strong fairness with LTL expressions.** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: - **Unconditional Fairness (Justice)** (1.5 marks): Requires that every fairness condition $f_i$ holds infinitely often, regardless of whether it is enabled: $$\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Box\Diamond f_i$$ - **Strong Fairness (Compassion)** (1.5 marks): Requires that if a condition is infinitely often enabled, it must be infinitely often taken: $$\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} (\Box\Diamond \text{en}(f_i) \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond f_i)$$ Strong fairness is strictly stronger; unconditional fairness does not consider the *enabled* status of actions. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Define a **Fair Kripke Structure**. Explain the three fairness strategies — *unconditional*, *strong*, and *weak* — with their LTL formulations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a token-ring protocol with **three processes P1, P2, P3** where the token circulates cyclically. Model this as a fair Kripke structure with fairness constraint $F = \{f_1, f_2, f_3\}$ where $f_i$ denotes "process $P_i$ holds the token". Verify whether the system satisfies the property $\Box \Diamond(\text{P2 holds token})$ under unconditional fairness. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Definition of Fair Kripke Structure (2 marks)**: A fair Kripke structure is a 6-tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, L, F, AP)$ where $F \subseteq 2^S$ is the set of fairness constraints, and the satisfaction relation $\models_F$ considers only paths $\pi$ such that $\forall f \in F, f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset$. **Three Fairness Strategies (5 marks)**: 1. **Unconditional Fairness (Justice)** — Each $f_i$ must hold infinitely often, *no precondition*: $$\text{UncondFair}(F) = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \Box\Diamond f_i$$ Use case: Abstract scheduling assumptions, hardware bus arbitration. 2. **Strong Fairness (Compassion)** — If $f_i$ is *infinitely often* enabled, it must fire *infinitely often*: $$\text{StrongFair}(F) = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \left(\Box\Diamond \,\text{en}(f_i) \implies \Box\Diamond f_i\right)$$ Use case: Starvation-freedom proofs, lock-acquisition protocols. 3. **Weak Fairness** — If $f_i$ is *eventually always* enabled, it must occur infinitely often: $$\text{WeakFair}(F) = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} \left(\Diamond\Box \,\text{en}(f_i) \implies \Box\Diamond f_i\right)$$ Use case: Initial-delay-tolerant scheduling. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Define the model (2 marks)**: - $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ - $S_0 = \{s_0\}$ - Transitions: $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_0$ (cyclic token passing) - $f_1 = \{s_1\}$, $f_2 = \{s_2\}$, $f_3 = \{s_3\}$ - $L(s_1) = \{\text{P1 holds}\}$, $L(s_2) = \{\text{P2 holds}\}$, $L(s_3) = \{\text{P3 holds}\}$ **Step 2 — Identify all paths (1 mark)**: The single infinite path: $\pi = s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_0 \to s_1 \to \cdots$ **Step 3 — Compute $\text{Inf}(\pi)$ (1 mark)**: $$\text{Inf}(\pi) = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$$ **Step 4 — Test fairness (2 marks)**: - $f_1 \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) = \{s_1\} \neq \emptyset$ ✓ - $f_2 \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) = \{s_2\} \neq \emptyset$ ✓ - $f_3 \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) = \{s_3\} \neq \emptyset$ ✓ - Therefore $\pi$ is **fair**. **Step 5 — Verify the property (1 mark)**: Property: $\Box\Diamond(\text{P2 holds token})$ Since $s_2$ appears in $\pi$ infinitely often, the property **HOLDS** under unconditional fairness. $\blacksquare$ --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Explain the **fair satisfaction relation** in CTL. How does the computation of EX, EU, and EG operators change under fairness? **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a producer-consumer system with a buffer of size 1, define a fair Kripke structure where fairness constraints ensure that the producer is not starved. Show that without the fairness constraint, the property $\Box\Diamond(\text{consumer consumes})$ is **false** in the model, but with the fairness constraint, it becomes **true**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Fair CTL Satisfaction (3 marks)**: The fair satisfaction relation $\models_F$ is defined as: $$M, s \models_F \varphi \iff \forall \pi \text{ starting at } s, \text{ if } \pi \text{ fair then } \pi \models \varphi$$ For path quantifiers: - $M, s \models_F E \psi$ iff there exists a **fair** path $\pi$ from $s$ with $\pi \models \psi$. - $M, s \models_F A \psi$ iff for **all fair** paths $\pi$ from $s$, $\pi \models \psi$. **Operator Modifications (4 marks)**: | Operator | Standard | Under Fairness $F$ | |----------|----------|--------------------| | $EX \varphi$ | $s$ has a successor satisfying $\varphi$ | $s$ has a **fair successor** satisfying $\varphi$ | | $E(\varphi U \psi)$ | Standard least fixpoint $\mu Z.\psi \lor (\varphi \land EX Z)$ | Modified to intersect with $\nu Y.\bigvee_{f \in F}(EX Y \land f) \cup Z$ to enforce fairness | | $EG \varphi$ | Greatest fixpoint $\nu Z.\varphi \land EX Z$ | Greatest fixpoint restricted to **fair paths**: $\nu Z.\varphi \land EX_F Z$ | The **key insight** is that under fairness, successors are restricted to those that lie on at least one fair path, and the fixpoint characterizations must encode the requirement that fairness constraints are visited infinitely often. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Model definition (2 marks)**: - States: $s_0 = (\text{prod waiting}, \text{buf empty})$, $s_1 = (\text{prod producing})$, $s_2 = (\text{buf full}, \text{cons waiting})$, $s_3 = (\text{cons consuming})$ - Transitions: $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_0$, plus **spurious**: $s_0 \to s_0$ (producer never fires) - Without fairness: $F = \emptyset$. **Step 2 — Path analysis without fairness (2 marks)**: - Spurious path: $\pi_{\text{starve}} = s_0 \to s_0 \to s_0 \to \cdots$ with $\text{Inf}(\pi) = \{s_0\}$. - Property $\Box\Diamond(\text{cons consumes})$ fails on $\pi_{\text{starve}}$ since $s_3$ is never visited. - **Conclusion**: $M \models \Box\Diamond(\text{cons consumes})$ is **FALSE**. **Step 3 — Add fairness constraint (2 marks)**: Define $F = \{f_{\text{prod}}\}$ where $f_{\text{prod}} = \{s_1\}$ (producer must enter "producing" infinitely often). **Step 4 — Re-analyze (1 mark)**: - $\pi_{\text{starve}}$ has $\text{Inf}(\pi) = \{s_0\}$, so $f_{\text{prod}} \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) = \emptyset$ → **not fair**, excluded. - The only fair path $\pi_{\text{fair}} = s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_0 \to \cdots$ visits $s_3$ infinitely often. - **Conclusion**: $M, s_0 \models_F \Box\Diamond(\text{cons consumes})$ is **TRUE**. $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting to check Inf(π) intersection** — Students often declare a path fair without verifying $\forall f \in F, f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset$. This loses 2-3 marks. > 2. **Confusing strong vs. unconditional fairness** — Strong fairness requires *enabled* precondition; unconditional does not. Mark deduction: 1.5 marks. > 3. **Not listing all paths** — In KTU valuations, partial path enumeration leads to incorrect "FAIR" verdicts. Always enumerate *all* infinite paths exhaustively. > 4. **Mixing |= with |=_F** — The fair satisfaction relation has a stricter quantifier range; using plain satisfaction loses 1 mark. > 5. **Forgetting $\mu$ (least) vs. $\nu$ (greatest) fixpoint distinction** in fair CTL operators — common 1-mark error. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Fairness** = constraint on paths so that fairness conditions are visited **infinitely often**. - ✅ **Fair Kripke Structure** = 6-tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, L, F, AP)$ with $F \subseteq 2^S$. - ✅ **Path Fairness Predicate**: $\pi \text{ fair} \iff \forall f \in F : f \cap \text{Inf}(\pi) \neq \emptyset$. - ✅ **Three Strategies**: - **Unconditional (Justice)**: $\bigwedge \Box\Diamond f_i$ - **Strong (Compassion)**: $\bigwedge (\Box\Diamond \text{en}(f_i) \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond f_i)$ - **Weak**: $\bigwedge (\Diamond\Box \text{en}(f_i) \Rightarrow \Box\Diamond f_i)$ - ✅ **Hierarchy**: Strong $\Rightarrow$ Unconditional $\Rightarrow$ Weak. - ✅ **Fair Satisfaction**: $M, s \models_F \varphi$ restricts both $\forall$ and $\exists$ path quantifiers to *fair paths only*. - ✅ **CTL Operator Modification** under $F$: $EX \to EX_F$, $EU \to E_FU$, $EG \to E_FG$ via fixpoint intersection. - ✅ **LTL Reduction**: Product of Kripke structure, Büchi($\neg\varphi$), and Büchi($F$); check emptiness. - ✅ **Elimination of Spurious Counterexamples**: Fairness is the *only* way to validate liveness properties like $\Box\Diamond p$. - ✅ **Tool Support**: SPIN (PROMELA `fairness` block), NuSMV (`FAIRNESS` declaration), UPPAAL. - ✅ **Engineering Use**: Process scheduling, mutual exclusion, token passing, consensus protocols, cache coherency, leader election. - ✅ **Always check Inf(π) intersection** — the heart of fair path identification. - ✅ **Liveness properties** (`◇p`, `□◇p`) are *only* meaningful under some fairness assumption. - ✅ **Safety properties** (`□p`, `¬□◇p`) are insensitive to fairness — note this distinction for exams.

topics.title: Fairness and Safety (Definition and examples only for all topics - no proof required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fairness and Safety (Definition and examples only for all topics - no proof required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Safety Properties

A **Safety Property** is a property of a reactive or concurrent system that asserts *“nothing bad ever happens”* during the execution of the system. Formally, a property $P$ is a safety property if and only if for every execution (trace) $\sigma$ that violates $P$, there exists a finite prefix $\hat{\sigma}$ of $\sigma$ such that every extension of $\hat{\sigma}$ also violates $P$. In simpler words, a violation of a safety property can always be detected by examining a *finite* portion of the execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Sistla's Safety Classification):**
> A property $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ is a **safety property** iff for every infinite word $w \notin P$, there exists a *finite* prefix $u$ of $w$ such that $u v \notin P$ for all $v \in \Sigma^{\omega}$. This is the canonical definition examiners expect in **PECNT526**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an **elevator door**. A safety property says: *"The elevator door shall never open when the elevator is between floors."* You do not need to observe the elevator forever — the very moment the door starts opening between floors, the bad event has happened. Finite observation is sufficient to confirm a violation.

### Common Examples of Safety Properties
- **Mutual Exclusion:** Two processes are never in the critical section simultaneously.
- **Deadlock Freedom:** The system never reaches a state where all processes are blocked.
- **Type Safety:** A variable of type *integer* is never assigned a string value.
- **Partial Correctness:** If the program terminates, then the result is correct.
- **No division by zero.**
- **Buffer Overflow Avoidance:** The buffer index is always within $[0, n-1]$.

---

## 1.2 Liveness Properties

A **Liveness Property** is a property that asserts *“something good eventually happens”*. Formally, $P$ is a liveness property iff for every finite trace $u \in \Sigma^{\star}$, there exists an infinite extension $v$ such that $uv \in P$. Equivalently, no finite prefix of a trace can ever disprove the property — violations can only be detected in the infinite limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Consider a **printer queue**. A liveness property says: *"Every print job that is submitted will eventually be printed."* You cannot disprove this by watching for 5 minutes — the job might still be printed tomorrow. Only an *infinite* wait with no printing ever happening can violate the property.

### Common Examples of Liveness Properties
