- **Termination:** Every program halts eventually.
- **Starvation Freedom:** Every waiting process eventually enters the critical section.
- **Response:** Every request is eventually followed by a response.
- **Total Correctness:** The program terminates and the result is correct.

---

## 1.3 Fairness Properties

**Fairness** is a special class of *liveness* properties that constrain the scheduling of concurrent processes. Fairness ensures that no process (or transition) that is *continuously enabled* (or *infinitely often enabled*) is *permanently ignored* by the scheduler. It bridges the gap between an abstract system model and real-world concurrent execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:**
> Fairness is a scheduling assumption that guarantees reasonable, non-discriminatory progress of enabled components in a concurrent/reactive system. It is essential for compositional reasoning about liveness.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **round-robin parent serving four children ice-cream**. A *fair* parent will not let one child wait forever while serving the others repeatedly. Fairness formalizes this intuitive scheduling justice in concurrent systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Fairness Matters in Verification:** Without fairness, a model checker may produce a counter-example in which a process is starved *unrealistically*. Fairness constraints prune such unrealistic traces, making verification results meaningful for real schedulers.

---

## 1.4 The Property Hierarchy (Quick Map)

$$
\boxed{\text{System Properties}}
\;\supset\;
\boxed{\text{Liveness}} \;\supset\; \boxed{\text{Fairness (Weak, Strong)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{System Properties}}
\;\supset\;
\boxed{\text{Safety}} \;\supset\; \boxed{\text{Mutual Exclusion, Deadlock Freedom, ...}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** The single most-tested high-yield question in this module is: *"Classify the given property as Safety or Liveness."* Memorize the canonical examples in Sections 1.1 and 1.2 verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three Classical Variants of Fairness

Fairness is not a single concept. The KTU syllabus (and the standard reference *Manna & Pnueli, 1991*) recognises three principal variants. They differ in **how often** a process/transition must be enabled for the scheduler to be obligated to schedule it.

### 2.1.1 Impartiality (Unconditional Fairness)
Every process is executed infinitely often in every infinite computation — *regardless* of whether it is enabled or not. This is the strongest (and often unrealistic) form.

### 2.1.2 Weak Fairness (Justice)
**Definition (KTU):** A transition (or process) $t$ satisfies *weak fairness* if, in every infinite execution, *whenever* $t$ is *continuously enabled* from some point onward, $t$ is eventually taken.

$$
WF(t) : \quad \Box \Diamond \;\text{enabled}(t) \;\Rightarrow\; \Box \Diamond \;\text{executed}(t)
$$

> Equivalently: $\Box \Diamond \text{enabled}(t) \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \text{executed}(t)$.

### 2.1.3 Strong Fairness (Compassion)
**Definition (KTU):** A transition (or process) $t$ satisfies *strong fairness* if, in every infinite execution, *whenever* $t$ is *infinitely often enabled*, $t$ is eventually taken.

$$
SF(t) : \quad \Box \Diamond \;\text{enabled}(t) \;\Rightarrow\; \Box \Diamond \;\text{executed}(t)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Strength:** $\;SF(t) \;\Rightarrow\; WF(t) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Impartiality on enabled actions}$. Strong fairness is strictly stronger than weak fairness. Any system proven correct under SF is also correct under WF — *but not vice-versa*.

---

## 2.2 Why Proofs Are Excluded in This Module

As per the **KTU 2024 scheme module specification**, the topic *"Fairness and Safety"* is restricted to **definitions and examples only — no proof required**. Hence:

- ❌ No derivation of $SF \Rightarrow WF$ is required.
- ❌ No proof that mutual exclusion is a safety property is required.
- ✅ Only the *recognition* of the property, its formal statement, and worked examples are needed.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Statement (LTL / Set-Theoretic) | Symbol / Shorthand | Typical Example |
|---|---------|---------------------------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Safety Property | $\forall w \notin P,\; \exists$ finite prefix $u$ of $w$ such that $uv \notin P$ for all $v$ | $\text{Safety}(P)$ | Mutual exclusion |
| 2 | Liveness Property | $\forall u \in \Sigma^{\star},\; \exists v \in \Sigma^{\omega}$ such that $uv \in P$ | $\text{Liveness}(P)$ | Termination |
| 3 | Weak Fairness | $\Box \Diamond \text{enabled}(t) \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \text{executed}(t)$ | $WF(t)$ | "If a process is always ready, it must run" |
| 4 | Strong Fairness | $\Diamond \Box \text{enabled}(t) \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \text{executed}(t)$ | $SF(t)$ | "If a process becomes ready infinitely often, it must run" |
| 5 | Impartiality | Every process is taken infinitely often | $IF$ | Round-robin scheduling |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for LTL Patterns:**
> - $\Box$ (Box) = "always / henceforth" → represents *continuous* enabledness.
> - $\Diamond$ (Diamond) = "eventually / sometimes" → represents *intermittent* enabledness.
> - $WF$: $\Box \Diamond$ on left, $SF$: $\Diamond \Box$ on left (the *order* of $\Box, \Diamond$ flips!).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Fairness & Safety |
|--------|----------------------------------|
| **Operating Systems** | Process schedulers (CFS, Round-Robin) implement weak fairness; mutex protocols (Peterson, Dekker) guarantee safety. |
| **Network Protocols** | TCP retransmission timer = liveness; no packet duplication = safety. |
| **Hardware Verification** | Intel/Cadence model checking uses fairness to rule out unrealistic arbiter traces. |
| **Embedded / IoT** | Watchdog timers = liveness; sensor value range = safety. |
| **Database Systems** | ACID transactions: atomicity = safety; eventual commit = liveness. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module Constraint Active:** This section provides *worked-out classification examples* and *trace analyses*. **No formal proofs** are required by the 2024 scheme, and therefore none are given. The focus is on **identifying** which class a property belongs to using the standard KTU decision procedure.

## 3.1 The KTU Decision Procedure: Safety vs. Liveness

For any given property $P$, ask the following two questions in order:

1. **Question 1 (Safety Test):** *"Can a finite prefix of execution disprove $P$?"*
   - If **YES** → $P$ is a **Safety** property.
   - If **NO** → proceed to Question 2.

2. **Question 2 (Liveness Test):** *"Is it true that for every finite trace, some extension satisfies $P$?"*
   - If **YES** → $P$ is a **Liveness** property.
   - If **NO** → $P$ is **Neither** (most properties are a conjunction of both).

> This 2-question decision procedure is the **highest-yield analytical tool** in this module.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Mutual Exclusion

**Property:** *"Processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ are never simultaneously in the critical section."*

**Step 1:** Consider a trace $\sigma$ in which $P_1$ enters CS at time $t=5$ and $P_2$ enters CS at time $t=5.001$.

**Step 2:** The finite prefix up to $t=5.001$ already violates the property. So a **finite** prefix is sufficient to disprove $P$.

**Step 3:** Therefore, $P$ is a **Safety Property**. ✅

$$
\boxed{\text{Mutual Exclusion} \in \text{Safety}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Starvation Freedom

**Property:** *"If a process requests the critical section, it will eventually enter."*

**Step 1:** Consider any finite trace in which a process requests CS at time $t=10$ but the trace ends at $t=20$ without the process ever entering CS. Has the property been violated?

**Step 2:** **No** — the property only says *eventually*. The process might still enter CS at $t=100$. So a finite observation cannot disprove the property.

**Step 3:** Test the liveness condition: for every finite trace $u$, can we extend $u$ to satisfy the property? Yes — simply let the waiting process enter CS after $u$ ends.

**Step 4:** Therefore, the property is a **Liveness Property**. ✅

$$
\boxed{\text{Starvation Freedom} \in \text{Liveness}}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Bounded Overtaking (Both!)

**Property:** *"Every request is eventually granted, AND no process is overtaken more than 5 times."*

- The *eventual granting* part is a **liveness** condition.
- The *bounded overtaking* part is a **safety** condition (a finite prefix of 6 overtakings already violates it).

Therefore, this property is a **conjunction of safety and liveness** — it cannot be classified as one alone. This is the standard KTU trap question.

$$
\boxed{P = \underbrace{\text{Bounded Overtaking}}_{\text{Safety}} \;\land\; \underbrace{\text{Eventual Grant}}_{\text{Liveness}}}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Classifying Fairness Variants

Consider a transition $t$ with the following execution trace:

$$
t \text{ enabled at: } 0,\,2,\,4,\,6,\,8,\,\dots \text{ (continuously)}
$$
$$
t \text{ taken at: } 1,\,5,\,9,\,\dots \text{ (eventually taken)}
$$

**Step 1 — Weak Fairness Check:** Is $t$ continuously enabled? **Yes** (it is enabled at every even time). Is $t$ eventually taken? **Yes** (at $t=1, 5, 9, \dots$). So **WF(t) holds**. ✅

**Step 2 — Strong Fairness Check:** Is $t$ infinitely often enabled? **Yes** (in fact continuously). Is $t$ eventually taken? **Yes**. So **SF(t) also holds**. ✅

> **Note:** When a transition is *continuously* enabled, WF and SF coincide. They differ only when enabledness is *intermittent*.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Property Classification Reference

| Property (KTU Standard Examples) | Safety? | Liveness? | Why? |
|----------------------------------|:-------:|:---------:|------|
| Mutual Exclusion | ✅ | ❌ | Finite prefix detects violation |
| Deadlock Freedom | ✅ | ❌ | Reaching deadlock state is a finite-prefix event |
| Partial Correctness | ✅ | ❌ | Wrong output on termination is finite-prefix |
| Type Safety | ✅ | ❌ | Type error occurs in finite steps |
| Termination | ❌ | ✅ | Only an infinite wait disproves |
| Starvation Freedom | ❌ | ✅ | Eventual progress, finite prefix cannot disprove |
| Eventual Response | ❌ | ✅ | Same as above |
| Bounded Overtaking | ✅ | ❌ | Counter exists in finite prefix |
| Weak Fairness $WF(t)$ | ❌ | ✅ | Scheduling-based liveness |
| Strong Fairness $SF(t)$ | ❌ | ✅ | Scheduling-based liveness |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** Whenever a property contains the words *“eventually”*, *“infinitely often”*, or *“forever”* — it is **Liveness**. Whenever it contains the words *“never”*, *“at all times”*, or *“no more than”* — it is **Safety**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of System Properties (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Properties P]:::root --> B[Trace Properties]:::cat
    A --> C[Non-Trace Properties e.g Hyperproperties]:::other
    B --> D[Safety]:::safe
    B --> E[Liveness]:::live
    D --> D1[Mutual Exclusion]:::ex
    D --> D2[Deadlock Freedom]:::ex
    D --> D3[Type Safety]:::ex
    D --> D4[Partial Correctness]:::ex
    E --> E1[Termination]:::ex2
    E --> E2[Starvation Freedom]:::ex2
    E --> E3[Response]:::ex2
    E --> F[Fairness]:::fair
    F --> F1[Impartiality IF]:::fairEx
    F --> F2[Weak Fairness WF]:::fairEx
    F --> F3[Strong Fairness SF]:::fairEx

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef cat fill:#4a6fa5,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef other fill:#888,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef safe fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef live fill:#c62828,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef ex fill:#a5d6a7,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef ex2 fill:#ef9a9a,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef fair fill:#f57c00,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef fairEx fill:#ffcc80,color:#000,stroke:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every reactive system property is a trace property (Alpern-Schneider, 1985). Trace properties split into **Safety** and **Liveness** — these are *disjoint* and *exhaustive* for the class of $\omega$-regular properties. Fairness is a sub-class of Liveness.

## 4.2 Fairness Sub-Variant Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Scheduler Decision]:::sched --> Q{Is action\ncontinuously\nenabled?}:::q
    Q -- YES --> A[Weak Fairness WF\napplies]:::wf
    Q -- NO --> R{Is action\ninfinitely often\nenabled?}:::q2
    R -- YES --> B[Strong Fairness SF\napplies]:::sf
    R -- NO --> C[No fairness\nguarantee needed]:::ng
    A --> D[Action must\nbe eventually\nexecuted]:::ex
    B --> D

    classDef sched fill:#283593,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef q fill:#fbc02d,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef q2 fill:#fbc02d,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef wf fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef sf fill:#c62828,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef ng fill:#757575,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef ex fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Finite-Prefix Test Schematic (Safety Detection)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sys as System Trace sigma
    participant Obs as Observer (Verifier)
    participant Out as Verdict

    Sys->>Obs: Emit event e1
    Sys->>Obs: Emit event e2
    Sys->>Obs: Emit event eN
    Obs->>Obs: Check finite prefix
    alt Bad event seen in finite prefix
        Obs->>Out: SAFETY VIOLATION DETECTED
    else No bad event seen
        Obs->>Out: Continue observing
    end
```

This sequence diagram illustrates the *defining operational characteristic* of safety properties: an observer can conclusively report a violation after observing only a *finite* number of events.

## 4.4 Classification Decision Tree (KTU Exam Shortcut)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Given Property P]:::p --> T1{Finite prefix\ncan disprove P?}:::dec
    T1 -- YES --> SAFE[Classify as SAFETY]:::safe
    T1 -- NO --> T2{Every finite trace\nhas an extension\nsatisfying P?}:::dec
    T2 -- YES --> LIVE[Classify as LIVENESS]:::live
    T2 -- NO --> BOTH[Property is a COMBINATION\nof Safety AND Liveness]:::both

    classDef p fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef dec fill:#fbc02d,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef safe fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef live fill:#c62828,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef both fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECNT526]`

**Define a safety property. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

> A property $P$ of a system is called a *safety property* if whenever an execution $\sigma$ violates $P$, there exists a finite prefix $\hat{\sigma}$ of $\sigma$ such that every extension of $\hat{\sigma}$ also violates $P$. In other words, a safety violation can be detected by observing only a finite portion of the execution. *(2 marks)*
>
> **Examples:** *(1 mark, any two)*
> 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — two processes are never simultaneously inside the critical section.
> 2. **Deadlock Freedom** — the system never reaches a state in which all processes are blocked.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECNT526]`

**Differentiate between weak fairness and strong fairness.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

| Aspect | Weak Fairness $WF(t)$ | Strong Fairness $SF(t)$ |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Enabledness condition | $t$ is *continuously* enabled ($\Box \Diamond$ equivalent) | $t$ is *infinitely often* enabled ($\Diamond \Box$ equivalent) |
| Guarantee | If always ready, $t$ is eventually taken | If repeatedly ready, $t$ is eventually taken |
| LTL Form | $\Box \Diamond \,\text{enabled}(t) \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \,\text{executed}(t)$ | $\Diamond \Box \,\text{enabled}(t) \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \,\text{executed}(t)$ |
| Strength | Weaker | Stronger ($SF \Rightarrow WF$ when enabledness is continuous) |

*(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for stating the relative strength).*

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Full Descriptive / Internal Choice)

> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, students must answer ONE choice out of TWO. Each sub-part is **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECNT526]`

**A. (a)** Define *Safety* and *Liveness* properties of a reactive system. Classify each of the following properties as Safety, Liveness, or a combination of both. Justify your classification. *(7 marks)*

1. *(i)* Absence of division by zero errors in a program.
2. *(ii)* Every request to a web server is eventually acknowledged.
3. *(iii)* A traffic light is never simultaneously red and green on the same road.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions (3 marks):**

- **Safety Property:** A property $P$ is a safety property if every violating infinite trace has a *finite* bad prefix. Equivalently, "nothing bad ever happens." Violations are *finitely* observable.
- **Liveness Property:** A property $P$ is a liveness property if for every finite trace $u$, there exists an infinite extension $v$ such that $uv \in P$. Equivalently, "something good eventually happens." Violations require *infinite* observation.

**(b) Classification of given properties (4 marks — 1 for each correct classification + 1 for the justification average):**

| # | Property | Classification | Justification |
|---|----------|----------------|---------------|
| (i) | No division by zero | **Safety** | A `DIV/0` instruction executes in a finite number of steps; a finite prefix of execution reveals the violation. |
| (ii) | Request eventually acknowledged | **Liveness** | The word "eventually" indicates an unbounded future guarantee; no finite prefix can disprove acknowledgement. |
| (iii) | Traffic light never red+green | **Safety** | A conflicting signal pair is an instantaneously observable state — finite-prefix violation. |

---

**A. (b)** Define *Weak Fairness* and *Strong Fairness*. State the formal LTL expressions. Give one real-world example for each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Weak Fairness (WF) — 2 marks:**
A transition (or process) $t$ is *weakly fair* if in every infinite execution, whenever $t$ is *continuously enabled* from some point onward, $t$ is *eventually executed*. Formally:

$$
\Box \Diamond\; \text{enabled}(t) \;\Longrightarrow\; \Box \Diamond\; \text{executed}(t)
$$

**Example (1 mark):** A printer spooler process that, once it receives a print job, is *always* ready to print, must not be starved indefinitely by the OS scheduler.

**(2) Strong Fairness (SF) — 2 marks:**
A transition (or process) $t$ is *strongly fair* if in every infinite execution, whenever $t$ is *infinitely often enabled*, $t$ is *eventually executed*. Formally:

$$
\Diamond \Box\; \text{enabled}(t) \;\Longrightarrow\; \Box \Diamond\; \text{executed}(t)
$$

**Example (1 mark):** A network interface card whose interrupt line is *intermittently* asserted (e.g., during packet bursts) must be serviced by the driver eventually — the interrupt is not *continuously* enabled, only infinitely often.

**(1 additional mark)** is awarded for correctly stating that **SF is strictly stronger than WF** when enabledness is intermittent.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PECNT526]`

**B. (a)** Discuss the three principal variants of fairness — *Impartiality*, *Weak Fairness*, and *Strong Fairness*. State one example use-case for each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Impartiality (Unconditional Fairness) — 2 marks:**
Every process (or transition) is taken *infinitely often* in every infinite execution, *regardless* of whether it is enabled. This is the strongest (and often unrealistic) variant.

**Example (1 mark):** A purely *round-robin* CPU scheduler with no concept of "readiness" — every process gets a turn in cyclic order. Used in real-time embedded controllers.

**(ii) Weak Fairness (Justice) — 2 marks:**
If a transition is *continuously enabled*, it must be eventually taken. Formal LTL: $\Box \Diamond \text{enabled}(t) \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \text{executed}(t)$.

**Example (1 mark):** A mutex-protected variable. If a process is *always* waiting to acquire the lock, fairness guarantees it will not starve.

**(iii) Strong Fairness (Compassion) — 2 marks:**
If a transition is *infinitely often enabled*, it must be eventually taken. Formal LTL: $\Diamond \Box \text{enabled}(t) \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond \text{executed}(t)$.

**Example (1 mark):** A consumer process in a producer-consumer queue. The consumer is enabled *only when* the queue is non-empty — i.e., intermittently. Strong fairness ensures eventual consumption.

---

**B. (b)** Explain the Alpern–Schneider dichotomy for trace properties. State why this classification is fundamental to model checking. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(1) The Alpern–Schneider Theorem (3 marks):**
For any system modelled as a state-transition graph over a finite alphabet $\Sigma$, every *trace property* $P \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ can be expressed as the intersection:

$$
P \;=\; P_{\text{safety}} \,\cap\, P_{\text{liveness}}
$$

where $P_{\text{safety}}$ is a safety property and $P_{\text{liveness}}$ is a liveness property. Moreover, safety and liveness are *disjoint* and *jointly exhaustive* over $\omega$-regular properties.

**(2) Intuition (2 marks):**
- A safety property constrains the *finite* behaviour (the bad prefix structure).
- A liveness property constrains the *infinite* behaviour (the tail / eventual-progress structure).
- Every property is a conjunction of one of each kind.

**(3) Why fundamental to model checking (2 marks):**
- Safety properties can be checked using *reachability analysis* (BFS/DFS over the state space) — efficient and decidable.
- Liveness properties require *fair cycle detection* (emptiness of Büchi automata) — more complex, but still polynomial.
- The dichotomy lets tool designers split the verification task into two *independent* sub-tasks, each with its own optimised algorithm (e.g., NuSMV, SPIN, CBMC).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PECNT526 Module 1 (Fairness & Safety):**
>
> 1. **LTL Notation Confusion:** Students often write $\Box \Diamond$ and $\Diamond \Box$ interchangeably. **Marks are deducted** if the order does not match the property — *Weak Fairness* uses $\Box \Diamond$ on the left, *Strong Fairness* uses $\Diamond \Box$ on the left. (Loss: up to 2 marks)
> 2. **Misclassifying "Deadlock Freedom" as Liveness:** Deadlock is a *state*, and reaching it is a *finite* event. Hence, deadlock freedom is a **safety** property, NOT liveness. (Loss: 3 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting the Justification:** A bare classification (e.g., "this is a safety property") without justification carries only **partial marks**. The KTU key demands at least one sentence of reasoning per property. (Loss: 1 mark per item)
> 4. **Conflating Fairness with Safety:** Some students write *"fairness is a safety property because it prevents starvation"*. This is incorrect — fairness is a **liveness** property. (Loss: full 7 marks in a sub-part)
> 5. **Not Mentioning the "Finite-Prefix Test":** Examiners reward students who explicitly state the *finite prefix* test as the decision procedure. Adding this phrase often bumps a 6-mark answer to 7 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for PECNT526 — Module 1**

- ✅ **Safety Property** = "Nothing bad happens." Detected by *finite* prefix. Canonical examples: mutual exclusion, deadlock freedom, type safety, partial correctness.
- ✅ **Liveness Property** = "Something good eventually happens." Detected only in the *infinite* limit. Canonical examples: termination, starvation freedom, response.
- ✅ **Alpern-Schneider Theorem** = Every trace property is `Safety ∧ Liveness`.
- ✅ **Fairness** is a *sub-class of liveness* — never a safety property.
- ✅ **Weak Fairness (WF)**: continuously enabled $\Rightarrow$ eventually executed. LTL: $\Box \Diamond\, \text{enc} \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond\, \text{exec}$.
- ✅ **Strong Fairness (SF)**: infinitely-often enabled $\Rightarrow$ eventually executed. LTL: $\Diamond \Box\, \text{enc} \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond\, \text{exec}$.
- ✅ **Impartiality**: every process runs infinitely often, regardless of enabledness.
- ✅ **Strength Ordering:** $SF \;\Rightarrow\; WF \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Impartiality}$ (on enabled actions); $SF$ is *strictly stronger* than $WF$.
- ✅ **Decision Procedure (use in every exam):**
   1. Can a *finite* prefix disprove the property? → **Safety**.
   2. Else, does *every* finite trace have an extension satisfying it? → **Liveness**.
   3. Else → property is a *conjunction* of both.
- ✅ **Keywords to remember:** "never", "at all times", "no more than" → **Safety**; "eventually", "infinitely often", "forever", "always eventually" → **Liveness**.
- ✅ **KTU Module Restriction:** No proofs are required. Only definitions, classifications, and examples carry marks. Always *state the type explicitly* and *give at least one real-world example*.

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1 verification! 🎓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regular Properties - Model Checking Regular Safety Properties - Regular Safety Property
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Regular Properties - Model Checking Regular Safety Properties - Regular Safety Property

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Safety Property

Let $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$ be a finite alphabet (the set of all truth assignments to a set of atomic propositions $AP$). A **property** over $\Sigma$ is a set $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ of infinite words. Formally, a property $P$ is called a **safety property** if and only if:

$$\forall \sigma \in \Sigma^\omega \setminus P : \exists \hat{u} \in \Sigma^* \text{ (a finite prefix of } \sigma) \text{ such that } \forall \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : \hat{u}\tau \notin P$$

In words: *every bad infinite behaviour already has a finite "witness" prefix whose every possible continuation is also bad.* The bad behaviour is *finitely refutable*.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Regular Safety Property

A safety property $P$ is called a **regular safety property** if and only if $P$ is also a **regular language** (i.e., $P$ is recognised by some finite automaton, deterministic or non-deterministic, over the alphabet $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$).

Equivalently, $P$ is a regular safety property iff there exists a regular language of **bad prefixes** $\mathrm{BadPref}(P) \subseteq \Sigma^*$ such that:

$$\sigma \notin P \iff \exists \hat{u} \sqsubseteq \sigma : \hat{u} \in \mathrm{BadPref}(P)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The set $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ must be **prefix-closed in the bad sense** — that is, if a word is bad, then so is *every* of its extensions. Formally: $\mathrm{BadPref}(P) = \Sigma^* \setminus \mathrm{goodPref}(P)$, where $\mathrm{goodPref}(P)$ is the set of *good prefixes* — those finite words that have at least one extension in $P$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine driving a car on a highway. A **safety property** here is the rule *"the driver is never intoxicated at any instant."* As soon as this rule is violated — say, at minute 47 of the trip when the driver is found drunk — we have a **finite, observable, definitive proof** of the violation. We don't need to watch the *entire future* of the trip to certify the rule was broken.

Now, this rule is a **regular safety property** if the "drunk detection check" can be performed by a **finite-memory device** (a finite automaton). It does not need an unbounded log of the past — only a bounded set of internal states. Examples of this in computer science:

| Domain | Safety Property | Why Regular |
|---|---|---|
| Mutual exclusion | Two processes are never simultaneously in their critical sections | DFAs can count processes mod 2 |
| Traffic lights | Light colours cycle Green $\to$ Yellow $\to$ Red $\to$ Green | A 3-state DFA suffices |
| Type safety | A well-typed expression never reduces to a type error | DFAs can be built on type environments |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Intuition:** A *safety* property says *"nothing bad ever happens."* A *regular safety* property additionally says *"the badness can be detected by a tiny, finite-memory inspector."*

## 1.4 Bad Prefixes vs. Good Prefixes — Precise Definitions

Let $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ be a property.

$$\mathrm{BadPref}(P) = \{ \hat{u} \in \Sigma^* \mid \forall \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : \hat{u}\tau \notin P \}$$

A finite word $\hat{u}$ is a *bad prefix* iff **no** infinite extension of $\hat{u}$ belongs to $P$.

$$\mathrm{GoodPref}(P) = \{ \hat{u} \in \Sigma^* \mid \exists \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : \hat{u}\tau \in P \}$$

A finite word $\hat{u}$ is a *good prefix* iff **some** infinite extension of $\hat{u}$ belongs to $P$.

> [!WARNING]
> These two sets are **not complementary** in general. There exist *fence words* — finite words that have *neither* an extension in $P$ nor a violation. Such words are excluded from $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ but also not in $\mathrm{GoodPref}(P)$ if the property $P$ is itself a safety property, this overlap vanishes.

## 1.5 Why Bother with Regular Safety Properties?

In the KTU 2024 scheme's automated verification pipeline, regular safety properties are *the most efficiently checkable class* of properties because:

1. They reduce to a **reachability problem in a finite product graph** (no need to explore infinite paths).
2. They are **decidable in linear time** in the size of the system.
3. They subsume the vast majority of practical correctness requirements (mutual exclusion, deadlock-freedom at the safety level, no use-after-free, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the Theory

### Layer 1 — The Underlying Alphabet

We work over $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$, where $AP$ is a finite set of atomic propositions. The cardinality of $\Sigma$ is therefore $2^{|AP|}$. Every letter $a \in \Sigma$ is a *labelling* of the system at one instant: a Boolean vector of which propositions are true.

### Layer 2 — The Property as a Set of Traces

A *behaviour* of the system is an infinite sequence of letters: $\sigma = a_0 a_1 a_2 \ldots \in \Sigma^\omega$. A *property* $P$ is a set of behaviours deemed *acceptable*. The model checking question $M \models P$ asks: *is every behaviour of $M$ in $P$?*

### Layer 3 — The Safety Condition

A property $P$ is a *safety* property iff it is **closed under taking bad-prefix-free prefixes** in the following strong sense: any bad infinite word is *already* rendered bad by some finite prefix. Algebraically, this is a *Galois-connection-like* closure condition.

### Layer 4 — The Regularity Condition

A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ (or $\Sigma^\omega$) is **regular** iff it is recognised by some finite automaton $\mathcal{A} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, Q_0, F)$ (DFA case) or NFA. By Kleene's theorem this is equivalent to being definable by a regular expression.

### Layer 5 — The Synthesis (Regular Safety)

Combine Layers 3 and 4: $P$ is a regular safety property iff $P$ is a regular language that is also a safety property. Equivalently, iff $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ is a regular language (or equivalently, $\mathrm{GoodPref}(P)$ is regular — since the complement of a regular language over a finite alphabet is regular).

## 2.2 The Core Theorem (Huth & Ryan, Theorem 5.25)

> **Theorem (Characterisation of Regular Safety Properties).** Let $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ be a property. Then $P$ is a regular safety property **if and only if** the set $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ of bad prefixes of $P$ is a regular language.

**Proof sketch (the "Why" behind it):**

- *If* $P$ is a regular safety property, then by definition $P$ is regular. The set $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ can be constructed as a DFA by determinising and complementing the automaton for $P$. Regularity of $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ follows.
- *Only if*: If $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ is regular, recognised by some DFA $\mathcal{A}$, then one can show that $P$ is closed under the safety condition by a direct case analysis on whether a prefix has an extension in $P$ (this is exactly the "no fence word" argument).

## 2.3 The Key Computation: Product Construction

To check whether a Kripke structure $M$ satisfies a regular safety property $P$, the algorithm builds a **product** $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$ where $\mathcal{A}$ is a DFA recognising $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$. Then:

$$M \models P \iff L(M) \cap \mathrm{BadPref}(P) = \emptyset \iff \text{no accepting state of } M \otimes \mathcal{A} \text{ is reachable}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$ | Alphabet of labels (truth assignments to atomic propositions) | All definitions |
| 2 | $\Sigma^\omega$ | Set of all infinite words over $\Sigma$ | Properties |
| 3 | $\Sigma^*$ | Set of all finite words over $\Sigma$ | Bad / good prefixes |
| 4 | $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ | A property is a set of acceptable infinite words | Definition of property |
| 5 | $\hat{u} \sqsubseteq \sigma$ | $\hat{u}$ is a finite prefix of $\sigma$ | Prefix relation |
| 6 | $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ | $\{\hat{u} \in \Sigma^* \mid \forall \tau : \hat{u}\tau \notin P\}$ | Safety characterisation |
| 7 | $\mathrm{GoodPref}(P)$ | $\{\hat{u} \in \Sigma^* \mid \exists \tau : \hat{u}\tau \in P\}$ | Duality with bad prefixes |
| 8 | $\mathcal{A} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | DFA (states, alphabet, transition, start, accept) | Regularity |
| 9 | $L(\mathcal{A})$ | Language accepted by $\mathcal{A}$ | Acceptance condition |
| 10 | $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$ | Product of Kripke structure $M$ and DFA $\mathcal{A}$ | Model checking algorithm |
| 11 | $M \models P$ | $M$ satisfies property $P$ iff $L(M) \subseteq P$ | Model checking |
| 12 | $L(M) \cap \mathrm{BadPref}(P) = \emptyset$ | Property holds iff no trace of $M$ has a bad prefix | Main check |
| 13 | $\vert L(\mathcal{A}) \vert = O(2^{\vert Q \vert})$ | Determinisation blow-up (subset construction) | Complexity |
| 14 | $\vert M \otimes \mathcal{A} \vert = \vert S_M \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert$ | Product state-space size | Complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric for KTU board questions:** The time complexity of regular safety property checking is $O(\vert S_M \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)$, which is *linear* in the product graph and the alphabet size.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Use of Regular Safety Properties |
|---|---|
| **Hardware verification (RTL)** | "No two outputs of a bus are ever simultaneously HIGH" — checked via DFA + BDD product |
| **Protocol verification (TLS, TCP)** | "Server never sends a FIN before SYN-ACK" — a regular safety property |
| **Compiler intermediate representation** | "A variable is never used before being defined" — NFA on SSA form |
| **Kernel / OS modules** | "No null-pointer dereference along any execution path" — modelled as safety |
| **Cyber-physical systems** | "Robot stays within the safety fence at all times" — DFA on position cells |

In all these domains, regular safety checking scales to **industrial-sized systems** because the underlying algorithm is essentially graph reachability in a finite product graph, for which BFS / DFS / symbolic (BDD-based) solvers are extremely mature.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Product Construction $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$

Let $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ be a Kripke structure and let $\mathcal{A} = (Q, 2^{AP}, \delta, q_0, F)$ be a DFA that recognises the set of bad prefixes $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$. We construct the product:

$$M \otimes \mathcal{A} = (S \times Q, \, S_0 \times \{q_0\}, \, R_{\otimes}, \, L_{\otimes})$$

### Step 1 — Define the state space

$$S_{\otimes} = S \times Q = \{ (s, q) \mid s \in S, \, q \in Q \}$$

A state in the product records *where the system currently is* (a Kripke state $s$) *and where the bad-prefix automaton currently is* (a DFA state $q$).

### Step 2 — Define the initial states

$$S_0^{\otimes} = S_0 \times \{q_0\} = \{ (s_0, q_0) \mid s_0 \in S_0 \}$$

The DFA starts at its initial state $q_0$ because no input has been consumed yet.

### Step 3 — Define the transition relation

For $a \in 2^{AP}$:

$$((s, q), a, (s', q')) \in R_{\otimes} \iff (s, a, s') \in R \;\land\; \delta(q, a) = q'$$

The product moves *only* if both the Kripke structure and the DFA can move on the same letter $a$. This synchronises the two machines on a common alphabet.

### Step 4 — Define the set of accepting (error) states

$$F_{\otimes} = S \times F = \{ (s, q) \mid s \in S, \, q \in F \}$$

Any product state whose DFA component is in $F$ (i.e., a final / accepting state of the bad-prefix DFA) is an **error state** — it represents having just consumed a finite word that is already a *bad prefix*.

### Step 5 — The Correctness Theorem

> **Theorem (Correctness of Product Construction).** $M \models P$ if and only if **no** state in $F_{\otimes}$ is reachable from $S_0^{\otimes}$ in $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$.

**Derivation:**

- *Forward direction.* Suppose $M \models P$, i.e., $L(M) \subseteq P$. Then for every finite word $\hat{u}$ that labels a path of $M$, $\hat{u}$ has an extension in $P$ (namely, extend along the path). Hence $\hat{u} \notin \mathrm{BadPref}(P) = L(\mathcal{A})$. So no path of $M$ corresponds to a word accepted by $\mathcal{A}$, i.e., $F_{\otimes}$ is unreachable.
- *Reverse direction.* Suppose no error state of $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$ is reachable. Then for every path $\pi$ of $M$ labelled by $\hat{u} \in \Sigma^*$, the DFA on input $\hat{u}$ does *not* reach $F$. So $\hat{u} \notin L(\mathcal{A}) = \mathrm{BadPref}(P)$. Thus $\hat{u}$ has some extension in $P$. Extending along the rest of $\pi$ shows $\pi \in P$. So $L(M) \subseteq P$, i.e., $M \models P$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Mutual Exclusion

Let $AP = \{c_1, c_2\}$ where $c_i$ means *"process $i$ is in its critical section."* The safety property is:

$$P_{\text{ME}} = \{ \sigma \in (2^{AP})^\omega \mid \forall i \geq 0 : c_1 \notin \sigma[i] \lor c_2 \notin \sigma[i] \}$$

i.e., *at no time are both $c_1$ and $c_2$ true simultaneously.* This is a regular safety property. Let us construct the bad-prefix DFA $\mathcal{A}_{\text{ME}}$.

### Step 1 — Identify bad letters

A letter $a \in 2^{AP}$ is *bad on its own* iff $c_1 \in a$ and $c_2 \in a$. The bad letters are:

$$B = \{ \{c_1, c_2\} \} = \{ 11 \}$$

### Step 2 — Construct the DFA

The DFA has two states: $q_0$ (start, "no violation seen yet") and $q_1$ (trap, "violation seen"). The transitions:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, \, 00) &= q_0 \\
\delta(q_0, \, 01) &= q_0 \\
\delta(q_0, \, 10) &= q_0 \\
\delta(q_0, \, 11) &= q_1 \\
\delta(q_1, \, a) &= q_1 \quad \forall a \in \Sigma
\end{aligned}$$

The accepting (error) state is $F = \{q_1\}$.

### Step 3 — Run the product algorithm

Given a Kripke structure $M$, construct $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_{\text{ME}}$. Run BFS/DFS from $(s_0, q_0)$:

- If we ever reach a state $(s, q_1)$ — any state with $q_1$ — then the trace up to that point is a bad prefix. **Mutual exclusion is violated.**
- Otherwise, **mutual exclusion holds.**

## 3.3 The Complete Algorithm (Written Out, No Step Skipped)

**Input:** Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$; DFA $\mathcal{A} = (Q, 2^{AP}, \delta, q_0, F)$ recognising $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$.

**Output:** Boolean — `true` if $M \models P$, `false` otherwise (with counterexample).

```
1.  Let Visited := empty set
2.  Let Worklist := empty queue
3.  for each s_0 in S_0 do
4.      push (s_0, q_0) into Worklist
5.      insert (s_0, q_0) into Visited
6.  end for
7.  while Worklist is not empty do
8.      pop (s, q) from Worklist
9.      if q in F then
10.         return FALSE       // M does not satisfy P
11.         // and reconstruct counter-example from BFS parents
12.     end if
13.     for each (s, a, s') in R do
14.         let q' := delta(q, a)
15.         if (s', q') not in Visited then
16.             insert (s', q') into Visited
17.             push (s', q') into Worklist
18.         end if
19.     end for
20. end while
21. return TRUE                 // M satisfies P
```

### Termination & Complexity Argument

- **Termination:** $\vert \text{Visited} \vert \leq \vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert$, which is finite. So the `while` loop runs at most $\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert$ times.
- **Time complexity:** Each pop processes $|R|$ transitions in the worst case, so total time $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert R \vert / \vert S \vert) = O(\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert R \vert)$, or simply $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)$ for alphabet-synchronous transitions.
- **Space complexity:** $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert)$ for the visited set and worklist.

## 3.4 Relationship to LTL

Many (but not all) regular safety properties are expressible in **LTL**. Specifically, a property is expressible in LTL *and* is a safety property iff it is the *safety fragment* of LTL, which uses only the **G** (globally) modality, Boolean connectives, and atomic propositions:

$$\varphi \; ::= \; p \;\mid\; \neg \varphi \;\mid\; \varphi \wedge \varphi \;\mid\; \mathbf{G}\varphi$$

The formula $\mathbf{G}(\neg(c_1 \wedge c_2))$ expresses mutual exclusion and is in the LTL safety fragment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-example:** Not every regular safety property is LTL-expressible. Some require *finite automata with counting*, which is not definable in LTL's first-order-on-traces logic. LTL cannot count modulo a fixed number beyond what its propositional syntax allows.

## 3.5 Summary of the Analytical Pipeline

| Step | Mathematical Object | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Property $P$ | Identify it as a safety property (check finite-refutability) |
| 2 | $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ | Construct the set of finite bad prefixes |
| 3 | DFA $\mathcal{A}$ | Build a DFA that accepts $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ |
| 4 | Product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$ | Synchronise $M$ and $\mathcal{A}$ on the same alphabet |
| 5 | Reachability of $F_{\otimes}$ | Run graph search; if reachable, $M \not\models P$ |
| 6 | Counter-example | Extract the path from $S_0^\otimes$ to a state in $F_{\otimes}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Conceptual Hierarchy of Properties

This diagram maps the universe of properties to the subclass of regular safety properties. The `Mermaid` block is sanitised (alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[AllProperties] --> B[DecidableProperties]
    A --> C[UndecidableProperties]
    B --> D[OmegaRegularProperties]
    B --> E[NonRegularProperties]
    D --> F[SafetyProperties]
    D --> G[LivenessProperties]
    D --> H[CombinedProperties]
    F --> I[RegularSafetyProperties]
    F --> J[NonRegularSafetyProperties]
    I --> K[LTLSafetyFragment]
    I --> L[NonLTLSafety]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Every **regular safety property** is a **safety property** and an **$\omega$-regular property**.
- The **LTL safety fragment** (using only $\mathbf{G}, \wedge, \neg, p$) sits *inside* the regular safety properties — a strict subset.
- **Non-regular safety properties** exist (e.g., "the $i$-th occurrence of $a$ is followed by the $i$-th occurrence of $b$" for all $i \in \mathbb{N}$ — this requires unbounded counting).

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The Product Construction $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$

A block-level functional architecture of the synchronous product.

```mermaid
graph LR
    sys[KripkeStructure M] -->|current state s| prod[ProductBuilder]
    dfa[DFA A for BadPref] -->|current state q| prod
    prod -->|transition on letter a| newpair[New Product State s-prime, q-prime]
    newpair --> check{Is q-prime in F?}
    check -->|Yes| error[ERROR: Property Violated]
    check -->|No| cont[Continue BFS or DFS]
    cont --> sys
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Full Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Start: Input M and DFA A]) --> init[Initialise Worklist with S0 times q0]
    init --> dequeue[Dequeue pair s comma q]
    dequeue --> test{Is q an accepting state of A?}
    test -->|Yes| fail[Return FALSE: M does not satisfy P]
    test -->|No| expand[For each transition s to s-prime in M on letter a]
    expand --> trans[Compute q-prime equals delta of q comma a]
    trans --> visit{Is pair s-prime comma q-prime already visited?}
    visit -->|No| enqueue[Enqueue s-prime comma q-prime, mark visited]
    visit -->|Yes| dequeue
    enqueue --> dequeue
    dequeue --> empty{Worklist empty?}
    empty -->|No| dequeue
    empty -->|Yes| pass[Return TRUE: M satisfies P]
    pass --> stop([Stop])
    fail --> stop
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — State Explosion Visualisation

A common pitfall in regular safety property checking: the product state space is the **Cartesian product** of $M$ and $\mathcal{A}$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1[S1 in M] --- q1[Q1 in A]
    s1 --- q2[Q2 in A]
    s1 --- q3[Q3 in A]
    s2[S2 in M] --- q1
    s2 --- q2
    s2 --- q3
    s3[S3 in M] --- q1
    s3 --- q2
    s3 --- q3
```

Each grid point $(S_i, Q_j)$ is a potential product state. For $n$ Kripke states and $m$ DFA states, the product has $n \cdot m$ states. This is the **state-explosion problem** that drives research into symbolic (BDD-based) verification.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Acceptance / Rejection in Product

A schematic of which paths lead to property satisfaction and which lead to violation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    init0[(s0, q0)] --> step1[(s1, q1)]
    step1 --> step2a[(s2a, q2a)]
    step1 --> step2b[(s2b, q2b)]
    step2a --> step3ok[(s3, q_good)]
    step2b --> step3bad[(s3, q_bad)]
    step3bad --> accept[Accepting State of A]
    accept --> result_fail[Result: M does not satisfy P]
    step3ok --> result_pass[Result: M satisfies P]
```

A path in the product whose DFA component **reaches an accepting state** is a *bad-prefix witness* — the corresponding Kripke trace is a counter-example.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

> **Q: Define a regular safety property. How does it differ from a general safety property?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **regular safety property** is a safety property $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ that is also a **regular language**, i.e., $P$ is recognisable by a finite automaton. The key difference is:

- A **general safety property** only requires that every violating infinite trace has a finite bad prefix (a finite-refutability condition).
- A **regular safety property** further requires that the set of bad prefixes $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ is itself a regular language, recognisable by a finite automaton.

Thus, every regular safety property is a safety property, but the converse is not true — there exist safety properties whose set of bad prefixes is not regular (e.g., properties requiring unbounded counting).

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition of safety, 1 mark for adding the regularity clause, 1 mark for the contrast.)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

> **Q: What is a bad prefix of a property $P$? Why is the regularity of $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ crucial for algorithmic model checking?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bad prefix** of $P$ is a finite word $\hat{u} \in \Sigma^*$ such that **no** infinite extension of $\hat{u}$ belongs to $P$:

$$\hat{u} \in \mathrm{BadPref}(P) \iff \forall \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : \hat{u}\tau \notin P$$

The regularity of $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ is crucial because:

1. It allows us to **construct a finite DFA** $\mathcal{A}$ that recognises all bad prefixes.
2. This DFA can be **synchronised with the Kripke structure** $M$ to form a finite product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$.
3. Property satisfaction then reduces to a **finite graph reachability problem** in the product, decidable in $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert)$ time.

If $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ were not regular, no such finite automaton could exist, and the simple reachability-based checking algorithm would not apply.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for the formal definition, 1 mark for the DFA point, 1 mark for the complexity / reachability point.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> As per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) norms, Part B questions carry 14 marks with a typical sub-division of **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The module-internal choice pattern is reproduced below.

### ⭐ Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

> **(a)** Define a *regular safety property* formally. State and prove (sketch) the theorem that characterises regular safety properties in terms of regular sets of bad prefixes. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Describe the product-construction algorithm for checking whether a Kripke structure $M$ satisfies a regular safety property $P$. Discuss its time and space complexity. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Formal definition.** A property $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ (where $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$) is a *safety property* iff:

$$\forall \sigma \in \Sigma^\omega \setminus P : \exists \hat{u} \sqsubseteq \sigma \text{ such that } \forall \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : \hat{u}\tau \notin P$$

A *regular safety property* is a safety property such that there exists a finite automaton (DFA or NFA) accepting $P$ — equivalently, such that $P$ is a regular language.

**Step 2 — Theorem statement.**

> **Theorem.** A property $P$ is a regular safety property if and only if the set $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ of its bad prefixes is a regular language.

**Step 3 — Proof sketch (Forward direction).** Suppose $P$ is a regular safety property. Then $P$ is recognised by a DFA $\mathcal{A}_P = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$. Construct a DFA for $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ as follows:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } \mathcal{B} &= (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q \setminus F_{\text{coacc}}) \\
\text{where } F_{\text{coacc}} &= \{ q \in Q \mid \exists \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : \hat{\delta}(q, \tau) \in F \}
\end{aligned}$$

That is, an accepting state of $\mathcal{B}$ is a state from which **no** accepting state of $\mathcal{A}_P$ is reachable on any continuation. The resulting DFA accepts exactly $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$, proving regularity.

**Step 4 — Proof sketch (Converse).** Conversely, suppose $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ is regular, recognised by DFA $\mathcal{A} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$. Define:

$$P' = \{ \sigma \in \Sigma^\omega \mid \forall \hat{u} \sqsubseteq \sigma : \hat{u} \notin \mathrm{BadPref}(P) \}$$

One can show $P = P'$ (using the safety condition that bad prefixes *fence off* all violations), and $P'$ is regular because the set of words avoiding a regular set of finite prefixes on every prefix is a regular $\omega$-language. Hence $P$ is a regular safety property. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Input.** We are given:
- A Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$.
- A DFA $\mathcal{A} = (Q, 2^{AP}, \delta, q_0, F)$ that recognises $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$.

**Step 2 — Product construction.** Form the synchronous product $M \otimes \mathcal{A} = (S \times Q, S_0 \times \{q_0\}, R_\otimes, L_\otimes)$ with the transition relation:

$$((s, q), a, (s', q')) \in R_\otimes \iff (s, a, s') \in R \;\land\; \delta(q, a) = q'$$

**Step 3 — Error states.** The set of *error states* (accepting states of the product) is $F_\otimes = S \times F$.

**Step 4 — Reachability check.** Run a BFS or DFS from $S_0 \times \{q_0\}$ in $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$.

- If no state in $F_\otimes$ is reachable: return `TRUE` — $M \models P$.
- If some state $(s, q) \in F_\otimes$ is reachable: return `FALSE` — $M \not\models P$. The path to that state is a counter-example.

**Step 5 — Correctness argument.** If a state in $F_\otimes$ is reachable via path labelled $\hat{u}$, then $\hat{u} \in L(\mathcal{A}) = \mathrm{BadPref}(P)$, so $\hat{u}$ has no extension in $P$, but the corresponding path of $M$ shows that the system has exhibited a bad prefix. Conversely, if no such state is reachable, no trace of $M$ is a bad prefix, so every trace has a good extension in $P$, so $L(M) \subseteq P$.

**Step 6 — Complexity.**

- **Time:** $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert R \vert / \vert S \vert) = O(\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert R \vert)$, or more conservatively $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)$.
- **Space:** $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert)$ for the visited set plus the worklist.

The algorithm is **linear** in the size of the product graph, which is the fundamental reason regular safety properties are so efficiently checkable. $\blacksquare$

---

### ⭐ Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

> **(a)** Consider a Kripke structure $M$ modelling two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ accessing a shared resource. Construct a DFA that recognises the bad prefixes of the property *"the two processes are never simultaneously in their critical section."* Explain how the product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$ is built and how reachability of error states in the product determines whether $M$ satisfies the property. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss why regular safety properties are checkable in linear time whereas general $\omega$-regular properties require more complex algorithms (e.g., nested DFS, SCC-based emptiness). How does symbolic verification (BDDs) alleviate the state explosion problem for regular safety property checking? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Atomic propositions.** Let $AP = \{c_1, c_2\}$ where $c_i$ is true iff process $P_i$ is in its critical section. The alphabet is $\Sigma = 2^{AP} = \{00, 01, 10, 11\}$ (using binary notation for $\{c_1, c_2\}$).

**Step 2 — Property.** $P_{\text{ME}} = \{ \sigma \in \Sigma^\omega \mid \forall i \geq 0 : \sigma[i] \neq 11 \}$.

**Step 3 — DFA for bad prefixes.** A finite word $\hat{u}$ is a bad prefix of $P_{\text{ME}}$ iff $\hat{u}$ contains the letter $11$ at some position. The DFA:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_0 &: \text{no violation seen} \\
q_1 &: \text{violation has occurred (trap state)} \\
\delta(q_0, a) &= \begin{cases} q_1 & \text{if } a = 11 \\ q_0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= q_1 \quad \forall a \\
F &= \{q_1\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$.** For each Kripke state $s$ and DFA state $q$, the product has a state $(s, q)$. Transitions:

- From $(s, q_0)$ on letter $a$: go to $(s', q_1)$ if $a = 11$ and $(s, a, s') \in R$; otherwise go to $(s', q_0)$.
- From $(s, q_1)$ on any letter $a$: go to $(s', q_1)$.

**Step 5 — Reachability check.** Run BFS from initial states $(s_0, q_0)$:

- If any state $(s, q_1)$ is reached, then there is a path of $M$ that produces a bad prefix — **mutual exclusion is violated**.
- If no such state is reached, **mutual exclusion holds** for $M$.

**Step 6 — Counter-example.** If the check fails, the BFS parent pointers yield a concrete path of $M$ that visits $c_1$ and $c_2$ simultaneously — a debugging artefact for the engineer.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Why regular safety is linear-time.**

A regular safety property is checked by a **single reachability pass** in the product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$. Reachability is in $O(\vert S \vert + \vert R \vert)$ per pass, and the product has size $\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert$, so the total cost is $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)$. This is **linear in the system size** (up to a constant factor of $\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert$).

**Step 2 — Why general $\omega$-regular is harder.**

For a general $\omega$-regular property (e.g., $\mathbf{GF} p$ — "$p$ holds infinitely often"), the automaton is a **Büchi automaton** with *accepting cycles* — the property is violated if *no* cycle of the product visits an accepting state. This is **emptiness of Büchi automata**, which requires:

- **Nested-DFS** (Courcoubetis et al., 1992): two-pass DFS to find accepting cycles.
- **SCC-based emptiness**: decompose the product into strongly connected components; the property holds iff no SCC containing an accepting state is reachable from an initial state.

Both are more complex than single BFS.

**Step 3 — State explosion mitigation via BDDs.**

Explicit-state product graphs suffer from the **state explosion problem**: $\vert M \otimes \mathcal{A} \vert$ can be $10^9$ or more for industrial designs. **Symbolic model checking** (Bryant, McMillan) represents sets of states and transition relations as **Binary Decision Diagrams (BDDs)**:

- A set $S' \subseteq S$ is encoded as a Boolean function $\chi_{S'} : \text{Bits}(S) \to \{0, 1\}$.
- The transition relation $R$ is encoded similarly as $\chi_R : \text{Bits}(S) \times \text{Bits}(S) \to \{0, 1\}$.
- Image computation $\text{Img}(S) = \{ s' \mid \exists s \in S : (s, s') \in R \}$ becomes a relational product followed by existential quantification — both efficiently expressed as BDD operations.
- The BFS of the previous explicit algorithm is replaced by an **iterative fixed-point computation**:
  $$\text{Reach} = \mu X . \, S_0 \cup \text{Img}(X)$$
  using BDD-based image computations. Each iteration handles *exponentially many* states in a single BDD operation.

**Step 4 — Industrial impact.** Symbolic regular safety checking, used in tools like **SMV (Cadence), NuSMV, and CBMC**, routinely handles systems with $10^{20}$ or more states — orders of magnitude beyond the reach of explicit-state enumeration. This is the workhorse of industrial hardware verification for safety properties (e.g., Intel, IBM, Motorola processor verification). $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks on regular safety property questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing safety with liveness:** A *safety* property says *"nothing bad happens."* A *liveness* property says *"something good eventually happens."* Students often mis-classify liveness properties as safety (e.g., $\mathbf{FG}\,p$ is *not* a safety property in general).
> 2. **Forgetting the prefix-closure of bad prefixes:** When defining a DFA for $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$, students sometimes forget to make the accepting state a *trap* (i.e., a state from which all transitions stay in $F$). Without this, the DFA accepts *extensions* of bad prefixes but not the prefixes themselves.
> 3. **Confusing $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ with $L(M)$:** $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ is a set of *finite* words over $2^{AP}$, not a set of Kripke states. The check is $L(M) \cap \mathrm{BadPref}(P) = \emptyset$, where $L(M)$ is the set of *traces* of $M$ projected as words. Mixing these leads to incorrect formulae.
> 4. **Ignoring the determinisation issue:** If the specification is given as an NFA, one must first **determinise** it via the subset construction (which can cause an exponential blow-up $2^{\vert Q \vert}$). Forgetting this step and feeding an NFA directly to the product is a common error.
> 5. **Missing the counter-example reconstruction:** The BFS / DFS must store *parent pointers* so that, when an error state is reached, the path to it can be unwound and reported as a counter-example. Examiners often deduct marks if the algorithm description omits this.
> 6. **Confusing "regular safety" with "LTL safety":** Not every LTL safety formula corresponds to a regular safety property in the sense of Huth & Ryan (actually, *every* LTL safety formula does — the converse fails; but the *syntactic* LTL safety fragment is a strict subset of all regular safety properties).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ☐ **Property** = set of acceptable infinite traces $P \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$.
- ☐ **Safety property** = every bad infinite trace has a *finite bad prefix* $\hat{u}$ such that no continuation of $\hat{u}$ is in $P$.
- ☐ **Regular safety property** = a safety property that is also a regular language, equivalently, a property whose set $\mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ of bad prefixes is regular.
- ☐ **Bad prefix** = finite word $\hat{u}$ with no extension in $P$.
- ☐ **Good prefix** = finite word $\hat{u}$ with at least one extension in $P$.
- ☐ **DFA for bad prefixes** has its accepting states as *trap* states to capture the prefix-closure property.
- ☐ **Product construction** $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$ synchronises the Kripke structure and the bad-prefix DFA on the alphabet $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$.
- ☐ **Error states** of the product = $S \times F$ (any Kripke state with a DFA accepting state).
- ☐ **Decidability** = reachability in a finite graph: $M \models P \iff$ no error state in $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$ is reachable from $S_0 \times \{q_0\}$.
- ☐ **Time complexity** = $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert)$ — linear in the product.
- ☐ **Space complexity** = $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert)$ — linear in the product.
- ☐ **Counter-example** = path in the product from an initial state to an error state.
- ☐ **LTL safety fragment** = $\{\mathbf{G}\varphi, \neg p, \varphi \wedge \psi, p\}$ is contained in regular safety properties.
- ☐ **Non-regular safety properties** exist (require unbounded counting).
- ☐ **Symbolic verification** (BDDs) mitigates state explosion by representing state sets as Boolean functions.
- ☐ **Industrial tools** for regular safety checking: SPIN, NuSMV, CBMC, BLAST, SLAM.
- ☐ **Key theorem to memorise:** $P$ is a regular safety property $\iff \mathrm{BadPref}(P)$ is regular (Huth & Ryan, Theorem 5.25).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-mile advice for KTU 2024 exam:** Whenever a question mentions *"model checking a property that nothing bad ever happens"*, your first reflex should be to ask *"is this a regular safety property?"* If yes, immediately invoke the product construction with a bad-prefix DFA. This single mental move earns full marks on roughly 60% of the Module 2 questions in past KTU university exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verifying Regular Safety Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Verifying Regular Safety Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **property** is a set of infinite sequences (behaviours) over the alphabet $2^{AP}$, where $AP$ is a finite set of atomic propositions that the system can observe. Formally, a property $P \subseteq (2^{AP})^\omega$ is a language of infinite words.

A **safety property** is a property $P_{safe}$ such that every infinite word $\sigma \notin P_{safe}$ has a *finite prefix* which cannot be extended to any word in $P_{safe}$. This finite prefix is called a **bad prefix**. Equivalently, a property $P$ is a safety property iff it is *prefix-closed* when lifted to finite words — i.e., its complement is characterised by a finite set of *forbidden finite traces*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Safety Property (Lichtenstein–Pnueli–Vardi Theorem):**
> A property $P$ is a safety property **iff** it is *prefix-closed* with respect to its good prefixes, or equivalently, the set of bad prefixes $\text{BadPref}(P)$ is a regular (finite-state, recognisable by a finite automaton) language of finite words.

A **regular safety property** is a safety property $P_{safe}$ whose set of *bad prefixes* $\text{BadPref}(P)$ is a **regular language** over $2^{AP}$. This means there exists a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) $\mathcal{A}_P$ that accepts exactly the bad prefixes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Regular"?** Regular languages are precisely those recognised by finite automata. Since the verification procedure needs to construct an automaton for the property and intersect it with the system's transition structure, requiring the property to be regular keeps the product finite-state and the algorithm decidable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **subway train network** running on automated tracks:

- **Atomic Propositions ($AP$)** = Sensors on the track (e.g., "Signal Green", "Door Closed", "Brake Engaged", "Speed = 0").
- **System Behaviour** = The infinite sequence of sensor readings generated by the train's operation.
- **Safety Property** = "The train **never** departs a station with its doors open."
  - A *bad prefix* = any finite sensor log that already shows "Doors Opened" immediately followed by "Train Departed". Once this happens, *no* future continuation can fix the violation.
- **Regular Safety Property** = The set of all such bad prefixes is finite and pattern-based, recognisable by a simple finite-state gadget (e.g., a counter or a small DFA).

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** Safety = "Something *bad* never happens." Liveness = "Something *good* eventually happens." The verification of regular safety properties reduces to a **reachability problem on the product** of the system and the property's bad-prefix automaton — and reachability is one of the easiest problems in model checking (linear in the product size using BFS/DFS).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions Used in KTU Valuation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $M$ | Kripke structure (system model) with $|S|$ states and $|R|$ transitions |
| $\mathcal{A}_P$ | Property automaton (DFA) for bad prefixes with $n$ states |
| $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_P$ | Synchronous product of $M$ and $\mathcal{A}_P$ |
| $|S_M \otimes S_P|$ | Number of product states = $|S| \cdot n$ |
| $\text{time complexity}$ | $O(|S| \cdot n \cdot \log(\text{max out-degree}))$ for BFS reachability |
| $\text{space complexity}$ | $O(|S| \cdot n)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Automata-Theoretic Verification Framework

The verification of regular safety properties proceeds in three canonical stages:

### Stage 1 — Model the System
The system under verification is formalised as a **Kripke structure** $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ where:
- $S$ is a finite set of states.
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of initial states.
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ is the total transition relation.
- $L : S \to 2^{AP}$ is the labelling function mapping each state to the set of atomic propositions true at it.

### Stage 2 — Encode the Property as a Bad-Prefix Automaton
The regular safety property $P$ is encoded as a DFA $\mathcal{A}_P = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ over the alphabet $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$, where:
- $Q$ is a finite set of automaton states.
- $q_0 \in Q$ is the initial state.
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ is the transition function.
- $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of *accepting* (i.e., "bad-prefix-reached") states.

The language accepted is $L(\mathcal{A}_P) = \text{BadPref}(P)$.

### Stage 3 — Build the Synchronous Product and Check Reachability
The product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_P$ is a finite directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V = S \times Q$.
- An edge $((s, q), (s', q'))$ exists iff $R(s, s')$ and $q' = \delta(q, L(s'))$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Theorem (Correctness of Product-Based Safety Verification):**
> $M \models P_{safe}$ if and only if **no accepting state** of $\mathcal{A}_P$ is reachable in $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_P$ from any initial state $(s_0, q_0)$ where $s_0 \in S_0$.

If an accepting state is reached, the path to it is a *counterexample* — a finite execution of $M$ that is a bad prefix, violating the safety property.

## 2.2 Why Finite Automata Suffice (and Why Not Pushdown Automata)

The restriction to *regular* safety properties is essential. If we allowed, say, context-free bad-prefix languages, the product construction would have to use a **pushdown automaton**, and reachability in pushdown systems is more expensive (though still decidable, via saturation). Regular safety properties are the sweet spot:
- Decidable in linear time in the product.
- Closed under union, intersection, and complement (within regular languages).
- Expressive enough for nearly all practical "bad-thing-never-happens" specifications (mutual exclusion, absence of deadlock, no buffer overflow, no use-after-free, etc.).

## 2.3 The Verification Algorithm (Pseudo-Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the *conceptual* structure of the algorithm. No programming language is used since the course is a formal/theoretical elective and KTU valuation rewards algorithmic understanding, not coding syntax.

**Algorithm** $\text{VerifyRegularSafety}(M, \mathcal{A}_P)$:
1. Compute the synchronous product graph $G = M \otimes \mathcal{A}_P$.
2. Initialise a worklist $W$ with $\{(s_0, q_0) \mid s_0 \in S_0\}$.
3. Mark all such initial product states as visited.
4. While $W$ is not empty:
   1. Pop a state $(s, q)$ from $W$.
   2. If $q \in F$, return $\text{VIOLATED}$ and emit the path to $(s, q)$ as counterexample.
   3. For each successor $(s', q')$ via $R(s, s')$ and $\delta(q, L(s'))$:
      - If $(s', q')$ is unvisited, mark visited and push to $W$.
5. Return $\text{VERIFIED}$.

The counterexample extraction in step 4.ii uses parent pointers (or a nested DFS over a spanning forest) to recover the shortest bad prefix.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Type |
|---|---|---|
| Product state space size | $\vert S \otimes Q \vert = \vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert$ | dimensionless (count) |
| Product transition count | $\vert R \vert \cdot \vert \delta \vert$ upper bound | dimensionless (count) |
| Verification time | $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert + \vert R \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert)$ | time steps |
| Verification space | $O(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert)$ | states |
| Bad prefix length bound | $\le \vert S \vert \cdot \vert Q \vert$ (product diameter) | transitions |
| $\models$ relation | $M \models P \iff \text{Traces}(M) \subseteq P$ | logical entailment |
| $M \not\models P$ witness | A path to $F$ in $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_P$ | finite path |
| Property automaton type | DFA, NFA, or NFA-$\varepsilon$ for $\text{BadPref}(P)$ | finite automaton |
| Alphabets | $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$ for both $M$ and $\mathcal{A}_P$ | power set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The same machinery is the engine inside **SPIN** (for the `never` claim automata), **NuSMV** (for CTL safety fragments), **CBMC**, and **JPF**. Modern hardware verification at Intel/AMD uses regular safety property verification (via "assertion-based verification") to catch silicon bugs before fabrication — each bug saved represents millions of dollars in re-spin cost.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Mutual Exclusion on a Two-Process Semaphore

### 3.1.1 System Model

Consider two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ competing for a critical section. We define:
- $AP = \{c_1, c_2\}$ where $c_i$ means "Process $i$ is in its critical section."
- The Kripke structure $M$ has four reachable states tracking combinations of $(c_1, c_2)$:
  - $s_0$: $(\text{false}, \text{false})$ — both in non-critical section (initial).
  - $s_1$: $(\text{true}, \text{false})$ — only $P_1$ in critical section.
  - $s_2$: $(\text{false}, \text{true})$ — only $P_2$ in critical section.
  - $s_3$: $(\text{true}, \text{true})$ — **both** in critical section (must never be reached).

Transitions: $s_0 \to s_1$, $s_0 \to s_2$, $s_1 \to s_0$, $s_2 \to s_0$, plus the *forbidden* transition $s_1 \to s_3$ and $s_2 \to s_3$ (to test violation).

### 3.1.2 Property as a Bad-Prefix Automaton

The mutual-exclusion property is:
$$P_{mutex} = \{\sigma \in (2^{AP})^\omega \mid \forall i \ge 0, \neg(c_1 \in L(s_i) \land c_2 \in L(s_i))\}$$

A bad prefix is any finite trace that already contains a state where both $c_1$ and $c_2$ are true simultaneously. The automaton $\mathcal{A}_P$ for $\text{BadPref}(P_{mutex})$ has two states:

- $q_0$ (safe, initial): Neither violation seen yet.
- $q_1$ (violating, accepting): Violation has occurred.

Transitions of $\mathcal{A}_P$:
- $\delta(q_0, A) = q_0$ for all $A \subseteq AP$ such that $\neg(c_1 \in A \land c_2 \in A)$.
- $\delta(q_0, \{c_1, c_2\}) = q_1$ (a state with both propositions true is the bad event).
- $\delta(q_1, A) = q_1$ for all $A$ (once violated, stays violated — "sticky" trap).

Accepting set $F = \{q_1\}$.

### 3.1.3 Building the Synchronous Product

The product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_P$ has $|S| \cdot |Q| = 4 \times 2 = 8$ product states. We enumerate them and compute the product transition relation step by step.

**Step 1 — Identify the 8 product states** $\{s_i, q_j\}$ for $i \in \{0,1,2,3\}$ and $j \in \{0,1\}$:
$(s_0, q_0), (s_0, q_1), (s_1, q_0), (s_1, q_1), (s_2, q_0), (s_2, q_1), (s_3, q_0), (s_3, q_1)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the product transitions** using the rule: an edge $((s, q) \to (s', q'))$ exists iff $R(s, s')$ and $q' = \delta(q, L(s'))$.

Recall the labelling function $L$:
- $L(s_0) = \emptyset$ (neither proposition holds).
- $L(s_1) = \{c_1\}$.
- $L(s_2) = \{c_2\}$.
- $L(s_3) = \{c_1, c_2\}$.

We tabulate transitions from each source state, applying $\delta$:

From $(s_0, q_0)$ with $L(s_0) = \emptyset$:
$$\delta(q_0, \emptyset) = q_0 \text{ (safe input)}$$
So successors in $M$ from $s_0$ are $s_1$ and $s_2$. Product successors:
- $((s_0, q_0) \to (s_1, q_0))$.
- $((s_0, q_0) \to (s_2, q_0))$.

From $(s_1, q_0)$ with $L(s_1) = \{c_1\}$:
$$\delta(q_0, \{c_1\}) = q_0 \text{ (no violation yet, only one in CS)}$$
Successors in $M$ from $s_1$ are $s_0$ and $s_3$. Product successors:
- $((s_1, q_0) \to (s_0, q_0))$.
- $((s_1, q_0) \to (s_3, q_0))$.

From $(s_2, q_0)$ with $L(s_2) = \{c_2\}$:
$$\delta(q_0, \{c_2\}) = q_0$$
Successors in $M$ from $s_2$ are $s_0$ and $s_3$. Product successors:
- $((s_2, q_0) \to (s_0, q_0))$.
- $((s_2, q_0) \to (s_3, q_0))$.

From $(s_3, q_0)$ with $L(s_3) = \{c_1, c_2\}$:
$$\delta(q_0, \{c_1, c_2\}) = q_1 \text{ (VIOLATION)}$$
Successors in $M$ from $s_3$ are $s_0$ (assume it can return to safe region). Product successors:
- $((s_3, q_0) \to (s_0, q_1))$.

From $(s_0, q_1)$, $(s_1, q_1)$, $(s_2, q_1)$, $(s_3, q_1)$:
$$\delta(q_1, A) = q_1 \text{ for all } A \subseteq AP \text{ (sticky trap)}$$
These states are "post-violation" — every product transition lands in another $q_1$ state.

**Step 3 — Initial states.** Initial product states are $(s_0, q_0)$ only.

**Step 4 — Reachability search.** Start BFS/DFS from $(s_0, q_0)$. Trace the frontier:
1. Visit $(s_0, q_0)$. Enqueue $(s_1, q_0)$ and $(s_2, q_0)$.
2. Visit $(s_1, q_0)$. Enqueue $(s_0, q_0)$ [already visited] and $(s_3, q_0)$.
3. Visit $(s_2, q_0)$. Enqueue $(s_0, q_0)$ [visited] and $(s_3, q_0)$ [will be visited next].
4. Visit $(s_3, q_0)$. This is **critical**: $\delta(q_0, \{c_1, c_2\}) = q_1$, so the product transition from $s_3$ yields $(s_0, q_1)$.
5. Visit $(s_0, q_1)$. The automaton component is $q_1 \in F$ — **VIOLATION DETECTED**.

**Step 5 — Counterexample extraction.** The shortest bad prefix recovered is:
$$s_0 \to s_1 \to s_3 \to s_0$$
with corresponding labels $\emptyset, \{c_1\}, \{c_1, c_2\}, \emptyset$. The third state $s_3$ carries the label $\{c_1, c_2\}$ which triggers $\mathcal{A}_P$ to enter $q_1$, the accepting "violated" state.

### 3.1.4 Conclusion of the Example

The verification reports $\text{VIOLATED}$ with the counterexample "Process $P_1$ enters its critical section, then both processes are simultaneously in their critical sections." If we remove the $s_1 \to s_3$ and $s_2 \to s_3$ transitions, no product state with $q_1$ is reachable, and the algorithm reports $\text{VERIFIED}$ — proving mutual exclusion.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Verifier

| Property Type | Automaton | Product Construction | Decision Problem | Complexity Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Regular safety (bad prefix) | DFA on $\Sigma^*$ | Finite graph $S \times Q$ | Reachability of $F$ | $NLOGSPACE$-complete, linear time on product |
| Context-free safety | Pushdown automaton | Pushdown system | Reachability | Polynomial (P-complete via saturation) |
| Recursive safety (Turing-recognisable) | Turing machine | Undecidable in general | Reachability | Undecidable |
| Liveness (Büchi acceptance) | Büchi automaton | Infinite-state exploration | Cycle detection with accepting state revisit | $P$-complete, polynomial |
| Fair liveness | Streett / Rabin automaton | Fair graph | Non-emptiness with fairness | $P$-complete |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**R**egular **S**afety is **R**eachability" — and reachability is solvable in **linear time** using graph search. This is why regular safety is the *easiest* fragment of verification and appears so frequently in board questions.
