**Step 1 — Direct product yields an NGA:** Define

$$\mathcal{A}_{\cap} = (Q_1 \times Q_2,\ \Sigma,\ \delta_{\cap},\ Q_{0,1} \times Q_{0,2},\ \{F^{(1)}, F^{(2)}\})$$

with $\delta_{\cap}((p,q), a) = \delta_1(p, a) \times \delta_2(q, a)$, $F^{(1)} = F_1 \times Q_2$, $F^{(2)} = Q_1 \times F_2$. **(2 marks)**

This is an **NGA** (not NBA) because it has **two** acceptance sets; the NGA acceptance condition requires $\inf(\rho) \cap F^{(1)} \neq \emptyset$ **and** $\inf(\rho) \cap F^{(2)} \neq \emptyset$, i.e., a conjunction rather than a single disjunction. **Why a direct NBA construction fails:** A naive attempt to set $F_{\cap} = F_1 \times F_2$ is incorrect. Consider $w$ where the run of $\mathcal{A}_{\cap}$ alternates between visiting $F^{(1)}$ and $F^{(2)}$ at different times: $F_1 \times F_2$ might be hit only finitely often, but both $F^{(1)}$ and $F^{(2)}$ are hit infinitely often — so the word *should* be accepted but would be rejected. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — NGA to NBA reduction:** Introduce $k = 2$ "phases" (one per acceptance set):

$$Q' = Q_1 \times Q_2 \times \{1, 2\}$$

Initial states: $Q_{0,1} \times Q_{0,2} \times \{1\}$. The accepting set is:

$$F' = (F_1 \times Q_2 \times \{1\}) \cup (Q_1 \times F_2 \times \{2\})$$

The transition advances the phase from $j$ to $j+1 \pmod{k}$ **only** if the current state is in $F^{(j)}$. Because the phase index must cycle through all $k$ values infinitely often, and each cycle through a phase requires a visit to $F^{(j)}$, every $F^{(j)}$ is hit infinitely often — exactly the NGA condition. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Correctness:** Any run of $\mathcal{A}_{\cap}$ satisfying the NGA condition can be "lifted" to a run of $\mathcal{A}'$ that visits $F'$ infinitely often (by tracking the phase), and conversely. So $L(\mathcal{A}_{\cap}) = L(\mathcal{A}')$ and the latter is a genuine NBA. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) Convert the LTL formula $\varphi = \mathbf{G}(a \rightarrow \mathbf{F} b)$ into an equivalent NBA. Explain each construction step. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**LTL semantics:** $\varphi = \mathbf{G}(a \rightarrow \mathbf{F} b)$ means "globally, whenever $a$ holds, $b$ will hold at some future moment". The negation $\neg \varphi = \mathbf{F}(a \land \mathbf{G} \neg b)$ says "there exists a position where $a$ holds and from then on $b$ never holds". An NBA for $\neg \varphi$ is constructed and then complemented (via Safra) — but for this problem, we construct the NBA for $\varphi$ directly by tableau-style construction. **(1 mark)**

**Step 1 — Closure of subformulas:** $\mathrm{cl}(\varphi) = \{a, b, a \rightarrow \mathbf{F} b, \mathbf{F} b, \neg a, \neg b, \neg(a \rightarrow \mathbf{F} b)\}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — States as consistent subsets:** Each state of the NBA is a maximally consistent subset of $\mathrm{cl}(\varphi)$ — a set containing either $\psi$ or $\neg \psi$ for every $\psi \in \mathrm{cl}(\varphi)$, satisfying local logical consistency. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Transition condition:** A state $s$ can transition to $s'$ on symbol $a$ (an interpretation of atomic propositions) iff:
- $s$ and $s'$ agree on the **current values** of atomic propositions (i.e., $a \in s$ iff $a$ is true at the current step),
- For every "next-time" obligation $\mathbf{X}\psi$: $\mathbf{X}\psi \in s$ iff $\psi \in s'$,
- For every "until/future" obligation: standard tableau expansion rules apply. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Accepting set $F$:** The Büchi condition requires that every "promise" $\mathbf{F} \psi$ that was issued in $s$ must eventually be **fulfilled**: any state where $\mathbf{F} b$ is pending but $b$ is not in scope must mark a "fulfillment" obligation. The standard construction partitions states into those that contain $\mathbf{F} b$ and require a future visit, and marks accepting states as those that have **just fulfilled** this obligation. For the formula $\mathbf{G}(a \rightarrow \mathbf{F} b)$, the set $F$ consists of states that contain $b$ (because each such state "fulfills" any pending $\mathbf{F} b$ promise). **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Resulting NBA:** The resulting automaton has at most $2^{|\mathrm{cl}(\varphi)|} = 2^{7} = 128$ states (in the worst case, fewer after simplification). It accepts an ω-word $w$ iff $w \models \varphi$. **(1 mark)**

**Verification trace:** On the input $w = a, b, a, b, a, b, \ldots$ (where $a$ and $b$ alternate), the run visits a state in $F$ at every step (because $b$ is true at every even step), so $\inf(\rho) \cap F \neq \emptyset$ and the word is accepted — which matches the LTL semantics. On the input $w = a, \neg b, \neg b, \neg b, \ldots$ the run enters a non-accepting cycle, and acceptance fails — also matching LTL. **(Included for full credit.)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nondeterministic Büchi Automata (NBA)**

- **Definition:** $\mathcal{A} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, Q_0, F)$ — a 5-tuple. Memorize the role of each component. **(high-weight)**
- **Acceptance condition:** $\inf(\rho) \cap F \neq \emptyset$ — **infinitely often**, not "once". **(most-missed concept)**
- **$\inf(\rho)$** = set of states occurring infinitely often in run $\rho$. By the pigeonhole principle, $\inf(\rho) \neq \emptyset$ for any infinite run on a finite-state automaton.
- **Nondeterminism** appears in $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ — set-valued, not single-valued.
- **NBA closure properties (memorize this exact list):**
  - ✅ Closed under **union** (direct product, $F_{\cup} = (F_1 \times Q_2) \cup (Q_1 \times F_2)$).
  - ✅ Closed under **intersection** (product gives NGA, then reduce to NBA via $k$-phase construction).
  - ❌ **Not closed under complementation** — requires Safra's $2^{O(n \log n)}$ construction.
  - ✅ Closed under projection (existential quantification) onto a sub-alphabet.
- **NBA → DRA determinization (Safra):** produces $2^{O(n \log n)}$ states; tight bound (no better general construction).
- **LTL → NBA:** exponential in formula length ($2^{\mathcal{O}(|\varphi|)}$); the standard tableau construction.
- **Model checking use:** system $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varphi$ iff $L(\mathcal{A}_{\mathcal{M}}) \cap L(\mathcal{A}_{\neg\varphi}) = \emptyset$ (language emptiness of the synchronized product).
- **Deterministic vs. Nondeterministic distinction:** every DFA is an NBA (with $F$ visited exactly once at the end is equivalent, since runs are unique). The converse is false.
- **Trap states / sinks:** non-accepting states with self-loops on every symbol; often used in constructions to enforce violation of properties.
- **Common exam pitfall:** confusing Büchi acceptance ($\inf$) with reachability (single visit), and confusing NGA acceptance (conjunction over acceptance sets) with NBA acceptance (disjunction over accepting states).
- **Sanity check for exam answers:** always specify **why** the accepting states are visited infinitely often — not just that they are visited at all.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deterministic Büchi Automata (DBA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deterministic Büchi Automata (DBA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Deterministic Büchi Automaton (DBA)** is a quintuple

$$
\mathcal{A} = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \delta,\ q_0,\ F)
$$

where each component is defined as follows:

- $Q$ is a **finite, non-empty** set of states.
- $\Sigma$ is a **finite alphabet** of input symbols.
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ is a **total deterministic transition function** (exactly one successor for every state-symbol pair).
- $q_0 \in Q$ is the unique **initial state**.
- $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of **accepting (final) states**.

A DBA is structurally identical to a DFA, but it consumes **infinite words** $w = a_0 a_1 a_2 \dots \in \Sigma^{\omega}$ and uses a **Büchi acceptance condition**: a run is accepting iff it visits $F$ **infinitely often**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Büchi Acceptance Rule:** A run $\rho = q_0\, q_1\, q_2\, \dots$ is *accepting* if and only if
> $$\inf(\rho) \cap F \neq \varnothing$$
> i.e., at least one accepting state appears infinitely many times in the run.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Night Security Guard

Imagine a **night security guard** patrolling a building with several rooms. He follows a strict schedule:

1. He is given an **infinite list** of instructions (the input word), one instruction per second.
2. From **any room, for any instruction, there is exactly one next room** he must walk to — no choice, no ambiguity (determinism).
3. A small set of rooms are marked "**VIP Rooms**" (the accepting set $F$).
4. The patrol is considered *successful* if the guard **keeps entering VIP rooms forever** — at least once every now and then, infinitely often.

If at some point he walks past VIP rooms only finitely many times, the patrol **fails**, even if he keeps walking. The DBA formalizes this exact idea: an input infinite word is accepted iff the deterministic walk revisits $F$ infinitely often.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** A DBA processes *infinite* traces because reactive systems (operating systems, hardware circuits, communication protocols) are non-terminating. The Büchi condition elegantly captures "good behavior occurs repeatedly forever".

## 1.3 Physical / Engineering Constants

- **Alphabet size** $|\Sigma|$ — typically $2$ for boolean signals, up to $2^k$ for $k$-bit data words.
- **State count** $|Q|$ — usually $10^1$ to $10^6$ in industrial model checkers.
- **Emptiness-check complexity** $\mathcal{O}(|Q|)$ — linear in the number of states (using Tarjan's SCC algorithm).
- **Safra's construction blowup** from NBA to DBA: at most $2^{n} \cdot n!$ states, where $n = |Q_{\text{NBA}}|$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Determinism vs. Nondeterminism — The Central Distinction

The transition function behaves fundamentally differently:

| Property | Nondeterministic Büchi Automaton (NBA) | Deterministic Büchi Automaton (DBA) |
|---|---|---|
| Transition type | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ |
| Successor count | Any number (incl. $0$, $>1$) | **Exactly one** |
| Run count per word | Many (tree of runs) | **Exactly one** |
| Expressive power | **Full** $\omega$-regular | **Strict subset** of $\omega$-regular |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Expressivity Gap:** There exist $\omega$-regular languages accepted by an NBA but **by no DBA** whatsoever. The classic counter-example is
> $$L = (a+b)^{*}a^{\omega} \cup b^{\omega}$$
> which is NBA-recognizable but **not** DBA-recognizable.

## 2.2 Operational Semantics (Step-by-Step)

Given input $\omega$-word $w = a_0\, a_1\, a_2\, \dots$:

1. **Initialize:** Set current state $q := q_0$, set $i := 0$.
2. **Loop forever:**
   - Read the next symbol $a_i$.
   - Update $q := \delta(q, a_i)$.
   - Record whether $q \in F$.
   - Increment $i := i+1$.
3. **Decide:** If accepting states have been visited *infinitely often*, **ACCEPT**; else **REJECT**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

$$
\begin{array}{|l|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Quantity} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Notes / Units} \\
\hline
\text{State-space size} & \vert Q \vert & \text{dimensionless integer} \\
\hline
\text{Transition function} & \delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q & \text{total function} \\
\hline
\text{Acceptance (Büchi)} & \inf(\rho) \cap F \neq \varnothing & \text{set intersection} \\
\hline
\text{Emptiness check} & \mathcal{O}(\vert Q \vert + \vert\delta\vert) & \text{Tarjan SCC} \\
\hline
\text{NBA} \rightarrow \text{DBA (Safra)} & 2^{n} \cdot n! & n = \vert Q_{\text{NBA}}\vert \\
\hline
\text{Complement of DBA} & \mathcal{O}(2^{n}) & \text{standard determinization} \\
\hline
\text{Product DBA} & \vert Q_1\vert \cdot \vert Q_2\vert & \text{component-wise product} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** DBAs underpin **SPIN's** LTL-to-Büchi translation, **Cadence SMV's** synchronous model-checking engine, and **hardware fair-arbitration verification** where every cycle must see every requester infinitely often.

## 2.4 Why DBAs Matter in Verification

- **Reactive system specs** (LTL formulas of the form $\Box \Diamond p$) reduce naturally to Büchi acceptance.
- **Determinism enables complementation cheaply** — a DBA for $\Sigma^{\omega} \setminus L$ is obtained by swapping $F \leftrightarrow Q \setminus F$ (incorrect for NBA, correct for DBA).
- **Canonical construction** for synchronous products in compositional verification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Emptiness-Check Algorithm for a DBA

**Theorem.** $L(\mathcal{A}) \neq \varnothing$ iff there exists a state $q \in F$ that lies on a cycle **reachable from $q_0$**.

**Proof.** We prove both directions explicitly.

### $(\Rightarrow)$ Assume $L(\mathcal{A}) \neq \varnothing$.

Then there exists an accepted infinite word $w = a_0 a_1 a_2 \dots \in \Sigma^{\omega}$.
Let $\rho = q_0\, q_1\, q_2\, \dots$ be the unique run on $w$.

Because $w$ is accepted, $\rho$ visits $F$ infinitely often. Pick two distinct positions $i < j$ with $q_i \in F$ and $q_j \in F$. Since the run is deterministic, the segment

$$
q_i \xrightarrow{a_i\, a_{i+1}\, \dots\, a_{j-1}} q_j
$$

is a cycle (state $q_i$ is revisited at position $j$), and $q_i \in F$. Hence an accepting state on a reachable cycle exists.

### $(\Leftarrow)$ Assume such $q \in F$ exists on a reachable cycle.

Reachability gives a finite path $\pi : q_0 \to q$. The cycle gives a non-empty finite loop $\gamma : q \to q$ reading some word $u \in \Sigma^{+}$.

Define the $\omega$-word

$$
w = \pi\ \gamma\ \gamma\ \gamma\ \dots
$$

which is the path to $q$ followed by infinitely many repetitions of the cycle. The corresponding run stays forever inside the cycle and visits $q \in F$ infinitely often. Hence $w \in L(\mathcal{A})$, so $L(\mathcal{A}) \neq \varnothing$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Subset Construction for NBA $\rightarrow$ DBA

To convert an NBA into an equivalent DBA, the standard approach (Safra) replaces the power-set construction with a *tree-of-subsets* construction. A simplified derivation follows.

**Step 1 — Initial state of DBA.**

$$
q_0^{\text{DBA}} = \text{wrap}(\{q_0^{\text{NBA}}\}, \text{level}=0)
$$

Each DBA state is a *node* holding a subset of NBA states.

**Step 2 — Transition on symbol $a$.**

$$
S' = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_{\text{NBA}}(q, a)
$$

**Step 3 — Accepting-state tracking.**

For each $S$ that contains at least one $q \in F_{\text{NBA}}$, we record a "fresh track" so that we know when the Büchi condition has been "freshly" satisfied since the last visit to $F$.

**Step 4 — State size bound.**

If the NBA has $n$ states, the number of distinct subsets is $2^{n}$. Tracks are subsets of the active subsets, giving at most $n$ ordered tracks. The total bound is therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
|Q_{\text{DBA}}| &\leq 2^{n} \cdot n! \\
\log_2 |Q_{\text{DBA}}| &\leq n + \log_2(n!) \\
&\leq n + n \log_2 n \quad \text{(using Stirling's approximation)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Acceptance of $L = (ab)^{\omega}$

Consider the DBA $\mathcal{A}_1$ for the language $(ab)^{\omega}$ over $\Sigma = \{a,b\}$.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Current state} & \textbf{On } a & \textbf{On } b & \textbf{Accepting?} \\
\hline
q_0 \text{ (start)} & q_1 & q_3 & \text{No} \\
\hline
q_1 & q_3 & q_2 & \text{No} \\
\hline
q_2 & q_1 & q_3 & \textbf{Yes} \\
\hline
q_3 \text{ (trap)} & q_3 & q_3 & \text{No} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Run on $w = ababab\dots$:**

$$
q_0 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} q_2 \xrightarrow{a} q_1 \xrightarrow{b} \dots
$$

State $q_2 \in F$ is visited at positions $2, 4, 6, \dots$ — **infinitely often**. Hence $w \in L(\mathcal{A}_1)$. $\checkmark$

Run on $w = aabab\dots$ hits $q_3$ and stays there; $q_3 \notin F$, so the run never revisits $F$ after that. **Rejected.** $\checkmark$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Diagram of the Running DBA $\mathcal{A}_1$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ0((q0\nstart))
    nodeQ1((q1))
    nodeQ2((q2\nACCEPT))
    nodeQ3((q3\ntrap))

    nodeQ0 -- a --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ0 -- b --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ1 -- a --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ1 -- b --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -- a --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ2 -- b --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ3 -- a --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ3 -- b --> nodeQ3
```

## 4.2 NBA-to-DBA Translation Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        NBA["NBA\n(Q, Sigma, delta_N, q0, F)"]
        InputWord["Infinite word w = a0 a1 a2 ..."]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE[Determinization Engine]
        Subset["Subset Builder\nS' = union of delta_N(q, a)"]
        Tracker["Acceptance Tracker\nMark when F is freshly hit"]
        SafraTree["Safra Tree Builder\nTrack ordering + pruning"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[DBA Layer]
        DBAState["DBA State\n(T, marking)"]
        AcceptLoop["Loop in Q_DBA\ninfinite visit to F"]
    end

    NBA --> Subset
    Subset --> Tracker
    Tracker --> SafraTree
    SafraTree --> DBAState
    InputWord --> DBAState
    DBAState --> AcceptLoop
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Model-Checking Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Spec["LTL Spec phi"] --> Neg["Negate phi"]
    Neg --> NBA["NBA for not phi"]
    NBA -->|"Safra\n2^n * n!"| DBA["Equivalent DBA"]
    DBA --> Product["Product with system K"]
    Product --> Emptiness["Emptiness Check\nO(|Q|)"]
    Emptiness --> Verdict["Counterexample\nOR Verified"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a Deterministic Büchi Automaton (DBA). State the Büchi acceptance condition precisely.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A DBA is a 5-tuple $\mathcal{A} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where
$Q$ is a finite set of states, $\Sigma$ a finite alphabet, $q_0 \in Q$ the initial state, $F \subseteq Q$ the accepting set, and $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ a **total** deterministic transition function. **(1 mark)**

For an infinite word $w = a_0 a_1 a_2 \dots \in \Sigma^{\omega}$ there is a unique run $\rho = q_0\, q_1\, q_2\, \dots$ with $q_{i+1} = \delta(q_i, a_i)$. **(1 mark)**

The word is *accepted* iff $\inf(\rho) \cap F \neq \varnothing$, i.e., some accepting state is visited **infinitely often**. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Give one $\omega$-regular language accepted by a DBA and one that is NBA-recognizable but NOT DBA-recognizable. Justify the second claim.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- DBA-acceptable: $L_1 = (ab)^{\omega}$ — accepted by a 4-state DBA (see §3.3). **(1 mark)**
- Not DBA-acceptable: $L_2 = (a+b)^{*}a^{\omega} \cup b^{\omega}$ — recognizable by a 3-state NBA. **(1 mark)**
- **Justification:** Any DBA must commit, on seeing its first $a$, to entering a cycle of $a$'s. But if $b$ is seen later, the run cannot recover and stay in $L_2$. The language violates the "deterministic prefix-class" property. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** *Construct a DBA for the language*
$$L = (a b^{*} a)^{\omega}.$$
*Clearly specify $Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F$, and show that your construction is deterministic. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (a):**

- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$.
- States: $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\}$.
  - $q_0$ = "start, expect first $a$".
  - $q_1$ = "inside block, reading $b^{*}$".
  - $q_2$ = "block completed, expect next $a$".
  - $q_3$ = "trap / dead".
- $q_0 = q_0$, $F = \{q_2\}$.
- Transition function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= q_1, & \delta(q_0, b) &= q_3 \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= q_2, & \delta(q_1, b) &= q_1 \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= q_1, & \delta(q_2, b) &= q_3 \\
\delta(q_3, a) &= q_3, & \delta(q_3, b) &= q_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Every row has **exactly one entry per column**, so the function is total and deterministic. **(7 marks breakdown: 1 for $Q,\Sigma$, 2 for $\delta$, 1 for $q_0,F$, 3 for determinism + run demonstration)**

**(b)** *Explain the Büchi acceptance condition. Prove that the emptiness problem for DBA is decidable in $\mathcal{O}(|Q|)$ time. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (b):**

**Büchi acceptance (2 marks):** As in §2.2 — a run is accepting iff $\inf(\rho) \cap F \neq \varnothing$.

**Emptiness is $\mathcal{O}(|Q|)$ (5 marks):**
- Build the directed graph of $\mathcal{A}$ (vertices $Q$, edges from $\delta$).
- Run **Tarjan's SCC algorithm**, which is linear in the number of edges.
- Check whether any SCC $C$ satisfies $C \cap F \neq \varnothing$ **and** $|C| \geq 1$ cycle (i.e., non-trivial SCC).
- If yes, the cycle yields an accepted $\omega$-word by repeating it forever. If no, $L(\mathcal{A}) = \varnothing$.

Tarjan's algorithm processes each vertex once: $\mathcal{O}(|Q| + |\delta|) = \mathcal{O}(|Q|)$ when the alphabet is fixed. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Convert the following NBA over $\Sigma = \{a,b\}$ to an equivalent DBA via the subset construction, and prove equivalence. **(7 marks)***
- $Q = \{p_0, p_1\}$, $q_0 = p_0$, $F = \{p_1\}$.
- $\delta(p_0, a) = \{p_0, p_1\}$, $\delta(p_0, b) = \{p_0\}$,
- $\delta(p_1, a) = \varnothing$, $\delta(p_1, b) = \{p_1\}$.

**Model Solution (a):**

Subset states of DBA: $2^{\{p_0, p_1\}} = \{\varnothing, \{p_0\}, \{p_1\}, \{p_0, p_1\}\}$.
- Initial: $S_0 = \{p_0\}$.
- $F_{\text{DBA}}$ = subsets containing some $p_1$ → $\{\{p_1\}, \{p_0, p_1\}\}$.
- Transitions computed via $\delta_{\text{DBA}}(S, x) = \bigcup_{q \in S} \delta_{\text{NBA}}(q, x)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{DBA}}(\{p_0\}, a) &= \{p_0, p_1\}, & \delta_{\text{DBA}}(\{p_0\}, b) &= \{p_0\} \\
\delta_{\text{DBA}}(\{p_0, p_1\}, a) &= \{p_0, p_1\}, & \delta_{\text{DBA}}(\{p_0, p_1\}, b) &= \{p_0, p_1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reachable states reduce to $\{\{p_0\}, \{p_0,p_1\}\}$. **Equivalence:** every NBA run has a leaf corresponding to the unique deterministic path of the DBA. Büchi condition preserved because the accepting subset is reached whenever NBA could be. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss the expressivity gap between NBA and DBA. Show that $L = (a+b)^{*}a^{\omega}$ has no DBA. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution (b):**

**Expressivity gap (2 marks):** NBA accept **all** $\omega$-regular languages; DBA accept only those that are *deterministic*, which form a strict subset. Safra's construction converts NBA → DBA with at most $2^{n} \cdot n!$ states, and the bound is essentially tight.

**No DBA for $L$ (5 marks):**
- Suppose, for contradiction, some DBA $\mathcal{A} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ accepts $L$.
- Consider the run on $a^{\omega}$: since $a^{\omega} \in L$, the run must visit $F$ infinitely often. So the run $\rho_a$ is eventually periodic, say entering a cycle $C_a \subseteq F$ consisting only of $a$-transitions.
- Now consider the run on $a^k b\, a^{\omega}$ for any $k \geq 0$. Because this word is in $L$ (it ends in $a^{\omega}$), the run must also be accepting.
- Because the DBA is deterministic, the prefix $a^k b$ forces a unique state $q_k$. From $q_k$, the continuation $a^{\omega}$ must drive the run to visit $F$ infinitely often — i.e., $q_k$ must be in a cycle of $a$-transitions intersecting $F$.
- But there are infinitely many $k$, while $Q$ is finite. By pigeonhole, two prefixes $a^{k_1} b$ and $a^{k_2} b$ end in the same state. Both lead to the same $a$-cycle, hence the word $a^{k_1} b a^{k_2} b a^{\omega}$ is accepted, yet it does **not** lie in $L$ (it has $b$'s after the first $b$). **Contradiction.** $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mark-Loss Hot Spots**
> 1. Forgetting to declare $\delta$ as **total** (DBA requires every (state, symbol) to have a transition). -1 mark.
> 2. Confusing "visited once" with "visited infinitely often" in the Büchi condition. -2 marks.
> 3. For 14-mark questions, omitting the **proof of equivalence** when asked to convert NBA → DBA. -3 marks.
> 4. Using the DFA-style "final state" condition instead of Büchi. -3 marks.
> 5. Claiming "all $\omega$-regular languages are DBA-recognizable" — the expressivity gap is a **favorite examiner trap**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- A **DBA** is a DFA over infinite words with Büchi acceptance.
- Components: $Q, \Sigma, \delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ (total!), $q_0, F$.
- **Acceptance:** $\inf(\rho) \cap F \neq \varnothing$ — visit $F$ **infinitely often**.
- **Expressivity:** strictly weaker than NBA; Safra's construction gives $2^{n} \cdot n!$ blowup.
- **Emptiness check:** $\mathcal{O}(|Q|)$ via SCCs — fast, deterministic.
- **Complementation:** trivial swap $F \leftrightarrow Q \setminus F$ (unlike NBA).
- **Counter-example:** $L = (a+b)^{*}a^{\omega} \cup b^{\omega}$ is NBA-only.
- **Engineering use:** LTL model checking, fair-termination verification, hardware liveness proofs.
- **Pitfalls:** total transition function, infinite vs. finite visits, DBA $\neq$ NBA power.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalised Büchi Automata (Definitions only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generalised Büchi Automata (Definitions only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Generalised Büchi Automaton (GBA)

A **Generalised Büchi Automaton (GBA)** is a non-deterministic finite automaton over infinite words whose acceptance condition requires that the automaton visits **multiple (a finite set of) accepting states infinitely often**, rather than just a single set of accepting states as in a classical Büchi automaton.

Formally, a GBA is defined as a 5-tuple:

$$
\mathcal{A} = (\Sigma,\ Q,\ Q_0,\ \delta,\ \mathcal{F})
$$

where:

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\Sigma$ | A finite, non-empty alphabet of input symbols |
| $Q$ | A finite, non-empty set of states |
| $Q_0 \subseteq Q$ | The set of initial states |
| $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ | The non-deterministic transition relation |
| $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k\}$ | A **finite non-empty family of accepting sets**, with each $F_i \subseteq Q$ |

The accepting condition is:

> A run $\rho = q_0 q_1 q_2 \ldots$ over an infinite word $w = a_0 a_1 a_2 \ldots \in \Sigma^{\omega}$ is **accepting** if and only if for **every** set $F_i \in \mathcal{F}$, the run visits $F_i$ **infinitely often**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Definitions Only)**
> For Module 2 of PECNT526, students are expected to know only the **formal tuple definition**, the **acceptance condition**, and the **relationship with classical Büchi Automata**. No construction or conversion algorithms are required at this stage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **quality inspector in a factory** with **multiple checkpoints** (say 3 checkpoints: A, B, C) along a conveyor belt carrying an infinite stream of products.

- A **classical Büchi automaton** says: "Every product that comes by must **pass through checkpoint A infinitely often**."
- A **Generalised Büchi automaton** says: "Every product that comes by must **pass through checkpoint A infinitely often AND checkpoint B infinitely often AND checkpoint C infinitely often**."

Each checkpoint corresponds to one accepting set $F_i \subseteq Q$. The product (word) is accepted only if it keeps coming back to **all** checkpoints, again and again, without end.

In simpler terms:

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a GBA as a Büchi automaton that has been given **a "to-do list" of multiple repeating tasks**, instead of just one. To be accepted, the word must complete **every task on the list infinitely often**, not just some of them.

This generalisation is critical in **model checking of Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)** because LTL formulas often contain multiple fairness constraints, and a single Büchi condition is not expressive enough — but a GBA can encode all of them natively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Tuple — Step-by-Step Meaning

- **Alphabet ($\Sigma$)**: The vocabulary of the input. For hardware verification, $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$ where $AP$ is the set of atomic propositions.
- **States ($Q$)**: Nodes of the automaton. Each state encodes a piece of memory about "what has been seen so far" along the run.
- **Initial States ($Q_0$)**: The starting configuration. Non-determinism allows multiple entry points.
- **Transition Relation ($\delta$)**: For each state $q$ and each input symbol $a \in \Sigma$, $\delta(q,a)$ gives the set of possible next states. Non-determinism means $| \delta(q,a) | \geq 0$.
- **Acceptance Family ($\mathcal{F}$)**: This is the **distinguishing feature** of a GBA. Instead of one subset $F \subseteq Q$, there is a **set of subsets** $\{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k\}$.

## 2.2 The Acceptance Condition — Unpacked

Let $\rho = q_0, q_1, q_2, \ldots$ be an infinite run over word $w \in \Sigma^{\omega}$. Define:
$$
\inf(\rho) = \{ q \in Q \mid q \text{ appears infinitely often in } \rho \}
$$

Then $\rho$ is **accepting** iff:

$$
\forall F_i \in \mathcal{F} \;:\; \inf(\rho) \cap F_i \neq \emptyset
$$

Equivalently: every set $F_i$ has at least one state that appears **infinitely often** in the run.

## 2.3 Why "Generalised"?

- A **classical Büchi Automaton (BA)** has $\mathcal{F} = \{ F \}$ — a singleton family with **one** accepting set.
- A **Generalised Büchi Automaton (GBA)** has $\mathcal{F} = \{ F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k \}$ with $k \geq 1$ accepting sets.
- Therefore: **every BA is a GBA** (with $k=1$), but not every GBA is a BA.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Description |
|---|---|
| $\mathcal{A} = (\Sigma, Q, Q_0, \delta, \mathcal{F})$ | Formal 5-tuple of a GBA |
| $\Sigma$ | Finite input alphabet |
| $Q$ | Finite set of states |
| $Q_0 \subseteq Q$ | Initial states |
| $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ | Transition function |
| $\mathcal{F} = \{ F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k \}$ | Family of accepting sets |
| $\rho = q_0 q_1 q_2 \ldots$ | An infinite run |
| $\inf(\rho)$ | States visited infinitely often in $\rho$ |
| $\forall F_i \in \mathcal{F} \;:\; \inf(\rho) \cap F_i \neq \emptyset$ | GBA acceptance condition |
| $L(\mathcal{A})$ | Language accepted by $\mathcal{A}$ |
| $L_{\omega}(\mathcal{A}) \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$ | $\omega$-regular language of infinite words |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

GBAs are the **canonical automaton model used in LTL model checking**:

- Every LTL formula $\varphi$ can be translated into a GBA $\mathcal{A}_{\varphi}$ of size $2^{O(\vert \varphi \vert)}$.
- The product $\mathcal{M} \times \mathcal{A}_{\varphi}$ is then checked for emptiness to decide whether the system $\mathcal{M}$ satisfies $\varphi$.
- Industrial model checkers (SPIN, NuSMV, VIS) exploit GBAs because they preserve the structure of the LTL formula more compactly than classical BAs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comparison Matrix: Büchi vs. Generalised Büchi Automaton

| Property | Büchi Automaton (BA) | Generalised Büchi Automaton (GBA) |
|---|---|---|
| Tuple | $(\Sigma, Q, Q_0, \delta, F)$ | $(\Sigma, Q, Q_0, \delta, \mathcal{F})$ |
| Accepting component | Single set $F \subseteq Q$ | Family $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, \ldots, F_k\}$, $k \geq 1$ |
| Acceptance rule | $\inf(\rho) \cap F \neq \emptyset$ | $\forall F_i \in \mathcal{F} : \inf(\rho) \cap F_i \neq \emptyset$ |
| Strength of condition | Weaker (one obligation) | Stronger (multiple obligations) |
| Expressive power over $\Sigma^{\omega}$ | Same as GBA (they are equivalent) | Same as BA |
| Compactness for LTL | Less compact (after translation) | More compact (preserves formula structure) |
| Used in | Theoretical proofs, automata theory | LTL model checking in practice |

## 3.2 Worked Logical Walkthrough

**Given:** A GBA $\mathcal{A} = (\Sigma, Q, Q_0, \delta, \mathcal{F})$ where $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, F_2\}$.

**Question:** When is a run $\rho$ accepted?

**Step 1.** Identify all states that appear in $\rho$ infinitely often. Call this set $\inf(\rho)$.

**Step 2.** Check the intersection with $F_1$:

$$
\inf(\rho) \cap F_1 \stackrel{?}{\neq} \emptyset
$$

If empty → reject.

**Step 3.** Check the intersection with $F_2$:

$$
\inf(\rho) \cap F_2 \stackrel{?}{\neq} \emptyset
$$

If empty → reject.

**Step 4.** Only if **both** checks succeed, the run is **accepting**.

**Step 5.** A word $w \in \Sigma^{\omega}$ is in $L_{\omega}(\mathcal{A})$ if **at least one** accepting run $\rho$ over $w$ exists.

## 3.3 Formal Property: Closure Under Union

GBAs are **closed under union**. Given two GBAs $\mathcal{A}_1$ and $\mathcal{A}_2$:

$$
L_{\omega}(\mathcal{A}_1) \cup L_{\omega}(\mathcal{A}_2) = L_{\omega}(\mathcal{A}_1 \oplus \mathcal{A}_2)
$$

where $\oplus$ denotes the standard product construction for automata. This property does **not hold** for intersection directly — intersection of GBAs is generally expressible but requires non-trivial construction.

## 3.4 Equivalence with Büchi Automata

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem (without proof, definitions-only module):**
> For every GBA $\mathcal{A}_G$, there exists a Büchi automaton $\mathcal{A}_B$ such that $L_{\omega}(\mathcal{A}_G) = L_{\omega}(\mathcal{A}_B)$.

This means GBA is **not more expressive** than BA, but it is **more convenient** as an intermediate representation when translating LTL formulas.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generalised Büchi Automaton — Functional Architecture

The diagram below illustrates a GBA with two accepting sets $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, F_2\}$. The blue states belong to $F_1$, the orange states belong to $F_2$. A valid accepting run must visit **both** sets infinitely often.

```mermaid
graph LR
    q0["q0 initial"] -->|a| q1["q1 in F1"]
    q1 -->|b| q2["q2 normal"]
    q2 -->|c| q3["q3 in F2"]
    q3 -->|d| q1
    q2 -->|e| q2
    q3 -->|f| q3

    style q1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style q3 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000000
```

**Interpretation:** Any accepted word must drive the automaton such that states $q_1 \in F_1$ and $q_3 \in F_2$ are revisited infinitely often — i.e., the cycle $\rho$ must contain both infinitely often.

## 4.2 Component-Level Architecture of GBA Definition

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Sigma: Alphabet"] --> B["Q: State Set"]
    C["Q0: Initial States"] --> B
    B --> D["delta: Transition Function"]
    D --> E["Q x Sigma -> 2 exp Q"]
    B --> F["Family F = F1, F2, ..., Fk"]
    F1["F1 subset Q"] --> F
    F2["F2 subset Q"] --> F
    F3["Fk subset Q"] --> F
    F --> G["Acceptance: inf rho intersect Fi non-empty for all i"]

    style G fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Comparison Flow: BA vs GBA Acceptance Check

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Infinite Run rho"] --> I["Compute inf rho"]
    I --> J{"Check each F_i in F"}
    J -->|All Fi visited infinitely often| K["ACCEPT rho"]
    J -->|Some Fi visited finitely often| L["REJECT rho"]
    K --> M["Word w is in L omega of A"]
    L --> N["Word w is NOT in L omega of A"]

    style K fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style L fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
    style M fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000000
    style N fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**Define a Generalised Büchi Automaton. State the acceptance condition clearly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Generalised Büchi Automaton is a 5-tuple
$$
\mathcal{A} = (\Sigma,\ Q,\ Q_0,\ \delta,\ \mathcal{F})
$$
where $\Sigma$ is a finite alphabet, $Q$ is a finite set of states, $Q_0 \subseteq Q$ is the set of initial states, $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ is the transition function, and $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k\}$ is a finite family of accepting subsets of $Q$. An infinite run $\rho$ is accepting if and only if for every $F_i \in \mathcal{F}$, $\inf(\rho) \cap F_i \neq \emptyset$, i.e., each accepting set is visited infinitely often by the run. (3 marks)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**How does a Generalised Büchi Automaton differ from a classical Büchi Automaton?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A classical Büchi Automaton has a **single** accepting set $F \subseteq Q$, and an infinite run is accepted if it visits some state in $F$ infinitely often. In contrast, a Generalised Büchi Automaton has a **family** of accepting sets $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, \ldots, F_k\}$ (with $k \geq 1$), and acceptance requires the run to visit **every** $F_i$ infinitely often. The expressive power of both models over $\Sigma^{\omega}$ is the same, but GBA preserves the modular structure of LTL formulas more naturally during translation. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question]`

**(a)** Define a Generalised Büchi Automaton (GBA) formally. List all components of its tuple with one-line explanations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain the GBA acceptance condition. How does it differ from the acceptance condition of a classical Büchi Automaton? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Formal Definition (7 marks):**

A Generalised Büchi Automaton over an infinite alphabet is a 5-tuple
$$
\mathcal{A} = (\Sigma,\ Q,\ Q_0,\ \delta,\ \mathcal{F})
$$

Components:

- $\Sigma$: a finite, non-empty alphabet.
- $Q$: a finite, non-empty set of states.
- $Q_0 \subseteq Q$: the set of initial states, where the run begins.
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$: the non-deterministic transition relation, giving the set of possible successors for each state-input pair.
- $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k\}$: a finite non-empty family of subsets of $Q$, each called an *accepting set*.

**(b) Acceptance Condition (7 marks):**

Let $\rho = q_0 q_1 q_2 \ldots$ be an infinite run of $\mathcal{A}$ over an infinite word $w \in \Sigma^{\omega}$. Define
$$
\inf(\rho) = \{ q \in Q \mid q \text{ occurs infinitely often in } \rho \}.
$$

The run $\rho$ is **accepting** iff for every $F_i \in \mathcal{F}$:
$$
\inf(\rho) \cap F_i \neq \emptyset.
$$

A word $w$ is accepted by $\mathcal{A}$ if **some** run over $w$ is accepting. The accepted language is $L_{\omega}(\mathcal{A}) \subseteq \Sigma^{\omega}$.

**Difference from BA:** For a classical BA, $|\mathcal{F}| = 1$; for a GBA, $|\mathcal{F}| \geq 1$ and **all** sets must be visited infinitely often. Hence GBA imposes a stronger conjunction of obligations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Question – Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Define the components of a Generalised Büchi Automaton formally. Explain why the component $\mathcal{F}$ is called a "family" of accepting sets rather than just a single set. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the acceptance condition of a GBA using $\inf(\rho)$. Explain with a small example (in words) how a word would fail to be accepted when even one accepting set $F_i$ is visited only finitely often. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Tuple & Meaning of "Family" (7 marks):**

A GBA is
$$
\mathcal{A} = (\Sigma,\ Q,\ Q_0,\ \delta,\ \mathcal{F})
$$
with components as defined above. The collection $\mathcal{F}$ is called a *family* because it is a **set of sets** — specifically,
$$
\mathcal{F} \subseteq 2^{Q} \quad \text{and} \quad \mathcal{F} = \{ F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k \}.
$$

It is not a single set because acceptance in a GBA requires the run to satisfy **multiple, simultaneous recurrence obligations** — one per $F_i$. A single set cannot capture more than one independent obligation, so we need a family (i.e., a set whose elements are themselves sets of states). When $k=1$, the family reduces to a single set and $\mathcal{A}$ becomes a classical Büchi Automaton.

**(b) Acceptance via $\inf(\rho)$ and Failure Example (7 marks):**

For a run $\rho$, the acceptance condition is
$$
\forall F_i \in \mathcal{F} \;:\; \inf(\rho) \cap F_i \neq \emptyset.
$$

**Failure Example:** Suppose $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, F_2\}$ with $F_1 = \{q_a\}$, $F_2 = \{q_b\}$. Consider a run $\rho$ that visits $q_a$ infinitely often but visits $q_b$ only twice and then never again. Then $\inf(\rho) \cap F_1 \neq \emptyset$ (good) but $\inf(\rho) \cap F_2 = \emptyset$ (fails). Since the universal quantifier requires **all** $F_i$ to be satisfied, the run is **rejected**. This illustrates the conjunctive nature of GBA acceptance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT** write a single accepting set $F$ in the GBA tuple. Examiners specifically look for the family notation $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, \ldots, F_k\}$. Writing $F$ instead of $\mathcal{F}$ is a 2-mark deduction.
> - **Do NOT** confuse "all $F_i$ visited at least once" with "visited infinitely often." The acceptance condition is **infinitely often**, not "at least once." This is the most common conceptual error in Module 2 answers.
> - **Do NOT** state that GBA is strictly more expressive than BA — they accept **the same class of $\omega$-regular languages**, but GBAs are structurally more convenient.
> - **Do NOT** omit the universal quantifier in the acceptance condition. The condition is $\forall F_i \in \mathcal{F}$, not $\exists F_i \in \mathcal{F}$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Generalised Büchi Automaton (GBA)** is a 5-tuple $(\Sigma, Q, Q_0, \delta, \mathcal{F})$.
- $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k\}$ is a **family (set of sets)** of accepting subsets of $Q$.
- Acceptance requires **every** $F_i \in \mathcal{F}$ to be visited **infinitely often**.
- Equivalent compact form: $\forall F_i \in \mathcal{F} \;:\; \inf(\rho) \cap F_i \neq \emptyset$.
- Every Büchi Automaton is a GBA with $k = 1$, but not vice versa in *form* — though both accept identical $\omega$-regular languages.
- GBAs are the **standard intermediate representation for LTL formulas** in model checking.
- $\inf(\rho)$ denotes the set of states appearing **infinitely often** in the run $\rho$.
- A word is accepted iff **at least one** run over it is accepting (existential over runs, universal over $F_i$).
- GBA acceptance is **conjunctive** across the family — weakest link rejects the entire run.
- Industrial relevance: SPIN, NuSMV, and other model checkers internally represent LTL properties as GBAs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Checking ω-Regular Properties - Persistence Properties and Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Model Checking ω-Regular Properties - Persistence Properties and Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Persistence Properties — Formal Definition

In the context of Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) and ω-regular model checking, a **persistence property** is a special class of liveness property whose canonical syntactic form is:

$$\varphi \;\equiv\; \mathbf{F}\,\mathbf{G}\, p \quad \text{(read as: "eventually globally } p\text{")}$$

where $p \in AP$ is an atomic proposition drawn from the set $AP$ of atomic propositions over which the Kripke structure is labelled. A run $\pi = s_0\, s_1\, s_2\, \dots$ of a Kripke structure $M$ satisfies $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$, written $\pi \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$, if and only if:

$$\exists\, i \;\geq\; 0 \;:\; \forall\, j \;\geq\; i \;:\; p \in L(\pi[j])$$

That is, **there exists a finite prefix after which $p$ is true at every step on the remaining infinite suffix of the run**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Persistence properties form one of the three canonical subclasses of ω-regular properties studied in Module 2, the other two being **safety** ($\mathbf{G}\,p$) and **recurrence** ($\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,p$). Together, these exhaust the canonical forms of LTL formulas used as benchmarks in industrial model checkers such as **SPIN**, **NuSMV**, and **CADP**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Duality Note:** Persistence and recurrence are duals of one another under negation:
> $$\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \;\equiv\; \neg\,\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$$
> This duality is the key algebraic identity that enables every persistence property to be checked via a *recurrence monitor* for the negated proposition.

## 1.2 The Persistence-Property Acceptance Game — Intuition

Picture a **traffic-light controller** for a railway crossing. The property *"eventually the gate stays closed forever after a train has fully passed"* is a persistence property:

- **Until** some instant $i$, the gate may be opening, closing, or anything (transient behaviour).
- **From instant $i$ onwards**, the gate is *guaranteed* to remain closed.

The "eventually" quantifier absorbs the finite warm-up of the controller, and the "globally" quantifier certifies the steady-state lock-in. This is exactly the shape of $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Light Switch):** A persistence property is like a light switch that is allowed to flicker for a while, but **must eventually settle in the ON position and stay there forever**. In contrast:
> - A *recurrence* property $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,p$ would be a switch that **flickers ON infinitely often** but is allowed to turn OFF in between.
> - A *safety* property $\mathbf{G}\,p$ would be a switch that **must be ON from the very first flip** with no exceptions.

## 1.3 The Product Construction — Why It Is Needed

A Kripke structure $M$ is a *finite-state* object with branching semantics, while an LTL formula $\varphi$ is a *linear-time* specification over infinite runs. The **product construction** bridges these two worlds by:

1. Translating $\varphi$ into a Büchi (or generalised Büchi / Streett) automaton $\mathcal{A}_{\neg\varphi}$.
2. Synchronising the Kripke structure with the automaton state-by-state, producing a new graph $M \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg\varphi}$ whose accepting cycles correspond exactly to behaviours of $M$ that *violate* $\varphi$.

The fundamental decision problem then reduces to **emptiness of an ω-automaton**, decidable in time $O(|M| \cdot |\mathcal{A}_{\neg\varphi}|)$ for Büchi automata.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Complexity Metric:** For a system with $n$ states and a Büchi automaton with $m$ states, the product $M \times \mathcal{A}$ has at most $n \cdot m$ states. Nested DFS emptiness runs in time $O(n \cdot m)$. This **polynomial complexity** is what makes ω-regular model checking tractable for finite-state systems — a central selling point emphasised in the KTU 2024 PECNT526 syllabus.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Syntactic Normal Form of a Persistence Property

A **persistence property** is canonically a *single* $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ formula or a Boolean combination thereof. The most general form used in industrial tools is:

$$\Phi \;=\; \bigwedge_{i=1}^{k}\; \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\, p_i$$

meaning *"eventually, all the $p_i$ hold forever and simultaneously"*. This is the **generalised persistence** form, and it is accepted by **Streett automata with $k$ Streett pairs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Boolean Normal Form Matters:** When model checking, the negation of a generalised persistence property becomes:
> $$\neg \Phi \;\equiv\; \bigvee_{i=1}^{k}\; \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\, \neg p_i$$
> The disjunction of $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\, \neg p_i$ is a *recurrence* (fairness) property — perfect for monitor-based verification.

## 2.2 From $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ to a Büchi Automaton

The atomic persistence $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ is recognised by a **2-state Büchi automaton** $\mathcal{A}_{\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p}$:

| Component | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| States | $Q$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ |
| Initial states | $Q_0$ | $\{q_0\}$ |
| Alphabet | $\Sigma$ | $2^{AP}$ |
| Transition relation | $\delta$ | $\delta(q_0, \sigma) = q_1$ if $p \in \sigma$, else $q_0$ ; $\delta(q_1, \sigma) = q_1$ |
| Accepting set | $F$ | $\{q_1\}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Automaton:** State $q_0$ is the "still searching" state. State $q_1$ is the "**have found a point where $p$ is true and must keep being true**" state. Büchi acceptance requires $q_1$ to be visited **infinitely often** — and because $q_1$ is a *sink* (self-loop on $p$), once the run reaches $q_1$ and $p$ continues to hold, the run is accepted.

For verification we use the complement **monitor** for $\neg \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \equiv \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$, a 2-state Büchi automaton $\mathcal{A}_{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p}$:

| Component | Definition |
|---|---|
| States | $\{q_0', q_1'\}$ |
| Initial | $\{q_0'\}$ |
| $\delta$ | $q_0' \xrightarrow{\neg p} q_1'$; $q_0' \xrightarrow{p} q_0'$; $q_1' \xrightarrow{\neg p} q_1'$; $q_1' \xrightarrow{p} q_0'$ |
| Accepting $F$ | $\{q_1'\}$ |

This is the **standard counter-example generator** in SPIN's `never` claim construction.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ | Canonical persistence property | "Stability" specs (e.g., deadlock-free steady state) |
| 2 | $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \;\Leftrightarrow\; \neg \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ | Logical duality | Automata-based model reduction |
| 3 | $\pi \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \;\Leftrightarrow\; \exists i \, \forall j \geq i : p \in L(\pi[j])$ | Satisfaction on a path | Run-based semantics |
| 4 | $M \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \;\Leftrightarrow\; \forall$ paths from every reachable state, $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ holds | Universal satisfaction | "All executions" spec (Kripke semantics) |
| 5 | $\lvert S \times Q \rvert \;=\; \lvert S \rvert \cdot \lvert Q \rvert$ | Product state-space size | Worst-case memory bound |
| 6 | $\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{A}) = \emptyset \;\Leftrightarrow\;$ no accepting cycle in $M \times \mathcal{A}$ | Emptiness test for counter-examples | Decision procedure |
| 7 | $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{k} \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p_i$ | Generalised persistence | Streett acceptance |
| 8 | Time complexity $O(\lvert S \rvert \cdot \lvert Q \rvert)$ | Nested-DFS emptiness | Industrial-tool benchmark |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary / Edge Case:** If $p$ is satisfied at $s_0$ (the initial state) of every run, then $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ collapses to the *safety* property $\mathbf{G}\,p$. Conversely, if no $p$ ever appears, the formula is unsatisfiable on that structure. Always check the *feasibility* of $p$ in the Kripke structure first.

## 2.4 Why Persistence Is Harder Than Safety — and Easier Than Recurrence

- **Safety** $\mathbf{G}\,p$: a *bad prefix* is finite. Trivial — search for one violating state.
- **Persistence** $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$: requires proving that *every infinite run* has a *stable suffix*. Needs cycle analysis.
- **Recurrence** $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,p$: needs *infinite* witnesses of $p$. Needs **fairness** (Streett) analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Persistence properties are pervasive in:
> - **Embedded controllers** (mode-locking, "controller enters LOCKED state and stays").
> - **Cache-coherence protocols** (MESI: "every line eventually settles in SHARED or EXCLUSIVE").
> - **Distributed consensus** ("eventually the cluster converges to a single leader").
> - **Safety-critical shutdown sequences** ("after the abort signal, the thrusters are disabled forever").

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Büchi Automaton for $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$

We construct the *monitor* $\mathcal{A}_{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p}$ explicitly because it is the automaton whose **accepting runs are exactly the counter-examples** to $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$.

**Goal:** $\mathcal{A}$ accepts an infinite word $\sigma = \sigma_0 \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \cdots \in (2^{AP})^{\omega}$ iff $\sigma \models \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$.

**Step 1.** Identify the **two logical phases** of a run satisfying $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$:

- "No $\neg p$ has been observed yet" — let this be state $q_0'$.
- "$\neg p$ has been observed, await the next one" — let this be state $q_1'$.

**Step 2.** Define $\delta$ such that $q_1'$ is entered precisely on a $\neg p$ letter, and is exited precisely on a $p$ letter:

$$\delta(q_0', \sigma) = \begin{cases} q_1' & \text{if } \neg p \in \sigma \\ q_0' & \text{if } p \in \sigma \end{cases}$$

$$\delta(q_1', \sigma) = \begin{cases} q_1' & \text{if } \neg p \in \sigma \\ q_0' & \text{if } p \in \sigma \end{cases}$$

**Step 3.** Set the accepting set $F = \{q_1'\}$, so Büchi acceptance demands infinitely many visits to $q_1'$ — i.e. $\neg p$ must occur infinitely often.

**Step 4.** Verify by induction on run length that an accepting run exists iff $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ holds:

$$\text{Run visits } q_1' \text{ at step } i \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \neg p \in \sigma_i$$
$$\text{Büchi acceptance} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; q_1' \text{ visited i.o.} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p \text{ holds on } \sigma$$

**Conclusion:** $\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{A}_{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p}) = \{\sigma \in (2^{AP})^{\omega} \mid \sigma \models \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p\}$.

## 3.2 The Product Automaton — Full Definition

Given a Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ over $AP$, and a Büchi automaton $\mathcal{A}_{\neg\varphi} = (Q, Q_0, \Sigma, \delta, F)$, the **synchronised product** is:

$$M \otimes \mathcal{A}_{\neg\varphi} \;=\; (S \times Q,\; S_0 \times Q_0,\; R_{\otimes},\; L_{\otimes},\; F_{\otimes})$$

with:

$$((s, q), (s', q')) \in R_{\otimes} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; (s, s') \in R \;\wedge\; q' \in \delta(q, L(s'))$$

$$L_{\otimes}((s, q)) = L(s)$$

$$F_{\otimes} \;=\; S \times F$$

The key theorem:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Product Emptiness):**
> $$M \models \varphi \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \mathcal{L}(M \otimes \mathcal{A}_{\neg\varphi}) = \emptyset \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{no accepting cycle in } M \otimes \mathcal{A}_{\neg\varphi}$$

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Model Checking $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$

Consider the Kripke structure $M$ with $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$, $R$ as drawn in the diagram of Section 4, and $L(s_0) = L(s_1) = \{q\}$ (i.e. $p$ is *false*), $L(s_2) = L(s_3) = \{p\}$.

**Step 1.** Build the monitor for $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$: $\mathcal{A} = (\{q_0', q_1'\}, \{q_0'\}, \delta, \{q_1'\})$ as derived above.

**Step 2.** Build the product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$ — state space $S \times Q$ has $4 \times 2 = 8$ states.

**Step 3.** Compute reachable product states from $S_0 \times Q_0 = \{(s_0, q_0')\}$:

| Product state | $L_{\otimes}$ | In $F_{\otimes}$? |
|---|---|---|
| $(s_0, q_0')$ | $\{q\}$ | No (state $q_0' \notin F$) |
| $(s_1, q_0')$ | $\{q\}$ | No |
| $(s_1, q_1')$ | $\{q\}$ | **Yes** |
| $(s_2, q_1')$ | $\{p\}$ | **Yes** |
| $(s_2, q_0')$ | $\{p\}$ | No |
| $(s_3, q_1')$ | $\{p\}$ | **Yes** |
| $(s_3, q_0')$ | $\{p\}$ | No |

**Step 4.** Search for an **accepting cycle** — i.e. an SCC containing at least one state from $F_{\otimes} = S \times \{q_1'\}$.

Suppose $R$ contains a cycle $s_2 \to s_3 \to s_2$. In the product, this lifts to a cycle $(s_2, q_1') \to (s_3, q_1') \to (s_2, q_1')$, and every state in this cycle is in $F_{\otimes}$. Therefore the product contains an **accepting cycle**.

**Step 5.** Decision:

$$\exists \text{ accepting cycle in } M \otimes \mathcal{A} \;\Longrightarrow\; M \;\not\models\; \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfall:** The *single* existence of an accepting state in the product is *not* enough to declare a counter-example. The accepting state must lie on a **cycle**, otherwise the run is finite and Büchi acceptance is not met. Examiners specifically deduct 2 marks for confusing "reachable accepting state" with "accepting cycle".

## 3.4 Set-Theoretic Characterisation of States Satisfying $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$

For completeness, we derive the set of states that *do* satisfy $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ via fixed-point computation. Define:

$$E \;=\; \{ s \in S \mid s \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \}$$

$E$ can be computed as the greatest fixed point of the following monotone operator on subsets of $S$:

$$\tau(X) \;=\; \{ s \in S \mid p \in L(s) \;\wedge\; \forall s' \in R(s) : s' \in X \}$$

In words: $s$ is in $\tau(X)$ iff $p$ holds at $s$ **and** every successor of $s$ is again in $X$. Iterating $\tau$ from the top of the lattice yields $E$ in $O(|S|^2)$ time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connection:** This greatest fixed-point corresponds to the *stable set* of a Petri net, and the algorithm is identical to the one used in the **TAPAAL** and **ITS-tools** model checkers for Petri-net reachability.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Kripke Structure $M$ (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

The diagram below shows a small Kripke structure $M$ with **four states** $s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3$. States shaded green satisfy the proposition $p$ (here $p \in L$); states shaded red do not.

```mermaid
graph LR
    s0[STATE s0: not p]:::redState
    s1[STATE s1: not p]:::redState
    s2[STATE s2: p]:::greenState
    s3[STATE s3: p]:::greenState

    s0 -- R --> s1
    s1 -- R --> s2
    s2 -- R --> s3
    s3 -- R --> s2
    s1 -- R --> s1

    classDef redState fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef greenState fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Büchi Monitor $\mathcal{A}_{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p}$

The following 2-state automaton is the **counter-example monitor**. State $q_1'$ is the *accepting* state (drawn with double circle).

```mermaid
graph LR
    q0p[STATE q0 prime: hunting]:::normal
    q1p[STATE q1 prime: accepting]:::accepting
    q0p -- label p --> q0p
    q0p -- label not p --> q1p
    q1p -- label p --> q0p
    q1p -- label not p --> q1p

    classDef normal fill:#e8e8ff,stroke:#2c3e50,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef accepting fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Synchronised Product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p}$

The product combines the two graphs above into a single 8-state graph. Nodes whose automaton-component is $q_1'$ are highlighted as **accepting** (yellow). Note the cycle at the bottom — it is the **accepting cycle** responsible for the counter-example.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[s0 q0 prime: not p]:::redState
    A1[s1 q0 prime: not p]:::redState
    B1[s1 q1 prime: not p ACCEPT]:::accepting
    C0[s2 q0 prime: p]:::greenState
    C1[s2 q1 prime: p ACCEPT]:::acceptingGreen
    D0[s3 q0 prime: p]:::greenState
    D1[s3 q1 prime: p ACCEPT]:::acceptingGreen
    s1self[s1 q0 prime self loop: not p]:::redState

    A0 -- R delta --> A1
    A1 -- R delta --> s1self
    A1 -- R delta --> B1
    s1self -- R delta --> s1self
    s1self -- R delta --> B1
    B1 -- R delta --> C0
    B1 -- R delta --> C1
    C0 -- R delta --> C1
    C0 -- R delta --> D0
    C1 -- R delta --> D0
    C1 -- R delta --> C1
    D0 -- R delta --> C0
    D0 -- R delta --> D1
    D1 -- R delta --> C0
    D1 -- R delta --> C1

    classDef redState fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef greenState fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef accepting fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef acceptingGreen fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Algorithm Block Diagram — Persistence Model Checking Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    INPUT[INPUT Kripke M and LTL F G p]:::io
    NEG[STEP 1 Negate: F G p becomes G F not p]:::process
    AUTO[STEP 2 Translate G F not p to Buchi automaton A]:::process
    PROD[STEP 3 Build synchronised product M tensor A]:::process
    REACH[STEP 4 Compute reachable product states]:::process
    SCC[STEP 5 Find SCCs and detect accepting cycle]:::process
    DECIDE[STEP 6 Decision: accepting cycle exists implies M does not satisfy F G p]:::decision
    OUT_SAT[OUTPUT M satisfies F G p]:::io
    OUT_VIO[OUTPUT Counter example trace returned]:::io

    INPUT --> NEG --> AUTO --> PROD --> REACH --> SCC --> DECIDE
    DECIDE -- YES --> OUT_VIO
    DECIDE -- NO --> OUT_SAT

    classDef io fill:#d6e8ff,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#2c3e50,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Synchronised Product Works:** The product enforces a *lock-step* execution: at every step, $M$ chooses a successor $s'$ and the Büchi automaton $\mathcal{A}$ simultaneously reads $L(s')$ and updates its own state. Accepting cycles in the product are exactly the infinite runs of $M$ that drive $\mathcal{A}$ to its accepting state infinitely often — i.e. counter-examples to $\varphi$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Exam 2024]**
*Define a persistence property in LTL. Give one real-world example from an embedded-control domain.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A *persistence property* is an LTL formula of the canonical form $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ (or a Boolean combination thereof), read *"eventually $p$ holds forever"*. Formally, a run $\pi$ satisfies $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ iff $\exists i \geq 0$ such that $\forall j \geq i$, $p \in L(\pi[j])$.
**Example:** In a railway-crossing controller, the requirement *"after the train has fully cleared, the gate remains closed forever"* is a persistence property with $p \equiv \text{gate\_closed}$.
*(1 mark each for definition, formal satisfaction, and example.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State and justify the logical duality between persistence and recurrence. Why is this duality important for model checking?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Duality:** $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \equiv \neg\, \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ — proved by De Morgan-style reasoning on the path quantifiers.
**Importance:** To check whether $M \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$, it suffices to check whether the *monitor* for $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ has an accepting run in the product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p}$. This converts a *universal* "all paths" persistence question into an *existential* "is there a counter-example?" recurrence question — much easier algorithmically (1.5 marks for duality statement, 1.5 marks for the algorithmic significance).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Construct a Büchi automaton $\mathcal{A}$ over $AP = \{p\}$ that recognises exactly the language $\{\sigma \in (2^{AP})^{\omega} \mid \sigma \models \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p\}$. Tabulate the transition function and clearly identify the accepting state(s).

**(b) (7 Marks)** Given the Kripke structure $M$ with states $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$, $L(s_0) = L(s_1) = \emptyset$, $L(s_2) = \{p\}$, and $R = \{(s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_1), (s_1, s_2), (s_2, s_2)\}$, determine whether $M \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$. Use the product construction with $\mathcal{A}$ from part (a) and report whether an accepting cycle exists.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Büchi Automaton Construction (7 Marks):**

The Büchi automaton is $\mathcal{A} = (Q, Q_0, \Sigma, \delta, F)$ with:

$$Q = \{q_0, q_1\}, \quad Q_0 = \{q_0\}, \quad \Sigma = 2^{\{p\}}, \quad F = \{q_1\}$$

| Current state | Letter | Next state |
|---|---|---|
| $q_0$ | $\{p\}$ (i.e. $p$ true) | $q_0$ |
| $q_0$ | $\emptyset$ (i.e. $\neg p$ true) | $q_1$ |
| $q_1$ | $\{p\}$ | $q_0$ |
| $q_1$ | $\emptyset$ | $q_1$ |

*(2 marks for state/accepting set definition, 3 marks for the transition table, 2 marks for justifying that this accepts exactly $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$.)*

**Part (b) — Product Construction & Decision (7 Marks):**

**Step 1.** Build the product $M \otimes \mathcal{A}$. State space: $S \times Q$ with $3 \times 2 = 6$ states.

**Step 2.** Enumerate reachable product states from $\{(s_0, q_0)\}$:

| Product state | Letter at $L(s)$ | In $F_{\otimes}$? |
|---|---|---|
| $(s_0, q_0)$ | $\emptyset$ | No |
| $(s_1, q_0)$ | $\emptyset$ | No |
| $(s_1, q_1)$ | $\emptyset$ | **Yes** |
| $(s_2, q_1)$ | $\{p\}$ | **Yes** |
| $(s_2, q_0)$ | $\{p\}$ | No |

**Step 3.** List product transitions using $\delta$ and $R$:

$$(s_0, q_0) \to (s_1, q_0)$$

$$(s_1, q_0) \to (s_1, q_0) \text{ (via self-loop on } s_1 \text{, letter } \emptyset \Rightarrow q_0 \xrightarrow{\emptyset} q_0)$$

$$(s_1, q_0) \to (s_1, q_1) \text{ (via self-loop on } s_1 \text{, letter } \emptyset \Rightarrow q_0 \xrightarrow{\emptyset} q_1 \text{ allowed by } \delta \text{ non-determinism? NO)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Refinement of the monitor:** The standard construction for $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ is *deterministic* in some presentations and non-deterministic in others. The KTU 2024 scheme accepts either; the cleanest version has $q_0 \xrightarrow{\neg p} q_1$ and $q_1 \xrightarrow{p} q_0$ and $q_1 \xrightarrow{\neg p} q_1$, $q_0 \xrightarrow{p} q_0$. With that version:
>
> $(s_1, q_0) \to (s_1, q_1)$ via the $s_1$-self-loop because $\emptyset \models \neg p$, so $q_0 \to q_1$.
>
> Continuing: $(s_1, q_1) \to (s_2, q_0)$ because $\delta(q_1, \{p\}) = q_0$. And $(s_2, q_0) \to (s_2, q_0)$ because $q_0 \xrightarrow{\{p\}} q_0$.

**Step 4.** Find SCCs:
- $\{(s_2, q_0)\}$ — trivial SCC, but its automaton component is $q_0 \notin F$, so it is **not accepting**.
- $\{(s_1, q_0), (s_1, q_1)\}$ — contains $(s_1, q_1) \in F_{\otimes}$, so it is an **accepting SCC**.

**Step 5.** Decision: an accepting cycle exists $\Rightarrow$ the monitor accepts a run of $M$ $\Rightarrow$ there is a run satisfying $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ $\Rightarrow$ $M \not\models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$.

*(2 marks for product states list, 2 marks for cycle detection, 1 mark for accepting-cycle identification, 2 marks for the final decision.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With the help of the greatest fixed-point equation
$$E = \{s \in S \mid p \in L(s) \wedge \forall s' \in R(s) : s' \in E\},$$
prove that the set $E$ of states satisfying $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ can be computed by iterating the operator $\tau(X) = \{s \mid p \in L(s) \wedge R(s) \subseteq X\}$ from $X_0 = S$ down to the greatest fixed point.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Apply this fixed-point algorithm to the Kripke structure with $S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$, $R$ the complete graph on $\{s_2, s_3, s_4\}$, $R(s_1) = \{s_2\}$, and $L(s_1) = \emptyset$, $L(s_2) = L(s_3) = L(s_4) = \{p\}$. Show every iteration step explicitly. Does $M \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$?

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theoretical Proof (7 Marks):**

We show that $\tau$ is *monotone* on the lattice $(2^S, \subseteq)$: if $X \subseteq Y$ then $\tau(X) \subseteq \tau(Y)$, because the condition $R(s) \subseteq X$ becomes easier to satisfy when $X$ grows. By the Knaster–Tarski theorem, the greatest fixed point $\mathrm{gfp}(\tau)$ exists and equals $\bigcap \{ X \mid \tau(X) \subseteq X \}$.

To see that $\mathrm{gfp}(\tau) = \{s \mid M, s \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p\}$:

- ($\subseteq$) Suppose $s \in \mathrm{gfp}(\tau)$. By definition $p \in L(s)$ and $R(s) \subseteq \mathrm{gfp}(\tau)$. By induction on iteration index $k$ of $\tau^k(S)$, every successor of $s$ is in $\mathrm{gfp}(\tau)$, so $p$ holds along every path from $s$ once $s$ is reached — i.e. $M, s \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$.
- ($\supseteq$) Conversely, suppose $M, s \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$. Then $p$ holds at $s$ and every path from $s$ eventually stays in $\{s' \mid p \in L(s')\}$. So every successor of $s$ also satisfies $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$. Thus $s \in \tau(\{s' \mid M, s' \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p\})$. Since $\{s' \mid M, s' \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p\}$ is a fixed point of $\tau$, it contains $\mathrm{gfp}(\tau)$ by definition.

*(2 marks for monotonicity argument, 2 marks for greatest fixed-point existence, 3 marks for the two inclusion proofs.)*

**Part (b) — Algorithm Application (7 Marks):**

Iteration table, with $X_0 = S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$:

| Iter $k$ | $X_k$ | Computation |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\{s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$ | $X_0 = S$ |
| 1 | $\{s_2, s_3, s_4\}$ | Drop $s_1$: $p \notin L(s_1)$ |
| 2 | $\{s_2, s_3, s_4\}$ | All remaining states have $p$, all successors are in $X_1$. $\tau(X_1) = X_1$ — fixed point reached. |

Conclusion: $E = \{s_2, s_3, s_4\}$.

Since $s_1 \notin E$, the property $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ does **not** hold globally on $M$. However, from any state in $\{s_2, s_3, s_4\}$ the property is satisfied (it holds trivially, since $p$ is true at every state in the reachable sub-graph).

Therefore: $M \not\models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ at $s_1$, but $M, s_2 \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$, $M, s_3 \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$, $M, s_4 \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$.

*(1 mark per iteration row, 1 mark for fixed-point detection, 1 mark for the global $M$-satisfaction decision, 1 mark for per-state satisfaction.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Forgetting the "eventually" quantifier:** Many students write $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,p$ (recurrence) instead of $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ (persistence). These are **not equivalent** — $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,p$ allows infinitely many violations, but $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ forbids any violation after the stable point. *2-mark penalty if the formula is wrong.*
> 2. **Reachable accepting state vs. accepting cycle:** Reaching an $F_{\otimes}$ state once is *not* a counter-example. The run must be **infinite** and revisit $F_{\otimes}$ i.o. — i.e. lie on an accepting cycle. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for confusing these.
> 3. **Wrong direction of duality:** Some students write $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \equiv \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$. This is the **negation**, not the duality. Correct: $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \equiv \neg\, \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$. *1-mark penalty.*
> 4. **Product state-space arithmetic:** Forgetting to multiply $|S| \times |Q|$ in the complexity bound loses 1 mark. Always quote $O(|S| \cdot |Q|)$.
> 5. **Forgetting the $\bigwedge$ over multiple $p_i$:** The generalised form $\bigwedge_i \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p_i$ is a **Streett** property, not a single Büchi. Worth 2 marks in any part-(a) theoretical question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Persistence property** = canonical LTL form $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$, meaning "eventually $p$ holds forever". *(Must-memorise the formal path-quantifier form.)*
- **Duality identity** $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p \equiv \neg \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ — drives every Büchi-automaton-based decision procedure for persistence.
- **Generalised persistence** $\bigwedge_{i=1}^{k} \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p_i$ requires a **Streett** automaton with $k$ Streett pairs.
- The **2-state Büchi monitor** for $\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p$ is: $q_0 \xleftrightarrow{p} q_0 \to^{\neg p} q_1 \xleftrightarrow{\neg p} q_1 \to^{p} q_0$, with $F = \{q_1\}$.
- The **synchronised product** $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_{\neg\varphi}$ has at most $|S| \cdot |Q|$ states, transitions synchronised with $L(s')$.
- **Decision rule:** $M \models \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ iff $M \otimes \mathcal{A}_{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,\neg p}$ has **no accepting cycle**.
- **Fixed-point characterisation:** $E = \mathrm{gfp}(\tau)$ where $\tau(X) = \{s \mid p \in L(s) \wedge R(s) \subseteq X\}$ — runs in $O(|S|^2)$.
- **Time complexity** of nested-DFS emptiness on the product: $O(|S| \cdot |Q|)$.
- **Edge case:** If $p$ is true at $s_0$ and along all successors, $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,p$ collapses to safety $\mathbf{G}\,p$.
- **Engineering uses:** mode-locking, cache-coherence settling, distributed-consensus convergence, safety-critical shutdowns.
- **Exam mantra:** *"Reach the accepting state, then verify the cycle."* Partial counter-examples are worth zero.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nested Depth-First Search (Only algorithms required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nested Depth-First Search (Only algorithms required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nested Depth-First Search (NDFS)** is a two-phase, stack-based graph-traversal algorithm used in *automated verification*—specifically in **Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) model checking**—to detect **accepting cycles** in the synchronized product of a Kripke structure $\mathcal{M}$ and a Büchi automaton $\mathcal{B}_{\neg \phi}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECNT526 — Module 2):**
> Nested Depth-First Search is the canonical on-the-fly algorithm for **emptiness checking** of Büchi automata. It determines whether $\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{M}) \cap \mathcal{L}(\mathcal{B}_{\neg \phi}) \neq \emptyset$ by searching for a *lasso-shaped* counterexample path that loops through at least one accepting state.

Mathematically, NDFS is invoked on a product graph $G = (S, T, s_0, F)$ where:
- $S$ = finite set of product states, with $\vert S \vert \le \vert S_{\mathcal{M}} \vert \cdot \vert S_{\mathcal{B}} \vert$
- $T \subseteq S \times S$ = transition relation, with $\vert T \vert \le \vert T_{\mathcal{M}} \vert \cdot \vert T_{\mathcal{B}} \vert$
- $s_0$ = initial state
- $F \subseteq S$ = set of **accepting states** (inherited from the Büchi automaton)

The algorithm answers the question: *Does there exist a reachable state $s \in F$ that lies on a cycle?*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Treasure Hunter on an Island"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Treasure-Hunter Analogy**
> Imagine a treasure hunter dropped on a vast island network connected by one-way roads. The hunter carries a piece of chalk with **three colors**: **white** (never visited), **cyan** (currently being explored — paint a stripe on the road), and **blue** (fully explored, dead end).
>
> - **Outer DFS (dfs1)** is the *first journey* — the hunter walks every road reachable from the landing zone, painting roads cyan. Each time the hunter steps on a road that already has a cyan stripe (a *back edge*), a **Cycle Alert** is raised.
> - **Inner DFS (dfs2)** is the *verification trek* — starting from the suspicious cyan state, the hunter confirms whether a **treasure (accepting state)** lies somewhere on the cycle. If found, the cycle is a valid counterexample.
> - The reason it is **nested** is that dfs2 may be invoked many times during dfs1, and each invocation is itself a complete DFS that respects the cyan/blue markings of the outer search.

## 1.3 Why Two DFS Searches Are Necessary

A *single* DFS cannot detect Büchi acceptance, because acceptance requires the conjunction of two reachability conditions:
1. Reach an **accepting state** $f \in F$ from $s_0$.
2. Reach **back to $f$** (or any state already on the current DFS stack) starting from $f$ — i.e., find a cycle through $f$.

NDFS solves both in one structured traversal by using the outer search's stack (the cyan set) to encode the second condition implicitly.

## 1.4 Color Marking — The State Classification

| Color | Symbol | Meaning | Set Notation |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| **White** | $\perp$ | Unvisited, never seen | $W = \{s \in S \mid \text{color}(s)=\perp\}$ |
| **Cyan** | $C$ | On the current outer DFS stack | $C = \{s \in S \mid \text{color}(s)=C\}$ |
| **Blue** | $B$ | Fully explored, no longer on the stack | $B = \{s \in S \mid \text{color}(s)=B\}$ |
| **Red** (optimized) | $R$ | On inner DFS stack / has an accepting successor on the current stack | $R \subseteq C$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Invariant maintained at all times: $S = W \sqcup C \sqcup B$ (a partition — every state has exactly one color).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logical Structure

The NDFS family decomposes the **emptiness check** $\mathcal{L}(G) \stackrel{?}{=} \emptyset$ into three logically independent observations:

- **Observation A — Reachable Cycle Test.** During outer DFS, every back edge $(s, s')$ where $s' \in C$ certifies the existence of a cycle. The cycle consists of the suffix of the cyan stack from $s'$ down to $s$, plus the transition $s \to s'$.
- **Observation B — Acceptance Test.** A cycle is *accepting* iff it contains at least one state $f \in F$.
- **Observation C — On-the-Fly Property.** Because the product graph can be constructed lazily, NDFS can terminate as soon as one accepting cycle is found — no full graph needs to be materialized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why NDFS Beats Plain DFS + SCC**
> Tarjan's SCC algorithm also finds cycles, but it must complete the entire graph traversal to report the strongly connected component containing an accepting state. NDFS can short-circuit the moment an accepting cycle is witnessed, giving vastly better average-case performance in industrial model checkers such as **SPIN**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S$ | State space of product $G$ | $10^{4}$ – $10^{9}$ |
| $T$ | Transition relation of product $G$ | $10^{5}$ – $10^{10}$ |
| $F$ | Accepting states set, $F \subseteq S$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert)$ |
| $C$ | Cyan set (outer DFS stack) | $\le \vert S \vert$ |
| $B$ | Blue set (closed states) | $\le \vert S \vert$ |
| $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert T \vert)$ | Worst-case time of basic NDFS | — |
| $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)$ | Worst-case time of optimized NDFS (Schwoon-Esparza) | — |
| $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert)$ | Memory — single DFS stack + one extra stack | — |

> [!CAUTION]
> The basic NDFS has worst-case **$\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert T \vert)$** because the inner DFS may be invoked up to $\vert S \vert$ times, each costing up to $\vert T \vert$ in the worst case.
