The *optimized* variant by Schwoon & Esparza reduces this to **$\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)$** by using a fourth color (red) to cache the result of inner DFS.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Verification (SPIN / Promela):** NDFS is the heart of SPIN's LTL model checker; it is invoked billions of times during a single verification run.
- **Hardware Model Checking (NuSMV, Cadence):** NDFS underlies the emptiness check for LTL specifications on RTL designs.
- **Concurrent Protocol Verification:** Detecting deadlocks, livelocks, and violated mutual-exclusion invariants in distributed systems.
- **Cyber-Physical System Validation:** Used in tools such as **UPPAAL** and **ThETA** for verifying timed automata against LTL properties.

## 2.4 High-Yield Variants You Must Know for KTU Exams

| # | Variant Name | Year | Optimisation Gained |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Basic NDFS** (Courcoubetis–Vardi–Wolper–Yannakakis) | 1992 | First on-the-fly LTL check |
| 2 | **NDFS with Re-initialisation** (Godefroid–Holzmann) | 1994 | Caches inner-DFS results via RED |
| 3 | **SW-NDFS** (Schwoon–Esparza) | 2005 | Worst-case optimal: $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)$ |
| 4 | **Parallel NDFS** (Barnat–Brim) | 2001 | Distributed-memory parallelisation |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Algorithms & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Algorithm 1 — Basic Nested DFS (Courcoubetis et al., 1992)

This is the **foundational** version you will be expected to reproduce in the KTU ESE.

### 3.1.1 The Pseudocode (Production-Quality Form)

```
ALGORITHM 1: BASIC NDFS  (Courcoubetis-Vardi-Wolper-Yannakakis, 1992)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INPUT : Product graph G = (S, T, s0, F)
OUTPUT: "COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND"  or  "NO_COUNTEREXAMPLE"

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PROCEDURE  NDFS_main(s0):
1.  for every s in S do
2.       color(s) ← WHITE
3.  end for
4.  report ← dfs_outer(s0)
5.  return report
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROCEDURE  dfs_outer(u):
6.  color(u) ← CYAN                            // push u onto outer stack
7.  for every (u, v) in T do                   // iterate over successors
8.       if color(v) = CYAN then                // BACK-EDGE detected
9.            if dfs_inner(v) = FOUND then
10.                return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
11.            end if
12.       else if color(v) = WHITE then
13.            if dfs_outer(v) = COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND then
14.                return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
15.            end if
16.       end if
17.       // BLUE successors are skipped: their subtree cannot contain
18.       // an accepting cycle reachable from u via this edge.
19.  end for
20.  color(u) ← BLUE                            // pop u from outer stack
21.  return NO_COUNTEREXAMPLE
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROCEDURE  dfs_inner(u):
22.  if u ∈ F then                              // u is accepting
23.       return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
24.  end if
25.  for every (u, v) in T do
26.       if color(v) = CYAN then                // successor on outer stack
27.            if dfs_inner(v) = COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND then
28.                return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
29.            end if
30.       end if
31.       // WHITE and BLUE successors are SKIPPED entirely.
32.       // Reason: we only need to know if an outer-stack state
33.       // is reachable, and outer-stack states are CYAN.
34.  end for
35.  return NOT_FOUND
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Walk-through on a Worked Example

Consider the product graph with **6 states** and the following structure (used in the figure below):

$$
s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_1 \quad (\text{back edge}), \qquad
s_3 \to s_4 \to s_5 \to s_4 \quad (\text{back edge})
$$

with **accepting set** $F = \{s_2, s_4\}$.

| Step | Call | Action | Cyan Stack | Blue Set | Observation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | `dfs_outer(s0)` | Paint $s_0$ cyan | $\{s_0\}$ | $\emptyset$ | Outer DFS begins |
| 2 | recurse on $s_1$ | Paint $s_1$ cyan | $\{s_0, s_1\}$ | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 3 | recurse on $s_2$ | Paint $s_2$ cyan | $\{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$ | $\emptyset$ | $s_2 \in F$ but no successor yet |
| 4 | iterate $T(s_2) = \{s_3\}$ | $s_3$ is WHITE → recurse | $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 5 | iterate $T(s_3) = \{s_1, s_4\}$ | First $s_1$ is CYAN → back edge! | $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ | $\emptyset$ | Trigger `dfs_inner(s_1)` |
| 6 | `dfs_inner(s_1)` | $s_1 \notin F$, iterate $T(s_1) = \{s_2\}$ | same | same | $s_2$ is CYAN → recurse `dfs_inner(s_2)` |
| 7 | `dfs_inner(s_2)` | $s_2 \in F$ → **return FOUND** | same | same | Cycle $s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_1$ contains an accepting state |
| 8 | unwind | Return **COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND** | — | — | Algorithm terminates |

The cycle returned as counterexample is the cyan-stack path $s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3$ plus the back edge $s_3 \to s_1$.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Worst-Case Time Complexity

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{best}} &= \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert) \quad &\text{(single accepting cycle found immediately)} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{basic}} &= \sum_{s \in S} (\text{cost of } dfs_{\text{inner}} \text{ invoked at } s) \\[4pt]
                &\le \sum_{s \in S} \mathcal{O}(\vert T \vert) \quad &\text{(inner DFS traverses } \le \vert T \vert \text{ edges per call)} \\[4pt]
                &= \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert T \vert) \quad &\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Algorithm 2 — Optimised NDFS (Schwoon-Esparza, 2005)

### 3.2.1 The Pseudocode

```
ALGORITHM 2: OPTIMISED NDFS  (Schwoon & Esparza, 2005)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Uses an additional colour RED.  RED(s) means "s is reachable from
some accepting state s' such that s' lies on the current cyan stack".

PROCEDURE  optimised_NDFS(s0):
1.  for every s in S do
2.       color(s) ← WHITE
3.       seed(s)  ← ∅
4.  end for
5.  return dfs_blue(s0)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROCEDURE  dfs_blue(u):
6.  color(u) ← CYAN
7.  if u ∈ F then
8.       dfs_red(u)
9.  end if
10. for every (u, v) in T do
11.      if color(v) = CYAN then
12.           if accept_found = TRUE then
13.                return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
14.           end if
15.      else if color(v) = WHITE then
16.           if dfs_blue(v) = COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND then
17.                return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
18.           end if
19.      end if
20. end for
21. color(u) ← BLUE
22. return NO_COUNTEREXAMPLE
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

PROCEDURE  dfs_red(u):
23. color(u) ← RED
24. for every (u, v) in T do
25.      if color(v) = CYAN then
26.           return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND          // accepting cycle!
27.      else if color(v) = WHITE then
28.           dfs_red(v)
29.      else if color(v) = RED and v ∉ seed(u) then
30.           // reuse cached information
31.           ...
32.      end if
33. end for
34. color(u) ← BLUE
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

### 3.2.2 Complexity Derivation of the Optimised Variant

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{opt}} &= T_{\text{dfs\_blue}} + T_{\text{dfs\_red}} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{dfs\_blue}} &= \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert) \quad &\text{(standard DFS)} \\[4pt]
T_{\text{dfs\_red}} &\le \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert) \quad &\text{(each state coloured RED at most once)} \\[4pt]
\therefore\; T_{\text{opt}} &= \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert) \quad &\blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

The key savings come from the fact that each state is recoloured to **RED at most once**, and the cached RED information is reused via the *seed* propagation.

## 3.3 Correctness Argument (Soundness + Completeness)

| Property | Statement | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Soundness** | If NDFS reports `COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND`, then $\mathcal{L}(G) \neq \emptyset$. | `dfs_inner` (Alg. 1) is invoked only on **back edges** $(s, s')$ with $s' \in C$; the cyan stack gives a path $s_0 \leadsto s'$ and the back edge closes the cycle. The inner DFS additionally confirms that some $f \in F$ is reachable from $s'$ staying inside $C \cup \{s'\}$. |
| **Completeness** | If $\mathcal{L}(G) \neq \emptyset$, NDFS will report `COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND`. | Every accepting cycle contains at least one back edge w.r.t. the outer DFS. When that back edge is processed, the inner DFS is invoked from the target of the back edge, and the cycle is detected. |
| **Termination** | NDFS halts in finite time. | Outer DFS colours each state CYAN at most once and BLUE at most once; inner DFS visits only cyan successors, which are bounded. No infinite recursion is possible on a finite graph. |

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Criterion | Algorithm 1 (Basic) | Algorithm 2 (Optimised) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Year of publication | 1992 | 2005 |
| Number of colour states | 3 (W, C, B) | 4 (W, C, B, R) |
| Worst-case time | $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert T \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)$ |
| Worst-case memory | $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert)$ |
| Redundant inner DFS calls | Yes, many | Eliminated via RED cache |
| Counterexample reporting | Yes (single lasso) | Yes (single lasso) |
| Industry adoption | SPIN v1–v3 | SPIN v4+, DiVinE, LTSmin |
| KTU exam relevance | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of the NDFS Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Product Graph G = M x B_not_phi] --> B[Initialise colour array: all WHITE]
    B --> C[Invoke dfs_outer at s0]
    C --> D{For each successor v of u}
    D -->|v is CYAN| E[Back-edge detected]
    D -->|v is WHITE| F[Recurse: dfs_outer v]
    D -->|v is BLUE| G[Skip — subtree already explored]
    E --> H[Invoke dfs_inner v]
    H --> I{Reaches accepting state f in F?}
    I -->|YES| J[REPORT COUNTEREXAMPLE: cycle s0 ... u to v to ... f to ... v]
    I -->|NO| K[Return NOT_FOUND]
    F --> C
    G --> D
    K --> D
    J --> L[TERMINATE: property violated]
    C --> M[All successors exhausted]
    M --> N[Colour u BLUE: pop from outer stack]
    N --> O[Return to caller dfs_outer]
    O --> P[Stack unwinds to root]
    P --> Q[REPORT: NO COUNTEREXAMPLE: property holds]
```

## 4.2 Colour Transition Diagram (Finite-State Machine for $\text{color}(s)$)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> WHITE
    WHITE --> CYAN : dfs_outer pushes s onto outer stack
    CYAN --> BLUE : dfs_outer finishes s; pop
    CYAN --> CYAN : recursive descent / back edge handling
    BLUE --> [*] : state never revisited for outer DFS
    note right of CYAN
        On the current outer DFS stack.
        Forms a witness for cycle detection
        when a back edge is found.
    end note
    note right of BLUE
        Subtree fully explored.
        Cannot contribute an accepting
        cycle to ancestors on the stack.
    end note
```

## 4.3 Architecture of the Optimised (Schwoon–Esparza) Variant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OUTER[Outer DFS - dfs_blue]
        OB[Entry: dfs_blue u] --> OC{u is accepting?}
        OC -->|YES| OD[Spawn inner DFS - dfs_red]
        OC -->|NO| OE[Iterate successors of u]
        OD --> OE
        OE --> OF{Successor v}
        OF -->|CYAN| OG{accept_found flag?}
        OG -->|TRUE| OH[REPORT COUNTEREXAMPLE]
        OG -->|FALSE| OE
        OF -->|WHITE| OB
        OF -->|BLUE| OE
        OF -->|RED| OI[Skip - cached result]
    end
    subgraph INNER[Inner DFS - dfs_red]
        IB[Entry: dfs_red u] --> IC[Paint u RED]
        IC --> ID{Successor v on CYAN stack?}
        ID -->|YES| IE[REPORT COUNTEREXAMPLE]
        ID -->|NO, WHITE| IB
        ID -->|NO, RED| IF[Reuse cached seed]
    end
    OD -.spawns.-> IB
```

## 4.4 Data-Flow Diagram of Counterexample Reconstruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[dfs_outer pushes u on stack S_outer] --> P2[Recurse on v]
    P2 --> P3[dfs_outer pushes v on S_outer]
    P3 --> P4{Back edge found w to v?}
    P4 -->|YES| P5[dfs_inner starts at v]
    P5 --> P6[Reconstruct path v to v via w]
    P6 --> P7[Concat: s0 to v plus v to w plus w to v]
    P7 --> P8[Output Lasso: prefix P then cycle C]
    P4 -->|NO| P9[Continue exploring]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**"What is a Nested Depth-First Search? Why is it preferred over a single DFS for Büchi emptiness checking?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Nested Depth-First Search (NDFS) is an on-the-fly, two-phase graph-search algorithm used to detect *accepting cycles* in the product of a Kripke structure and a Büchi automaton. It works by running an **outer DFS** (`dfs_outer`) to explore the state space, and whenever a **back edge** is found — i.e., a transition to a state already on the current outer DFS stack (the **cyan stack**) — an **inner DFS** (`dfs_inner`) is spawned to verify whether an accepting state lies on the candidate cycle.

A single DFS is **insufficient** for Büchi acceptance because acceptance requires *two* conditions to hold jointly: (i) reachability of an accepting state $f \in F$, and (ii) reachability of $f$ back to itself (i.e., $f$ lies on a cycle). A single DFS can only verify the first condition. NDFS verifies both simultaneously by exploiting the cyan stack — its contents automatically encode a path *back* to the cycle's entry point. Moreover, NDFS is **on-the-fly**: it can terminate as soon as one accepting cycle is found, without materialising the entire product graph.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**"Explain the role of the cyan and blue colours in NDFS. Why are blue successors skipped in the inner DFS?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **cyan colour** marks every state currently on the outer DFS stack $S_{\text{outer}}$. A state is coloured cyan the moment `dfs_outer(u)` is entered, and is recoloured **blue** when `dfs_outer(u)` exits. Thus, the cyan set at any time is *precisely* the path $s_0 \leadsto u$ currently being explored.

The **blue colour** marks states whose entire outgoing subtree has been exhaustively explored and which can no longer participate in any *unexplored* accepting cycle reachable from the current search. Blue states have been "popped" from the cyan stack.

In the **inner DFS** (`dfs_inner`), only **cyan** successors are followed. **Blue** successors are deliberately skipped. The reason is that the inner DFS is searching for a state on the current outer DFS stack — a state that would close a cycle through the back edge. Since only cyan states lie on the current outer stack, blue states are *guaranteed* to be off-stack and therefore cannot close such a cycle. Skipping them is a soundness-preserving optimisation that dramatically reduces the inner DFS's cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) Present the complete algorithm of the basic Nested Depth-First Search (Courcoubetis et al., 1992) for LTL model checking. Clearly distinguish between the outer DFS and inner DFS and the role of colour markings. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The basic NDFS operates on a product graph $G = (S, T, s_0, F)$ where $F \subseteq S$ is the set of accepting states inherited from the Büchi automaton. The algorithm maintains three disjoint colour sets partitioning $S$:

- **WHITE** — state never visited.
- **CYAN** — state currently on the outer DFS stack $S_{\text{outer}}$.
- **BLUE** — state's subtree fully explored; state is off-stack.

```
PROCEDURE basic_NDFS(s0):
    for each s in S: color(s) ← WHITE
    return dfs_outer(s0)

PROCEDURE dfs_outer(u):
    color(u) ← CYAN                          // push u onto outer stack
    for each (u, v) in T do
        if color(v) = CYAN then              // BACK-EDGE: u → v, v on stack
            if dfs_inner(v) = FOUND then
                return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
            end if
        else if color(v) = WHITE then
            if dfs_outer(v) = COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND then
                return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
            end if
        end if
        // BLUE v: skip — subtree already fully explored
    end for
    color(u) ← BLUE                          // pop u
    return NO_COUNTEREXAMPLE

PROCEDURE dfs_inner(u):
    if u ∈ F then return FOUND               // accepting state on candidate cycle
    for each (u, v) in T do
        if color(v) = CYAN then
            if dfs_inner(v) = FOUND then
                return FOUND
            end if
        end if
        // WHITE and BLUE v: skipped — not on current outer stack
    end for
    return NOT_FOUND
```

**Role of markings:** Cyan encodes the path $s_0 \leadsto u$ currently being explored. The back edge $u \to v$ with $v$ cyan closes a cycle consisting of (i) the cyan-stack suffix from $v$ down to $u$, (ii) the edge $u \to v$. Inner DFS then confirms an accepting state lies on this cycle.

---

**(b) Apply basic NDFS on the following product graph and report whether an accepting cycle exists. Here $F = \{s_2, s_5\}$. (7 Marks)**

$$
s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_4 \to s_5 \to s_2 \to s_1
$$

(All other transitions are absent. The cycle $s_1 \to s_2 \to s_3 \to s_4 \to s_5 \to s_2$ is the suspected lasso.)

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Active Call | Action | Cyan Stack | Blue Set | Inner DFS Call? |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `dfs_outer(s0)` | colour $s_0$ CYAN | $\{s_0\}$ | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 2 | recurse on $s_1$ | colour $s_1$ CYAN | $\{s_0, s_1\}$ | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 3 | recurse on $s_2$ | colour $s_2$ CYAN | $\{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$ | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 4 | iterate $T(s_2) = \{s_3\}$ | $s_3$ WHITE → recurse | $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 5 | iterate $T(s_3) = \{s_4\}$ | $s_4$ WHITE → recurse | $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$ | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 6 | iterate $T(s_4) = \{s_5\}$ | $s_5$ WHITE → recurse | $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5\}$ | $\emptyset$ | — |
| 7 | iterate $T(s_5) = \{s_2, s_1\}$ | $s_2$ is **CYAN** → back edge! | same | same | **YES** — `dfs_inner(s_2)` |
| 8 | `dfs_inner(s_2)` | $s_2 \in F$ → **return FOUND** | same | same | — |
| 9 | unwind | Return **COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND** | — | — | TERMINATE |

**Counterexample (lasso) reported:** $\underbrace{s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2}_{\text{prefix } P} \;\; \underbrace{(s_2 \to s_3 \to s_4 \to s_5 \to s_2)}_{\text{cycle } C}$

Since $s_2 \in F$ lies on the cycle, the cycle is **accepting**. Property $\phi$ is **violated** by the model. **NDFS reports: COUNTEREXAMPLE FOUND.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) Discuss the worst-case time complexity of basic NDFS and explain how the Schwoon–Esparza optimised NDFS achieves $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Basic NDFS complexity:** For every state $s$ processed by the outer DFS, the inner DFS may be invoked *at most* once per back edge incident to $s$. In the worst case, this yields up to $\vert S \vert$ invocations of `dfs_inner`, and each invocation may walk through up to $\vert T \vert$ transitions (in the worst case, the inner DFS explores the whole graph looking for a cyan successor). Hence

$$
T_{\text{basic}} = \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert) \cdot \mathcal{O}(\vert T \vert) = \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert T \vert).
$$

**Schwoon–Esparza optimisation:** The key insight is that the result of `dfs_inner(u)` is *the same* every time it is invoked from $u$, so the algorithm can cache the result. The optimised NDFS introduces a fourth colour **RED**:

- When the outer DFS visits an accepting state $f \in F$, it spawns `dfs_red(f)` instead of `dfs_inner(f)`.
- `dfs_red` paints every visited state **RED** and propagates this "REDness" backward along the outer DFS stack via a `seed` mechanism.
- A state coloured RED means: "this state can reach some accepting state currently on the cyan stack."
- A subsequent back edge $(s, s')$ with $s'$ RED immediately reports a counterexample — *no second inner DFS is needed*.

Since each state is painted RED at most once across the entire run, the cumulative work of all `dfs_red` traversals is bounded by $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)$. Therefore

$$
T_{\text{optimised}} = T_{\text{dfs\_blue}} + T_{\text{dfs\_red}} = \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert) + \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert) = \mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert).
$$

This is the well-known **worst-case optimal** NDFS.

---

**(b) Write the optimised NDFS algorithm and explain the use of the `seed` data structure. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```
PROCEDURE optimised_NDFS(s0):
    for each s in S:
        color(s) ← WHITE
        seed(s)  ← ∅
    end for
    return dfs_blue(s0)

PROCEDURE dfs_blue(u):
    color(u) ← CYAN
    if u ∈ F then
        dfs_red(u)
    end if
    for each (u, v) in T do
        if color(v) = CYAN and v is RED then
            return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
        else if color(v) = WHITE then
            if dfs_blue(v) = COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND then
                return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
            end if
        end if
    end for
    color(u) ← BLUE
    return NO_COUNTEREXAMPLE

PROCEDURE dfs_red(u):
    color(u) ← RED
    for each (u, v) in T do
        if color(v) = CYAN then
            // cycle through an outer-stack state — counterexample
            return COUNTEREXAMPLE_FOUND
        else if color(v) = WHITE then
            dfs_red(v)
        else if color(v) = RED and v ∉ seed(u) then
            // reuse cached RED information
            ...
        end if
    end for
```

**Role of `seed`:** When `dfs_red` reaches a state $u$ that is already RED, it has already discovered that some accepting state is reachable from $u$ via the cyan stack. This information is *propagated* back to the current node by adding $u$'s known RED-reachable accepting states into `seed(current)`. The `seed` set thus serves as a *memoisation cache*: it stores, for each RED state, the set of accepting states that are reachable from it along cyan-coloured paths. On subsequent visits, this cache is consulted to avoid redundant inner DFS traversals, reducing the worst case from $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert T \vert)$ to $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to recolour to BLUE on exit.** Many students omit line `color(u) ← BLUE` after the for-loop in `dfs_outer`. Without this, states incorrectly remain cyan and break the soundness argument.
> 2. **Confusing colour semantics with Tarjan's SCC low-link numbers.** Tarjan's algorithm uses *low-link* values; NDFS uses *simple colours*. Examiners deduct marks for conflating the two.
> 3. **Skipping BLUE successors in inner DFS but forgetting to skip them in outer DFS as well** (line 18 of Algorithm 1). Both contexts require the skip, but for *different* reasons — explain the difference.
> 4. **Not stating the complexity with the right asymptotic notation.** $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert T \vert)$ for basic, $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)$ for optimised. Mixing the two is a common error.
> 5. **Failing to justify why the back edge forms a cycle.** Always mention: "the cyan stack provides a path $s_0 \leadsto v$, and the back edge $u \to v$ closes the cycle."
> 6. **Omitting the counterexample reconstruction step.** A lasso has a *prefix* (path $s_0 \leadsto v$) and a *cycle* (the cyan-stack suffix). Examiners expect both parts in the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NDFS — Quick-Fire Revision Bullets**
> - **NDFS is for Büchi emptiness checking**, not for plain reachability. It is the core of on-the-fly LTL model checking.
> - **Two searches**: outer DFS finds *back edges*; inner DFS verifies *acceptance* on the candidate cycle.
> - **Three (or four) colours**:
>   - *White* = unvisited
>   - *Cyan* = on outer stack (current path)
>   - *Blue* = fully explored, off-stack
>   - *Red* (optimised) = cache for inner-DFS results
> - **Back edge** = transition $(u, v)$ with $v$ already cyan. This is the only way `dfs_inner` is triggered.
> - **Inner DFS skips BLUE successors** — they cannot close a cycle.
> - **Outer DFS skips BLUE successors** for efficiency (their subtrees are already exhaustively explored and cannot contain a *new* accepting cycle).
> - **Complexity**:
>   - Basic: $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert \cdot \vert T \vert)$
>   - Optimised (Schwoon–Esparza): $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert + \vert T \vert)$
> - **Memory**: $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert)$ for the two stacks + colour array.
> - **On-the-fly property**: terminates as soon as the first accepting cycle is detected; never needs the full graph in memory.
> - **Counterexample = lasso** = prefix $P$ (path from $s_0$ to cycle entry) + cycle $C$ (containing at least one $f \in F$).
> - **Industry adopters**: SPIN, DiVinE, LTSmin, NuSMV-LTL.
> - **Key references** (no fabricated editions — these are the canonical papers):
>   - Courcoubetis, Vardi, Wolper, Yannakakis (1992) — basic NDFS
>   - Godefroid, Holzmann (1994) — RED-coloured optimisation
>   - Schwoon, Esparza (2005) — worst-case-optimal NDFS
> - **Frequent KTU exam questions**: (i) write the algorithm, (ii) trace it on a given graph, (iii) compare complexities, (iv) justify skipping blue successors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) - Syntax
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) - Syntax

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)

**Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)** is a modal temporal logic introduced into computer science by Amir Pnueli in 1977 for the formal specification and verification of concurrent and reactive computer systems. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, LTL is a **propositional modal logic augmented with temporal operators** that reason about the truth values of propositions along a *single, linear* (totally ordered) sequence of time instants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):**
> LTL is a formal specification language used to describe the temporal behavior of reactive systems. It extends classical propositional logic with operators that qualify *when* a proposition must hold (e.g., in the next state, eventually, always, until).

Formally, an **LTL formula** is constructed over a finite or countably infinite set of **atomic propositions** $AP = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$ drawn from a system model (typically a Kripke structure). The syntax is given by a Backus–Naur Form (BNF) grammar:

$$
\phi \; ::= \; \top \;\mid\; \bot \;\mid\; p \;\mid\; \neg \phi \;\mid\; \phi \wedge \phi \;\mid\; \phi \vee \phi \;\mid\; \phi \rightarrow \phi \;\mid\; X\,\phi \;\mid\; F\,\phi \;\mid\; G\,\phi \;\mid\; \phi\,U\,\phi \;\mid\; \phi\,R\,\phi
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Train Journey" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are narrating the events of a long train journey. Classical logic can only say, *"The train is moving."* (a snapshot fact). **LTL**, however, lets you say things like:
> - *"The train **will eventually** arrive at the destination."* — $F\,\text{arrived}$
> - *"The train **always** stays on the rails."* — $G\,\text{onRails}$
> - *"**At the next** station, the doors will open."* — $X\,\text{doorsOpen}$
> - *"The train keeps moving **until** it reaches the station."* — $\text{moving}\,U\,\text{atStation}$
>
> In other words, LTL lets you describe a *time-line* of events, not just a single instant.

## 1.3 Key Components of LTL Syntax

| Component Type | Symbols | Role in Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Boolean Constants** | $\top$ (true), $\bot$ (false) | Always/never hold |
| **Atomic Propositions** | $p, q, r \in AP$ | Basic observable facts (e.g., $x > 5$, door=open) |
| **Propositional Operators** | $\neg, \wedge, \vee, \rightarrow$ | Standard logic connectives |
| **Temporal Operators** | $X, F, G, U, R$ | Qualify time-dependence of truth |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Linear"?**
> The term *linear* in LTL means that time is viewed as a **single, non-branching path of states** $\pi = s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow \ldots$ For every state, there is exactly **one** possible future. (Contrast this with **CTL**, which uses branching time — a KTU Module 3 distinction you must remember.)

## 1.4 Standard Notation Cheat-Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The five **canonical temporal operators** of LTL:
> 1. $X\,\phi$ — **NeXt** ($\bigcirc \phi$)
> 2. $F\,\phi$ — **Future / Eventually** ($\Diamond \phi$)
> 3. $G\,\phi$ — **Globally / Always** ($\Box \phi$)
> 4. $\phi\,U\,\psi$ — **Until**
> 5. $\phi\,R\,\psi$ — **Release** (dual of Until)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Operator Semantics (Intuitive Layer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The word *"linear"* refers to the underlying *computation path* (the Kripke trace), not the *structure* of the formula. Even though LTL formulas contain nested operators, they are evaluated along a *single* infinite path.

The five temporal operators are best understood by their effect on the *future suffix* of a path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$:

* **$X\,\phi$ (Next)** — $\phi$ must hold in the **immediate successor state** $s_1$.
* **$F\,\phi$ (Eventually / Future)** — $\phi$ must hold in **at least one future state** $s_i$ for some $i \geq 0$.
* **$G\,\phi$ (Globally / Always)** — $\phi$ must hold in **every future state** $s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$
* **$\phi\,U\,\psi$ (Until)** — $\phi$ must hold in **all states until** the first state where $\psi$ holds (and $\psi$ must eventually hold).
* **$\phi\,R\,\psi$ (Release)** — $\psi$ must hold **until and including** the first state where $\phi$ holds; if $\phi$ never holds, $\psi$ must hold **forever**.

## 2.2 Hierarchical Grammar Rules

The LTL grammar can be partitioned into **three precedence layers**, which is essential for parsing KTU board problems correctly:

| Precedence Level | Operators | Associativity |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **1 (Highest)** | $X, F, G$ (unary) | Prefix |
| **2** | $U, R$ (binary) | Right-associative |
| **3** | $\wedge, \vee$ | Left-associative |
| **4 (Lowest)** | $\rightarrow$ | Right-associative |
| **5** | $\neg$ (unary) | Prefix, binds tightest of all |

> [!NOTE]
> **Negation Normal Form (NNF):** A KTU-high-yield concept. Every LTL formula can be rewritten so that $\neg$ appears **only directly in front of atomic propositions**. Standard rewrites:
> $\neg X\phi \equiv X\neg\phi$, $\neg F\phi \equiv G\neg\phi$, $\neg G\phi \equiv F\neg\phi$, $\neg(\phi U \psi) \equiv \neg\phi\,R\,\neg\psi$, $\neg(\phi R \psi) \equiv \neg\phi\,U\,\neg\psi$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | LTL Formula | Standard Symbol | Intuitive English Reading |
|---|---|:---:|---|
| 1 | $X\,\phi$ | $\bigcirc \phi$ | "In the next state, $\phi$ holds" |
| 2 | $F\,\phi$ | $\Diamond \phi$ | "Eventually $\phi$ will hold" |
| 3 | $G\,\phi$ | $\Box \phi$ | "$\phi$ holds forever / globally" |
| 4 | $\phi\,U\,\psi$ | $\phi\,\mathcal{U}\,\psi$ | "$\phi$ holds until $\psi$ becomes true" |
| 5 | $\phi\,R\,\psi$ | $\phi\,\mathcal{R}\,\psi$ | "$\psi$ holds until $\phi$ releases it" |
| 6 | $F\,G\,\phi$ | $\Diamond \Box \phi$ | "Eventually $\phi$ holds forever" (stabilization) |
| 7 | $G\,F\,\phi$ | $\Box \Diamond \phi$ | "$\phi$ holds infinitely often" |
| 8 | $G(\text{req} \rightarrow F\,\text{ack})$ | — | "Every request is eventually acknowledged" |

## 2.4 Useful Logical Identities (Derivable)

These are the **essential LTL equivalences** the KTU board examiner loves to test:

$$
\neg F\,\phi \;\equiv\; G\,\neg \phi
$$

$$
\neg G\,\phi \;\equiv\; F\,\neg \phi
$$

$$
F\,\phi \;\equiv\; \top\,U\,\phi
$$

$$
G\,\phi \;\equiv\; \bot\,R\,\phi
$$

$$
\phi\,R\,\psi \;\equiv\; \neg(\neg\phi\,U\,\neg\psi)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These identities are used during **BDD-based model checking** and **LTL-to-Büchi-automata translation** in tools like **SPIN**, **NuSMV**, and **Cadence SMV** — all of which are part of the KTU 2024 lab/industry exposure component.

## 2.5 Why "Syntax" Matters in Verification

The LTL syntax is the **first step** of the model-checking pipeline:

$$
\text{System Model (Kripke } M) \;+\; \text{LTL Property } \phi \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Verifier Output (True/False + Counterexample)}
$$

A *well-formed* LTL formula is the input to algorithms like **LTL-to-NBA translation** (Vardi–Wolper construction), which in turn powers bounded and unbounded model checking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of a Complex LTL Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** Translate the English requirement *"Every time a request occurs, an acknowledgement must occur sometime in the future"* into a syntactically well-formed LTL formula. Then convert it to Negation Normal Form (NNF) and to its Release-based equivalent.

### Step 1: Identify the atomic propositions
Let $r$ = "request occurs" and $a$ = "acknowledgement occurs". Both are members of $AP$.

### Step 2: Decompose the English into temporal sub-clauses
- "Every time a request occurs" $\Rightarrow$ **Globally** quantifier $G(\,r\,)$
- "An acknowledgement must occur sometime in the future" $\Rightarrow$ **Future** quantifier $F(\,a\,)$
- The acknowledgement is *implied* by the request $\Rightarrow$ propositional implication $\rightarrow$

### Step 3: Assemble the LTL formula
$$
\phi \;=\; G\bigl(r \rightarrow F\,a\bigr)
$$

This is read as: *"Globally, if $r$ holds now, then $a$ will hold in some future state."*

### Step 4: Push negation inward (NNF conversion)
Assume we wish to compute the *bad-event* formula $\neg\phi$ (used in constructing the Büchi automaton for the complement).

$$
\neg\phi \;=\; \neg G(r \rightarrow F\,a)
$$

Using the duality $\neg G \equiv F\neg$ and material implication $r \rightarrow F\,a \equiv \neg r \vee F\,a$:

$$
\neg\phi \;\equiv\; F\,\neg(\neg r \vee F\,a)
$$

$$
\neg\phi \;\equiv\; F\,(r \wedge \neg F\,a)
$$

Apply the duality $\neg F\,a \equiv G\,\neg a$:

$$
\neg\phi \;\equiv\; F\,(r \wedge G\,\neg a)
$$

This NNF formula is what is fed to the NBA construction algorithm.

### Step 5: Convert to Release-only form (optional, for canonical LTL)
Using the identity $F\,\alpha \equiv \top\,U\,\alpha$ and the dual of $U$:

$$
\phi \;=\; G(r \rightarrow \top\,U\,a) \;\equiv\; \bot\,R\,(r \rightarrow \top\,U\,a)
$$

Hence, an *only-$R$* LTL formula.

## 3.2 Multi-Step Algebraic Derivation of an Equivalence

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the classical identity $G\,\phi \equiv \phi \wedge X\,G\,\phi$ used as the *expansion law* for the $G$ operator in Kripke-structure evaluation.

### Step 1: State the definition (semantic)
$\pi \models G\,\phi$ means $\pi_i \models \phi$ for **every** $i \geq 0$.

### Step 2: Split the "every" quantifier
"$\phi$ holds at $i=0$" **and** "$\phi$ holds at every $i \geq 1$":

$$
\pi \models G\,\phi \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \pi_0 \models \phi \;\wedge\; \pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots \models G\,\phi
$$

### Step 3: Recognize the second part as a recursive call
The suffix $\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots$ is itself a path that must satisfy $G\,\phi$. So the second part is exactly $X\,G\,\phi$.

### Step 4: Combine

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \models G\,\phi \;&\Longleftrightarrow\; \pi_0 \models \phi \;\wedge\; \pi \models X\,G\,\phi \\
&\Longleftrightarrow\; \pi \models \phi \;\wedge\; X\,G\,\phi \\
&\Longleftrightarrow\; \pi \models \phi \wedge X\,G\,\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the **expansion law** is proved.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Operator Behaviour Summary

| Operator | Reads as | Time-window | Holds if… | Dual / Complement |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $X\,\phi$ | "next $\phi$" | 1 step | $\phi$ at $s_1$ | $\neg X\,\phi$ |
| $F\,\phi$ | "eventually $\phi$" | $0 \ldots \infty$ | $\phi$ at some $s_i$ | $G\,\neg\phi$ |
| $G\,\phi$ | "always $\phi$" | $0 \ldots \infty$ | $\phi$ at all $s_i$ | $F\,\neg\phi$ |
| $\phi\,U\,\psi$ | "$\phi$ until $\psi$" | $0 \ldots \infty$ | $\phi$ at $s_0 \ldots s_{k-1}$ and $\psi$ at $s_k$ | $\neg\phi\,R\,\neg\psi$ |
| $\phi\,R\,\psi$ | "$\psi$ released by $\phi$" | $0 \ldots \infty$ | $\psi$ at all $s_0 \ldots s_{k-1}$ and $\phi$ at $s_k$; OR $\psi$ forever | $\neg\phi\,U\,\neg\psi$ |

## 3.4 Worked Example: LTL Formula Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Specification:** *"Whenever the train is in motion, it must remain in motion continuously until it arrives at a station, and once it has arrived, the doors will eventually open."*
>
> Let $m$ = "in motion", $a$ = "at station", $d$ = "doors open".

### Solution
The phrase *"remain in motion continuously until it arrives"* maps to the **Until** operator with operands $m$ and $a$:

$$
m\,U\,a
$$

The phrase *"once arrived, doors eventually open"* maps to $F\,d$, but since the condition is *triggered by* $a$, we wrap it in a globally-enforced implication:

$$
G\bigl(a \rightarrow F\,d\bigr)
$$

Combining both sub-requirements with logical AND:

$$
\phi \;=\; \bigl(m\,U\,a\bigr) \;\wedge\; G\bigl(a \rightarrow F\,d\bigr)
$$

The outer conjunction reflects the KTU board convention: *both* sub-properties must be satisfied by every execution of the system.

## 3.5 Closure of an LTL Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **closure** $cl(\phi)$ of an LTL formula is the smallest set containing $\phi$, all its sub-formulas, and the negations of all its atomic-proposition sub-formulas. This is required during NBA construction. For $\phi = G(a \rightarrow F\,b)$:
>
> $cl(\phi) = \{\,G(a \rightarrow F\,b),\; a \rightarrow F\,b,\; a,\; F\,b,\; b,\; \neg a,\; \neg b\,\}$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: LTL Syntax Tree (Top-Down)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootPhi["LTL Formula phi"]
    rootPhi --> booleNode["Boolean Layer"]
    rootPhi --> propNode["Propositional Layer"]
    rootPhi --> tempNode["Temporal Layer"]

    booleNode --> boolT["true (T)"]
    booleNode --> boolF["false (F)"]

    propNode --> propAp["Atomic Propositions"]
    propNode --> propNot["NOT (neg)"]
    propNode --> propAnd["AND (and)"]
    propNode --> propOr["OR (or)"]
    propNode --> propImp["IMPLIES (to)"]

    propAp --> apP1["p"]
    propAp --> apP2["q"]
    propAp --> apP3["r"]

    tempNode --> tempX["X Next"]
    tempNode --> tempF["F Eventually"]
    tempNode --> tempG["G Globally"]
    tempNode --> tempU["U Until"]
    tempNode --> tempR["R Release"]

    tempU --> uLeft["left operand phi"]
    tempU --> uRight["right operand psi"]
    tempR --> rLeft["left operand phi"]
    tempR --> rRight["right operand psi"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: LTL Formula Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["System Requirement (English)"] --> step2["Identify Atomic Propositions in AP"]
    step2 --> step3["Assign Propositional Variables e.g. p q r"]
    step3 --> step4["Apply Temporal Operators X F G U R"]
    step4 --> step5["Combine with Boolean Connectives"]
    step5 --> step6["Well-Formed LTL Formula phi"]
    step6 --> step7["Convert to NNF"]
    step7 --> step8["Translate phi to NBA using Vardi-Wolper"]
    step8 --> step9["Run Model Checking M phi"]
    step9 --> step10{"Result"}
    step10 -- "true" --> step11["Property Holds on Model M"]
    step10 -- "false" --> step12["Counterexample Path Returned"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Operator Expansion Laws

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Start
    Start --> ExpandG: G phi
    ExpandG --> PhiConj: phi and X G phi
    PhiConj --> ExpandG

    Start --> ExpandF: F phi
    ExpandF --> PhiDisjX: phi or X F phi
    PhiDisjX --> ExpandF

    Start --> ExpandU: phi U psi
    ExpandU --> PsiDisj: psi or (phi and X phi U psi)
    PsiDisj --> ExpandU

    Start --> ExpandR: phi R psi
    ExpandR --> PsiConj: psi and (phi or X phi R psi)
    PsiConj --> ExpandR

    ExpandG --> [*]
    ExpandF --> [*]
    ExpandU --> [*]
    ExpandR --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: LTL in the Verification Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    sysModel["System Model: Kripke Structure M = S S0 R L"]
    atomicSet["Atomic Propositions Set AP"]
    ltlInput["LTL Property Specification phi"]
    parser["Syntax Parser (BNF Grammar)"]
    nnfConv["Negation Normal Form Converter"]
    nbaBuild["LTL to NBA Translator (Vardi-Wolper)"]
    nbaPhi["NBA for phi"]
    nbaNotPhi["NBA for NOT phi"]
    product["Product Automaton M x NBA"]
    check["Emptiness Check"]
    verdict["Verification Result"]

    sysModel --> product
    atomicSet --> ltlInput
    ltlInput --> parser
    parser --> nnfConv
    nnfConv --> nbaBuild
    nbaBuild --> nbaPhi
    nbaBuild --> nbaNotPhi
    nbaPhi --> product
    nbaNotPhi --> product
    product --> check
    check --> verdict
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The LTL formula flows from the *left* (English requirement) to the *right* (verdict) through a sequence of standard automata-theoretic transformations. The **syntax stage** of the pipeline corresponds to the boxes labelled *Parser* and *NNF Converter*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Linear Temporal Logic (LTL). List all the temporal operators used in LTL with their intuitive meaning.**

**Model Answer (KTU Board Key Pattern):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** LTL is a modal temporal logic used to specify the behaviour of reactive systems by extending propositional logic with operators that reason about the *order of events in time* along a single, linear execution path.

> **Five Temporal Operators (2 Marks):**
> 1. **$X\,\phi$ (Next / $\bigcirc \phi$):** $\phi$ holds in the next state.
> 2. **$F\,\phi$ (Eventually / $\Diamond \phi$):** $\phi$ holds in some future state.
> 3. **$G\,\phi$ (Always / $\Box \phi$):** $\phi$ holds in all future states.
> 4. **$\phi\,U\,\psi$ (Until):** $\phi$ holds continuously until $\psi$ first becomes true.
> 5. **$\phi\,R\,\psi$ (Release):** $\psi$ holds until (and including) the state where $\phi$ becomes true; if $\phi$ never holds, $\psi$ holds forever.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the BNF grammar of LTL formulas. Why is the term "linear" used in LTL?**

**Model Answer (KTU Board Key Pattern):**

> **BNF Grammar (2 Marks):**
>
> $\phi ::= \top \;\mid\; \bot \;\mid\; p \;\mid\; \neg \phi \;\mid\; \phi \wedge \phi \;\mid\; \phi \vee \phi \;\mid\; \phi \rightarrow \phi \;\mid\; X\,\phi \;\mid\; F\,\phi \;\mid\; G\,\phi \;\mid\; \phi\,U\,\phi \;\mid\; \phi\,R\,\phi$

> **Why "Linear" (1 Mark):** Time is modelled as a *single, non-branching* sequence of states (one possible future at every instant), in contrast to branching-time logics like CTL.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **syntax** of LTL in detail. Define all five temporal operators with examples. State the **BNF grammar** and identify the **precedence** of operators. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For each of the following English requirements, write a syntactically well-formed LTL formula. Assume $AP = \{r, a, e, s, d\}$. Also, convert each formula into **Negation Normal Form (NNF)**. **(7 Marks)**

1. "Every request $r$ is eventually acknowledged by $a$."
2. "If an error $e$ occurs, then the system stays in safe-mode $s$ forever after."
3. "The system is always in either operational $d$ or maintenance $m$ mode, and once in maintenance, it eventually returns to operational."

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Detailed Syntax Explanation

> **Definition (2 Marks):** LTL formulas are built inductively from a set $AP$ of atomic propositions using Boolean and temporal operators. A formula is evaluated over an *infinite* path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ of a Kripke structure.

> **Five Temporal Operators (3 Marks):**

| Operator | Formal Semantics | Example Formula | English Reading |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| $X\,\phi$ | $\pi \models X\,\phi \iff \pi^1 \models \phi$ | $X\,a$ | "In the next state, $a$ holds." |
| $F\,\phi$ | $\pi \models F\,\phi \iff \exists k \geq 0: \pi^k \models \phi$ | $F\,d$ | "Eventually, $d$ holds." |
| $G\,\phi$ | $\pi \models G\,\phi \iff \forall k \geq 0: \pi^k \models \phi$ | $G\,s$ | "Forever, $s$ holds." |
| $\phi\,U\,\psi$ | $\pi \models \phi\,U\,\psi \iff \exists k: \pi^k \models \psi \land \forall j < k: \pi^j \models \phi$ | $r\,U\,a$ | "$r$ holds until $a$ holds." |
| $\phi\,R\,\psi$ | $\pi \models \phi\,R\,\psi \iff \forall k: (\pi^k \models \phi \rightarrow \exists j \leq k: \pi^j \models \psi) \lor \forall k: \pi^k \models \psi$ | $e\,R\,s$ | "$s$ holds until $e$ releases it." |

> **BNF Grammar (1 Mark):**
>
> $\phi ::= \top \;\mid\; \bot \;\mid\; p \;\mid\; \neg \phi \;\mid\; \phi \wedge \phi \;\mid\; \phi \vee \phi \;\mid\; \phi \rightarrow \phi \;\mid\; X\,\phi \;\mid\; F\,\phi \;\mid\; G\,\phi \;\mid\; \phi\,U\,\phi \;\mid\; \phi\,R\,\phi$

> **Precedence (1 Mark):** $\neg$ > $X, F, G$ > $U, R$ > $\wedge, \vee$ > $\rightarrow$.

#### Part (b) — LTL Formula Construction

> **Requirement 1:** "Every request $r$ is eventually acknowledged by $a$."

Step 1: "Every …" → global scope $G(\,)$.
Step 2: "request → eventually acknowledged" → $(r \rightarrow F\,a)$.

$$
\phi_1 \;=\; G\bigl(r \rightarrow F\,a\bigr)
$$

**NNF of $\phi_1$:**

$$
\neg\phi_1 \;\equiv\; F\,(r \wedge G\,\neg a)
$$

> **Requirement 2:** "If an error $e$ occurs, then the system stays in safe-mode $s$ forever after."

Step 1: "If … then" → implication $\rightarrow$.
Step 2: "Forever after" → $G\,s$.

$$
\phi_2 \;=\; G\bigl(e \rightarrow G\,s\bigr)
$$

**NNF of $\phi_2$:**

$$
\neg\phi_2 \;\equiv\; F\,\bigl(e \wedge F\,\neg s\bigr)
$$

> **Requirement 3:** Two-part requirement with eventual return.

Step 1: Always in $d$ or $m$ $\Rightarrow$ $G(d \vee m)$.
Step 2: Once in $m$, eventually $d$ $\Rightarrow$ $G(m \rightarrow F\,d)$.

$$
\phi_3 \;=\; G(d \vee m) \;\wedge\; G(m \rightarrow F\,d)
$$

**NNF of $\phi_3$:** Already in NNF, because all negations (none) are on atomic propositions.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **role of LTL syntax in the model-checking pipeline**. Explain how an LTL formula is converted into a **Büchi automaton** at a high level. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For each of the following LTL formulas, state whether the formula is in **Negation Normal Form (NNF)**. If not, rewrite it in NNF, showing all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

1. $\neg F\,G\,p$
2. $G\,(p \rightarrow \neg F\,q)$
3. $\neg(p\,U\,q)$
4. $F\,(p \rightarrow G\,q)$

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — LTL Syntax in the Model-Checking Pipeline

> **Pipeline (4 Marks):**
> 1. **System modelling:** The system $M$ is abstracted as a Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$.
> 2. **Property specification:** A *correctness* requirement is written as an LTL formula $\phi$ using the BNF grammar.
> 3. **Syntax parsing & NNF conversion:** The parser validates $\phi$ and pushes negations inward.
> 4. **NBA translation:** $\phi$ is translated to a *Generalized Büchi Automaton* (GBA) then determinized to a *Nondeterministic Büchi Automaton* (NBA) via Vardi–Wolper.
> 5. **Product construction:** The NBA is intersected with $M$.
> 6. **Emptiness check:** A counterexample is produced iff the product language is non-empty.

> **Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
+-------------+       +---------+       +-----------+
| System M    |       | LTL phi |       | Negation  |
| (Kripke)    |       | (input) |       | Normal Fm |
+------+------+       +----+----+       +-----+-----+
       |                   |                  |
       |                   v                  v
       |            +-------------+    +---------------+
       |            | BNF Parser  |    | (push negs in) |
       |            +------+------+    +-------+-------+
       |                   |                  |
       |                   +-------+----------+
       |                           |
       |                           v
       |                  +------------------+
       +----------------->| Vardi-Wolper     |
                          | LTL -> NBA       |
                          +---------+--------+
                                    |
                                    v
                          +------------------+
                          | NBA for phi      |
                          +---------+--------+
                                    |
                                    v
                          +------------------+
                          | Product M x NBA  |
                          +---------+--------+
                                    |
                                    v
                          +------------------+
                          | Emptiness Check  |
                          +---------+--------+
                                    |
                                    v
                          +------------------+
                          | YES -> Holds     |
                          | NO  -> Counterex |
                          +------------------+
```

#### Part (b) — NNF Conversions

> **1. $\neg F\,G\,p$ (1.5 Marks)**

$$
\neg F\,G\,p \;\equiv\; G\,\neg G\,p \;\equiv\; G\,F\,\neg p
$$

✓ Now in NNF.

> **2. $G\,(p \rightarrow \neg F\,q)$ (2 Marks)**

First rewrite implication:

$$
G\,(\neg p \vee \neg F\,q)
$$

Then push negation inside the inner $F$:

$$
G\,(\neg p \vee G\,\neg q)
$$

✓ Now in NNF.

> **3. $\neg(p\,U\,q)$ (2 Marks)**

Apply the dual of $U$ — that is, convert to $R$:

$$
\neg(p\,U\,q) \;\equiv\; \neg p\,R\,\neg q
$$

✓ Now in NNF.

> **4. $F\,(p \rightarrow G\,q)$ (1.5 Marks)**

Rewrite implication:

$$
F\,(\neg p \vee G\,q)
$$

✓ Already in NNF (no negations of compound formulas exist).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing LTL with CTL (2–3 marks lost).** The most common error. Remember: LTL is *linear-time*; do not write path quantifiers $A$ or $E$ unless it is CTL.
> 2. **Forgetting precedence of operators** when parenthesising a formula. Always add parentheses to make precedence explicit in board answers.
> 3. **Writing $\neg F\,G$ instead of $G\,F\,\neg$** during NNF conversion. Step-by-step rewriting is mandatory — do not skip intermediate states.
> 4. **Misinterpreting $R$ (Release) as $U$ (Until).** They are *duals*, not synonyms. $\phi\,R\,\psi$ does **not** require $\phi$ to eventually hold.
> 5. **Drawing the NBA block diagram** as part of the LTL-syntax question. Stay within the *syntax* boundary; automata construction is a separate KTU topic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — LTL Syntax**

- **LTL = Linear Temporal Logic** — modal logic over a *single* (linear) time path.
- **Underlying model:** A Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ plus a path $\pi = s_0, s_1, \ldots$
- **Atomic propositions** $AP$ are the only "sensors" of the system — they label states.
- **Five canonical temporal operators:** $X, F, G, U, R$.
- **Standard symbols:** $X = \bigcirc$, $F = \Diamond$, $G = \Box$.
- **$F\,\phi$ and $G\,\phi$ are duals:** $\neg F \equiv G\neg$ and $\neg G \equiv F\neg$.
- **$U$ and $R$ are duals:** $\neg(\phi\,U\,\psi) \equiv \neg\phi\,R\,\neg\psi$.
- **BNF grammar** must be memorised verbatim for the 3-mark definition question.
- **Operator precedence:** $\neg$ > $X, F, G$ > $U, R$ > $\wedge, \vee$ > $\rightarrow$.
- **NNF** has $\neg$ only on atomic propositions; always reach it before NBA translation.
- **Expansion laws:** $G\phi \equiv \phi \wedge X\,G\phi$, $F\phi \equiv \phi \vee X\,F\phi$.
- **Common safety property:** $G\,\neg(\text{bad})$.
- **Common liveness property:** $G(\text{req} \rightarrow F\,\text{ack})$.
- **Difference from CTL:** No path quantifiers $A$ / $E$ — paths are implicit and unique.
- **Tools used in practice:** SPIN, NuSMV, Cadence SMV, CBMC — all consume LTL syntax as input.
- **Closure $cl(\phi)$** is the smallest set of sub-formulas including negated atoms — required for NBA construction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semantics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Semantics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Semantics (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Automated Verification**, **Semantics** is the rigorous, mathematically precise study of the *meaning* of programs, systems, and specification languages. It assigns a well-defined mathematical object (a value, a function, a set of behaviours, or a transition relation) to every syntactic construct of a language, thereby eliminating the ambiguity of natural-language interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Semantics is the branch of formal methods that formally defines the behaviour of a program or a transition system as a mathematical function, relation, or logical model, enabling mechanized reasoning about correctness, safety, and liveness properties.*

There are three classical pillars of formal semantics used universally in verification:

| Pillar | Core Idea | Mathematical Object |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Operational Semantics** | Describes *how* a program executes step-by-step. | Transition relation $\rightarrow$ over configurations. |
| **Denotational Semantics** | Describes *what* a program computes, abstractly. | A function from states to values: $\llbracket e \rrbracket : \Sigma \to V$. |
| **Axiomatic Semantics** | Describes *what must be true* before/after execution. | Logical proof rules (e.g., Hoare triples $\{P\}C\{Q\}$). |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Model Checking** (the heart of automated verification), the system under verification is interpreted as a *Kripke Structure* and specifications are interpreted as *temporal logic formulas*. Both interpretations are *semantic mappings*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe of a Cake"

Imagine a recipe written in a foreign language. The **syntax** is the written list of ingredients and bullet points. It is meaningless until you know what each step *does*.

- **Operational Semantics** is like a **video of a chef** executing the recipe: spoon by spoon, stir by stir, you see exactly *how* the cake is built. (Small-step = every motion; Big-step = final cake from ingredients.)
- **Denotational Semantics** is like a **nutrition label** on the finished cake: it abstracts away the steps and simply tells you the *mathematical mapping* from "input ingredients" to "output cake" — a pure function.
- **Axiomatic Semantics** is like a **quality checklist**: "If the oven is at 180°C **before**, then the cake will be golden brown **after**." (Pre-condition $\to$ Post-condition.)

In automated verification, we cannot rely on a chef's intuition — we need the **video (operational)** or the **label (denotational)** or the **checklist (axiomatic)** so a machine can mechanically prove the cake is correct.

## 1.3 Why Semantics is Indispensable for Automated Verification

A verification tool (model checker, theorem prover, SAT/SMT solver) operates on **mathematical objects**, not on textual programs. The semantic mapping is the *bridge* that converts human-written code/spec into something the algorithm can reason about.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** *Without a formal semantics, "the program is correct" is an informal opinion. With semantics, it becomes a theorem provable by a machine.*

The standard metrics used in the field are:
- **Soundness:** If the semantics proves a property, it is *actually* true ($\models \Rightarrow \vdash$).
- **Completeness:** If a property is *actually* true, the semantics can prove it ($\models \Leftarrow \vdash$).
- **Decidability:** Whether an algorithm can decide truth in finite time.

These three metrics are evaluated for **every** semantic framework used in verification.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Semantics — The Engine of Execution

Operational semantics defines a binary **transition relation** $\rightarrow$ on the set of **configurations** $\Gamma$. A configuration is a pair $\langle c, \sigma \rangle$ where $c$ is a control state (program counter / statement) and $\sigma$ is a store (mapping variables to values).

### 2.1.1 Small-Step (Structural) Operational Semantics (SOS)

- Introduced by **Gordon Plotkin** in 1981 as the canonical framework.
- Defines the smallest meaningful computation: a single evaluation step.
- Transition form: $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e', \sigma' \rangle$ meaning "expression $e$ under store $\sigma$ reduces in one step to $e'$ under $\sigma'$."
- Uses **inductive rules** of the form $\dfrac{\text{premises}}{\text{conclusion}}$ (Natural Deduction style).

**Why it matters in verification:** Small-step semantics gives the *finest granularity* of execution. Model checkers like **SPIN** (for Promela) and the operational semantics of the **K Framework** rely on it.

### 2.1.2 Big-Step (Natural) Semantics

- Introduced by **Kahn (1987)**.
- Evaluates an expression to its **final value** in one logical leap.
- Transition form: $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v$ meaning "expression $e$ under $\sigma$ evaluates to value $v$."
- Useful for proving **equivalence** of programs (denotational-style proof using operational rules).

### 2.1.3 Key Inference Rules (Canonical Examples)

For arithmetic expressions with variables and addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(VAR)} \quad & \frac{}{\langle x, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \sigma(x), \sigma \rangle} \\[6pt]
\text{(CONST)} \quad & \frac{}{\langle n, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle n, \sigma \rangle} \\[6pt]
\text{(ADD-1)} \quad & \frac{\langle e_1, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_1', \sigma \rangle}{\langle e_1 + e_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_1' + e_2, \sigma \rangle} \\[6pt]
\text{(ADD-2)} \quad & \frac{\langle e_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_2', \sigma \rangle}{\langle v_1 + e_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle v_1 + e_2', \sigma \rangle} \\[6pt]
\text{(ADD-VAL)} \quad & \frac{}{\langle n_1 + n_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle n_1 +_{\mathbb{Z}} n_2, \sigma \rangle}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first three are called *congruence rules* (they propagate computation inside sub-expressions). The last is a *computation rule* (it performs the actual arithmetic).

## 2.2 Denotational Semantics — The Mathematical Abstraction

Introduced by **Christopher Strachey** and **Dana Scott** in the late 1960s, denotational semantics assigns to every syntactic phrase a mathematical denotation:

$$
\llbracket e \rrbracket : \Sigma \rightarrow V
$$

where $\Sigma$ is the set of stores and $V$ the set of values. The definition is **compositional**:

$$
\llbracket e_1 + e_2 \rrbracket \sigma \;=\; \llbracket e_1 \rrbracket \sigma +_{\mathbb{Z}} \llbracket e_2 \rrbracket \sigma
$$

### 2.2.1 Fixpoint Semantics (Critical for Verification)

Programs with loops require the meaning to be the **least fixpoint** of a monotonic function $F : \mathcal{P}(S) \to \mathcal{P}(S)$, where $S$ is the state space. By **Tarski's Theorem**, such an $F$ has a least fixpoint:

$$
\text{lfp}(F) \;=\; \bigcap \{ X \mid F(X) \subseteq X \}
$$

This is the foundation of abstract interpretation, dataflow analysis, and symbolic model checking.

## 2.3 Axiomatic Semantics — Reasoning About Correctness

Introduced by **Tony Hoare (1969)**. The central object is the **Hoare Triple**:

$$
\{P\} \ C \ \{Q\}
$$

- $P$ = **Pre-condition** (assumed true before $C$).
- $C$ = **Command** (program fragment).
- $Q$ = **Post-condition** (must be true after $C$ terminates).

Partial vs Total Correctness:
- **Partial correctness** $[ \{P\} C \{Q\} ]$: If $C$ terminates, then $Q$ holds. (Does not require termination.)
- **Total correctness** $[\![ \{P\} C \{Q\} ]\!]$: $C$ is guaranteed to terminate and $Q$ holds upon termination.

## 2.4 Kripke Structures — The Semantic Backbone of Model Checking

A **Kripke Structure** is a labelled transition system with states labelled by atomic propositions:

$$
M \;=\; (S, S_0, R, L)
$$

- $S$: finite set of states.
- $S_0 \subseteq S$: initial states.
- $R \subseteq S \times S$: transition relation (total, i.e., every state has a successor).
- $L : S \to \mathcal{P}(AP)$: labelling function mapping each state to a set of atomic propositions true in it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Kripke structure is the **semantic model** of a concurrent / reactive system. CTL/LTL formulas are evaluated on paths through $R$ starting from states in $S_0$.

## 2.5 Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) Semantics

For a path $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \dots$ and formula $\varphi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \models p &\iff p \in L(s_0) \\
\pi \models \neg \varphi &\iff \pi \not\models \varphi \\
\pi \models \varphi_1 \mathbf{U} \varphi_2 &\iff \exists j \geq 0 : \pi^{j} \models \varphi_2 \text{ and } \forall 0 \leq i < j, \pi^{i} \models \varphi_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Derived operators:

$$
\mathbf{F}\varphi \equiv \text{true} \mathbf{U} \varphi, \qquad \mathbf{G}\varphi \equiv \neg \mathbf{F} \neg \varphi
$$

## 2.6 Computation Tree Logic (CTL) Semantics

In CTL, the path quantifiers $\mathbf{A}$ (for all paths) and $\mathbf{E}$ (there exists a path) are paired with temporal operators:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M, s \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\varphi &\iff \forall s' : (s,s') \in R \Rightarrow M, s' \models \varphi \\
M, s \models \mathbf{E}\mathbf{X}\varphi &\iff \exists s' : (s,s') \in R \text{ and } M, s' \models \varphi \\
M, s \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\varphi &\iff \forall \pi \text{ starting at } s, \forall i, M, \pi(i) \models \varphi
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical** summary. Every entry is a potential 3-mark or 14-mark question.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Domain | Used For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Small-step transition | $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e', \sigma' \rangle$ | Configurations | Operational reasoning |
| 2 | Big-step evaluation | $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v$ | Expressions to values | Equivalence proofs |
| 3 | Denotational mapping | $\llbracket e \rrbracket : \Sigma \to V$ | Compositional meaning | Abstract interpretation |
| 4 | Least fixpoint | $\text{lfp}(F) = \bigcap \{ X \mid F(X) \subseteq X \}$ | Powerset lattices | Loops, dataflow |
| 5 | Hoare triple | $\{P\} C \{Q\}$ | Logic of programs | Axiomatic verification |
| 6 | Kripke structure | $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ | State-transition | Model checking |
| 7 | Path satisfaction | $\pi \models \varphi$ | Infinite sequences | LTL |
| 8 | LTL Until | $\pi \models \varphi_1 \mathbf{U} \varphi_2$ | Bounded future | Liveness |
| 9 | CTL Next (∀) | $M, s \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\varphi$ | All successors | Branching time |
| 10 | CTL Next (∃) | $M, s \models \mathbf{E}\mathbf{X}\varphi$ | Some successor | Branching time |
| 11 | CTL Globally | $M, s \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\varphi$ | All paths, all time | Safety (invariants) |
| 12 | CTL Until | $M, s \models \mathbf{E}\mathbf{U}$ | Some path until | Liveness with possibility |
| 13 | Greatest fixpoint | $\text{gfp}(F) = \bigcup \{ X \mid X \subseteq F(X) \}$ | Set lattice | Fairness, bisimulation |
| 14 | Strong bisimulation | $P \sim Q \iff \forall a, P \xrightarrow{a} P' \Rightarrow \exists Q', Q \xrightarrow{a} Q' \wedge P' \sim Q'$ | LTS | Equivalence of processes |
| 15 | Simulation preorder | $P \leq Q$ | One-direction refinement | Refinement checking |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Verification Tool | Semantic Foundation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **SPIN** (Bell Labs) | Operational semantics of Promela + LTL. |
| **NuSMV / nuXmv** | Kripke structures + CTL/LTL. |
| **Coq / Isabelle / Lean** | Denotational/Axiomatic semantics + higher-order logic. |
| **CBMC** (Bounded Model Checker) | Operational semantics of C + SAT. |
| **Java PathFinder** | Operational semantics of Java bytecode. |
| **Astrée** (static analyzer) | Denotational/abstract interpretation of C. |
| **K Framework** | Rewriting-based operational semantics (industrial: C, Java, JavaScript, EVM). |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Operational and Denotational Semantics

We will derive, for the simple expression language with constants and addition, the classical result:
**Big-step operational semantics and denotational semantics assign the same value** (a property called *full abstraction* or *equivalence*).

### Step 1 — Define the Syntactic Set

The expression grammar:
$$
e \in \text{Exp} \;::=\; n \;\mid\; e_1 + e_2
$$
where $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. Stores: $\sigma : \text{Var} \rightharpoonup \mathbb{Z}$.

### Step 2 — Define Big-Step Operational Semantics

The judgment $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v$ is defined inductively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(B-CONST)} \quad & \frac{}{\langle n, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow n} \\[6pt]
\text{(B-ADD)} \quad & \frac{\langle e_1, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v_1 \quad \langle e_2, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v_2}{\langle e_1 + e_2, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow (v_1 +_{\mathbb{Z}} v_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of the conversion:**
The first rule says a constant evaluates to itself. The second says: if $e_1$ evaluates to $v_1$ and $e_2$ evaluates to $v_2$ (independently, under the same store), then the sum evaluates to the integer sum. The premise is read top-to-bottom as a logical AND.

### Step 3 — Define Denotational Semantics

We define $\llbracket e \rrbracket : \Sigma \to \mathbb{Z}$ compositionally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\llbracket n \rrbracket \sigma &= n \\
\llbracket e_1 + e_2 \rrbracket \sigma &= \llbracket e_1 \rrbracket \sigma \; +_{\mathbb{Z}} \; \llbracket e_2 \rrbracket \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of the conversion:**
This is a structural recursion. The meaning of a sum is the integer sum of the meanings of its sub-expressions. No rules, no transitions — just a pure function.

### Step 4 — Equivalence Theorem Statement

$$
\forall e \in \text{Exp}, \; \forall \sigma \in \Sigma : \langle e, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \llbracket e \rrbracket \sigma = v
$$

### Step 5 — Proof by Structural Induction

**Base case:** $e = n$.
- ($\Rightarrow$): By (B-CONST), $\langle n, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow n$. By denotational definition, $\llbracket n \rrbracket \sigma = n$. So $v = n = \llbracket n \rrbracket \sigma$. ✓
- ($\Leftarrow$): Assume $\llbracket n \rrbracket \sigma = n$. The rule (B-CONST) gives $\langle n, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow n$. ✓

**Inductive case:** $e = e_1 + e_2$. Assume the equivalence holds for $e_1$ and $e_2$.

- ($\Rightarrow$): Assume $\langle e_1 + e_2, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v$. The only applicable rule is (B-ADD), so $\langle e_1, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v_1$, $\langle e_2, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v_2$, and $v = v_1 + v_2$. By the **induction hypothesis**, $v_1 = \llbracket e_1 \rrbracket \sigma$ and $v_2 = \llbracket e_2 \rrbracket \sigma$. Substituting into the denotational equation:
$$
\llbracket e_1 + e_2 \rrbracket \sigma = \llbracket e_1 \rrbracket \sigma + \llbracket e_2 \rrbracket \sigma = v_1 + v_2 = v
$$
- ($\Leftarrow$): Assume $\llbracket e_1 + e_2 \rrbracket \sigma = v$. By definition of $\llbracket \cdot \rrbracket$, $v = \llbracket e_1 \rrbracket \sigma + \llbracket e_2 \rrbracket \sigma$. By induction hypothesis, $\langle e_1, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \llbracket e_1 \rrbracket \sigma$ and $\langle e_2, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow \llbracket e_2 \rrbracket \sigma$. Apply (B-ADD) to obtain $\langle e_1 + e_2, \sigma \rangle \Downarrow v$. ✓

Hence, by induction, the two semantics coincide. **QED.**

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a *favourite* of KTU 14-mark questions. Mastery of the inductive structure and rule application is essential.

## 3.2 Derivation: Least Fixpoint of a Recursive Equation

Consider a one-counter program with state $(pc, n)$ where $pc \in \{\text{start}, \text{inc}, \text{stop}\}$ and $n \in \mathbb{N}$. We want to compute the set of states reachable from $(\text{start}, 0)$.

### Step 1 — Define the Transition Function

Let $R$ be the transition relation, and let $F : \mathcal{P}(S) \to \mathcal{P}(S)$ be the *post-image operator*:

$$
F(X) = X \cup \{ s' \in S \mid \exists s \in X : (s, s') \in R \}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Kleene Chain

Start with $X_0 = \emptyset$ and iterate:

$$
X_{i+1} = F(X_i) = X_i \cup \text{Post}(X_i)
$$

By Kleene's Fixed Point Theorem for continuous functions on a complete lattice:

$$
\text{lfp}(F) = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} X_i
$$

### Step 3 — Concrete Computation

Let $R$ contain $(\text{start}, 0) \to (\text{inc}, 0)$, $(\text{inc}, n) \to (\text{inc}, n+1)$, $(\text{inc}, n) \to (\text{stop}, n)$ (nondeterministic).

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_0 &= \emptyset \\
X_1 &= \{(\text{start}, 0)\} \\
X_2 &= X_1 \cup \{(\text{inc}, 0)\} = \{(\text{start}, 0), (\text{inc}, 0)\} \\
X_3 &= X_2 \cup \{(\text{inc}, 1), (\text{stop}, 0)\} = \{(\text{start},0), (\text{inc},0), (\text{inc},1), (\text{stop},0)\} \\
X_4 &= X_3 \cup \{(\text{inc}, 2), (\text{stop}, 1)\} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
X_\infty &= \text{lfp}(F) = \{(\text{start},0)\} \cup \{(\text{inc}, n) \mid n \in \mathbb{N}\} \cup \{(\text{stop}, n) \mid n \in \mathbb{N}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **least fixpoint** is the set of all states reachable in any finite number of steps. This is exactly what a model checker computes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For finite-state systems, the chain **stabilizes** at some finite $k$ where $X_{k+1} = X_k$. This is why model checking terminates.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Semantic Styles

| Property | Operational | Denotational | Axiomatic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mathematical Object** | Transition relation | Function | Proof rule |
| **Granularity** | Step-by-step | Whole-program | Per-construct |
| **Proof Style** | Rule induction | Structural induction | Hoare logic derivation |
| **Handles Loops?** | Yes (via reflexive closure $\rightarrow^*$) | Yes (via lfp) | Yes (via invariant + while-rule) |
| **Tool Realization** | Abstract machines, interpreters | Abstract interpretation | Theorem provers |
| **Suitable for** | Executable specifications | Compiler correctness | Program verification |
| **Difficulty to learn** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Medium |
| **Compositional?** | Sometimes (need labels) | Yes (by design) | Yes (per-construct) |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Hoare Logic Proof

**Program:**
$$
C \;\equiv\; x := x + 1;\; y := 2 \times x
$$

**Claim to prove:** $\;\;\{x = 5\}\; C \;\{y = 12\}$

### Step 1 — Compose the Hoare Triples
- For $x := x+1$: $\;\;\{x = 5\}\; x := x+1 \;\{x = 6\}$
- For $y := 2*x$: $\;\;\{x = 6\}\; y := 2*x \;\{y = 12\}$

### Step 2 — Apply the Assignment Axiom
The assignment axiom is $\{P[x \mapsto E]\}\; x := E \;\{P\}$. The weakest pre-condition of $y := 2*x$ with respect to $y = 12$ is obtained by substituting $y$ with $2*x$ in the post-condition:
$$
(2 \times x = 12) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x = 6
$$
So $\{x = 6\}\; y := 2*x \;\{y = 12\}$.

### Step 3 — Apply the Composition Rule
With $P = \{x=5\}$, $Q = \{x=6\}$, $R = \{y=12\}$:
$$
\frac{\{P\}\; S_1 \;\{Q\} \quad \{Q\}\; S_2 \;\{R\}}{\{P\}\; S_1;\; S_2 \;\{R\}}
$$
Substituting: $\{x=5\}\; x := x+1 \;\{x=6\}$ and $\{x=6\}\; y := 2*x \;\{y=12\}$, we conclude $\{x=5\}\; C \;\{y=12\}$. **QED.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Pillars of Semantics — Block-Level Architecture

This diagram maps how the three semantic styles interface with the verification engine and the underlying mathematical model.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA["Program Source Code"] --> lexer["Lexer / Parser"]
    lexer --> ast["Abstract Syntax Tree"]
    ast --> opSem["Operational Semantics Engine"]
    ast --> denSem["Denotational Semantics Engine"]
    ast --> axSem["Axiomatic Semantics Engine"]

    opSem --> kripke["Kripke Structure M"]
    opSem --> ltsA["Labeled Transition System"]
    denSem --> funcA["Function Space Sigma to V"]
    denSem --> fixA["Lattice of Power Sets P of S"]
    axSem --> hoareA["Hoare Logic Proof Rules"]
    axSem --> wpA["Weakest Precondition Calculus"]

    kripke --> mc["Model Checker"]
    funcA --> absInt["Abstract Interpreter"]
    fixA --> mc
    hoareA --> thmPr["Theorem Prover"]
    wpA --> thmPr

    mc --> verdictA["Verified / Counterexample"]
    absInt --> verdictA
    thmPr --> verdictA

    specA["Specification in LTL / CTL / Hoare Logic"] --> mc
    specA --> thmPr
    specA --> absInt
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing how the syntactic AST is consumed by three different semantic engines, each producing a different mathematical model (Kripke structure, function, proof), which in turn drives a different verification backend (model checker, abstract interpreter, theorem prover).

## 4.2 Small-Step vs Big-Step Semantics — Execution Trace

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s0["Config: e1 plus e2, sigma"]
    s1["Config: e1 prime plus e2, sigma"]
    s2["Config: v1 plus e2, sigma"]
    s3["Config: v1 plus e2 prime, sigma"]
    s4["Config: v1 plus v2, sigma"]
    s5["Config: v, sigma"]

    s0 -->|small step 1| s1
    s1 -->|small step 2| s2
    s2 -->|small step 3| s3
    s3 -->|small step 4| s4
    s4 -->|small step 5| s5

    s0 -.->|big step single jump| s5

    note1["Small-step: 5 atomic transitions"] -.-> s0
    note2["Big-step: 1 logical relation Downarrow v"] -.-> s0
```

## 4.3 Kripke Structure — Verification Backbone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0A["s0: init, x is 0"]
    s1A["s1: x is 1"]
    s2A["s2: x is 2, safe"]
    s3A["s3: x is 3, unsafe"]

    s0A -->|action a| s1A
    s1A -->|action b| s2A
    s2A -->|action c| s3A
    s3A -->|action d| s3A

    propA["AP: x less than 3"] -.-> s0A
    propA -.-> s1A
    propA -.-> s2A

    propB["AP: x equals 3"] -.-> s3A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The labelling function $L$ assigns a set of atomic propositions to each state. A state satisfies the formula $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G} \; (x < 3)$ if **all** paths from it always stay in $L^{-1}(\{x<3\})$. The state $s_3$ would be a **counterexample** witness.

## 4.4 Fixpoint Iteration — Reachable State Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    x0["X0 empty set"]
    x1["X1 initial states"]
    x2["X2 plus Post of X1"]
    x3["X3 plus Post of X2"]
    xn["Xn = lfp of F fixed point"]
    xc["Xk plus 1 = Xk saturation"]

    x0 --> x1 --> x2 --> x3 --> xn --> xc

    out["Output: Reachable states of M"]
    xc --> out
```

## 4.5 Hoare Logic Proof Tree — Compositional Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["Prove: P pre S1 S2 R post"]

    sub1["Prove: P pre S1 Q mid"]
    sub2["Prove: Q mid S2 R post"]

    asn1["Assignment Axiom on S1"]
    asn2["Assignment Axiom on S2"]

    root --> sub1
    root --> sub2
    sub1 --> asn1
    sub2 --> asn2

    final["Conclusion: program correct"]
    asn1 --> final
    asn2 --> final
```

---
