# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Concepts)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between operational semantics and denotational semantics. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Operational Semantics | Denotational Semantics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Meaning** | Defined via an abstract *execution machine* using a transition relation. | Defined as a *mathematical function* from states to values. |
| **Representation** | Set of inference rules for $\rightarrow$ (or $\Downarrow$). | Compositional function $\llbracket e \rrbracket$. |
| **Example** | $\langle x+1, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle 5+1, \sigma \rangle$ | $\llbracket x+1 \rrbracket \sigma = \llbracket x \rrbracket \sigma + 1$ |
| **Verification Use** | Direct translation to a model checker / interpreter. | Used in compiler correctness, abstract interpretation. |

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Define a Kripke Structure. Why is it important in automated verification?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Kripke Structure** is a tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ where:
- $S$ is a finite set of *states*,
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of *initial* states,
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ is a *total* transition relation (every state has at least one successor), and
- $L : S \to \mathcal{P}(AP)$ is a *labelling function* that maps each state to the set of atomic propositions true in it.

**Importance:** It is the *semantic model* on which temporal logic formulas (LTL / CTL) are evaluated. A model checker takes (System, Specification) and translates the system into a Kripke structure and the specification into a formula, then decides whether $M \models \varphi$. It is the mathematical bridge that makes automated verification *possible*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** With suitable inference rules, define the **small-step (structural) operational semantics** for the language:
$$
e \;::=\; n \;\mid\; e_1 + e_2 \;\mid\; x
$$
Clearly show the **computation rule** and **congruence rule** for addition. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Prove by **structural induction** that for every expression $e$ and store $\sigma$, the small-step relation $\rightarrow$ is **deterministic**: if $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_1, \sigma_1 \rangle$ and $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_2, \sigma_2 \rangle$, then $e_1 = e_2$ and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The set of values is $V = \mathbb{Z}$. The set of stores is $\Sigma = \text{Var} \to \mathbb{Z}$. Configurations are pairs $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \in \text{Exp} \times \Sigma$.

**Inference Rules:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(CONST)} \quad & \frac{}{\langle n, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle n, \sigma \rangle} \\[6pt]
\text{(VAR)} \quad & \frac{}{\langle x, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \sigma(x), \sigma \rangle} \\[6pt]
\text{(ADD-1)} \quad & \frac{\langle e_1, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_1', \sigma \rangle}{\langle e_1 + e_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_1' + e_2, \sigma \rangle} \quad \text{(Congruence: left)} \\[6pt]
\text{(ADD-2)} \quad & \frac{\langle e_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_2', \sigma \rangle}{\langle v_1 + e_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle v_1 + e_2', \sigma \rangle} \quad \text{(Congruence: right)} \\[6pt]
\text{(ADD-VAL)} \quad & \frac{}{\langle n_1 + n_2, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle n_1 +_{\mathbb{Z}} n_2, \sigma \rangle} \quad \text{(Computation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Identification:**
- **Computation rule:** (ADD-VAL) — performs the actual integer addition in one step.
- **Congruence rules:** (ADD-1) and (ADD-2) — propagate a single sub-step of evaluation from a sub-expression to the whole expression. (CONST) and (VAR) are the *axioms* (no premises) for atomic expressions.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Statement:** $\forall e, \sigma$. If $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_1, \sigma_1 \rangle$ and $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_2, \sigma_2 \rangle$, then $e_1 = e_2$ and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$.

**Proof by Structural Induction on $e$:**

**Case 1:** $e = n$. Only rule (CONST) applies. $\langle n, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle n, \sigma \rangle$. So $e_1 = e_2 = n$ and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$. ✓

**Case 2:** $e = x$. Only rule (VAR) applies. $\langle x, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle \sigma(x), \sigma \rangle$. So $e_1 = e_2 = \sigma(x)$ and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$. ✓

**Case 3:** $e = e_L + e_R$. We consider the rule applied to derive $\langle e, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_1, \sigma_1 \rangle$:

- **Sub-case 3.1 — (ADD-1) was used:** Then $\langle e_L, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_1', \sigma \rangle$ and $e_1 = e_1' + e_R$, $\sigma_1 = \sigma$. The other derivation must also be by (ADD-1) (since (ADD-2) requires the LHS to be a value, and (ADD-VAL) requires both operands to be values, but $e_L$ is not a value). So $\langle e_L, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_2', \sigma \rangle$ and $e_2 = e_2' + e_R$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma$. By **induction hypothesis** on $e_L$: $e_1' = e_2'$ and $\sigma = \sigma$. Hence $e_1 = e_2$ and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$. ✓

- **Sub-case 3.2 — (ADD-2) was used:** Then $e_L$ is a value $v_1$, and $\langle e_R, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_1'', \sigma \rangle$ with $e_1 = v_1 + e_1''$. The other derivation must also start with $e_L$ as a value, so by (ADD-2): $\langle e_R, \sigma \rangle \rightarrow \langle e_2'', \sigma \rangle$ and $e_2 = v_1 + e_2''$. By induction on $e_R$: $e_1'' = e_2''$. Hence $e_1 = e_2$. ✓

- **Sub-case 3.3 — (ADD-VAL) was used:** Then $e_L = n_1, e_R = n_2$ are both values, and $e_1 = n_1 + n_2 = e_2$ with $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$. ✓

In all cases, the conclusion holds. **QED.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define a **Kripke Structure** and the **CTL satisfaction relation** $M, s \models \varphi$ for the operators $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}$, $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}$, $\mathbf{E}\mathbf{U}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Consider a Kripke structure $M$ with three states $\{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, initial state $s_0$, transitions $s_0 \to s_1$, $s_1 \to s_1$, $s_1 \to s_2$, $s_2 \to s_0$, and labels $L(s_0) = \{p\}$, $L(s_1) = \{q\}$, $L(s_2) = \{\}$. Determine whether the following hold:
- (i) $M, s_0 \models \mathbf{E}\mathbf{F}\, q$
- (ii) $M, s_0 \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\, (p \lor q)$
- (iii) $M, s_0 \models \mathbf{E}(p \mathbf{U}\, q)$

Show all reasoning. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Kripke Structure Definition:** A tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ as defined in §2.4. $R$ is *total*: $\forall s \in S, \exists s' \in S : (s, s') \in R$.

**CTL Satisfaction Relation $M, s \models \varphi$:**

Let $\pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \dots$ be a path starting at $s_0 = s$. We use $\pi^i$ to denote the suffix starting at index $i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M, s \models p &\iff p \in L(s) \quad (p \in AP) \\
M, s \models \neg \varphi &\iff M, s \not\models \varphi \\
M, s \models \varphi_1 \wedge \varphi_2 &\iff M, s \models \varphi_1 \text{ and } M, s \models \varphi_2 \\
M, s \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\varphi &\iff \forall s' \in S, (s, s') \in R \Rightarrow M, s' \models \varphi \\
M, s \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\varphi &\iff \forall \text{ paths } \pi \text{ from } s, \forall i \geq 0, M, \pi(i) \models \varphi \\
M, s \models \mathbf{E}(\varphi_1 \mathbf{U}\varphi_2) &\iff \exists \text{ path } \pi \text{ from } s, \exists j \geq 0 : M, \pi(j) \models \varphi_2 \text{ and } \forall 0 \leq i < j, M, \pi(i) \models \varphi_1
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The dual operators are derivable: $\mathbf{E}\mathbf{X}\varphi \equiv \neg \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}\neg \varphi$, and $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{F}\varphi \equiv \neg \mathbf{E}\mathbf{G}\neg \varphi$.

#### Model Solution to (b)

Given: $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$, $R = \{(s_0,s_1), (s_1,s_1), (s_1,s_2), (s_2,s_0)\}$, $L(s_0)=\{p\}, L(s_1)=\{q\}, L(s_2)=\emptyset$.

**(i) $M, s_0 \models \mathbf{E}\mathbf{F}\, q$ ?**

$\mathbf{E}\mathbf{F}\, q \equiv \mathbf{E}(\text{true} \mathbf{U}\, q)$. We need a path from $s_0$ that eventually visits a state where $q$ holds.
- Take path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_1, s_1, \dots$. At index 1, $\pi(1) = s_1$ and $L(s_1) = \{q\} \ni q$. So $\exists$ such a path. **SATISFIED: TRUE.**

**(ii) $M, s_0 \models \mathbf{A}\mathbf{G}\, (p \lor q)$ ?**

We need: for **every** path from $s_0$, **every** state on it satisfies $p \lor q$.
- Consider path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ (going $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_0$).
- At index 2, $\pi(2) = s_2$ and $L(s_2) = \emptyset$. So $p \lor q$ is false at $s_2$.
- Hence this path violates $\mathbf{G}(p \lor q)$. Since **all** paths must satisfy it, the formula fails. **SATISFIED: FALSE.**

> A counterexample trace is: $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to s_0 \to \dots$ (the cycle through $s_2$).

**(iii) $M, s_0 \models \mathbf{E}(p \mathbf{U}\, q)$ ?**

We need a path $\pi$ from $s_0$ and an index $j$ such that:
- $\pi(j) \models q$, and
- $\pi(i) \models p$ for all $0 \leq i < j$.

- Take $\pi = s_0, s_1, \dots$ At $i=0$, $\pi(0) = s_0$, $L(s_0) = \{p\}$, so $p$ holds. ✓
- At $i=j=1$, $\pi(1) = s_1$, $L(s_1) = \{q\}$, so $q$ holds. ✓
- The prefix (only $s_0$) satisfies $p$. ✓
- Hence the formula is satisfied. **SATISFIED: TRUE.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $\rightarrow$ (small-step) and $\Downarrow$ (big-step):** Examiners deduct heavily for mixing them. Always use the correct symbol.
> 2. **Forgetting totality in Kripke structures:** $R$ must be total (every state has at least one successor). A state with no successor is *not allowed* — students often forget dangling states.
> 3. **Path quantifier confusion in CTL:** $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{E}$ are *path quantifiers* (quantify over paths); $\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{U}, \mathbf{G}, \mathbf{F}$ are *state quantifiers on a path*. A common mistake is to swap them.
> 4. **Hoare triple pre-condition weakening:** The *Consequence Rule* is bidirectional: you can weaken the pre-condition and strengthen the post-condition. Forgetting the implication direction costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply the assignment axiom correctly:** The substitution $P[x \mapsto E]$ is the *syntactic* replacement, not an algebraic one. E.g., $\{x = 5\}\; y := x + 1 \;\{x = 5\}$ — the post-condition must be $y = 6$, *not* $x = 5$ (the variable $x$ is unchanged, but $y$ is now $x+1$).
> 6. **Induction on the wrong index:** When proving properties of small-step semantics, induction must be on the *structure* of $e$, not the *length* of derivation (this is structural, not natural, induction).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Semantics = formal meaning** of syntactic constructs. Without it, automated verification is impossible.
- **Three pillars:** Operational (how it runs), Denotational (what it computes), Axiomatic (what must hold).
- **Small-step SOS** uses $\rightarrow$ on configurations; **big-step** uses $\Downarrow$ from expression to value.
- **Inference rules** have premises (above the line) and a conclusion (below the line). Computation rules and congruence rules must be distinguished.
- **Kripke Structure** $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ is the universal model for CTL/LTL model checking. $R$ is total.
- **LTL** reasons about a *single path*: $\mathbf{F}, \mathbf{G}, \mathbf{U}, \mathbf{X}$ with no path quantifier.
- **CTL** reasons about *branching*: every temporal operator must be preceded by $\mathbf{A}$ or $\mathbf{E}$ (paired form).
- **Hoare Triple** $\{P\}C\{Q\}$: $P$ is pre-condition, $Q$ is post-condition. The **assignment axiom** is $\{P[x \mapsto E]\}\; x := E \;\{P\}$.
- **Partial vs Total Correctness:** Partial = if terminates, $Q$ holds. Total = $C$ *must* terminate and $Q$ holds.
- **Least fixpoint** $\text{lfp}(F) = \bigcup_{i \geq 0} F^i(\emptyset)$ computes reachable states. The Kleene chain always stabilizes for *finite-state* systems.
- **Compositionality** is the defining feature of denotational semantics: meaning of a phrase is built from meanings of its parts.
- **Equivalence proof** between operational and denotational semantics is by **structural induction** on the syntax of $e$ — this is a *guaranteed* 14-mark question pattern.
- Always check: **Is the system finite-state?** If yes, model checking is decidable and terminates. If no, fixpoints may diverge and you need **abstract interpretation** or **inductive invariants**.
- Exam mantra: **"State the semantics formally, prove by structural induction, apply the relevant rule, conclude with a QED-style box."**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence of LTL Formulae
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Equivalence of LTL Formulae # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of LTL Equivalence **Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)** is a modal temporal logic used to reason about the infinite sequences of states generated by a concurrent system. Two LTL formulae $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are said to be **semantically equivalent** (denoted $\varphi \equiv \psi$) if and only if they are satisfied by exactly the same set of infinite $\omega$-words (paths) over the underlying Kripke structure's propositional alphabet $2^{AP}$. Formally, given a path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ over a Kripke structure $M$: $$\varphi \equiv \psi \iff \forall \pi \in \text{Paths}(M) : \pi \models \varphi \iff \pi \models \psi$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Boards-Ready):** > Two LTL formulae $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are **equivalent** if every model that satisfies $\varphi$ also satisfies $\psi$, and every model that satisfies $\psi$ also satisfies $\varphi$. That is, for all paths $\pi$: > $$\varphi \equiv \psi \iff (\pi \models \varphi) \iff (\pi \models \psi)$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are writing an **employment contract** in two different languages, say English and Malayalam. Both versions of the contract describe the *exact same legal obligations and rights*. Although the words and sentences look different on paper, **their legal meaning is identical**. No matter what scenario (workplace situation) you test them against, both documents will be considered "true" or "false" together. LTL equivalence works exactly like this. Two temporal logic formulae may have *completely different syntactic structures* — different temporal operators, different nested sub-formulae — yet they are **logically indistinguishable** on any execution trace. They are the "same contract in two languages." | English Contract (Formula A) | Malayalam Contract (Formula B) | | :--- | :--- | | $G(\text{request} \rightarrow F(\text{grant}))$ | $G(\neg\text{request}) \lor G(\text{request} \rightarrow F(\text{grant}))$ | | "Every request is eventually granted" | "Either no requests ever occur, or every request is eventually granted" | | ✅ Same meaning | ✅ Same meaning | ## 1.3 Why LTL Equivalence Matters in Automated Verification > [!NOTE] > In **model checking**, an LTL formula $\varphi$ is converted into a Büchi automaton $\mathcal{B}_{\neg\varphi}$. The size of this automaton (number of states) is **exponential** in the length of $\varphi$. By applying equivalence-based reductions **before** automaton construction, the state-space explosion can be **drastically mitigated** — often from $2^{n}$ states down to a small polynomial. This makes equivalence a **board-favorite high-yield topic**. Key Engineering Metrics in the KTU Syllabus: - **Expressive completeness** of LTL: **$2^{O(|\varphi|)}$** states in the resulting Büchi automaton. - **Canonical form** (Negation Normal Form) ensures uniqueness up to equivalence. - **Soundness & Completeness** of the equivalence system must be provable. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Categories of LTL Equivalences The set of LTL equivalences can be logically grouped into **eight distinct families**, each capturing a specific structural transformation. ### 2.1.1 Propositional Logic Foundations (Base Layer) These hold because LTL extends propositional logic, and the temporal layer does not alter Boolean identities. | Law | Equivalence | | :--- | :--- | | Idempotence (∨) | $\varphi \lor \varphi \equiv \varphi$ | | Idempotence (∧) | $\varphi \land \varphi \equiv \varphi$ | | Commutativity (∨) | $\varphi \lor \psi \equiv \psi \lor \varphi$ | | Commutativity (∧) | $\varphi \land \psi \equiv \psi \land \varphi$ | | Associativity (∨) | $(\varphi \lor \psi) \lor \rho \equiv \varphi \lor (\psi \lor \rho)$ | | Associativity (∧) | $(\varphi \land \psi) \land \rho \equiv \varphi \land (\psi \land \rho)$ | | Distributivity | $\varphi \lor (\psi \land \rho) \equiv (\varphi \lor \psi) \land (\varphi \lor \rho)$ | | De Morgan | $\neg(\varphi \lor \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi \land \neg\psi$ | | De Morgan | $\neg(\varphi \land \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi \lor \neg\psi$ | | Double Negation | $\neg\neg\varphi \equiv \varphi$ | | Excluded Middle | $\varphi \lor \neg\varphi \equiv \top$ | | Contradiction | $\varphi \land \neg\varphi \equiv \bot$ | | Implication | $\varphi \rightarrow \psi \equiv \neg\varphi \lor \psi$ | ### 2.1.2 Fixed-Point Expansion Laws (The Heart of LTL) These are the **most exam-critical** equivalences, derived from the fixed-point semantics of $U$ and $R$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Until and Release** are the **canonical** LTL operators. Every other temporal operator can be expressed as a derived form of these two. The fixed-point expansions use the **next-time operator $X$** as the recursive descent mechanism. | Operator | Fixed-Point Expansion | | :--- | :--- | | Until ($\varphi U \psi$) | $\varphi U \psi \equiv \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$ | | Release ($\varphi R \psi$) | $\varphi R \psi \equiv \psi \land (\varphi \lor X(\varphi R \psi))$ | ### 2.1.3 Duality Laws (Negation-Operator Pairs) | Duality | Equivalence | | :--- | :--- | | Next | $\neg X \varphi \equiv X \neg \varphi$ | | Until ↔ Release | $\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv (\neg\varphi) R (\neg\psi)$ | | Release ↔ Until | $\neg(\varphi R \psi) \equiv (\neg\varphi) U (\neg\psi)$ | | Globally ↔ Finally | $\neg G \varphi \equiv F \neg\varphi$ | | Finally ↔ Globally | $\neg F \varphi \equiv G \neg\varphi$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv (\neg\varphi) U (\neg\psi)$. This is **FALSE**. The correct dual of $U$ is $R$ (Release), not $U$. This single error costs 2 marks in Part B derivations. ### 2.1.4 Derived Operator Abbreviations | Derived Form | Definition | | :--- | :--- | | Finally ($F \varphi$) | $F\varphi \equiv \top U \varphi$ | | Globally ($G \varphi$) | $G\varphi \equiv \bot R \varphi$ | | Weak Until ($\varphi W \psi$) | $\varphi W \psi \equiv (\varphi U \psi) \lor G\varphi$ | | Strong Release ($\varphi M \psi$) | $\varphi M \psi \equiv (\varphi R \psi) \land F\varphi$ | ### 2.1.5 Idempotent / Absorption Properties of Temporal Operators | Property | Equivalence | | :--- | :--- | | $U$ Idempotence | $\varphi U \varphi \equiv \varphi$ | | $R$ Idempotence | $\varphi R \varphi \equiv \varphi$ | | $F$ Idempotence | $F F \varphi \equiv F \varphi$ | | $G$ Idempotence | $G G \varphi \equiv G \varphi$ | | $X$ Idempotence | $X X \varphi \equiv X X \varphi$ (no simplification) | | $U$ Absorption (left) | $(\varphi U \psi) U \psi \equiv \varphi U \psi$ | | $U$ Absorption (right) | $\varphi U (\varphi U \psi) \equiv \varphi U \psi$ | ### 2.1.6 Distributive Properties | Distributive Law | Equivalence | | :--- | :--- | | $U$ over ∨ | $(\varphi U \psi) \lor (\varphi U \rho) \equiv \varphi U (\psi \lor \rho)$ | | $U$ over ∧ (when $\psi$ releases) | $(\varphi U \psi) \land (\varphi U \rho) \equiv \varphi U (\psi \land \rho)$ (if $\psi \lor \rho$ is reachable) | | $G$ over ∧ | $G\varphi \land G\psi \equiv G(\varphi \land \psi)$ | | $F$ over ∨ | $F\varphi \lor F\psi \equiv F(\varphi \lor \psi)$ | | $G$ distributes over → | $G(\varphi \rightarrow \psi) \equiv G(\neg\varphi \lor \psi)$ | ### 2.1.7 Implication and Release Special Cases | Property | Equivalence | | :--- | :--- | | $\top$ as $U$ identity | $\top U \varphi \equiv F\varphi$ | | $\bot$ as $R$ identity | $\bot R \varphi \equiv G\varphi$ | | $\top$ as $W$ identity | $\varphi W \top \equiv G\varphi$ | ### 2.1.8 Closure Properties The set of LTL formulae is **closed under**: - Boolean connectives: $\neg, \land, \lor$ - Temporal operators: $X, U, R$ (and their derived $F, G$) - Negation Normal Form (NNF) transformation: every LTL formula $\varphi$ has an equivalent NNF form $\text{nnf}(\varphi)$ with negation pushed to atomic propositions only. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equivalence Name | Formula | KTU Priority | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $U$ Expansion | $\varphi U \psi \equiv \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 2 | $R$ Expansion | $\varphi R \psi \equiv \psi \land (\varphi \lor X(\varphi R \psi))$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 3 | $F$ Expansion | $F\varphi \equiv \varphi \lor XF\varphi$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 4 | $G$ Expansion | $G\varphi \equiv \varphi \land XG\varphi$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 5 | $U \leftrightarrow R$ Duality | $\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi R \neg\psi$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 6 | $F \leftrightarrow G$ Duality | $\neg F\varphi \equiv G\neg\varphi$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | | 7 | $F$ via $U$ | $F\varphi \equiv \top U \varphi$ | ⭐⭐ | | 8 | $G$ via $R$ | $G\varphi \equiv \bot R \varphi$ | ⭐⭐ | | 9 | $F$ Idempotence | $FF\varphi \equiv F\varphi$ | ⭐⭐ | | 10 | $G$ Idempotence | $GG\varphi \equiv G\varphi$ | ⭐⭐ | | 11 | $U$ Absorption | $\varphi U (\varphi U \psi) \equiv \varphi U \psi$ | ⭐⭐ | | 12 | $W$ to $U/G$ | $\varphi W \psi \equiv (\varphi U \psi) \lor G\varphi$ | ⭐ | | 13 | $X$ over $\neg$ | $\neg X\varphi \equiv X\neg\varphi$ | ⭐ | | 14 | $G$ over $\land$ | $G\varphi \land G\psi \equiv G(\varphi \land \psi)$ | ⭐ | | 15 | $F$ over $\lor$ | $F\varphi \lor F\psi \equiv F(\varphi \lor \psi)$ | ⭐ | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application of LTL Equivalence | | :--- | :--- | | **Hardware Verification (RTL)** | Equivalence checking between specification $\varphi_1$ and optimized implementation $\varphi_2$ for sequential circuits. | | **Protocol Verification** | Proving $G(\text{req} \rightarrow F \text{ack}) \equiv G \text{req} \rightarrow G F \text{ack}$ (causal vs. global eventual response). | | **Software Model Checking** | LTL equivalence reduction in **SPIN**, **NuSMV**, and **Cadence** model checkers to reduce Büchi automaton size. | | **Cyber-Physical Systems** | Equivalence between discrete LTL and hybrid-time logic specifications for control synthesis. | | **AI Planning** | Translating LTL goals into equivalent DFA (Deterministic Finite Automaton) representations for plan synthesis. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Theorem 1: Fixed-Point Expansion of Until > **Claim:** $\varphi U \psi \equiv \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$ ### Proof by Semantic Argument (KTU Preferred Style) Let $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ be an arbitrary infinite path. We must show: $$\pi \models \varphi U \psi \iff \pi \models \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$$ **($\Rightarrow$ Direction — Forward Implication)** Assume $\pi \models \varphi U \psi$. By the standard LTL semantics, this means $\exists j \geq 0$ such that: - $\pi^j \models \psi$, and - $\forall k : 0 \leq k < j \Rightarrow \pi^k \models \varphi$. We now consider **two exhaustive sub-cases** based on the value of $j$. **Case 1: $j = 0$.** This means $\pi^0 \models \psi$ (i.e., $s_0 \models \psi$). Therefore, the disjunct $\psi$ is true at the first state, and: $$\pi \models \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$$ **Case 2: $j \geq 1$.** Then $s_0 \models \varphi$ (since $0 < j$). Define the suffix path $\pi' = s_1, s_2, s_3, \ldots$. By definition of $U$ on the suffix $\pi'$ starting from index $j-1$: $$\pi' \models \varphi U \psi$$ Now, applying the LTL semantics of the **Next** operator: $\pi \models X(\varphi U \psi)$ iff the suffix of $\pi$ from $s_1$ onward satisfies $\varphi U \psi$. This is precisely $\pi' \models \varphi U \psi$, which holds by the case assumption. Combined with $s_0 \models \varphi$: $$\pi \models \varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi) \implies \pi \models \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$$ **($\Leftarrow$ Direction — Reverse Implication)** Assume $\pi \models \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$. Either: - **Sub-case (a):** $\pi \models \psi$. Then taking $j = 0$, the witness is immediate, so $\pi \models \varphi U \psi$. ∎ - **Sub-case (b):** $\pi \models \varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi)$. Then $s_0 \models \varphi$, and the suffix $\pi' = s_1, s_2, \ldots$ satisfies $\pi' \models \varphi U \psi$. This means $\exists j' \geq 0$ with $\pi'^{j'} \models \psi$ and the prefix conditions on $\pi'$. Translating back, $\exists j = j' + 1 \geq 1$ with $\pi^j \models \psi$ and $\pi^k \models \varphi$ for $0 \leq k < j$. Thus $\pi \models \varphi U \psi$. ∎ $$\boxed{\therefore \varphi U \psi \equiv \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))}$$ ## 3.2 Theorem 2: Duality of $U$ and $R$ > **Claim:** $\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi R \neg\psi$ ### Proof by Duality Chain $$\begin{aligned} \neg(\varphi U \psi) &\equiv \neg[\psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))] \quad &\text{(by $U$ expansion, Theorem 1)}\\ &\equiv \neg\psi \land \neg(\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi)) \quad &\text{(by De Morgan's Law)}\\ &\equiv \neg\psi \land (\neg\varphi \lor \neg X(\varphi U \psi)) \quad &\text{(by De Morgan's Law)}\\ &\equiv \neg\psi \land (\neg\varphi \lor X \neg(\varphi U \psi)) \quad &\text{(by $X$ over $\neg$ equivalence)}\\ &\equiv \neg\psi \land (\neg\varphi \lor X(\neg\varphi R \neg\psi)) \quad &\text{(by induction on formula structure)} \end{aligned}$$ Now we observe that the right-hand side is precisely the **Release expansion** applied to $\neg\varphi R \neg\psi$. Recall: $$\varphi R \psi \equiv \psi \land (\varphi \lor X(\varphi R \psi))$$ Substituting $\varphi \mapsto \neg\varphi$ and $\psi \mapsto \neg\psi$: $$\neg\varphi R \neg\psi \equiv \neg\psi \land (\neg\varphi \lor X(\neg\varphi R \neg\psi))$$ This matches our derived expression exactly. Therefore: $$\boxed{\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi R \neg\psi}$$ ## 3.3 Theorem 3: $F$ and $G$ Mutual Duality > **Claim:** $\neg F \varphi \equiv G \neg\varphi$ ### Proof $$\begin{aligned} \neg F \varphi &\equiv \neg(\top U \varphi) \quad &\text{(by definition } F\varphi \equiv \top U \varphi\text{)}\\ &\equiv \neg\top R \neg\varphi \quad &\text{(by Theorem 2: $U \leftrightarrow R$ duality)}\\ &\equiv \bot R \neg\varphi \quad &\text{(since } \neg\top \equiv \bot\text{)}\\ &\equiv G \neg\varphi \quad &\text{(by definition } G\varphi \equiv \bot R \varphi\text{)} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \neg F \varphi \equiv G \neg\varphi}$$ ## 3.4 Theorem 4: $F$ Idempotence ($FF\varphi \equiv F\varphi$) ### Proof by Semantic Argument We prove $\pi \models FF\varphi \iff \pi \models F\varphi$. **($\Rightarrow$):** $\pi \models FF\varphi$ means $\exists j \geq 0$ such that $\pi^j \models F\varphi$. The latter means $\exists k \geq 0$ such that $\pi^{j+k} \models \varphi$. Letting $i = j + k \geq 0$, we have $\pi^i \models \varphi$, so $\pi \models F\varphi$. ∎ **($\Leftarrow$):** $\pi \models F\varphi$ means $\exists i \geq 0$ with $\pi^i \models \varphi$. Let $j = 0$. Then $\pi^0 \models F\varphi$ (since the path eventually reaches $\varphi$ at index $i$). Therefore $\pi \models FF\varphi$. ∎ $$\boxed{\therefore FF\varphi \equiv F\varphi}$$ ## 3.5 Theorem 5: $G$ Distributes over $\land$ > **Claim:** $G\varphi \land G\psi \equiv G(\varphi \land \psi)$ ### Proof $$\begin{aligned} G\varphi \land G\psi &\equiv (\bot R \varphi) \land (\bot R \psi) \quad &\text{(by } G \text{ definition)}\\ &\equiv \bot R (\varphi \land \psi) \quad &\text{(by $R$ distributive law over } \land\text{)}\\ &\equiv G(\varphi \land \psi) \quad &\text{(by } G \text{ definition)} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.6 Worked Example: Reducing a Complex LTL Formula > **Problem:** Reduce $\neg F(\varphi \rightarrow \psi)$ to a simpler equivalent form using NNF and equivalences. **Step 1:** Push the outermost negation using duality. $$\neg F(\varphi \rightarrow \psi) \equiv G \neg(\varphi \rightarrow \psi)$$ **Step 2:** Eliminate the implication inside the $G$. $$G \neg(\varphi \rightarrow \psi) \equiv G \neg(\neg\varphi \lor \psi) \equiv G(\varphi \land \neg\psi)$$ **Step 3:** Apply $G$ distribution is not possible here since the form is already a $G$ over a conjunction — this is the **canonical reduced form**. $$\boxed{\neg F(\varphi \rightarrow \psi) \equiv G(\varphi \land \neg\psi)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification of Reduction Correctness:** > The original formula says "it is **not** the case that eventually $\varphi$ implies $\psi$." The reduction $G(\varphi \land \neg\psi)$ says "globally, $\varphi$ is true and $\psi$ is false." These are logically the same — a permanent violation of the implication. ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Operator Equivalence Map The following matrix summarizes the **mutual duality and reduction** of every LTL operator. Each cell shows the equivalent dual form. | Operator | $\neg$ Applied | Dual Operator | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $X \varphi$ | $\neg X \varphi$ | $X \neg\varphi$ | | $\varphi U \psi$ | $\neg(\varphi U \psi)$ | $\neg\varphi R \neg\psi$ | | $\varphi R \psi$ | $\neg(\varphi R \psi)$ | $\neg\varphi U \neg\psi$ | | $F \varphi$ | $\neg F \varphi$ | $G \neg\varphi$ | | $G \varphi$ | $\neg G \varphi$ | $F \neg\varphi$ | | $\varphi W \psi$ | $\neg(\varphi W \psi)$ | $\neg\varphi M \neg\psi$ | | $\varphi M \psi$ | $\neg(\varphi M \psi)$ | $\neg\varphi W \neg\psi$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: LTL Operator Duality Network The following diagram visualizes the **complete duality relationship** between all canonical LTL operators. Each arrow represents an equivalence transformation, and operator boxes are color-coded by category. ```mermaid graph TD A["Boolean Core: phi, psi"] --> B["X phi
Next"] A --> C["phi U psi
Until"] A --> D["phi R psi
Release"] C -->|equiv F phi| E["F phi = top U phi
Finally"] D -->|equiv G phi| F["G phi = bot R phi
Globally"] B -->|neg equiv| G["X neg phi"] C -->|neg equiv| H["neg phi R neg psi"] D -->|neg equiv| I["neg phi U neg psi"] E -->|neg equiv| J["G neg phi"] F -->|neg equiv| K["F neg phi"] H -.dismantles.-> D I -.dismantles.-> C J -.dismantles.-> F K -.dismantles.-> E E -->|idempotent| L["FF phi equiv F phi"] F -->|idempotent| M["GG phi equiv G phi"] C -->|absorbs| N["phi U phi U psi equiv phi U psi"] D -->|absorbs| O["phi R phi R psi equiv phi R psi"] classDef canonical fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0d3d6e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef derived fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#a04a00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef negated fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#155115,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef reduced fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a1313,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px class B,C,D canonical class E,F derived class G,H,I,J,K negated class L,M,N,O reduced ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Equivalence Reduction Pipeline This block-level flow chart depicts the **standard KTU-style reduction algorithm** applied to an arbitrary LTL formula before Büchi automaton construction. ```mermaid flowchart TD P0["Step 0: Input
Arbitrary LTL Formula phi"] P1["Step 1: Eliminate Implications
phi to neg phi OR psi"] P2["Step 2: Push Negations Inward
NNF Transformation"] P3["Step 3: Eliminate Derived Operators
F to top U, G to bot R"] P4["Step 4: Apply Idempotence
FF phi to F phi, GG phi to G phi"] P5["Step 5: Apply Absorption
phi U phi U psi to phi U psi"] P6["Step 6: Distributive Reduction
G phi AND G psi to G phi AND psi"] P7["Step 7: Canonical NNF Formula
nnf phi"] P0 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 P6 --> P7 P7 --> Q0["Output: Reduced Formula
Size drops from 2 to the power n to O of n"] P7 --> Q1["Output: Convert to GNBA
Generalized NBW"] classDef process fill:#9467bd,stroke:#3a1f5c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#8c564b,stroke:#3a1d17,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px class P0,P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 process class Q0,Q1 output ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Procedure for LTL Equivalence This schematic illustrates how two LTL formulae are tested for semantic equivalence using the **automata-theoretic approach** — a board-favorite sub-topic in KTU Module 3. ```mermaid graph LR IN1["LTL Formula phi"] --> N1["Convert to NBA
B sub neg phi"] IN2["LTL Formula psi"] --> N2["Convert to NBA
B sub neg psi"] N1 --> R1["Reverse B sub neg phi
to get B sub phi"] N2 --> C1["Construct B sub psi"] R1 --> P["Product Construction
B sub phi intersect B sub psi"] C1 --> P P --> CHK["Emptiness Check
Language of Product empty?"] CHK -->|YES| OUT1["Equivalence Holds
phi equiv psi"] CHK -->|NO| OUT2["Counterexample Path
phi not equiv psi"] classDef input fill:#17becf,stroke:#0a5e6e,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef process fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#5e5e0a,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px classDef decision fill:#e377c2,stroke:#7a3a6e,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef output fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#333333,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px class IN1,IN2 input class N1,N2,R1,C1,P process class CHK decision class OUT1,OUT2 output ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023] > **Q: Define semantic equivalence of two LTL formulae. State any four standard LTL equivalences involving the $U$ (Until) operator.** **Model Answer:** Two LTL formulae $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are **semantically equivalent** (written $\varphi \equiv \psi$) if and only if for every infinite path $\pi$ over the Kripke structure: $$\pi \models \varphi \iff \pi \models \psi$$ This means both formulae are satisfied by **exactly the same set of paths**, even if their syntactic structure differs. **Four standard LTL equivalences involving Until ($U$):** 1. **Fixed-point expansion:** $\varphi U \psi \equiv \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$ 2. **Idempotence:** $\varphi U \varphi \equiv \varphi$ 3. **Absorption (right):** $\varphi U (\varphi U \psi) \equiv \varphi U \psi$ 4. **Duality with Release:** $\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi R \neg\psi$ **Marking Distribution:** Definition (1.5) + Equivalences (1.5) = **3 marks**. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] > **Q: State and prove the LTL equivalence $F\varphi \equiv \varphi \lor XF\varphi$.** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** For any LTL formula $\varphi$, the "Finally" operator satisfies: $$F\varphi \equiv \varphi \lor XF\varphi$$ **Proof:** Using the definition $F\varphi \equiv \top U \varphi$ and the fixed-point expansion of Until ($\varphi U \psi \equiv \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$): $$\begin{aligned} F\varphi &\equiv \top U \varphi \\ &\equiv \varphi \lor (\top \land X(\top U \varphi)) \\ &\equiv \varphi \lor X(\top U \varphi) \\ &\equiv \varphi \lor XF\varphi \end{aligned}$$ This equivalence captures the **least fixed-point** semantics of $F$: "$\varphi$ holds eventually" if and only if "$\varphi$ holds now, or it holds eventually from the next state onward." **Marking Distribution:** Statement (1) + Proof (2) = **3 marks**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] > **Q (a)** [7 Marks] **Prove the LTL equivalence: $G\varphi \equiv \varphi \land XG\varphi$. State the corresponding expansion for $\varphi R \psi$ and explain the connection.** > **Q (b)** [7 Marks] **Using LTL equivalences, reduce the formula $\neg F(p \rightarrow q)$ to Negation Normal Form (NNF) and hence to an equivalent form using only $U, R,$ and $X$ operators.** --- #### Model Solution for Q (a) **Claim:** $G\varphi \equiv \varphi \land XG\varphi$ **Proof by semantic analysis:** We prove both directions. **($\Rightarrow$) Assume $\pi \models G\varphi$.** By LTL semantics, this means $\forall j \geq 0 : \pi^j \models \varphi$. - In particular, taking $j = 0$: $\pi^0 \models \varphi$, i.e., the first state satisfies $\varphi$. - Considering the suffix path $\pi' = s_1, s_2, \ldots$ and the original $G\varphi$ condition for all $j \geq 1$, we have $\forall j \geq 1 : \pi^j \models \varphi$, which is exactly $\pi' \models G\varphi$. Therefore $\pi \models XG\varphi$. Combining: $\pi \models \varphi \land XG\varphi$. ∎ **($\Leftarrow$) Assume $\pi \models \varphi \land XG\varphi$.** This means $s_0 \models \varphi$ and the suffix $\pi' \models G\varphi$, i.e., $\forall j \geq 1 : \pi^j \models \varphi$. Together with $s_0 \models \varphi$, we get $\forall j \geq 0 : \pi^j \models \varphi$, which is precisely $\pi \models G\varphi$. ∎ $$\boxed{\therefore G\varphi \equiv \varphi \land XG\varphi}$$ **Connection to Release ($\varphi R \psi$):** Since $G\varphi$ is defined as $\bot R \varphi$ (Release with $\bot$ as the first operand), the $G$ expansion mirrors the $R$ fixed-point expansion: $$\varphi R \psi \equiv \psi \land (\varphi \lor X(\varphi R \psi))$$ Substituting $\varphi \mapsto \bot$ and $\psi \mapsto \varphi$: $$\bot R \varphi \equiv \varphi \land (\bot \lor X(\bot R \varphi)) \equiv \varphi \land X(\bot R \varphi) \equiv \varphi \land XG\varphi$$ This confirms $G$ is a **special case of Release**. **Marking Distribution:** Forward proof (2.5) + Reverse proof (2.5) + Connection (2) = **7 marks**. --- #### Model Solution for Q (b) **Goal:** Reduce $\neg F(p \rightarrow q)$ using LTL equivalences, ending with only $U, R, X$. **Step 1:** Apply duality $\neg F \equiv G \neg$: $$\neg F(p \rightarrow q) \equiv G \neg(p \rightarrow q)$$ **Step 2:** Eliminate implication ($p \rightarrow q \equiv \neg p \lor q$): $$G \neg(p \rightarrow q) \equiv G \neg(\neg p \lor q) \equiv G(p \land \neg q)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $G \equiv \bot R$: $$G(p \land \neg q) \equiv \bot R (p \land \neg q)$$ **Final Reduced Form:** $$\boxed{\neg F(p \rightarrow q) \equiv G(p \land \neg q) \equiv \bot R (p \land \neg q)}$$ **Intuition:** "It is not the case that $p$ implies $q$ eventually" is the same as "globally, we have $p$ true and $q$ false." **Marking Distribution:** Duality (2) + Implication (2) + NNF (1.5) + Final form with only $U/R/X$ (1.5) = **7 marks**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] > **Q (a)** [7 Marks] **State and prove the LTL duality: $\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi R \neg\psi$. Use the fixed-point expansion of Until as the starting point.** > **Q (b)** [7 Marks] **Reduce the LTL formula $G(p \rightarrow Fq)$ to an equivalent form that uses no $G$ or $F$ operators. Show every step with the equivalence applied.** --- #### Model Solution for Q (a) **Claim:** $\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi R \neg\psi$ **Proof:** Starting from the fixed-point expansion of Until: $$\varphi U \psi \equiv \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$$ Apply negation to both sides: $$\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg[\psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))]$$ Apply De Morgan's law: $$\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\psi \land \neg(\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$$ Apply De Morgan's law to the inner conjunction: $$\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\psi \land (\neg\varphi \lor \neg X(\varphi U \psi))$$ Apply the $X$ over $\neg$ equivalence ($\neg X \alpha \equiv X \neg \alpha$): $$\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\psi \land (\neg\varphi \lor X \neg(\varphi U \psi))$$ By **structural induction** (assuming the equivalence holds for $\varphi$ and $\psi$ in the recursive position of $X$): $$\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\psi \land (\neg\varphi \lor X(\neg\varphi R \neg\psi))$$ The right-hand side is precisely the **fixed-point expansion of $\neg\varphi R \neg\psi$**, which by definition of Release is: $$\neg\varphi R \neg\psi \equiv \neg\psi \land (\neg\varphi \lor X(\neg\varphi R \neg\psi))$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi R \neg\psi} \quad \blacksquare$$ **Marking Distribution:** Starting equation (1) + De Morgan (2) + $X$ duality (1.5) + Induction closure (1.5) + Final conclusion (1) = **7 marks**. --- #### Model Solution for Q (b) **Goal:** Reduce $G(p \rightarrow Fq)$ to a form using only $U, R, X, \neg, \land, \lor$. **Step 1:** Substitute $G \equiv \bot R$: $$G(p \rightarrow Fq) \equiv \bot R (p \rightarrow Fq)$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $F \equiv \top U$ inside the implication: $$\bot R (p \rightarrow (\top U q))$$ **Step 3:** Eliminate the implication $p \rightarrow (\top U q) \equiv \neg p \lor (\top U q)$: $$\bot R (\neg p \lor (\top U q))$$ **Step 4:** Apply De Morgan-like distributive form for $R$ over $\lor$ (using the dual distributive law): $$\bot R (\neg p \lor (\top U q)) \equiv (\bot R \neg p) \lor (\bot R (\top U q))$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back $G$ and $G(\top U q) \equiv G F q$: $$\equiv G(\neg p) \lor G(\top U q) \equiv G\neg p \lor GFq$$ **Step 6:** Convert back to $R$ form: $$G\neg p \lor GFq \equiv \bot R \neg p \lor \bot R (\top U q)$$ **Final Reduced Form (using only $R, U, X, \neg, \lor$):** $$\boxed{G(p \rightarrow Fq) \equiv \bot R \neg p \lor \bot R (\top U q)}$$ **Alternative cleaner form using $U$ and $R$:** $$G(p \rightarrow Fq) \equiv (\bot R \neg p) \lor G F q$$ **Marking Distribution:** $G$ substitution (1.5) + $F$ substitution (1.5) + Implication elimination (1.5) + Distributive step (1.5) + Final form (1) = **7 marks**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Negation-Duality Confusion (Most Common Error — 2 marks lost):** Students frequently write $\neg(\varphi U \psi) \equiv \neg\varphi U \neg\psi$. This is **FALSE**. The correct dual of $U$ is $R$, not $U$. Always remember: $U$ and $R$ are **negation duals**, just like $F$ and $G$. > > 2. **Fixed-Point Expansion Direction (1 mark lost):** The correct expansion is $\varphi U \psi \equiv \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$. Students sometimes write it as $\psi \land (\varphi \lor X(\varphi U \psi))$, which is the **Release** expansion, not Until. > > 3. **Forgetting the $X$ Operator (1 mark lost):** When applying fixed-point expansions, the recursion is mediated by $X$. Omitting $X$ produces a non-equivalent formula that fails the second state onwards. > > 4. **Confusing $W$ and $U$:** Weak Until $W$ allows $\varphi$ to hold globally without ever reaching $\psi$, while strong Until $U$ requires $\psi$ to eventually hold. The equivalence $\varphi W \psi \equiv (\varphi U \psi) \lor G\varphi$ is **frequently miswritten** as $\varphi W \psi \equiv \varphi U \psi$ — a 1.5 mark deduction in Part B. > > 5. **Skipping Steps in Proofs (Up to 2 marks lost):** KTU examiners expect a **chain of equivalences with the law cited at each step**. Writing "after applying De Morgan's law we get..." without showing the intermediate expression loses credit. **Always write the formula after each transformation**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Semantic equivalence** of $\varphi$ and $\psi$ requires that for **every** path $\pi$: $\pi \models \varphi \iff \pi \models \psi$. This is a **stronger** condition than mutual implication. - ✅ The **canonical LTL operators** are $U$ (Until) and $R$ (Release). All others ($X, F, G, W, M$) are **derived operators**. - ✅ **Fixed-point expansion** of $U$: $\varphi U \psi \equiv \psi \lor (\varphi \land X(\varphi U \psi))$ — uses **least fixed-point** semantics. - ✅ **Fixed-point expansion** of $R$: $\varphi R \psi \equiv \psi \land (\varphi \lor X(\varphi R \psi))$ — uses **greatest fixed-point** semantics. - ✅ **Duality map:** $U \leftrightarrow R$, $F \leftrightarrow G$, $W \leftrightarrow M$ under negation. - ✅ **Idempotence table:** $FF\varphi \equiv F\varphi$, $GG\varphi \equiv G\varphi$, $\varphi U \varphi \equiv \varphi$, $\varphi R \varphi \equiv \varphi$. - ✅ **Absorption laws:** $\varphi U (\varphi U \psi) \equiv \varphi U \psi$, $(\varphi U \psi) U \psi \equiv \varphi U \psi$. - ✅ **Distributive laws:** $F(\varphi \lor \psi) \equiv F\varphi \lor F\psi$, $G(\varphi \land \psi) \equiv G\varphi \land G\psi$. - ✅ **$X$ over $\neg$:** $\neg X\varphi \equiv X\neg\varphi$ — the Next operator **commutes** with negation. - ✅ **Derived operator definitions:** $F\varphi \equiv \top U \varphi$ and $G\varphi \equiv \bot R \varphi$. - ✅ **Negation Normal Form (NNF):** Every LTL formula can be transformed into an equivalent form where negation appears **only on atomic propositions**. This is essential for Büchi automaton construction. - ✅ **Equivalence checking complexity:** Deciding LTL equivalence is **PSPACE-complete** (a board-favorite fact). - ✅ **Practical use:** Equivalence reduction shrinks the Büchi automaton from $2^{O(n)}$ states to a much smaller manageable size, **directly addressing the state-space explosion** in model checking. - ✅ **Equivalence vs. Satisfiability:** Equivalence is **reducible to satisfiability** via the identity $\varphi \equiv \psi \iff (\varphi \land \neg\psi) \lor (\psi \land \neg\varphi) \text{ is unsatisfiable}$. - ✅ **Counterexample method:** If $\varphi \not\equiv \psi$, the language $\mathcal{L}(\varphi) \oplus \mathcal{L}(\psi)$ (symmetric difference) is non-empty, and an **accepting path** in the product NBA serves as a **witness** distinguishing the two formulae.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Weak Until

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Weak Until: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the temporal logics **CTL (Computation Tree Logic)** and **LTL (Linear Temporal Logic)**, used for hardware/software verification, **Weak Until** is a binary temporal connective denoted by the operator **W**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Weak Until (W) — Formal Syntax & Semantics**  
> Let $f$ and $g$ be CTL/LTL state formulas. The **Weak Until** formula is written as:  
> $$\phi \; ::= \; f \;\mathbf{W}\; g$$  
> **Semantics (on a Kripke structure $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$):**  
> A state $s \in S$ satisfies $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ (denoted $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$) **if and only if** there exists a path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ starting at $s$ such that either:  
> 1. $g$ holds at some position $k \geq 0$ on the path, and $f$ holds at all positions $j < k$ on the path, **OR**  
> 2. $f$ holds at **every** position on the path (i.e., $g$ never holds — $f$ persists forever).

Mathematically, this is expressed as the disjunction:
$$f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \;\equiv\; (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \;\lor\; \mathbf{G}\,f$$

where **U** is the *Strong Until* operator and **G** is the *Globally* (always) operator.

### 1.1.1 CTL Path-Quantified Variants

In CTL, Weak Until is prefixed with the path quantifiers **A** (for all paths) or **E** (there exists a path):

| Operator | Meaning | Interpretation |
|----------|---------|----------------|
| $\mathbf{E}[f \,\mathbf{W}\, g]$ | There Exists a path where $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ holds | At least one execution trace satisfies the weak-until condition |
| $\mathbf{A}[f \,\mathbf{W}\, g]$ | For All paths, $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ holds | Every possible execution trace satisfies the weak-until condition |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why the name "Weak"?**  
> The operator is termed *weak* because the **right-hand side formula $g$ is NOT obliged to ever become true**. The obligation on $f$ to "wait" is relaxed — $f$ is allowed to hold *indefinitely* without $g$ ever appearing. In contrast, **Strong Until** ($\mathbf{U}$) *demands* that $g$ eventually holds.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **waiting at a bus stop** for your friend to arrive, but you have an **unbreakable appointment** at 6 PM.

- **Strong Until ($f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$)** is like saying: *"I will keep waiting **until** my friend shows up, AND my friend **must** show up."* If the friend never arrives, the promise is *broken*.

- **Weak Until ($f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$)** is like saying: *"I will keep waiting **until** my friend shows up. **OR**, if my friend never arrives, I will just keep waiting at the bus stop forever — that's also acceptable."* The promise is *not* broken if $g$ never happens.

**Another analogy — Traffic Light:**
Let $f$ = "light is red" and $g$ = "light turns green".  
- $f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$ says: "The light is red *until* it turns green, and it **must** turn green eventually."  
- $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ says: "The light is red *until* it turns green, *or* it just stays red forever (a broken light!), which is also fine."

In **automated verification** (e.g., verifying a CPU cache coherence protocol), this matters because some liveness properties (something *must* eventually happen) are too strict — you often only need **safety** guarantees (something *bad* never happens), which Weak Until expresses elegantly.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **CTL Hierarchy Placement:** Weak Until is a **monopole-free, future-only temporal operator** residing in the class of *invariant-type* connectives. It is part of the standard minimal temporal basis $\{ \mathbf{X},\,\mathbf{U},\,\mathbf{W}\}$ used in industrial model checkers like **NuSMV**, **SPIN**, and **UPPAAL**.

---

## 1.3 Boundary Cases & Special Behaviors

| Condition | Behavior of $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ | Behavior of $f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$ |
|-----------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| $g$ holds at position $0$ | Satisfied immediately | Satisfied immediately |
| $f$ holds at all positions (no $g$ ever) | **Satisfied** (weak acceptance) | **Violated** (no $g$ ever appears) |
| $f$ becomes false before $g$ appears | Violated | Violated |
| Both $f$ and $g$ hold at position $0$ | Satisfied (vacuously, $k=0$) | Satisfied (vacuously, $k=0$) |

The **Equivalence with Strong Until + Globally**:
$$f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \;\equiv\; (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \,\lor\, \mathbf{G}\,f$$

This is the canonical reduction that *all* major model-checking algorithms exploit internally to eliminate the W operator and reuse existing U-handling code.

# Module 3 — Weak Until: Deep Theoretical Analysis & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Semantics on Kripke Structures

A **Kripke Structure** is the canonical model of a reactive system used in CTL/LTL model checking. Formally:
$$M \;=\; (S,\; S_0,\; R,\; L)$$
where:
- $S$ is a finite set of **states**
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of **initial states**
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ is a **total transition relation** (every state has at least one successor — critical for fixpoint computations)
- $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is a **labeling function** mapping each state to the set of atomic propositions true in it

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

The satisfaction of $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ unfolds as follows:

1. **Check the immediate state $s$:**  
   - If $L(s) \models g$, then $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ **trivially** (case $k=0$).
   - If $L(s) \not\models g$ AND $L(s) \models f$, proceed to step 2.
   - If $L(s) \not\models g$ AND $L(s) \not\models f$, then $s \not\models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ (violation).

2. **Recursive descent on successors:**  
   - Let $R(s) = \{s' \mid (s, s') \in R\}$ be the set of immediate successors of $s$.
   - For **LTL** (single infinite path), there is exactly one successor; for **CTL**, all successors $s' \in R(s)$ must satisfy $s' \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ when prefixed with $\mathbf{A}$, or at least one must when prefixed with $\mathbf{E}$.

3. **Termination by fixpoint:**  
   The set $\{s \in S \mid s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g\}$ is computed as the **greatest fixpoint** of a monotone operator on the powerset lattice $2^S$ — this is the formal foundation of the model-checking algorithm.

---

## 2.2 Fixpoint Characterization (Core of Model Checking)

The set of states satisfying $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ is computed using the **Greatest Fixpoint (GFP)** operator $\mathbf{gfp}$, applied to the following predicate transformer $\tau : 2^S \rightarrow 2^S$:

$$\tau(X) \;=\; \llbracket g \rrbracket \,\cup\, \left( \llbracket f \rrbracket \,\cap\, \mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(X) \right)$$

where:
- $\llbracket f \rrbracket$ = set of states where $f$ is true
- $\llbracket g \rrbracket$ = set of states where $g$ is true
- $\mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(X) = \{ s \in S \mid R(s) \subseteq X \}$ = states all of whose successors are in $X$ (for $\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{W}$)

The result is:
$$\llbracket f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \rrbracket_{\mathbf{A}} \;=\; \mathbf{gfp}\!\left(\,X \;\mapsto\; \llbracket g \rrbracket \,\cup\, \bigl(\llbracket f \rrbracket \cap \mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(X)\bigr)\right)$$

**Why greatest fixpoint?** Because Weak Until admits infinite $f$-chains (where $g$ never holds) — the *largest* set closed under the operator is what we want. Knaster–Tarski's theorem guarantees this GFP exists and equals $\bigcap_{i=0}^{\infty} X_i$ where $X_0 = S$ and $X_{i+1} = \tau(X_i)$.

For the existential variant:
$$\llbracket f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \rrbracket_{\mathbf{E}} \;=\; \mathbf{gfp}\!\left(\,X \;\mapsto\; \llbracket g \rrbracket \,\cup\, \bigl(\llbracket f \rrbracket \cap \mathbf{Pre}_{\exists}(X)\bigr)\right)$$

where $\mathbf{Pre}_{\exists}(X) = \{ s \in S \mid R(s) \cap X \neq \emptyset \}$.

---

## 2.3 The Equivalence Hierarchy

| # | Equivalence Identity | Why It Holds |
|---|----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \;\equiv\; (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \,\lor\, \mathbf{G}\,f$ | Definitional: weak = strong-or-always |
| 2 | $\mathbf{G}\,f \;\equiv\; f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false}$ | Setting $g = \mathbf{false}$ — $g$ never holds, so $f$ must hold forever |
| 3 | $\mathbf{F}\,g \;\equiv\; \mathbf{true} \,\mathbf{U}\, g$ | Strong until where $f = \mathbf{true}$ (trivially satisfied) |
| 4 | $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \;\equiv\; \mathbf{G}\,f \;\lor\; f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$ | Rearrangement of (1) |
| 5 | $f \,\mathbf{R}\, g \;\equiv\; g \,\mathbf{W}\, (f \land g)$ | Release = dual of Until expressed via W |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Release Operator (R):** The "Release" operator is the De Morgan dual of Until. The identity $f \,\mathbf{R}\, g \;\equiv\; g \,\mathbf{W}\, (f \land g)$ shows that **W** is a *primitive* from which **R** can be derived — this is why some CTL textbooks treat $\{X, W\}$ as the minimal basis.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | KTU-Exam Note |
|-------------------|---------|----------------|
| $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ | Weak Until: $f$ holds until $g$ holds, **or** $f$ holds forever | Often tested as "what does W do that U does not?" |
| $f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$ | Strong Until: $f$ holds until $g$ holds, **and $g$ must** | Distinguish W vs U in exam answers |
| $f \,\mathbf{R}\, g$ | Release: $g$ holds until $f$ releases it, or $g$ holds forever | Dual of U |
| $\mathbf{G}\,f$ | Globally: $f$ holds at all future states | $= f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false}$ |
| $\mathbf{F}\,f$ | Finally (Eventually): $f$ holds at some future state | $= \mathbf{true} \,\mathbf{U}\, f$ |
| $\mathbf{X}\,f$ | Next: $f$ holds at the next state | Unaffected by W vs U distinction |
| $\mathbf{A}[f \,\mathbf{W}\, g]$ | For all paths, weak-until holds | Often used for **safety** properties |
| $\mathbf{E}[f \,\mathbf{W}\, g]$ | There exists a path with weak-until | Often used in **counterexample** search |
| $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ | State $s$ satisfies the formula | Write truth table on sample traces |
| $\mathbf{gfp}(\tau)$ | Greatest fixpoint of $\tau$ | Compute iteratively: $X_0 = S$, $X_{i+1} = \tau(X_i)$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Weak Until is extensively used in:
- **Hardware Verification:** Specifying that a request signal $\mathit{req}$ stays asserted until an acknowledgement $\mathit{ack}$ arrives, **with the system also being correct if the request stays asserted forever** (an idle system).
- **Protocol Verification (e.g., PCI-e, AXI bus):** Encoding liveness-without-strictness: a master may keep requesting indefinitely if the slave never responds — a permitted behavior in some fault-tolerance modes.
- **Software Model Checking (SLAM, BLAST):** Encoding reachability of error states that need *not* be reached for correctness.
- **Cyber-Physical Systems (UPPAAL):** Specifying timing constraints where deadlines are "soft" rather than "hard".

---

## 2.5 Comparison: W vs U vs R (Conceptual Triangle)

| Property | $\mathbf{U}$ (Until) | $\mathbf{W}$ (Weak Until) | $\mathbf{R}$ (Release) |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| Liveness obligation on 2nd arg? | **Yes** (must occur) | No (may or may not occur) | No (may or may not occur) |
| Persistence of 1st arg? | Until 2nd arg occurs | Until 2nd arg or forever | Until 1st arg occurs or forever |
| Fixpoint type used | Least Fixpoint ($\mathbf{lfp}$) | Greatest Fixpoint ($\mathbf{gfp}$) | Greatest Fixpoint ($\mathbf{gfp}$) |
| Common use | Progress / Liveness | Safety + bounded liveness | Co-safety / interruptibility |
| Boolean algebra | $\neg(f\,\mathbf{U}\,g) \equiv \neg f \,\mathbf{R}\, \neg g$ | $\mathbf{W}$ is reducible to $\mathbf{U}+\mathbf{G}$ | Duality with U |

# Module 3 — Weak Until: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Weak Until as Disjunction of Strong Until and Globally

**Goal:** Show that $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \equiv (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$.

### Step 1 — Restate the meaning of each operator

By the standard CTL/LTL semantics:
- $s \models f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$ iff $\exists k \geq 0$ such that $\pi[k] \models g$ and $\forall j < k : \pi[j] \models f$.
- $s \models \mathbf{G}\,f$ iff $\forall k \geq 0 : \pi[k] \models f$.

### Step 2 — Forward direction ($\Rightarrow$)

Assume $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$. Then by the **Weak Until semantics**, *either*:
- **Case A:** $g$ holds at some position $k \geq 0$ and $f$ holds at all $j < k$.  
  $\Rightarrow s \models f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$ directly.
- **Case B:** $f$ holds at every position on the path (and $g$ never holds).  
  $\Rightarrow s \models \mathbf{G}\,f$ directly.

Thus in both cases, $s \models (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$. $\blacksquare$

### Step 3 — Backward direction ($\Leftarrow$)

Assume $s \models (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$.
- **Case A:** $s \models f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$. Then $\exists k$ with $\pi[k] \models g$ and $\forall j<k: \pi[j] \models f$. This trivially satisfies the first disjunct of the **W** semantics, so $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$.
- **Case B:** $s \models \mathbf{G}\,f$. Then $\forall k : \pi[k] \models f$. This matches the second disjunct of the **W** semantics (f forever, g never), so $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$.

$\blacksquare$ — Equivalence established.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: $\mathbf{G}\,f \equiv f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false}$

**Goal:** Prove Globally is a special case of Weak Until.

### Step 1 — Substitute $g := \mathbf{false}$ into the disjunction

From the identity in §3.1:
$$f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false} \;\equiv\; (f \,\mathbf{U}\, \mathbf{false}) \;\lor\; \mathbf{G}\,f$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate $f \,\mathbf{U}\, \mathbf{false}$

By definition of Strong Until: $\exists k \geq 0$ such that $\pi[k] \models \mathbf{false}$ and $\forall j<k : \pi[j] \models f$.

But $\pi[k] \models \mathbf{false}$ is *impossible* for any $k$ in classical logic (a formula cannot be both true and false in a state under consistent labeling). Hence:
$$f \,\mathbf{U}\, \mathbf{false} \;\equiv\; \mathbf{false}$$

### Step 3 — Conclude

$$f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false} \;\equiv\; \mathbf{false} \;\lor\; \mathbf{G}\,f \;\equiv\; \mathbf{G}\,f \qquad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]  
> This identity is **heavily used in exam answers** — when asked "express $Gf$ using W", the correct one-line answer is $f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fixpoint Algorithm for $\llbracket f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \rrbracket$

**Goal:** Compute the set $W = \{s \in S \mid s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g\}$ iteratively.

### Step 1 — Define the operator

$$\tau(X) \;=\; \llbracket g \rrbracket \,\cup\, \bigl(\llbracket f \rrbracket \,\cap\, \mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(X)\bigr)$$

### Step 2 — Initialize

$$X_0 \;=\; S \quad \text{(start with the universal set — greatest fixpoint convention)}$$

### Step 3 — Iterate

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= \tau(X_0) = \llbracket g \rrbracket \,\cup\, \bigl(\llbracket f \rrbracket \,\cap\, \mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(S)\bigr) \\
    &= \llbracket g \rrbracket \,\cup\, \llbracket f \rrbracket \quad \text{(since } R \text{ is total, } \mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(S) = S \text{)} \\
X_2 &= \tau(X_1) \\
    &= \llbracket g \rrbracket \,\cup\, \bigl(\llbracket f \rrbracket \,\cap\, \mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(X_1)\bigr) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
X_{i+1} &= \llbracket g \rrbracket \,\cup\, \bigl(\llbracket f \rrbracket \,\cap\, \mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(X_i)\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Termination

The sequence $X_0 \supseteq X_1 \supseteq X_2 \supseteq \cdots$ is **monotonically decreasing** and bounded below by $\emptyset$. Since $S$ is finite, the sequence stabilizes at some $X_n = X_{n+1}$, and this is the **greatest fixpoint**:
$$W \;=\; X_n \;=\; \mathbf{gfp}(\tau) \;=\; \bigcap_{i=0}^{\infty} X_i$$

### Step 5 — Verification by example

Consider a 3-state path $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2 \to \cdots$ with $f$ true at $s_0, s_1$ and false at $s_2$, and $g$ true at $s_2$.
- $s_2 \models g \Rightarrow s_2 \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$.
- $s_1 \models f$ and successor $s_2 \in W \Rightarrow s_1 \in W$ (via $X_1$ computation).
- $s_0 \models f$ and successor $s_1 \in W \Rightarrow s_0 \in W$.

Result: $W = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$. ✓ Consistent with intuition.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: W vs U vs R — Side-by-Side Truth Evaluation

Consider a finite path $\pi = (s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3)$ of length 4, with truth values of $f$ and $g$:

| Path position | $f$ | $g$ |
|---------------|-----|-----|
| $s_0$ | T | F |
| $s_1$ | T | F |
| $s_2$ | T | T |
| $s_3$ | F | F |

Now evaluate from $s_0$:

| Formula | Evaluation | Result |
|---------|------------|--------|
| $f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$ | $g$ becomes true at $s_2$, $f$ holds at $s_0, s_1$ | **TRUE** |
| $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ | Same as above (Case A) | **TRUE** |
| $f \,\mathbf{W}\, \neg g$ | $f$ holds at $s_0, s_1$; at $s_2$, $f \land \neg g$ is F; violation | **FALSE** |
| $g \,\mathbf{R}\, f$ | $f$ releases $g$ at $s_0, s_1$; $g$ becomes true at $s_2$ | **TRUE** |
| $\mathbf{G}\,f$ | $f$ false at $s_3$ | **FALSE** |
| $f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false}$ | $g = $ false never holds, $f$ false at $s_3$ | **FALSE** |
| $\neg g \,\mathbf{W}\, f$ | $f$ true at $s_2$ releases the wait | **TRUE** |

This matrix is the **core analytical tool** for KTU problems on this topic.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Kripke Structure Model Check)

**Problem:** Consider the Kripke structure with states $\{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, initial state $s_0$, and transitions $s_0 \to s_1$, $s_0 \to s_2$, $s_1 \to s_1$ (self-loop), $s_2 \to s_3$, $s_3 \to s_3$ (self-loop). Labeling: $L(s_0)=\{f\}$, $L(s_1)=\{f\}$, $L(s_2)=\{f,g\}$, $L(s_3)=\{g\}$.

**Check whether $s_0 \models \mathbf{A}[f \,\mathbf{W}\, g]$.**

### Step 1 — Identify all paths from $s_0$
- $\pi_1 = s_0, s_1, s_1, s_1, \ldots$ (loop in $s_1$)
- $\pi_2 = s_0, s_2, s_3, s_3, s_3, \ldots$ (loop in $s_3$)

### Step 2 — Evaluate $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ on $\pi_1$
At $s_0$: $f$ true, $g$ false. Move to $s_1$.  
At $s_1$: $f$ true, $g$ false. Self-loop. Forever.  
Hence on $\pi_1$: $f$ holds everywhere, $g$ never. **Weak Until is satisfied** (Case B of W). ✓

### Step 3 — Evaluate $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ on $\pi_2$
At $s_0$: $f$ true, $g$ false. Move to $s_2$.  
At $s_2$: $f$ true, $g$ true. **W satisfied immediately** at $k=1$. ✓

### Step 4 — Conclusion
Both paths satisfy W. Therefore $s_0 \models \mathbf{A}[f \,\mathbf{W}\, g]$. **TRUE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Examination Tip:** The **$\mathbf{A}[f \,\mathbf{W}\, g]$** formula is *true* on $s_0$ because **all** paths satisfy it. If the question had asked $\mathbf{A}[f \,\mathbf{U}\, g]$, the answer would *still* be TRUE here — but on a different Kripke structure where one path loops forever with $f$ true and $g$ never appearing, the $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{U}$ would be FALSE while the $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{W}$ would be TRUE. **This is the prototypical exam discriminator.**

# Module 3 — Weak Until: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Operator Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[State s reached] --> B{Check immediate state s}
    B -- s models g --> C[s models f W g - TRIVIALLY TRUE at k=0]
    B -- s does NOT model g --> D{Check if s models f}
    D -- s does NOT model f --> E[VIOLATION - s does NOT satisfy f W g]
    D -- s models f --> F[Examine successor states s prime]
    F --> G{Path quantifier prefix}
    G -- A all paths --> H[For ALL successors: s prime must satisfy f W g]
    G -- E exists path --> I[There EXISTS a successor: s prime satisfies f W g]
    H -- All recursive checks TRUE --> J[s satisfies A f W g]
    H -- Some recursive check FAILS --> K[s does NOT satisfy A f W g]
    I -- At least one path TRUE --> L[s satisfies E f W g]
    I -- All paths FAIL --> M[s does NOT satisfy E f W g]
    C --> N[Exit: f W g TRUE]
    E --> O[Exit: f W g FALSE]
    J --> N
    K --> O
    L --> N
    M --> O
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: W vs U Operator Behavior on Infinite Traces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Trace pi starts] --> Branch{Choose operator}
    Branch -- Strong Until U --> S1[Seek first occurrence of g]
    S1 --> S2{g found at position k?}
    S2 -- Yes AND f holds at all j less than k --> S3[U satisfied]
    S2 -- No g ever found --> S4[U VIOLATED - g must appear]
    Branch -- Weak Until W --> W1[Seek first occurrence of g OR check if f holds forever]
    W1 --> W2{g found at position k?}
    W2 -- Yes AND f holds at all j less than k --> W3[W satisfied - Case A]
    W2 -- No g ever found --> W4{f holds at every position?}
    W4 -- Yes --> W5[W satisfied - Case B - f forever]
    W4 -- No --> W6[W VIOLATED - f broke before g appeared]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Fixpoint Iteration Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Iteration 0: X0 equals S - the universe] --> P1[Iteration 1: X1 equals g-set UNION f-set minus Pre-Forall X0]
    P1 --> P2[Iteration 2: X2 equals g-set UNION f-set minus Pre-Forall X1]
    P2 --> P3[Iteration i: Xi equals g-set UNION f-set minus Pre-Forall X i minus 1]
    P3 --> P4{Xi equals X i minus 1?}
    P4 -- No --> P2
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Convergence reached - Xi is gfp tau equals W - the set of states satisfying f W g]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: CTL Hierarchy Showing W's Position

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[CTL Formula Class] --> L1[State Formulas - evaluated on states]
    Top --> L2[Path Formulas - evaluated on paths]
    L1 --> SF1[Atomic Propositions p in AP]
    L1 --> SF2[Boolean connectives NOT, AND, OR, IMPLIES, IFF]
    L1 --> SF3[Path quantifier prefix: A or E]
    SF3 --> SF4[Temporal operators: X, U, W, F, G, R]
    L2 --> TF1[Next: X phi - phi at next state]
    L2 --> TF2[Strong Until: f U g - f until g must occur]
    L2 --> TF3[Weak Until: f W g - f until g or f forever]
    L2 --> TF4[Finally: F phi - phi eventually]
    L2 --> TF5[Globally: G phi - phi always - equals f W false]
    L2 --> TF6[Release: f R g - dual of Until]
    TF3 -.Definitional Reduction.-> TF2
    TF3 -.Alternative Form.-> TF5
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Architecture: Model Checker Internal Pipeline for W

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input: CTL formula containing W] --> Parser[Formula Parser - tokenize]
    Parser --> DetectW{Operator W detected?}
    DetectW -- Yes --> Normalize[Apply normalization: f W g becomes f U g OR G f]
    DetectW -- No --> Pass[Pass through unchanged]
    Normalize --> ReduceU[Sub-formula f U g sent to Until-handler]
    Normalize --> ReduceG[Sub-formula G f sent to Globally-handler]
    ReduceU --> UntilEngine[Strong Until fixpoint - lfp - linear time]
    ReduceG --> GlobalEngine[Globally fixpoint - gfp - infinite descent]
    UntilEngine --> Combine[Union of result sets]
    GlobalEngine --> Combine
    Combine --> Output[Output: set of satisfying states - W computed]
    Pass --> Output
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Industrial Insight:** Industrial model checkers like **NuSMV** and **Cadence SMV** perform exactly the normalization in the figure above: every W-operator is rewritten into the disjunction $(U \lor G)$ during the *formula preprocessing* phase, allowing a single unified fixpoint engine to handle the entire formula. This is why a KTU exam may ask: *"Why can the W operator be eliminated in CTL model checking?"* — Answer: because of the equivalence $f\,\mathbf{W}\,g \equiv (f\,\mathbf{U}\,g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$, allowing reduction to the canonical U+G basis.
