# Module 3 — Weak Until: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**  
**Q: Define the Weak Until (W) operator in CTL/LTL. State how it differs from the Strong Until (U) operator with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**  
Weak Until is a binary temporal connective denoted $\mathbf{W}$. For state formulas $f$ and $g$, the formula $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ holds at a state $s$ if, along a path from $s$, $f$ holds continuously *until* $g$ holds, **OR** $f$ holds at *every* future state (i.e., $g$ need not ever hold). Formally:
$$s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \iff \exists \text{ path } \pi \text{ from } s \text{ s.t. } (\exists k \geq 0: \pi[k] \models g \land \forall j<k: \pi[j] \models f) \;\lor\; (\forall k \geq 0: \pi[k] \models f)$$

**Difference with U + Example (1.5 Marks):**  
- **Strong Until** ($f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$) *requires* $g$ to eventually become true on the path.  
- **Weak Until** ($f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$) does *not* require $g$ to ever hold — it permits $f$ to hold forever.  

**Example:** Let $f = \text{``request''}$ and $g = \text{``acknowledge''}$. The property $\mathbf{A}[f \,\mathbf{W}\, g]$ states: "Every path keeps the request asserted until an ack is received, **or** the request may remain asserted forever." In contrast, $\mathbf{A}[f \,\mathbf{U}\, g]$ insists that **every path** must eventually receive an ack — a stricter liveness demand.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**  
**Q: Prove that the operator $\mathbf{G}$ (Globally) can be expressed using the Weak Until operator. Also write the canonical reduction rule for W.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Global as Weak Until (2 Marks):**  
Substituting $g := \mathbf{false}$ into the Weak Until definition:
$$f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false} \;\equiv\; (f \,\mathbf{U}\, \mathbf{false}) \;\lor\; \mathbf{G}\,f$$
Since $f \,\mathbf{U}\, \mathbf{false} \equiv \mathbf{false}$ (no state can satisfy "false"), we obtain:
$$\boxed{\mathbf{G}\,f \;\equiv\; f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false}}$$

**Canonical Reduction Rule for W (1 Mark):**  
$$f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \;\equiv\; (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \;\lor\; \mathbf{G}\,f$$

This reduction permits model checkers to eliminate W from input formulas, reusing existing U- and G-handling subroutines.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**  
**Q: (a)** Define Kripke structures and explain how the Weak Until operator is evaluated on them. Discuss the role of the greatest fixpoint in computing the set of states satisfying $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$. **(7 Marks)**  
**(b)** Consider the Kripke structure $M$ with states $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$, initial state $s_0$, transitions $\{s_0 \to s_1, s_1 \to s_1, s_1 \to s_2, s_2 \to s_2, s_2 \to s_3, s_3 \to s_3\}$, and labels $L(s_0) = \{f\}$, $L(s_1) = \{f\}$, $L(s_2) = \{f, g\}$, $L(s_3) = \{g\}$. Using the fixpoint algorithm, compute the set of states satisfying $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Kripke Structure Definition (2 Marks):**  
A Kripke structure is a 4-tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$:
- $S$: finite set of **states**
- $S_0 \subseteq S$: **initial** states
- $R \subseteq S \times S$: **transition relation** (must be total — every state has a successor)
- $L: S \to 2^{AP}$: **labeling function** assigning to each state the set of atomic propositions true in it

**Weak Until Evaluation (2 Marks):**  
A state $s$ satisfies $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ on $M$ if there exists an infinite path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ from $s$ such that either (i) some $\pi[k] \models g$ with all earlier positions satisfying $f$, or (ii) every position on $\pi$ satisfies $f$. The operator is evaluated recursively, examining each state's outgoing transitions and the satisfaction status of its successors.

**Role of Greatest Fixpoint (3 Marks):**  
The set $W = \{s \in S \mid s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g\}$ is computed as the greatest fixpoint of the operator:
$$\tau(X) = \llbracket g \rrbracket \cup (\llbracket f \rrbracket \cap \mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(X))$$
Starting from $X_0 = S$ and iterating $X_{i+1} = \tau(X_i)$, the sequence monotonically decreases and stabilizes. The greatest fixpoint is required (not least) because Weak Until admits infinite $f$-chains where $g$ never appears — the **largest** set closed under the W-condition correctly captures all such states. Knaster–Tarski's theorem guarantees termination in $O(|S|)$ iterations on finite structures, yielding a model-checking complexity of $O(|S| \cdot |f| \cdot |g|)$.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Initialize $X_0 = S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}$ (1 Mark)**

**Step 2 — Compute $X_1$ (2 Marks):**  
Since $R$ is total, $\mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(X_0) = X_0 = S$. Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= \llbracket g \rrbracket \cup (\llbracket f \rrbracket \cap S) \\
    &= \{s_2, s_3\} \cup (\{s_0, s_1, s_2\} \cap S) \\
    &= \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $X_2$ (2 Marks):**  
$\mathbf{Pre}_{\forall}(X_1)$: which states have **all** successors in $X_1 = S$? Since $R$ is total and $X_1 = S$, this is $S$. Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X_2 &= \llbracket g \rrbracket \cup (\llbracket f \rrbracket \cap S) \\
    &= \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convergence (1 Mark):**  
$X_1 = X_2$, so the fixpoint has stabilized.

**Result (1 Mark):**  
$$\boxed{\llbracket f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \rrbracket = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3\} = S}$$

**Verification:** All four states satisfy $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ — $s_0, s_1$ via the $f$-forever path, $s_2$ via $g$ becoming true, $s_3$ trivially since $g$ is already true.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Internal Choice]**  
**Q: (a)** State and prove the equivalence $f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \equiv (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$. Show clearly both directions of the proof. **(7 Marks)**  
**(b)** Using the above equivalence, derive expressions for (i) $\mathbf{G}\,f$ and (ii) $\mathbf{F}\,g$ in terms of W only. Justify each step. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Statement (1 Mark):**  
For any state formulas $f, g$ in CTL/LTL and any state $s$ in a Kripke structure $M$:
$$s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g \iff s \models (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$$

**Proof ($\Rightarrow$) (3 Marks):**  
Assume $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$. By the W-semantics, one of two cases holds:  
*Case 1:* $g$ holds at some $k \geq 0$ with $f$ at all $j<k$. Then $s \models f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$, so the disjunction holds.  
*Case 2:* $f$ holds at every position on the path, with $g$ never holding. Then $s \models \mathbf{G}\,f$, so the disjunction holds.  
In both cases, $s \models (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$.

**Proof ($\Leftarrow$) (3 Marks):**  
Assume $s \models (f \,\mathbf{U}\, g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$.  
*Case 1:* $s \models f \,\mathbf{U}\, g$. Then $\exists k$ with $\pi[k] \models g$ and $\forall j<k: \pi[j] \models f$. This matches the first disjunct of W-semantics, so $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$.  
*Case 2:* $s \models \mathbf{G}\,f$. Then $\forall k: \pi[k] \models f$. This matches the second disjunct (f-forever, g-never), so $s \models f \,\mathbf{W}\, g$.  
In both cases, the equivalence is established. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Deriving $\mathbf{G}\,f$ in terms of W (3.5 Marks):**  
Set $g := \mathbf{false}$ in the equivalence:
$$f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false} \equiv (f \,\mathbf{U}\, \mathbf{false}) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$$
By definition of Strong Until, $f \,\mathbf{U}\, \mathbf{false}$ requires a position where $\mathbf{false}$ holds — impossible. Therefore $f \,\mathbf{U}\, \mathbf{false} \equiv \mathbf{false}$. Hence:
$$\boxed{\mathbf{G}\,f \equiv f \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false}}$$

**(ii) Deriving $\mathbf{F}\,g$ in terms of W (3.5 Marks):**  
We use the standard identity $\mathbf{F}\,g \equiv \mathbf{true} \,\mathbf{U}\, g$. We must now express this using W. Observe that:
$$\mathbf{F}\,g \equiv \mathbf{true} \,\mathbf{U}\, g \equiv \neg \mathbf{G}\,\neg g \equiv \neg (\neg g \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false})$$
Using the identity from part (i), $\mathbf{G}\,\neg g \equiv \neg g \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false}$, so:
$$\boxed{\mathbf{F}\,g \equiv \neg(\neg g \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false})}$$
Alternative: $\mathbf{F}\,g \equiv \mathbf{true} \,\mathbf{W}\, (g \lor \mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{false}) \equiv \mathbf{true} \,\mathbf{W}\, g$ **only if** we allow $\mathbf{G}\,\mathbf{false}$ as a marker. The most concise form acceptable in exams is $\mathbf{F}\,g \equiv \neg(\neg g \,\mathbf{W}\, \mathbf{false})$.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Weak Until Questions**  
> 1. **Confusing W and U:** The single most common error. Students write $f\,\mathbf{W}\,g \equiv f\,\mathbf{U}\,g$ and lose 2-3 marks instantly. Always state the W-semantics *explicitly* with the "or f holds forever" clause.  
> 2. **Forgetting the "or G f" clause in the equivalence:** The reduction $f\,\mathbf{W}\,g \equiv (f\,\mathbf{U}\,g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$ must be stated in *full* — partial marks if the $\mathbf{G}\,f$ term is omitted.  
> 3. **Path quantifier confusion:** Writing $\mathbf{E}[f\,\mathbf{W}\,g]$ when the question requires $\mathbf{A}[f\,\mathbf{W}\,g]$. A is "all", E is "exists" — examiner will not award marks for swapped quantifiers.  
> 4. **Fixpoint initial value:** For *greatest* fixpoint, start with $X_0 = S$ (top element), not $X_0 = \emptyset$ (which is the *least* fixpoint for U). Mixing these up yields wrong model-checking results.  
> 5. **Mis-stating $\mathbf{G}\,f \equiv f\,\mathbf{W}\,\mathbf{false}$:** Some students write $f\,\mathbf{W}\,\mathbf{true}$ — which collapses trivially to $\mathbf{true}$. Always substitute $g = \mathbf{false}$ (the formula that is *never* satisfied), not $g = \mathbf{true}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Weak Until**

- ✅ **Symbol:** $\mathbf{W}$ — a *binary*, *future*, *path-based* temporal operator.
- ✅ **Definitional Identity:** $f\,\mathbf{W}\,g \equiv (f\,\mathbf{U}\,g) \lor \mathbf{G}\,f$.
- ✅ **Key Distinction from U:** Weak Until **does NOT require** $g$ to ever hold; Strong Until **does**.
- ✅ **Two semantic cases:** (1) $g$ eventually holds with $f$ persisting, OR (2) $f$ holds forever.
- ✅ **Globally as W:** $\mathbf{G}\,f \equiv f\,\mathbf{W}\,\mathbf{false}$.
- ✅ **Finally via W:** $\mathbf{F}\,g \equiv \neg(\neg g\,\mathbf{W}\,\mathbf{false})$.
- ✅ **Release via W:** $f\,\mathbf{R}\,g \equiv g\,\mathbf{W}\,(f \land g)$.
- ✅ **Fixpoint type:** **Greatest Fixpoint (gfp)** — iterative computation starts from $X_0 = S$, monotonically decreases.
- ✅ **CTL forms:** $\mathbf{A}[f\,\mathbf{W}\,g]$ (universal) and $\mathbf{E}[f\,\mathbf{W}\,g]$ (existential).
- ✅ **Industrial use:** Model checkers (NuSMV, SPIN, UPPAAL) normalize W into U+G at preprocessing stage.
- ✅ **LTL vs CTL placement:** In LTL, W is a primitive path formula; in CTL, it must appear immediately after a path quantifier.
- ✅ **Engineering role:** Expresses **safety** properties and **soft liveness** — preferred when strict eventual occurrence is not warranted.
- ✅ **Exam mnemonic:** **"W = Wait, or Walk away forever."** — $f$ waits for $g$, OR $f$ walks on forever without $g$ (no obligation broken).
- ✅ **Algorithmic complexity:** Model checking $f\,\mathbf{W}\,g$ takes $O(|S| \cdot |\phi|)$ time — linear in state space and formula size.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Do NOT confuse $\mathbf{A}[f\,\mathbf{W}\,g]$ with $\mathbf{AG}\,f$. The former permits $g$ to appear; the latter forbids $f$ from ever being false. Trace carefully on a sample path before answering.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Release and Positive Normal Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Release and Positive Normal Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Release (R) Operator in Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Release operator**, denoted by the symbol $R$, is a binary temporal connective in **Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)**. For two LTL formulas $\phi$ and $\psi$, the formula $\phi \, R \, \psi$ is interpreted over an infinite computation path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$

$$(\phi \, R \, \psi) \;\equiv\; \text{"}\psi \text{ holds UNTIL } \phi \text{ holds, OR } \psi \text{ holds forever}"$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal LTL Semantics (KTU Board Definition):**
> $$\pi \models \phi \, R \, \psi \iff \exists k \geq 0 \; \text{such that} \; \pi^k \models \phi \; \text{AND} \; \forall j, \; 0 \leq j < k \Rightarrow \pi^j \models \psi$$
> If **no such $k$ exists**, then $\psi$ must hold at **every** state on the path ($\forall j \geq 0, \; \pi^j \models \psi$).

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> 🎯 **"The Handcuffs" Analogy**
>
> Imagine $\psi$ (e.g., *"The alarm is OFF"*) is a prisoner and $\phi$ (e.g., *"An intrusion occurs"*) is the key. The formula $\phi \, R \, \psi$ says:
>
> *"The alarm remains OFF — it is RELEASED from being OFF only when an intrusion occurs. If no intrusion ever occurs, the alarm stays OFF forever."*
>
> The "release" event is $\phi$ — it is what *releases* $\psi$ from its obligation to hold. This is the **exact dual** of the Until operator, where the obligation is reversed.

---

## 1.2 Positive Normal Form (PNF) — Canonical LTL Form

### Formal Definition

A **Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) formula is in Positive Normal Form (PNF)** if and only if it satisfies **three structural constraints simultaneously**:

1. **No derived connectives**: The implication ($\rightarrow$) and equivalence ($\leftrightarrow$) operators are **completely absent**.
2. **Negation depth = 1**: The logical NOT ($\neg$) appears **only immediately before atomic propositions** (i.e., literals, not compound sub-formulas).
3. **Restricted temporal vocabulary**: The only temporal operators permitted are $X$ (Next), $U$ (Until), and $R$ (Release). Derived operators $F$ (Eventually) and $G$ (Always) are eliminated and re-expressed via $U$ and $R$.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> 🧮 **"The Calculator's RPN" Analogy**
>
> Just as **Reverse Polish Notation (RPN)** is a standardized, parenthesis-light form that every calculator can mechanically evaluate, **PNF** is the standardized, negation-minimal form that every model checker (SPIN, NuSMV, etc.) can mechanically handle. You cannot plug an arbitrary LTL formula straight into a tableau-based model checker — you first "compile" it into PNF.

### Why Does PNF Matter in Automated Verification?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Tableau construction** for LTL model checking (used in tools like **SPIN**) operates only on PNF formulas. Without PNF, tableau nodes would have to handle negations in every direction, exploding the state space.
> - **SAT-based bounded model checking (BMC)** translates PNF into a propositional formula much more cleanly.
> - **Anti-prenexing** of negation makes the duality $U \leftrightarrow R$ directly visible, allowing algorithms to process both operators in a single, uniform pass.

---

## 1.3 The Vital Link: Why *Release* Appears in the *Same Module* as PNF

The two concepts are **mathematically inseparable** in LTL theory:

| Reason | Explanation |
| --- | --- |
| **Duality** | $\neg(\phi \, U \, \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \, R \, \neg\psi$ — the Release operator is the **negation-dual** of Until. |
| **PNF Requirement** | You **cannot** push a $\neg$ past a $U$ using the dual form unless you **introduce** the $R$ operator. |
| **Canonical Form** | Every LTL formula, after PNF conversion, contains only $\{ \wedge, \vee, X, U, R\}$ — making $R$ mandatory, not optional. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Semantics of the Release Operator

Let $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ denote an infinite sequence of states. We use the notation $\pi^i$ to denote the suffix of $\pi$ starting at state $s_i$.

### Truth Table (Path-Indexed Interpretation)

| Case | Path Behaviour | Verdict |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Case 1** | $\phi$ holds at the **current** state ($i = 0$) | Formula is **TRIVIALLY TRUE** (released immediately) |
| **Case 2** | $\phi$ first holds at state $k > 0$, and $\psi$ holds at all states $0, 1, \ldots, k-1$ | **SATISFIED** — release occurs at $k$ |
| **Case 3** | $\phi$ **never** holds on the entire infinite path | **SATISFIED iff** $\psi$ holds at **every** state $s_i$ for $i \geq 0$ |
| **Case 4** | $\phi$ never holds AND $\psi$ fails at some state $i$ | **VIOLATED** |

### Why is "Release" the Dual of "Until"?

$$
\phi \, R \, \psi \;\equiv\; \neg(\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi)
$$

This is the single most important duality identity in LTL. To remember it: **"Release is Until's negative mirror."** Swapping $U$ for $R$ is the **only** structural change that occurs when a single global $\neg$ is pushed past a temporal operator.

---

## 2.2 The $F$ and $G$ Operators — Expressed via $R$

This is a **high-yield KTU 14-mark question** topic. Memorize the following identities:

| Derived Operator | Read As | PNF Equivalent using $R$ |
| --- | --- | --- |
| $F \, \phi$ | "Eventually $\phi$" | $true \, R \, \phi$ |
| $G \, \phi$ | "Globally $\phi$" (always) | $false \, R \, \phi$ |
| $\neg F \, \phi$ | "Never $\phi$" | $false \, R \, \neg\phi$ (i.e., $G \neg\phi$) |
| $\neg G \, \phi$ | "Not always $\phi$" | $true \, R \, \neg\phi$ (i.e., $F \neg\phi$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for $F$ and $G$ as Releases:**
> - $F \phi \equiv true \, R \, \phi$: The "$true$" operand is released the moment $\phi$ first becomes true. Since $true$ always holds, the *only* way to satisfy the formula is for $\phi$ to eventually occur.
> - $G \phi \equiv false \, R \, \phi$: The "$false$" operand is *never* released (since $false$ is never true). So $\phi$ must hold forever.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table consolidates **every equivalence required for PNF conversion and Release operator manipulation** in KTU ESE answers.

| # | Identity / Law | Symbolic Form | Used For |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Implication elimination | $\phi \to \psi \equiv \neg\phi \vee \psi$ | PNF step 1 |
| 2 | Equivalence elimination | $\phi \leftrightarrow \psi \equiv (\neg\phi \vee \psi) \wedge (\neg\psi \vee \phi)$ | PNF step 1 |
| 3 | Double negation | $\neg\neg\phi \equiv \phi$ | PNF step 2 |
| 4 | De Morgan (∧) | $\neg(\phi \wedge \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \vee \neg\psi$ | PNF step 2 |
| 5 | De Morgan (∨) | $\neg(\phi \vee \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \wedge \neg\psi$ | PNF step 2 |
| 6 | Negation of $X$ | $\neg X \phi \equiv X \neg\phi$ | PNF step 3 |
| 7 | **Negation of $U$** | $\neg(\phi \, U \, \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \, R \, \neg\psi$ | PNF step 3 (**introduces R**) |
| 8 | **Negation of $R$** | $\neg(\phi \, R \, \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi$ | PNF step 3 |
| 9 | $F$ as Release | $F \phi \equiv true \, R \, \phi$ | PNF step 4 |
| 10 | $G$ as Release | $G \phi \equiv false \, R \, \phi$ | PNF step 4 |
| 11 | Duality $F$/$G$ | $F \phi \equiv \neg G \neg\phi$ | Often used before PNF |
| 12 | Duality $G$/$F$ | $G \phi \equiv \neg F \neg\phi$ | Often used before PNF |
| 13 | **$R$ definition via Until** | $\phi \, R \, \psi \equiv \neg(\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi)$ | Foundational duality |
| 14 | $U$ via $R$ | $\phi \, U \, \psi \equiv \neg(\neg\phi \, R \, \neg\psi)$ | Symmetric counterpart |
| 15 | Fixed point of $R$ | $\phi \, R \, \psi \equiv \psi \vee (\phi \wedge X(\phi \, R \, \psi))$ | Used in tableau-based model checking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These identities are the backbone of the **SPIN model checker's LTL translator (`spin -f`)** and the **LTL $\to$ Büchi automaton** pipeline used in every modern verification toolchain. Mastery of these laws directly enables you to trace any tool-generated automaton back to its source formula.

---

## 2.4 Why PNF Conversion is Algorithmically Important

Consider an LTL formula that contains *nested* negations, implications, and $F$/$G$ operators. A model checker's tableau algorithm must process a formula one connective at a time. If the formula is *not* in PNF, the algorithm must either:

1. Apply these rewrite rules **on the fly** at every tableau node (expensive), **OR**
2. **Pre-process** the formula once into PNF (cheap, one-time cost).

The standard approach in tools like **SPIN** is option 2 — hence PNF is a **mandatory preprocessing step**. For a formula of size $n$, PNF conversion runs in **linear time** $O(n)$, since each connective is rewritten exactly once.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Conversions

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Proving the $R$–$U$ Duality

**Claim:** $\phi \, R \, \psi \equiv \neg(\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi)$

### Proof (Direct Semantic Argument)

We prove this by showing both sides are true on exactly the same set of paths.

**Forward direction ($\Rightarrow$):** Suppose $\pi \models \phi \, R \, \psi$.

* **Case A:** $\phi$ holds at some position $k$ on $\pi$. Then by the $R$ semantics, $\psi$ holds at all positions $j < k$. Taking negations, $\neg\psi$ fails at $j < k$, so the formula $\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi$ **cannot** be satisfied — but this is fine: we are showing that $\pi \not\models \neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi$, which means $\pi \models \neg(\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi)$.
* **Case B:** $\phi$ never holds, and $\psi$ holds at every position (else $R$ would fail). Then $\neg\psi$ fails everywhere, so $\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi$ fails, i.e., $\pi \models \neg(\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi)$.

**Reverse direction ($\Leftarrow$):** Suppose $\pi \models \neg(\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi)$, i.e., $\pi \not\models \neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi$.

By the $U$ semantics, $\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi$ fails precisely when either (i) $\neg\psi$ holds at all positions AND $\neg\phi$ holds at all positions, OR (ii) $\neg\psi$ fails at some position $m$ before $\neg\phi$ ever holds. Combining these two cases, we deduce that **$\psi$ holds at all positions up to and including the first position where $\phi$ holds** — which is exactly the $R$ semantics. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Full PNF Conversion of a Complex Formula

**Task:** Convert the following LTL formula to **Positive Normal Form**, showing **every intermediate step**:

$$
\varphi \;=\; \neg \big( \, (p \wedge q) \;\to\; (F \, r \;\vee\; G \, s) \, \big)
$$

### Step 1: Eliminate the Implication ($\to$)

Apply identity $\#1$ from the cheat sheet: $\phi \to \psi \equiv \neg\phi \vee \psi$. Here, $\phi \equiv (p \wedge q)$ and $\psi \equiv (F r \vee G s)$.

$$
\varphi \;=\; \neg \big( \, \neg(p \wedge q) \;\vee\; (F \, r \;\vee\; G \, s) \, \big)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** We have replaced the single $\to$ with $\neg \phi \vee \psi$. The outer $\neg$ is now surrounding a $\vee$ — this is the trigger for the next De Morgan step.

### Step 2: Apply De Morgan's Law to the Outer Negation

Apply identity $\#5$: $\neg(\phi \vee \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \wedge \neg\psi$.

$$
\varphi \;=\; \neg\neg(p \wedge q) \;\wedge\; \neg(F \, r \;\vee\; G \, s)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The outer $\neg$ distributes inward over the top-level $\vee$, yielding an $\wedge$ of two $\neg$-applied sub-formulas. We have **not** yet pushed $\neg$ past $F$ or $G$.

### Step 3: Eliminate the Double Negation

Apply identity $\#3$: $\neg\neg(p \wedge q) \equiv (p \wedge q)$.

$$
\varphi \;=\; (p \wedge q) \;\wedge\; \neg(F \, r \;\vee\; G \, s)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The left conjunct is now in clean form. The right conjunct still has a $\neg$ surrounding $F$ and $G$.

### Step 4: Apply De Morgan Inside the Right Conjunct

Apply identity $\#5$ to $\neg(F \, r \vee G \, s)$:

$$
\varphi \;=\; (p \wedge q) \;\wedge\; \big( \neg F \, r \;\wedge\; \neg G \, s \big)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The $\neg$ has been pushed through the $\vee$, leaving an $\wedge$ and two negated temporal operators $F$ and $G$.

### Step 5: Convert $\neg F$ and $\neg G$ Using Release Duality

Apply identities $\#11$ and $\#12$: $\neg F \phi \equiv G \neg\phi$ and $\neg G \phi \equiv F \neg\phi$.

$$
\varphi \;=\; (p \wedge q) \;\wedge\; \big( G \neg r \;\wedge\; F \neg s \big)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The negations have now flipped the temporal operators. Note carefully: $F$ became $G$ and vice-versa, with the atomic proposition being negated.

### Step 6: Rewrite $G$ and $F$ in Terms of $R$ (Final PNF)

Apply identities $\#9$ and $\#10$: $F \phi \equiv true \, R \, \phi$ and $G \phi \equiv false \, R \, \phi$.

$$
\varphi \;=\; (p \wedge q) \;\wedge\; \big( (false \, R \, \neg r) \;\wedge\; (true \, R \, \neg s) \big)
$$

### Verification of PNF Compliance

| Constraint | Status |
| --- | --- |
| No $\rightarrow$ or $\leftrightarrow$ | ✅ Satisfied (eliminated in Step 1) |
| No $\neg$ on compound sub-formulas | ✅ Satisfied (only $\neg r$, $\neg s$ remain) |
| No $F$ or $G$ | ✅ Satisfied (rewritten in Step 6) |
| Only $\{ \wedge, \vee, X, U, R \}$ used | ✅ Satisfied |

$$
\boxed{\;\varphi_{\text{PNF}} \;=\; (p \wedge q) \;\wedge\; (false \, R \, \neg r) \;\wedge\; (true \, R \, \neg s)\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Push Negation Through Nested Temporal Operators

**Task:** Convert $\psi = \neg X (\neg p \, U \, q)$ to PNF.

### Step 1: Push $\neg$ Through $X$

Apply identity $\#6$: $\neg X \phi \equiv X \neg\phi$.

$$
\psi \;=\; X \, \neg(\neg p \, U \, q)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The $\neg$ slides through the $X$ cleanly because $X$ acts on a single position.

### Step 2: Apply $U \to R$ Duality

Apply identity $\#7$: $\neg(\phi \, U \, \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \, R \, \neg\psi$. Here, $\phi \equiv \neg p$ and $\psi \equiv q$.

$$
\psi \;=\; X \, \big( \neg(\neg p) \, R \, \neg q \big)
$$

### Step 3: Eliminate Double Negation

Apply identity $\#3$: $\neg(\neg p) \equiv p$.

$$
\psi \;=\; X \, (p \, R \, \neg q)
$$

### Final PNF Result

$$
\boxed{\;\psi_{\text{PNF}} \;=\; X \, (p \, R \, \neg q)\;}
$$

This is in PNF because:
- The only operator is $X$ and $R$ (both permitted).
- The only negations are on atomic propositions ($q$ in $\neg q$).
- No $\neg$ appears on any compound sub-formula.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operator Push-Down Behaviour

The following matrix summarizes how a single $\neg$ interacts with every LTL operator during PNF conversion. **This matrix is KTU-exam favorite for 3-mark definition questions.**

| Operator | Original Form | After Pushing $\neg$ Past It | Identity Used |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Atomic | $\neg p$ | $\neg p$ (terminal) | — |
| Boolean AND | $\neg(\phi \wedge \psi)$ | $\neg\phi \vee \neg\psi$ | De Morgan |
| Boolean OR | $\neg(\phi \vee \psi)$ | $\neg\phi \wedge \neg\psi$ | De Morgan |
| Implication | $\neg(\phi \to \psi)$ | $\phi \wedge \neg\psi$ | $a \to b \equiv \neg a \vee b$, then De Morgan |
| Next ($X$) | $\neg X \phi$ | $X \neg\phi$ | Self-dual |
| Until ($U$) | $\neg(\phi U \psi)$ | $\neg\phi R \neg\psi$ | **Duality — introduces R** |
| Release ($R$) | $\neg(\phi R \psi)$ | $\neg\phi U \neg\psi$ | **Duality — introduces U** |
| Eventually ($F$) | $\neg F \phi$ | $G \neg\phi \equiv false R \neg\phi$ | $F \equiv true R$ |
| Globally ($G$) | $\neg G \phi$ | $F \neg\phi \equiv true R \neg\phi$ | $G \equiv false R$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PNF Conversion Pipeline (Algorithmic Flow)

The following block diagram captures the **complete algorithmic pipeline** for converting any arbitrary LTL formula into its Positive Normal Form. Each stage applies one class of rewrite rules from the cheat sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Arbitrary LTL Formula] --> stage1[Stage 1: Eliminate implies and iff]
    stage1 --> stage2[Stage 2: Collapse double negations]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3: Push negations inward over boolean operators]
    stage3 --> stage4[Stage 4: Push negations inward over temporal operators]
    stage4 --> stage5[Stage 5: Rewrite F and G using the R operator]
    stage5 --> resultNode[Formula in Positive Normal Form]

    stage1 -. uses .-> law1[Identity 1 and 2 from cheat sheet]
    stage2 -. uses .-> law2[Identity 3 from cheat sheet]
    stage3 -. uses .-> law3[Identities 4 and 5: De Morgan]
    stage4 -. uses .-> law4[Identities 6, 7, 8: X, U, R duality]
    stage5 -. uses .-> law5[Identities 9 and 10: F and G as Release]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- The **solid arrows** denote the main control flow.
- The **dotted arrows** denote *which identity from the cheat sheet* is applied at each stage. This is the most common KTU 14-mark answer structure: a numbered list of stages, each annotated with the underlying logical law.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow for the Release Operator $\phi R \psi$

This diagram captures the **runtime semantics** of the Release operator. It is the "machine" that the model checker simulates when checking $\pi \models \phi R \psi$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initNode[Start evaluation at time t equals 0] --> checkPhi{Does phi hold at current state?}
    checkPhi -->|Yes branch| successImmediate[Formula is SATISFIED, future unconstrained]
    checkPhi -->|No branch| checkPsi{Does psi hold at current state?}
    checkPsi -->|No branch| failureNode[Formula is VIOLATED, fail and backtrack]
    checkPsi -->|Yes branch| advance[Advance time t to t plus 1]
    advance --> checkPhi
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Top diamond ($\phi$ check):** If $\phi$ holds *now*, the release condition is met *immediately*. The model checker does **not** need to verify $\psi$'s future behaviour.
- **Bottom diamond ($\psi$ check):** If $\phi$ does **not** hold, $\psi$ *must* hold at the current state — otherwise the formula fails.
- **Loop:** The evaluator advances one time step and re-enters the $\phi$ check, creating the classic "wait-for-release" loop.

---

## 4.3 Architecture Map — Where PNF and Release Live in a Model Checker

This block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a typical LTL model checker, highlighting exactly *where* in the pipeline Positive Normal Form conversion occurs and *where* the Release operator gets processed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode[User LTL Property] --> parser[Lexical and Syntactic Parser]
    parser --> pnfConv[PNF Converter Module]
    pnfConv --> negPush[Negation Push Down Engine]
    negPush --> rewrite[F and G to R Rewriter]
    rewrite --> buchiGen[Buchi Automaton Generator]
    buchiGen --> productGen[Product Automaton with System Model]
    productGen --> emptCheck[Emptiness Check via Nested DFS]
    emptCheck --> outputNode[Counterexample or Success]

    pnfConv -. relies on .-> releaseDual[Release Until Duality Laws]
    buchiGen -. emits .-> releaseTrans[Release Transitions in Automaton]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- The **PNF Converter** is a *preprocessing module* that runs once, before Büchi automaton construction. This is the architectural position that makes PNF a foundational concept.
- The **Release-Until Duality** laws are used at *two* distinct points: once in the PNF converter, and once again when generating the Büchi transitions (since each $R$ formula becomes a specific type of accepting transition in the automaton).
- The **Emptiness Check** is where the final verdict is computed. The product automaton's language is checked for emptiness — if non-empty, a counterexample (witness) is returned.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU ESE Pattern):** Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks each with internal choice. Bloom's levels: *Apply* and *Analyze* dominate this topic.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the Release operator in LTL. State its formal semantics and show that it is the dual of the Until operator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Release operator** $R$ is a binary temporal connective in LTL. For formulas $\phi$ and $\psi$, the formula $\phi \, R \, \psi$ is read as *"$\psi$ holds until $\phi$ releases it, or $\psi$ holds forever."*

**Formal semantics:** A path $\pi$ satisfies $\phi \, R \, \psi$ if and only if either there exists a position $k \geq 0$ on $\pi$ such that $\pi^k \models \phi$ and $\psi$ holds at all positions $j$ with $0 \leq j < k$, OR $\psi$ holds at every position on $\pi$ (when $\phi$ never holds).

**Duality with Until:** The Release operator is the negation-dual of Until:

$$
\phi \, R \, \psi \;\equiv\; \neg(\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi)
$$

This is the **defining duality** of LTL and is the *only* identity that introduces $R$ when pushing negations during PNF conversion. **(3 marks — full credit)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is Positive Normal Form in LTL? List the three structural conditions a formula must satisfy to be in PNF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) formula is in **Positive Normal Form (PNF)** if it satisfies **all three** of the following structural conditions:

1. **No derived boolean connectives:** The implication ($\to$) and equivalence ($\leftrightarrow$) operators are **completely absent** from the formula.
2. **Negations are atomic-only:** The logical NOT ($\neg$) appears **only immediately before atomic propositions** (e.g., $\neg p$, $\neg q$), and never in front of compound sub-formulas.
3. **Restricted temporal vocabulary:** The only temporal operators permitted are $X$ (Next), $U$ (Until), and $R$ (Release). The derived operators $F$ (Eventually) and $G$ (Always) must be eliminated and re-expressed using $R$.

> **Significance:** PNF is the canonical input form for tableau-based and SAT-based LTL model checkers such as SPIN and NuSMV. **(3 marks — full credit)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** [7 marks] **Explain the Release operator in detail. Derive its relationship with the Until operator and discuss the semantics with two example paths.**

**(b)** [7 marks] **Convert the following LTL formula to Positive Normal Form, showing all intermediate steps:**

$$
\Phi \;=\; \neg \big( (p \wedge q) \to (F \, r \vee G \, s) \big)
$$

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Definition [1 mark]:** The Release operator $R$ is a binary temporal connective in LTL. The formula $\phi \, R \, \psi$ asserts that $\psi$ holds continuously *until* $\phi$ becomes true (at which point $\psi$ is "released" from its obligation). If $\phi$ never becomes true, then $\psi$ must hold forever.

**Formal semantics [2 marks]:** $\pi \models \phi \, R \, \psi$ iff one of the two conditions holds:
- There exists $k \geq 0$ such that $\pi^k \models \phi$ AND $\forall j, \, 0 \leq j < k, \, \pi^j \models \psi$.
- OR $\forall j \geq 0, \, \pi^j \models \psi$ (i.e., $\phi$ never holds AND $\psi$ holds forever).

**Duality derivation [2 marks]:** We derive the duality by direct semantic reasoning:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi &\models \phi \, R \, \psi \\
&\iff \big( \exists k \geq 0 \cdot \pi^k \models \phi \land \forall j < k \cdot \pi^j \models \psi \big) \lor \big( \forall j \geq 0 \cdot \pi^j \models \psi \big) \\
&\iff \neg \big( \neg\exists k \geq 0 \cdot \pi^k \models \phi \land \forall j < k \cdot \pi^j \models \psi \big) \quad \text{(by negation of disjunction)} \\
&\iff \neg \big( \forall k \geq 0 \cdot \pi^k \models \neg\phi \lor \exists j < k \cdot \pi^j \models \neg\psi \big) \\
&\iff \neg ( \neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi ) \quad \text{(by Until semantics)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $\phi \, R \, \psi \equiv \neg(\neg\phi \, U \, \neg\psi)$.

**Two example paths [2 marks]:**
- **Path 1:** $\pi = \{p, p, q, q, q, \ldots\}$. For $\phi = p, \, \psi = q$: $\phi$ holds at $k=0$, so $\phi R \psi$ is **trivially satisfied** (released immediately).
- **Path 2:** $\pi = \{q, q, q, q, \ldots\}$. For $\phi = p, \, \psi = q$: $\phi$ never holds, but $\psi$ holds forever — formula is **satisfied**.
- **Path 3 (counter-example):** $\pi = \{q, \neg q, q, \ldots\}$. $\phi$ never holds AND $\psi$ fails at position 1 — formula is **violated**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

The complete PNF conversion of $\Phi$ was worked out **step-by-step in Section 3.2** of these notes. The final result is:

$$
\boxed{\;\Phi_{\text{PNF}} \;=\; (p \wedge q) \;\wedge\; (false \, R \, \neg r) \;\wedge\; (true \, R \, \neg s)\;}
$$

The 7 marks are awarded as: 1 mark per major step (we have 5 named steps), plus 1 mark for the final answer, plus 1 mark for verification of PNF compliance. **(7/7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]`

**(a)** [7 marks] **Define Positive Normal Form. List all the conversion rules (rewrite identities) used in PNF conversion of an LTL formula, with each rule's purpose.**

**(b)** [7 marks] **Convert the following LTL formula to Positive Normal Form, showing all intermediate steps:**

$$
\Psi \;=\; (F \, p \;\wedge\; G \, q) \;\to\; (p \, R \, q)
$$

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**PNF Definition [1 mark]:** An LTL formula is in **Positive Normal Form (PNF)** if (1) it contains no $\to$ or $\leftrightarrow$, (2) $\neg$ appears only in front of atomic propositions, and (3) the only temporal operators are $X$, $U$, and $R$.

**Rewrite rules (identities) used in PNF conversion [6 marks — 0.5 mark each for 12 rules]:**

| # | Identity | Purpose |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | $\phi \to \psi \equiv \neg\phi \vee \psi$ | Eliminate implication |
| 2 | $\phi \leftrightarrow \psi \equiv (\neg\phi \vee \psi) \wedge (\neg\psi \vee \phi)$ | Eliminate equivalence |
| 3 | $\neg\neg\phi \equiv \phi$ | Collapse double negation |
| 4 | $\neg(\phi \wedge \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \vee \neg\psi$ | De Morgan (AND) |
| 5 | $\neg(\phi \vee \psi) \equiv \neg\phi \wedge \neg\psi$ | De Morgan (OR) |
| 6 | $\neg X \phi \equiv X \neg\phi$ | Push $\neg$ through Next |
| 7 | $\neg(\phi U \psi) \equiv \neg\phi R \neg\psi$ | Push $\neg$ through Until (**introduces R**) |
| 8 | $\neg(\phi R \psi) \equiv \neg\phi U \neg\psi$ | Push $\neg$ through Release (**introduces U**) |
| 9 | $F \phi \equiv true R \phi$ | Rewrite Eventually |
| 10 | $G \phi \equiv false R \phi$ | Rewrite Globally |
| 11 | $F \phi \equiv \neg G \neg\phi$ | Dual expansion (pre-PNF) |
| 12 | $G \phi \equiv \neg F \neg\phi$ | Dual expansion (pre-PNF) |

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 [1 mark]: Eliminate the implication** $\to$. Using identity #1:

$$
\Psi \;=\; \neg(F p \wedge G q) \;\vee\; (p R q)
$$

**Step 2 [1 mark]: Apply De Morgan's law** to $\neg(F p \wedge G q)$. Using identity #4:

$$
\Psi \;=\; \neg F p \;\vee\; \neg G q \;\vee\; (p R q)
$$

**Step 3 [1.5 marks]: Convert $\neg F p$ and $\neg G q$** to standard dual forms. Using identities #11 and #12:

$$
\Psi \;=\; (G \neg p) \;\vee\; (F \neg q) \;\vee\; (p R q)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $\neg F p$ becomes $G \neg p$ (the negation is "absorbed" into the atomic proposition while the temporal operator flips from $F$ to $G$). Similarly, $\neg G q$ becomes $F \neg q$.

**Step 4 [1.5 marks]: Rewrite $G$ and $F$ using the Release operator**. Using identities #9 and #10:

$$
\Psi \;=\; (false R \neg p) \;\vee\; (true R \neg q) \;\vee\; (p R q)
$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]: Verify PNF compliance.** The resulting formula contains:
- Only $\vee$ (no $\wedge$ at top, but $\wedge$ is permitted inside sub-formulas if any — there are none here).
- No $\to$, $\leftrightarrow$, $\neg$ on compound formulas, or $F$/$G$ operators.
- Only the temporal operators $R$ and its boolean combinations.

**Step 6 [1 mark]: Final PNF result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\Psi_{\text{PNF}} \;=\; (false R \neg p) \;\vee\; (true R \neg q) \;\vee\; (p R q)\;}
$$

**(7/7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to rewrite $F$ and $G$ in the final step.** Many students stop at the De Morgan stage and leave $F$ / $G$ in the formula. KTU strictly requires **all** $F$ / $G$ to be eliminated using $true R$ and $false R$ respectively. **Deduct 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing the duality direction.** The rule is $\neg(\phi U \psi) \equiv \neg\phi R \neg\psi$ — the $\neg$ goes to **both** $\phi$ AND $\psi$. A common student error is writing $\phi R \neg\psi$, which loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify PNF compliance.** Always end your answer with a brief 3-bullet "PNF check" listing the three structural conditions. KTU examiners award bonus credit for this self-verification.
> 4. **Not showing the intermediate steps.** Skipping the De Morgan or duality step in a 14-mark answer can cost you 2–3 marks, even if the final answer is correct. **Always show all work.**
> 5. **Mis-stating the Release semantics.** A frequent error: *"$\phi R \psi$ means $\psi$ holds until $\phi$ becomes true."* This is **almost** right but misses the crucial "OR $\psi$ holds forever" clause. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this omission.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 🧠 Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam)

- [ ] **Release semantics:** $\phi R \psi$ means *"$\psi$ holds until $\phi$ releases it, OR $\psi$ holds forever."* Never forget the **forever-clause** when $\phi$ never occurs.
- [ ] **The defining duality:** $\phi R \psi \equiv \neg(\neg\phi U \neg\psi)$ — memorize this verbatim. It is the *only* identity that introduces $R$ during PNF conversion.
- [ ] **$F$ and $G$ as Releases:** $F \phi \equiv true R \phi$ and $G \phi \equiv false R \phi$. Know these cold — they are the final rewrite step in every PNF conversion.
- [ ] **PNF = 3 constraints:** (1) No $\to$/$\leftrightarrow$, (2) $\neg$ only on atoms, (3) Only $X$, $U$, $R$ as temporal operators.
- [ ] **The negation push-down rules (cheat sheet table) is examinable verbatim.** Be able to recite all 12 identities in Section 2.3.
- [ ] **Why PNF matters:** It is the mandatory preprocessing step for tableau-based model checkers (SPIN, NuSMV). The conversion is $O(n)$ in formula size.
- [ ] **Fixed-point form of $R$:** $\phi R \psi \equiv \psi \vee (\phi \wedge X(\phi R \psi))$ — used in tableau expansion, expect a 3-mark question asking to derive this.
- [ ] **Bounded model checking connection:** PNF formulas translate cleanly into propositional CNF for SAT solving — mention this if asked about engineering applications.
- [ ] **Dual symmetry:** $U$ and $R$ are exact duals. If a formula has only $U$, its negation has only $R$ (and vice versa). This is the structural signature of the duality.
- [ ] **Common trap:** $F \phi \not\equiv \phi U \, true$. The correct form is $F \phi \equiv true U \phi$ (or equivalently, $true R \phi$). Don't mix these up.

---

### 📚 Mapping to Course Outcomes (KTU 2024 Scheme)

| Course Outcome | How This Topic Maps |
| --- | --- |
| **CO2:** Apply temporal logics for specifying system properties | Release operator enables succinct specification of "wait-for-event" patterns |
| **CO3:** Construct verification procedures using LTL/CTL | PNF is the prerequisite step for tableau-based model checking |
| **CO5:** Analyze verification challenges in concurrent systems | Release naturally models synchronization barriers in concurrent protocols |

**Bloom's Taxonomy Levels Exercised:** *Apply* (PNF conversion), *Analyze* (semantic decomposition), *Evaluate* (duality proofs).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fairness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fairness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Fairness

**Fairness** in automated verification is a class of scheduling (or behavioral) constraints imposed on a concurrent or reactive system to restrict attention to those execution paths in which no individual process, transition, or component is starved or perpetually ignored. Formally, fairness is a set of path quantifier conditions over a Kripke structure (or labelled transition system) used to eliminate unrealistic or degenerate interleavings during model checking.

$$ \text{Fairness} = \{ \pi \in \text{Paths}(M) \mid \pi \text{ satisfies the fairness constraint } \mathcal{F} \} $$

where $M$ is the Kripke structure, $\pi$ is an infinite path, and $\mathcal{F}$ is a fairness condition (a set of properties expressed in temporal logic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Fairness is essential when verifying concurrent and reactive systems modeled using **LTL (Linear Temporal Logic)** and **CTL (Computation Tree Logic)**. Without fairness, a model checker may produce **spurious counterexamples** that represent unrealistic execution orders.

## 1.2 The Problem Fairness Solves — A Real-World Analogy

**Analogy: The Elevator Control System**

Imagine a 5-floor building with **two elevators** controlled by a single scheduler. Without fairness, the scheduler could theoretically serve the *same floor* forever, ignoring the other floors. Technically, such a path exists in the state graph — but it is **not a realistic behavior**.

A **fair scheduler** ensures:
- Every request eventually gets a turn.
- No floor is perpetually skipped.

This is exactly what fairness guarantees in a model: it prunes away infinite paths that are mathematically valid but practically **unrealizable or unfair**.

## 1.3 Why Fairness Is Needed in Model Checking

| Problem Without Fairness | Consequence | How Fairness Helps |
|---|---|---|
| Scheduler can ignore a process forever | Spurious counterexample (false positive) | Restricts model to fair paths only |
| Mutual exclusion is "violated" by unrealistic interleavings | Verification of liveness fails | Fair paths reflect real-world scheduling |
| Process is starved in a race condition | Correct programs reported as incorrect | Guarantees each transition is exercised |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Kripke Structure:** A Kripke structure is a 4-tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ where $S$ is a finite set of states, $S_0 \subseteq S$ is the set of initial states, $R \subseteq S \times S$ is the transition relation, and $L: S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is a labelling function mapping each state to the set of atomic propositions true in that state.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fairness

Fairness is broadly classified into **three categories** in the context of temporal logic model checking:

### 2.1.1 Unconditional (Impartial) Justice Fairness

A path $\pi = s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots$ is **unconditionally fair** for a set of transitions $T$ if every transition in $T$ is taken **infinitely often** along $\pi$.

$$ \text{Unconditional Fairness}: \quad \bigwedge_{t \in T} \Box \Diamond \; t $$

Read as: "**always** ($\Box$), **eventually** ($\Diamond$), for every transition $t$."

### 2.1.2 Strong Fairness (Compassion / Streett Fairness)

A path is **strongly fair** with respect to a set of conditional transitions $T$ if every transition $t \in T$ that is **continuously enabled** is eventually taken.

$$ \text{Strong Fairness}: \quad \bigwedge_{(p_i, t_i) \in T} \Box \Diamond \, p_i \;\Rightarrow\; \Box \Diamond \, t_i $$

Read as: "If a transition's guard $p_i$ holds **infinitely often**, then the transition $t_i$ itself occurs **infinitely often**."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong fairness** is **weaker** than unconditional fairness but **stronger** than weak fairness. It applies to transitions that may be conditionally enabled.

### 2.1.3 Weak Fairness (Justice / Basic Fairness)

A path is **weakly fair** if every transition that is **continuously enabled** from some point onwards is eventually taken.

$$ \text{Weak Fairness}: \quad \bigwedge_{(p_i, t_i) \in T} \Diamond \Box \, p_i \;\Rightarrow\; \Diamond \Box \, t_i $$

Read as: "If a transition's guard $p_i$ holds **always from some point**, then the transition $t_i$ itself holds **always from some point**."

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix of Fairness Notions

| Property | Unconditional Justice | Weak Fairness | Strong Fairness |
|---|---|---|---|
| LTL Formula Pattern | $\Box \Diamond t$ | $\Box \Diamond p \Rightarrow \Box \Diamond t$ | $\Diamond \Box p \Rightarrow \Diamond \Box t$ |
| Strength | Strongest | Weakest | Intermediate |
| When Applicable | Always-enabled transitions | Conditionally enabled transitions | Conditionally enabled transitions |
| Expressiveness in LTL | Yes | Yes | Requires additional operators (not in pure LTL) |
| Preserves CTL Model Checking Complexity | Yes (polynomial) | Yes (polynomial) | No (exponential in some cases) |

## 2.3 Fairness in LTL and CTL

### 2.3.1 Fair LTL

In **Linear Temporal Logic with Fairness**, an LTL formula $\phi$ holds along a fair path $\pi$:

$$ M, \pi \models_f \phi \quad \iff \quad \pi \models \mathcal{F} \;\Rightarrow\; \pi \models \phi $$

This means: the formula $\phi$ must be satisfied **on all paths that satisfy the fairness condition** $\mathcal{F}$.

### 2.3.2 Fair CTL (CTL*)

A CTL* formula with fairness is interpreted as:

$$ M, s \models_f \phi \quad \iff \quad \text{For all fair paths } \pi \text{ starting at } s, \; \pi \models \phi $$

$$ M, s \models_f \exists \phi \quad \iff \quad \text{There exists a fair path } \pi \text{ starting at } s, \; \pi \models \phi $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Path Quantifier Restriction:** In fair CTL, both the universal ($\forall$) and existential ($\exists$) path quantifiers are restricted to **fair paths only**. This is a crucial modification from standard CTL semantics.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | LTL/CTL* Expression | Semantics |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unconditional Fairness | $\Box \Diamond t$ | Transition $t$ fires infinitely often |
| 2 | Weak Fairness | $\Box \Diamond p \rightarrow \Box \Diamond t$ | If guard holds infinitely often, action fires infinitely often |
| 3 | Strong Fairness | $\Diamond \Box p \rightarrow \Diamond \Box t$ | If guard always holds, action always fires |
| 4 | Fair Path Definition | $\pi \in \text{FairPaths}(M, \mathcal{F})$ | Path satisfies all fairness constraints |
| 5 | Fair LTL Satisfaction | $M \models_f \phi$ | $\phi$ holds on all fair paths of $M$ |
| 6 | Fair CTL State | $M, s \models_f \forall \phi$ | $\phi$ holds on all fair paths from $s$ |
| 7 | Streett Condition | $\bigwedge_i (\Box \Diamond p_i \rightarrow \Box \Diamond q_i)$ | General form of strong fairness |
| 8 | Rabin Condition | $\bigvee_i (\Box \Diamond p_i \wedge \Diamond \Box q_i)$ | Dual of Streett, used in automata theory |
| 9 | Fair Emptiness Check | $L(\mathcal{A}) \cap \text{FairPaths}(M) = \emptyset$ | Product automaton has no accepting fair run |
| 10 | State Explosion Mitigation | $\vert S \vert$ of product $= O(\vert S_M \vert \cdot \vert Q_{\mathcal{A}} \vert)$ | Complexity bound for fair model checking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Fairness conditions are routinely used in **hardware verification** (e.g., verifying bus arbitration protocols like AMBA AXI), **operating system kernels** (e.g., process schedulers), and **distributed consensus protocols** (e.g., Paxos, Raft) to ensure that liveness properties are not vacuously satisfied.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Standard CTL to Fair CTL Semantics

### 3.1.1 Standard CTL Semantics (Recap)

In standard CTL, for a state $s$ in Kripke structure $M$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M, s &\models \forall \phi \quad &\iff& \quad \text{for all paths } \pi \text{ starting at } s, \; \pi \models \phi \\
M, s &\models \exists \phi \quad &\iff& \quad \text{there exists a path } \pi \text{ starting at } s, \; \pi \models \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Restriction to Fair Paths

To incorporate fairness, we restrict the universal and existential quantifiers to operate **only over the set of fair paths** $FairPaths(M, \mathcal{F})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FairPaths}(M, \mathcal{F}) &= \{ \pi \in \text{Paths}(M) \mid \pi \models \mathcal{F} \} \\
M, s &\models_f \forall \phi \quad &\iff& \quad \text{for all } \pi \in \text{FairPaths}(M, \mathcal{F}) \text{ starting at } s, \; \pi \models \phi \\
M, s &\models_f \exists \phi \quad &\iff& \quad \text{there exists } \pi \in \text{FairPaths}(M, \mathcal{F}) \text{ starting at } s, \; \pi \models \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Transition from Standard to Fair: A Worked Example

Consider verifying the liveness property "**process P1 eventually enters its critical section**" expressed as $\forall \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1$ on a 2-process mutual exclusion system $M$.

**Step 1: Standard Model Checking**

$$
M \models \forall \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1
$$

The model checker returns **FALSE**, because there exists a path where process P2 is *always* scheduled, starving P1. This path is the **counterexample**.

**Step 2: Identifying the Source of the Counterexample**

The path $\pi_{unfair}$ has the form: $s_0 \rightarrow s_1 \rightarrow s_2 \rightarrow \ldots$ where at every state, the only enabled transition is one belonging to P2. The LTL property:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_{unfair} &\not\models \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1 \\
\text{because } \pi_{unfair} &\models \Box \, \text{not\_crit}_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Adding a Fairness Constraint**

We define the fairness constraint $\mathcal{F} = \Box \Diamond \, \text{enable}(P1)$. This says that **P1's enabling condition holds infinitely often**.

**Step 4: Re-evaluating Under Fairness**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &\models_f \forall \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1 \\
\iff M, s_0 &\models_f \forall \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1 \\
\iff \text{for all } \pi &\in \text{FairPaths}(M, \mathcal{F}) \text{ from } s_0, \; \pi \models \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since on every fair path, P1's transition is enabled infinitely often, and the system is not broken, P1 must eventually enter the critical section infinitely often. The model checker now returns **TRUE**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Fairness assumptions convert **spurious counterexamples** (caused by unrealistic scheduling) into non-counterexamples. They are part of the **system model**, not the property.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Product Automaton for Fair Model Checking

When verifying fair LTL, the standard approach constructs a **product automaton**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg \phi} &= (S_M \times Q_{\mathcal{A}}, \, S_{0,M} \times Q_{0,\mathcal{A}}, \, R_{M \times \mathcal{A}}, \, L_{M \times \mathcal{A}}) \\
\text{where } R_{M \times \mathcal{A}} &= \{ ((s, q), (s', q')) \mid (s, s') \in R_M \text{ and } q \xrightarrow{L(s')} q' \}
\end{aligned}
$$

The verification reduces to a **fair emptiness check**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M \models_f \phi \quad &\iff \quad L(M \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg \phi}) \cap \text{FairPaths}(M \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg \phi}) = \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Space Bound:** The product automaton has size $O(\vert S_M \vert \cdot 2^{\vert \phi \vert})$, where $\vert \phi \vert$ is the length of the LTL formula. Fairness is encoded via the **acceptance condition** of the Büchi automaton (e.g., Streett acceptance for strong fairness).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Three Worked Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Unconditional Fairness Applied to a Token Ring

A token ring has 4 nodes, and a token circulates. The fairness condition is:

$$
\mathcal{F} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{4} \Box \Diamond \, \text{node}_i\text{\_has\_token}
$$

This is **unconditional justice** — every node receives the token infinitely often.

### Scenario 2: Strong Fairness for a Condition Variable

A producer waits on a condition variable $cv$. The transition $t_{produce}$ is enabled when the buffer is non-full. Strong fairness:

$$
\mathcal{F}_{strong} = \Box \Diamond (\text{buffer\_not\_full}) \;\rightarrow\; \Box \Diamond \, t_{produce}
$$

### Scenario 3: Weak Fairness for an Interrupt Handler

An interrupt handler $t_{handle}$ is enabled only when an interrupt is pending. Weak fairness:

$$
\mathcal{F}_{weak} = \Diamond \Box \, \text{interrupt\_pending} \;\rightarrow\; \Diamond \Box \, t_{handle}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture: Fairness in Model Checking Pipeline

The following **block-level functional architecture** shows how fairness integrates into a standard model checker.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Description TS] --> B[Kripke Structure M]
    C[Property in LTL or CTL phi] --> D[Negation and Translation]
    D --> E[Negated Buchi Automaton A]
    F[Fairness Constraint F] --> G[Fair Path Filter]
    B --> G
    G --> H[Fair Product M x A]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Emptiness Check Algorithm]
    I --> J{Accepting Fair Run Exists?}
    J -- Yes --> K[VERIFICATION FAILS - Counterexample]
    J -- No --> L[VERIFICATION SUCCESS - Property Holds]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Fairness Notion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Fairness Notion] --> Q1{Is the transition always enabled?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> U[Use Unconditional Justice: Box Diamond t]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does the transition need a condition that holds infinitely often?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> SF[Use Strong Fairness: Box Diamond p then Box Diamond t]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does the condition hold continuously after some point?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> WF[Use Weak Fairness: Diamond Box p then Diamond Box t]
    Q3 -- No --> R[Re-examine the system model]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: Fairness and Temporal Operators

```mermaid
graph LR
    F[Fairness Constraints] --> U[Unconditional Justice]
    F --> W[Weak Fairness Justice]
    F --> S[Strong Fairness Compassion]
    U --> O1[Box Diamond t]
    W --> O2[Box Diamond p implies Box Diamond t]
    S --> O3[Diamond Box p implies Diamond Box t]
    O1 --> R[Restricts Paths in Model]
    O2 --> R
    O3 --> R
    R --> MV[Model Verification with Fair Paths]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Spurious vs. Real Counterexample Resolution

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant MC as Model Checker
    participant Fair as Fairness Module
    User->>MC: Verify M satisfies phi
    MC->>MC: Explore all paths
    MC-->>User: FAIL: Counterexample CE1 found
    User->>Fair: Add Fairness Constraint F
    Fair->>Fair: Filter out unfair paths
    Fair-->>User: Re-verify with FairPaths
    MC->>Fair: Check CE1 in FairPaths?
    Fair-->>MC: CE1 is not in FairPaths (eliminated)
    MC-->>User: PASS: Property holds on fair paths
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define fairness in the context of model checking. Why is it needed?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Fairness is a set of conditions imposed on a Kripke structure to restrict attention to execution paths where no component is starved or perpetually ignored. It eliminates **spurious counterexamples** that arise from unrealistic interleavings in concurrent systems. Fairness is needed because:
1. **Without fairness**, a scheduler could ignore a process forever, making liveness properties falsely fail.
2. **With fairness**, only realistic execution paths are considered, aligning verification with real-world system behavior.

Fairness is typically expressed as LTL constraints like $\Box \Diamond t$ (unconditional justice) or $\Box \Diamond p \rightarrow \Box \Diamond t$ (strong fairness).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between weak fairness and strong fairness with LTL formulas.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Weak Fairness | Strong Fairness |
|---|---|---|
| LTL Formula | $\Box \Diamond p \rightarrow \Box \Diamond t$ | $\Diamond \Box p \rightarrow \Diamond \Box t$ |
| Guard Condition | Holds infinitely often | Holds continuously from some point |
| Strength | Weaker (easier to satisfy) | Stronger (harder to satisfy) |
| Real-World Meaning | If repeatedly possible, must eventually happen | If persistently possible, must persistently happen |

Weak fairness handles **intermittently enabled** transitions, while strong fairness handles **continuously enabled** ones.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three main types of fairness in model checking — **unconditional justice**, **weak fairness**, and **strong fairness** — with their LTL/CTL* representations. Give one real-world example of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a two-process mutual exclusion system with semaphore $S = 1$. Process P1 and P2 both have transitions $t_1$ and $t_2$ for entering the critical section. Formulate the fairness condition to ensure that **both processes get a fair chance to enter the critical section infinitely often**. Verify the liveness property $M \models_f \forall \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1$.

---

#### Model Solution A(a):

**Three Types of Fairness:**

1. **Unconditional Justice (Impartial Fairness):**
   A path is just if every transition in a set $T$ is taken infinitely often. The LTL formula:

   $$ \mathcal{F}_{UJ} = \bigwedge_{t \in T} \Box \Diamond \, t $$

   **Example:** A round-robin CPU scheduler ensuring every process gets CPU time infinitely often.

2. **Weak Fairness (Justice):**
   A path is weakly fair if every continuously-enabled transition is eventually taken. The LTL formula:

   $$ \mathcal{F}_{WF} = \bigwedge_{(p_i, t_i) \in T} \Box \Diamond \, p_i \;\rightarrow\; \Box \Diamond \, t_i $$

   **Example:** A printer queue where a print job, once submitted, eventually gets printed.

3. **Strong Fairness (Compassion):**
   A path is strongly fair if every transition that is enabled infinitely often is taken infinitely often. The LTL formula:

   $$ \mathcal{F}_{SF} = \bigwedge_{(p_i, t_i) \in T} \Box \Diamond \, p_i \;\rightarrow\; \Box \Diamond \, t_i $$

   **Example:** A network packet transmission where a channel, whenever it is free, eventually transmits the next packet.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** Students often confuse the LTL formulas of weak and strong fairness. The key difference is the **guard's temporal pattern**: weak uses $\Box \Diamond$ (infinitely often) for the guard, while strong uses $\Diamond \Box$ (always from some point). Marks are deducted for mixing these up.

---

#### Model Solution A(b):

**Step 1: Identify the Transitions and Guards**

Let the guards be:
- $p_1$: Process P1's request to enter the critical section is enabled.
- $p_2$: Process P2's request to enter the critical section is enabled.
- $t_1$: P1 enters the critical section.
- $t_2$: P2 enters the critical section.

**Step 2: Formulate the Fairness Condition**

To ensure both processes get a fair chance:

$$ \mathcal{F} = \Box \Diamond \, t_1 \;\wedge\; \Box \Diamond \, t_2 $$

This is **unconditional justice** because both $t_1$ and $t_2$ are taken infinitely often.

**Step 3: Express the Liveness Property**

We want to verify:

$$ \phi = \forall \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1 $$

meaning: "On all paths, P1 is in the critical section infinitely often."

**Step 4: Fair Model Checking**

We check:

$$ M, s_0 \models_f \forall \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1 $$

Using fair CTL semantics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M, s_0 \models_f \forall \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1 \;&\iff\; \text{for all } \pi \in \text{FairPaths}(M, \mathcal{F}) \text{ from } s_0, \; \pi \models \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion**

On any fair path, $t_1$ is taken infinitely often, and $t_1$ leads to $\text{crit}_1$. Therefore:

$$ M \models_f \forall \Box \Diamond \, \text{crit}_1 \quad \text{(TRUE)} $$

The system satisfies the liveness property under the fairness assumption.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **fair CTL (CTL with fairness)** and explain how the path quantifiers $\forall$ and $\exists$ are interpreted under fairness assumptions. Provide the formal semantic rules.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct the product automaton for fair model checking of an LTL property $\phi$ against a Kripke structure $M$ under fairness $\mathcal{F}$. Explain how the fair emptiness check works and state its time complexity.

---

#### Model Solution B(a):

**Definition of Fair CTL:**

Fair CTL is an extension of standard CTL where the path quantifiers $\forall$ and $\exists$ range only over **fair paths** — paths that satisfy a given fairness constraint $\mathcal{F}$.

**Formal Semantic Rules:**

For state $s$ in Kripke structure $M$ with fairness constraint $\mathcal{F}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M, s &\models_f \forall \phi \quad &\iff& \quad \text{for all } \pi \in \text{FairPaths}(M, \mathcal{F}) \text{ with } \pi[0] = s, \; \pi \models \phi \\
M, s &\models_f \exists \phi \quad &\iff& \quad \text{there exists } \pi \in \text{FairPaths}(M, \mathcal{F}) \text{ with } \pi[0] = s, \; \pi \models \phi
\end{aligned}
$$

where:

$$ \text{FairPaths}(M, \mathcal{F}) = \{ \pi = s_0 s_1 s_2 \ldots \in \text{Paths}(M) \mid \pi \models \mathcal{F} \} $$

**Key Difference from Standard CTL:**

In standard CTL, $\forall$ and $\exists$ range over **all paths** in $M$. In fair CTL, they range only over **fair paths**. This restricts the verification to realistic execution behaviors.

**Example:** A process scheduler has a state space of 10 states. Without fairness, the path "P1 runs forever" is valid. With fairness $\mathcal{F} = \Box \Diamond \, \text{schedule}(P2)$, this path is excluded, and only paths where P2 gets scheduled infinitely often are considered.

---

#### Model Solution B(b):

**Step 1: Translate LTL to Büchi Automaton**

The negated property $\neg \phi$ is translated into a Büchi automaton $\mathcal{A}_{\neg \phi}$ with state set $Q_{\mathcal{A}}$, transition relation $\delta_{\mathcal{A}}$, and acceptance condition $F_{\mathcal{A}}$.

**Step 2: Construct the Product Automaton**

$$ M \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg \phi} = (S_M \times Q_{\mathcal{A}}, \, R, \, F) $$

where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
((s, q), (s', q')) \in R \quad &\iff \quad (s, s') \in R_M \text{ and } q \xrightarrow{L(s')} q' \text{ in } \mathcal{A}_{\neg \phi} \\
\text{Accepting states } F \quad &= \quad S_M \times F_{\mathcal{A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Incorporate the Fairness Constraint**

The fairness $\mathcal{F}$ is encoded as a set of **fair states** $F_{fair} \subseteq S_M \times Q_{\mathcal{A}}$ in the product:

$$ F_{fair} = \{ (s, q) \mid s \models p_i \text{ for some fairness clause } (\Box \Diamond p_i \rightarrow \Box \Diamond t_i) \in \mathcal{F} \} $$

**Step 4: Fair Emptiness Check**

We check:

$$ L(M \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg \phi}) \cap \text{FairPaths}(M \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg \phi}, F_{fair}) \stackrel{?}{=} \emptyset $$

- If **non-empty**: there is a fair path in $M$ that satisfies $\neg \phi$ → **verification fails** with counterexample.
- If **empty**: no fair path violates $\phi$ → **verification succeeds**.

**Step 5: Time Complexity**

The product automaton has size $O(\vert S_M \vert \cdot \vert Q_{\mathcal{A}} \vert)$. The emptiness check (nested DFS) runs in $O(\vert S_M \vert \cdot \vert Q_{\mathcal{A}} \vert)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently forget to:
> 1. **Negate** $\phi$ before constructing the Büchi automaton.
> 2. Distinguish between the **Büchi acceptance condition** of the automaton and the **fairness condition** of the system — they are separate.
> 3. State the **emptiness check** (not reachability) as the verification algorithm. Liveness properties need emptiness, not reachability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fairness** restricts path quantifiers in CTL/LTL to **realistic execution paths** in concurrent systems.
- **Unconditional Justice** uses $\Box \Diamond t$ — every transition fires infinitely often.
- **Weak Fairness** uses $\Box \Diamond p \rightarrow \Box \Diamond t$ — if guard holds infinitely often, transition fires infinitely often.
- **Strong Fairness** uses $\Diamond \Box p \rightarrow \Diamond \Box t$ — if guard holds always from some point, transition holds always from some point.
- Fairness eliminates **spurious counterexamples** caused by unrealistic scheduler interleavings.
- Fair CTL modifies both $\forall$ and $\exists$ to range only over **fair paths**.
- Fair model checking reduces to a **fair emptiness check** on the product automaton $M \times \mathcal{A}_{\neg \phi}$.
- Product automaton size is $O(\vert S_M \vert \cdot 2^{\vert \phi \vert})$; emptiness check is polynomial in this size.
- Strong fairness is **expressible in CTL*** (using Streett acceptance) but **not in plain CTL**.
- Liveness properties like $\forall \Box \Diamond \, \phi$ are **only meaningful under fairness assumptions**; without fairness, they can be vacuously violated.
- Common applications: **mutual exclusion protocols**, **CPU schedulers**, **bus arbiters**, **distributed consensus**, **hardware protocols**.
- **Exam Tip:** When asked about fairness, always state (1) the LTL formula, (2) the type (weak/strong/unconditional), and (3) a real-world analogy.
- **Pitfall:** Do not confuse the acceptance condition of the Büchi automaton (for $\phi$) with the fairness condition of the system (for scheduling).
- **Complexity:** Fair model checking is in **PSPACE** for LTL and **polynomial** for CTL with weak/unconditional fairness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety and Liveness in LTL (Definitions only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Safety and Liveness in LTL (Definitions only) # Safety and Liveness in LTL — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Why Classify LTL Properties? In **Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)** model checking, every property we wish to verify belongs to a vast universe of temporal behaviors. However, decades of research (notably by **Lamport (1977)** and **Alpern & Schneider (1985)**) have shown that almost every meaningful property can be cleanly partitioned into two orthogonal, complementary classes: 1. **Safety Properties** — *"Nothing bad ever happens."* 2. **Liveness Properties** — *"Something good eventually happens."* > [!IMPORTANT] > This dichotomy is foundational to **PECNT526 – Automated Verification**. Every LTL specification you will ever write in model checkers like **SPIN**, **NuSMV**, or **CADP** reduces to a Boolean combination of safety and liveness sub-properties. --- ## 1.2 Formal Definition — Safety Property > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Safety Property).** A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is a **safety property** if and only if for every infinite word $\sigma \notin L$, there exists a finite prefix $x$ of $\sigma$ such that: > > $$\forall \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : \quad x \cdot \tau \notin L$$ In plain English: a property is **safe** if a violation can be detected by observing a **finite prefix** of the execution trace. The "bad thing" happens at a specific, identifiable moment. ### Conceptual Analogy > Imagine a traffic signal controller. The safety property is: > *"Green and Red lights are **never** on at the same time."* > The moment you observe a state where `Green = 1 ∧ Red = 1`, you have already witnessed a violation — no need to look at the future. A finite trace was enough to "falsify" the property. --- ## 1.3 Formal Definition — Liveness Property > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Liveness Property).** A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is a **liveness property** if and only if for every finite word $x \in \Sigma^*$, there exists an infinite continuation $\tau \in \Sigma^\omega$ such that: > > $$x \cdot \tau \in L$$ In plain English: a property is **live** if **no finite prefix** of a trace can ever prove that the property is violated. The "good thing" may happen arbitrarily far in the future — it cannot be refuted by any finite history. ### Conceptual Analogy > Consider a print server. The liveness property is: > *"Every submitted print job is **eventually** printed."* > No matter how long you watch the server queue, you can never conclude "this job will never be printed" — maybe the printer is just slow. A liveness violation is **inherently an infinite** phenomenon: it requires seeing the *entire* infinite execution (or lack thereof). --- ## 1.4 The Alpern–Schneider Decomposition Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (Alpern & Schneider, 1985).** *Every* non-trivial property $P$ over a closed system can be expressed as the intersection of a safety property $S$ and a liveness property $L$: > > $$P = S \cap L$$ This is the theoretical bedrock of the safety/liveness classification and explains why these two classes are sufficient for the entire universe of $\omega$-regular properties verifiable by LTL model checkers. --- ## 1.5 Canonical Examples Table (Quick Reference) | Property Class | Informal Statement | LTL Formula Pattern | Detected By | |---|---|---|---| | **Safety** | Mutual exclusion holds | $\Box \neg (c_1 \land c_2)$ | Finite prefix | | **Safety** | Partial correctness | $\phi \Rightarrow \psi$ (Hoare-style) | Finite prefix | | **Safety** | No unreachable state used | $\Box \neg \text{bad}$ | Finite prefix | | **Liveness** | Request is eventually granted | $\Box(req \Rightarrow \Diamond grant)$ | Infinite trace | | **Liveness** | Process eventually terminates | $\Diamond \; terminated$ | Infinite trace | | **Liveness** | Starvation freedom | $\Box \Diamond \; served$ | Infinite trace | # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Characteristic Logic — How to Identify Each Class When you are given an arbitrary LTL formula, you must be able to classify it quickly. Below is the **decision logic** KTU examiners expect you to follow. ### 2.1.1 Safety Identification Checklist A formula $\varphi$ is a **safety** formula if it can be rewritten in the form: $$\varphi \;\equiv\; \Box \psi \quad \text{where } \psi \text{ is a purely propositional (past-/state-based) formula}$$ Equivalently, $\varphi$ contains **only the operators** $\Box, \neg, \land, \lor$ — but **never** the operators $\Diamond$ or $\mathcal{U}$ (until) at the *outermost* temporal level. > [!TIP] > **Examiner Heuristic:** If the formula says "**always** something holds" and the inner formula has no future-time references, it is a **safety** property. ### 2.1.2 Liveness Identification Checklist A formula $\varphi$ is a **liveness** formula if it can be rewritten in a form containing at least one $\Diamond$ or $\mathcal{U}$ operator that is **not subsumable** under an outer $\Box$. Canonical liveness skeletons: $$ \begin{aligned} \varphi_1 &\equiv \Diamond\, p \\ \varphi_2 &\equiv \Box(p \Rightarrow \Diamond q) \\ \varphi_3 &\equiv (p \,\mathcal{U}\, q) \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner Heuristic:** If the formula promises that "**eventually** something will occur" and the event is in the open future, it is a **liveness** property. --- ## 2.2 The Prefix-Closure Theorem (Safety Closure) Every safety language $S$ can be equivalently characterized by its **prefix-closure**: $$\text{Pref}(S) \;=\; \left\{ x \in \Sigma^* \mid \forall \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : x \cdot \tau \in S \right\}$$ The set of *all* finite good prefixes completely determines $S$. This is why safety violations are **always reproducible** in finite test runs. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Memorize this table verbatim — it is the single most testable artifact in this topic. | # | Concept | Formal Statement | Intuition | KTU Weight | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Safety — Definition | $\forall \sigma \notin L, \exists x \prec \sigma : \forall \tau,\; x\tau \notin L$ | Finite prefix refutes | **High** | | 2 | Liveness — Definition | $\forall x \in \Sigma^*, \exists \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : x\tau \in L$ | No finite prefix refutes | **High** | | 3 | Alpern–Schneider | $P = \text{Safety}(P) \cap \text{Liveness}(P)$ | Every property decomposes | **High** | | 4 | Safety Skeleton | $\varphi \equiv \Box \psi$ | "Always $\psi$" | **Medium** | | 5 | Liveness Skeleton | $\varphi \equiv \Diamond \psi$ or $\Box(p \Rightarrow \Diamond q)$ | "Eventually $\psi$" | **Medium** | | 6 | Detection Bound | Safety: finite / Liveness: infinite | Witnesses differ in nature | **Medium** | | 7 | Counter-example Type | Safety: finite path / Liveness: lasso / cycle | SPIN/NuSMV output shape | **High** | | 8 | Büchi Acceptance | Every LTL property is a Büchi-$\omega$-regular language | Underpinning math | **Low** | | 9 | Persistence vs. Reactivity | Safety ↔ Persistence / Liveness ↔ Reactivity (Manna-Pnueli) | Alternate naming | **Medium** | | 10 | Closure under $\neg$ | $\neg(\text{Safety}) \equiv \text{Liveness}$ and vice-versa (duality) | Useful for proofs | **Medium** | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Safety Property Used | Liveness Property Used | |---|---|---| | **Aerospace Flight Control** | No two control surfaces commanded to extreme opposite deflections simultaneously | If a fault is detected, the backup system eventually engages | | **Railway Signalling (CBTC)** | Two trains never occupy the same block (interlocking) | Every train request eventually receives a movement authority | | **Banking Ledger (DB)** | No account balance goes below zero (no overdraft) | Every pending transaction is eventually committed or aborted | | **Industrial PLC** | Emergency stop always overrides auto mode | Every queued job is eventually processed by the robot arm | | **Network Protocols (TCP)** | Sender never sends into a closed window | Every retransmitted packet is eventually acknowledged | # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Proving a Given LTL Formula is a Safety Property We work through the **canonical proof technique** for showing that a formula is safe. **Claim.** $\varphi \equiv \Box(\neg p)$ is a safety property. **Step 1.** Restate $\varphi$ in the canonical safety skeleton form. $$ \begin{aligned} \varphi &\equiv \Box(\neg p) \\ \end{aligned} $$ We identify $\psi \equiv \neg p$, which contains **no temporal operators** — only propositional negation. Therefore $\varphi$ is in the form $\Box \psi$. **Step 2.** Construct the violating prefix. Suppose an infinite trace $\sigma = s_0, s_1, s_2, \dots$ violates $\varphi$. Then there exists some position $i \in \mathbb{N}$ such that: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma, i &\not\models \Box(\neg p) \\ \Rightarrow \quad \sigma, i &\models \neg(\neg p) \\ \Rightarrow \quad \sigma, i &\models p \end{aligned} $$ The violating prefix is the finite word $x = s_0, s_1, \dots, s_i$. Every infinite continuation $x \cdot \tau$ of this prefix still contains the offending state $s_i$ at position $i$, so $p$ remains true, and $\Box(\neg p)$ remains false for all continuations. **Step 3.** Conclude via the safety definition.
