We have shown that for every $\sigma \notin L_\varphi$, a finite prefix $x$ exists with the required refutation property. Therefore $L_\varphi$ is a safety language. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Proving a Given LTL Formula is a Liveness Property **Claim.** $\varphi \equiv (p \,\mathcal{U}\, q)$ (strong until) is a liveness property. **Step 1.** Verify the liveness structure. The formula $\varphi$ contains the $\mathcal{U}$ operator at the top level, which is **not** dominated by an outer $\Box$. Hence it is a candidate liveness formula. **Step 2.** Show no finite prefix can refute it. Let $x = s_0, s_1, \dots, s_n$ be **any** finite trace. We must exhibit an infinite continuation $\tau$ such that $x \cdot \tau \models (p \,\mathcal{U}\, q)$. Construct $\tau$ as follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \tau &\equiv s_n, s_{n+1}, s_{n+2}, \dots \\ &\text{where} \quad s_{n+k} \models q \quad \text{for } k = 0 \end{aligned} $$ That is, we make the next state after $x$ satisfy $q$ and then extend arbitrarily. In this continuation, $q$ holds immediately after the prefix, so $(p \,\mathcal{U}\, q)$ is satisfied along $x \cdot \tau$. **Step 3.** Universality over all finite prefixes. Because the construction in Step 2 works for **every** finite $x \in \Sigma^*$, the liveness definition is satisfied: $$ \begin{aligned} \forall x \in \Sigma^*, \exists \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : x \cdot \tau \in L_\varphi \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ Hence $L_\varphi$ is a liveness language. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classifying Common LTL Patterns The following table is the **single most important reference** for answering KTU Part A classification questions. The student must be able to fill column 2 and column 3 from memory. | LTL Formula $\varphi$ | Class | Reasoning (Brief) | |---|---|---| | $\Box(p \land q)$ | **Safety** | Pure $\Box$ over propositional $\psi$ | | $\Box \Diamond p$ | **Liveness** | $\Diamond$ not subsumable under $\Box$ — strong fairness flavor | | $\Diamond \Box p$ | **Liveness** | Eventual stability, cannot be refuted finitely | | $\Box(p \Rightarrow \Diamond q)$ | **Liveness** | Standard *response* pattern | | $\Diamond(p \lor q)$ | **Liveness** | Pure $\Diamond$ | | $\Box \Diamond p \lor \Box \Diamond q$ | **Liveness** | Disjunction of liveness is liveness | | $\Box p \lor \Diamond q$ | **Mixed** (decomposes) | Has both safety and liveness components | | $(p \,\mathcal{U}\, q)$ | **Liveness** | Until is intrinsically liveness | | $\Box(\neg(p \land q))$ | **Safety** | Mutual-exclusion style | | $\Diamond (p \land q)$ | **Liveness** | Co-occurrence in future | | $\Box p \Rightarrow \Diamond q$ | **Mixed** (decomposes) | Antecedent is safety, consequent is liveness | | $\neg \Box \neg p$ (i.e. $\Diamond p$) | **Liveness** | Duality of $\Box$ | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Alpern–Schneider Decomposition of a Mixed Property **Problem.** Decompose the property *"Every request is eventually granted, and no two requests are processed simultaneously"* into safety and liveness components. **Step 1.** Write the mixed property in LTL. $$ \begin{aligned} P &\equiv \Box(req \Rightarrow \Diamond grant) \;\land\; \Box \neg(p_1 \land p_2) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2.** Identify the safety component $S$. The sub-formula $\Box \neg(p_1 \land p_2)$ depends only on state propositions and is universally quantifed over time. It is a pure safety property: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\equiv \Box \neg(p_1 \land p_2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3.** Identify the liveness component $L$. The sub-formula $\Box(req \Rightarrow \Diamond grant)$ is the textbook *response* pattern — it cannot be refuted by any finite prefix, because a pending request may still be granted in the future: $$ \begin{aligned} L &\equiv \Box(req \Rightarrow \Diamond grant) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4.** Verify the decomposition. By the Alpern–Schneider theorem, $P = S \cap L$. We have partitioned $P$ into a safety and a liveness factor — verification can now be performed by two independent algorithms: a **reachability** check for $S$ and a **cycle (fairness)** check for $L$. $\blacksquare$ # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Safety–Liveness Universe (Conceptual Map) The diagram below shows how the universe of all LTL properties partitions into the two Alpern–Schneider classes, with examples mapped into each region. ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([All LTL Properties P]) --> Decomp{Alpern-Schneider
Decomposition} Decomp --> SafetyRegion[Safety Properties S] Decomp --> LivenessRegion[Liveness Properties L] Decomp --> MixedRegion[Mixed Properties S ∩ L] SafetyRegion --> S1[Box negation p
Mutual Exclusion] SafetyRegion --> S2[Box p land q
Invariant] SafetyRegion --> S3[Box not deadlock
No Deadlock] LivenessRegion --> L1[Diamond p
Reachability] LivenessRegion --> L2[Box req implies Diamond grant
Response] LivenessRegion --> L3[Box Diamond served
Starvation Freedom] MixedRegion --> M1[Box req implies Diamond grant
AND Box not bad
= Full Spec] style Start fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff style Decomp fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style SafetyRegion fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff style LivenessRegion fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff style MixedRegion fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff style S3 fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff style L1 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff style L2 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff style L3 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff style M1 fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.2 Detection Mechanism — Finite Prefix vs. Infinite Trace The diagram below illustrates the **fundamental asymmetry** in how safety and liveness violations are witnessed. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Infinite Trace sigma] --> B{Type of Property} B -->|Safety| C[Check Finite Prefix x] B -->|Liveness| D[Need Entire Trace sigma] C --> E{Finite prefix
satisfies property?} E -->|NO| F[VIOLATION DETECTED
at position i] E -->|YES| G[Continue - may still hold] D --> H{Does good event
occur eventually?} H -->|YES| G H -->|NO - infinite wait| I[VIOLATION DETECTED
via lasso / cycle] style A fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff style B fill:#f57c00,color:#000000 style C fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff style D fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff style F fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff style I fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff style G fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style E fill:#0288d1,color:#ffffff style H fill:#e64a19,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## 4.3 Verification Algorithm Flow (Block-Level Architecture) This block diagram shows how a model checker (SPIN, NuSMV) processes a property after classification. ```mermaid flowchart TB P[Input LTL Property phi] --> Cls{Classify phi} Cls -->|Safety Box psi| SAF[Reachability Check
on negation psi] Cls -->|Liveness Diamond| LIV[Cycle Detection
on Buchi Automaton] Cls -->|Mixed| DEC[Decompose phi = S ∩ L] DEC --> SAF DEC --> LIV SAF --> SAFR{Finite counterexample
found?} SAFR -->|Yes| FAIL1[OUTPUT: Safety Violation
Finite Path] SAFR -->|No| OK1[SAFETY HOLDS] LIV --> LIVR{Infinite counterexample
lasso found?} LIVR -->|Yes| FAIL2[OUTPUT: Liveness Violation
Accepting Cycle] LIVR -->|No| OK2[LIVENESS HOLDS] OK1 --> DONE[VERIFIED] OK2 --> DONE FAIL1 --> DONE FAIL2 --> DONE style P fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff style Cls fill:#f57c00,color:#000000 style SAF fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff style LIV fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff style DEC fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff style SAFR fill:#0288d1,color:#ffffff style LIVR fill:#e64a19,color:#ffffff style FAIL1 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff style FAIL2 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff style OK1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style OK2 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff style DONE fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PECNT526]** > Define **safety property** in the context of LTL model checking. Give one example. **Model Answer:** A property $L \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is a **safety property** if for every infinite word $\sigma \notin L$, there exists a finite prefix $x$ of $\sigma$ such that for all infinite continuations $\tau \in \Sigma^\omega$, the word $x \cdot \tau \notin L$. In other words, a violation of the property can be detected from a finite prefix of the execution trace. **Example:** Mutual exclusion between two processes, expressed in LTL as $\Box \neg (c_1 \land c_2)$. The moment a state is observed where both $c_1$ and $c_2$ are true, the property is violated — a finite prefix suffices. **[3 marks — definition 2 marks, example 1 mark]** --- > **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECNT526]** > What is a **liveness property**? How does it differ from a safety property in terms of the prefix structure? **Model Answer:** A property $L \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is a **liveness property** if for **every** finite word $x \in \Sigma^*$, there exists an infinite continuation $\tau \in \Sigma^\omega$ such that $x \cdot \tau \in L$. This means that no finite prefix of any trace can ever refute the property. The structural difference is as follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Safety} &: \forall \sigma \notin L,\; \exists x \prec \sigma : \forall \tau,\; x\tau \notin L \\ \text{Liveness} &: \forall x \in \Sigma^*,\; \exists \tau : x\tau \in L \end{aligned} $$ Safety violations are **finitely witnessed**; liveness violations require an **infinite** (or cyclic) witness. **Example of a liveness property:** $\Box(req \Rightarrow \Diamond grant)$ — every request is eventually granted. A pending request may be granted arbitrarily far in the future. **[3 marks — definition 1.5 marks, contrast 1 mark, example 0.5 marks]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Question A.** **[KTU University Exam – PECNT526, Module 3 Typical Pattern]** > > **(a) [7 marks]** State and explain the formal definitions of **safety** and **liveness** properties in LTL. Use the Alpern–Schneider decomposition theorem to show that any property can be written as an intersection of a safety and a liveness component. > > **(b) [7 marks]** For each of the following LTL formulas, classify it as a **safety**, **liveness**, or **mixed** property. Justify each classification with a one-line reasoning: > > 1. $\Box \neg (c_1 \land c_2)$ > 2. $\Diamond\, terminated$ > 3. $\Box(req \Rightarrow \Diamond grant)$ > 4. $\Box(\text{no deadlock}) \;\land\; \Diamond(\text{goal reached})$ ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Definitions and Decomposition **Safety definition (3 marks):** A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is a safety property iff: $$ \forall \sigma \in \Sigma^\omega : \sigma \notin L \;\Rightarrow\; \exists x \in \Sigma^* : x \prec \sigma \;\land\; \forall \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : x \cdot \tau \notin L $$ Liveness definition (3 marks): A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is a liveness property iff: $$ \forall x \in \Sigma^* : \exists \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : x \cdot \tau \in L $$ **Alpern–Schneider Decomposition (1 mark):** For any property $P$, define: - $\text{Safe}(P) = \{ \sigma \mid \forall \sigma' \prec \sigma : \sigma' \in \text{Pref}(P) \}$ - $\text{Live}(P) = P \cup (\Sigma^\omega \setminus P) \cap \text{closure}(P)$ Then $P = \text{Safe}(P) \cap \text{Live}(P)$. #### Part (b) — Classification (7 marks) | # | Formula | Class | Justification | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Box \neg (c_1 \land c_2)$ | **Safety** | Pure invariant — violation finitely witnessed when both critical sections entered | | 2 | $\Diamond\, terminated$ | **Liveness** | Pure reachability — no finite prefix can prove non-termination | | 3 | $\Box(req \Rightarrow \Diamond grant)$ | **Liveness** | Standard *response* pattern — request may be granted arbitrarily far ahead | | 4 | $\Box(\text{no deadlock}) \;\land\; \Diamond(\text{goal reached})$ | **Mixed** | Conjunction of a safety ($\Box$…) and a liveness ($\Diamond$…) component | **[Marks: 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1 mark for the conjunction reasoning = 7]** --- > **Question B.** **[KTU University Exam – PECNT526, Module 3 Alternate Choice]** > > **(a) [7 marks]** Define a **safety property** formally. Explain, with a real-world analogy, why safety properties are said to be "finitely refutable". Provide two examples from distinct engineering domains. > > **(b) [7 marks]** Define a **liveness property** formally. Explain why the counter-example generated by a model checker for a liveness violation is structurally different from that of a safety violation. Illustrate with a concrete trace. ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Safety: Definition + Analogy (7 marks) **Definition (3 marks):** A property $L \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is safe iff $\forall \sigma \notin L$, $\exists x \prec \sigma$ such that $\forall \tau$, $x \cdot \tau \notin L$. **Real-world analogy (2 marks):** Consider a **kitchen pressure cooker** with safety valve monitoring. The safety property is: *"The pressure never exceeds the safety threshold."* The instant the gauge reads above the threshold — even a single sample — the violation is conclusive. You do not need to wait and see what happens next. The "bad event" is observed at a *specific* finite instant. **Two examples (2 marks):** 1. *Avionics:* $\Box \neg(\text{autopilot engaged} \land \text{manual override active})$ — incompatible flight modes never co-occur. 2. *Database:* $\Box(\text{balance} \geq 0)$ — no account ever goes overdrawn. #### Part (b) — Liveness: Definition + Counter-example Structure (7 marks) **Definition (3 marks):** A property $L \subseteq \Sigma^\omega$ is live iff $\forall x \in \Sigma^*$, $\exists \tau$ such that $x \cdot \tau \in L$. **Structural difference of counter-examples (3 marks):** - A **safety** counter-example is a **finite** path from an initial state to a state where the invariant is broken. It terminates at the point of violation. - A **liveness** counter-example is an **infinite** path that, in practice, is represented as a **lasso (prefix + accepting cycle)**. The path reaches a "good event" candidate state but then **loops forever** without ever satisfying the event. **Concrete trace illustration (1 mark):** For $\Box(req \Rightarrow \Diamond grant)$, suppose: - Finite prefix: $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2$ where at $s_2$, $req$ becomes true. - Accepting cycle: $s_2 \to s_3 \to s_4 \to s_2$ — the cycle repeats with $req$ continuously true but $grant$ never true. The lasso $s_0 s_1 (s_2 s_3 s_4)^\omega$ is the model checker's witness of liveness violation. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PECNT526 — Safety & Liveness Definitions:** > > 1. **Swapping the quantifiers (–2 marks):** Students frequently write the liveness condition as $\forall \tau, \exists x$ — this is the **wrong order**. The correct order is $\forall x, \exists \tau$. Mixing the quantifiers makes the property *co*-liveness or a *safety* property. > > 2. **Confusing $\Box \Diamond$ with safety (–1 mark):** A formula like $\Box \Diamond p$ is **liveness**, not safety, because the inner $\Diamond p$ cannot be verified by a finite observation. Do not blindly classify based on the leading $\Box$. > > 3. **Omitting the word "prefix" in the safety definition (–1 mark):** The KTU 2024 marking scheme explicitly awards the "finite prefix" clause separately. Writing only "a trace" instead of "a finite prefix of the trace" loses 1 full mark. > > 4. **Claiming every property is purely safety OR purely liveness (–1 mark):** Many KTU questions are deliberately mixed. Always check for **conjunctions** of safety and liveness sub-formulas. > > 5. **Forgetting to give an example (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Definitions alone are insufficient; the KTU valuation key requires at least one **illustrative example** for full marks on a 3-mark Part A question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PECNT526 — Module 3, Safety & Liveness (Definitions only)** - [ ] **Safety** = "**Nothing bad ever happens**" — finitely refutable by a finite prefix of a violating trace. - [ ] **Liveness** = "**Something good eventually happens**" — no finite prefix can refute it; requires infinite (lasso) witness. - [ ] Formal safety: $\forall \sigma \notin L, \exists x \prec \sigma : \forall \tau,\; x\tau \notin L$. - [ ] Formal liveness: $\forall x \in \Sigma^*, \exists \tau \in \Sigma^\omega : x\tau \in L$. - [ ] **Alpern–Schneider Theorem:** Every $\omega$-regular property $P = \text{Safety}(P) \cap \text{Liveness}(P)$. - [ ] **Safety skeleton:** $\Box \psi$ where $\psi$ is propositional / past-only. - [ ] **Liveness skeleton:** any formula containing a non-subsumable $\Diamond$ or $\mathcal{U}$. - [ ] **Counter-example shapes differ:** Safety → finite path. Liveness → lasso (prefix + accepting cycle). - [ ] **Canonical safety examples:** mutual exclusion, no-overdraft, no-deadlock, partial correctness. - [ ] **Canonical liveness examples:** eventual response, termination, starvation freedom, strong fairness ($\Box \Diamond p$). - [ ] **Duality:** $\neg(\text{Safety}) \equiv \text{Liveness}$; useful for proof manipulations. - [ ] **Alternate names:** Persistence (Manna–Pnueli) = Safety; Reactivity (Manna–Pnueli) = Liveness. - [ ] **Büchi automaton connection:** Both safety and liveness properties are accepted by Büchi automata — the differing structure of these automata is what makes their verification algorithms differ. - [ ] **Engineering utility:** Aerospace, railway, banking, PLC, and network protocols all rely on this dichotomy for certification arguments (e.g., DO-178C, EN 50128, IEC 61508).

topics.title: Automata Based LTL Model Checking (Algorithms and examples only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Automata Based LTL Model Checking (Algorithms and examples only) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Automata-Based LTL Model Checking **Automata-Based LTL Model Checking** is the procedure of verifying whether a concurrent reactive system, modeled as a state-transition graph (Kripke structure $M$), satisfies a temporal-logic specification given as a **Linear Temporal Logic (LTL)** formula $\varphi$, by reducing the satisfiability problem to a graph-theoretic search over **Büchi automata** and the **product automaton** of $M$ with the negation of $\varphi$. Formally, the model-checking problem is stated as: $$M, s_0 \models \varphi \iff \mathcal{L}(M) \subseteq \mathcal{L}(\varphi)$$ Equivalently, the dual formulation is used algorithmically: $$M, s_0 \models \varphi \iff \mathcal{L}(M) \cap \mathcal{L}(\neg\varphi) = \emptyset$$ where: - $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ is a Kripke structure with state set $S$, initial states $S_0$, transition relation $R$, and labeling $L$. - $\mathcal{L}(M)$ is the set of all infinite paths (traces) of $M$. - $\mathcal{L}(\varphi)$ is the language accepted by a Büchi automaton built from $\varphi$. - $\neg\varphi$ is the LTL negation of the specification. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This module focuses strictly on *algorithms* and *worked-out examples*. The construction of the Büchi automaton from an LTL formula is assumed known (covered in Module 2); the focus here is the **product construction**, **nested DFS**, and **counterexample extraction**. ### 1.2 The Two Standard Algorithm Families | Algorithm Family | Core Idea | Typical Tool | Reference | |---|---|---|---| | **Global Model Checking** | Compute $\text{Sat}(\varphi)$ sets iteratively on $M$ using fixpoints | SPIN, NuSMV (tableau) | Clarke/Emerson | | **Automata-Based (Local)** | Build NBW for $\neg\varphi$, take product with $M$, search for an accepting cycle | SPIN's `never claim` | Vardi/Wolper | The KTU Module 3 syllabus targets the **automata-based local** approach. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Detective Trail" Analogy** > Imagine the Kripke structure $M$ as the floor plan of a hotel with infinite corridors, and every state is a room the system can be in. The specification $\varphi$ is a *house rule* ("the light must turn on after the door opens"). To check the rule, the detective does not search the whole hotel. Instead, he translates the *violation* of the rule ($\neg\varphi$) into a **criminal profile** — a Büchi automaton that represents exactly those behaviours that *break* the rule. He then plays the profile against the floor plan (forming a **product automaton**) and asks: "Is there a walk through the hotel that matches the criminal profile forever?" If yes, the rule is broken and a counterexample is captured as the witness path. If no, the system is **safe**. ### 1.4 Central Objects Recap (5-tuple for NBW) A **Nondeterministic Büchi Word Automaton (NBW)** is $A = (\Sigma, Q, Q_0, \delta, F)$ where: - $\Sigma$ = alphabet (here, $2^{AP}$, the power set of atomic propositions). - $Q$ = finite set of states. - $Q_0 \subseteq Q$ = initial states. - $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to 2^{Q}$ = transition function. - $F \subseteq Q$ = accepting states (a run is *accepting* iff it visits $F$ infinitely often). > [!IMPORTANT] > In automata-based LTL model checking, every LTL formula $\varphi$ is converted into an NBW $A_{\neg\varphi}$ whose language equals $\mathcal{L}(\neg\varphi)$ (the set of infinite words violating $\varphi$). The model $M$ is itself an NBW accepting $\mathcal{L}(M)$. The product is then a generalized Büchi automaton reduced to NBW for cycle search. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Algorithmic Pipeline (End-to-End) The full procedure follows four logically ordered stages: 1. **Stage 1 — Build $A_{\neg\varphi}$**: Convert the negated LTL formula $\neg\varphi$ into an NBW $A_{\neg\varphi}$ such that $\mathcal{L}(A_{\neg\varphi}) = \mathcal{L}(\neg\varphi)$. (Done by tableau/Giannakolaou–Bowman construction; treated as input in this module.) 2. **Stage 2 — Build the product automaton** $M \otimes A_{\neg\varphi}$: An NBW whose accepted language is $\mathcal{L}(M) \cap \mathcal{L}(A_{\neg\varphi})$. 3. **Stage 3 — Search for an accepting cycle**: Find a reachable accepting cycle in the product. If none exists, $M \models \varphi$ holds; otherwise the cycle yields a counterexample. 4. **Stage 4 — Project back**: Project the accepting cycle onto $M$ to obtain a concrete counterexample path. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Construction of the Product Automaton Let $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ and $A_{\neg\varphi} = (\Sigma, Q, Q_0, \delta, F)$ with $\Sigma = 2^{AP}$. The product NBW is defined as: $$M \otimes A_{\neg\varphi} = (S \times Q,\ (S_0 \times Q_0),\ R_{\otimes},\ F_{\otimes})$$ where the product transition relation is: $$R_{\otimes} = \{\, ((s, q), (s', q')) \mid s' \in R(s) \ \text{and} \ q' \in \delta(q, L(s')) \,\}$$ and the set of accepting states is: $$F_{\otimes} = S \times F = \{\, (s, q) \mid q \in F \,\}$$ **Why this construction works (the key insight):** A run of the product is a sequence $\rho = (s_0, q_0), (s_1, q_1), \dots$ such that the second component is a run of $A_{\neg\varphi}$ and the first is a run of $M$ over the *same* labeled word $L(s_0)L(s_1)\dots$. Therefore the accepted language of the product is exactly the set of labeled words belonging to **both** $\mathcal{L}(M)$ and $\mathcal{L}(A_{\neg\varphi})$ — which is the set of behaviors that simultaneously *exist in $M$* and *violate $\varphi$*. ### 2.3 Accepting Cycle Definition A cycle in a directed graph is **accepting** if and only if it contains at least one accepting state, i.e. at least one vertex from $F_{\otimes}$. A **reachable** accepting cycle is one that lies in the subgraph induced by states reachable from $S_0 \times Q_0$. > [!NOTE] > **Büchi acceptance intuition:** A non-accepting cycle may be traversed forever in the model but the automaton's run does not "win" — there is no match with a violating trace. An *accepting* cycle corresponds to a true counterexample: the system can loop through a state forever while the run of $A_{\neg\varphi}$ keeps revisiting an accepting state, i.e. a violation is sustained. ### 2.4 Nested Depth-First Search (NDFS) — Classical SPIN Algorithm The NDFS algorithm of **Courcoubetis, Vardi, Wolper & Yannakakis (1992)** finds an accepting cycle in linear time, using two interleaved DFS traversals on the product graph. **Outer DFS** explores the product from every initial state. Upon visiting an accepting state $v \in F_{\otimes}$ for the first time, it **suspends** and launches an **inner DFS** from $v$. **Inner DFS** searches only for a back-edge to any vertex on the outer-DFS stack. If it finds one, the cycle formed by that stack suffix + back-edge contains an accepting state and is **reported as a counterexample**. ```text Algorithm NDFS(M ⊗ A¬φ): for all v0 ∈ S0 × Q0 do if v0 not visited then dfs1(v0) Procedure dfs1(v): visited[v] := true; stack.push(v) for each successor u of v do if u not visited then dfs1(u) if v ∈ F⊗ then dfs2(v) // launch inner search stack.pop(v) Procedure dfs2(v): for each successor u of v do if u ∈ stack then // back edge to outer stack report cycle: stack from u to v + edge (v,u) else if u not innerVisited then innerVisited[u] := true dfs2(u) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Complexity:** NDFS runs in $O(|V| + |E|)$ time, matching a single DFS. The key optimization is that the inner DFS restarts only from accepting states and uses a `innerVisited` flag to avoid redundant work. ### 2.5 Counterexample Extraction Once NDFS reports a cycle $C$ in the product, the **counterexample in $M$** is obtained by projecting each product state $(s, q)$ onto its $s$ component, and tracing from the cycle back through the path recorded by the outer DFS stack. The result is an infinite path $s_0 \to s_1 \to \dots$ that: - is feasible in $M$, - satisfies the negation $\neg\varphi$ of the specification (i.e., witnesses a violation). > [!TIP] > Because Büchi acceptance is *infinite*, the counterexample is reported to the user as a finite *lasso* (stem + cycle), which is the standard output of SPIN-style model checkers. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Definition / Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Model Checking Question | $M, s_0 \models \varphi$ | Boolean verdict | | 2 | Dual Formulation | $M \models \varphi \iff \mathcal{L}(M) \cap \mathcal{L}(\neg\varphi) = \emptyset$ | Drives automata approach | | 3 | Product Automaton States | $S \times Q$ | Pairwise state product | | 4 | Product Transitions | $((s,q),(s',q')) \in R_{\otimes} \iff s' \in R(s) \land q' \in \delta(q, L(s'))$ | Label-driven sync | | 5 | Product Accepting Set | $F_{\otimes} = S \times F$ | All pairs with $q \in F$ | | 6 | Cycle is accepting | $\exists\ v \in C \cdot v \in F_{\otimes}$ | Büchi: visit accepting state on cycle | | 7 | NDFS complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Linear in product size | | 8 | NDFS output | Lasso = stem $\sigma$ + cycle $\pi$ | Counterexample in $M$ | | 9 | Worst-case automaton size | $\mathcal{O}(2^{\vert \varphi \vert})$ states in $A_{\neg\varphi}$ | Exponential blow-up | | 10 | Tableau acceptance set | $F = \{\ \text{states with no open obligation}\}$ | Builds $A_{\neg\varphi}$ | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Automata-based LTL model checking is the workhorse of **hardware verification** (CPU cache protocols, bus arbiters in industrial SPIN/SMV toolchains) and **protocol verification** (e.g., leader election, sliding-window protocols, IEEE 1394 FireWire). The product construction is the conceptual backbone of every industrial **explicit-state model checker**, and the nested DFS is the heart of SPIN's `pan.c` search engine. Mastering it allows engineers to read and customize real verification tool output. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Product Automaton Construction (Hand-Computed) **Problem:** Let $M$ be a Kripke structure with $AP = \{a, b\}$ and two states $s_0, s_1$: - $L(s_0) = \{a\}$, $L(s_1) = \{b\}$, - $R = \{(s_0, s_0), (s_0, s_1), (s_1, s_1)\}$, - $S_0 = \{s_0\}$. Let the LTL formula be $\varphi = \mathbf{G}\,a$ ("globally $a$"). Build $A_{\neg\varphi}$ for $\neg\varphi = \mathbf{F}\,\neg a$ ("eventually not $a$"). **Step 1 — Build $A_{\neg\varphi}$ manually.** $\mathbf{F}\,\neg a$ is satisfied when $\neg a$ is seen. A minimal NBW with $Q = \{q_0, q_1\}$: - $\Sigma = 2^{\{a,b\}} = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{b\}, \{a,b\}\}$. - $Q_0 = \{q_0\}$, $F = \{q_1\}$. - Transitions: from $q_0$ on any letter, loop to $q_0$ (waiting) *or* jump to $q_1$ if $\neg a$ holds (i.e. letter in $\{\emptyset, \{b\}\}$); from $q_1$ on any letter, stay in $q_1$. $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(q_0,\{a\}) &= \{q_0\} \\ \delta(q_0,\emptyset) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \\ \delta(q_0,\{b\}) &= \{q_0, q_1\} \\ \delta(q_0,\{a,b\}) &= \{q_0\} \\ \delta(q_1,x) &= \{q_1\} \quad \text{for all } x \in \Sigma \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Form the product $M \otimes A_{\neg\varphi}$.** States $S \times Q = \{(s_0,q_0), (s_0,q_1), (s_1,q_0), (s_1,q_1)\}$. Initial: $\{(s_0,q_0)\}$. Accepting: $F_{\otimes} = S \times F = \{(s_0,q_1),(s_1,q_1)\}$. Compute transitions state by state, using the rule: $$ ((s,q),(s',q')) \in R_{\otimes} \iff s' \in R(s) \land q' \in \delta(q, L(s')) $$ **Transition for $(s_0, q_0)$:** - $R(s_0) = \{s_0, s_1\}$, $L(s_0) = \{a\}$, $\delta(q_0, \{a\}) = \{q_0\}$. - $s' = s_0$: only $q' = q_0 \Rightarrow (s_0,q_0) \to (s_0,q_0)$. - $s' = s_1$: $L(s_1)=\{b\}$, but $q_0$ on $\{b\}$ has $\delta=\{q_0,q_1\}$. - $q' = q_0 \Rightarrow (s_0,q_0) \to (s_1,q_0)$. - $q' = q_1 \Rightarrow (s_0,q_0) \to (s_1,q_1)$. **Transition for $(s_0, q_1)$:** - $R(s_0) = \{s_0, s_1\}$, $L(s_0)=\{a\}$, $\delta(q_1, \{a\}) = \{q_1\}$. - $s' = s_0, q' = q_1$: $(s_0,q_1) \to (s_0,q_1)$. - $s' = s_1, q' = q_1$: $(s_0,q_1) \to (s_1,q_1)$. **Transition for $(s_1, q_0)$:** - $R(s_1) = \{s_1\}$, $L(s_1)=\{b\}$, $\delta(q_0,\{b\}) = \{q_0, q_1\}$. - $s' = s_1, q' = q_0$: $(s_1,q_0) \to (s_1,q_0)$. - $s' = s_1, q' = q_1$: $(s_1,q_0) \to (s_1,q_1)$. **Transition for $(s_1, q_1)$:** - $R(s_1) = \{s_1\}$, $L(s_1)=\{b\}$, $\delta(q_1,\{b\}) = \{q_1\}$. - $s' = s_1, q' = q_1$: $(s_1,q_1) \to (s_1,q_1)$. **Step 3 — Verify the answer (accepting-cycle search).** The reachable product subgraph from $\{(s_0,q_0)\}$ is: - $(s_0,q_0) \to (s_0,q_0)$ (self-loop), - $(s_0,q_0) \to (s_1,q_0) \to (s_1,q_1) \to (s_1,q_1) \dots$ (self-loop on accepting state). The cycle $(s_1,q_1) \to (s_1,q_1)$ contains $(s_1,q_1) \in F_{\otimes}$ ⇒ **accepting cycle exists**. Project: counterexample is the infinite path $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_1 \to s_1 \to \dots$ on which $a$ fails after the first step, violating $\mathbf{G}a$. So $M \not\models \mathbf{G}\,a$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering sanity check:** The cycle in the product reflects the self-loop on $s_1$ in $M$, which is the *witness* that $M$ can stay in a non-$a$ state forever. This is exactly the intuitive violation we expect. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Nested DFS Trace Consider a 4-state product $V = \{v_0, v_1, v_2, v_3\}$ with $F_{\otimes} = \{v_2\}$ and edges: $v_0 \to v_1, v_0 \to v_2, v_1 \to v_3, v_2 \to v_3, v_3 \to v_3, v_2 \to v_2$. **Outer DFS (dfs1) starting at $v_0$:** - Visit $v_0$. Stack = $[v_0]$. - Go to $v_1$. Stack = $[v_0, v_1]$. - Go to $v_3$. Stack = $[v_0, v_1, v_3]$. - $v_3$ has no unvisited successors. Pop $v_3$. Stack = $[v_0, v_1]$. - $v_1$ has no remaining successors. Pop $v_1$. Stack = $[v_0]$. - Go to $v_2$ (next successor of $v_0$). Stack = $[v_0, v_2]$. - $v_2 \in F_{\otimes}$ — *launch inner dfs2 from $v_2$*. **Inner DFS (dfs2) from $v_2$:** - Successor $v_3$: $v_3 \notin$ stack. Mark innerVisited. Recurse. - Successor $v_3$: $v_3 \in$ stack? No (already popped). $v_3 \notin$ innerVisited? No. Skip. - $v_3$ returns. - Successor $v_2$: $v_2 \in$ stack $\Rightarrow$ **back edge detected**! Report cycle $v_2 \to v_2$. - Pop $v_2$. Done. **Result:** Counterexample lasso is stem $v_0 \to v_2$ and cycle $v_2 \to v_2$. Projecting onto $M$ yields the path violating $\varphi$. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm vs. State Space Type | Algorithm | State-Space | Memory | Detects | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | Plain DFS cycle search | Tiny / educational | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | All cycles | Misses accepting requirement | | Standard BFS for cycles | Small | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | All cycles | Not Büchi-aware | | **NDFS (CVWY)** | **Explicit-state, large** | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | **Accepting cycles** | Linear; SPIN default | | Tarjan SCC | Explicit, large | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | Accepting cycle if SCC $\cap F \ne \emptyset$ | Used in NuSMV-like tools | | On-the-fly NDFS | Very large, partial | $O(\vert V_{visited} \vert)$ | Accepting cycles | Avoids full product | | Symbolic (BDD) | Huge | $O(\vert \text{BDD} \vert)$ | Fixpoint | Complements explicit approach | > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU examinations, students are expected to recognize the **trade-off** between **NDFS** (used in explicit-state checkers like SPIN) and **SCC-based** approaches (used in symbolic tools). ### 3.4 Complexity Derivation Let $n = \vert S \vert$ and $m = \vert A_{\neg\varphi} \vert$. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} \vert S \times Q \vert &= n \cdot m \\ \vert R_{\otimes} \vert &\leq \vert R \vert \cdot m \quad \text{(one Büchi branch per $M$ edge)} \\ \text{NDFS time} &= O(n \cdot m + \vert R \vert \cdot m) = O(m \cdot (n + \vert R \vert)) \\ \text{Automaton size } m &= \mathcal{O}(2^{\vert \varphi \vert}) \end{aligned} $$ Hence the overall model checking complexity is: $$ \mathcal{O}\!\left( 2^{\vert \varphi \vert} \cdot (n + \vert R \vert) \right) $$ This is the canonical PSPACE-complete cost of LTL model checking. ### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Liveness Property (GFa) Let $\varphi = \mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,a$ ("infinitely often $a$"). Then $\neg\varphi = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,\neg a$ ("eventually always not $a$"). A standard NBW for $\mathbf{F}\mathbf{G}\,\neg a$ is the two-state automaton with $F = \{q_1\}$ where $q_1$ is "abandoned in $\neg a$" — once entered, it stays. An accepting run must keep visiting $F$. To find a counterexample, the NDFS must locate a cycle in $M$ that lies entirely inside states labeled $\{\neg a\}$ (i.e. $a \notin L(s)$). This is exactly the intuition: "is there a part of $M$ that can be reached and then stays in non-$a$ forever?" If yes, liveness is violated. > [!TIP] > The reachability of such a region is decided by NDFS in linear time on the product. The reduction is the same algorithm — only the Büchi automaton changes. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Spec: LTL formula phi] --> B[Compute negation not phi] B --> C[Build NBW A_not_phi
via tableau construction] D[Kripke Structure M] --> E[Build Product
M tensor A_not_phi] C --> E E --> F[Run Nested DFS
on product] F --> G{Accepting
cycle found?} G -- Yes --> H[Report Counterexample
project to M] G -- No --> I[VERDICT: M models phi] H --> J[Output lasso
stem + cycle] I --> K[Verification complete] J --> K ``` ### 4.2 Product Automaton Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph M[Kripke Structure M] s0((s0)) s1((s1)) s2((s2)) s0 -- a --> s1 s1 -- b --> s2 s2 -- c --> s0 end subgraph A[NBW A_not_phi] q0((q0)) q1((q1 accept)) q0 -- x --> q1 q1 -- y --> q1 end M -. synchronization via L s .-> A P0[Product States
s0,q0 s1,q0 s2,q0
s0,q1 s1,q1 s2,q1] M --> P0 A --> P0 P0 --> R[NDFS Search
for back edge into F tensor] ``` ### 4.3 Nested DFS Control Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: outer DFS from initial state]) --> Push[Push state to outer stack] Push --> Explore{Unvisited
successor?} Explore -- Yes --> Recurse[Recursive dfs1 on successor] Recurse --> Explore Explore -- No --> Check{Is current
state in F tensor?} Check -- Yes --> Inner[Launch inner dfs2] Inner --> InnerSearch{Successor on
outer stack?} InnerSearch -- Yes --> Report[Report lasso
back edge forms accepting cycle] InnerSearch -- No --> InnerCont[Continue inner DFS] InnerCont --> InnerSearch Report --> Done([Exit with counterexample]) Check -- No --> Pop[Pop state from outer stack] Pop --> Up[Return to caller dfs1] Up --> Explore ``` ### 4.4 Counterexample Lasso Schema ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Stem[Stem sigma: path to cycle entry] d0((v0)) --> d1((v1)) --> d2((v2)) --> d3((v3)) end d3 -- begin cycle --> d4((v4 accept)) subgraph Cycle[Cycle pi: contains accepting state] d4 --> d5((v5)) --> d6((v6)) --> d7((v7)) --> d4 end Stem --> Cycle ``` > [!NOTE] > The lasso visualizes how a finite *stem* leads into a *cycle* that contains an accepting state — the canonical shape of an LTL counterexample. It is also the exact output format that SPIN prints via its trail files. ### 4.5 Algorithmic Complexity Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD L[Input: LTL formula phi] --> N[Negation: not phi] N --> T[Tableau construction: 2 to the power vert phi vert states] T --> P[Product: n times m vertices] P --> D[NDFS: O of n times m + E] D --> Out{Accepting cycle?} Out -- Yes --> CE[Counterexample in O of n plus m time] Out -- No --> OK[Verified: M models phi] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`** **State the dual formulation of the LTL model checking problem used in automata-based verification.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The LTL model checking problem $M \models \varphi$ (read: "Kripke structure $M$ satisfies LTL formula $\varphi$") is reduced to a language emptiness problem over Büchi automata using the dual formulation: $$M \models \varphi \iff \mathcal{L}(M) \cap \mathcal{L}(\neg\varphi) = \emptyset$$ That is, $M$ satisfies $\varphi$ if and only if no behaviour of $M$ is a model of $\neg\varphi$. To check this algorithmically, we construct an NBW $A_{\neg\varphi}$ for $\neg\varphi$, build the product $M \otimes A_{\neg\varphi}$, and search for an accepting cycle. If none exists, the intersection is empty and $M \models \varphi$; otherwise, the accepting cycle yields a counterexample. **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`** **Define the accepting states of the product automaton $M \otimes A_{\neg\varphi}$. Why is it sufficient to take $F_{\otimes} = S \times F$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The accepting states of the product automaton are defined as $$F_{\otimes} = \{\, (s, q) \in S \times Q \mid q \in F \,\} = S \times F$$ This is sufficient because a run of the product automaton, when projected to its second component, must be an accepting run of $A_{\neg\varphi}$ (i.e., it must visit $F$ infinitely often). A product state $(s, q)$ is therefore accepting precisely when the Büchi component $q$ is accepting. The $M$-component $s$ does not affect the Büchi acceptance condition, so we take the Cartesian product with the entire $S$ to keep the synchronization constraint. --- ### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`** > **(a)** With a clear algorithm, explain the construction of the product automaton between a Kripke structure $M$ and an NBW $A_{\neg\varphi}$. State the size of the product in terms of $|S|$, $|Q|$, and $|R|$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Apply the nested depth-first search (NDFS) algorithm on the product graph you construct for the following inputs and report whether an accepting cycle exists. > - $M$: $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2\}$, $S_0 = \{s_0\}$, $R = \{(s_0,s_1), (s_1,s_1), (s_1,s_2), (s_2,s_2)\}$, $L(s_0)=\{a\}, L(s_1)=\{b\}, L(s_2)=\{a,b\}$. > - $A_{\neg\varphi}$: $Q = \{q_0, q_1\}$, $Q_0 = \{q_0\}$, $F = \{q_1\}$, $\delta(q_0, \{a\}) = \{q_0\}$, $\delta(q_0, \{a,b\}) = \{q_0, q_1\}$, $\delta(q_0, \text{others}) = \{q_0\}$, $\delta(q_1, \cdot) = \{q_1\}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks:** - **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define product components. Given $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ and $A_{\neg\varphi} = (\Sigma, Q, Q_0, \delta, F)$, the product NBW is $M \otimes A_{\neg\varphi} = (S \times Q,\ S_0 \times Q_0,\ R_{\otimes},\ F_{\otimes})$ where $F_{\otimes} = S \times F$. - **Step 2 (2 marks):** Define $R_{\otimes}$. A transition $((s, q), (s', q'))$ exists iff $s' \in R(s) \ \text{and} \ q' \in \delta(q, L(s'))$. This ensures that the $M$-component moves along an edge of $M$ while the $A_{\neg\varphi}$-component reads the label $L(s')$ of the *new* $M$-state. - **Step 3 (2 marks):** State the size. The number of product states is $|S| \cdot |Q|$ and the number of product edges is at most $|R| \cdot |Q|$ (one edge in $A$ per $M$-edge). - **Step 4 (2 marks):** State the language. $\mathcal{L}(M \otimes A_{\neg\varphi}) = \mathcal{L}(M) \cap \mathcal{L}(A_{\neg\varphi}) = \mathcal{L}(M) \cap \mathcal{L}(\neg\varphi)$. A run is accepting iff the $A_{\neg\varphi}$-component visits $F$ infinitely often. Therefore $M \models \varphi$ iff the product has **no reachable accepting cycle**. **(b) — 7 marks:** - **Step 1 (1 mark):** Product state set: $S \times Q = \{(s_0,q_0), (s_0,q_1), (s_1,q_0), (s_1,q_1), (s_2,q_0), (s_2,q_1)\}$. Initial: $\{(s_0, q_0)\}$. Accepting: $F_{\otimes} = S \times \{q_1\}$. - **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute transitions using $R_{\otimes}$. From $(s_0, q_0)$: $R(s_0)=\{s_1\}$, $L(s_1)=\{b\}$, $\delta(q_0, \{b\})=\{q_0\}$. So $(s_0,q_0) \to (s_1,q_0)$. From $(s_1, q_0)$: $R(s_1)=\{s_1, s_2\}$, $L(s_1)=\{b\}$, $\delta(q_0, \{b\})=\{q_0\}$. - $(s_1,q_0) \to (s_1,q_0)$. - $L(s_2)=\{a,b\}$, $\delta(q_0, \{a,b\})=\{q_0, q_1\}$. - $(s_1,q_0) \to (s_2,q_0)$ and $(s_1,q_0) \to (s_2,q_1)$. From $(s_2, q_0)$: $R(s_2)=\{s_2\}$, $L(s_2)=\{a,b\}$, $\delta(q_0, \{a,b\})=\{q_0, q_1\}$. - $(s_2,q_0) \to (s_2,q_0)$ and $(s_2,q_0) \to (s_2,q_1)$. From $(s_2, q_1)$: $R(s_2)=\{s_2\}$, $L(s_2)=\{a,b\}$, $\delta(q_1,\cdot)=\{q_1\}$. - $(s_2,q_1) \to (s_2,q_1)$. From $(s_1, q_1)$ and $(s_0, q_1)$: not reachable from initial state (no incoming edges from reachable region). - **Step 3 (2 marks):** NDFS trace. Outer DFS from $(s_0,q_0)$: - Visit $(s_0,q_0)$, push to outer stack. - Go to $(s_1,q_0)$, push. - First successor $(s_1,q_0)$: visited. Try $(s_2,q_0)$: visit, push. - Successors: $(s_2,q_0)$ (visited), $(s_2,q_1)$: visit, push. - $(s_2,q_1) \in F_{\otimes}$ — *launch inner dfs2*. - Inner DFS from $(s_2,q_1)$: only successor is $(s_2,q_1)$ itself, which is on the outer stack ⇒ **back edge detected**! - Report lasso: stem $=(s_0,q_0) \to (s_1,q_0) \to (s_2,q_0) \to (s_2,q_1)$, cycle $= (s_2,q_1) \to (s_2,q_1)$. - **Step 4 (2 marks):** Project the cycle back to $M$. The counterexample in $M$ is the lasso with stem $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2$ and cycle $s_2 \to s_2$. Hence $M \not\models \varphi$ and the system has a violating trace (it enters $s_2$ and stays there forever; whether this violates the original $\varphi$ depends on $A_{\neg\varphi}$'s design — here the trace *does* match a run of $A_{\neg\varphi}$ that visits $q_1$ forever). --- **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`** > **(a)** Describe the Nested Depth-First Search (NDFS) algorithm. State its time and space complexity and explain why two DFS passes are needed. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Consider a product automaton with vertices $\{v_0, v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4\}$, $F_{\otimes} = \{v_2, v_4\}$, and edges: > $v_0 \to v_1, v_0 \to v_2, v_1 \to v_3, v_2 \to v_3, v_2 \to v_2, v_3 \to v_4, v_4 \to v_4, v_3 \to v_0$. > Initial state: $v_0$. Run NDFS by hand and report the counterexample lasso if one exists. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks:** - **Step 1 (2 marks):** NDFS is a two-pass DFS scheme that searches for a reachable accepting cycle in a Büchi product. The outer DFS (`dfs1`) explores the graph and, every time it first visits an accepting state $v \in F_{\otimes}$, it suspends and invokes the inner DFS (`dfs2`) starting from $v$. The inner DFS restricts its search to vertices reachable from $v$ that are not yet "innerVisited", and checks for a back edge to a vertex currently on the outer DFS stack. - **Step 2 (2 marks):** Why two passes? A single DFS can detect cycles but cannot certify that the cycle *contains* an accepting state without an auxiliary structure. By suspending exactly at accepting states and running a bounded inner DFS, NDFS guarantees: (i) any reported cycle contains an accepting state (so it is a Büchi-accepting cycle), and (ii) every accepting cycle is reported by some inner DFS — coverage is preserved by the `innerVisited` set that prunes redundant inner searches. - **Step 3 (2 marks):** Complexity. Each edge is traversed at most once by `dfs1` and at most once by `dfs2` (in a directed graph with `innerVisited` pruning), giving total time $O(|V| + |E|)$. The space used is $O(|V|)$ for the visited, innerVisited, and stack arrays. - **Step 4 (1 mark):** NDFS is the standard algorithm in explicit-state LTL model checkers such as SPIN, and is the classical reference: Courcoubetis, Vardi, Wolper, Yannakakis (1992). **(b) — 7 marks:** - **Step 1 (1 mark):** Edges: $v_0 \to v_1, v_0 \to v_2, v_1 \to v_3, v_2 \to v_3, v_2 \to v_2, v_3 \to v_4, v_4 \to v_4, v_3 \to v_0$. Accepting: $F_{\otimes} = \{v_2, v_4\}$. - **Step 2 (2 marks):** Outer DFS from $v_0$: - Visit $v_0$. Stack = $[v_0]$. - Go to $v_1$. Stack = $[v_0, v_1]$. - Go to $v_3$. Stack = $[v_0, v_1, v_3]$. - $v_3$ successors: $v_4$ and $v_0$. - Visit $v_4$. Stack = $[v_0, v_1, v_3, v_4]$. - $v_4 \in F_{\otimes}$ — *launch inner dfs2 from $v_4$*. - Inner DFS from $v_4$: only successor $v_4$ itself. $v_4 \in$ outer stack ⇒ **back edge**! Report cycle $v_4 \to v_4$. - Pop $v_4$. Stack = $[v_0, v_1, v_3]$. - $v_0 \in$ outer stack? Yes. (Inner DFS doesn't run on $v_0$ here because $v_0$ was not the launching point.) - Pop $v_3$, pop $v_1$. Stack = $[v_0]$. - Go to $v_2$ (next successor of $v_0$). Stack = $[v_0, v_2]$. - $v_2 \in F_{\otimes}$ — *launch inner dfs2 from $v_2$*. - Inner DFS from $v_2$: successors $v_3, v_2$. - $v_3 \notin$ outer stack (already popped) and $\notin$ innerVisited. Mark and recurse. - $v_3$ successors: $v_4, v_0$. Both $\notin$ outer stack and $\notin$ innerVisited. - $v_4$: mark, recurse. Successor $v_4 \in$ inner stack (visited) but not outer. No cycle through outer. - $v_0$: mark, recurse. Successor $v_1$: visited outer-state, not on stack. Continue. - Return. - $v_2$: on outer stack ⇒ **back edge**! Report cycle $v_2 \to v_2$. - Pop $v_2$. Done. - **Step 3 (2 marks):** First accepting cycle reported: lasso = stem $v_0 \to v_1 \to v_3 \to v_4$ + cycle $v_4 \to v_4$. (The search also reports $v_2 \to v_2$ via the second inner launch, but the first is sufficient.) - **Step 4 (2 marks):** Project onto $M$ to obtain a counterexample path; the lasso shape (stem + self-loop on an accepting state) is what is reported to the user as the witness of the violation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to take $F_{\otimes} = S \times F$. Students often wrongly restrict $F_{\otimes}$ to "states in $F$ that are also reachable in $M$ at the right moment". The product *defines* acceptance purely on the Büchi component, so $F_{\otimes} = S \times F$ is canonical. > - **Common mistake 2 (–2 marks):** During NDFS, students frequently use a *single* DFS and check "is there any cycle containing $F$?" — this misses cycles that emerge only after exploring deeper subtrees. Always reason via the two-phase scheme with the outer stack as the cycle witness. > - **Common mistake 3 (–1 to 2 marks):** Confusing labels in the transition rule. The Büchi component reads $L(s')$, **not** $L(s)$. Writing $\delta(q, L(s))$ instead of $\delta(q, L(s'))$ is a frequent error worth at least 1–2 marks in valuation. > - **Common mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Reporting the counterexample as the product path itself; you must *project* onto the $S$-component of $M$. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] The **dual formulation** $M \models \varphi \iff \mathcal{L}(M) \cap \mathcal{L}(\neg\varphi) = \emptyset$ is the cornerstone of automata-based LTL model checking. - [ ] The **product automaton** has states $S \times Q$, initial states $S_0 \times Q_0$, transitions $((s,q),(s',q'))$ iff $s' \in R(s) \land q' \in \delta(q, L(s'))$, and **accepting set $F_{\otimes} = S \times F$**. - [ ] Büchi acceptance of a run: the $A_{\neg\varphi}$-component must visit $F$ *infinitely often*, equivalently the product run must contain an **accepting cycle** (a cycle with at least one $F_{\otimes}$ vertex). - [ ] $M \models \varphi$ holds **iff** the product automaton has **no reachable accepting cycle**. - [ ] **Nested DFS (NDFS)** finds an accepting cycle in $O(|V| + |E|)$ time, with $O(|V|)$ space — linear in the product size. - [ ] NDFS uses an **outer stack**, an `innerVisited` set, and an **inner DFS** launched at each newly visited accepting state. - [ ] A **counterexample** is reported as a **lasso** (finite stem + cycle containing $F_{\otimes}$), and must be **projected back to $M$** by dropping the $Q$-component. - [ ] Worst-case complexity: $\mathcal{O}\!\left(2^{|\varphi|} \cdot (n + |R|)\right)$ — exponential in the formula, linear in the model. This matches the **PSPACE-completeness** of LTL MC. - [ ] Liveness properties ($\mathbf{G}\mathbf{F}\,p$) reduce to finding a *reachable* non-$p$ cycle; safety properties ($\mathbf{G}\,p$) reduce to finding a *reachable* state with $\neg p$. - [ ] Industrial tools: **SPIN** uses on-the-fly NDFS; **NuSMV** uses BDD-based SCC computation; both are unified by the automata-based framework. - [ ] Always check the **label convention**: $L(s')$ is read by the Büchi component when transitioning from $s$ to $s'$. - [ ] In the product, the **accepting set is the full Cartesian product $S \times F$** — not just reachable pairs. - [ ] For KTU 2024 ESE, the most tested sub-topics are: (i) product construction by hand, (ii) NDFS trace, (iii) counterexample extraction.

topics.title: Introduction - Introduction to the Tool Symbolic Analysis Laboratory (SAL)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction - Introduction to the Tool Symbolic Analysis Laboratory (SAL)** # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Symbolic Analysis Laboratory (SAL)** is a framework and tool suite developed by **SRI International's Computer Science Laboratory** for the analysis of **state-transition systems**, particularly those modeling **concurrent and reactive systems**. It provides a common intermediate language to describe transition systems and integrates multiple analysis engines (model checkers, simulators, and theorem provers) that operate on the same system description. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECNT526 - Module 4):** > *SAL (Symbolic Analysis Laboratory) is a model-checking framework developed at SRI International that provides a common language for describing transition systems and integrates multiple symbolic analysis tools (sal-bmc, sal-inf-bmc, sal-wmc, sal-smc, sal-sim) over a uniform context, enabling verification of infinite-state and finite-state concurrent systems.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have designed a complex **railway interlocking system** at a major junction. Multiple trains can arrive from different directions, switches must be set correctly, and signals must follow precise timing rules. You want to prove that **no two trains can ever collide**, no matter what sequence of inputs arrives. Doing this by hand is nearly impossible because the system has thousands of possible states. **SAL is like a sophisticated "smart auditor"** for such systems: - **You describe the railway system once** — tracks, signals, trains, switches — using a precise language. - **You hand that description to a panel of expert auditors**, each with a different specialty (one checks the next 50 steps, one looks for repeating patterns, one simulates random operation). - **Each auditor uses a different mathematical technique** (BDDs, SAT solvers, k-induction, simulation). - **If any auditor finds a flaw, it reports the exact counter-example** (the sequence of events leading to a collision). This "panel of auditors" is what SAL provides for any finite- or infinite-state system — a **uniform environment with multiple specialized symbolic analysis back-ends**. ## 1.3 Key Terminology & Metrics The following bold terms form the **vocabulary of SAL** and appear frequently in KTU questions: - **Context** — A SAL module that declares variables, their types, initializations, and transition relations. It is the *static description* of the system. - **Module** — A SAL file containing a `CONTEXT` and one or more `MODULE` definitions. The module captures the *dynamic behaviour* (transitions, invariants). - **Transition System** — A directed graph whose nodes are system states and whose edges are labelled by transitions; SAL is fundamentally a *symbolic engine over such graphs*. - **Symbolic Analysis** — Reasoning about *sets* of states simultaneously (using Boolean encodings, BDDs, or propositional formulas) rather than enumerating states one by one. - **Bounded Model Checking (BMC)** — Unrolling the transition relation for a fixed number $k$ of steps and checking reachability of an error state using a SAT solver. - **k-Induction** — A generalization of BMC that proves properties for *all* $k \geq 0$ by an inductive argument. - **Counter-example** — A concrete sequence of states that violates the property, returned by SAL when verification fails. - **Liveness & Safety Properties** — SAL can verify both: safety ("bad things never happen") and liveness ("good things eventually happen"). > [!NOTE] > **Historical Context for KTU Exam:** SAL was developed in the early 2000s by **Shankar, Bensalem, de Moura, Owre, Rushby, and colleagues at SRI International**. It is freely available and remains one of the benchmark tools for teaching symbolic model checking in academic curricula. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The SAL Tool Architecture — Components and Their Roles SAL is best understood as a **two-layer architecture**: a *front-end language* and a *family of back-end analysis engines*. ### 2.1.1 The Language Layer (Front-End) The SAL language is designed to be **declarative, modular, and strongly typed**. Its core constructs are: 1. **TYPE declarations** — Boolean, integer, real, enumerated, array, and record types. 2. **CONTEXT block** — Declares global types, constants, and variables shared across modules. 3. **MODULE block** — Encapsulates local state, initialization (`INITIALIZATION`), and a `TRANSITION` relation. 4. **Definition syntax** — Supports guarded commands, non-deterministic assignments, and synchronous/asynchronous composition. 5. **Property specification** — Through auxiliary modules and temporal logic assertions checked by back-ends. ### 2.1.2 The Back-End Analysis Tools SAL ships with **five major analysis tools**, each tailored to a particular verification technique: | Back-End Tool | Underlying Technique | Best Suited For | |---|---|---| | **sal-bmc** | Bounded Model Checking via SAT solvers (e.g., ICS, CVC3) | Finding bugs up to a depth $k$ | | **sal-inf-bmc** | Infinite BMC with $k$ as a symbolic parameter | Parametric reachability | | **sal-wmc** | Well-Founded Model Checker using $k$-induction & PDR | Proving safety for *all* depths | | **sal-smc** | Symbolic Model Checker using BDDs (via CUDD) | Finite-state exhaustive verification | | **sal-sim** | Random/corner-case simulator | Quick sanity checks & counter-example generation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why multiple back-ends?** A single technique is rarely optimal for *all* systems. SAT-based BMC is brilliant at finding deep bugs; BDDs are strong at finite-state exhaustive proofs; $k$-induction extends BMC to proofs. SAL lets the user **try them all on the same description** without rewriting the model. ## 2.2 Operational Workflow in SAL The verification workflow follows a precise pipeline: - **Step 1 — Author the model:** Write `Context` and `Module` in `.sal` files describing the system variables, initial states, and transitions. - **Step 2 — Specify the property:** Express the desired property as another SAL module (often as an observer) or as a temporal logic assertion. - **Step 3 — Choose the back-end:** Select one of `sal-bmc`, `sal-wmc`, `sal-smc`, or `sal-inf-bmc` based on whether the system is finite/infinite and whether the goal is bug-finding or proof. - **Step 4 — Parse & translate:** The SAL front-end parses the file and converts it into an internal **SAL Transition System (STS)** representation. - **Step 5 — Symbolic analysis:** The chosen back-end applies its algorithm (SAT, BDD, induction) on the STS. - **Step 6 — Report:** The tool outputs either **VERIFIED** (property holds), **FAILED** with a counter-example trace, or **UNKNOWN** if the bound is insufficient. ## 2.3 Formal Foundations — The Underlying Logic SAL's verification is grounded in classical logic. The key constructs the back-ends manipulate are: ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbolic Form | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | State | $s \in S$ | A valuation of all program variables | | Initial States | $I(s)$ | Predicate true exactly for starting states | | Transition Relation | $T(s, s')$ | True iff system can move from $s$ to $s'$ in one step | | Reachable States (depth $k$) | $R_k(s) = I(s) \vee \bigvee_{i=0}^{k-1} \text{Post}^i(I)(s)$ | States reachable in $\leq k$ steps | | Property Violation | $\phi(s) = \text{true}$ at an "error" state | Counter-example witness | | BMC Unrolling (depth $k$) | $I(s_0) \wedge \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k-1} T(s_i, s_{i+1}) \wedge \bigvee_{i=0}^{k} \lnot\phi(s_i)$ | A SAT instance; satisfiable $\Rightarrow$ bug found | | $k$-Induction Base | $\forall s_0,\dots,s_k : I(s_0) \wedge \bigwedge T(s_i,s_{i+1}) \Rightarrow \bigvee \phi(s_i)$ | Property holds for $k$ steps from initial state | | $k$-Induction Step | $\forall s_0,\dots,s_k : \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k-1}\phi(s_i) \wedge \bigwedge T(s_i,s_{i+1}) \Rightarrow \phi(s_k)$ | If property holds for $k$ steps it continues to hold | | Liveness (Fair CTL*) | $\text{AF } p$ or $\text{A[ } p \text{ U } q \text{ ]}$ | Eventual / until properties in `sal-smc` | > [!NOTE] > **For KTU answers:** When asked to explain SAL's approach, always mention that it uses **symbolic encodings** (Boolean formulas, BDDs, or SAT clauses) so that it can handle state spaces with **$10^{20}$ states or more**, far beyond what explicit-state tools can manage. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Embedded & safety-critical software** — verifying mutual exclusion, absence of deadlock, and timing constraints in avionics and railway control. - **Protocol verification** — security protocols, communication protocols, and cache-coherence protocols. - **Hardware verification** — micro-architectural models and pipeline correctness. - **Pedagogical value** — widely adopted in graduate courses on formal methods because one model can be analysed with several engines for comparison. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How a SAL Back-End Reaches a Verdict The following exhaustive walkthrough shows precisely how SAL's principal back-ends answer the question *"Can an error state be reached?"*. This is the **analytical core** of every KTU 14-mark question on SAL. ### 3.1.1 The Bounded Model Checking (sal-bmc) Pipeline Consider a system with initial-state predicate $I$, transition relation $T$, and property $\phi$ we want to prove unreachable. SAL's `sal-bmc` performs the following for a user-specified depth $k$: - **Step 1 — Unroll $I$ at $s_0$:** Take $I(s_0)$, the predicate describing the initial state at step 0. - **Step 2 — Unroll $T$ for $k$ steps:** Generate the conjunction of $k$ copies of the transition relation: $$ T(s_0,s_1) \wedge T(s_1,s_2) \wedge \dots \wedge T(s_{k-1}, s_k) $$ - **Step 3 — Negate $\phi$ at every step:** Form the disjunction $\bigvee_{i=0}^{k} \lnot\phi(s_i)$, expressing *"property $\phi$ is violated somewhere in the first $k$ steps"*. - **Step 4 — Build the SAT instance:** Combine the three components into a single Boolean formula: $$ \Phi_k \;\equiv\; I(s_0) \;\wedge\; \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k-1} T(s_i, s_{i+1}) \;\wedge\; \bigvee_{i=0}^{k} \lnot\phi(s_i) $$ - **Step 5 — Invoke the SAT solver:** The propositional formula $\Phi_k$ is passed to an underlying SAT solver (ICS, CVC3, etc.). - **Step 6 — Interpret the result:** - If $\Phi_k$ is **satisfiable** $\Rightarrow$ the SAT solver returns a satisfying assignment. SAL decodes this into a concrete counter-example trace: $s_0 \to s_1 \to \dots \to s_j$ where $\lnot\phi(s_j)$ holds. Result: **FAILED with counter-example**. - If $\Phi_k$ is **unsatisfiable** $\Rightarrow$ no bug exists within $k$ steps. SAL reports **VERIFIED up to depth $k$** (not a full proof). - **Step 7 — Increment $k$ if needed:** The user may increase $k$ and repeat. Note that BMC alone *cannot* prove correctness for all depths unless complemented by $k$-induction. ### 3.1.2 The $k$-Induction (sal-wmc) Pipeline To turn BMC into a *full proof*, SAL's `sal-wmc` adds two inductive obligations: - **Base Case (Base-$k$):** Property $\phi$ holds in every reachable state up to depth $k$: $$ \text{Base}_k \;\equiv\; \forall s_0,\dots,s_k \;:\; I(s_0) \wedge \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k-1} T(s_i, s_{i+1}) \;\Rightarrow\; \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k} \phi(s_i) $$ - **Inductive Step (Step-$k$):** If $\phi$ holds for $k$ consecutive states reachable by transitions, then it holds at the $k$-th next state: $$ \text{Step}_k \;\equiv\; \forall s_0,\dots,s_k \;:\; \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k-1}\phi(s_i) \wedge \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k-1} T(s_i, s_{i+1}) \;\Rightarrow\; \phi(s_k) $$ - **Decision Logic in SAL:** $$ \text{If } \text{Base}_k \text{ is unsat AND } \text{Step}_k \text{ is unsat, then } \phi \text{ is a full invariant.} $$ - **Why this works:** If the property holds for the first $k$ reachable steps **and** whenever it has held for $k$ steps in any reachable trajectory it continues to hold, then by induction on path length $\phi$ is true everywhere — including infinite paths. ### 3.1.3 The Symbolic Model Checking (sal-smc) Pipeline For finite-state systems, `sal-smc` follows the classical symbolic CTL model-checking approach: - **Step 1 — Encode the transition relation as a BDD:** SAL converts $T$ into a *Binary Decision Diagram* (BDD) using the CUDD library. - **Step 2 — Compute reachable states via BDD image computation:** $$ R_{i+1} \;=\; R_i \cup \text{Image}(R_i, T) $$ where $\text{Image}(R, T) = \exists s : R(s) \wedge T(s, s')$, computed symbolically as a BDD operation. - **Step 3 — Fixpoint detection:** Continue until $R_{i+1} = R_i$. This BDD represents the *exact* set of all reachable states. - **Step 4 — Property check:** Check whether any reachable state violates $\phi$ by computing $R \cap \lvert\phi\vert$ (a BDD conjunction). If the resulting BDD is **FALSE**, the property holds on all reachable states — SAL outputs **VERIFIED**. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Mutual Exclusion in SAL Consider two processes $P_1$ and $P_2$ competing for a critical section. The property to prove is that they are never *both* in the critical section simultaneously. Using SAL-style reasoning: - **Step 1 — Identify state variables.** For each process, a state variable $x_i \in \{N, T, C\}$ denoting "Non-critical", "Trying", and "Critical". Add a shared Boolean flag $y$ (or use a turn variable). - **Step 2 — Write the transition relation.** The transition $\text{trans}(P_1, P_2)$ allows $P_1$ to enter $C$ only if the other is not in $C$. - **Step 3 — Express the property as a SAL module.** A monitor module observes that $x_1 = C \wedge x_2 = C$ is unreachable. - **Step 4 — Run `sal-bmc` with $k = 10$.** SAL builds the SAT instance $\Phi_{10}$; if a schedule exists that lets both processes enter $C$, it is returned as a counter-example. - **Step 5 — Run `sal-wmc` for the full proof.** SAL attempts Base-$k$ and Step-$k$; if both are unsatisfiable, mutual exclusion is *proven* for all runs. - **Step 6 — Run `sal-smc`** to compute the exact reachable state set as a BDD and confirm visually that no reachable state has $(x_1, x_2) = (C, C)$. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SAL Tool Architecture — High-Level Block Diagram The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete functional architecture of the SAL framework**, showing how the language front-end feeds multiple back-end analysis engines. ```mermaid flowchart TB userInput["User Authored SAL Model
Context and Module files"] parser["SAL Front End Parser
Type Checking and Scope Analysis"] stsGen["SAL Transition System STS
Internal Intermediate Representation"] propSpec["Property Specification
Observer Module or Assertion"] dispatcher["Tool Dispatcher
User selects back end via command line"] bmc["sal bmc
Bounded Model Checker SAT Based"] infbmc["sal inf bmc
Infinite BMC Parameterized k"] wmc["sal wmc
Well Founded k Induction and PDR"] smc["sal smc
Symbolic Model Checker BDD based"] sim["sal sim
Random and Interactive Simulator"] satSolver["External SAT Solvers
ICS and CVC3"] cuddBdd["CUDD BDD Library
Symbolic Encoding Engine"] resultVerifier["Result Interpreter
Verified or Counter Example"] outputFinal["Final Output to User
PASS or FAIL with Trace"] userInput --> parser parser --> stsGen propSpec --> stsGen stsGen --> dispatcher dispatcher --> bmc dispatcher --> infbmc dispatcher --> wmc dispatcher --> smc dispatcher --> sim bmc --> satSolver wmc --> satSolver infbmc --> satSolver smc --> cuddBdd bmc --> resultVerifier infbmc --> resultVerifier wmc --> resultVerifier smc --> resultVerifier sim --> resultVerifier resultVerifier --> outputFinal ``` ## 4.2 SAL Verification Workflow — Sequence of Operations This Mermaid diagram captures the **operational sequence** followed when a user invokes SAL with a particular back-end to verify a property. ```mermaid flowchart LR startNode["Start User has SAL file mymodel sal"] stepA["Step A Write Context declaring variables and types"] stepB["Step B Write Module defining INITIALIZATION and TRANSITION"] stepC["Step C Write Observer module for property phi"] stepD["Step D Choose back end sal bmc or sal wmc or sal smc"] stepE["Step E Front end parses and builds STS representation"] stepF["Step F Back end applies symbolic algorithm"] stepG["Step G SAT solver or BDD engine evaluates formula"] stepH{"Decision
Is formula satisfiable"} stepI["Output VERIFIED
Property holds for given bound"] stepJ["Output FAILED with counter example trace"] stepK["User decides increase k or try another back end"] startNode --> stepA stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepH -- Satisfiable --> stepJ stepH -- Unsatisfiable --> stepI stepJ --> stepK stepI --> stepK ``` ## 4.3 Comparison of SAL Back-Ends — Selection Guide This Mermaid block diagram helps a verifier **choose the right SAL tool** based on the nature of the system and the verification goal. ```mermaid flowchart TB decision1{"Is the system
infinite state"} decision2{"Is the goal bug finding
or full proof"} finitePath["System is finite state"] infinitePath["System is infinite state"] bugPath["Goal is bug finding"] proofPath["Goal is full proof"] chooseSmc["Use sal smc
BDD based exhaustive check"] chooseBmc["Use sal bmc
Bounded Model Checking"] chooseInfBmc["Use sal inf bmc
Parametric BMC"] chooseWmc["Use sal wmc
k induction and PDR"] chooseSim["Use sal sim
Quick simulation for sanity"] decision1 -- No --> finitePath decision1 -- Yes --> infinitePath finitePath --> decision2 infinitePath --> decision2 decision2 -- Bug finding --> bugPath decision2 -- Full proof --> proofPath bugPath --> chooseBmc proofPath --> chooseSmc infinitePath --> chooseInfBmc infinitePath --> chooseWmc finitePath --> chooseSim ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **Define the Symbolic Analysis Laboratory (SAL). List any four analysis back-end tools available in the SAL framework.** **Model Answer:** The Symbolic Analysis Laboratory (SAL) is a model-checking framework developed at SRI International's Computer Science Laboratory for the symbolic analysis of state-transition systems. It provides a common intermediate language to describe transition systems and integrates multiple analysis engines over a uniform context. Four back-end tools in SAL are: - **sal-bmc** — Bounded Model Checker (SAT-based). - **sal-inf-bmc** — Infinite Bounded Model Checker with parameterized $k$. - **sal-wmc** — Well-Founded Model Checker using $k$-induction and PDR. - **sal-smc** — Symbolic Model Checker using BDDs. - **sal-sim** — Simulator for random and interactive execution. *(Any four of the above.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model]` **Explain the role of the `CONTEXT` and `MODULE` constructs in a SAL model. How do they differ?** **Model Answer:** - A **CONTEXT** in SAL is a top-level declarative block that defines the **types, constants, and global variables** shared by all modules. It does not itself contain transitions; it is the *static* description of the data. - A **MODULE** in SAL encapsulates the **dynamic behaviour** of a component. It contains an `INITIALIZATION` predicate (specifying the start states) and a `TRANSITION` relation (specifying one-step moves). - **Key difference:** the `CONTEXT` describes *what exists* (the universe of variables and types), while the `MODULE` describes *how it evolves* (the state changes). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the architecture of the SAL tool suite. With a neat block diagram, describe how the SAL front-end, intermediate representation, and back-end analysis engines interact during a typical verification run. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Compare the techniques used by **sal-bmc** and **sal-wmc**. Show how the Bounded Model Checking formula $\Phi_k$ is constructed, and explain how $k$-induction extends BMC to a full proof by adding the Base-$k$ and Step-$k$ obligations. **Model Answer for (a):** The SAL tool suite is built as a **two-layer architecture** — a *front-end language* and a *family of back-end analysis engines* — both linked through a common intermediate representation called the **SAL Transition System (STS)**. - **Front-end (Language Layer):** Accepts `.sal` source files containing `CONTEXT` and `MODULE` blocks. The parser performs lexical analysis, type checking, and name resolution, then produces the STS — a normalized representation of variables, initial states, and the transition relation. - **Back-end (Analysis Layer):** A dispatcher selects one of `sal-bmc`, `sal-inf-bmc`, `sal-wmc`, `sal-smc`, or `sal-sim` based on the user's command-line choice. Each back-end manipulates the STS using its native algorithm: - `sal-bmc` and `sal-wmc` translate the STS into a propositional formula and hand it to an external SAT solver. - `sal-smc` translates the STS into BDDs using the CUDD library and performs image computation. - `sal-sim` interprets the STS as a runnable transition system. - **Result interpretation:** Each back-end returns a verdict — `VERIFIED`, `FAILED` with a counter-example trace, or `UNKNOWN` — which SAL formats for the user. The block diagram (see SECTION 4.1) shows the flow: **SAL file → Parser → STS → Dispatcher → chosen back-end → external engine (SAT/BDD) → result → user output**. **Model Answer for (b):** - **sal-bmc (Bounded Model Checker):** - Technique: SAT-based bounded model checking. - Input: A bound $k$ from the user. - Procedure: Builds a propositional formula $\Phi_k$ that is satisfiable **iff** there is a path of length $\leq k$ that violates the property. - Strength: Excellent at finding deep bugs quickly. - Limitation: Cannot prove the property for unbounded systems — it only certifies correctness *up to depth $k$*. - **sal-wmc (Well-Founded Model Checker):** - Technique: $k$-induction (and Property-Directed Reachability) on top of SAT solving. - Procedure: Verifies the property for *all* depths by checking two obligations: - **Base-$k$:** Property holds for the first $k$ reachable steps. - **Step-$k$:** If the property holds for $k$ consecutive reachable states, it continues to hold at the next state. - Strength: Can produce a *full proof* of safety for many infinite-state and parameterized systems. - Limitation: Requires choosing the right $k$; may need $k$ to be large. - **Construction of $\Phi_k$ in BMC:** $$ \Phi_k \;\equiv\; I(s_0) \;\wedge\; \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k-1} T(s_i, s_{i+1}) \;\wedge\; \bigvee_{i=0}^{k} \lnot\phi(s_i) $$ The first conjunct encodes the initial state; the second encodes $k$ consecutive transitions; the third asserts that the property $\phi$ is violated at some step within the unrolling. - **How $k$-induction extends BMC:** - BMC alone returns `VERIFIED up to k` when $\Phi_k$ is unsatisfiable. This is *not* a proof for all runs. - $k$-induction adds two **first-order** queries that, when both unsatisfiable, imply the property holds for *every* path length. - The combination **sal-bmc + Base-$k$ + Step-$k$** = full verification of safety properties. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]` **(a)** *(7 marks)* With an example, describe the structure of a SAL model file. Show how variables, types, initial state, and transition relation are declared in a typical SAL context and module. **(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the verification methodology used by **sal-smc**. Discuss how BDD-based image computation enables exhaustive verification of finite-state systems and contrast this with the SAT-based approach of sal-bmc. **Model Answer for (a):** A typical SAL model file consists of **two main blocks**: a `CONTEXT` for the static description and one or more `MODULE`s for the dynamic behaviour. **Example — A simple counter:** ```sal CONTEXT TYPE count = [0..3]; END MODULE counter OUTPUT c : count INITIALIZATION c = 0 TRANSITION c' = (c + 1) mod 4 END ``` - **CONTEXT block:** Declares the abstract type `count` as the integer range 0 to 3. - **MODULE `counter`:** - Declares the **output variable** `c` of type `count`. - The `INITIALIZATION` clause sets the starting value $c = 0$. - The `TRANSITION` clause defines the next-state relation $c' = (c + 1) \bmod 4$. **A more realistic mutual-exclusion example (Peterson-style sketch):** ```sal CONTEXT TYPE state = {idle, trying, critical}; END MODULE process1 OUTPUT s1 : state INITIALIZATION s1 = idle TRANSITION s1' IN {idle : TRUE; -- non-deterministic move trying : s1 = idle; critical: s1 = trying}; END ``` - **Key observations:** - **Types** in SAL can be enumerated (`{idle, trying, critical}`), bounded integers, Booleans, arrays, or records. - **Initialization** is a predicate over the state variables; non-determinism is allowed. - **Transitions** can use guarded commands, non-deterministic choice (`IN { ... }`), and primed variables (`s1'`) to denote the next state. **Model Answer for (b):** - **The sal-smc verification methodology:** - `sal-smc` is a **symbolic model checker** targeted at *finite-state* systems. It encodes the entire state space using **Binary Decision Diagrams (BDDs)**, with the CUDD library as the underlying engine. - The transition relation $T(s, s')$, initial states $I(s)$, and the property $\phi(s)$ are all represented as BDDs. - The reachable-state set is computed iteratively using **BDD image computation**: $$ R_0 \;=\; I, \qquad R_{i+1} \;=\; R_i \cup \text{Image}(R_i, T) $$ where $\text{Image}(R, T) = \exists v.\, R(v) \wedge T(v, v')$ is a Boolean quantification computed symbolically as a BDD operation. - The iteration continues until a **fixpoint** $R_{i+1} = R_i$ is reached. The final BDD represents the exact set of all reachable states. - The property check reduces to a BDD conjunction: $R \wedge \lnot\phi$. If this is the **FALSE** BDD, the property holds on all reachable states — SAL prints `VERIFIED`. Otherwise, a counter-example can be extracted from the satisfying assignment of $R \wedge \lnot\phi$. - **BDD-based image computation vs. SAT-based BMC:** - **BDD approach (`sal-smc`):** - Operates on *sets* of states encoded as Boolean functions. - Provides a *complete* answer: VERIFIED or counter-example, for the entire finite state space. - Strength: elegant, handles concurrency and fairness naturally. - Weakness: BDD size can blow up in the worst case (the *state-space explosion* issue). - **SAT approach (`sal-bmc`):** - Operates on a *single* unrolled path of length $k$. - Provides a *bounded* answer: correct up to depth $k$ only. - Strength: scales well in practice for bug-finding; SAT solvers have improved dramatically. - Weakness: cannot prove unbounded correctness without induction. - **Complementarity in SAL:** The SAL framework offers both styles precisely because each excels in different scenarios — BDDs for *finite-state proofs*, SAT for *deep bug-hunting*. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on SAL Questions (and How to Avoid Them):** > - **Confusing `sal-bmc` with `sal-smc`.** A frequent error is claiming that `sal-bmc` gives a *full proof*. It does not — it only certifies the property *up to bound $k$*. Only $k$-induction (`sal-wmc`) or BDD model checking (`sal-smc`) can yield a full proof. > - **Writing generic definitions of "model checking" instead of SAL-specific content.** Examiners deduct marks if the answer does not mention SAL's back-ends, the STS representation, or the front-end/back-end split. > - **Omitting the formula $\Phi_k$.** For 7-mark sub-parts asking about BMC, the unrolling formula is *expected* — write it explicitly using the $I, T, \phi$ notation. > - **Mixing up `CONTEXT` and `MODULE` semantics.** The `CONTEXT` is *static* (types and shared variables); the `MODULE` is *dynamic* (initialization and transitions). Examiners check this distinction carefully. > - **Forgetting to mention the external engines.** SAL relies on third-party solvers (ICS, CVC3, CUDD).
