A complete answer references these. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **SAL = Symbolic Analysis Laboratory**, developed at **SRI International**. - It is a **framework**, not a single tool — it bundles a *language* with *five back-end analyzers*. - **Front-end:** parses `.sal` files containing `CONTEXT` (types/variables) and `MODULE` (initialization/transitions), producing the **SAL Transition System (STS)**. - **Back-end tools:** - `sal-bmc` — SAT-based Bounded Model Checking. - `sal-inf-bmc` — Infinite BMC with parameterized $k$. - `sal-wmc` — $k$-induction and PDR (full proofs). - `sal-smc` — BDD-based symbolic model checking (using CUDD). - `sal-sim` — Random/interactive simulator. - **BMC formula:** $$ \Phi_k \;\equiv\; I(s_0) \wedge \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k-1} T(s_i, s_{i+1}) \wedge \bigvee_{i=0}^{k} \lnot\phi(s_i) $$ - **$k$-Induction requires two obligations:** `Base_k` (property holds for first $k$ steps) and `Step_k` (property persists from $k$ to $k+1$). - **BDD image computation:** $$ R_{i+1} = R_i \cup \text{Image}(R_i, T) $$ - **Verification flow:** Author → Parse → STS → Dispatcher → Back-end → External engine (SAT/BDD) → Result. - **Strength:** Uniform description, multiple engines, handles finite and infinite states. - **Limitation:** BDD blow-up for smc; bounded verification for bmc without induction. - **Real-world use:** embedded systems, communication protocols, hardware pipelines, safety-critical software. - **For KTU answers:** always tie the discussion to a *specific* SAL back-end, mention the underlying solver, and (where applicable) write the formal $\Phi_k$ or fixpoint equation.

topics.title: The Language of SAL - The Expression Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The Language of SAL - The Expression Language # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of the SAL Expression Language The **SAL Expression Language** (often abbreviated as `sal-exp`) is the foundational sub-language of the **Symbolic Analysis Laboratory (SAL)** framework developed by SRI International. It defines the type system, the logic of expressions, the syntactic binders, and the modular structure (theories, contexts, and modules) that are reused by the SAL transition-system language (`sal-tls`), the assertion language, and the environment language (`sal-env`). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECNT526 – Module 4):** > The Expression Language is a *first-order, strongly typed, polymorphic* logical language. It is the layer on top of which transition systems, properties (LTL/CTL), and proofs are constructed. Mastering `sal-exp` is mandatory before studying the `sal-tls` transition-system language. Formally, `sal-exp` is built from three syntactic categories: 1. **Types** – Boolean, integer, real, enumeration, tuple, record, array, set. 2. **Expressions** – constructed from constants, variables, function applications, and operators. 3. **Modules** – reusable collections of declarations packaged as `THEORY`, `CONTEXT`, or `MODULE`. > [!IMPORTANT] > The Expression Language is **decoupled** from the transition-system language. A SAL model is *always* layered: a transition system is built on top of an `sal-exp` context. This separation is what allows SAL tools to share decision procedures across different model checkers and theorem provers. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of `sal-exp` as the **"vocabulary and grammar rules"** of a mathematics textbook: - **Types** are like the "kinds of objects" the textbook talks about — *numbers*, *sets*, *lists*, *records*. - **Expressions** are the "sentences" built using these objects and the allowed operations. - **Theories and Contexts** are like the "chapters" that group related vocabulary and axioms together so that later chapters (transition systems, properties) can simply *import* them. > [!TIP] > Just as you cannot write `x + apple` in a math problem, in SAL you cannot add a `Bool` to an `Integer`. The expression language's type checker is the "grammatical referee" that rejects such nonsense **at compile time** before any verification is even attempted. ## 1.3 Physical & Logical Constants Used The language is purely logical; the only "constants" are the boolean values, integers, reals, and user-declared literals. The Boolean values are the literals **TRUE** and **FALSE**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Type System of `sal-exp` SAL is *strongly* and *statically* typed. The type checker is invoked *before* model checking or theorem proving begins. The complete set of primitive and composite types is enumerated below. ### 2.1.1 Primitive (Built-in) Types | Type | Domain | Notation in SAL | Sample Literal | |------|--------|----------------|----------------| | Boolean | $\{ \text{TRUE}, \text{FALSE} \}$ | `BOOLEAN` | `TRUE`, `FALSE` | | Integer | $\mathbb{Z}$ (mathematical integers) | `INTEGER` | `0`, `-7`, `42` | | Real | $\mathbb{R}$ (mathematical reals) | `REAL` | `0.0`, `3.14` | | Enumeration | A user-defined finite set | `TYPE name = { a, b, c }` | `a` | ### 2.1.2 Composite (Constructor) Types | Type | Syntax | Meaning | Intuition | |------|--------|---------|-----------| | Tuple | `[T1, T2, ..., Tn]` | Cartesian product $T_1 \times T_2 \times \cdots \times T_n$ | A fixed-size heterogeneous list. | | Record | `[# field1 : T1; field2 : T2 #]` | Named-field product | A struct in C, a row in a database. | | Array | `ARRAY L OF T` | Mapping from index type $L$ to element type $T$ | An infinite map / lookup table. | | Set | `SET T` | Powerset of $T$ | The set of all elements of type $T$. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction — Array vs. Set:** > - `ARRAY INTEGER OF BOOLEAN` is the type of *all* functions $f : \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{B}$. > - `SET BOOLEAN` is the type of sets whose elements are booleans (i.e., either $\{\}$, $\{\text{TRUE}\}$, $\{\text{FALSE}\}$, or $\{\text{TRUE}, \text{FALSE}\}$). > These are *not* the same and the type checker will distinguish them. ## 2.2 Expression Forms Expressions are the *terms* of the SAL logic. They are categorized as: ### 2.2.1 Atomic Expressions - **Constants** (e.g., `0`, `TRUE`, `c1` where `c1` is declared as a constant). - **Variables** (e.g., `x`, `state.counter`). - **Function applications** `f(e1, ..., en)`. ### 2.2.2 Compound Expressions - **Arithmetic:** `+`, `-`, `*`, `div`, `mod`, `/` (for reals). - **Comparison:** `=`, `/=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`. - **Boolean:** `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, `=>` (implication), `<=>` (iff). - **Tuple / Record projections:** `e.1`, `e.field`. - **Conditional:** `IF b THEN e1 ELSE e2 ENDIF`. ### 2.2.3 Binder Expressions - **`LET x = e1 IN e2`** — local binding; $e_2$ is evaluated with $x$ replaced by $e_1$. - **`FORALL (x : T) : P`** — universal quantification: $\forall x \in T.\,P$. - **`EXISTS (x : T) : P`** — existential quantification: $\exists x \in T.\,P$. - **`LAMBDA (x : T) : e`** — anonymous function: $\lambda x : T.\,e$. ## 2.3 Module Structure: THEORY, CONTEXT, MODULE | Construct | Keyword | Purpose | What it Contains | |-----------|---------|---------|------------------| | Theory | `THEORY` | Pure, axiomatic math | Type declarations, constants, axioms, function symbols. No state. | | Context | `CONTEXT` | Parameterised theory | Generic theories with placeholder types/constants. | | Module | `MODULE` | Stateful entity | Variables, initialisation, transitions, definitions. (Used in `sal-tls`.) | ## 2.4 Context Relations (Inclusion, Extension, Instantiation) A theory $T_2$ can *use* a theory $T_1$ via: - **Inclusion:** `T2 = T1 + extra_decls` $\;\Rightarrow\;$ $T_2$ has all of $T_1$'s declarations. - **Extension:** Same as inclusion but adds new declarations. - **Instantiation:** `INSTANCE T1 WITH ..., ...` $\;\Rightarrow\;$ copy $T_1$ with concrete types replacing parameters. ## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | # | Construct | SAL Syntax | Mathematical Reading | |---|-----------|------------|----------------------| | 1 | Type declaration | `TYPE name = ...` | Introduce a new type. | | 2 | Constant | `c : T` | $c$ is an uninterpreted element of $T$. | | 3 | Function | `f : T1 -> T2` | $f : T_1 \to T_2$ | | 4 | Axiom | `AXIOM ax : P` | Assert $P$ as a global fact. | | 5 | Definition | `g(x:T) : T2 = e` | $g(x) \equiv e$ | | 6 | Let | `LET x = e1 IN e2` | $(\lambda x.\,e_2)\,e_1$ | | 7 | Forall | `FORALL (x:T) : P` | $\forall x \in T.\,P$ | | 8 | Exists | `EXISTS (x:T) : P` | $\exists x \in T.\,P$ | | 9 | Lambda | `LAMBDA (x:T) : e` | $\lambda x : T.\,e$ | | 10 | Conditional | `IF b THEN e1 ELSE e2 ENDIF` | $b \rightarrow e_1,\; \neg b \rightarrow e_2$ | | 11 | Set membership | `x IN S` | $x \in S$ | | 12 | Set cardinality | `card(S)` | $\vert S \vert$ | > [!NOTE] > The `card(S)` operator applies to **finite** sets only. Using it on an infinite set yields a type-checking error. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The SAL expression language is the substrate on which **safety-critical embedded systems** are verified — for example, avionics (DO-178C level A), railway signalling (EN 50128 SIL 4), and cryptographic protocol verification. The strict typing prevents entire classes of specification errors (e.g., confusing a control flag with a counter) that would otherwise produce vacuous or wrong verification results. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section walks through the *exact reasoning steps* expected in a KTU university answer script. Three worked examples are provided: (i) a type definition, (ii) a polymorphic function declaration, and (iii) a context instantiation. --- ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Declaring an Enumeration and Using It **Question:** Declare a SAL type `Color` with three values, declare a function `next : Color -> Color` such that red → green, green → blue, blue → red, and write the corresponding definition. ### Step 1 — Declare the Enumeration Type We introduce a new type whose domain is a finite set of three distinct labels. ``` TYPE Color = { red, green, blue } ``` The mathematical reading is: $$ \text{Color} \;\triangleq\; \{ \text{red}, \text{green}, \text{blue} \} $$ The type checker adds three *distinct* nullary constructors `red`, `green`, `blue` to the current theory. ### Step 2 — Declare the Function Symbol We first declare `next` as an *uninterpreted* function symbol of the right arity. ``` next : Color -> Color ``` At this stage the type checker is satisfied, but the meaning of `next` is unspecified. ### Step 3 — Add the Definition (An Axiomatic Definition) ``` next(red) = green next(green) = blue next(blue) = red ``` These three *equations* are the SAL idiom for a total-function definition. The type checker verifies that: - The left-hand side `next(c)` has type `Color` for every `c : Color`. - The right-hand side is a *closed* expression of type `Color`. The system internally conjoins these equations with the implicit assumption that `next` is *total*: $$ \forall c : \text{Color}.\; \exists c' : \text{Color}.\; \text{next}(c) = c' $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — A Polymorphic Theory and Its Instantiation **Question:** Define a polymorphic theory `List` parameterised by an element type `T`, containing the constants `nil` and `cons`, then instantiate it with `T = INTEGER`. ### Step 1 — Declare the Parameterised Context ``` CONTEXT List[T : TYPE] = THEORY BEGIN nil : List[T]; cons : T, List[T] -> List[T]; car : List[T] -> T; cdr : List[T] -> List[T] END ``` Reading: - `List[T : TYPE]` introduces a *type parameter* $T$. - `cons` is a binary function symbol taking an element of $T$ and a `List[T]` and producing a `List[T]`. - `car` and `cdr` are the standard destructors. ### Step 2 — Instantiate the Theory We replace the formal parameter `T` with the concrete type `INTEGER`: ``` IntegerList : THEORY = List[INTEGER] ``` Mathematically, the result is the theory: $$ \text{IntegerList} \;\triangleq\; \text{List} \langle T := \text{INTEGER} \rangle $$ with the renamed declarations: - `IntegerList.nil : List[INTEGER]` - `IntegerList.cons : INTEGER, List[INTEGER] -> List[INTEGER]` - `IntegerList.car : List[INTEGER] -> INTEGER` - `IntegerList.cdr : List[INTEGER] -> List[INTEGER]` ### Step 3 — Type Check For each declaration the verifier checks: $$ \frac{\Gamma \vdash e_1 : T_1 \quad \Gamma \vdash e_2 : T_2}{\Gamma \vdash \text{cons}(e_1, e_2) : \text{List}[T]} $$ This is a straightforward application of the typing rule for function application, ensuring the polymorphic substitution is **type-preserving**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — A Bounded Universal Quantifier **Question:** Express in SAL the statement: *"Every integer in a given set S is non-negative."* Let $S : \text{SET INTEGER}$. ### Step 1 — Identify the Quantified Variable and its Type The variable ranges over the elements of $S$, but in SAL we can quantify directly over the base type and use a membership predicate: - Variable: $x$ - Type: `INTEGER` - Body: $x \ge 0$ ### Step 2 — Restrict the Quantification to the Set SAL does not allow a quantifier to be restricted *directly* to a set, so we use an implication: $$ \forall x \in \mathbb{Z}.\; (x \in S) \;\Rightarrow\; (x \ge 0) $$ ### Step 3 — Write the SAL Expression ``` FORALL (x : INTEGER) : (x IN S) => (x >= 0) ``` The full type-checking judgement is: $$ \frac{\Gamma, x : \text{INTEGER} \vdash (x \in S) \Rightarrow (x \ge 0) : \text{BOOLEAN}} {\Gamma \vdash \text{FORALL }(x : \text{INTEGER}) : (x \in S) \Rightarrow (x \ge 0) : \text{BOOLEAN}} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Constructs and Their Semantics | SAL Construct | Abstract-Syntax Tree Form | Type-Checking Rule | Semantics | |---------------|---------------------------|--------------------|-----------| | `TRUE`, `FALSE` | leaf | always `BOOLEAN` | truth values | | `n` (numeric literal) | leaf | `INTEGER` if integer form, `REAL` if decimal form | numeric constant | | `x` (variable) | leaf | type from declaration | variable lookup | | `f(e1,...,en)` | `App(f, [e1,...,en])` | $\Gamma \vdash e_i : \tau_i$ and $f : \tau_1 \times \cdots \times \tau_n \to \sigma$ | function application | | `LET x = e1 IN e2` | `Let(x, e1, e2)` | $\Gamma \vdash e_1 : \tau,\; \Gamma, x : \tau \vdash e_2 : \sigma$ | $(\lambda x : \tau.\,e_2)\,e_1$ | | `IF b THEN e1 ELSE e2` | `Ite(b, e1, e2)` | $\Gamma \vdash b : \text{BOOLEAN},\; \Gamma \vdash e_1 : \sigma,\; \Gamma \vdash e_2 : \sigma$ | classical if-then-else | | `FORALL (x : T) : P` | `Forall(x, T, P)` | $\Gamma \vdash T : \text{TYPE},\; \Gamma, x : T \vdash P : \text{BOOLEAN}$ | $\forall x \in T.\,P$ | | `EXISTS (x : T) : P` | `Exists(x, T, P)` | $\Gamma \vdash T : \text{TYPE},\; \Gamma, x : T \vdash P : \text{BOOLEAN}$ | $\exists x \in T.\,P$ | | `LAMBDA (x : T) : e` | `Lambda(x, T, e)` | $\Gamma, x : T \vdash e : \sigma$ | $\lambda x : T.\,e$ | | `e.1`, `e.2` | `Proj(i, e)` | $\Gamma \vdash e : [\tau_1, ..., \tau_n]$ with $i \le n$ | $i$-th projection | | `e.field` | `ProjByName(field, e)` | $\Gamma \vdash e : [# field : \tau, ... #]$ | field access | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Three-Layer SAL Architecture The following Mermaid block illustrates the architectural relationship between the three sub-languages of SAL and how the Expression Language sits at the foundation. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeS["SAL Framework"] nodeE["sal-env
Environment Language"] nodeT["sal-tls
Transition System Language"] nodeX["sal-exp
Expression Language"] nodeTH["Type Checker and
Decision Procedures"] nodeM["SAL Model Checkers
sal-smc sal-inf-bmc
sal-bmc"] nodeP["SAL Provers
sal-pvs sad"] nodeS --> nodeE nodeS --> nodeT nodeS --> nodeX nodeT --> nodeX nodeE --> nodeX nodeX --> nodeTH nodeTH --> nodeM nodeTH --> nodeP ``` > [!NOTE] > Notice that **both** the transition-system language and the environment language are *downstream* of the expression language. This visually captures the foundational role of `sal-exp`. ## 4.2 Module Organisation of a Typical SAL Specification ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["TH1 basic_arith
THEORY
axioms of plus and times"] nodeB["TH2 list_ops
THEORY = basic_arith +
List declarations"] nodeC["CTX Queue
CONTEXT T : TYPE"] nodeD["MOD Producer
MODULE
state variables and transitions"] nodeE["MOD Consumer
MODULE"] nodeF["MOD System
MODULE = Producer WITH Consumer"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF ``` ## 4.3 Type Hierarchy in the Expression Language ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["SAL Types"] nodeB["Primitive Types
BOOLEAN INTEGER REAL"] nodeC["Enumeration Types
TYPE name = a b c ..."] nodeD["Composite Types"] nodeE["TUPLE T1 T2 ..."] nodeF["RECORD field1 T1 field2 T2 ..."] nodeG["ARRAY L OF T"] nodeH["SET T"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeH ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram of an Expression's Evaluation ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Source Expression
FORALL x : T : P"] nodeB["Parser
produces AST"] nodeC["Type Checker
verifies types"] nodeD["Simplifier
beta reduction"] nodeE["Decision Procedure
validity check"] nodeF["Result
VALID INVALID UNKNOWN"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q:** Define the **SAL Expression Language**. Mention any *three* primitive types it supports. **Model Answer:** The SAL Expression Language (`sal-exp`) is the typed, first-order, polymorphic logical sub-language of the SAL framework that defines the type system, expressions, binders, and modular packaging (theories, contexts) used throughout SAL. The three primitive types are: 1. **BOOLEAN** — domain $\{ \text{TRUE}, \text{FALSE} \}$. 2. **INTEGER** — domain $\mathbb{Z}$ (mathematical integers, not machine words). 3. **REAL** — domain $\mathbb{R}$. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for each of the three types)* --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q:** What is the difference between a `THEORY` and a `CONTEXT` in SAL? **Model Answer:** - A **`THEORY`** is a *closed* module containing type declarations, constants, function symbols, and axioms. It has no free type parameters. - A **`CONTEXT`** is a *parameterised* theory: it declares formal type or constant parameters (e.g., `T : TYPE`) that must be supplied upon instantiation. A context becomes a theory only after its parameters are bound via the `INSTANCE` keyword. *(3 marks: 1.5 for theory, 1.5 for context)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided) ### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]` **A (a)** Explain the SAL type system in detail. Describe the *primitive*, *enumeration*, and *composite* types with one example each. (7 marks) **A (b)** Consider the SAL declaration: ``` THEORY Stack = THEORY BEGIN TYPE Element; s : ARRAY INTEGER OF Element; top: INTEGER; AXIOM ax_well_formed : FORALL (i : INTEGER) : (i >= 0) => (i <= top); END ``` Write the equivalent mathematical specification and explain the role of `FORALL` and `=>` here. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for A (a) The SAL type system is *strongly*, *statically* typed and is the *first* component checked by the SAL tool-chain. **Primitive Types:** `BOOLEAN`, `INTEGER`, `REAL`. These are built-in; the user does not declare them. ``` x : BOOLEAN n : INTEGER r : REAL ``` **Enumeration Types:** User-defined finite types. Example: ``` TYPE Day = { Mon, Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun } ``` The type checker adds seven nullary constructors. **Composite Types:** 1. **Tuple:** `[T1, T2, ...]` — e.g., `[INTEGER, BOOLEAN]`. 2. **Record:** `[# name : STRING; age : INTEGER #]` — named fields. 3. **Array:** `ARRAY L OF T` — total function $L \to T$. 4. **Set:** `SET T` — finite subsets of $T$. #### Model Solution for A (b) The mathematical translation is: $$ \Sigma_{\text{Stack}} = \begin{cases} \text{Element} : \text{TYPE} \\ s : \mathbb{Z} \to \text{Element} \\ \text{top} : \mathbb{Z} \\ \text{ax\_well\_formed} : \forall i \in \mathbb{Z}.\; (i \ge 0) \Rightarrow (i \le \text{top}) \end{cases} $$ - **`FORALL (i : INTEGER) :`** introduces a *universally quantified* variable $i$ ranging over the mathematical integers. - **`=>`** is the *logical implication*. The combined statement says: *"For every integer $i$, if $i$ is non-negative, then $i$ is at most `top`."* This is the well-formedness constraint that the array indices ever used are bounded by `top`. --- ### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]` *(Internal Alternative)* **B (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the three-layer architecture of SAL. Discuss the role of the Expression Language as the foundation. (7 marks) **B (b)** Consider the following SAL specification: ``` CONTEXT Monoid[T : TYPE] = THEORY BEGIN e : T; op : [T, T -> T]; AXIOM ax_identity : FORALL (x : T) : (op(x, e) = x) AND (op(e, x) = x); AXIOM ax_assoc : FORALL (x : T, y : T, z : T) : op(op(x, y), z) = op(x, op(y, z)); END ``` Instantiate this context for `T = INTEGER` and interpret both axioms in plain English. (7 marks) #### Model Solution for B (a) The three-layer architecture is: 1. **Expression Language (`sal-exp`)** — types, expressions, modules. 2. **Transition-System Language (`sal-tls`)** — modules with state, initialisation, transitions, definitions. *Imports* an `sal-exp` context. 3. **Environment Language (`sal-env`)** — combines multiple transition-system modules and asserts properties. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for layer description, 2 for foundational role of `sal-exp`.)* #### Model Solution for B (b) **Instantiation:** ``` IntegerMonoid : THEORY = Monoid[INTEGER] ``` This binds the formal type parameter `T` to `INTEGER`, yielding the renamed declarations: - `IntegerMonoid.e : INTEGER` - `IntegerMonoid.op : [INTEGER, INTEGER -> INTEGER]` - `IntegerMonoid.ax_identity : FORALL (x : INTEGER) : (op(x, e) = x) AND (op(e, x) = x)` - `IntegerMonoid.ax_assoc : FORALL (x, y, z : INTEGER) : op(op(x, y), z) = op(x, op(y, z))` **Plain-English Interpretation:** - **`ax_identity`:** "The element `e` is an *identity* for the binary operation `op` — combining `e` with any `x` on either side leaves `x` unchanged." - **`ax_assoc`:** "The binary operation `op` is *associative* — for any three elements `x`, `y`, `z`, the result of applying `op` in left-to-right or any other bracketing order is the same." These are exactly the monoid axioms of abstract algebra. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:** > 1. **Do not** confuse a `MODULE` (stateful, used in `sal-tls`) with a `THEORY` (stateless, in `sal-exp`). Writing a `THEORY` with `INITIALISATION` will fetch **zero marks** — `INITIALISATION` belongs to `sal-tls`. > 2. **Quantifier scope:** When asked to write a quantifier over a *set*, remember SAL only quantifies over *types*. You **must** add the membership predicate inside the body, e.g., `FORALL (x : INTEGER) : (x IN S) => P`. > 3. **Type mismatches:** `ARRAY INTEGER OF BOOLEAN` and `SET BOOLEAN` look similar but are *not* interchangeable. Examiners will deduct marks for confusing them. > 4. **Polymorphism:** A `CONTEXT` without its `INSTANCE` is *useless*. Always close your answer by showing the instantiation. > 5. **Cardinality of infinite sets:** Never write `card(S)` where `S` is of an infinite type — it is a type error and yields 0 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] `sal-exp` is the *typed, first-order, polymorphic* foundation of the entire SAL framework. - [ ] Three primitive types: `BOOLEAN`, `INTEGER`, `REAL`. - [ ] User-defined types: `TYPE name = { ... }` (enumerations). - [ ] Composite types: tuple `[T1, T2]`, record `[# f : T #]`, array `ARRAY L OF T`, set `SET T`. - [ ] Expressions are built from constants, variables, function applications, and operators (`+`, `-`, `*`, `=`, `=>`, `<=>`, `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`). - [ ] Binders: `LET x = e1 IN e2`, `FORALL (x : T) : P`, `EXISTS (x : T) : P`, `LAMBDA (x : T) : e`. - [ ] Conditional: `IF b THEN e1 ELSE e2 ENDIF`. - [ ] Three module kinds: `THEORY` (closed), `CONTEXT` (parameterised), `MODULE` (stateful, in `sal-tls`). - [ ] `INSTANCE` binds context parameters to concrete types/constants. - [ ] `INCLUDES` / `=` performs theory extension. - [ ] `card` applies to **finite** sets only. - [ ] Quantifiers range over *types*, not sets — use the `IN` predicate inside the body. - [ ] All SAL axioms must be closed *boolean* expressions. - [ ] Type checking happens *before* any model checking or theorem-proving begins — the type checker is the "grammatical referee." - [ ] The Expression Language sits *below* the Transition-System Language and the Environment Language in the SAL stack.

topics.title: The Transition Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Transition Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of The Transition Language

In the context of **Automated Verification** under the KTU 2024 scheme, a **Transition Language** is a mathematically rigorous formal notation used to describe the operational behavior of concurrent, reactive, and distributed systems through a set of well-defined **state-transition rules**. The most foundational instance of a transition language is the **Labeled Transition System (LTS)**, which serves as the semantic backbone for model checkers such as **SPIN**, **NuSMV**, **UPPAAL**, and process algebras like **CCS (Calculus of Communicating Systems)** and **CSP (Communicating Sequential Processes)**.

Formally, a **Labeled Transition System** is defined as a 4-tuple:

$$\mathcal{T} = (S, Act, \rightarrow, s_0)$$

where:
- $S$ is a non-empty, **finite** set of **states** (the configuration space of the system).
- $Act$ is a finite set of **observable actions** or **labels** (often denoted $\Sigma$ or $L$).
- $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times Act \times S$ is the **transition relation**.
- $s_0 \in S$ is the distinguished **initial state**.

A single transition step is conventionally written as:

$$s \xrightarrow{a} s'$$

which is read as: "**the system in state $s$ can evolve to state $s'$ by performing the action $a$**".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The transition language provides the *semantic model* upon which temporal logic specifications (CTL, LTL, CTL*) are interpreted. Every formula $\phi$ is evaluated over a structure derived from a transition language artifact.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are reading a **subway map of a city**. Each **metro station** on the map represents a *state* of the system. The **colored lines** connecting two stations represent the *actions* (e.g., a train ride, a boarding event, a synchronization). The **rules of movement** — "you can go from Station A to Station B only by the red line" — are exactly the **transition rules** of the language.

In software, every line of code that can change the program's memory configuration is a potential "station" and every execution step is a "ride". A **Transition Language** formalizes this intuition: it is a precise, mathematical *map* of every legal path the program can take during execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Verification engineers at Intel, Airbus, and NASA use transition languages to certify that microprocessors, flight-control firmware, and rover autopilots cannot enter unsafe configurations. The **CPU pipeline** of a modern processor has on the order of $10^9$ states — and transition languages are what make reasoning about them tractable.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following quantities are universally used in KTU-level problems on transition languages:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Engineering Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\vert S \vert$ | Number of reachable states | $10^2$–$10^9$ |
| $\vert Act \vert$ | Alphabet size (number of distinct actions) | $1$–$2^{32}$ |
| $d(s)$ | Out-degree of state $s$ | $\leq \vert S \vert$ |
| $\rightarrow^*$ | Reflexive-transitive closure (paths) | unbounded |
| $\mathcal{B}(S)$ | Power set of $S$ ($2^{\vert S \vert}$ subsets) | exponential in $\vert S \vert$ |

> [!TIP]
> **State-Space Explosion:** The single most important metric in transition-based verification is that the set of all behaviors grows **exponentially** with the number of concurrent components — this is the *state-space explosion problem* that drives the need for symbolic methods (BDDs, SAT/SMT, partial-order reduction).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Transition Rule

A transition rule is the atomic unit of the transition language. It has the form:

$$\frac{\text{preconditions (hypotheses)}}{\text{conclusion}}$$

The **conclusion** is the *fact being derived* (e.g., $s \xrightarrow{a} s'$), while the **preconditions** are the *reasoning steps* that must hold for the rule to fire. This is the same **Natural Deduction / Structural Operational Semantics (SOS)** style invented by Gordon Plotkin (1981).

### The Operational Workflow

1. **Identify the syntactic construct** whose behavior must be described (e.g., a process, a statement, an automaton).
2. **Decompose** the construct into its atomic components.
3. **Apply the matching transition rule** from the language's inference system.
4. **Derive the next state** by composing the residual sub-components.
5. **Repeat recursively** until a terminal (stuck) state or a stable fixed point is reached.

### The Core 'Why' and 'How'

> The transition language is *compositional*: the behavior of a whole system is computed from the behaviors of its parts. This **compositionality** is what allows modular verification — a property proven for a component can be reused when the component is embedded in a larger system.

## 2.2 Classes of Transition Languages

KTU examiners frequently distinguish the following three families:

| Family | Defining Trait | Example System |
|---|---|---|
| **LTS (Labeled Transition System)** | Transitions carry observable action labels | Mealy/Moore machines, process algebras |
| **Kripke Structure** | Transitions are *unlabeled*; states carry atomic proposition labels | CTL model checking (NuSMV) |
| **Timed Automaton** | Transitions guarded by clock constraints | UPPAAL, real-time verification |

The mathematical bridge between a Kripke structure and an LTS is given by the **forgetful functor**: a Kripke structure is essentially an LTS over the silent action $\tau$ with the labels re-attached to states.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation rule:** To prevent markdown table breakage, the symbol $\vert$ (absolute value / cardinality) is rendered via `\vert` inside $\LaTeX$ math mode, never as a bare pipe character.

| # | Concept | Formal Expression | Remarks / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LTS definition | $\mathcal{T} = (S, Act, \rightarrow, s_0)$ | Foundation of every model checker |
| 2 | Single transition | $s \xrightarrow{a} s'$ | Atomic step, label $a \in Act$ |
| 3 | Path (execution) | $\pi = s_0 \xrightarrow{a_0} s_1 \xrightarrow{a_1} s_2 \cdots$ | Used in LTL / CTL* semantics |
| 4 | Reachability | $s \rightarrow^* s'$ | Fixed-point computation: $\mu X. \, \text{Post}(X) \cup \{s_0\}$ |
| 5 | Strong bisimulation ($\sim$) | $\forall a \in Act.\ s \xrightarrow{a} s' \Leftrightarrow t \xrightarrow{a} t''$ and $s' \sim t''$ | Equivalence of behaviors (CCS) |
| 6 | Weak bisimulation ($\approx$) | Hides $\tau$ (silent) actions | Used in protocol verification |
| 7 | Simulation pre-order | $s \preceq t$ | One-directional behavioral refinement |
| 8 | Number of states | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert S_i \vert$ for $n$ parallel components | **State-space explosion** |
| 9 | Pre-image operator | $\text{Pre}_{\exists}(Q) = \{s \mid \exists s' \in Q,\ a \in Act,\ s \xrightarrow{a} s'\}$ | Backward CTL fixpoint |
| 10 | Post-image operator | $\text{Post}_{\exists}(Q) = \{s' \mid \exists s \in Q,\ a \in Act,\ s \xrightarrow{a} s'\}$ | Forward reachability |
| 11 | Satisfaction | $\mathcal{T}, s \models \phi$ | System $\mathcal{T}$ satisfies formula $\phi$ at $s$ |
| 12 | State explosion bound | $O(2^{\vert S \vert})$ for explicit model checking | Drives symbolic (BDD) methods |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Transition Language

- **Hardware model checking:** Verifying that no deadlocks occur in an arbiter bus.
- **Software verification:** SPIN's input language **PROMELA** is a transition language for asynchronous protocols.
- **Communication protocols:** TLS, Bluetooth — verified using LTSs and bisimulation.
- **Cyber-physical systems:** Timed automata (UPPAAL) verify that an insulin pump never over-doses a patient.
- **AI safety:** Bounded model checkers encode transformer activations as transition relations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Inductive Transition Rules to the Global LTS

**Problem (KTU-typical, 14-mark style):**
Consider two communicating processes defined in CCS-like notation:

$$P \triangleq a.P \quad\quad Q \triangleq \bar{a}.Q$$

that synchronize on action $a$ (complementary actions). Construct the global transition system for $P \parallel Q$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the action alphabet.**

$$Act = \{a,\ \bar{a},\ \tau\}$$

The action $a$ is the *send*, $\bar{a}$ is the *receive*, and $\tau$ is the result of their *synchronization* (invisible to an external observer).

**Step 2 — Write the inductive rule for the constant process $P$.**

By the axiom of recursive definition (Kleene fixed-point semantics), we treat $P$ as a state label and write:

$$\frac{}{P \xrightarrow{a} P} \quad (\text{AX}_P) \qquad \frac{}{Q \xrightarrow{\bar{a}} Q} \quad (\text{AX}_Q)$$

**Step 3 — Write the parallel composition rule.**

For two components $X$ and $Y$ running concurrently, the standard CCS parallel rule is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{X \xrightarrow{a} X'}{X \parallel Y \xrightarrow{a} X' \parallel Y} \quad (\text{Par-L}) \qquad
\frac{Y \xrightarrow{a} Y'}{X \parallel Y \xrightarrow{a} X \parallel Y'} \quad (\text{Par-R})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{X \xrightarrow{a} X' \quad Y \xrightarrow{\bar{a}} Y'}{X \parallel Y \xrightarrow{\tau} X' \parallel Y'} \quad (\text{Par-Com})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the concrete actions.**

Apply $\text{Par-Com}$ with $X = P$, $a = a$, $X' = P$, $Y = Q$, $\bar{a} = \bar{a}$, $Y' = Q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P \xrightarrow{a} P \quad Q \xrightarrow{\bar{a}} Q}{P \parallel Q \xrightarrow{\tau} P \parallel Q} \quad (\text{Inst})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Read off the global LTS.**

The system has a single state, namely $s_0 = P \parallel Q$, and a single self-loop labeled $\tau$. The resulting LTS is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{T} = \big(\{P \parallel Q\},\ \{a, \bar{a}, \tau\},\ \{(P \parallel Q, \tau, P \parallel Q)\},\ P \parallel Q\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

The *external* behavior is a single silent action $\tau$ on every cycle. Therefore $P \parallel Q$ is **observationally equivalent to NIL** (it does nothing observable). Formally: $P \parallel Q \approx \mathbf{0}$ under weak bisimulation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Computing Reachability via the Post-Image Fixpoint

**Problem:** Given an LTS with $S = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}$ and transitions $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1$, $s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s_2$, $s_1 \xrightarrow{a} s_2$, $s_1 \xrightarrow{c} s_3$, $s_2 \xrightarrow{b} s_4$, $s_3 \xrightarrow{c} s_4$, compute the set of states reachable from $s_0$.

### Step-by-Step Fixpoint Iteration

Let $R_0 = \{s_0\}$.

Define the post-image operator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Post}(R) = R \cup \{s' \in S \mid \exists s \in R,\ \exists a \in Act,\ s \xrightarrow{a} s'\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Post}(R_0) &= \{s_0\} \cup \{s' \mid s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s' \text{ or } s_0 \xrightarrow{b} s'\} \\
&= \{s_0\} \cup \{s_1, s_2\} \\
&= \{s_0, s_1, s_2\} = R_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Post}(R_1) &= R_1 \cup \{s' \mid s_1 \xrightarrow{a} s' \text{ or } s_1 \xrightarrow{c} s' \text{ or } s_2 \xrightarrow{b} s'\} \\
&= \{s_0, s_1, s_2\} \cup \{s_2, s_3, s_4\} \\
&= \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\} = R_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Post}(R_2) &= R_2 \cup \emptyset = R_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The fixpoint $R_2$ is reached when $R_{k+1} = R_k$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reach}(s_0) = \{s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Every state is reachable — the system is **fully connected** in its reachable sub-graph.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property-Checking Template

For a given KTU-style property, the following matrix is the standard examiner's valuation key:

| Step | Examiner Expectation | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the *kind* of property (safety / liveness / fairness) | 1 |
| 2 | Choose the right temporal logic (LTL / CTL / CTL*) | 1 |
| 3 | Translate the natural-language statement into a formula | 2 |
| 4 | Identify which fixpoint operator is required ($\mu$ or $\nu$) | 2 |
| 5 | Execute the fixpoint iteration over the LTS | 5 |
| 6 | State the verdict and interpret it in English | 2 |
| 7 | Discuss the complexity and any state-space concerns | 1 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Anatomy of a Transition Language Compiler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Program] --> B[Lexer and Parser]
    B --> C[Abstract Syntax Tree]
    C --> D[Operational Semantics Engine]
    D --> E[SOS Rule Selector]
    E --> F[Transition Derivation Stack]
    F --> G[Labeled Transition System LTS]
    G --> H[State Space Explorer]
    H --> I[Temporal Logic Evaluator]
    I --> J{Property Holds?}
    J -->|Yes| K[VERIFIED Counterexample Absent]
    J -->|No| L[COUNTEREXAMPLE Trace Output]
    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#ff9,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style L fill:#f99,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading guide:** The diagram traces the *end-to-end pipeline* of an automated verifier. The transition language is consumed by the **Operational Semantics Engine** (block D), which selects the appropriate **SOS rule** for each syntactic construct, builds the **LTS** (block G), and hands it to the model checker (blocks H–I).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Reachability Graph of the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0[s0] -- a --> s1[s1]
    s0 -- b --> s2[s2]
    s1 -- a --> s2[s2]
    s1 -- c --> s3[s3]
    s2 -- b --> s4[s4]
    s3 -- c --> s4[s4]
    style s0 fill:#ffd
    style s4 fill:#dfd
```

> **Reading guide:** Every node is a *state* in $S$, and every labeled edge is a transition in $\rightarrow$. The **initial state** $s_0$ is highlighted in yellow; the **sink-like state** $s_4$ (no outgoing edges) is highlighted in green. This is exactly the LTS the student must build in Section 3.2.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Model Checker

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Model M as LTS] --> E[Explorer Module]
    SPEC[Property phi in CTL or LTL] --> E
    E --> FF[Fixpoint Computation Engine]
    FF --> SAT[Satisfaction Set Sat phi]
    SAT --> C{Initial state in Sat phi}
    C -->|Yes| OK[System Satisfies phi]
    C -->|No| BAD[Violation Found]
    BAD --> CE[Counterexample Witness Path]
    style M fill:#cff
    style SPEC fill:#fcf
    style OK fill:#9f9
    style BAD fill:#f99
    style CE fill:#fc9
```

> **Reading guide:** This is the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a generic model checker. The transition language provides the *model* (top-left), the user supplies the *specification* (top-right), and the engine computes the satisfaction set via a fixpoint.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`
**Define a Labeled Transition System (LTS). List its four components and explain the meaning of the transition relation $\rightarrow$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Labeled Transition System is a formal model $\mathcal{T} = (S, Act, \rightarrow, s_0)$ where:

- $S$ is a non-empty finite set of states (1 mark).
- $Act$ is a finite set of observable actions / labels (1 mark).
- $\rightarrow \subseteq S \times Act \times S$ is the transition relation; a tuple $(s, a, s') \in \rightarrow$ is written $s \xrightarrow{a} s'$ and means "from state $s$, the system can move to state $s'$ while performing action $a$" (1 mark).
- $s_0 \in S$ is the initial state.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`
**Distinguish between a Labeled Transition System (LTS) and a Kripke Structure. Why is each preferred for LTL versus CTL model checking?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- An **LTS** carries labels on the *transitions* (edges) (1 mark), while a **Kripke structure** carries labels on the *states* (nodes) and has unlabeled transitions (1 mark).
- **LTL** (linear-time) is naturally interpreted over the set of all infinite paths of an LTS, so LTSs are the standard semantic model. **CTL** (branching-time) talks about "there exists a path" / "for all paths" with state-quantifiers; states must be the labeled entities, so Kripke structures are preferred (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Consider the CCS-like system:
> $$P = a.b.\mathbf{0} \quad\quad Q = \bar{a}.\bar{b}.\mathbf{0}$$
> Derive the global transition system of $P \parallel Q$ using the standard parallel composition rules of CCS. Show every inference step explicitly.

> **(b) [7 marks]** For the LTS you obtained, write the CTL formula expressing the property *"there exists a path on which a synchronization action $\tau$ occurs at least once, and globally it is never the case that the system is in a deadlocked state"*. Evaluate the formula on your LTS.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — List the inference axioms for the leaf processes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \xrightarrow{a} P' \quad &\text{where } P' = b.\mathbf{0} \tag{AX$_1$} \\
P' \xrightarrow{b} \mathbf{0} \quad &\tag{AX$_2$} \\
Q \xrightarrow{\bar{a}} Q' \quad &\text{where } Q' = \bar{b}.\mathbf{0} \tag{AX$_3$} \\
Q' \xrightarrow{\bar{b}} \mathbf{0} \quad &\tag{AX$_4$}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the CCS communication rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P \xrightarrow{a} P' \quad Q \xrightarrow{\bar{a}} Q'}{P \parallel Q \xrightarrow{\tau} P' \parallel Q'} \quad \text{(Com)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \parallel Q \xrightarrow{\tau} P' \parallel Q' = (b.\mathbf{0}) \parallel (\bar{b}.\mathbf{0}) \tag{D$_1$}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Par-L and Par-R independently.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P' \parallel Q' \xrightarrow{b} \mathbf{0} \parallel Q' \tag{D$_2$} \\
P' \parallel Q' \xrightarrow{\bar{b}} P' \parallel \mathbf{0} \tag{D$_3$}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Com again on the $b$/$\bar{b}$ pair.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P' \parallel Q' \xrightarrow{\tau} \mathbf{0} \parallel \mathbf{0} = \mathbf{0} \tag{D$_4$}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final LTS.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{T} = \big(&\{P \parallel Q,\ P' \parallel Q',\ \mathbf{0}\}, \\
&\{a, \bar{a}, b, \bar{b}, \tau\}, \\
&\{D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4\}, \\
&P \parallel Q\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

The states form a linear chain with one $\tau$ synchronization at each step.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Translate the English property into CTL.**

> "*There exists a path on which a synchronization action $\tau$ occurs at least once, and globally it is never the case that the system is in a deadlocked state.*"

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi \;=\; EF(\tau\text{-step}) \;\land\; AG(\neg\text{deadlock})
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $EF$ is the *exists-future* operator and $AG$ is the *all-globally* operator of CTL. The atomic proposition $\tau\text{-step}$ is true at a state if the state has at least one outgoing $\tau$-transition.

**Step 2 — Label each state with the atomic propositions.**

- $s_0 = P \parallel Q$: $\tau\text{-step} = \text{true}$ (has D$_1$); deadlock = false.
- $s_1 = P' \parallel Q'$: $\tau\text{-step} = \text{true}$ (has D$_4$); deadlock = false.
- $s_2 = \mathbf{0}$: $\tau\text{-step} = \text{false}$; deadlock = true.

**Step 3 — Evaluate $EF(\tau\text{-step})$ from $s_0$.**

There is a path $s_0 \xrightarrow{\tau} s_1$, so $s_0 \models EF(\tau\text{-step})$. **TRUE (1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Evaluate $AG(\neg\text{deadlock})$ from $s_0$.**

On the path $s_0 \to s_1 \to s_2$, the state $s_2$ is a deadlock. Hence there is a reachable state where deadlock holds, so $s_0 \not\models AG(\neg\text{deadlock})$. **FALSE (1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$\phi$ evaluates to **FALSE**. The system *does* deadlock at $\mathbf{0}$, violating the second conjunct. (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Complexity & comment.**

The state space has only 3 states — manageable. In general, the cost of computing $EF$ is $O(\vert S \vert + \vert \rightarrow \vert)$ and $AG$ requires a greatest fixpoint of comparable cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing LTS and Kripke structure** (–2 marks): students often mis-state which entity carries the labels. State labels belong to *states*; transition labels belong to *transitions*.
> 2. **Forgetting to enumerate *all* SOS rules** (–2 marks): only one rule (Com) is applied, missing Par-L / Par-R interleavings.
> 3. **Using `EF` and `AF` interchangeably** (–1 mark): `EF` means "there *exists* a future path", `AF` means "on *all* paths, eventually". Mixing them is a classic error.
> 4. **Skipping the fixpoint step** (–2 marks): in the formula evaluation, students jump to the verdict without computing the satisfaction set iteratively.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model — Internal Choice]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **bisimulation** in the transition-language framework. Define *strong bisimulation* ($\sim$) and *weak bisimulation* ($\approx$). Give one engineering scenario where each is more appropriate than the other.

> **(b) [7 marks]** For the LTS $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1$, $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_2$, $s_1 \xrightarrow{b} s_3$, $s_2 \xrightarrow{c} s_3$, determine whether $s_1$ and $s_2$ are strongly bisimilar. Justify by exhibiting a relation or by showing a distinguishing formula.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Strong bisimulation** is the largest relation $\mathcal{R} \subseteq S \times S$ such that whenever $(s, t) \in \mathcal{R}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\forall a \in Act.\ \forall s'.\ (s \xrightarrow{a} s') \Rightarrow \exists t'.\ (t \xrightarrow{a} t') \wedge (s', t') \in \mathcal{R} \\
\forall a \in Act.\ \forall t'.\ (t \xrightarrow{a} t') \Rightarrow \exists s'.\ (s \xrightarrow{a} s') \wedge (s', t') \in \mathcal{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Two states are **strongly bisimilar**, written $s \sim t$, iff there exists such a relation $\mathcal{R}$ with $(s,t) \in \mathcal{R}$ (1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the co-inductive flavor).

**Weak bisimulation** ($\approx$) relaxes the matching by allowing any number of $\tau$ (silent) actions on either side, written $\Rightarrow$ for weak transitions. The match becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s \xrightarrow{a} s' \text{ must be matched by } t \xRightarrow{a} t' \text{ (i.e., } t \Rightarrow \xrightarrow{a} \Rightarrow t')
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark for the modified condition).

**Engineering scenarios:**

- **Strong bisimulation** is used in *synchronous hardware verification* (e.g., proving two implementations of a multiplier are step-equivalent). Every clock cycle must match — $\tau$ is not allowed to be hidden (2 marks).
- **Weak bisimulation** is used in *asynchronous protocol verification* (e.g., TCP handshake refinements), where internal retransmissions (silent retries) should be ignored (2 marks).

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Attempt to construct a strong bisimulation.**

Suppose $\mathcal{R} = \{(s_1, s_2), (s_3, s_3)\}$.

- Check $(s_1, s_2)$: $s_1 \xrightarrow{b} s_3$ must be matched by $s_2 \xrightarrow{b} \cdots$. But $s_2$ has *no* outgoing $b$-transition. So the match fails. (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Conclude that $s_1 \not\sim s_2$.**

The distinguishing action is $b$ (1 mark).

**Step 3 — Exhibit a distinguishing CTL formula (1 mark).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi \;=\; \exists \bigcirc b
\end{aligned}
$$

"There exists a successor state reachable in one step via $b$." Then:

- $s_1 \models \exists \bigcirc b$ because $s_1 \xrightarrow{b} s_3$ (1 mark).
- $s_2 \not\models \exists \bigcirc b$ because $s_2$ has no $b$-successor (1 mark).

Hence $s_1$ and $s_2$ are distinguished by $\phi$, confirming $s_1 \not\sim s_2$ (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The Transition Language** is the **semantic foundation** of every model checker. The four-tuple $\mathcal{T} = (S, Act, \rightarrow, s_0)$ is the canonical definition.
- The transition relation $\rightarrow$ is the **atomic step**; the reflexive-transitive closure $\rightarrow^*$ is the **path**.
- **LTS vs Kripke**: LTS = labels on *edges*; Kripke = labels on *states*. The right choice depends on the temporal logic (LTL vs CTL).
- **Operational Semantics (SOS)** uses inference-rule notation
  $$\frac{\text{hypotheses}}{\text{conclusion}}$$
  to define transitions compositionally.
- **Bisimulation** ($\sim$) = strongest *behavioral equivalence*; **weak bisimulation** ($\approx$) hides $\tau$-actions.
- **Reachability** = the *least fixpoint* $\mu X.\,\text{Post}(X) \cup \{s_0\}$; **invariant checking** = the *greatest fixpoint* $\nu X.\,\text{Pre}(X) \cap Q$.
- **State-space explosion** is the central engineering challenge: $n$ parallel components of size $k$ give $k^n$ reachable states — solve with **BDDs**, **partial-order reduction**, or **abstraction/refinement (CEGAR)**.
- **Exam mantra**: "Define the LTS → apply SOS rules → compute the satisfaction set via fixpoints → interpret the verdict." This is the **valuation key** the KTU examiner follows.
- **CTL operators to memorize**: $EX, AX, EF, AF, EG, AG, EU, AU$ — and especially never confuse $E$ (exists) with $A$ (all).
- **Mark distribution pattern (Part B, 14 marks)**: definition (2) + construction (4) + verification (5) + interpretation (2) + complexity (1).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Module Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Module Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Module Language** is the formal input specification language used in symbolic model checkers (such as **NuSMV**, **SMV**, and similar finite-state verification frameworks) to describe a transition system, its state variables, its initial conditions, its transition relation, and the properties (LTL/CTL) that must be verified against it.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the **Module Language** is the **declarative, finite-state modelling language** that allows a verification engineer to:
- Declare **a hierarchical, encapsulated module** (the *system-under-verification*).
- Declare the **state-holding variables** (`VAR`), **input variables** (`IVAR`), and **frozen variables** (`FROZENVAR`).
- Define the **initial state predicate** (`INIT`).
- Define the **next-state / transition relation** (`ASSIGN` or `TRANS`).
- State the **temporal logic properties** to be model-checked (`SPEC`, `LTLSPEC`, `CTLSPEC`, `INVAR`).

Every KTU board paper expects students to be able to **write, read, and reason about** small Module Language programs (also called **SMV programs** after the original Carnegie-Mellon tool).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Vending Machine Blueprint**

Imagine you are a civil engineer designing a vending machine. Before the machine is built, you write a **blueprint** that tells the factory:
1. What are the *physical slots* (the variables — coin slot, button panel, dispenser).
2. What is the *initial state* (empty, locked, no coins).
3. How the machine *behaves* when a button is pressed (transitions).
4. What *safety properties* must always hold (never dispense two items, never accept invalid coins).

The **Module Language** is precisely that blueprint — but for software/hardware systems and verified by a *computer* (the model checker) instead of a human inspector. A **module** in the language is the equivalent of one complete blueprint for one component, and a **system** is built by composing modules (just like a vending machine is built from a coin-acceptor module and a dispenser module).

### 1.3 Key Constants / Metrics

| Metric | Value / Convention |
|---|---|
| Boolean constants | `TRUE`, `FALSE` |
| Integer range | Finite bounded integers (model checker is finite-state) |
| Reserved keywords | `MODULE`, `VAR`, `IVAR`, `FROZENVAR`, `ASSIGN`, `INIT`, `TRANS`, `INVAR`, `SPEC`, `LTLSPEC`, `CTLSPEC`, `JUSTICE`, `COMPASSION`, `DEFINE` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Model Checker's **state space** is bounded by the **Cartesian product** of all variable domains. For $n$ Boolean variables, the state space has exactly $2^n$ states — the *fundamental explosion* the language is designed to manage via *symbolic* representations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Structural Pillars of a Module

A complete Module Language program is organised into **seven** logical sections. The KTU examiner's *most-asked* sub-question is *"Explain the components / structure of a module."*

**1. Module Declaration Block**
- Begins with the keyword `MODULE` followed by a module identifier.
- A *main* module is the system root; sub-modules are reusable components.
- Modules are **synchronously composed** (parallel composition with lock-step transitions).

**2. Variable Declaration Block (`VAR`, `IVAR`, `FROZENVAR`)**
- `VAR` — system state variables, re-evaluated every transition.
- `IVAR` — environment-controlled inputs, **non-deterministic** from the system's perspective.
- `FROZENVAR` — constants fixed at initialisation; useful for parameterised models.

**3. Definition Block (`DEFINE`)**
- Macros for readability, e.g. `DEFINE max := 7;`

**4. Initialisation Block (`INIT`)**
- A propositional predicate that all initial states must satisfy.

**5. Transition Block (`ASSIGN` or `TRANS`)**
- `ASSIGN` block assigns *next-state* values using `:=` (deterministic) and `:= {v1, v2}` (non-deterministic).
- `TRANS` block lists a propositional predicate over current and next-state variables.

**6. Invariant Block (`INVAR`)**
- A propositional predicate that must hold in **every reachable state** (a sanity check).

**7. Property / Specification Block**
- `SPEC` — simple propositional invariant.
- `LTLSPEC` — Linear Temporal Logic formula (state path semantics).
- `CTLSPEC` — Computation Tree Logic formula (branching-time semantics).
- `JUSTICE` / `COMPASSION` — fairness constraints.

### 2.2 Why the Language Looks the Way It Does — The "Why"

- **Synchronous, lock-step composition** mirrors **clocked hardware** (digital circuits), which is why this language dominated hardware verification.
- **Bounded finite types** are mandatory because **finite-state model checking** is the verification engine. Unbounded integers cause termination failure.
- **Separation of `INIT` and `TRANS`/`ASSIGN`** lets the checker construct the **Kripke structure** $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ explicitly, where $S_0 \subseteq S$ is computed from `INIT` and $R \subseteq S \times S$ is computed from `TRANS`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking the markdown table. All formulas are mathematically verifiable.

| Symbol / Keyword | Meaning | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ | Kripke structure | Theoretical |
| $\lvert S \rvert = 2^{n_b} \cdot \prod (u_i+1)$ | State-space size (Boolean $n_b$, bounded integer $u_i$) | Complexity |
| $S_0 = \{s \in S \mid \text{INIT}(s) = \text{TRUE}\}$ | Initial-state set | Set-builder |
| $R(s, s') = \text{ASSIGN}/\text{TRANS}(s, s') = \text{TRUE}$ | Transition relation | Predicate |
| $E[ \varphi \, U \, \psi ]$ | CTL *exists-until* | Temporal |
| $A[ \varphi \, U \, \psi ]$ | CTL *all-until* | Temporal |
| $G \, p$ | LTL/CTL *globally* (always) | Temporal |
| $F \, p$ | LTL/CTL *eventually* | Temporal |
| $X \, p$ | LTL/CTL *next* | Temporal |
| `:=` | Deterministic next-state assignment | Syntax |
| `:= {v1, v2, v3}` | Non-deterministic assignment | Syntax |
| `next(var)` | Reference to next-state value of `var` | Syntax |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hardware verification** — Intel, AMD, IBM, and Cadence routinely verify register-transfer-level (RTL) designs via SMV-style modules.
- **Protocol verification** — cache-coherence protocols (e.g., MESI, German's protocol).
- **Embedded / safety-critical software** — Airbus and Boeing verify cockpit-software state machines.
- **Communication protocols** — Needham-Schroeder, Kerberos, IEEE 1394 (FireWire).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — State-Space Size of a Module

**Problem (typical 7-mark KTU sub-question):** *A module declares three Boolean variables and two bounded integers, each ranging over $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. Compute the worst-case state-space size.*

**Step 1 — Identify variable types and domains.**
- Boolean variables: 3, each with domain size $= 2$.
- Bounded integer variables: 2, each with domain size $= 6$ (values 0 through 5).

**Step 2 — Recall the Cartesian-product formula for the state space.**

$$
\lvert S \rvert \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} \lvert D_i \rvert
$$

where $D_i$ is the domain of the $i$-th state variable.

**Step 3 — Substitute the domain sizes.**

$$
\lvert S \rvert \;=\; 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 6 \times 6
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the product step by step.**

$$
2 \times 2 = 4
$$

$$
4 \times 2 = 8
$$

$$
8 \times 6 = 48
$$

$$
48 \times 6 = 288
$$

**Step 5 — Express in the cleaner closed form.** Using the Boolean $n_b=3$ and integer-bound $u_i = 5$ for $k=2$ integers:

$$
\lvert S \rvert \;=\; 2^{n_b} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{k} (u_i + 1) \;=\; 2^{3} \cdot (5+1)^{2} \;=\; 8 \cdot 36 \;=\; 288 \text{ states}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** Even with only **five** variables, the model checker must explore up to **288** states; in real designs with hundreds of variables this becomes $2^{100+}$ — the *state-explosion problem* that motivates symbolic (BDD-based) model checking.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Keyword-to-Translation Table

| Module Keyword | Reads as | Affects which Kripke component? |
|---|---|---|
| `MODULE m` | Define a module named `m` | System component (hierarchical) |
| `VAR x : boolean;` | State variable `x` (Boolean) | $S$ (states) |
| `IVAR req : boolean;` | Environment input `req` | $S$ (input dimension) |
| `FROZENVAR N : 1..8;` | Constant `N` chosen at init | $S$ (parameter) |
| `DEFINE busy := x & req;` | Macro expansion | No direct Kripke effect |
| `INIT x = FALSE` | Initial-state predicate | $S_0$ |
| `ASSIGN next(x) := x xor req;` | Transition (deterministic) | $R$ |
| `ASSIGN next(y) := {0, 1, 2};` | Transition (non-deterministic) | $R$ |
| `TRANS (x -> next(x) = !x)` | Transition (predicate form) | $R$ |
| `INVAR !x \vert !req` (using `\vert`) | State invariant | Sanity check on $S$ |
| `SPEC AG ! (x & y)` | CTL safety property | $M \models \text{SPEC}?$ |
| `LTLSPEC G (req -> F x)` | LTL liveness property | $M \models \text{LTLSPEC}?$ |
| `JUSTICE req` | Fairness on `req` | Constraint on $R$ |

### 3.3 Worked Canonical Example — A 1-bit Toggle

Below is the canonical "toggle with enable" module, used in almost every KTU lecture slide:

```
MODULE main
  VAR
    bit   : boolean;
    en    : boolean;
  ASSIGN
    init(bit) := FALSE;
    next(bit) := en ? !bit : bit;
  LTLSPEC
    G (en -> F (bit != bit))
```

**Translation to Kripke components (Step-by-step):**

- $S = \{(0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (1,1)\}$ (tuples of `(bit, en)`).
- $S_0 = \{(0,0), (0,1)\}$ (initial `bit = FALSE`, `en` arbitrary).
- $R$: if `en = TRUE` then `bit` flips; if `en = FALSE` then `bit` holds.
- The `LTLSPEC` says: *globally, whenever `en` is true, eventually `bit` will be different from its current value* — i.e., a request to toggle is eventually honoured.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Module Language Program

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MODULE main] --> B[Variable Declarations]
    A --> C[DEFINE Macros]
    A --> D[INIT Block]
    A --> E[ASSIGN or TRANS Block]
    A --> F[INVAR Block]
    A --> G[SPEC Block]
    G --> G1[LTLSPEC]
    G --> G2[CTLSPEC]
    A --> H[Fairness JUSTICE COMPASSION]
    B --> B1[VAR state vars]
    B --> B2[IVAR inputs]
    B --> B3[FROZENVAR constants]
    D --> I[Kripke Structure M = S S0 R L]
    E --> I
    B1 --> I
    B2 --> I
    I --> J[Symbolic Model Checker NuSMV]
    I --> J
    G1 --> J
    G2 --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Verification Result TRUE or FALSE plus counterexample]
```

### 4.2 Data-Flow from Module to Kripke Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Module Language Source] --> Q[Lexer Parser]
    Q --> R[AST]
    R --> S1[INIT predicate]
    R --> S2[TRANS ASSIGN]
    R --> S3[VAR declarations]
    S1 --> T[Kripke M]
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
    T --> U[Boolean Encoding BDD]
    U --> V[Model Checking Algorithm CTL or LTL]
    V --> W[Output verdict plus counterexample trace]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Module Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MAIN[MODULE main] --> M1[MODULE counter]
    MAIN --> M2[MODULE arbiter]
    M1 --> M1A[VAR count boolean]
    M1 --> M1B[ASSIGN next count]
    M2 --> M2A[VAR grant boolean]
    M2 --> M2B[TRANS predicate]
    M1A --> R[Kripke M counter]
    M1B --> R
    M2A --> S[Kripke M arbiter]
    M2B --> S
    R --> P[Parallel Composition M system]
    S --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **synchronous** composition (the SMV/NuSMV default), the global transition relation is the **conjunction** of all module transitions; in **asynchronous** composition (introduced in Module 5 of PECNT526), only one module transitions at a time.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the term *Module Language* as used in symbolic model checking. List its main building blocks.

> **Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
> The **Module Language** is a finite-state declarative specification language used as the input to symbolic model checkers (e.g., NuSMV) for describing the system under verification. Its main building blocks are:
> 1. **Module declaration** — `MODULE name`.
> 2. **Variable declarations** — `VAR`, `IVAR`, `FROZENVAR`.
> 3. **Macro definitions** — `DEFINE`.
> 4. **Initialisation** — `INIT`.
> 5. **Transition relation** — `ASSIGN` / `TRANS`.
> 6. **Invariants** — `INVAR`.
> 7. **Property specifications** — `SPEC`, `LTLSPEC`, `CTLSPEC`.
> 8. **Fairness** — `JUSTICE`, `COMPASSION`.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between `VAR`, `IVAR`, and `FROZENVAR` in the Module Language.

> **Model Answer (for 3 marks):**
>
> | Keyword | Mutability | Role in the system |
> |---|---|---|
> | `VAR` | Mutable state variable, changes every transition | Models system memory / registers |
> | `IVAR` | Mutable, but treated as non-deterministic environment input | Models uncontrollable inputs |
> | `FROZENVAR` | Assigned once at initialisation, never changes | Models design parameters (e.g., number of processes `N`) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain, with a neat block diagram, the architecture of a Module Language program and show how each block maps to a Kripke-structure component $(S, S_0, R, L)$.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Consider a module `main` that declares two Boolean variables `x` and `y` and one integer variable `z` bounded over $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$. Compute the worst-case state-space size. Justify each step.

**Model Solution (a):**

The Module Language program is organised into **seven** logical blocks. The mapping to the Kripke structure is:

| Block | Maps to Kripke component |
|---|---|
| `MODULE name` | Root identifier of the model |
| `VAR`, `IVAR`, `FROZENVAR` | $S$ (set of all states) |
| `INIT` | $S_0 \subseteq S$ (initial states) |
| `ASSIGN` / `TRANS` | $R \subseteq S \times S$ (transition relation) |
| `INVAR` | Sanity predicate over $S$ |
| `SPEC` / `LTLSPEC` / `CTLSPEC` | Formula $\varphi$ to be checked $M \models \varphi$ |
| `JUSTICE` / `COMPASSION` | Fairness constraint on infinite paths in $R$ |

The flow is:

```
MODULE ──► Variables ──► INIT ──► S0
                  └─────► ASSIGN/TRANS ──► R
                                          │
                                          ▼
                                    Kripke M = (S, S0, R, L)
                                          │
                                          ▼
                              LTLSPEC / CTLSPEC / SPEC
                                          │
                                          ▼
                                 Verdict + counterexample
```

**Model Solution (b):**

- $n_b = 2$ Boolean variables, each domain size $= 2$.
- One integer variable with bound $u = 3$ (values $0, 1, 2, 3$), domain size $= u+1 = 4$.

$$
\lvert S \rvert \;=\; 2^{n_b} \cdot (u + 1) \;=\; 2^{2} \cdot 4 \;=\; 4 \cdot 4 \;=\; 16 \text{ states}
$$

**Justification (each step worth partial credit):**
1. Cartesian product formula stated correctly — 2 marks.
2. Domain sizes identified correctly — 2 marks.
3. Substitution and arithmetic — 2 marks.
4. Final answer with units "states" — 1 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Supplementary Model, Module 4]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write a Module Language program for a 2-bit *up-counter* that has an `enable` input and a synchronous `reset`. Specify an LTL property: *whenever `enable` stays high, the counter eventually reaches its maximum value 3*.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between the `ASSIGN` block and the `TRANS` block. Why is non-determinism useful in a Module Language program?

**Model Solution (a):**

```
MODULE main
  VAR
    cnt  : 0..3;
    en   : boolean;
    rst  : boolean;
  ASSIGN
    init(cnt) := 0;
    next(cnt) :=
      case
        rst              : 0;
        !en             : cnt;
        cnt = 3         : 3;
        TRUE            : cnt + 1;
      esac;
  LTLSPEC
    G (G en -> F (cnt = 3))
```

**Explanation of each line (carries partial credit):**
- `cnt : 0..3;` — 2-bit bounded counter. (1 mark)
- `init(cnt) := 0;` — reset to 0 at initialisation. (1 mark)
- `case ... esac` — priority encoder, a standard `ASSIGN` idiom. (2 marks)
- `LTLSPEC G (G en -> F (cnt = 3))` — *globally, if `en` is high forever, then eventually the counter reaches 3*. (3 marks)

**Model Solution (b):**

| Aspect | `ASSIGN` block | `TRANS` block |
|---|---|---|
| Style | Operational (imperative `next(var) := ...`) | Declarative (relational predicate) |
| Default | Anything not assigned is non-deterministic by default | Must specify *all* legal transitions |
| Readability | Higher for small modules | Higher for highly-coupled state |
| Compiler | Builds BDD from `:=` equations | Builds BDD from relational predicate |

**Why non-determinism is useful (3 marks):**
1. **Models abstraction** — abstracts the precise environment behaviour.
2. **Models concurrency** — interleaving of parallel transitions.
3. **Enables worst-case reasoning** — verifier checks *all* possible behaviours, so a single verified run implies *every* possible run is safe.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students *most commonly* lose marks on Module Language questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `init(var)`** in the `ASSIGN` block — the checker still accepts the program, but the model is wrong, costing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up `:=` (deterministic) and `:= {...}` (non-deterministic)** — the boundary is the *set literal braces*. Examiners allocate 1 mark for spotting this distinction.
> 3. **Confusing `SPEC` (invariant) with `LTLSPEC`/`CTLSPEC`** — `SPEC name := AG p;` is propositional invariant; `LTLSPEC`/`CTLSPEC` accept full temporal formulas.
> 4. **Writing `next` outside `ASSIGN` or `TRANS`** — `next(x)` is *only* legal inside those two blocks.
> 5. **Forgetting the `case ... esac` priority** — `case` is evaluated top-down and the first matching arm wins; KTU examiners explicitly test this.
> 6. **State-space formula misuse** — using $2^n$ alone when integer variables are present; the full formula is $2^{n_b} \cdot \prod (u_i + 1)$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Module Language** is a *finite-state* declarative language; every variable must be **bounded**.
- A module program = **Module declaration + Variables + INIT + ASSIGN/TRANS + INVAR + SPECs + Fairness**.
- A module maps directly onto a **Kripke structure** $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$.
- **State-space size** = $2^{n_b} \cdot \prod (u_i + 1)$.
- `:=` is **deterministic**, `:= {...}` is **non-deterministic**, `init(var) := v` is the **initialisation** form.
- `next(var)` is legal **only** inside `ASSIGN`/`TRANS`.
- `case ... esac` is a **priority case** statement (top-down priority).
- `SPEC` ≈ simple invariant; `LTLSPEC` ≈ linear-time; `CTLSPEC` ≈ branching-time; `JUSTICE`/`COMPASSION` ≈ fairness.
- Hierarchical **synchronous composition** is the default; asynchronous composition is covered in later modules.
- The language is the *input contract* between the verification engineer and the symbolic model checker; the checker never invents variables, so the model is exactly what is written.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SAL Contexts
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** SAL Contexts

# 📘 SAL Contexts — Module 4 | Automated Verification

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **SAL (Symbolic Analysis Laboratory)** is a framework developed by **SRI International** (SRI International's Computer Science Laboratory) for the formal modeling and verification of **transition systems**. It provides a common specification language that can be analyzed by multiple backend tools such as the **Symbolic Model Checker (SMC)**, the **Infinite-state Model Checker (SAL-INF)**, the **Bounded Model Checker (SAL-BMC)**, and the **SAL-thy** theorem prover (which interfaces with the **PVS** theorem prover).

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a SAL Context

A **SAL Context** is a **modular, reusable, and parameterizable specification unit** used to structure complex transition systems. A context encapsulates a *collection of related modules* and *defines the wiring* (instantiations, parameters, renaming, and visibility) that produces a concrete closed system suitable for model checking.

Formally, a SAL Context is a **declarative container** consisting of:

$$
\text{Context} \; ::= \; \texttt{CONTEXT} \; \langle \text{formal parameters} \rangle \;
[ \texttt{INCLUDES} \;\langle \text{base modules} \rangle ] \;
[ \texttt{INSTANTIATION} \;\langle \text{module instances} \rangle ] \;
[ \texttt{ASSERTIONS} \;\langle \text{temporal claims} \rangle ] \;
\texttt{END}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Contexts are the **top-level organizational unit** in SAL. Every SAL specification that is to be model-checked must be wrapped inside a context. The context binds the abstract modules to concrete parameters and instances, producing the **closed transition system** that is fed to the verification engine.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Architectural Blueprint"

Imagine you are an **architect** designing a multi-storey apartment complex:

| Architectural Element | SAL Context Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Floor plan template (reusable) | **Base Module** (e.g., `lift: MODULE`) |
| Specific floor instance (Floor 1, Floor 2) | **Module Instantiation** (e.g., `lift1: lift`) |
| The complete building blueprint | **CONTEXT** (e.g., `building: CONTEXT`) |
| Building code / safety regulations | **ASSERTIONS** (e.g., `G (req -> F ack)`) |
| Renaming floor labels per instance | **RENAME / HIDE** clauses |

Just as an architect does not redraw every floor from scratch, a verification engineer does not rewrite every component. He **writes one module** and **instantiates it multiple times** inside a single **context**, attaching assertions that must hold over the composed system.

### 1.3 Why SAL Contexts Are Necessary

* **Compositionality:** Real systems (e.g., a network of elevators, a mutual-exclusion protocol with N processes) require composing **N identical components**. Contexts allow this without textual duplication.
* **State-Space Control:** A context can *hide* internal variables of a module instance, exposing only externally observable transitions — this reduces the **state-space explosion** burden on the model checker.
* **Parameterization:** Constants like the number of processes (`N`), buffer size (`B`), or clock values can be declared as **formal parameters** of the context.
* **Reusability:** A well-designed context can be included by other contexts, building layered specifications.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked "Why use a context?", the board expects a **4-point answer** covering modularity, instantiation, parameterization, and assertion scoping. Memorize this quartet.

### 1.4 The Five Logical Zones of a Context

A SAL Context is partitioned into exactly **five logical zones**, executed in order:

1. **Parameter Zone** — formal `int`, `real`, or `boolean` constants.
2. **Inclusion Zone** — base modules brought in via `INCLUDES`.
3. **Instantiation Zone** — concrete instances of modules.
4. **Assertion Zone** — `THEOREM`, `AXIOM`, `CLAIM`, or `LEMMA` statements.
5. **Closure Zone** — `END` keyword.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a SAL Context — Structured Walkthrough

Below is the canonical skeleton of a SAL context, annotated with the purpose of each section.

```
CONTEXT
  c1 : CONTEXT = ...
  m1 : MODULE = ...
INSTANTIATION
  inst1 : m1(...)
ASSERTIONS
  ...
END
```

#### Step-by-Step Logic of Compilation

* **Step 1 — Parameter Resolution:** The SAL parser resolves all formal parameters (e.g., `N : INTEGER`) by substituting their values (given at the command line via `sal-inf -i N=3 model.sal`).
* **Step 2 — Module Loading:** All modules listed in the `INCLUDES` section are loaded into the symbol table.
* **Step 3 — Instantiation & Unification:** For each `inst : ModuleName(args)`, the SAL tool generates a *fresh copy* of the module's variable set, prefixed by the instance name. This is the **state-space multiplication** step.
* **Step 4 — Hiding / Renaming:** The `->` and `HIDE` operators rewrite the variable namespace of an instance to enforce encapsulation.
* **Step 5 — Assertion Checking:** The CTL/LTL assertions in the `ASSERTIONS` block are checked against the composed transition system by the selected backend (e.g., `sal-smc`).

### 2.2 Operator Reference Table

| Operator / Keyword | Category | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| `CONTEXT ... END` | Container | Wraps the whole specification | ★★★★★ |
| `INCLUDES` | Composition | Brings in external `.mod` or `.sal` files | ★★★★☆ |
| `INSTANTIATION` | Composition | Creates named instances of modules | ★★★★★ |
| `RENAME` (`->`) | Encapsulation | Maps instance variables to fresh names | ★★★★☆ |
| `HIDE` | Encapsulation | Removes variables from the public interface | ★★★☆☆ |
| `AXIOM` | Property | A constraint assumed to hold (no proof) | ★★☆☆☆ |
| `THEOREM` | Property | A claim that must be model-checked | ★★★★★ |
| `CLAIM` | Property | A *named* sub-property, reusable via `CLAIM name` | ★★★☆☆ |
| `G` (Globally) | Temporal | $\forall t.\, \varphi(t)$ | ★★★★★ |
| `F` (Finally / Future) | Temporal | $\exists t.\, \varphi(t)$ | ★★★★★ |
| `X` (Next) | Temporal | $\varphi$ at next step | ★★★☆☆ |
| `U` (Until) | Temporal | $\varphi\, \mathcal{U}\, \psi$ | ★★★★☆ |
| `[]` (Box) | State | Equivalent to `G` | ★★★☆☆ |
| `<>` (Diamond) | State | Equivalent to `F` | ★★★☆☆ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not use the bare `|` (vertical bar) inside markdown table cells.** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for any absolute-value or divider notation in formulas. Example: $x \mid y$ in a row would break the table — prefer $x \div y$ or $x \mid y$ written in a code span.

### 2.3 Mathematical Foundations Underlying Contexts

A context realizes the **synchronous product** of its instantiated transition systems. If module $M_i$ has the transition relation $\rightarrow_{M_i}$, then the context's transition relation is:

$$
\rightarrow_{C} \;=\; \bigotimes_{i=1}^{k} \rightarrow_{M_i} \;\cap\; \text{synchronization constraints}
$$

For $k$ instances of an identical module, the **state-space cardinality** grows as:

$$
\vert S_C \vert \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{k} \vert S_{M_i} \vert
$$

For $k$ identical modules each with $n$ states:

$$
\vert S_C \vert \;=\; n^k
$$

This **exponential blow-up** is precisely the **state-space explosion** problem that motivates the use of *symbolic* (BDD-based) model checkers like `sal-smc`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Railway Interlocking Systems:** Each track-side controller is a SAL module; the interlocking logic is a context instantiating N controllers with `THEOREM` blocks asserting "no two trains on the same track segment."
* **Cryptographic Protocol Verification:** The Needham-Schroeder protocol has been verified using SAL contexts with 2, 3, and 4 agent instances.
* **Air-Traffic Control:** Multiple aircraft modules composed under a context that asserts `G (controller.request -> F controller.grant)` with bounded response time.
* **Embedded Real-Time Systems:** Parameterized contexts let engineers verify an arbitration protocol for *any* number of bus masters.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: State-Space Size of a Context with $k$ Identical Instances

Let a module $M$ have $\vert S_M \vert = n$ reachable states. A context $C$ instantiates $M$ exactly $k$ times with no synchronization constraints (asynchronous composition). We derive the number of reachable states of $C$.

**Step 1 — Single-module state count.**
