The module $M$ has $n$ states, written as:

$$
\vert S_M \vert = n
$$

**Step 2 — Independent composition of two instances.**
For two instances, the joint state is the Cartesian product of the individual state sets:

$$
\vert S_{M_1 \otimes M_2} \vert = \vert S_{M_1} \vert \cdot \vert S_{M_2} \vert = n \cdot n = n^2
$$

**Step 3 — Generalization to $k$ instances.**
By induction, the joint state space is the $k$-fold product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert S_{C_k} \vert &= \vert S_{C_{k-1}} \vert \cdot \vert S_{M_k} \vert \\
&= n^{k-1} \cdot n \\
&= n^k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Synchronous product (worst case).**
If the instances are tightly synchronized (every transition occurs simultaneously), the reachable states may be strictly fewer than $n^k$ because of the synchronization constraints. However, the **upper bound** remains:

$$
\vert S_{C_k} \vert \;\leq\; n^k
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numeric example.**
Let $n = 10$ states per process and $k = 4$ processes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert S_{C_4} \vert &= 10^4 \\
&= 10{,}000 \text{ states}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is tractable. But for $n = 50$ and $k = 6$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert S_{C_6} \vert &= 50^6 \\
&= 15{,}625{,}000{,}000 \text{ states}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **15.6 billion states** — clearly infeasible for explicit-state model checking, motivating **symbolic BDD-based** checking.

### 3.2 Derivation: BDD Size Growth for a Counter Module

A counter module `C : MODULE` has a single integer variable `x : INTEGER`. The number of distinct BDD nodes required to encode $x$ for $b$ bits is:

$$
\boxed{\;N_{\text{BDD}}(b) \;\leq\; b \cdot c\;}
$$

where $c$ is a small constant (typically 2–4). The variable ordering has a dramatic effect: a good ordering yields a BDD of size $O(b)$, while a bad ordering can yield $O(2^b)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in SAL Contexts:** A context with $k$ counter instances effectively has $k$ variables $x_1, \ldots, x_k$. If the BDD variable order interleaves $x_i$ bits, the BDD size remains manageable; if not, it explodes. The `sal-smc` tool provides heuristics for variable ordering, but the *specification* (i.e., the context) determines the structure.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Context-Element Decision Table

| If the system has … | Then declare … | Then use … | Example Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ identical processes | A module + $N$ instances | `INSTANTIATION proc1: P, ..., procN: P` | `G (mutex)` |
| A single shared resource | A `Resource` module | `INCLUDES` or `INSTANTIATION` | `G (granted -> X !granted \/ granted)` |
| Parameterized count | `n : INTEGER` in context | Pass to module: `proc(i: n): P` | `THEOREM forall(x: n) => G(...)` |
| Hidden internal state | Variables private to module | `HIDE x IN inst` | (assertions see only `inst.x`) |
| Renamed public interface | `RENAME x IN inst -> y` | `y` becomes the public name | Reference `inst.y` in theorems |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Anatomy of a SAL Context

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: CONTEXT keyword"]
    P["Parameter Zone\nn : INTEGER, B : BOOLEAN"]
    I["INCLUDES Zone\nBase modules loaded"]
    S["INSTANTIATION Zone\nproc1 : P, proc2 : P, ..."]
    R["RENAME / HIDE Zone\nEncapsulation of internals"]
    AS["ASSERTIONS Zone\nTHEOREM, AXIOM, CLAIM"]
    E["End: END keyword"]

    A --> P
    P --> I
    I --> S
    S --> R
    R --> AS
    AS --> E

    P -. "Formal parameters\nfed from CLI" .-> S
    I -. "Symbol table\nresolution" .-> S
    S -. "State-space\nproduct S^n^k" .-> AS
    R -. "Encapsulation" .-> AS
    AS -. "Passed to\nsal-smc / sal-inf" .-> E
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Process Mutual Exclusion Context

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["proc1 : Process\nLocal: state, crit"]
    P2["proc2 : Process\nLocal: state, crit"]
    P3["proc3 : Process\nLocal: state, crit"]
    R["Resource : Arbiter\nGlobal: grant_1, grant_2, grant_3"]

    P1 <-->|shared grant| R
    P2 <-->|shared grant| R
    P3 <-->|shared grant| R

    P1 -. "G not crit1 and crit2" .-> ASSERT["ASSERTIONS\nTHEOREM mutex"]
    P2 -. "G not crit2 and crit3" .-> ASSERT
    P3 -. "G not crit3 and crit1" .-> ASSERT
    R --> ASSERT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each `proc_i` is an **instance** of the same `Process` module, encapsulated by the `Resource` (arbiter) module. The `ASSERTIONS` node collects the **mutual-exclusion theorem** that the SAL model checker will attempt to prove.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: SAL Tool Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["User writes\ncontext .sal file"]
    PARSE["SAL Parser\nLex + Yacc"]
    AST["Abstract Syntax Tree"]
    TYPE["Type Checker\nResolve INT/REAL/BOOL"]
    BDD["BDD Manager\nsal-smc backend"]
    MC["Model Checker\nCTL/LTL Eval"]
    RES["Result: PROVED /\nCOUNTER-EXAMPLE"]

    SRC --> PARSE --> AST --> TYPE --> BDD --> MC --> RES

    ALT["Alternative Backend\nsal-bmc / sal-inf / sal-thy"]
    TYPE --> ALT
    ALT --> MC
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Context Composition Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Stack1["Composition Style A: Instantiation"]
        BA["Base: M : MODULE"]
        CA["inst1 : M, inst2 : M"]
    end
    subgraph Stack2["Composition Style B: Inclusion"]
        BB["Base: M : MODULE\n(in separate file)"]
        CB["INCLUDES M"]
    end
    subgraph Stack3["Composition Style C: Nested Context"]
        BC["Inner CONTEXT\nsubctx : CONTEXT"]
        CC["Outer: subctx(params)"]
    end

    BA --> CA
    BB --> CB
    BC --> CC

    CA --> MERGE["Produces single\nclosed transition system"]
    CB --> MERGE
    CC --> MERGE
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns. The mark distribution is split as **3-mark (Part A)** and **14-mark (Part B)** with internal choice, exactly as per the official regulation.

### 📝 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

#### **Q1. Define a SAL Context. List its three primary functional roles.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **SAL Context** is a top-level declarative container in the SAL specification language that aggregates parameters, included modules, module instantiations, and temporal assertions into a single closed specification suitable for model checking.

Its three primary roles are:
1. **Modular composition** — bringing together multiple base modules into one system.
2. **Parameterization** — accepting formal parameters (e.g., the number of processes `n`) that can be instantiated at analysis time.
3. **Assertion scoping** — declaring the theorems/claims that the model checker must verify over the composed system.

> [!TIP]
> For full marks, the examiner expects **all three roles** plus a *one-sentence* definition. Do not exceed 80 words.

#### **Q2. Distinguish between `RENAME` and `HIDE` in a SAL Context.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `RENAME (->)` | `HIDE` |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Maps an instance's internal variable to a *new public name* | Removes a variable from the *public* observable interface |
| Visibility | Variable **remains** accessible, but under the new name | Variable **becomes inaccessible** outside the instance |
| Use case | Disambiguating variables when including the same module twice | Encapsulating private state for state-space reduction |

---

### 📝 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU Standard):** You must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each choice is divided into part (a) worth 7 marks and part (b) worth 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a clearly annotated SAL example, explain the **five logical zones** of a context. State one practical reason for each zone. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 1-bit sender and 1-bit receiver communicating over a lossy channel. Model this using:
   * a `Sender` module,
   * a `Channel` module, and
   * a `Receiver` module,
   inside a single context `CommProtocol`. Write the **THEOREM** that "every sent bit is eventually delivered" using LTL. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — The Five Zones (7 marks):**

A SAL context has **five zones**, each serving a specific purpose.

1. **Parameter Zone** — declares formal parameters such as `n : INTEGER`. *Purpose:* allows the *same* context to be reused for different system sizes (1 process, 5 processes, etc.).
2. **INCLUDES Zone** — references external modules. *Purpose:* promotes code reuse; a `Counter` module can be included by many contexts.
3. **INSTANTIATION Zone** — creates concrete instances. *Purpose:* multiplies a base module's behaviour $k$ times without code duplication.
4. **RENAME / HIDE Zone** — controls variable visibility. *Purpose:* prevents name clashes and reduces the public state space for the model checker.
5. **ASSERTIONS Zone** — declares `THEOREM` and `AXIOM` blocks. *Purpose:* defines the *contract* the composed system must satisfy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often list only 4 zones, merging RENAME/HIDE with INSTANTIATION. That costs **1 mark**. Always list all five distinctly.

**(b) — The Communication Protocol (7 marks):**

SAL sketch:

```
Sender: MODULE
  INPUT  ack : BOOLEAN
  OUTPUT bit : BOOLEAN
  INITIALIZATION bit = FALSE
  TRANSITION
    bit' IN {TRUE, FALSE};
  END;

Channel: MODULE
  INPUT  sbit : BOOLEAN
  OUTPUT rbit : BOOLEAN
  TRANSITION
    rbit' = sbit OR (rbit' IN {TRUE, FALSE});  %% lossy
  END;

Receiver: MODULE
  INPUT  rbit : BOOLEAN
  OUTPUT ack  : BOOLEAN
  TRANSITION
    ack' = rbit;  %% echo back
  END;

CommProtocol: CONTEXT
  s : MODULE = Sender;
  c : MODULE = Channel;
  r : MODULE = Receiver;
INSTANTIATION
  sender  : s;
  channel : c(s.bit);
  receiver: r(channel.rbit);
ASSERTIONS
  THEOREM eventuallyDelivered : G (sender.bit -> F receiver.ack);
END
```

**Explanation of THEOREM:**

The LTL formula $G(\text{sender.bit} \rightarrow F\,\text{receiver.ack})$ reads as:

> *"Globally, whenever a bit is sent, it is **eventually acknowledged** by the receiver."*

The `G` operator ranges over all time instants, and the `F` operator asserts existence of a future time. The model checker will either **prove** the formula or return a **counter-example trace** showing when a bit was lost.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between **synchronous** and **asynchronous** composition of two SAL modules. Show with a state-transition diagram how the joint state space grows. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A traffic-light controller has three lights (Red, Yellow, Green). Model it as a SAL context with one `Light` module instantiated three times. Write the safety theorem: *"Red and Green are never simultaneously ON"*. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Sync vs. Async Composition (7 marks):**

* **Synchronous composition** means every component transitions in lockstep on every global step. The joint transition relation is:
$$
(s_1, s_2) \rightarrow_{sync} (s_1', s_2') \iff s_1 \rightarrow s_1' \;\land\; s_2 \rightarrow s_2'
$$
The number of joint states remains $\vert S_1 \vert \cdot \vert S_2 \vert$ (assuming the components are *independent*), but the reachable states may be fewer due to coupling.

* **Asynchronous composition** allows one component to transition while the other stays. The transition relation is:
$$
(s_1, s_2) \rightarrow_{async} (s_1', s_2) \;\lor\; (s_1, s_2) \rightarrow_{async} (s_1, s_2')
$$
This produces a **larger** reachable state graph because interleavings are added.

**Diagram — Growth Pattern:**

```
Module A:  s0 ──► s1
Module B:  t0 ──► t1

Synchronous joint states:  (s0,t0) → (s1,t1)        -- 2 reachable
Asynchronous joint states: (s0,t0) → (s1,t0) → (s1,t1)
                           (s0,t0) → (s0,t1) → (s1,t1)  -- 4 reachable
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students write *asynchronous* when they mean *synchronous*. Remember — *async = interleaving*; *sync = simultaneous*.

**(b) — Traffic-Light Context (7 marks):**

```
Light: MODULE
  OUTPUT color : {RED, YELLOW, GREEN}
  INITIALIZATION color = RED
  TRANSITION
    color' =
      IF color = RED    THEN YELLOW
      ELSE IF color = YELLOW THEN GREEN
      ELSE RED ENDIF ENDIF;
  END;

TrafficSystem: CONTEXT
INSTANTIATION
  northLight  : Light;
  eastLight   : Light;
  southLight  : Light;
ASSERTIONS
  THEOREM safety :
    G NOT (northLight.color = GREEN AND
          (eastLight.color = GREEN OR
           southLight.color = GREEN));
END
```

**Justification of THEOREM:**
The expression $\text{northLight.color} = \text{GREEN} \,\land\, (\text{eastLight.color} = \text{GREEN} \lor \text{southLight.color} = \text{GREEN})$ is enclosed within `G NOT` to express the invariant: *"At no time are conflicting green lights active."* The model checker will search for any reachable state where two greens co-exist.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write the *negation* incorrectly as `G (north = GREEN -> east != GREEN)`. While logically equivalent in this case, the canonical form expected by the board is `G NOT (conflict)`. Using the `NOT` form is the safer answer.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final-day revision checklist — SAL Contexts:**

* ✅ A **SAL Context** is the *outermost* specification unit; every checked model must be wrapped in one.
* ✅ The **five zones**: Parameter → INCLUDES → INSTANTIATION → RENAME/HIDE → ASSERTIONS.
* ✅ A context produces a **closed transition system** suitable for `sal-smc`, `sal-bmc`, `sal-inf`, or `sal-thy`.
* ✅ `INSTANTIATION` is the **state-space multiplication** step — it grows the BDD as $n^k$ for $k$ identical instances.
* ✅ `HIDE` removes variables from the public interface; `RENAME` gives them a new public name.
* ✅ The **THEOREM** keyword forces the model checker to verify a temporal claim; **AXIOM** is assumed without proof.
* ✅ Common LTL operators to memorize: $G$ (globally), $F$ (finally), $X$ (next), $U$ (until), $\square$ (box = G), $\Diamond$ (diamond = F).
* ✅ Always **list all five zones** distinctly in a "define and list" question — merging zones costs a mark.
* ✅ In traffic-light / mutual-exclusion theorems, prefer the `G NOT (conflict)` form to the implication form.
* ✅ Remember the upper-bound state-space formula: $\vert S_C \vert \le n^k$ — this is the exponential blow-up that motivates symbolic checking.
* ✅ **Examination tip:** A 14-mark "explain with example" question on contexts always expects a *small* working SAL snippet — practice writing one with at least one `MODULE`, one `INSTANTIATION`, and one `THEOREM`.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SAL Examples - Mutual Exclusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** SAL Examples - Mutual Exclusion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is SAL? **SAL (Symbolic Analysis Laboratory)** is an open-source framework developed by **SRI International** (Computer Science Laboratory) for the *symbolic* analysis and verification of **finite-state** and **infinite-state** transition systems. It bundles together several model checkers, theorem provers, and a common modeling language. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 4, SAL is studied as a *concrete model checker* used to verify safety and liveness properties of concurrent programs, with the **Mutual Exclusion** problem serving as the canonical pedagogical example. SAL is particularly significant because it supports: - **Symbolic Model Checking** using **BDDs (Binary Decision Diagrams)** via the `sal-smc` tool. - **Bounded Model Checking (BMC)** using SAT solvers via `sal-bmc` and `sal-inf-bmc`. - **Induction-based** and *invariant generation* proofs via `sal-inf-bmc` and `PVS`. - A common, expressive, *guarded-command* modeling language. ## 1.2 What is Mutual Exclusion? **Mutual Exclusion** is a fundamental **concurrency property** that guarantees that **no two processes can be inside their Critical Section (CS)** at the same instant. Each concurrent process is structured as a sequence of four logical regions: $$ \text{NCS} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Try} \;\rightarrow\; \text{CS} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Exit} \;\rightarrow\; \text{NCS} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Terminology):** A concurrent system composed of $n$ processes $\{P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n\}$ satisfies mutual exclusion if and only if for all reachable global states $s$, > $$\forall\, i \neq j : \neg\bigl(\text{inCS}_i(s) \wedge \text{inCS}_j(s)\bigr)$$ > where $\text{inCS}_k(s)$ is the predicate asserting that process $P_k$ is executing inside its critical section in state $s$. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Single Bathroom" Intuition Imagine a house with **one washroom** but **three roommates**. The bathroom is a *shared resource* that cannot tolerate simultaneous occupancy. The roommates (processes) use a *whiteboard protocol* (a shared variable like `turn` or array of `flag[]`) to coordinate who enters next. The fundamental rule, **mutual exclusion**, simply means: *"At any given moment, at most one roommate can be inside the bathroom."* In SAL terms: - The **bathroom** is the *Critical Section*. - The **whiteboard** is a *shared global variable* (e.g., `turn`). - The **behavioral contract** — "no two inside, but eventually everyone gets in" — is the *property to be model-checked*. ## 1.4 Why Verify Mutual Exclusion? Hand-written proofs of mutual exclusion (e.g., for Peterson's, Dekker's, or Lamport's Bakery algorithm) are notoriously error-prone, especially under: - **Weak memory models** (out-of-order execution). - **Compiler reordering** of statements. - **Arbitrary process interleaving**. SAL provides an *automated, exhaustive* verification of these algorithms by exploring **all** reachable states, eliminating human reasoning gaps. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for Exams:** Mutual exclusion is a **safety property** ("a bad state is never reached"), while *starvation-freedom* ("every request is eventually granted") is a **liveness property**. SAL is typically used to discharge both, and your exam answers should clearly *classify* which type of property is being proved. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Architecture of a SAL Specification for Mutual Exclusion A SAL model for mutual exclusion is organized into three logical layers: ### Layer 1 — The **Context** (Type Declarations) Declares the *base types* (e.g., `BOOLEAN`, `NATURAL`) and *global constants* (e.g., the number of processes $N = 2$). ### Layer 2 — The **Modules** (Process Models) Each process $P_i$ is defined as a *guarded transition system* of the form: - A set of *input* variables (read by $P_i$ but written by others), - A set of *output/local* variables (written by $P_i$), - An `INITIALIZATION` clause, - A `TRANSITION` clause with one or more *guarded commands*. A guarded command has the form: $$ [\,\text{name}:\; G \;\Longrightarrow\; v_1' = E_1 \;\wedge\; v_2' = E_2 \;\wedge\; \cdots\,] $$ where $G$ is the *guard predicate* and the right-hand side is the *next-state assignment*. A command is **enabled** in a state $s$ if $G$ holds in $s$. ### Layer 3 — The **System Composition** (Synchronous/Asynchronous Parallelism) The composed system is either: - **Synchronous** (`P1 || P2`): all enabled transitions fire simultaneously, or - **Asynchronous** (`P1 [] P2`): only one enabled transition fires at a time. For mutual exclusion, the *asynchronous* semantics is the standard choice, modeling *true concurrency*. ## 2.2 The Two-Process Mutex: Logical State Machine Each process $P_i$ has the following operational semantics: | State Symbol | Meaning | Guard Conditions | |:---:|:---|:---| | $\text{NCS}_i$ | Non-Critical Section | No constraint; default rest state | | $\text{Try}_i$ | Trying to enter CS | $P_i$ is *requesting* access | | $\text{CS}_i$ | Critical Section | $P_i$ is *executing* the protected code | | $\text{Exit}_i$ | Leaving the CS | $P_i$ is *releasing* access | The transitions must satisfy the SAL "always enabled" rule: in *every* state, at least one outgoing transition must be enabled, so the model cannot deadlock unexpectedly. ## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula & Property Sheet** — Memorize this table; it appears almost verbatim in KTU university exam questions. | # | Construct / Property | Symbolic Form in SAL / CTL | Category | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | Global variable declaration | `GLOBAL g : BOOLEAN` | Modeling | | 2 | Asynchronous composition | `SYSTEM = P1 [] P2` | Modeling | | 3 | Synchronous composition | `SYSTEM = P1 || P2` | Modeling | | 4 | Safety: Mutual Exclusion | `AG NOT (cs1 AND cs2)` | **Safety** | | 5 | Liveness: No Starvation for $P_1$ | `AG (try1 => AF cs1)` | **Liveness** | | 6 | Invariant: $\text{turn} \in \{0,1\}$ | `AG (turn = 0 OR turn = 1)` | **Type-Safety** | | 7 | Reachable state count | $\vert S \vert = \sum_{i} \vert S_i \vert$ (worst-case) | Metric | | 8 | Initial state predicate | $I(s) \equiv s(g) = \text{FALSE}$ | Logic | | 9 | Transition relation | $R(s,s') \equiv \bigvee_k G_k(s) \wedge s' = \delta_k(s)$ | Logic | | 10 | Fixed-point for `AG` | $\text{AG}\,\phi \equiv \mu Z.\; \phi \wedge \text{AX}\,Z$ | $\mu$-Calculus | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Mutual-exclusion verification via SAL is applied in: - **Embedded Operating Systems** (RTEMS, FreeRTOS) for kernel mutex proofs. - **Aerospace Control Software** (NASA uses symbolic model checkers for flight-control mutex). - **Multi-core Processor Cache Coherency Protocols** (MESI, MOESI). - **Hardware Bus Arbitration** logic in System-on-Chip (SoC) designs. - **Automotive ECUs** for shared-sensor arbitration in CAN-bus stacks. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Tip:** In industry, SAL-style symbolic model checking is frequently used in the **DO-178C** (avionics) and **ISO 26262** (automotive) certification processes, where formal proofs of safety properties are legally required. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete SAL Model — A 2-Process Mutex (Turn-Based Protocol) We now derive, line-by-line, a SAL model for the simplest correct mutex: a **turn-based** protocol. Two processes alternate using a shared variable `turn`. $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Algorithm (informal):}\quad &\textbf{Process } P_i \text{ (for } i \in \{1, 2\})\\ &1.\; \text{Loop: } \text{NCS}\\ &2.\; \text{while } \text{turn} \neq i : \text{skip} \quad \text{(spinning)}\\ &3.\; \text{CS}_i\\ &4.\; \text{turn} \leftarrow 3 - i \quad \text{(yield to the other)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step A — Context Declaration We declare the *base type* and the *shared global* variable. ``` MUTEX: CONTEXT = BEGIN GLOBAL turn : NATURAL END ``` *Derivation Note:* The variable `turn` is declared `GLOBAL` so it is shared across all modules. Its type `NATURAL` must be bounded; SAL's `sal-smc` requires finite domains, so we implicitly restrict $\text{turn} \in \{1, 2\}$. ### Step B — Module for Process $P_1$ ``` P1: MODULE = BEGIN LOCAL flag1 : BOOLEAN INITIALIZATION flag1 = FALSE TRANSITION [ request1 : flag1' = TRUE ] [ enter1 : flag1 = TRUE AND turn = 1 --> flag1' = FALSE ] [ release1 : flag1 = FALSE --> skip ] END ``` *Derivation Note:* - `flag1` is *local* to $P_1$ (it cannot be read or written by $P_2$). - The `enter1` command has the guard `flag1 = TRUE AND turn = 1` — this is the precise logical condition under which $P_1$ may enter its CS. - The `-->` separates the guard from the next-state assignment (SAL guarded-command syntax). ### Step C — Module for Process $P_2$ (Symmetric) ``` P2: MODULE = BEGIN LOCAL flag2 : BOOLEAN INITIALIZATION flag2 = FALSE TRANSITION [ request2 : flag2' = TRUE ] [ enter2 : flag2 = TRUE AND turn = 2 --> flag2' = FALSE ] [ release2 : flag2 = FALSE --> skip ] END ``` ### Step D — System Composition (Asynchronous) ``` SYSTEM: MODULE = P1 [] P2 ``` *Derivation Note:* The `[]` operator denotes *asynchronous* interleaving. In every step of the global transition, **exactly one** of the two processes executes *one* of its enabled commands. This is the standard model for *true concurrent execution* on a single processor and corresponds to the interleaving semantics of Kripke structures used in CTL. ### Step E — Initialization of the Global State ``` % Initial condition MUTEX_init: THEOREM SYSTEM |- AG (turn = 1 OR turn = 2) ``` *Derivation Note:* This `THEOREM` is a *type-invariant*: at all reachable states, `turn` is either 1 or 2. SAL's `sal-smc` verifies it automatically by fixed-point computation: $$ \text{AG}\,\phi \;=\; \mu Z.\; \phi \wedge \text{AX}\,Z $$ The fixed-point is computed over the *symbolic* state space encoded as a BDD. ## 3.2 Safety Property — Mutual Exclusion The central verification goal is: ``` MUTEX_safe: THEOREM SYSTEM |- AG (NOT (P1.flag1 = FALSE AND P2.flag2 = FALSE AND P1.cs = TRUE AND P2.cs = TRUE)) ``` ### Step-by-Step Proof Sketch via Fixed-Point $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } \phi &\equiv \neg(\text{cs}_1 \wedge \text{cs}_2) \\ \text{Basis:}\quad Z_0(s) &= \text{True} \quad \text{(over-approximation)} \\ \text{Iteration:}\quad Z_{k+1}(s) &= \phi(s) \;\wedge\; \forall s'.\, R(s,s') \Rightarrow Z_k(s') \\ \text{Limit:}\quad Z^*(s) &= \bigwedge_{k \geq 0} Z_k(s) \\ \text{Result:}\quad \text{AG}\,\phi &= Z^* \end{aligned} $$ *Conversion Logic:* - $Z_0$ is the trivial over-approximation `True`. - $Z_1$ removes states where $\phi$ is false, *and* all their predecessors. - $Z_2$ removes the new predecessors created by $Z_1$, and so on. - Because the state space is *finite*, the chain stabilizes at some $Z^* = Z_{k^*} = Z_{k^*+1}$, and $Z^* \equiv \text{AG}\,\phi$. If the initial state satisfies $Z^*$ (i.e., $Z^*(\text{init}) = \text{True}$), the safety property is discharged. ## 3.3 Liveness Property — Starvation Freedom A *stronger* property, often required, is that no process starves: $$ \text{Liveness:}\quad \text{AG}\bigl(\text{try}_1 \Rightarrow \text{AF}\,\text{cs}_1\bigr) $$ *Derivation:* In CTL, $\text{AF}\,\psi$ means "on *all* paths, $\psi$ eventually holds." The full formula asserts: *globally, whenever $P_1$ is in the trying state, it will eventually reach its CS*. SAL's `sal-inf-bmc` proves this using **k-induction**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Base case:}\quad & I \Rightarrow \text{AF}\,\text{cs}_1 \quad \text{(checked via BMC up to depth } k\text{)} \\ \text{Inductive step:}\quad & I \wedge \text{AG}\,\text{try}_1 \wedge \text{AG}\,\neg\text{cs}_1 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{false} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse `EF` (Exists-Eventually) with `AF` (All-Eventually). Liveness **requires** `AF`; using `EF` would only check that *some* path leads to the CS, which is trivially true and not what is intended. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing SAL Tools | Tool | Algorithm Used | Suited For | Limitation | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | `sal-smc` | BDD-based symbolic model checking | Finite-state safety & liveness | State-space explosion | | `sal-bmc` | SAT-based bounded model checking | Finite-state bug-hunting up to depth $k$ | Cannot prove unbounded properties | | `sal-inf-bmc` | k-induction with invariant generation | Infinite-state systems (e.g., counters) | Requires manual lemma hints | | `sal-deadlock-checker` | Local cycle detection | Deadlock-freedom analysis | Cannot prove liveness | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SAL Verification Pipeline for Mutual Exclusion The following Mermaid block depicts the **end-to-end SAL workflow** for verifying the mutex specification. Each node represents a *stage* in the pipeline, and the arrows indicate the *control flow* and *feedback loops*. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Problem Specification
Mutual Exclusion Protocol] --> B[SAL Model File
mutex.sal] B --> C[Parse and Type-Check
sal-parser] C --> D{Property Type} D -- Safety AG phi --> E[sal-smc
BDD-based Model Checker] D -- Liveness AF psi --> F[sal-inf-bmc
k-Induction Engine] E --> G{Property Holds} F --> G G -- Yes --> H[Generate Proof Certificate
counterexample NONE] G -- No --> I[Produce Counterexample Trace
minimal witness] I --> J[Refine Model or Add Lemma] J --> B H --> K[Verification Report
VERIFIED] ``` *Reading the Diagram:* - The *parse* stage produces a typed AST (Abstract Syntax Tree). - The *decision* node `D` routes to either BDD-based or induction-based checking depending on the property class. - A *counterexample* is a witness trace; the loop `I -> J -> B` indicates the **Counterexample-Guided Abstraction Refinement (CEGAR)** style iterative workflow. ## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — The 2-Process Turn-Based Mutex The following diagram captures the **reachable global state space** of the 2-process turn-based protocol. Each node is a pair $(P_1, P_2)$ where $P_i \in \{\text{NCS}, \text{Try}, \text{CS}\}$. Only mutually exclusive regions are shown. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NCS_NCS : initialization NCS_NCS --> Try_NCS : P1 requests
turn = 1 NCS_NCS --> NCS_Try : P2 requests
turn = 2 Try_NCS --> CS_NCS : turn = 1 and P1 enters NCS_Try --> NCS_CS : turn = 2 and P2 enters CS_NCS --> NCS_Try : P1 releases
turn becomes 2 NCS_CS --> Try_NCS : P2 releases
turn becomes 1 Try_NCS --> Try_Try : both request
turn is fixed Try_Try --> CS_Try : P1 wins Try_Try --> Try_CS : P2 wins CS_NCS --> NCS_NCS : full release NCS_CS --> NCS_NCS : full release ``` *Reading the State Diagram:* - The states `CS_CS` is **deliberately absent**, encoding the safety property. - The transition `Try_Try` is *not* a violation — it represents a benign race that the `turn` variable resolves deterministically. ## 4.3 Property Hierarchy in SAL (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CTL[CTL Property Hierarchy] direction TB A1[State Formulae
phi] A2[Path Formulae
psi] A1 --> A3[AG phi
Invariant] A1 --> A4[AF phi
Liveness] A2 --> A5[AX phi
Next State] A2 --> A6[A U phi
Until] end subgraph APP[Applied to Mutex] direction TB B1[AG NOT cs1 AND cs2] B2[AG try1 => AF cs1] B3[AG turn IN 1,2] end A3 --> B1 A3 --> B3 A4 --> B2 ``` *Reading the Architecture:* - The left block shows the *abstract CTL taxonomy*. - The right block instantiates these generic operators on the **mutex problem**. - The mapping $\text{AG}\,\phi \to \text{AG}\,\neg(\text{cs}_1 \wedge \text{cs}_2)$ is the canonical instantiation used in KTU exam answers. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE):** Part A carries **3 marks each** (no choice). Part B carries **14 marks** with *internal choice* — attempt either the full OR-group. Every sub-question below is tagged with a KTU-style difficulty level and Bloom's cognitive domain. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Remember / Understand]` **Explain the role of SAL in the context of automated verification of concurrent systems. How does it differ from traditional testing?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** SAL is a symbolic model-checking framework developed by SRI International that uses BDDs and SAT solvers to exhaustively explore the state space of a transition system. - **(1 mark)** Unlike testing, which samples a finite number of execution traces, SAL explores **all** reachable states and verifies *temporal logic* properties (CTL/LTL) over every possible interleaving. - **(1 mark)** This makes SAL especially suitable for verifying concurrent algorithms like mutual exclusion, where a bug may appear only in a *specific* non-deterministic interleaving that testing can easily miss. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Understand]` **Differentiate between safety and liveness properties. Give one example of each from the mutual-exclusion problem.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Safety | Liveness | |:---|:---|:---| | **Meaning** | "A bad thing never happens" | "A good thing eventually happens" | | **CTL form** | $\text{AG}\,\neg(\text{cs}_1 \wedge \text{cs}_2)$ | $\text{AG}(\text{try}_1 \Rightarrow \text{AF}\,\text{cs}_1)$ | | **Mutex example** | No two processes are simultaneously in CS | Every request is eventually granted | | **Verification tool** | `sal-smc` | `sal-inf-bmc` (k-induction) | - **(1 mark)** Correct definition of safety with mutex example. - **(1 mark)** Correct definition of liveness with mutex example. - **(1 mark)** Accurate distinction in CTL form. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting to classify the property** as safety or liveness costs **at least 1 mark**. > 2. **Confusing `[]` (asynchronous) and `||` (synchronous) composition** in SAL is a recurring error — for mutex problems, the *correct* operator is **`[]`**. > 3. **Omitting the guard condition** in a SAL transition (e.g., writing `enter1: skip` without `flag1 = TRUE AND turn = 1`) will be marked as a **logical gap** (-2 marks). > 4. **Forgetting to state the tool used** (e.g., `sal-smc` vs `sal-bmc`) for a given property is a frequent KTU valuation deduction (-1 mark). --- ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Apply / Analyze]` **(a)** Construct a complete SAL model for a 2-process mutual-exclusion protocol using a shared `turn` variable. Specify the **modules**, the **initialization**, the **transitions**, and the **system composition**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** State the safety property *"no two processes are simultaneously in the critical section"* as a CTL formula. Explain, with a step-by-step fixed-point argument, how SAL's `sal-smc` verifies it. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):** ``` MUTEX: CONTEXT = BEGIN GLOBAL turn : NATURAL END P1: MODULE = BEGIN LOCAL flag1 : BOOLEAN INITIALIZATION flag1 = FALSE TRANSITION [ req1 : flag1' = TRUE ] [ ent1 : flag1 = TRUE AND turn = 1 --> flag1' = FALSE ] [ rel1 : flag1 = FALSE --> skip ] END P2: MODULE = BEGIN LOCAL flag2 : BOOLEAN INITIALIZATION flag2 = FALSE TRANSITION [ req2 : flag2' = TRUE ] [ ent2 : flag2 = TRUE AND turn = 2 --> flag2' = FALSE ] [ rel2 : flag2 = FALSE --> skip ] END SYSTEM: MODULE = P1 [] P2 ``` **Mark Allocation:** - (2 marks) Correct context and global variable declaration. - (2 marks) Correct module structure for $P_1$ and $P_2$ with valid guards. - (2 marks) Correct asynchronous composition `P1 [] P2`. - (1 mark) `INITIALIZATION` clause correctly defined. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):** **CTL Formula (2 marks):** $$ \text{MUTEX\_safe : THEOREM } \text{SYSTEM} \models \text{AG}\,\neg(\text{cs}_1 \wedge \text{cs}_2) $$ where $\text{cs}_i$ is a derived predicate true when process $P_i$ is in its critical section. **Fixed-Point Argument (5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } \phi(s) &\equiv \neg(\text{cs}_1(s) \wedge \text{cs}_2(s)) \\ Z_0(s) &= \text{True} \\ Z_{k+1}(s) &= \phi(s) \;\wedge\; \forall s'.\, R(s,s') \Rightarrow Z_k(s') \\ \text{AG}\,\phi(s) &= \mu Z.\; \phi(s) \wedge \text{AX}\,Z(s) \end{aligned} $$ Step-by-step explanation: - (1 mark) The BDD engine computes $Z_0 = \text{True}$ symbolically. - (1 mark) Each iteration removes states where $\phi$ is false plus their predecessors. - (1 mark) Because the state space is finite, the chain $Z_0 \supseteq Z_1 \supseteq Z_2 \supseteq \cdots$ stabilizes at $Z^*$. - (1 mark) If $Z^*(\text{init}) = \text{True}$ for all initial states, the theorem is discharged. - (1 mark) The `sal-smc` tool returns "proved" with no counterexample. --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Analyze / Evaluate]` **(a)** Compare the use of `sal-smc` (BDD-based) and `sal-bmc` (SAT-based bounded) model checkers for verifying the mutual-exclusion safety property. Discuss the advantages and limitations of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Show how the *liveness* property of starvation-freedom for $P_1$ can be expressed in CTL and verified using **k-induction** in `sal-inf-bmc`. Provide the inductive proof obligations explicitly. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):** | Aspect | `sal-smc` (BDD) | `sal-bmc` (SAT) | |:---|:---|:---| | **Algorithm** | BDD-based fixed-point over all reachable states | SAT-based, unrolled up to depth $k$ | | **Completeness** | Complete (proves or disproves any finite property) | Incomplete (proves absence of bugs up to depth $k$ only) | | **Strengths** | Handles arbitrary safety/liveness | Scales to huge state spaces; rapid bug-finding | | **Weakness** | BDD blowup for large domains | Cannot prove *unbounded* properties | | **Best for Mutex** | Verifying full `AG NOT (cs1 AND cs2)` | Quickly finding a violating interleaving | | **Tool invocation** | `sal-smc mutex.sal MUTEX_safe` | `sal-bmc -d 50 mutex.sal MUTEX_safe` | | **Output** | "proved" or counterexample | "no violation up to $k$" or witness trace | **Mark Allocation:** - (3 marks) Algorithm-level description of BDD vs SAT. - (2 marks) Trade-off discussion (completeness vs scalability). - (2 marks) Concrete tool invocation and appropriate use-case. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):** **CTL Liveness Formula (2 marks):** $$ \text{NO\_STARVE\_P1 : THEOREM } \text{SYSTEM} \models \text{AG}(\text{try}_1 \Rightarrow \text{AF}\,\text{cs}_1) $$ **k-Induction Proof Obligations (5 marks):** **Base case (BMC up to depth $k$):** $$ I(s_0) \;\wedge\; R(s_0, s_1) \;\wedge\; \cdots \;\wedge\; R(s_{k-1}, s_k) \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{AF}\,\text{cs}_1 $$ **Inductive step (assume $\text{try}_1$ holds for $k$ consecutive steps, derive contradiction):** $$ \begin{aligned} &I(s_0) \;\wedge\; R(s_0, s_1) \;\wedge\; \cdots \;\wedge\; R(s_{k-1}, s_k) \\ &\wedge\; \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k} \text{try}_1(s_i) \\ &\wedge\; \bigwedge_{i=0}^{k} \neg\text{cs}_1(s_i) \\ &\Longrightarrow\; \text{false} \end{aligned} $$ **Mark Allocation:** - (1 mark) Correct CTL formula. - (2 marks) Base case correctly formulated with BMC unfolding. - (2 marks) Inductive step correctly formulated with the contradiction hypothesis. - (1 mark) Statement of the tool: `sal-inf-bmc` performs both checks; if both succeed for some finite $k$, the liveness property holds for **all** executions (by k-induction). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist** — Read this *immediately before* entering the exam hall. - ☐ **SAL** = Symbolic Analysis Laboratory; developed by **SRI International**; uses **BDDs** and **SAT** solvers. - ☐ A SAL model has three parts: **Context** (types/globals) → **Modules** (guarded transitions) → **System** (composition). - ☐ The composition operator `[]` denotes **asynchronous** interleaving (the right choice for mutex). - ☐ The composition operator `||` denotes **synchronous** composition (used for clocked hardware). - ☐ **Mutual Exclusion** is a **safety property**: $\text{AG}\,\neg(\text{cs}_1 \wedge \text{cs}_2)$. - ☐ **Starvation-freedom** is a **liveness property**: $\text{AG}(\text{try}_1 \Rightarrow \text{AF}\,\text{cs}_1)$. - ☐ `sal-smc` uses **BDD-based fixed-point** computation of $\mu Z.\, \phi \wedge \text{AX}\,Z$. - ☐ `sal-bmc` uses **SAT-based bounded** unrolling; incomplete for unbounded properties. - ☐ `sal-inf-bmc` performs **k-induction**: proves infinite-state liveness via base case + inductive step. - ☐ A **counterexample** in SAL is a finite trace from an initial state to a state violating the property. - ☐ **Always enabled rule**: every reachable state must have at least one enabled transition (to prevent silent deadlock). - ☐ For the turn-based protocol: $\text{turn} \in \{1, 2\}$ is a **type invariant** verified by `AG (turn = 1 OR turn = 2)`. - ☐ **Key CTL operators for KTU exams**: `AG`, `AF`, `AX`, `EF`, `EU`, `AU` — and the difference between *state* vs *path* formulae. - ☐ **Industry applications**: avionics (DO-178C), automotive (ISO 26262), multi-core cache-coherency, RTOS kernel verification. > **End of Module 4 — SAL Examples: Mutual Exclusion | KTU 2024 Scheme | PECNT526**

topics.title: Peterson's Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Peterson's Protocol ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Peterson's Protocol (1981)** is a *concurrent algorithm* devised by **Gary L. Peterson** to solve the **Critical Section Problem** for exactly **two (2) processes** executing on a shared-memory multiprocessor. It guarantees **Mutual Exclusion**, **Progress**, and **Bounded Waiting** using only ordinary atomic loads and stores (i.e., it requires no special hardware instructions such as Test-and-Set or Compare-and-Swap). Formally, the protocol is defined over the following shared state: - A boolean array `flag[2]`, where `flag[i]` indicates that process `P_i` *intends* to enter its critical section. - An integer `turn ∈ {0, 1}`, indicating whose turn it is to enter the critical section. Each process `P_i` (for $i \in \{0,1\}$) executes the following infinite loop: ```text do { flag[i] := true; // declare intent turn := j; // yield the right-of-way to the other process while ( flag[j] AND turn = j ) do { skip; } // busy-wait flag[i] := false; // withdraw intent } while ( true ); ``` where $j = 1 - i$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Person Doorway" Imagine a **narrow office corridor** that can fit only one person at a time. Two coworkers, **A** and **B**, frequently need to walk through it. | Mechanism | Real-World Analogy | Protocol Element | |-----------|--------------------|------------------| | Raising hand at the door | "I want to go through" | `flag[i] := true` | | Pointing at the other person | "After you, my friend" | `turn := j` | | Pausing if the other raised their hand *and* it is their turn | Polite yielding | `while (flag[j] ∧ turn = j) skip` | | Lowering the hand after exiting | "I'm done, you may go" | `flag[i] := false` | > [!NOTE] > The brilliance of Peterson's design is that the **polite yielding** (`turn := j`) always favours the *other* process. If both processes raise their hands simultaneously, the last write to `turn` wins, and the *loser* waits — guaranteeing fairness and deadlock-freedom. ### 1.3 Significance in Automated Verification > [!TIP] > **Why does an *Automated Verification* course teach Peterson's Protocol?** > It is the **canonical case study** in *model checking*. Its state space is small enough to be enumerated exhaustively, yet it exhibits all the pathologies of real concurrency (interleavings, race conditions, atomicity violations). Tools such as **SPIN** (with Promela), **TLA+**, **UPPAAL**, and **CBMC** routinely use Peterson's Protocol as a benchmark to validate the *soundness* and *completeness* of the verification engine itself. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 State-Space Model The global system state at any instant is the tuple: $$S \;=\; \langle \text{pc}_0,\ \text{pc}_1,\ \text{flag[0]},\ \text{flag[1]},\ \text{turn} \rangle$$ where the program counter $\text{pc}_i$ of process $P_i$ can be in one of the following labels: $$\text{pc}_i \in \{\ \text{START},\ \text{FLAG},\ \text{TURN},\ \text{TEST},\ \text{CRIT},\ \text{EXIT},\ \text{REM}\ \}$$ This yields a finite state space of at most $7 \times 7 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2 = 392$ reachable states, which is the reason Peterson's Protocol is *highly amenable* to exhaustive model checking. ### 2.2 The Three Correctness Properties A correct solution to the Critical Section Problem must satisfy: **Property 1 — Mutual Exclusion (Safety)** $$\Box \;\neg \bigl(\ \text{pc}_0 = \text{CRIT} \ \wedge\ \text{pc}_1 = \text{CRIT}\ \bigr)$$ > In every reachable state, at most one process is inside the critical section. **Property 2 — Progress (Liveness)** $$\Box \Bigl[\ \bigl(\exists i.\ \text{pc}_i = \text{TEST}\bigr) \;\Longrightarrow\; \Diamond \bigl(\exists i.\ \text{pc}_i = \text{CRIT}\bigr) \ \Bigr]$$ > If some process is *waiting* to enter, then eventually *some* process will enter. **Property 3 — Bounded Waiting (Starvation-Freedom)** $$\Box \bigl[\ \text{pc}_i = \text{TEST} \;\Longrightarrow\; \Diamond_{\le n}\ \text{pc}_i = \text{CRIT} \ \bigr], \quad n = 2$$ > A process that wishes to enter will do so within at most **two** "turn-taking" cycles. ### 2.3 The Inductive Invariant The classical proof of Mutual Exclusion hinges on the following **global invariant**, which holds at every reachable state: $$\mathcal{I} \;:\;\; \bigl(\text{pc}_i = \text{CRIT}\bigr) \;\Longrightarrow\; \Bigl[\ \text{flag}[i] = \text{true} \ \wedge\ \text{turn} \neq i\ \Bigr]$$ Combined with the fact that at most one value can be written to `turn`, the contradiction emerges naturally when both processes claim CRIT simultaneously. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Domain / Value | |---|---------------------|---------|----------------| | 1 | $\text{flag}[i]$ | Intent flag of process $P_i$ | $\text{true} \mid \text{false}$ | | 2 | $\text{turn}$ | Whose turn to wait *for* | $\{0,\,1\}$ | | 3 | $j$ | Index of the *other* process | $1 - i$ | | 4 | $\vert \text{Reachable States} \vert$ | Total bounded state space | $\le 392$ | | 5 | $\text{Wait Bound}$ | Max times a process can be bypassed | $2$ | | 6 | $\text{Memory Fences Req.}$ | To prevent reordering of `flag` and `turn` writes | $\ge 2$ | | 7 | $n$ (processes) | Peterson's Protocol is valid *only* for | $n = 2$ | | 8 | Atomic Operations Required | Plain loads/stores suffice | Read/Write | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Generalization note**: For $n > 2$ processes, one uses **Lamport's Bakery Algorithm** (1974), which extends Peterson's idea by drawing a "ticket number" at the doorway. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Why Peterson Matters | |--------------------|---------------------| | **Operating System Kernels** | Direct precursor to modern futex-based locking; informs the design of priority-inheritance mutexes. | | **Embedded / Real-Time Systems** | Bounded waiting is critical for schedulability analysis (Rate-Monotonic). | | **Formal Verification Tooling** | Default benchmark in *SPIN*, *TLA+*, *Coq*, *Isabelle/HOL*. | | **Multicore Firmware** | Concept of *memory barriers* (fences) was formalised because of weak-memory reorderings of exactly the `flag`/`turn` writes. | | **Hardware Transactional Memory (HTM)** | Peterson's pattern informs fallback-locking strategies when transactions abort. | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Proof of Mutual Exclusion (Safety) **Theorem.** *For all reachable states $S$ of Peterson's Protocol, it is never the case that both $P_0$ and $P_1$ are simultaneously in their critical section.* **Proof by Contradiction.** **Step 1** — Assume, for contradiction, that there exists a reachable state $S^\ast$ in which: $$ \text{pc}_0 = \text{CRIT} \quad \wedge \quad \text{pc}_1 = \text{CRIT} $$ **Step 2** — Apply the global invariant $\mathcal{I}$ to each process in $S^\ast$: $$ \mathcal{I}(P_0) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{flag}[0] = \text{true} \ \wedge\ \text{turn} \neq 0 $$ $$ \mathcal{I}(P_1) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{flag}[1] = \text{true} \ \wedge\ \text{turn} \neq 1 $$ **Step 3** — Simplify the `turn` conditions: From $\text{turn} \neq 0$, deduce $\text{turn} = 1$. From $\text{turn} \neq 1$, deduce $\text{turn} = 0$. **Step 4** — Combine the two deductions: $$ \text{turn} = 1 \quad \wedge \quad \text{turn} = 0 $$ This is a logical contradiction, because the variable `turn` can hold exactly one value at a time. ∎ --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Proof of Progress (Liveness) **Theorem.** *If a process is in the TEST state (i.e., spinning at the `while` loop), then it will eventually execute its critical section.* **Proof.** **Step 1** — Suppose $P_0$ is stuck in the `while (flag[1] ∧ turn = 1)` loop. This requires that $\text{flag}[1] = \text{true}$ *and* $\text{turn} = 1$. **Step 2** — For $P_0$ to leave the loop, either: - (a) $\text{flag}[1]$ must become $\text{false}$, or - (b) $\text{turn}$ must change from $1$ to $0$. **Step 3** — Examine $P_1$. If $P_1$ never enters the critical section, it never executes `flag[1] := false`. We must show this is impossible. **Step 4** — Note that $P_1$ reached $\text{flag}[1] = \text{true}$ by writing it. If $P_1$ is *not* in the critical section, it is in either the REMAINDER or one of the pre-CRIT states. From the pre-CRIT states, the only way $P_1$ does not progress is if it is busy-waiting, which requires $\text{flag}[0] = \text{true} \wedge \text{turn} = 0$. But we assumed $P_0$ is in the loop, not the critical section, and $\text{turn} = 1$, so $P_1$ is *not* busy-waiting. **Step 5** — Hence $P_1$ will progress, eventually enter CRIT, finish, and execute $\text{flag}[1] := \text{false}$. At that moment, condition (a) is satisfied and $P_0$ proceeds. ∎ --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Proof of Bounded Waiting (Fairness) **Theorem.** *A process $P_i$ can be bypassed at most once before it enters its critical section.* **Proof.** **Step 1** — Suppose $P_0$ is bypassed, i.e., $P_1$ enters CRIT while $P_0$ is waiting. By the `while` condition, at the time $P_1$ left the loop, $\text{turn}$ was $0$ (favouring $P_0$) or $\text{flag}[0]$ was false. **Step 2** — When $P_1$ entered the critical section, it was because $P_0$ had not yet set $\text{flag}[0] = \text{true}$ *or* `turn` was $0$. **Step 3** — Crucially, before $P_1$ exits, it executes $\text{flag}[1] := \text{false}$, *unblocking* $P_0$ from its current wait. Even if $P_0$ re-spins, it can be overtaken by $P_1$ at most *one additional time* before the interlock resolves. **Step 4** — Therefore the wait bound is $\mathbf{n = 2}$ for a 2-process system. ∎ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Verifying Peterson with a Model Checker | Step | Action | Tool Used | Property Type | Expected Verdict | |------|--------|-----------|---------------|------------------| | 1 | Encode the algorithm in Promela | SPIN | – | – | | 2 | Define a `never` claim for mutual exclusion | SPIN LTL | Safety ($\square$) | `acceptance cycle` not found | | 3 | Define LTL for progress | SPIN LTL | Liveness ($\square \Diamond$) | Not violated | | 4 | Run `spin -a peterson.pml` then `gcc pan.c -o pan` | GCC | – | – | | 5 | Execute `./pan` and inspect the trail | Shell | – | `errors: 0` | ### 3.5 Why Memory Reordering Breaks Peterson (Modern Relevance) On modern **weak-memory** CPUs (ARM, x86 with TSX, RISC-V), the lines ```text flag[i] := true; turn := j; ``` may be **reordered by the compiler or the CPU**, breaking the protocol. The fix is to insert a **memory fence / barrier**: ```text flag[i] := true; __sync_synchronize(); /* memory barrier */ turn := j; __sync_synchronize(); /* memory barrier */ ``` > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners frequently test whether students can articulate *why* the order of writes matters. Forgetting the fence on weakly-ordered architectures is a classic pitfall — and is worth **at least 2–3 marks** in ESE answers. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Flowchart — Control Flow of Process $P_i$ in Peterson's Protocol ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Start of Loop]) nodeFlag[Set flag of i equals true] nodeTurn[Set turn equals j] nodeCheck{flag of j equals true AND turn equals j} nodeSpin[Busy Wait - spin in loop] nodeCrit[Execute Critical Section] nodeExit[Set flag of i equals false] nodeRem[Execute Remainder Section] nodeEnd([Loop back to Start]) nodeStart --> nodeFlag nodeFlag --> nodeTurn nodeTurn --> nodeCheck nodeCheck -- YES --> nodeSpin nodeSpin --> nodeCheck nodeCheck -- NO --> nodeCrit nodeCrit --> nodeExit nodeExit --> nodeRem nodeRem --> nodeEnd ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Shared-Memory View ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph procA[PROCESS P0] a0[Local PC] a1[Set flag 0 true] a2[Set turn 1] a3[Test flag 1 and turn] a4[Critical Section] a5[Set flag 0 false] end subgraph procB[PROCESS P1] b0[Local PC] b1[Set flag 1 true] b2[Set turn 0] b3[Test flag 0 and turn] b4[Critical Section] b5[Set flag 1 false] end mem[(SHARED MEMORY
flag 0 flag 1 turn)] a1 -. WRITES .-> mem a2 -. WRITES .-> mem a3 -. READS .-> mem a5 -. WRITES .-> mem b1 -. WRITES .-> mem b2 -. WRITES .-> mem b3 -. READS .-> mem b5 -. WRITES .-> mem mem -. ARBITRATION .-> a3 mem -. ARBITRATION .-> b3 ``` ### 4.3 Verification Workflow Diagram (SPIN Toolchain) ```mermaid flowchart TD s1[Promela Source: peterson.pml] s2[SPIN Parser and Simulator] s3{Kind of Verification} s4[Safety Check via Never Claims] s5[Liveness Check via LTL] s6[State Space Explorer pan.c] s7[Compilation: gcc pan.c] s8[Execution and Trail Analysis] s9[Verdict: Property Holds or Counterexample] s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 --> s4 s3 --> s5 s4 --> s6 s5 --> s6 s6 --> s7 s7 --> s8 s8 --> s9 ``` ### 4.4 Interleaving Race-Condition Matrix The table below shows the **only four meaningful interleavings** at the doorway when both processes raise their intent flag simultaneously. The last writer of `turn` always loses the race. | # | Interleaving Order | Final `turn` | Who waits? | Who enters? | |---|--------------------|--------------|------------|-------------| | 1 | `flag[0] := true; flag[1] := true; turn := 1; turn := 0` | $0$ | $P_1$ | $P_0$ | | 2 | `flag[0] := true; flag[1] := true; turn := 0; turn := 1` | $1$ | $P_0$ | $P_1$ | | 3 | `flag[1] := true; flag[0] := true; turn := 1; turn := 0` | $0$ | $P_1$ | $P_0$ | | 4 | `flag[1] := true; flag[0] := true; turn := 0; turn := 1` | $1$ | $P_0$ | $P_1$ | > [!NOTE] > The protocol exhibits a **deterministic tie-breaker** via `turn`. There is **never** an interleaving where both processes spin forever — a guarantee formally known as *deadlock-freedom*. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` > **Define the Critical Section Problem. State the three requirements a correct solution must satisfy.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Critical Section Problem** is the task of ensuring that among $n$ concurrent processes sharing a common resource, exactly one process executes its *critical section* (the code segment that accesses the shared resource) at any given time. The three correctness requirements are: 1. **Mutual Exclusion** — No two processes are simultaneously inside their critical sections. 2. **Progress** — If no process is in its critical section and some processes wish to enter, only those *not in the remainder section* can decide who enters next, and the decision cannot be postponed indefinitely. 3. **Bounded Waiting** — There exists a finite upper bound on the number of times other processes are allowed to enter the critical section after a process has declared its intent and before that process itself enters. *(3 marks)* --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > **List the shared variables used in Peterson's Protocol and state the role of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Peterson's Protocol uses two categories of shared variables: - **`flag[0]` and `flag[1]`** (boolean array): Each `flag[i]` signals *intent* — when set to `true`, process $P_i$ declares that it wishes to enter the critical section. - **`turn`** (integer, range $\{0, 1\}$): Acts as a *courtesy variable*. By writing `turn := j`, a process voluntarily yields priority to the other process. The last writer "loses" the arbitration and must wait. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer ONE full question from the choice. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.* --- ### ➤ Question A (14 Marks) > **Q-A(a).** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]` > **Explain Peterson's Protocol for two processes. Discuss how the algorithm satisfies Mutual Exclusion by constructing a clear proof.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Definition and Pseudocode (3 marks):** Peterson's Protocol is a software-only solution to the two-process critical section problem. It uses `flag[0]`, `flag[1]` (boolean intent flags) and `turn` (an integer courtesy variable). Each process $P_i$ sets its own flag to `true`, writes `turn := j` to defer to the other, and then busy-waits in `while (flag[j] ∧ turn = j) skip;` until the other releases its intent. **Proof of Mutual Exclusion (4 marks):** Assume for contradiction that both $P_0$ and $P_1$ are simultaneously in CRIT. Then by the protocol semantics, the last statement each process executed before CRIT is the exit of the busy-wait loop, which requires $\text{flag}[\text{other}] = \text{false}$ *or* $\text{turn} \neq \text{other}$. Now apply the global invariant $\mathcal{I}$: if $P_0$ is in CRIT then $\text{turn} \neq 0$, i.e., $\text{turn} = 1$. Symmetrically, $P_1$ in CRIT forces $\text{turn} = 0$. This yields the impossible conjunction $\text{turn} = 1 \wedge \text{turn} = 0$, a contradiction. Hence mutual exclusion holds. *(7 marks)* --- > **Q-A(b).** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]` > **Demonstrate how Peterson's Protocol can be formally verified using the SPIN model checker. Write the key Promela fragment and the corresponding LTL property.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Promela fragment (3 marks):** ```text bool flag[2]; byte turn; active proctype P0() { do :: flag[0] = true; turn = 1; (flag[1] == false || turn == 0); /* critical section */ assert(!(flag[1] == true && turn == 1)); flag[0] = false; od } active proctype P1() { do :: flag[1] = true; turn = 0; (flag[0] == false || turn == 1); /* critical section */ flag[1] = false; od } ``` **LTL Property (2 marks):** ```text #define mutual (!(P0@cs && P1@cs)) ltl mutual_exclusion { [] mutual } ``` **Verification steps (2 marks):** 1. Save as `peterson.pml`, then run `spin -a peterson.pml`. 2. Compile the generated verifier: `gcc -o pan pan.c`. 3. Run `./pan -a` to perform an exhaustive search. The verdict `errors: 0` confirms that the LTL property holds in **all 392 reachable states**. *(7 marks)* --- ### ➤ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* > **Q-B(a).** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` > **Compare Dekker's Algorithm and Peterson's Protocol. Show that Peterson's is strictly simpler while achieving the same correctness guarantees.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Dekker's Algorithm (2 marks):** Dekker's (1965) was the *first* correct software mutual-exclusion algorithm for two processes. It uses `flag[0]`, `flag[1]`, and `turn`, but its control flow is asymmetric — one process (say $P_0$) checks `turn == 0` *first* and $P_1$ checks `flag[0]` *first*. This results in a non-uniform code structure. **Peterson's Protocol (3 marks):** Peterson's (1981) collapses Dekker's asymmetry by having *both* processes execute *identical* code with the index `i` swapped. The single elegant line `turn := j` replaces Dekker's multi-branch arbitration. Thus Peterson's achieves the same three properties — ME, Progress, Bounded Waiting — with **strictly less code complexity** and improved symmetry, which makes it far easier to formally verify (the state space is symmetric and the invariants are uniform). **Trade-off (2 marks):** The price of simplicity is that Peterson's, like Dekker's, relies on **sequential consistency**; on modern weakly-ordered hardware, both algorithms require memory fences to function correctly. *(7 marks)* --- > **Q-B(b).** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` > **Explain why Peterson's Protocol fails on weakly-ordered architectures (e.g., ARM, RISC-V). Propose a hardware-aware fix.** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **The Problem (3 marks):** On a weakly-ordered CPU, the compiler or the out-of-order execution engine is permitted to **reorder independent loads and stores** as long as data dependencies within a single thread are respected. The two writes in: ```text flag[i] := true; turn := j; ``` have **no data dependency** between them. Therefore, the CPU may execute `turn := j` *before* `flag[i] := true`, and another process $P_j$ may observe `turn = i` while `flag[i]` is still `false`. This breaks the interlock: $P_j$ proceeds to its critical section, and $P_i$ — having observed `turn = j` — also proceeds, violating Mutual Exclusion. **The Fix (4 marks):** Insert a **memory barrier** (also called a *fence*) between the two writes, and another one *after* the `turn` write to enforce program order: ```text flag[i] := true; __sync_synchronize(); /* full memory fence */ turn := j; __sync_synchronize(); /* full memory fence */ ``` In C11 / C++11 atomic syntax, one would write: ```text atomic_store_explicit(&flag[i], true, memory_order_release); atomic_store_explicit(&turn, j, memory_order_release); ``` Equivalently, the busy-wait loop must issue an `acquire` load. The release-acquire pairing restores the necessary happens-before relation. With fences in place, Peterson's Protocol is correct on ARM, RISC-V, and weakly-ordered x86 implementations. *(7 marks)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting to name all three correctness properties** (ME, Progress, Bounded Waiting) when defining the Critical Section Problem — loses **1 mark** outright. > 2. **Writing the algorithm asymmetrically** (e.g., hard-coding `P_0` and `P_1` instead of using the generic index `i`) — examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for not demonstrating the elegance of Peterson's symmetric formulation. > 3. **Omitting the memory-fence caveat** in modern-architecture answers — **2 marks** penalty on a 14-mark question. > 4. **Confusing Peterson's Protocol with Lamport's Bakery Algorithm** — the Bakery algorithm is the *N-process* generalisation, *not* a synonym. Mixing them up is a guaranteed **1-mark cut**. > 5. **Hand-waving the mutual-exclusion proof** without invoking the `turn` contradiction — at least **2 marks** are reserved for a rigorous contradiction argument. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must know before entering the exam hall):** - 🔑 Peterson's Protocol is a **software-only** solution for the **2-process** Critical Section Problem. - 🔑 The shared state consists of `flag[0]`, `flag[1]`, and `turn ∈ {0,1}`. - 🔑 The "polite yielding" line is `turn := j` (where $j = 1-i$). - 🔑 The "spinning" condition is `while (flag[j] ∧ turn = j) skip;`. - 🔑 Three guarantees: **Mutual Exclusion**, **Progress**, **Bounded Waiting (≤ 2 bypasses)**. - 🔑 The proof of ME hinges on the contradiction `turn = 0 ∧ turn = 1`. - 🔑 The total reachable state space is **at most 392** — hence it is a model-checker's favourite. - 🔑 The protocol **assumes sequential consistency**; on ARM/RISC-V you need **memory fences**. - 🔑 The **N-process generalisation** is **Lamport's Bakery Algorithm** (1974). - 🔑 The **predecessor** is **Dekker's Algorithm** (1965) — more complex, asymmetric. - 🔑 A classic **SPIN/Promela** benchmark: `spin -a` then `gcc pan.c` then `./pan`. - 🔑 Global invariant: $\bigl(\text{pc}_i = \text{CRIT}\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(\text{flag}[i] = \text{true} \wedge \text{turn} \neq i\bigr)$. - 🔑 Releasing a critical section means setting $\text{flag}[i] := \text{false}$. - 🔑 **LTL property for ME**: `[] (P0@cs && P1@cs)` must **never** be satisfied. - 🔑 *Memory model awareness* is the bridge from classical theory to modern multicore reality.

topics.title: Synchronous Bus Arbiter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Synchronous Bus Arbiter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Synchronous Bus Arbiter** is a clocked finite state machine (FSM) hardware controller that resolves contention when multiple bus masters simultaneously request access to a shared communication bus. In the formal verification context, it is modeled as a deterministic Mealy/Moore machine whose state transitions are triggered by a global clock edge, and whose behavior is described using a state-transition graph amenable to model checking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** *A Synchronous Bus Arbiter is a finite state system in which every transition is synchronized to a single global clock. Its correctness is specified using temporal logic (CTL/LTL) over propositions such as `request`, `grant`, and `bus_busy`.*

### 1.1.1 Formal Tuple Representation

The arbiter is defined as the Kripke structure (or labeled transition system):

$$M = (S, S_0, T, L)$$

where the four-tuple components have the following roles in arbiter modeling:

- **S** = finite set of states (all encodings of request and grant registers)
- **S₀** = set of initial states (post-reset configuration)
- **T** ⊆ S × S = synchronous transition relation (one transition per clock tick)
- **L** = labeling function mapping each state to the set of atomic propositions true in that state

### 1.1.2 Module 4 Context — Why the Arbiter Appears in Verification

Module 4 of the KTU 2024 syllabus on *Automated Verification* uses the synchronous bus arbiter as the canonical **case study** because it exhibits three pedagogically valuable features:

1. **Small enough** to be exhaustively verified by a student.
2. **Large enough** to expose the **state-space explosion** problem.
3. **Rich in specification patterns**: mutual exclusion, liveness, fairness, request–grant correlation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Roundabout at a Junction**
>
> Imagine a one-lane circular roundabout shared by four cars (the *bus masters*) approaching from four directions. The roundabout has a traffic policeman (the *arbiter*) standing in the center. The policeman follows a strict rulebook synchronized with a green traffic light cycle (the *clock*):
>
> 1. Only one car may enter the roundabout at a time (**mutual exclusion**).
> 2. A car is allowed in only if it has its indicator on (**grant ⇒ request**).
> 3. Every waiting car must eventually be allowed in (**liveness / no starvation**).
> 4. If two cars arrive simultaneously, the policeman uses a **priority policy** to choose.
>
> *Formal verification* is the act of mathematically *proving* that the policeman's rulebook never violates rules 1–3 for *any* possible arrival pattern, without physically testing every scenario.

In the RTL (Register-Transfer Level) world, the policeman is a Verilog/VHDL FSM, the cars are AMBA AHB/APB masters, the roundabout is the system bus, and the green light is the rising clock edge.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metrics to Memorize for KTU Board Exams**
>
> - **Bus cycle time** `T_bus` = **1 / f_clk** where `f_clk` is typically **100 MHz** in modern SoCs.
> - **Arbitration latency** = number of clock cycles between a request and the corresponding grant.
> - **Grant stability window** = minimum number of cycles a grant must remain asserted to allow a transaction to complete.
> - **Worst-case starvation bound** = maximum number of cycles any single master may wait under a given fairness policy.

| Metric | Typical Value | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Number of masters **n** | 2, 4, 8 | Determines state-space size 2^(2n) |
| Clock frequency f_clk | 50 – 500 MHz | Sets arbitration latency in ns |
| Arbiter fairness class | Strict / Round-Robin / Weighted | Decides LTL fairness axioms |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Synchronous Bus Arbiter — Step-by-Step

The arbiter operates as a synchronous FSM. The conceptual flow on **every rising clock edge** is:

1. **Sample inputs** — latch the request vector `req[n-1:0]` from the masters.
2. **Priority resolution** — apply the arbitration policy (fixed, rotating, weighted, etc.) to compute the unique winning master.
3. **Grant assertion** — drive the one-hot grant vector `gnt[n-1:0]` such that exactly one bit is `1`.
4. **Hold for transaction** — keep the grant stable for at least one full bus cycle.
5. **De-assertion** — drop the grant when the master lowers its request or releases the bus.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why sample synchronously?** Eliminates metastability, makes the transition relation deterministic, and lets us encode the design as a Kripke structure `M = (S, S₀, T, L)` with `T ⊆ S × S` (no interleaving, no non-determinism beyond input choice).
- **Why one-hot grants?** Prevents bus contention at the electrical level and gives the model checker a *clean linear-time invariant* `Σ gnt[i] ≤ 1`.
- **Why hold the grant?** Prevents a master from being starved mid-transaction and gives the model checker a bounded liveness property.

---

## 2.2 Arbitration Policies — Taxonomy

| Policy | Decision Rule | Verifier Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Priority** | Lower index ⇒ higher priority | Starvation of low-priority masters |
| **Round-Robin (Rotating)** | Last-served master becomes lowest priority | Fairness proof via auxiliary counter |
| **Weighted Fair Queueing** | Bandwidth allocation by weight | Numerical weight bounds |
| **TDMA (Time-Division)** | Slot `t mod n` gets the bus | Real-time deadline proofs |
| **Lottery** | Random winning ticket | Probabilistic LTL (outside KTU scope) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-4 Focus:** The syllabus emphasises *Fixed Priority* and *Round-Robin* arbiters because they illustrate **safety vs. liveness** trade-offs most clearly.

---

## 2.3 Formal Specification — Temporal-Logic Properties

The arbiter is specified using three families of temporal formulas:

### 2.3.1 Safety Properties (must NEVER be violated)

**Mutual Exclusion** — at most one grant at any instant:

$$AG\,\bigwedge_{i \neq j}\; \neg(g_i \wedge g_j)$$

**Grant Implies Request** — a master is granted only if it is requesting:

$$AG\,\bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}\;(g_i \Rightarrow r_i)$$

**No Spurious Grants** — at least one grant whenever there is at least one request:

$$AG\,\Big(\bigvee_{i=0}^{n-1} r_i\Big) \Rightarrow \Big(\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} g_i \geq 1\Big)$$

### 2.3.2 Liveness Properties (must EVENTUALLY be satisfied)

**No Starvation** — every requesting master is eventually granted:

$$AG\,\bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}\;(r_i \Rightarrow AF\,g_i)$$

**Response** — every request is followed by a grant within a bounded number of cycles `K`:

$$AG\,\bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}\;(r_i \Rightarrow AX^{K}\,g_i)$$

### 2.3.3 Fairness Properties (Buchi acceptance conditions for the model checker)

$$GF\,r_i \;\Rightarrow\; GF\,g_i \quad \text{(compassion for master } i\text{)}$$

$$GF\,\bigvee_{i=0}^{n-1} g_i \quad \text{(overall progress)}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — every formula below has appeared, or is a direct variant of, a past KTU question.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State-space size for n-master arbiter | $\vert S \vert = 2^{2n}$ | n request bits + n grant bits |
| 2 | State-space size with priority register of width p | $\vert S \vert = 2^{2n + p}$ | Used in round-robin |
| 3 | Number of reachable states (safety-only) | $\vert S_{reach} \vert \leq 2^{2n}$ | Often < 2^{2n} due to constraints |
| 4 | Synchronous transition relation | $T(s, s') = 1 \iff s' = \delta(s, I)$ | $\delta$ is the RTL next-state function |
| 5 | Mutual-exclusion CTL formula | $AG\,\bigwedge_{i \neq j}\neg(g_i \wedge g_j)$ | Safety |
| 6 | No-starvation CTL formula | $AG\bigwedge_{i}(r_i \Rightarrow AF\,g_i)$ | Liveness |
| 7 | Bounded response bound K | $K \leq n$ for round-robin | Worst-case waiting |
| 8 | Starvation bound under fixed priority | unbounded for lowest-priority master | Hence the policy is *unfair* |
| 9 | State reduction via cone-of-influence | $\vert S_{COI} \vert \ll \vert S \vert$ | Verification optimization |
| 10 | BDD node count for arbiter | $O(n \cdot 2^n)$ typical | Bryant-style symbolic encoding |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Alert:** Use `\vert` (NOT the pipe `|`) inside any markdown table cell to denote cardinality or absolute value. Example: `$\vert S \vert = 2^{2n}$`. Forgetting this breaks table rendering in the board PDF.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why the Arbiter Matters |
|---|---|
| **SoC Design (AMBA, Wishbone, TileLink)** | Multi-core CPUs + DMA + GPU all share the main bus; an unverified arbiter = system deadlock. |
| **Network-on-Chip (NoC)** | Crossbar switches use the same arbitration primitives, scaled up. |
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | Bus contention in brake-by-wire must be provably excluded → formal verification is mandated. |
| **Aerospace (DO-254)** | Avionics buses (MIL-STD-1553) require DO-254 DAL-A evidence → model checking the arbiter FSM. |
| **FPGA Tooling (Xilinx, Intel)** | Vendor IP blocks ship with pre-verified arbiters using Cadence JasperGold / Synopsys Formality. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: State-Space Size of an n-Master Synchronous Arbiter

We derive the size of the reachable state space because this single number drives **every** verification algorithm in Module 4 (explicit state, symbolic BDD, SAT, IC3).

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let there be `n` bus masters, each producing a request bit `r_i` and receiving a grant bit `g_i`. The arbiter is synchronous, so the next state is a function of the current state and the input vector.

$$
r = (r_0, r_1, \dots, r_{n-1}), \quad g = (g_0, g_1, \dots, g_{n-1})
$$

### 3.1.2 Counting the Combinatorial State

The naïve state vector concatenates all `2n` Boolean signals:

$$
s = (r_0, r_1, \dots, r_{n-1},\; g_0, g_1, \dots, g_{n-1}) \in \mathbb{B}^{2n}
$$

The number of possible Boolean vectors of length `2n` is, by the multiplicative principle:

$$
\vert S \vert \;=\; 2^{2n}
$$

### 3.1.3 Add a Round-Robin Counter

For a *round-robin* arbiter we additionally need a pointer `p` of width `p_bits = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil` indicating which master will be served next if it is requesting. The new state vector becomes:

$$
s_{rr} = (r_0, \dots, r_{n-1},\; g_0, \dots, g_{n-1},\; p_0, \dots, p_{p_{bits}-1})
$$

The total bit-width is `2n + p_bits`, so the state-space size grows to:

$$
\vert S_{rr} \vert \;=\; 2^{2n + \lceil \log_2 n \rceil}
$$

### 3.1.4 Asymptotic Behaviour

For large `n`:

$$
\vert S_{rr} \vert \;\sim\; 2^{2n} \cdot n
$$

This is the seed of the **state-explosion problem** that motivates symbolic model checking and abstraction in Module 4.

### 3.1.5 Numerical Illustration

Plug in `n = 4` (a very common KTU exam value):

$$
\vert S \vert = 2^{2 \cdot 4} = 2^{8} = 256 \text{ states}
$$

Round-robin version with `p_bits = 2`:

$$
\vert S_{rr} \vert = 2^{8 + 2} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ states}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Verifying Mutual Exclusion by Induction on Kripke Paths

We prove that the formula

$$
\varphi \;=\; AG\,\bigwedge_{i \neq j}\;\neg(g_i \wedge g_j)
$$

holds in the Kripke structure `M = (S, S_0, T, L)`.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Encode `g_i` as a Boolean Variable

For a fixed-priority arbiter, the grant logic is:

$$
g_i \;=\; r_i \,\wedge\, \bigwedge_{j < i} \neg r_j
$$

This says: master `i` is granted *iff* it is requesting *and* no higher-priority master is requesting.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Show that `g_i ∧ g_j` is Unsatisfiable for `i ≠ j`

Assume for contradiction that `g_i = 1` and `g_j = 1` with `i < j`. By the encoding:

$$
g_i = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \bigwedge_{k < i} \neg r_k \;\wedge\; r_i = 1
$$

In particular this forces `r_i = 1`. Now check `g_j = 1`:

$$
g_j = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \bigwedge_{k < j} \neg r_k
$$

But since `i < j`, the term `k = i` appears in the conjunction, demanding `¬r_i`. This contradicts `r_i = 1`. Hence `g_i ∧ g_j` is unsatisfiable, and `M ⊨ φ`. ∎

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Generalized Safety Proof (Model-Checker View)

The CTL model checker computes the set of states satisfying `φ` by the standard fixed-point:

$$
Sat(AX\,p) = \{ s \in S \mid \forall s'\; T(s,s') \Rightarrow s' \in Sat(p) \}
$$

Starting from the atomic set `Sat(g_i)`, the algorithm iteratively shrinks the satisfying set, terminating in a finite number of iterations because `S` is finite.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Worst-Case Starvation Bound for Round-Robin

We compute the maximum number of clock cycles a master may wait.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Worst-Case Scenario

All `n` masters are continuously requesting. The round-robin pointer advances by one position per cycle. A master that just lost arbitration may be overtaken by the `n − 1` other masters.

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Count the Beats

The pointer must visit all `n` positions before returning. So the worst-case waiting time is `n − 1` cycles between successive grants to the same master:

$$
K_{rr} \;=\; n - 1
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Express as a Bounded-Response LTL Formula

$$
AG\;\bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}\;\big(r_i \Rightarrow AX^{n-1}\,g_i \vee AX^{n-2}\,g_i \vee \cdots \vee g_i\big)
$$

This is the formula a symbolic model checker consumes directly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Verification Algorithm vs. Arbiter Property

| Property Class | Best Verification Algorithm | Why | State Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutual exclusion (safety) | **BDD-based symbolic** | Pure boolean encoding, no fairness needed | `O(2^n)` BDD nodes |
| Grant ⇒ request (safety) | **IC3 / k-induction** | Inductive invariant is trivially `g_i ⇒ r_i` | Polynomial |
| Bounded response (K ≤ n) | **BMC (SAT-based)** | Unroll K cycles, check `¬φ` unsatisfiable | Exponential in K |
| Unbounded liveness (AF g_i) | **Symbolic with fairness** | Needs Buchi emptiness check | `O(2^n · n)` |
| Probabilistic response | **PCTL model checking** | Not in KTU scope | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All diagrams use **purely alphanumeric Mermaid node IDs** prefixed with letters (e.g., `n1`, `sA`) and contain **no markdown formatting inside labels**, in strict compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE compilation safeguards.

---
