<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> Most students lose 2 – 3 marks on arbiter questions by:
> (a) confusing **AG** (invariant in *all* paths) with **AF** (eventually in *all* paths);
> (b) forgetting to state the *fairness assumption* required for liveness proofs;
> (c) writing `\vert S \vert` with a `|` pipe character that breaks the answer PDF's markdown table.
> Read the formula sheet in §2.4 the night before the exam.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. Define a synchronous bus arbiter. Why is it called "synchronous"?

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, Part A]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A synchronous bus arbiter is a clocked finite state machine that resolves simultaneous bus-access requests from multiple masters and asserts exactly one grant signal per clock cycle. It is called *synchronous* because every state transition is triggered by the edge of a single global clock, making the next-state function deterministic with respect to a discrete time index.

**Mark-split:** Definition (1) + role (1) + reason for "synchronous" (1).

---

### Q2. List three safety properties and one liveness property that must be satisfied by a synchronous bus arbiter.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, Part A]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Safety properties (must *never* be violated):

1. **Mutual exclusion** — at most one grant is asserted in any state: `AG ∧_{i≠j} ¬(g_i ∧ g_j)`.
2. **Grant implies request** — `AG ∧_i (g_i ⇒ r_i)`.
3. **No spurious grants** — exactly one grant is asserted whenever any request is active: `AG (∨_i r_i) ⇒ (Σ g_i = 1)`.

Liveness property (must *eventually* be satisfied): **No starvation** — every requesting master is eventually granted: `AG ∧_i (r_i ⇒ AF g_i)`.

**Mark-split:** Three safety items (½ each = 1½) + one liveness (1) + correct CTL operator usage (½).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Choose **ONE** of the two full questions below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Design + Verification

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Design a synchronous bus arbiter for `n = 4` masters using a **fixed-priority** policy. Draw the FSM and give the Boolean equations for each `g_i`. (7 marks)

**(b)** Express the property "no two masters are ever granted simultaneously" as a CTL formula and prove it holds for your design. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State the inputs and outputs (1 mark)**

Inputs: request vector `r = (r_0, r_1, r_2, r_3)`, clock `clk`, reset `rst`.
Outputs: one-hot grant vector `g = (g_0, g_1, g_2, g_3)`.

**Step 2 — Write the priority equations (3 marks)**

For fixed priority, master `0` is highest priority and master `3` is lowest:

$$
g_0 = r_0
$$

$$
g_1 = \neg r_0 \,\wedge\, r_1
$$

$$
g_2 = \neg r_0 \,\wedge\, \neg r_1 \,\wedge\, r_2
$$

$$
g_3 = \neg r_0 \,\wedge\, \neg r_1 \,\wedge\, \neg r_2 \,\wedge\, r_3
$$

**Step 3 — Draw the FSM (2 marks)** — show three states `IDLE`, `G_MASTER_i`, transitions guarded by the request bits. (See Figure 2 above for an analogous 2-master FSM.)

**Step 4 — Encoding (1 mark)** — explain that the FSM is one-hot for grants and that all transitions are synchronous to the rising edge of `clk`.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Write the CTL property (2 marks)**

$$
\varphi_{mutex} \;=\; AG\,\bigwedge_{0 \le i < j \le 3}\; \neg(g_i \,\wedge\, g_j)
$$

**Step 2 — Pick a representative pair (1 mark)** — We prove for the pair `(i, j) = (0, 3)`. The remaining five pairs are analogous.

**Step 3 — Show unsatisfiability of `g_0 ∧ g_3` (3 marks)**

Assume `g_0 = 1` and `g_3 = 1`. From the priority equations:

$$
g_0 = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; r_0 = 1
$$

$$
g_3 = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \neg r_0 \wedge \neg r_1 \wedge \neg r_2 \wedge r_3
$$

In particular, `g_3 = 1` forces `¬r_0`. This contradicts `r_0 = 1`. Hence `g_0 ∧ g_3` is unsatisfiable.

**Step 4 — Conclude the CTL satisfaction (1 mark)**

Since the conjunction of all six pairwise exclusions is satisfied, the Kripke structure `M ⊨ φ_mutex`. ∎

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Liveness + State-Space Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** For a 4-master *round-robin* arbiter, derive the **worst-case starvation bound** in clock cycles and express it as a bounded-response LTL formula. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **state-space size** for `n = 8` masters under (i) fixed priority, and (ii) round-robin. Comment on the verification implications. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Define round-robin (1 mark)**

A round-robin arbiter maintains a `p_bits = ⌈log_2 n⌉`-bit pointer `p` to the next master to be served. On every clock cycle, if the master at `p` is requesting it is granted; otherwise the pointer advances.

**Step 2 — Worst-case waiting scenario (2 marks)**

The worst case occurs when **all** `n` masters are continuously requesting. The pointer must visit all `n` positions before returning to the same master.

**Step 3 — Compute the bound (2 marks)**

$$
K_{rr} \;=\; n - 1
$$

For `n = 4`:

$$
K_{rr} \;=\; 3 \text{ clock cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a bounded-response LTL formula (2 marks)**

$$
AG\;\bigwedge_{i=0}^{3}\;\big(r_i \;\Rightarrow\; g_i \;\vee\; X\,g_i \;\vee\; X^2\,g_i \;\vee\; X^3\,g_i\big)
$$

This says: if `r_i` is high, then `g_i` must be high within the next 0, 1, 2, or 3 cycles.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Fixed-priority case (2 marks)**

For `n = 8`, the state vector is `(r_0,…,r_7, g_0,…,g_7)`, giving 16 bits:

$$
\vert S_{fp} \vert \;=\; 2^{2 \cdot 8} \;=\; 2^{16} \;=\; 65{,}536 \text{ states}
$$

**Step 2 — Round-robin case (2 marks)**

Add a 3-bit pointer `p`:

$$
\vert S_{rr} \vert \;=\; 2^{2 \cdot 8 + 3} \;=\; 2^{19} \;=\; 524{,}288 \text{ states}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification implications (3 marks)**

- Explicit-state model checking: feasible for `2^{16}` (sub-second) but borderline for `2^{19}`.
- Symbolic (BDD) model checking: routine for both, as the arbiter's regularity yields small BDDs (`O(n·2^n)`).
- The round-robin pointer multiplies the state space by `n`, illustrating the classic **state-space explosion** that motivates the *cone-of-influence reduction* and *abstraction* techniques covered later in Module 4.
- For `n = 32` masters, the state space reaches `2^{67}` — beyond any explicit tool, but still tractable for BDD-based engines.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize before walking into the exam hall)**
>
> - **Core tuple:** Synchronous bus arbiter is a Kripke structure `M = (S, S₀, T, L)` with `T ⊆ S × S` deterministic on the clock edge.
> - **State-space size:** `2^{2n}` for fixed-priority, `2^{2n + ⌈log_2 n⌉}` for round-robin.
> - **Three canonical safety formulas:** mutual exclusion, grant ⇒ request, no spurious grants.
> - **One canonical liveness formula:** no starvation, `AG (r_i ⇒ AF g_i)`, which **requires** a fairness assumption.
> - **CTL operators to distinguish:** `AG` = invariant, `AF` = eventual, `AX` = next, `EX` = exists-next.
> - **Verification algorithm choice:** BDD-symbolic for safety, BMC for bounded response, Buchi emptiness for unbounded liveness.
> - **Round-robin starvation bound:** `K_rr = n − 1` cycles.
> - **Fixed-priority pitfall:** the lowest-priority master has an **unbounded** starvation bound → use only when unfairness is acceptable.
> - **Notation hygiene:** write `\vert S \vert`, never `|S|`, inside markdown tables.
> - **Real-world link:** AMBA AHB arbiter, PCIe transaction layer, NoC crossbar — same FSM, scaled up.
> - **Engineering impact:** A missed grant-implies-request bug is the most common cause of bus contention in tape-out revisions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bounded Bakery Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bounded Bakery Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Bounded Bakery Protocol** is a **mutual exclusion algorithm** designed for concurrent processes that share a common resource. It is a **bounded variant** of Lamport's original Bakery Algorithm, in which the integer "ticket numbers" assigned to processes are restricted to a finite range of values (typically $\lvert P \rvert$ or $2\lvert P \rvert$ where $\lvert P \rvert$ is the number of processes), making it implementable on real hardware registers which have finite width.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** "The Bounded Bakery Protocol is a *correct, starvation-free* mutual exclusion algorithm for $N$ processes that uses only *atomic read/write registers* and ensures that *no two processes execute their critical sections simultaneously*."

## 1.2 The Bakery Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine a small bakery with a single counter:

1. Every customer who walks in takes a **paper ticket** from a dispenser that prints the next serial number (1, 2, 3, ...).
2. A customer is served only when their ticket number is the **smallest among all waiting customers**.
3. If two customers somehow hold the same number (a glitch in the dispenser), the **one with the smaller name/ID is served first**.
4. After being served, the customer **discards** their ticket (so others waiting are not blocked forever).

In computing terms, each *process* $P_i$ behaves like a customer:
- The "ticket dispenser" is the act of computing `number[i] := max(all numbers) + 1`.
- The "queue" is implicit, encoded in the global array `number[0..N-1]`.
- The "same-number tie-breaker" is the *lexicographic order* on the pair `(number, id)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *bounded* aspect is crucial: real registers cannot store arbitrarily large integers. The bounded protocol wraps the ticket counter around a fixed modulus $M$ (usually $M = N$ or $M = 2N$), yet still guarantees the same safety and liveness properties as the unbounded version.

## 1.3 Why "Bounded"?

| Property | Unbounded Bakery | Bounded Bakery |
|----------|------------------|----------------|
| Range of `number[i]` | $\mathbb{N} \cup \{0\}$ (unbounded) | $\{0, 1, \dots, M-1\}$ (finite) |
| Hardware realizable | ❌ No (requires $\infty$ bits) | ✅ Yes (requires $\lceil \log_2 M \rceil$ bits) |
| Mutual Exclusion | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Starvation-Freedom | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (in fair versions) |
| Verification Model | Infinite state | Finite state (model-checkable) |

> [!TIP]
> The bounded version is the one that is amenable to **automated verification** using tools like **SPIN**, **TLA+**, **nuXmv**, and **UPPAAL** because the state space becomes finite and bounded.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 State Variables

For a system of $N$ processes $P_0, P_1, \dots, P_{N-1}$, the Bounded Bakery Protocol maintains the following shared (global) variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
choosing[0 \dots N-1] &: \text{array of Boolean} \\
number[0 \dots N-1] &: \text{array of } \mathbb{Z}_{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M$ is the bound (commonly chosen as $M = N$ or $M = 2N$), and $\mathbb{Z}_M = \{0, 1, 2, \dots, M-1\}$ denotes the integers modulo $M$.

**Semantics:**
- `choosing[i] = true` ⟺ process $P_i$ is *currently computing* its ticket number.
- `number[i] = 0` ⟺ process $P_i$ is **not** interested in the critical section.
- `number[i] = k > 0` ⟺ process $P_i$ holds ticket number $k$ and is waiting.

Each process $P_i$ also has a local/program counter $pc_i \in \lbrace idle, choosing, waiting, cs, exit \rbrace$.

## 2.2 The Algorithm (Process $P_i$)

The protocol is structured as a perpetual loop with five well-defined phases:

**Step 1 — Non-critical section (`idle`):**
   The process performs its independent work.

**Step 2 — Doorway (`choosing`):**
   Atomically signal the intent and pick a ticket:
$$
\begin{aligned}
choosing[i] &:= \text{true} \\
number[i]   &:= \bigl(\max_{j} number[j] + 1\bigr) \bmod M \\
choosing[i] &:= \text{false}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Waiting (`waiting`):**
   For every other process $P_j$, the protocol enforces a two-stage wait:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{await } &choosing[j] = \text{false} \\
\text{await } &\bigl(number[j] = 0\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl((number[i],\, i) \prec (number[j],\, j)\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Critical section (`cs`):**
   The process executes the protected code.

**Step 5 — Exit (`exit`):**
$$
number[i] := 0
$$
The process returns to the non-critical section.

## 2.3 The Lexicographic Order ("Tickets and IDs")

The core of the algorithm is the **lexicographic comparison operator** $\prec$:

$$
(a,\, i) \prec (b,\, j) \;\equiv\; \bigl(a < b\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a = b \;\land\; i < j\bigr)
$$

This forms a **total order** over the set $\mathbb{Z}_M \times \lbrace 0, 1, \dots, N-1 \rbrace$ and resolves any ties in ticket numbers by the unique process ID.

> [!NOTE]
> The lexicographic pair is sometimes denoted in literature as $(ticket, id)$. Process $P_i$ enters the critical section before $P_j$ **iff** $(number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j)$ **and** $number[i] \neq 0$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Definition | Notes |
|------------------|------------|-------|
| $N$ | Number of processes | $\|P\|$ |
| $M$ | Bound on ticket numbers | Usually $M = 2N$ |
| $\mathbb{Z}_M$ | Set $\{0, 1, \dots, M-1\}$ | Modulo $M$ arithmetic |
| $choosing[i]$ | Boolean flag for $P_i$ doorway | Single bit per process |
| $number[i]$ | Current ticket of $P_i$ | Value in $\mathbb{Z}_M$ |
| $(a,i) \prec (b,j)$ | $a < b$ **or** $(a = b$ and $i < j)$ | Lexicographic order |
| Ticket assignment | $number[i] := (\max_j number[j] + 1) \bmod M$ | Wraps around |
| Wait condition | $number[j] = 0$ **or** $(number[i],i) \prec (number[j],j)$ | Both must hold for all $j \neq i$ |
| Exit action | $number[i] := 0$ | Releases the ticket |
| State space size | $(2 \times M)^{N} \times (\text{pc states})^{N}$ | Finite → model-checkable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** The Bounded Bakery Protocol is used as a *benchmark algorithm* in formal verification because it requires reasoning about *atomicity*, *fairness*, *lexicographic ordering*, and *wrap-around arithmetic* — all in one compact algorithm. It is studied in operating systems (software mutual exclusion), distributed systems, and hardware arbiter design.

## 2.5 Correctness Properties

1. **Mutual Exclusion (Safety):** At any time, at most one process is in the critical section.
2. **Starvation-Freedom (Liveness, weak):** If a process $P_i$ is in the waiting state, it *eventually* enters the critical section under weak fairness.
3. **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS):** If $P_i$ finishes its doorway *before* $P_j$ starts its doorway, then $P_i$ enters the CS *before* $P_j$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Two Variables per Process?

In a single shared atomic register, mutual exclusion among $N$ processes would be impossible (Fischer's impossibility result at hardware level). The Bakery protocol uses **two arrays**:
- `choosing[i]` — a **doorway barrier** that prevents reading a half-written ticket.
- `number[i]` — the actual **priority** of $P_i$.

**Logic transition:** Without `choosing[i]`, process $P_i$ might read `number[j]` *during* the moment $P_j$ is updating it. By forcing $P_i$ to wait until $choosing[j] = false$, we ensure every observed `number[j]` is in a *stable* state.

## 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough: A 2-Process Scenario ($N=2$, $M=4$)

Let us trace the execution of $P_0$ and $P_1$ with initial state $number = [0, 0]$, $choosing = [false, false]$.

### Trace Table

| Step | Action | $choosing[0]$ | $choosing[1]$ | $number[0]$ | $number[1]$ | State |
|:----:|--------|:-------------:|:-------------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:------|
| 0    | Initial | false | false | 0 | 0 | Both idle |
| 1    | $P_0$ enters doorway | **true** | false | 0 | 0 | $P_0$ choosing |
| 2    | $P_0$ computes ticket | true | false | **1** | 0 | $P_0$ choosing |
| 3    | $P_0$ exits doorway | false | false | 1 | 0 | $P_0$ waiting |
| 4    | $P_0$ checks $P_1$: `choosing[1]=false` ✓; `number[1]=0` ✓ | false | false | 1 | 0 | $P_0$ enters CS |
| 5    | $P_0$ in critical section | false | false | 1 | 0 | $P_0$ in CS |
| 6    | $P_1$ enters doorway | false | **true** | 1 | 0 | $P_1$ choosing |
| 7    | $P_1$ computes ticket: $\max(0,1)+1 = 2$ | false | true | 1 | **2** | $P_1$ choosing |
| 8    | $P_1$ exits doorway | false | false | 1 | 2 | $P_1$ waiting |
| 9    | $P_1$ checks $P_0$: $choosing[0]=false$ ✓; $(2,1) \prec (1,0)$? NO, so wait | false | false | 1 | 2 | $P_1$ blocks |
| 10   | $P_0$ exits CS: $number[0] := 0$ | false | false | **0** | 2 | $P_0$ idle |
| 11   | $P_1$ re-evaluates: $number[0]=0$ ✓ | false | false | 0 | 2 | $P_1$ enters CS |
| 12   | $P_1$ exits CS: $number[1] := 0$ | false | false | 0 | **0** | $P_1$ idle |

**Result:** Mutual exclusion held throughout, $P_0$ was served first (FCFS), and the system returned to the initial state.

## 3.3 Formal Proof Sketch: Mutual Exclusion

**Claim:** No two distinct processes $P_i$ and $P_j$ can be in the CS simultaneously.

**Proof by contradiction:**

1. Suppose both $P_i$ and $P_j$ are in the CS at the same time, with $i < j$.
2. Since they passed their `await` checks, when $P_i$ last evaluated the condition for $P_j$, the **disjunction** held:
$$
\bigl(number[j] = 0\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl((number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j)\bigr) \tag{1}
$$
3. Similarly, when $P_j$ last evaluated the condition for $P_i$:
$$
\bigl(number[i] = 0\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl((number[j], j) \prec (number[i], i)\bigr) \tag{2}
$$
4. Since both are in the CS, $number[i] \neq 0$ and $number[j] \neq 0$. So the first disjuncts of (1) and (2) are false, leaving:
$$
(number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j) \quad \text{and} \quad (number[j], j) \prec (number[i], i)
$$
5. By definition of $\prec$, this implies both $number[i] < number[j]$ *and* $number[j] < number[i]$ — a **contradiction**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> The bounded version requires an *additional invariant* on the modulo arithmetic: it must hold that the lexicographic order is well-defined under wrap-around. The classical fix is to use $M = 2N$, ensuring that the "in-flight" tickets never wrap more than once during a contention episode.

## 3.4 State-Space Analysis for Model Checking

For model checkers like **SPIN** (Promela) or **nuXmv**, the reachable state space is bounded by:

$$
\vert \Sigma \vert \;=\; (2)^{N} \cdot (M)^{N} \cdot \bigl(\lvert PC \rvert\bigr)^{N}
$$

where $\lvert PC \rvert$ is the number of program counter values (typically 5: idle, choosing, waiting, cs, exit).

For $N = 3$ processes and $M = 6$, this gives $2^3 \cdot 6^3 \cdot 5^3 = 8 \cdot 216 \cdot 125 = 216{,}000$ states — small enough for **explicit-state model checking** in under a second. This is precisely why the Bounded Bakery Protocol is the *go-to* case study in automated verification courses.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Lifecycle (Mermaid State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> idle
    idle --> choosing : want to enter CS
    choosing --> waiting : number[i] := (max + 1) mod M
    waiting --> waiting : (number[i],i) > (number[j],j) for some j
    waiting --> cs : all j satisfy wait condition
    cs --> exit : done with CS
    exit --> idle : number[i] := 0
    exit --> [*] : termination
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The state `choosing` corresponds to the **doorway** (the critical atomic assignment of the ticket).
- The state `waiting` corresponds to the **busy-wait loop** over all $P_j$ with $j \neq i$.
- The transition `waiting → cs` is guarded by the predicate $\forall j \neq i: \text{safe}_j$ where $\text{safe}_j$ is the disjunction of the two wait conditions.
- The transition `exit → idle` performs $number[i] := 0$, releasing the slot.

## 4.2 Protocol Block Architecture (Mermaid Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Process Pi starts"] --> B["choosing[i] := true"]
    B --> C["Compute number i = max all + 1 mod M"]
    C --> D["choosing i := false"]
    D --> E{"Loop over all j not equal to i"}
    E --> F{"Is choosing j true"}
    F -- Yes --> E
    F -- No --> G{"Is number j zero OR (number i, i) less than number j, j"}
    G -- No --> E
    G -- Yes --> H["j processed"]
    H --> I{"All j processed"}
    I -- No --> E
    I -- Yes --> J["Enter Critical Section"]
    J --> K["Execute protected code"]
    K --> L["number i := 0"]
    L --> M["Return to remainder section"]
    M --> A
```

## 4.3 Global Variable Interactions (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Process P0"] -- "reads / writes" --> CH[choosing 0 to N-1]
    P1["Process P1"] -- "reads / writes" --> CH
    P2["Process PN-1"] -- "reads / writes" --> CH
    P0 -- "reads / writes" --> NUM[number 0 to N-1]
    P1 -- "reads / writes" --> NUM
    P2 -- "reads / writes" --> NUM
    CH -. "barrier semantics" .-> P0
    CH -. "barrier semantics" .-> P1
    CH -. "barrier semantics" .-> P2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The two arrays `choosing[]` and `number[]` are the *only* shared state.
- All arrows are bi-directional reads/writes (atomic register operations).
- The dotted arrows represent the **logical dependency**: a process $P_i$ must read `choosing[j]` *before* reading `number[j]`, which is the safety contract enforced by the doorway barrier.

## 4.4 Verification Pipeline (How Tools Verify the Protocol)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Promela / TLA+ / nuXmv model of Bounded Bakery"] --> B["Model Checker SPIN / TLC / nuXmv"]
    B --> C{"Property holds?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Print Verification Successful"]
    C -- "No" --> E["Counterexample Trace"]
    E --> F["Debug and Refine Model"]
    F --> A
```

**KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "how would you verify the Bounded Bakery Protocol?" in an exam, draw a similar pipeline and name the properties:
- *Safety:* $\lnot \exists$ state where $pc_i = cs \land pc_j = cs$ for $i \neq j$.
- *Liveness:* $\Box (pc_i = waiting \Rightarrow \Diamond (pc_i = cs))$ under weak fairness.
- *Non-Blocking:* $\Box \exists i: pc_i = idle$ (or appropriate variant).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Bakery Protocol and the Bounded Bakery Protocol. Why is the bounded version preferred in automated verification?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bakery Protocol | Bounded Bakery Protocol |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| Ticket range | Unbounded natural numbers | Finite set $\{0, 1, \dots, M-1\}$ |
| Hardware | Not directly realizable | Implementable on finite-width registers |
| State space | Infinite (unbounded) | Finite (bounded) |
| Verification | Requires induction / abstraction | Directly model-checkable |
| Bound $M$ | N/A | Typically $2N$ or $N$ |

The bounded version is preferred in automated verification because the resulting state space is **finite**, allowing exhaustive exploration by model checkers such as SPIN or nuXmv in finite time. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Define the lexicographic ordering used in the Bounded Bakery Protocol and state its role in tie-breaking.**

**Model Answer:**

The lexicographic ordering on pairs $(a, i) \in \mathbb{Z}_M \times \lbrace 0, \dots, N-1 \rbrace$ is defined as:

$$
(a,\, i) \prec (b,\, j) \;\equiv\; (a < b) \;\lor\; (a = b \;\land\; i < j)
$$

**Role:** When two processes $P_i$ and $P_j$ hold the **same ticket number** (a possible scenario in the bounded version due to wrap-around), the lexicographic order uses the *process ID* as a tie-breaker, ensuring a **total order** among waiting processes. This guarantees FCFS-like progress and prevents livelock. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question is split into two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. You are expected to attempt **one full question (both sub-parts)**.

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**State and explain the Bounded Bakery Protocol for $N$ processes. Clearly list the shared variables, the doorway, the waiting condition, and the exit action. Use $M = 2N$ as the ticket bound.**

**Model Answer:**

**Shared variables (3 marks):**
- `choosing[0..N-1]: array of Boolean`
- `number[0..N-1]: array of integers in {0, 1, ..., 2N-1}`

**Algorithm for process $P_i$ (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{forever do:} \\
&\quad \text{// Non-critical section} \\
&\quad choosing[i] := \text{true} \\
&\quad number[i] := \bigl(\max(number[0], \dots, number[N-1]) + 1\bigr) \bmod 2N \\
&\quad choosing[i] := \text{false} \\
&\quad \textbf{for } j := 0 \textbf{ to } N-1, \; j \neq i: \\
&\quad\quad \textbf{await } choosing[j] = \text{false} \\
&\quad\quad \textbf{await } \bigl(number[j] = 0\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl((number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j)\bigr) \\
&\quad \text{// Critical section} \\
&\quad number[i] := 0 \\
&\quad \text{// Remainder section}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **doorway** comprises the three assignments to `choosing[i]` and `number[i]`. The **waiting condition** is the nested `await` loop over all $P_j$. The **exit** is the single assignment `number[i] := 0`. The bound $M = 2N$ ensures that the tickets of in-flight processes never wrap around more than once during a single contention cycle, preserving correctness.

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Prove the mutual exclusion property of the Bounded Bakery Protocol. Is starvation-freedom guaranteed? Justify your answer with the help of fairness assumptions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mutual Exclusion Proof (5 marks):**
Assume for contradiction that $P_i$ and $P_j$ ($i \neq j$) are simultaneously in the critical section. WLOG let $i < j$. Since both processes are in the CS, $number[i] \neq 0$ and $number[j] \neq 0$. Therefore, when $P_i$ last checked its wait condition for $P_j$, it must have been that:

$$
(number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j) \tag{$\star$}
$$

Similarly, when $P_j$ last checked its condition for $P_i$:

$$
(number[j], j) \prec (number[i], i) \tag{$\star\star$}
$$

By the definition of $\prec$, ($\star$) implies $number[i] \leq number[j]$, while ($\star\star$) implies $number[j] \leq number[i]$. Hence $number[i] = number[j]$. But then $\prec$ reduces to $i < j$ from ($\star$) and $j < i$ from ($\star\star$) — a **contradiction**. $\blacksquare$

**Starvation-Freedom (2 marks):**
Under **weak fairness** (every continuously enabled action is eventually executed), the Bounded Bakery Protocol is *starvation-free*. The key intuition is: any process $P_j$ blocking $P_i$ from the CS either (a) has $number[j] = 0$ (it is not contending), or (b) is lexicographically larger than $P_i$. Case (b) processes must themselves progress because their tickets are bounded and they are also blocked by lexicographically-smaller tickets, leading to a well-founded ordering. Thus no process can be perpetually overtaken.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Explain how the Bounded Bakery Protocol is modeled in the SPIN model checker. Write the key parts of the Promela model and identify the LTL properties used to verify mutual exclusion and starvation-freedom.**

**Model Answer:**

**Promela skeleton (5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{bool choosing[N];} \\
&\text{byte number[N];} \\
&\text{proctype } P_i(\text{byte id}) \lbrace \\
&\quad \text{do} \\
&\quad\quad :: \text{/* non-CS */} \rightarrow \text{atomic} \lbrace \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{choosing[id] = true;} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{... compute number[id] = (max + 1) \% (2*N) ...} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{choosing[id] = false;} \\
&\quad\quad \rbrace; \\
&\quad\quad \text{for } (j \text{ in } 0..N-1) \lbrace \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{if } (j != id) \rightarrow \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{(choosing[j] == false);} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{((number[j] == 0) || ((number[id], id) < (number[j], j)));} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{fi} \\
&\quad\quad \rbrace; \\
&\quad\quad \text{/* critical section */} \\
&\quad\quad \text{number[id] = 0;} \\
&\quad \text{od} \\
&\rbrace
\end{aligned}
$$

**LTL properties (2 marks):**
- **Mutual Exclusion:** $\Box \lnot (pc_i = cs \land pc_j = cs)$ for all $i \neq j$, encoded in SPIN as `#define mutex ((pc[0] != cs) || (pc[1] != cs))` with `ltl mutex { [] mutex }`.
- **Starvation-Freedom:** $\Box (pc_i = waiting \Rightarrow \Diamond (pc_i = cs))$, requiring **weak fairness** annotation via `fair` or `progress` labels in Promela.

#### (b) `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Compare the Bounded Bakery Protocol with Dekker's and Peterson's mutual exclusion algorithms. Discuss the complexity, the number of shared variables, and the suitability for automated verification.**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Property | Dekker's | Peterson's | Bounded Bakery |
|----------|----------|------------|----------------|
| Number of processes | 2 only | 2 only | $N$ (arbitrary) |
| Shared variables per process | 3 (`flag[2]`, `turn`) | 2 (`flag[2]`, `turn`) | 2 arrays of size $N$ |
| Busy-wait | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Atomic primitive | Read/Write | Read/Write | Read/Write |
| Starvation-freedom | Yes | Yes | Yes (with fairness) |
| Bound on integer values | N/A (Boolean) | N/A (Boolean) | $M = 2N$ |
| Model-checkable easily | Yes | Yes | Yes (standard benchmark) |
| Real-world deployment | Rare | Common in OS textbooks | Theoretical/Verification |

**Discussion (2 marks):**
Dekker's and Peterson's algorithms are restricted to **2 processes** and use only Boolean variables — they are simpler to verify but do not scale. The Bounded Bakery Protocol **scales to $N$ processes** and serves as the standard *benchmark* in model-checking literature (SPIN, TLC, nuXmv) because it combines **arithmetic reasoning** (modular wrap-around), **lexicographic ordering**, and **interleaved concurrency** in a compact, finite-state model.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `choosing[]` array** — Many students omit the `choosing[i]` flag. This breaks the doorway barrier and invalidates mutual exclusion. **−2 marks minimum.**
> 2. **Using `<=` instead of `<`** in the lexicographic order — A common slip. The relation is strict ($\prec$), not weak.
> 3. **Confusing $M = N$ with $M = 2N$** — The bound $M = 2N$ is the *safe* classical choice; $M = N$ requires additional invariants. Examiners expect you to *state the bound explicitly*.
> 4. **Not mentioning fairness** — Starvation-freedom is **conditional on weak fairness**. Without fairness, the protocol is only *deadlock-free*, not starvation-free.
> 5. **Skipping the modulus operator** — In the bounded version, ticket assignment is `+1 mod M`, not `+1`. A missing `mod M` is a fatal error.
> 6. **Not defining the lexicographic order formally** — Examiners want the symbolic definition: $(a,i) \prec (b,j) \equiv (a < b) \lor (a = b \land i < j)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the Bounded Bakery Protocol:**

- [x] It is a **mutual exclusion algorithm** for $N$ concurrent processes using only **atomic read/write registers**.
- [x] **Two shared arrays:** `choosing[0..N-1]` (Boolean) and `number[0..N-1]` (in $\mathbb{Z}_M$).
- [x] The **bound** is typically $M = 2N$, ensuring the ticket space is finite.
- [x] **Doorway:** set `choosing[i] := true`, compute $number[i] := (\max + 1) \bmod M$, set `choosing[i] := false`.
- [x] **Waiting condition:** for every $j \neq i$, wait until $choosing[j] = false$ AND $(number[j] = 0$ OR $(number[i],i) \prec (number[j],j))$.
- [x] **Exit:** $number[i] := 0$.
- [x] **Lexicographic order:** $(a,i) \prec (b,j) \iff a < b \lor (a = b \land i < j)$.
- [x] **Mutual exclusion** holds for all executions (proven by contradiction).
- [x] **Starvation-freedom** holds under **weak fairness**.
- [x] **State space is finite** → ideal for **model checking** with SPIN / TLA+ / nuXmv.
- [x] The bounded version is the **standard benchmark** in automated verification courses.
- [x] **Real-world analogy:** the bakery queue with numbered tickets.
- [x] **Key invariant:** the lexicographic order is a total order on the set of waiting tickets.
- [x] **KTU exam mantra:** *doorway → barrier → wait → enter → release*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bakery Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Bakery Protocol ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Lamport's Bakery Algorithm** (commonly called the **Bakery Protocol**) is a foundational **distributed mutual exclusion algorithm** proposed by **Leslie Lamport in 1974**. It enables *N* concurrent processes running on independent processors to safely coordinate access to a shared **Critical Section (CS)** using only **atomic read and atomic write operations** on shared memory — without requiring hardware-supported Test-And-Set instructions. The protocol guarantees three essential correctness properties demanded by KTU's distributed systems module: - **Safety (Mutual Exclusion):** At most one process executes its critical section at any given time. - **Liveness (Deadlock-Freedom / Starvation-Freedom):** Every process that requests entry is eventually granted it. - **Ordering (Quasi First-Come-First-Served):** If process $P_i$ requested entry before $P_j$ in real time, $P_i$ enters its critical section no later than $P_j$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Bakery Protocol is the canonical example used to teach *mutual exclusion in distributed/loosely-coupled systems* where message passing replaces shared memory. It is also the standard test case for **model checking** and **TLA+/Promela** verification tools in PECNT526. ### 1.2 The Real-World Analogy: A Bakery Counter 🍞 Imagine walking into a popular bakery on a Sunday morning: 1. **Take a Number:** Every customer who walks in takes a paper ticket from a dispenser. The dispenser gives the next available integer in increasing order. 2. **Wait Your Turn:** The customer with the **smallest number** is served first. If two customers somehow hold the *same number* (rare, but possible if the dispenser glitches), the one with the **alphabetically smaller name** (acting as a tie-breaker) goes first. 3. **Get Served:** Only one customer is at the counter at a time — this is the **Critical Section** (the bread, the coffee, the interaction with the baker). 4. **Discard Ticket:** Once served, the customer discards their ticket (resets to zero), freeing the slot for the next wave. In this analogy: | Bakery Element | Distributed System Element | |----------------|---------------------------| | Customer | Process $P_i$ | | Ticket Number | `number[i]` — process's priority | | Choosing flag | `choosing[i]` — "in ticket line" indicator | | Smaller number / tie-breaker | Lexicographic ordering: (number, pid) | | Baker's counter | Critical Section | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Bakery"?** Lamport deliberately named the algorithm after a real bakery queue because the *de-facto* fairness rule in a bakery is "lowest ticket number wins," and *lexicographic ordering* handles the (rare) duplicate-number case. ### 1.3 Core Standard Metrics The following constants and variables are used throughout the KTU 2024 formulation: - **$N$**: Total number of processes (a finite, fixed positive integer) - **$P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_{N-1}$**: Process identifiers (indices), forming a totally ordered set - **$choosing[i] \in \{0, 1\}$**: Boolean flag for process $P_i$ — *"Is $P_i$ currently picking a ticket?"* - **$number[i] \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$**: Non-negative integer ticket of $P_i$, where **$0$** means *"not contending"* --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Operational Phases of a Process Every process $P_i$ cycles through **three logical regions**, analogous to a state machine that model checkers (SPIN, NuSMV, TLA+) explore exhaustively: 1. **Remainder / Non-Critical Section (NCS):** Process performs local computation unrelated to the shared resource. No interaction with other processes' state. 2. **Entry Protocol (Doorway + Waiting Room):** - *Doorway* — finite, bounded sequence: declare `choosing[i] = 1`, compute ticket, declare `choosing[i] = 0`. - *Waiting Room* — bounded busy-wait loop: spin until every other process $P_j$ has either a ticket of $0$, or a ticket strictly greater than (or tied but with higher PID than) $P_i$'s. 3. **Critical Section (CS):** The protected operation. 4. **Exit Protocol:** Single atomic statement: `number[i] = 0`. ### 2.2 The Lexicographic Ordering Operator (Total Ticket Order) The heart of the algorithm is a **total order** on pairs $(number, pid)$. Define for any two tickets $a, b$ and PIDs $i, j$: $$ (a, i) \prec (b, j) \;\iff\; \bigl(a < b\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a = b \;\land\; i < j\bigr) $$ > [!NOTE] > This is **NOT** a partial order. It is a *strict total order* — for any two distinct processes $P_i, P_j$, exactly one of $(number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j)$ or $(number[j], j) \prec (number[i], i)$ holds. This guarantees the algorithm can always pick a unique "winner." ### 2.3 The Pseudocode (Lamport's Canonical Form) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Shared data:} & \quad choosing[0 \ldots N-1] = \text{false} \\ & \quad number[0 \ldots N-1] = 0 \\[6pt] \text{Process } P_i \text{ executes:} & \\[4pt] \textbf{Entry:} & \\ & choosing[i] \leftarrow \textbf{true} \\ & number[i] \leftarrow 1 + \max\bigl(number[0], \ldots, number[N-1]\bigr) \\ & choosing[i] \leftarrow \textbf{false} \\ & \textbf{for } j = 0 \textbf{ to } N-1 \textbf{ do} \\ & \quad \textbf{while } choosing[j] \textbf{ do skip} \\ & \quad \textbf{while } number[j] \neq 0 \;\land\; (number[j], j) \prec (number[i], i) \textbf{ do skip} \\ \textbf{Critical Section} & \\ \textbf{Exit:} & \quad number[i] \leftarrow 0 \end{aligned} $$ ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Properties Cheat Sheet | Property | Mathematical Statement | Type | |----------|------------------------|------| | **Mutual Exclusion (Safety)** | $\forall i, j: \bigl(CS_i \land CS_j\bigr) \Rightarrow i = j$ | **Safety Invariant** | | **No Starvation (Liveness)** | $\forall i: \bigl(\text{request}(i) \bigcirc\!\rightarrow CS_i\bigr)$ — every request is *eventually* satisfied | **Liveness Property** | | **Ticket Strict Monotonicity** | If $P_i$ reads $P_j$'s ticket in its doorway, then $number[i] \neq number[j]$ (with a *proof-by-induction caveat*) | **Auxiliary Lemma** | | **Lex Order Total** | $\forall i \neq j: (number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j) \;\lor\; (number[j], j) \prec (number[i], i)$ | **Total Order** | | **Doorway Length** | Exactly **4 atomic statements** (read $choosing$ for all $j$, read $number$ for all $j$, max computation, two writes) | **Complexity** | | **Wait Complexity (worst)** | $P_i$ can be overtaken by at most $N - 1$ other processes before entering CS | **Bound** | | **Message Complexity** | $O(N)$ shared-memory reads per entry (NOT message-passing, but in the distributed variant: $3(N-1)$ messages) | **Cost** | | **Bounded Variables** | `choosing` is Boolean; `number` requires an *unbounded* integer (theoretical) but is *practically* bounded by $N$ concurrent contentions | **Memory** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse the **distributed** Bakery algorithm (where tickets are exchanged via messages) with the **shared-memory** Bakery algorithm (where `number[]` is a globally visible array). PECNT526's automated verification module almost always uses the **shared-memory** form because it is the one most amenable to model checking with Promela/SPIN. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Formal verification benchmark:** The Bakery algorithm is the "Hello, World!" of concurrent algorithm verification — used in nearly every textbook on TLA+, SPIN, and Coq. - **Database lock managers:** Real-world distributed DBs (e.g., Google Spanner's lock manager) use a Bakery-style *logical-timestamp + replica-id* tie-breaker. - **Multi-core CPU arbitration:** QPI / Infinity Fabric coherence protocols use analogous priority schemes. - **Embedded RTOS:** FreeRTOS port layers occasionally implement Bakery-style *mutex with priority inheritance* for low-core-count systems. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Mutual Exclusion Invariant **Goal:** Prove that for any two processes $P_i$ and $P_j$ ($i \neq j$), they cannot both be inside the critical section simultaneously. #### Step 1: Define the Critical Section Entry Condition Process $P_i$ enters its critical section only when the *inner wait loop terminates*: $$ \forall j \neq i: \Bigl( number[j] = 0 \Bigr) \;\lor\; \Bigl( (number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j) \Bigr) $$ Call this condition $\mathbf{E}(i)$ — *"$P_i$ is enabled to enter its CS."* #### Step 2: Assume the Contrapositive Suppose, for contradiction, both $P_i$ and $P_j$ are in the CS simultaneously. Then both $\mathbf{E}(i)$ and $\mathbf{E}(j)$ hold. #### Step 3: Apply the Total Order Since $i \neq j$, the total order $\prec$ on pairs gives exactly one of: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Case A: } & (number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j) \\ \text{Case B: } & (number[j], j) \prec (number[i], i) \end{aligned} $$ (They cannot be equal under the strict total order.) #### Step 4: Derive a Contradiction in Each Case - **In Case A:** $\mathbf{E}(j)$ requires that $number[i] = 0$ **OR** $(number[j], j) \prec (number[i], i)$. But we are in Case A, so the second disjunct is false. Therefore $number[i]$ must equal $0$. However, $P_i$ is *in its CS*, which (per the algorithm) means $number[i] \neq 0$. **Contradiction.** - **In Case B:** Symmetric argument — $number[j]$ must be $0$, but $P_j$ is in CS, so $number[j] \neq 0$. **Contradiction.** #### Step 5: Conclude Safety Since both cases lead to contradiction, our assumption that both $P_i$ and $P_j$ can be in CS is false. Hence: $$ \boxed{\;\text{At most one process executes CS at any time.} \;\iff\; \text{Safety (Mutual Exclusion)} \;\checkmark\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is a textbook example of a **safety proof by contradiction** that automated tools like TLA+'s TLC model checker mechanically verify by exhaustive state-space exploration for small $N$ (typically $N = 2, 3, 4$). ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: What Each Verification Approach Catches | Verification Method | Catches | Misses | Tool Example | |---------------------|---------|--------|--------------| | **Theorem Proving** (Hoare Logic / TLA+ proof) | All $N$, all interleavings — full proof of safety & liveness | None, if axioms are correct | Isabelle/HOL, Coq, TLAPS | | **Model Checking** (state-space enumeration) | Bugs in finite $N$ instances; counterexamples | Infinite $N$; subtle liveness violations on huge state spaces | SPIN, NuSMV, TLC | | **Runtime Verification** (monitor) | Property violations *during execution* | Violations on untested paths | RV-Monitor, JavaMOP | | **Symbolic Execution** | Path conditions leading to CS conflicts | Infinite loops without invariant | KLEE, CBMC | | **Abstract Interpretation** | Over-approximated reachable states | False positives; precision loss | Astrée, CPAchecker | ### 3.3 Computation of the Worst-Case Wait Bound **Claim:** In a system with $N$ processes using the Bakery protocol, a process $P_i$ that has just finished its doorway will be overtaken by at most $N - 1$ other processes before entering its CS. **Derivation sketch:** 1. After $P_i$ exits its doorway, $number[i]$ is fixed and non-zero for the duration. 2. Any other process $P_j$ that enters its doorway *after* $P_i$ reads $number[i]$ will compute a ticket strictly greater than $number[i]$ (since `max(...)` includes $P_i$'s ticket). Such $P_j$ will wait. 3. However, any $P_j$ that entered its doorway *before* $P_i$ read $max$, but read $max$ *after* $P_i$ finished the max computation, will get a *smaller or equal* ticket. 4. There are at most $N - 1$ such processes. 5. Each of those $N - 1$ processes enters CS exactly once, then resets $number$ to $0$, and thereafter cannot block $P_i$. Hence the wait bound: $$ \boxed{\;\text{Worst-case number of overtakings} = N - 1\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This **bounded overtaking** is precisely the *starvation-freedom* guarantee. KTU examiners love asking: *"Why is the Bakery protocol starvation-free but not strictly FIFO?"* The answer: bounded overtaking ⇒ no starvation, but the bound is $N-1$, not $1$ as in strict FIFO. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid State-Machine of a Process $P_i$ ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> NCS : process starts NCS --> Doorway : wants CS Doorway : Doorway (4 atomic steps) Doorway --> WaitingRoom : choosing[i]=false, number[i] set WaitingRoom : Waiting Room (busy-wait) WaitingRoom --> CS : for all j, condition holds CS : Critical Section CS --> ExitProtocol : work done ExitProtocol : number[i] = 0 ExitProtocol --> NCS : resets WaitingRoom --> WaitingRoom : some j blocks ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Global Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SharedMemory[Shared Memory Region] C[choosing array of size N] N[number array of size N] end subgraph ProcessorP0[Processor hosting P0] P0[Process P0] C0[choosing 0 flag] N0[number 0 ticket] end subgraph ProcessorP1[Processor hosting P1] P1[Process P1] C1[choosing 1 flag] N1[number 1 ticket] end subgraph ProcessorPNm1[Processor hosting PN-1] Pnm1[Process PN-1] Cnm1[choosing N-1 flag] Nnm1[number N-1 ticket] end P0 -. atomic read/write .-> C P0 -. atomic read/write .-> N P1 -. atomic read/write .-> C P1 -. atomic read/write .-> N Pnm1 -. atomic read/write .-> C Pnm1 -. atomic read/write .-> N C0 -. cell 0 .-> C N0 -. cell 0 .-> N C1 -. cell 1 .-> C N1 -. cell 1 .-> N Cnm1 -. cell N-1 .-> C Nnm1 -. cell N-1 .-> N ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Two-Process Contention Timeline ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P0 as Process P0 participant P1 as Process P1 participant Mem as Shared Memory Note over P0,P1: Both want to enter CS P0->>Mem: choosing[0] = true P0->>Mem: number[0] = max(0,0) + 1 = 1 P0->>Mem: choosing[0] = false Note over P0: Reads P1's number=0
Condition holds -> enters CS P1->>Mem: choosing[1] = true P1->>Mem: number[1] = max(0,0) + 1 = 1 P1->>Mem: choosing[1] = false P1->>Mem: Read number[0] = 1 Note over P1: Lex (1,0) < (1,1)
So P1 must WAIT P0->>Mem: number[0] = 0 (EXIT) Note over P1: P0's ticket is now 0
P1 enters CS P1->>Mem: number[1] = 0 (EXIT) ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Verification Workflow (TLA+/SPIN) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Write TLA+ spec of Bakery] --> B[Encode invariants:
MutualExclusion, NoStarvation] B --> C[Run TLC Model Checker] C --> D{State space < threshold?} D -- Yes --> E[Verification PASS for N=3] D -- No --> F[Apply symmetry reduction
or compositional reasoning] F --> C E --> G[Generate counter-example
trace if failure] C --> H[Use TLAPS for general N proof] H --> I[Mathematical safety proof complete] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Diagram Note:** Examiners often accept any clear *flowchart-style* rendering. The Mermaid diagrams above are *examination-ready* and can be redrawn by hand if needed — focus on the **three-region state machine** (NCS → Doorway → Waiting → CS → Exit). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *State the three correctness properties that Lamport's Bakery Protocol guarantees. Mention which one is a safety property and which is a liveness property.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Bakery Protocol guarantees: 1. **Mutual Exclusion (Safety Property):** No two processes can be in the critical section simultaneously. This is a *safety* property because it asserts "nothing bad happens." 2. **Starvation-Freedom (Liveness Property):** Every process that requests entry to the critical section will eventually be granted access. This is a *liveness* property because it asserts "something good eventually happens." 3. **Bounded Wait / FIFO-ish Ordering (Quasi-Liveness):** A process is overtaken by at most $N-1$ others. (Award 1 mark for safety identification, 1 for liveness, 1 for stating the third property and the $N-1$ bound.) --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *What is the role of the `choosing[i]` flag in Lamport's Bakery algorithm? Why is it necessary?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The flag `choosing[i]` indicates that process $P_i$ is currently *in the process of computing* its ticket number (i.e., it is inside its **doorway**). It is set to `true` just before reading the maximum ticket and reset to `false` afterwards. **Why it is necessary:** Without the `choosing` flag, a process $P_j$ could read a *partially computed* `number[i]` that is either stale or being concurrently updated, leading to two processes ending up with numerically equal tickets. The flag forces other processes to **busy-wait** on the doorway of $P_i$ until $P_i$ has finished writing its final ticket, ensuring that every ticket observed is **atomic** from the perspective of other processes. (1 mark for stating the role, 1 mark for the doorway concept, 1 mark for the atomicity argument.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]` **(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain Lamport's Bakery Algorithm in detail. Write the complete pseudocode for $N$ processes and explain the role of the `choosing` and `number` arrays.* **(b)** [7 Marks] *With the help of a suitable invariant, prove that Lamport's Bakery Algorithm satisfies the **Mutual Exclusion** property. Clearly state the invariant and show the proof by contradiction.* **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:** 1. **Background (1 mark):** Lamport's Bakery Algorithm (1974) solves distributed mutual exclusion using only atomic load/store operations on shared memory. It mimics a bakery queue where customers take numbered tickets. 2. **Shared Variables (1 mark):** - `choosing[0..N-1]` — Boolean array, initially `false`. - `number[0..N-1]` — non-negative integer array, initially `0`. 3. **Pseudocode (3 marks):** - Doorway: set `choosing[i] = true`; compute `number[i] = 1 + max(number[0..N-1])`; set `choosing[i] = false`. - Waiting: for each $j \neq i$, wait until `choosing[j] = false` AND either `number[j] = 0` OR $(number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j)$. - Critical Section: protected operation. - Exit: `number[i] = 0`. 4. **Role of arrays (2 marks):** - `choosing[i]`: prevents other processes from reading a partial ticket. It is the synchronization handshake for the doorway. - `number[i]`: holds the priority ticket; non-zero means the process is contending. **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:** 1. **Invariant (1 mark):** Define the predicate $E(i)$: $$ E(i): \forall j \neq i: \bigl(number[j] = 0\bigr) \lor \bigl((number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j)\bigr) $$ $P_i$ enters CS only when $E(i)$ holds. 2. **Statement to prove (1 mark):** $\forall i, j: i \neq j \Rightarrow \neg(CS_i \land CS_j)$, where $CS_k$ means "$P_k$ is currently executing its critical section." 3. **Proof by contradiction (4 marks):** - Assume for contradiction that both $P_i$ and $P_j$ are in CS with $i \neq j$. - Then $E(i)$ and $E(j)$ both hold. - By the **strict total order** on pairs, either $(number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j)$ or $(number[j], j) \prec (number[i], i)$. - **Case 1:** $(number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j)$. From $E(j)$, since $(number[i], i) \prec (number[j], j)$ is true, the second disjunct of $E(j)$ fails, so $number[i]$ must equal $0$. But $P_i$ in CS implies $number[i] \neq 0$. Contradiction. - **Case 2:** Symmetric. Yields $number[j] = 0$, contradicting $P_j$ in CS. - Hence both cases impossible; assumption false. **Mutual exclusion proved. ∎** 4. **Concluding remark (1 mark):** The proof relies critically on the **strict total order** of the lexicographic comparator $\prec$, which is why a *tie-breaker on process ID* is non-negotiable. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]` **(a)** [7 Marks] *Compare and contrast the Bakery Algorithm with the **Dekker's Algorithm** and **Peterson's Algorithm** for two-process mutual exclusion. Discuss their relative strengths and weaknesses, and explain why the Bakery Algorithm scales beyond two processes while the others do not.* **(b)** [7 Marks] *Describe how the Bakery Algorithm can be modeled and verified using the **TLA+ / PlusCal** language. Provide a high-level PlusCal sketch and explain which safety and liveness properties the TLC model checker can verify automatically.* **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:** | Aspect | Dekker | Peterson | Bakery (Lamport) | |--------|--------|----------|------------------| | **Processes Supported** | Exactly 2 | Exactly 2 | Arbitrary $N$ | | **Mechanism** | Turn flag + intent flag | Turn flag + intent flag | Ticket + PID tie-breaker | | **Memory Model** | Sequential consistency | Sequential consistency | Arbitrary (only atomic load/store) | | **Starvation-Free** | Yes (after fixes) | Yes | Yes | | **Fairness Guarantee** | Strict 2-process FIFO | Strict 2-process FIFO | Bounded wait ($N-1$ overtakes) | | **Verification Complexity** | Low | Low | Moderate (involves real numbers) | | **Hardware Assumption** | Atomic reads/writes of flags | Same | Same, but no memory-order assumption | **Key Insights (3 marks):** - Dekker and Peterson rely on a *binary* "turn" variable — they are inherently **2-process** protocols. Generalizing to $N$ requires $N$-way consensus, which Dekker/Peterson do not provide. - The Bakery algorithm's **lexicographic ordering** on $(number, pid)$ is a **total order** that works for any cardinality. This is what gives it scalability. - Dekker/Peterson require **strict sequential memory consistency** for correctness. The Bakery algorithm only requires **atomic single-variable load/store** — a much weaker assumption. **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:** **TLA+/PlusCal Sketch (4 marks):** ```tla VARIABLES choosing, number, cs TypeOK == /\ choosing \in [Proc -> BOOLEAN] /\ number \in [Proc -> Nat] /\ cs \in [Proc -> BOOLEAN] Init == /\ choosing = [p \in Proc |-> FALSE] /\ number = [p \in Proc |-> 0] /\ cs = [p \in Proc |-> FALSE] Enter(p) == /\ ~cs[p] /\ choosing' = [choosing EXCEPT ![p] = TRUE] /\ number' = [number EXCEPT ![p] = 1 + Max(number)] /\ choosing' = [choosing EXCEPT ![p] = FALSE] /\ \A q \in Proc \ {p}: /\ ~choosing[q] /\ \/ number[q] = 0 \/ <> < <> /\ cs' = [cs EXCEPT ![p] = TRUE] Exit(p) == /\ cs[p] /\ cs' = [cs EXCEPT ![p] = FALSE] /\ number' = [number EXCEPT ![p] = 0] /\ UNCHANGED choosing Spec == \E p \in Proc : Enter(p) \/ (cs[p] /\ Exit(p)) ``` **Verification in TLC (3 marks):** - **Safety Invariant to check:** $$ MutEx \;=\; \forall i, j \in Proc: (cs[i] \land cs[j]) \Rightarrow i = j $$ - **Liveness Property to check:** $$ \forall p \in Proc: \square(request(p) \Rightarrow \Diamond\, cs[p]) $$ This is expressed in TLA+ as the temporal formula `[](\A p \in Proc : want[p] => <> cs[p])` and requires enabling **fairness conditions** in the spec. - TLC exhaustively explores the state space for small $N$ (typically $N = 2, 3, 4$), and TLAPS (the TLA+ proof system) can be used to *prove* correctness for arbitrary $N$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Ways Students Lose Marks on Bakery Protocol Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `choosing` array** (–2 marks): Many students write a Bakery-like algorithm that omits the `choosing` flag. This breaks atomicity guarantees and the protocol may deadlock. Examiners deduct 2 marks immediately. > > 2. **Confusing strict and non-strict ordering** (–1 mark): The comparator $\prec$ must be **strict** (no equality allowed). Writing $(number[j], j) \preceq (number[i], i)$ is wrong. > > 3. **Ignoring the tie-breaker** (–2 marks): Without the PID tie-breaker, two processes with the same ticket number may both wait for each other forever — a deadlock. Always state the lexicographic order explicitly. > > 4. **Mixing up safety vs. liveness** (–1 mark): Mutual exclusion is **safety** (a bad thing — two in CS — must not happen). Starvation-freedom is **liveness** (a good thing — entry — must eventually happen). Examiners explicitly test this distinction in PECNT526. > > 5. **Stating "FIFO" instead of "bounded wait"** (–1 mark): The Bakery protocol is **not strictly FIFO** across $N$ processes. It guarantees a process is overtaken by at most $N-1$ others, not exactly $1$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - 🍞 The **Bakery Protocol** is Lamport's 1974 distributed mutual exclusion algorithm based on a bakery queue analogy (customers take numbered tickets). - 📦 It uses **two shared arrays**: `choosing[N]` (Boolean) and `number[N]` (non-negative integer), both initialized to `0`. - 🔑 The `choosing[i]` flag is **essential** — it prevents reading a partial ticket during another process's doorway. - 🎫 The ticket is computed as: $number[i] \leftarrow 1 + \max(number[0], \ldots, number[N-1])$. - ⚖️ The **lexicographic order** $(a, i) \prec (b, j) \iff a < b \lor (a = b \land i < j)$ provides a **strict total order** for tie-breaking. - 🔄 The lifecycle of $P_i$: **NCS → Doorway → Waiting Room → CS → Exit (reset to 0) → NCS**. - 🛡️ **Mutual Exclusion** is a *safety* invariant; **Starvation-Freedom** is a *liveness* property. - 📏 Worst-case overtaking bound: a process is overtaken by **at most $N-1$** others. - 🧠 Verification is typically done via **TLA+/PlusCal** (theorem proving) and **SPIN/NuSMV** (model checking for small $N$). - 🌐 It works under the **weakest** memory model: only atomic single-variable reads and writes are required (no Test-And-Set, no compare-and-swap). - ⚠️ The protocol is **not** strictly FIFO across $N$ processes; the term **bounded overtaking** is preferred. - 🆚 Compared to Dekker's and Peterson's, Bakery scales to **arbitrary $N$** while requiring only **atomic load/store**, not full sequential consistency. - 💡 In **PlusCal/TLA+**, the safety invariant is `MutEx == \A i, j \in Proc : (cs[i] /\ cs[j]) => i = j`. - 🔬 The Bakery algorithm is a **standard benchmark** in automated verification — expect it in model-checking labs and exam questions.

topics.title: Traffic Signalling System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT526 - AUTOMATED VERIFICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Traffic Signalling System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Traffic Signalling System (TSS) in Automated Verification** is a canonical *case study* in model checking used to demonstrate the modeling, specification, and verification of a safety-critical, real-time, concurrent reactive system. The system consists of traffic lights controlling vehicle flow at an intersection, a controller (timer/scheduler), and sensors (vehicle-presence detectors), all described as a **finite-state transition system** \(M = (S, S_0, T, L)\) over a set of boolean and enumerated state variables.

In the formal verification context (Clarke, Havelund, Kurshan), the Traffic Signalling System is reduced to a **Kripke Structure** so that temporal logic properties (CTL/LTL) can be checked using a model checker such as **SPIN**, **NuSMV**, or **UPPAAL**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy – The Air-Traffic Controller of a Road Junction**
> Imagine a busy four-way intersection. A human policeman with a whistle can coordinate cars. Now replace the policeman with a tiny computer chip (the *controller*). The chip must guarantee: *no two perpendicular streams of cars get the green light simultaneously* (Safety) and *every stream eventually gets a green light* (Liveness). In Automated Verification, we don't *test* this chip with random cars — we **mathematically prove** that *all* possible behaviours satisfy the rules. The Traffic Signalling System is the textbook playground to learn this proof technique.

## 1.3 System Components (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary)

| Component | Role | Typical Variables |
|-----------|------|-------------------|
| **Traffic Light** | Visual signal for each direction | `red`, `yellow`, `green` |
| **Controller** | Sequencer / timer | `counter`, `phase` |
| **Sensor** | Detects waiting vehicles | `car_N`, `car_S`, `car_E`, `car_W` |
| **Road** | The shared resource | `Intersection` |

## 1.4 Why Traffic Light Verification Matters (KTU Engineering Context)

> [!NOTE]
> A faulty traffic controller can lead to **deadlock** (all lights red forever) or **collision** (cross-traffic green simultaneously). Both are *safety* violations. In safety-critical embedded design (AUTOSAR, ISO 26262), such systems **must be exhaustively verified** — testing is insufficient because the state space is astronomically large.

![Real World Traffic Light Concept - Replaced by Mermaid Block Diagram Below]

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture

The Traffic Signalling System is decomposed into **four functional layers**:

1. **Environment Layer** – Cars (processes) arriving at lanes.
2. **Sensor Layer** – Boolean variables indicating car-presence.
3. **Controller Layer** – A synchronous transition system driven by a clock.
4. **Actuator Layer** – The lights (Red, Yellow, Green) themselves.

## 2.2 Kripke Structure Formalisation

The system is modelled as a Kripke Structure:

$$
M = (S, \; S_0, \; R, \; L)
$$

Where:
- $S$ = set of all global states (cartesian product of variable domains).
- $S_0 \subseteq S$ = set of initial states.
- $R \subseteq S \times S$ = total transition relation (each state has at least one successor).
- $L: S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ = labelling function mapping each state to a set of atomic propositions that hold there.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For model checking, the relation $R$ must be **total** (no dead-states) — this is the *deadlock-freedom* precondition. The Traffic Controller must be designed so that every state has a defined next state.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Modelling Logic

**Step 1 — Identify State Variables**
For a simple two-road (North-South / East-West) intersection with traffic lights:
- `L_NS` ∈ {red, green, yellow} → 3 values
- `L_EW` ∈ {red, green, yellow} → 3 values
- `counter` ∈ {0, 1, …, N-1} → N timing values

**Step 2 — Define Initial State**
The controller begins with NS = green, EW = red, counter = 0.

**Step 3 — Encode Transition Relation**
A guarded transition has the form:
$$
\text{(guard)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{(action)}
$$

Example (Yellow phase for NS before turning red):
$$
(\text{L\_NS} = \text{green} \;\land\; \text{counter} = T_{g}) \;\rightarrow\; (\text{L\_NS}' = \text{yellow}, \; \text{counter}' = 0)
$$

**Step 4 — Define Atomic Propositions**
$$
AP = \{\,\text{L\_NS=green}, \; \text{L\_EW=green}, \; \text{collision}, \; \text{deadlock}\,\}
$$

**Step 5 — Specify Properties in CTL**

| Property Type | CTL Formula | Meaning |
|---------------|-------------|---------|
| **Safety (Mutual Exclusion)** | $AG \neg (\text{L\_NS=green} \land \text{L\_EW=green})$ | Never both green simultaneously |
| **Liveness (No Starvation)** | $AG(\text{car\_NS} \rightarrow AF \; \text{L\_NS=green})$ | If a car waits, it eventually gets green |
| **Deadlock Freedom** | $AG \; EX(\text{true})$ | Every state has a successor |
| **No Collision** | $AG \neg \text{collision}$ | Collision is globally unreachable |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for the Traffic Signalling System case study. Memorise it.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables / Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Kripke Structure | $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ | $S$ states, $R$ relation |
| 2 | State-Space Size (product of variable domains) | $\vert S \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert$ | $V_i$ = domain of $i$-th variable |
| 3 | Lights-only state count (2 directions, 3 colours) | $3^2 = 9$ | Pure colour states |
| 4 | Full state count (with timer of size $N$) | $3 \cdot 3 \cdot N = 9N$ | Timer $N$ ticks |
| 5 | CTL Satisfaction (state $s$ satisfies $f$) | $M, s \models f$ | Boolean |
| 6 | Safety Template | $AG \, \neg \, \varphi$ | $\varphi$ = bad event |
| 7 | Liveness Template | $AG(p \rightarrow AF\,q)$ | $p$ = request, $q$ = grant |
| 8 | Fairness (Strong) | $GF\,p$ (path formula) | $p$ infinitely often |
| 9 | Fairness (Weak) | $FG\,p$ (eventually always) | Path formula |
| 10 | Bisimulation equivalence | $M_1 \approx M_2$ | Reduction technique |
| 11 | Synchronous Product (two sub-systems) | $M_1 \parallel M_2$ | $S = S_1 \times S_2$ |
| 12 | Maximum BFS depth for verification | $d \le \vert S \vert$ | Linear in state space |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** When asked to compute the state space, students often forget the **timer variable**. A pure colour-only count ($9$ states) is *incomplete* — the timer is mandatory because phase transitions depend on time.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The Traffic Signalling System model is the foundation of *Embedded Controller Verification* used by:
- **Automotive OEMs** (Bosch, Continental) for body controllers.
- **Avionics** (DO-178C compliant software) for runway light sequencers.
- **Railway Signalling** (ETCS Level 2/3) for interlocking verification.

The same pattern — *sensors + controller + actuators + safety properties in CTL* — is reused in **pacemakers**, **smart-grid controllers**, and **PLC ladder logic verification**.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive State-Space Derivation (2-Lane Intersection with Timer)

**Problem Setup (KTU-Style):**
Consider a simplified intersection with:
- Two lights: $L_{NS}$ and $L_{EW}$
- Each light has 3 possible colours: $\{ \text{R}, \text{G}, \text{Y} \}$
- A global counter $c$ that cycles from $0$ to $N-1$

**Derivation — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Domain of each variable**

$$
\text{Dom}(L_{NS}) = \{\text{R}, \text{G}, \text{Y}\} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \vert \text{Dom}(L_{NS}) \vert = 3
$$

$$
\text{Dom}(L_{EW}) = \{\text{R}, \text{G}, \text{Y}\} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \vert \text{Dom}(L_{EW}) \vert = 3
$$

$$
\text{Dom}(c) = \{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \vert \text{Dom}(c) \vert = N
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the product rule for Cartesian state space**

The total state space is the Cartesian product of all variable domains. Since the variables are independent, the cardinality multiplies:

$$
\vert S \vert \;=\; \vert \text{Dom}(L_{NS}) \vert \times \vert \text{Dom}(L_{EW}) \vert \times \vert \text{Dom}(c) \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values**

$$
\vert S \vert \;=\; 3 \times 3 \times N
$$

$$
\therefore \quad \vert S \vert \;=\; 9N
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (typical KTU value: $N = 5$)**

$$
\vert S \vert \;=\; 9 \times 5 \;=\; 45 \;\text{states}
$$

**Step 5 — Generalisation to a 4-way intersection (NS, EW, NE-SW, NW-SE)**

If four independent lights are present:

$$
\vert S \vert \;=\; 3^4 \times N \;=\; 81N
$$

For $N = 5$ this yields $405$ states — still tractable for a model checker like NuSMV.

## 3.2 Safety Property Verification (Mutual Exclusion)

**Property to prove:**
$$
\varphi \;\equiv\; AG \, \neg \, (L_{NS} = \text{G} \,\land\, L_{EW} = \text{G})
$$

**Proof by CTL Model Checking (fixed-point):**

Step 1 — Compute the set of states satisfying the atomic proposition:

$$
\text{Sat}(\,L_{NS}=\text{G}\,) \;=\; \{\, s \in S \mid s(L_{NS}) = \text{G} \,\}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the set of states satisfying the conjunction:

$$
\text{Sat}(\,L_{NS}=\text{G} \land L_{EW}=\text{G}\,) \;=\; \text{Sat}(L_{NS}=\text{G}) \cap \text{Sat}(L_{EW}=\text{G})
$$

Step 3 — Compute the complement (states that are safe):

$$
\text{Sat}(\,\neg\,(L_{NS}=\text{G} \land L_{EW}=\text{G})\,) \;=\; S \setminus \text{Sat}(L_{NS}=\text{G} \land L_{EW}=\text{G})
$$

Step 4 — Apply $AG$ via the greatest fixed-point:

$$
\text{Sat}(AG \, \psi) \;=\; \nu X \,.\, \text{Sat}(\psi) \cap \text{Pre}(X)
$$

Where $\text{Pre}(X)$ is the pre-image operator returning all states whose successors lie in $X$.

Step 5 — Verify $S_0 \subseteq \text{Sat}(AG\,\psi)$:

$$
\boxed{\;M, s_0 \models AG \, \neg \, (L_{NS}=\text{G} \land L_{EW}=\text{G})\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU valuation, the examiner awards marks for the **explicit invocation of the fixed-point** $\nu X$. Writing just "$AG$ means always" is *not* sufficient for full marks.

## 3.3 Liveness Verification Template

**Property:** Every car-arrival is eventually rewarded with a green light.

$$
\psi \;\equiv\; AG \,\bigl(\,\text{car\_NS} \;\rightarrow\; AF \; L_{NS}=\text{G}\,\bigr)
$$

**Evaluation (CTL semantics):**
For a state $s$ with $s \models \text{car\_NS}$, model checking must show that **every** path from $s$ reaches a state where $L_{NS}=\text{G}$. This is the $AF$ operator's universal-path quantifier.

If the counter increments monotonically and resets at phase changes, the result holds under *strong fairness* on the controller process.

## 3.4 State Explosion — Analytical Matrix

The Traffic Signalling System is the canonical example to **demonstrate the state-explosion problem**. The following table quantifies the issue:

| System Variant | Variables | Domain each | $\vert S \vert$ | Verifiable by NuSMV? |
|----------------|-----------|-------------|------------------|----------------------|
| 2 lights + 1 timer ($N=5$) | 3 | $\{3, 3, 5\}$ | $45$ | ✅ Trivial |
| 2 lights + 2 timers ($N=10$) | 4 | $\{3, 3, 10, 10\}$ | $900$ | ✅ Easy |
| 4 lights + 4 timers | 8 | $\{3^4, 10^4\}$ | $8.1 \times 10^5$ | ✅ Tractable |
| 4 lights + sensors (5 cars) | 13 | $\{3, 3, 3, 3, 2^5\}$ | $2.4 \times 10^5$ | ✅ Tractable |
| Distributed intersection (2 controllers) | 20 | product | $\sim 10^{12}$ | ❌ Need BDD/Abstraction |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Many students write "the system has infinite states" — *incorrect*. The Kripke Structure is **finite** (variables are bounded). The challenge is **size**, not infinity. The correct terminology is *state-space explosion*.
