# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define a Kripke Structure and explain how it is used to model a Traffic Signalling System. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A Kripke Structure is a 4-tuple $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$ where $S$ is a finite set of states, $S_0 \subseteq S$ are the initial states, $R \subseteq S \times S$ is a total transition relation, and $L : S \rightarrow 2^{AP}$ is a labelling function over atomic propositions.
For the Traffic Signalling System, each state is an assignment of values to variables $\{L_{NS}, L_{EW}, \text{counter}\}$. The transitions encode the controller logic. The labelling function identifies which lights are green, yellow, or red in each state, enabling CTL model checking of safety and liveness properties. (3 Marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **safety** and **liveness** properties with reference to the Traffic Signalling System. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A **safety property** states that "something bad never happens". In TSS, $AG\, \neg\,(L_{NS}=\text{G} \land L_{EW}=\text{G})$ is a safety property ensuring no perpendicular collision. A **liveness property** states that "something good eventually happens", e.g., $AG(\text{car\_NS} \rightarrow AF\;L_{NS}=\text{G})$ ensures that a waiting car is eventually granted a green light. Safety is verified using the $\nu$ (greatest) fixed-point, while liveness uses the $\mu$ (least) fixed-point in CTL model checking. (3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Model a two-way traffic intersection (North-South and East-West roads) as a Kripke Structure suitable for model checking. Define the variables, initial state, and transition relation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Specify the following properties in CTL and explain their satisfaction on your model:
   (i) Mutual exclusion between NS and EW green signals.
   (ii) Liveness: a waiting NS car eventually gets a green light.
   (iii) Deadlock-freedom. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Kripke Modelling — 7 Marks**

**Variables (2 Marks):**
- $L_{NS} \in \{\text{R}, \text{G}, \text{Y}\}$
- $L_{EW} \in \{\text{R}, \text{G}, \text{Y}\}$
- $c \in \{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\}$ (timer)
- $car_{NS}, car_{EW} \in \{\text{true}, \text{false}\}$ (sensors)

**Initial State (1 Mark):**
$$
S_0 = \{\, s \mid s(L_{NS}) = \text{G},\; s(L_{EW}) = \text{R},\; s(c) = 0,\; s(car_{NS}) = \text{false},\; s(car_{EW}) = \text{false} \,\}
$$

**Transition Relation (4 Marks):** Defined by guarded rules, e.g.,

| Guard | Action (Next State) |
|-------|---------------------|
| $L_{NS}=\text{G} \land c < T_g$ | increment $c$ |
| $L_{NS}=\text{G} \land c = T_g$ | $L_{NS}'=\text{Y}, c'=0$ |
| $L_{NS}=\text{Y} \land c = T_y$ | $L_{NS}'=\text{R},\; L_{EW}'=\text{G},\; c'=0$ |
| $L_{EW}=\text{G} \land c = T_g$ | $L_{EW}'=\text{Y},\; c'=0$ |
| $L_{EW}=\text{Y} \land c = T_y$ | $L_{EW}'=\text{R},\; L_{NS}'=\text{G},\; c'=0$ |

**State-Space (bonus, in-depth):**
$$
\vert S \vert = 3 \times 3 \times N \times 2 \times 2 = 36N
$$

---

**(b) CTL Properties — 7 Marks**

**(i) Mutual Exclusion (2 Marks):**
$$
\varphi_1 \;\equiv\; AG\,\neg\,(L_{NS}=\text{G} \land L_{EW}=\text{G})
$$
Satisfaction: $M, s_0 \models \varphi_1$ because the transition rules never allow both lights to be green simultaneously.

**(ii) Liveness (3 Marks):**
$$
\varphi_2 \;\equiv\; AG\,\bigl(\,car_{NS} \rightarrow AF\, L_{NS}=\text{G}\,\bigr)
$$
Satisfaction requires that from every state with $car_{NS}=\text{true}$, **all** infinite paths reach a state where $L_{NS}=\text{G}$. This is provable because the controller's round-robin schedule guarantees NS gets green within at most $T_g + T_y$ time units.

**(iii) Deadlock-Freedom (2 Marks):**
$$
\varphi_3 \;\equiv\; AG\; EX(\text{true})
$$
Satisfaction: every state has at least one successor (the counter is always non-stuck because the timer strictly increments modulo $N$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A)**
> - ❌ Forgetting the timer variable $c$ → loses 2 marks.
> - ❌ Writing $EG$ instead of $AG$ for safety → loses 1 mark.
> - ❌ Confusing $AF$ (universal path) with $EF$ (existential path) for liveness → loses 2 marks.
> - ✅ Always state the **fixed-point** used ($\nu$ for $AG$, $\mu$ for $AF$) to score full marks.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the *state-space explosion problem* with reference to the Traffic Signalling System. Compute the number of reachable states for: (i) 2 lights + 1 timer ($N=4$); (ii) 4 lights + 4 timers ($N=10$); (iii) A distributed system of 2 such intersections. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss two *mitigation techniques* (BDD-based symbolic model checking, partial-order reduction, or abstraction refinement) applicable to the Traffic Signalling System. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) State-Space Explosion (7 Marks)**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
State-space explosion occurs when the number of global states grows exponentially with the number of variables, making explicit-state model checking infeasible. In the Traffic Signalling System, adding sensors, lanes, or interconnected intersections causes combinatorial blow-up.

**Computations (5 Marks):**

**(i) 2 lights + 1 timer ($N=4$):**
$$
\vert S \vert = 3 \times 3 \times 4 = 36 \;\text{states}
$$

**(ii) 4 lights + 4 timers ($N=10$):**
$$
\vert S \vert = 3^4 \times 10^4 = 81 \times 10000 = 810{,}000 \;\text{states}
$$

**(iii) 2 distributed intersections (synchronous product):**
$$
\vert S \vert = (3^2 \times 4)^2 = 36^2 = 1{,}296 \;\text{states}
$$

For richer models with sensor vectors of 10 booleans and 6 lights, the count easily exceeds $10^9$, exceeding RAM limits of explicit-state tools.

---

**(b) Mitigation Techniques (7 Marks)**

**Technique 1 — BDD-Based Symbolic Model Checking (3.5 Marks):**
Binary Decision Diagrams (Bryant, 1986) encode the transition relation and state sets as compressed boolean functions. The Traffic Light model $M$ is represented as a single BDD; CTL checking operates on BDDs using fixed-point computations. State sets with $10^{20}$ elements can often be represented in megabytes, since the BDD exploits structural regularity (e.g., the round-robin schedule has high redundancy).

**Technique 2 — Partial-Order Reduction (POR) (3.5 Marks):**
Independent transitions (e.g., $L_{NS}$'s colour change and $c$'s increment) can be explored in any order without affecting property satisfaction. Ample-set and persistent-set POR algorithms prune the explored state graph. For the Traffic Signalling System, POR reduces the effective state space to only one representative interleaving per equivalence class, often cutting the graph by 60–80%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B)**
> - ❌ Stating *abstraction* without specifying *what* is abstracted → loses 2 marks.
> - ❌ Confusing BDDs with Decision Trees → loses 1 mark.
> - ❌ Giving only the formula for state-space but not numerical answers → loses 1 mark per sub-part.
> - ✅ Always tie the technique back to the **Traffic Signalling System** specifically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Traffic Signalling System**

- 🔹 The Traffic Signalling System is modelled as a **Kripke Structure** $M = (S, S_0, R, L)$.
- 🔹 Standard variables: $L_{NS}$, $L_{EW}$, $counter$, $car_{NS}$, $car_{EW}$.
- 🔹 State-space formula: $\vert S \vert = \prod_i \vert V_i \vert$ (product of variable domains).
- 🔹 For 2 lights + timer of size $N$: $\vert S \vert = 9N$.
- 🔹 **Safety** properties use the template $AG\, \neg\, \varphi$ (no collision).
- 🔹 **Liveness** properties use $AG(p \rightarrow AF\,q)$ (eventual green).
- 🔹 **Deadlock-freedom** is encoded as $AG\, EX(\text{true})$.
- 🔹 **Fairness** conditions (strong $GF\,p$ / weak $FG\,p$) are crucial for proving liveness.
- 🔹 $AG$ uses the **greatest fixed point** $\nu X$; $AF$ uses the **least fixed point** $\mu X$.
- 🔹 State-space explosion is a **size** problem, not infinity. Mitigations: BDDs, POR, Abstraction.
- 🔹 Tools: **NuSMV** (symbolic), **SPIN** (explicit-state), **UPPAAL** (timed automata).
- 🔹 Application domains: automotive ECUs, avionics, railway interlocking, medical devices.
- 🔹 Key distinction: $AF$ = *all* paths eventually; $EF$ = *some* path eventually. Confusing them costs marks.
- 🔹 The total transition relation is mandatory — partial relations violate model-checking preconditions.
- 🔹 KTU board expects the *fixed-point operator* to appear in any $AG/AF$ justification for full marks.
